title: COMPONENT DISPOSAL
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Visible evidence includes disposal logs, chain-of-custody documentation, and digital audit trails that record when and how components were disposed of. Observers should see physical signs of disposal processes (e.g., severed cables on decommissioned routers, physically shredded media, or documented disk cleaning certificate).
• Technical artifacts include screenshots or extracts from systems showing the execution of disposal scripts, configuration files that define approved disposal methods, and system alerts that verify key removals and secure data erasures.
• Operational patterns indicating control activity are periodic reconciliations between inventory and disposal logs, evidence of automated notifications of disposal actions, and regular audits confirming that defined techniques (e.g., cryptographic key destruction, disk sanitization) are executed at appropriate times.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations may simply produce a policy statement or checklists that claim disposal is performed, but without actual records or evidence of executed disposal actions.
• Surface-level implementations may include manually signing off on disposed components without maintaining chain-of-custody records or relying solely on technology that superficially erases data without verifying that recovery is impossible.
• Technical gaps include failure to verify the destruction of embedded electronic data (e.g., in firmware or cryptographic keys) and a lack of integration between disposal processes and inventory management systems.

VALIDATION STEPS:
1. CHECK: Review and correlate disposal logs with inventory records.
   WHY: Ensures that all disposed components have a recorded, evidence-based, and verifiable disposal event.
   HOW: Interview asset management personnel; check system logs; confirm that disposal entries include timestamps, responsible personnel, and clearly indicate the disposal technique used.
   FAIL: Missing log entries, mismatches between inventory and disposal records, or evidence that disposal actions were not logged in real-time.

2. CHECK: Verify that disposal-specific configuration files and automated scripts are present.
   WHY: Demonstrates that organizations have technical controls in place to perform consistent disposal actions (e.g., disk sanitization scripts, automated cryptographic key deletion routines).
   HOW: Access configuration management databases or source repositories to review the existence and recent use of scripts/tools; test a disposal process in a controlled environment if permissible.
   FAIL: Scripts or tools are outdated, non-existent, or have never been executed as per change/tracking logs.

3. CHECK: Inspect physical evidence of disposal for hardware components.
   WHY: Physical evidence such as sealed destruction certificates, witnesses for on-site destruction, or signs of physical tampering supports that the component has actually been rendered unusable.
   HOW: Visit destruction sites or review third-party destruction reports; examine documentation of physical destruction for routers, servers, or storage devices.
   FAIL: No third-party verification, inadequate chain-of-custody documentation, or missing destruction certificates.

4. CHECK: Evaluate disposal process audit trails (system logs or alert histories) for evidence of cryptographic key removal and secure file deletion.
   WHY: Authenticated audit trails that include secure deletion events provide strong evidence of compliance with technical disposal controls.
   HOW: Query system logs for events tagged with key removal, file sanitization, or secure deletion; verify with IT security logs that disposal commands have been executed successfully.
   FAIL: Absence of audit trail data, uncorrelated deletions, or signs of manual override that bypass automated audit mechanisms.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Verification Shortcuts: Quickly check for integration between the asset inventory system and disposal logs; look for recent disposal action timestamps that correlate with known decommissioning cycles.
• Critical Indicators: A lack of chain-of-custody details or missing cryptographic key revocation events often signals a superficial implementation. A robust log that records both physical and digital disposal actions is critical.
• Details That Matter: Consistency in script/tool usage, regularly updated logs, and documented review processes are key. Pay attention to unusual gaps in operational records and discrepancies between documented disposal events and actual asset inventory records.
• Remember: Automated evidence is difficult to fake. Always triangulate findings with physical evidence (e.g., hardware destruction certificates) and key removal audit trails to ensure comprehensive implementation.
content:
Statement:
Dispose of [Assignment: organization-defined data, documentation, tools, or system components] using the following techniques and methods: [Assignment: organization-defined techniques and methods].

Guidance:
Data, documentation, tools, or system components can be disposed of at any time during the system development life cycle (not only in the disposal or retirement phase of the life cycle). For example, disposal can occur during research and development, design, prototyping, or operations/maintenance and include methods such as disk cleaning, removal of cryptographic keys, partial reuse of components. Opportunities for compromise during disposal affect physical and logical data, including system documentation in paper-based or digital files; shipping and delivery documentation; memory sticks with software code; or complete routers or servers that include permanent media, which contain sensitive or proprietary information. Additionally, proper disposal of system components helps to prevent such components from entering the gray market.

title: COMPONENT AUTHENTICITY
methodology:
Control ID: SR-11 – COMPONENT AUTHENTICITY

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Look for documented anti-counterfeit policies and procedures placed in operational manuals and asset management records. Physical labeling on components, accompanied by chain-of-custody tracking, can serve as evidence. Records of configuration control for service/repair components also indicate adherence.
• Technical Artifacts: Automated logs and system alerts from anti-counterfeit scanning tools; validated inventories showing authorized component sources; digital certificates or vendor verifications available in system management tools; training logs showing personnel attended anti-counterfeit training sessions.
• Operational Patterns: Regularly scheduled scans with verifiable results; proactive reporting of suspect components to designated external entities (e.g., CISA); evidence of configuration control for components awaiting service or repair; recurring reviews of process logs and checklists in daily operations.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Faked Compliance: Organizations might simply maintain updated policy documents and checklists without actual follow-through in operational practices. Uploaded logs to satisfy audit checkboxes may be manipulated or incomplete.
• Surface-Level Implementations: Relying solely on documentation without correlating with physical or technical evidence, such as a lack of real-time scanning logs or absence of physical component verification.
• Frequently Missed Technical Gaps: Inconsistencies in the application of anti-counterfeit scanning (e.g., infrequent scans), poorly documented repair/service component configuration controls, or failure to tie training records directly to counterfeit detection procedures. Reporting structures might exist only on paper without integration into the incident response system.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Verify automated anti-counterfeit scanning logs for recent activities.
WHY: Consistent and traceable scanning logs demonstrate that the system is actively monitoring for counterfeit components.
HOW: Access logging systems, review scan schedules and outputs, and correlate timestamps with organizational policy requirements.
FAIL: Lack of recent scans or irregular scan frequency, or missing log details.

CHECK: Review configuration management records for components in repair or service.
WHY: Demonstrates effective control over component configuration and secure handling until return to service.
HOW: Examine configuration control databases, repair logs, and change management systems for verification that components are tracked throughout their lifecycle.
FAIL: Absence of detailed records, untracked revisions, or irregular documentation of components under service.

CHECK: Conduct interviews and training record reviews for personnel tasked with counterfeit detection.
WHY: Ensures that staff are properly trained and informed on detecting counterfeit components, which is crucial for real operational effectiveness.
HOW: Review training logs, interview a sample of technical personnel to confirm understanding, and verify training frequency relative to policy and compliance requirements.
FAIL: Outdated or missing training records, or personnel being unable to articulate counterfeit detection methods.

CHECK: Validate incident reporting practices by reviewing evidence of past counterfeit component reports.
WHY: Reporting incidents to designated external entities establishes that the control not only exists but is actively used when anomalies are detected.
HOW: Request incident response/notification records, check for evidence that counterfeit detections were reported to either component vendors, manufacturers, or external bodies like CISA.
FAIL: No incident reports on record, or identical repeat reports indicating that issues are not being properly addressed.

CHECK: Cross-check procurement records with inventory and vendor certifications.
WHY: Ensures that all components are sourced only from legitimate, verified suppliers, reducing the risk of counterfeit parts.
HOW: Review procurement documentation, corresponding inventory records, and vendor certificates. Look for automated cross-verification in supply chain systems.
FAIL: Discrepancies between purchased components and inventory sources, or absence of vendor validation artifacts.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut by correlating automated scanning alerts with physical checklists; genuine control should always produce consistent digital and physical evidence.
• Critical Indicators of Poor Implementation: Missing or outdated scanning logs, lack of correlation between configuration records and physical assets, and personnel who are unaware of counterfeit detection procedures.
• Key Details: The frequency of anti-counterfeit scans, the audit trail in configuration control systems, and the integration of training with real operational processes are vital. Don’t rely solely on policy documentation that can be easily faked – look for dynamic, operational evidence.
• Extra Verification: Validate that repair/service records are updated promptly and reviewed periodically, highlighting any outlier components or unresolved reports.

This methodology focuses on concrete, operational evidence over mere documentation, ensuring that each control is both implemented and effective in protecting against counterfeit system components.
content:
Statement:
a. Develop and implement anti-counterfeit policy and procedures that include the means to detect and prevent counterfeit components from entering the system; and
b. Report counterfeit system components to [Selection (one or more): source of counterfeit component; [Assignment: organization-defined external reporting organizations]; [Assignment: organization-defined definition of roles]].

Guidance:
Sources of counterfeit components include manufacturers, developers, vendors, and contractors. Anti-counterfeiting policies and procedures support tamper resistance and provide a level of protection against the introduction of malicious code. External reporting organizations include CISA.

Enhancements:
(1) COMPONENT AUTHENTICITY | ANTI-COUNTERFEIT TRAINING
Train [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles] to detect counterfeit system components (including hardware, software, and firmware).
(2) COMPONENT AUTHENTICITY | CONFIGURATION CONTROL FOR COMPONENT SERVICE AND REPAIR
Maintain configuration control over the following system components awaiting service or repair and serviced or repaired components awaiting return to service: [Assignment: organization-defined system components].
(3) COMPONENT AUTHENTICITY | ANTI-COUNTERFEIT SCANNING
Scan for counterfeit system components [Assignment: organization-defined frequency].
Discussion: The type of component determines the type of scanning to be conducted (e.g., web application scanning if the component is a web application).
Related Controls: RA-5.

title: INSPECTION OF SYSTEMS OR COMPONENTS
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for SR-10 - INSPECTION OF SYSTEMS OR COMPONENTS.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Evidence of routine physical or remote inspection activities (e.g., dated inspection logs, photographic evidence, video surveillance) is a clear indicator the control is active.
• Digital artifacts such as inspection logs from automated systems, audit trail entries, or system alerts on tampering incidents demonstrate compliance.
• Operational patterns that confirm inspections are performed at organization-defined frequencies or upon specific trigger events (e.g., post-travel, dispatch from a high-risk area) indicate the control’s active enforcement.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may manually fabricate logs or simply include inspection dates without actual operational follow-up. For example, printed logs might be backdated or include generic text rather than system-generated entries.
• Surface-level implementations often rely solely on documented procedures without establishing traceable physical/digital evidence; merely having policies on paper does not indicate effective tamper detection.
• Technical gaps include failure to actually correlate indicator events (e.g., travel from high-risk regions) with triggered inspection events; lack of automated log correlations can hide non-compliance.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Verify Inspection Log Integrity
WHY: Authentic, system-generated logs provide evidence that inspections are being performed at the defined intervals and in response to specific events, thereby reducing manual log tampering.
HOW: Review timestamped, digitally-signed inspection logs and cross-reference with trigger events (e.g., receipt of employee travel notifications, changes in packaging or shipment details). Verify logs entries are generated by automated systems or authenticated inspection tools.
FAIL: Inspection records are only documented on paper or show signs of manual alteration (e.g., uniform, repetitive entries without corresponding event details).

CHECK: Assess Triggered Inspection Procedures for High-risk Scenarios
WHY: Ensuring that systems are inspected upon return from high-risk travel or after encountering suspicious supply chain changes confirms the control responds to indications of tampering.
HOW: Interview responsible personnel and corroborate with inspection reports; review incident records that document follow-up inspections for employees returning from or components sourced from high-risk locations.
FAIL: No evidence exists of increased or triggered inspections when high-risk indicators (such as flagged travel routes or packaging anomalies) occur, highlighting a gap in operational responsiveness.

CHECK: Validate Random Inspection Process
WHY: Regular, randomly selected inspections help deter tampering by reducing predictability for potential attackers.
HOW: Obtain and analyze audit trails that show random selection of systems/components for inspection over a defined period. Verify that the random sample complies with the organization’s defined frequency and selection methodology.
FAIL: Evidence shows a lack of randomness (e.g., only on predetermined systems) or no documentation confirms the use of a defined random selection process.

CHECK: Confirm Tamper Detection Mechanisms
WHY: Effective detection mechanisms should identify physical or logical tampering with systems/components removed from controlled areas.
HOW: Inspect system/component tamper-evident features (such as seals, sensor logs, chassis integrity sensors) and validate that any anomalies or alerts generated were investigated and documented.
FAIL: Absence of tamper-evident markers, or investigation logs are missing/incomplete, indicating that tampering signs are not reviewed or logged appropriately.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Verification Shortcut: Directly correlate system-generated inspection notifications with external trigger events (e.g., employee travel records) to quickly establish if reactive inspections are functioning.
• Critical Indicator: Ensure inspection log entries include non-replicable details like timestamps, digital signatures, and cross-referenced incident IDs. Uniform, generic logs are a red flag.
• Key Detail: Look for evidence of automated alerts and physical sensor data logs — these are far less likely to be backdated or fabricated compared to manual entries.
• Consider interviewing operational staff as their insight on whether the inspection process actually happens in real time is as crucial as the logs themselves.

By confirming these checks and looking for the outlined signs, you can effectively validate that the tamper-resistance inspection process is not only documented but operationally enforced in a manner that protects the organization against both physical and logical tampering risks.
content:
Statement:
Inspect the following systems or system components [Selection (one or more): at random; at [Assignment: organization-defined frequency], upon [Assignment: organization-defined indications of need for inspection]] to detect tampering: [Assignment: organization-defined systems or system components].

Guidance:
The inspection of systems or systems components for tamper resistance and detection addresses physical and logical tampering and is applied to systems and system components removed from organization-controlled areas. Indications of a need for inspection include changes in packaging, specifications, factory location, or entity in which the part is purchased, and when individuals return from travel to high-risk locations.

title: TAMPER RESISTANCE AND DETECTION
methodology:
Below is a detailed validation methodology for SR-9: TAMPER RESISTANCE AND DETECTION.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible evidence might include tamper-evident seals on hardware, logs showing anomaly detection related to firmware or configuration changes, and visible markers of system self-check routines during start-up or operation.
   • Technical artifacts include cryptographic signatures on firmware images, checksum validations, and logging outputs from integrity scanning tools. Automated notifications that trigger when a modification is detected are strong evidence.
   • Operational patterns include recurring self-integrity tests, automated alerts to system administrators on detection events, and periodic cryptographic re-verification of critical system components throughout the system development life cycle.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations sometimes claim tamper detection simply by relying on static policies or documentation without active technical measures. Relying solely on off-line assessments or manual inspections can be superficial.
   • Some implementations may include anti-tamper measures only in a subset of the system (for instance, only at boot time) rather than a comprehensive coverage across the entire life cycle. 
   • Common gaps include lack of cryptographic validation, no dynamic self-check routines, and failure to secure update mechanisms – often these are implemented in theory (as policy) but not executed in operational workflows.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify Cryptographic Integrity Checks
   WHY: The presence of cryptographic signatures and runtime checksum validations on firmware and critical software proves that tamper detection is integrated into the boot process and operation.
   HOW: Inspect bootloader configuration settings for cryptographic signature enforcement, review audit logs showing integrity check failures or confirmations, and test the reaction when a checksum mismatch is simulated.
   FAIL: Lack of signature validation steps, disabled integrity checks, or absence of audit logs on verification outcomes indicate a failed check.

   CHECK: Inspect Tamper-Evident Hardware Measures
   WHY: Physical tamper resistance such as sealed enclosures or tamper-evident labels on hardware confirm that tamper protection is considered in the design and manufacturing processes.
   HOW: Conduct a physical inspection of system components for tamper-evident seals, review manufacturing process documentation for anti-tamper packaging standards, and check maintenance logs for any seal breaches alongside system alerts.
   FAIL: Observing unsealed hardware or mismatches between physical inspections and expected tamper-evident markers suggests poor implementation.

   CHECK: Review Automated Tamper Detection Logs and Alerts
   WHY: Operational logs and automated alerts offer real-time evidence of active tamper detection mechanisms that trigger when anomalies occur.
   HOW: Access system management consoles to retrieve logs from integrity scanning tools or intrusion detection systems integrated into the device’s firmware. Verify that logs record events properly and that alert notifications are sent to appropriate security operations centers.
   FAIL: If logs lack entries when modifications are simulated, or if alerts are absent, this indicates that detection mechanisms are either not operational or not properly configured.

   CHECK: Assess Integration Across the System Development Life Cycle
   WHY: True tamper resistance should span the entire life cycle – from design through manufacturing, distribution, and maintenance. This comprehensive integration demonstrates proactive defense.
   HOW: Interview engineering and operations teams, review technical artifacts from development (source code obfuscation records, self-check routines) to manufacturing (tamper-evident packaging procedures) and operations (runtime integrity checks). Look for evidence that anti-tamper measures are continuously validated and updated.
   FAIL: If evidence is found only at the operational phase or missing during key phases (e.g., manufacturing), it indicates incomplete lifecycle coverage.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: Directly request a live demonstration of simulated tamper events – a properly configured system should trigger immediate alerts and log entries.
   • Indicator: Absence or delay in logging integrity check failures is a critical red flag that tamper detection is not functioning as designed.
   • Note: Pay close attention to update mechanisms. Ensure changes to system firmware/software are accompanied by re-verification steps, as weak update controls can be a common bypass.
   • Emphasize observational evidence over mere policy documentation – always seek dynamic testing results and automated alerts that are difficult to fake.

This multifaceted approach helps ensure that tamper resistance and detection controls are not only documented but are effectively implemented and operational in real-world scenarios.
content:
Statement:
Implement a tamper protection program for the system, system component, or system service.

Guidance:
Anti-tamper technologies, tools, and techniques provide a level of protection for systems, system components, and services against many threats, including reverse engineering, modification, and substitution. Strong identification combined with tamper resistance and/or tamper detection is essential to protecting systems and components during distribution and when in use.

Enhancements:
(1) TAMPER RESISTANCE AND DETECTION | MULTIPLE STAGES OF SYSTEM DEVELOPMENT LIFE CYCLE
Employ anti-tamper technologies, tools, and techniques throughout the system development life cycle.
Discussion: The system development life cycle includes research and development, design, manufacturing, acquisition, delivery, integration, operations and maintenance, and disposal. Organizations use a combination of hardware and software techniques for tamper resistance and detection. Organizations use obfuscation and self-checking to make reverse engineering and modifications more difficult, time-consuming, and expensive for adversaries. The customization of systems and system components can make substitutions easier to detect and therefore limit damage.
Related Controls: SA-3.

title: NOTIFICATION AGREEMENTS
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Visible evidence includes documented agreements, contracts, or Memoranda of Understanding (MOUs) with supply chain entities that specifically outline notification triggers related to supply chain compromises, audit results, or other organization-defined information exchanges. 
• Technical artifacts may include logs of automated notifications, incident response tickets referencing such notifications, or email chains from supply chain partners confirming the execution of the established procedures. 
• Operational patterns that indicate active control include periodic reviews or tests of the notification process, and consistent, timely communications when potential supply chain issues arise.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations might present static documentation (agreements or policies) without demonstrating timely and effective operational evidence of actual notifications.
• Some may fail to integrate the notification procedures with automated alert systems, instead relying solely on ad hoc or manual processes that are difficult to verify.
• Superficial compliance might include outdated or incomplete agreements that don’t cover all relevant notification scenarios, or agreements that exist only on paper without operational enforcement.

VALIDATION STEPS:
1. CHECK: Verify the existence of formal agreements/MOUs with supply chain entities that include clear notification triggers and responsibilities.
   WHY: This proves that the organization has established a contractual basis for sharing information on supply chain-related incidents.
   HOW: Request and review copies of agreements or contracts; match the documented requirements with the operational criteria defined by the organization.
   FAIL: Absence of formal agreements or evidence that the agreements don’t include specific notification triggers for supply chain compromises.

2. CHECK: Review system logs and incident/ticketing records for instances of supply chain compromise notifications.
   WHY: Automated notifications and documented follow-ups provide tangible evidence that the procedures are actively used, not just on paper.
   HOW: Query incident management and email logging systems for notifications related to supply chain issues over a defined period; verify timestamps and recipient correspondence.
   FAIL: No log entries or notifications related to supply chain issues during the period, or incidents handled outside the documented process.

3. CHECK: Interview operational staff or auditors to confirm routine testing and exercising of the notification procedures.
   WHY: Operational effectiveness is corroborated by firsthand accounts of drill exercises, testing, or real events where the notification agreements were enacted.
   HOW: Conduct interviews or review meeting minutes that reference tests of supply chain compromise notifications; cross-reference with system logs.
   FAIL: Inconsistent or no evidence of testing procedures, or staff unable to detail how notifications were executed during recent drills or events.

4. CHECK: Assess integrated communication tools (e.g., secure messaging platforms, email systems) to ensure they are configured per the notification agreements for automated alerts.
   WHY: Proper technical configuration validates that the control is embedded within the IT environment to prompt timely responses.
   HOW: Review configuration settings, automated alert rules, and system notifications; test with a simulated trigger if possible.
   FAIL: Misconfigurations, disabled alert rules, or absence of integration with the established notification procedures.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Focus on correlating procedural documents with real-world system logs; outdated or mere written policies are a red flag if not supported by operational evidence.
• Prioritize automated processes as they are less subject to manipulation—check system configuration and audit trails for authenticity.
• Be wary of agreements that lack clear criteria for triggering notifications or do not have an associated operational review process; critical details include defined roles, communication channels, and timelines.
• Consider using a simulated test (where allowed) to observe if the notification procedure triggers as documented, ensuring that the technical and operational elements are fully integrated.

This methodology ensures that organizations are not only documenting notification agreements but are actively and effectively implementing them across the supply chain.
content:
Statement:
Establish agreements and procedures with entities involved in the supply chain for the [Selection (one or more): notification of supply chain compromises; results of assessments or audits; [Assignment: organization-defined information]].

Guidance:
The establishment of agreements and procedures facilitates communications among supply chain entities. Early notification of compromises and potential compromises in the supply chain that can potentially adversely affect or have adversely affected organizational systems or system components is essential for organizations to effectively respond to such incidents. The results of assessments or audits may include open-source information that contributed to a decision or result and could be used to help the supply chain entity resolve a concern or improve its processes.

title: SUPPLY CHAIN OPERATIONS SECURITY
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Visible evidence includes documented and implemented procedures that specifically address the protection of supply chain-related information. Look for policy revisions that incorporate supplier and supply chain risk management, supplier contract addendums mandating operational security controls, and operational logs demonstrating active monitoring of supply chain interactions.
• Technical artifacts such as system configuration files, audit logs of supplier communications, monitoring alerts, and integration points between supply chain management systems and corporate IT security monitoring indicate compliance. Evidence might also be seen in security assessment reports that include supply chain risk evaluations.
• Operational patterns such as scheduled risk assessments that include supply chain elements, regular supplier security reviews, and incident response processes tailored for supply chain exposures show that the control is active.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations may fake compliance by producing solely policy documents that mention supply chain security without evidence of real-world operational enforcement or technical integration.
• Surface-level implementations include having supplier contracts that state security requirements but failing to enforce them with technical controls, such as not monitoring or segmenting supplier-related systems.
• Technical gaps frequently missed include not integrating logging and monitoring for supplier interactions, omitting supply chain risk in periodic assessments, and the absence of automated enforcement mechanisms bridging supplier controls with internal monitoring systems.

VALIDATION STEPS:
CHECK: Validate that documented procedures and security configurations explicitly address supply chain OPSEC controls.
WHY: Demonstrates that the organization has formally recognized and operationalized supply chain security risks.
HOW: Interview risk management and supply chain security personnel; review documented procedures, security configuration management records, and policy revisions that include supplier-specific measures.
FAIL: Absence of documented procedures addressing supply chain risks or evidence that configurations were not updated to incorporate supplier risk factors.

CHECK: Verify technical controls are in place for systems interacting with suppliers (e.g., network segmentation, specific access controls, and logging).
WHY: Ensures technical measures prevent unauthorized access to sensitive supply chain information.
HOW: Access system and network configuration files; inspect firewall and access control rules specifically applied to supplier communication points; review event logs and change management records for these systems.
FAIL: Discovery of default, loosely defined configurations or lack of logging for systems handling supplier-related information.

CHECK: Confirm that monitoring and logging are capturing and alerting on supply chain-related communications or operations.
WHY: Effective monitoring provides early detection of unauthorized or anomalous supply chain activities, which is critical for timely incident response.
HOW: Examine audit logs and SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) alerts that track interactions with supplier systems; verify that anomalies trigger appropriate notifications; review historical logs for regular pattern checks.
FAIL: Lack of comprehensive logs or absence of automated alerts regarding supplier system exchanges.

CHECK: Assess the integration of supply chain risk assessments into overall operational security reviews.
WHY: Regular risk assessments that explicitly incorporate supply chain concerns indicate proactive risk management and the ability to adapt controls based on identified vulnerabilities.
HOW: Review risk assessment reports; verify that supply chain elements are included in the threat analysis; interview risk assessment teams about how supply chain risks are mitigated operationally.
FAIL: Risk assessments that lack any mention of supply chain risks or demonstrate no correlation with implemented operational security updates.

CHECK: Evaluate the contractual integration of security requirements into supplier agreements, ensuring technical enforcement.
WHY: Binding contractual agreements that require technical and operational controls help enforce security measures across the supply chain.
HOW: Review supplier contracts and SLAs for clauses on operational security requirements; confirm that these requirements are reflected in system access controls and auditing mechanisms; verify that periodic compliance audits include supplier systems.
FAIL: Contracts that list security requirements in theory but show no evidence of translation into enforced technical measures or periodic review.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Verification shortcuts include leveraging SIEM dashboards to quickly filter and review supplier communication logs which must show anomalies or periodic access reviews.
• Critical indicators of poor implementation are missing or incomplete audit trails on supplier interactions, and lack of alignment between risk assessment outcomes and technical deployments.
• Focus on verifying not just the existence of documentation but on automated processes and periodically generated evidence (like logs, alerts, and audit reports) that confirm the active enforcement of supply chain operational security controls.
• Always cross-reference supplier contract details with technical configurations—if either side is lacking, it could point to a gap that adversaries might exploit.
content:
Statement:
Employ the following Operations Security (OPSEC) controls to protect supply chain-related information for the system, system component, or system service: [Assignment: organization-defined Operations Security (OPSEC) controls].

Guidance:
Supply chain OPSEC expands the scope of OPSEC to include suppliers and potential suppliers. OPSEC is a process that includes identifying critical information, analyzing friendly actions related to operations and other activities to identify actions that can be observed by potential adversaries, determining indicators that potential adversaries might obtain that could be interpreted or pieced together to derive information in sufficient time to cause harm to organizations, implementing safeguards or countermeasures to eliminate or reduce exploitable vulnerabilities and risk to an acceptable level, and considering how aggregated information may expose users or specific uses of the supply chain. Supply chain information includes user identities; uses for systems, system components, and system services; supplier identities; security and privacy requirements; system and component configurations; supplier processes; design specifications; and testing and evaluation results.

title: SUPPLIER ASSESSMENTS AND REVIEWS
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for NIST SR-6: Supplier Assessments and Reviews

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible evidence includes documented supplier risk assessment processes with defined review frequencies, including meeting records, review schedules, and reports.
   • Technical artifacts may include logs from automated monitoring systems tracking open-source intelligence and risk indicators, contracts evidencing independent third‐party evaluation, and integration of supplier risk data into broader risk management systems.
   • Operational patterns include regular updates to risk ratings, corrective actions based on assessment outcomes, and evidence that assessments extend to supply chain elements like quality control, shipping, and development processes.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations may simply produce outdated or superficial documentation that declares a risk assessment process without evidence of active, ongoing reviews.
   • Some may fake compliance by providing one-off reports rather than demonstrating continuous monitoring or periodic independent reviews.
   • Technical gaps often lie in incomplete integration of open-source intelligence feeds, inadequately addressing second- or third-tier supplier risks, or failing to act upon assessment findings.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   
   CHECK: Verify the existence and currency of a formal, documented supplier risk assessment program that specifies review frequency.
   WHY: A formal, current process confirms that assessments are not only planned but are actively managed and updated, in line with defined frequencies.
   HOW: Review internal process documents, meeting minutes, and scheduled review calendars; check that the documentation reflects recent updates and actions taken.
   FAIL: If documentation is outdated, incomplete, or only exists on paper without evidence of recurring reviews, actual control effectiveness is in doubt.
   
   CHECK: Validate the involvement of independent third-party assessments or reviews of the supplier risk evaluations.
   WHY: External reviews enhance objectivity and can reveal blind spots in internally conducted assessments.
   HOW: Examine contracts, review reports, and certifications from independent parties; interview staff to confirm regular use of third-party evaluations.
   FAIL: Absence of any third-party feedback or reliance solely on internal reviews may indicate a compromised review process.
   
   CHECK: Confirm the presence and functioning of automated monitoring systems that integrate open-source intelligence and other relevant risk indicators into the supplier review process.
   WHY: Automated systems provide continuous, real-time data ensuring that emerging risks are quickly identified and addressed.
   HOW: Validate system logs, alert histories, and dashboard displays that track supplier risk signals; verify integration points with risk management applications.
   FAIL: If there are no logs of automated alerts or if monitoring systems are not linked with risk assessment processes, the control may be inadequately implemented.
   
   CHECK: Assess that the supplier assessments encompass detailed evaluations of specific supply chain elements—including development, manufacturing, quality control, and shipping processes—as well as oversight of second- and third-tier suppliers.
   WHY: Comprehensive coverage ensures that the entire risk landscape of the supply chain is being considered, not just high-level supplier attributes.
   HOW: Review risk assessment frameworks and sample detailed reports; conduct interviews with supply chain and risk management personnel to confirm that multiple risk aspects are evaluated.
   FAIL: If assessments are generic, missing critical supply chain stages, or neglect input from subordinate tiers, then the evaluation is likely incomplete.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: Ask for real-time dashboards and recent automated alert logs; these cannot be easily faked compared to merely presenting policy documents.
   • Critical Indicator: Look for evidence of corrective actions – a reactive trail following identified risks signals that the process is operationally sound.
   • Due Diligence: Validate that the review frequency in documentation matches the operational evidence; discrepancies here often signal superficial compliance.
   • Detail Focus: Review evidence related to second-tier and third-tier supplier assessments – these are frequently overlooked yet critical areas for supply chain integrity.

By focusing on these practical validation steps and observable evidence, auditors can effectively determine whether the organization not only has a documented process, but also implements and maintains an operationally effective supplier assessment and review program.
content:
Statement:
Assess and review the supply chain-related risks associated with suppliers or contractors and the system, system component, or system service they provide [Assignment: organization-defined frequency].

Guidance:
An assessment and review of supplier risk includes security and supply chain risk management processes, foreign ownership, control or influence (FOCI), and the ability of the supplier to effectively assess subordinate second-tier and third-tier suppliers and contractors. The reviews may be conducted by the organization or by an independent third party. The reviews consider documented processes, documented controls, all-source intelligence, and publicly available information related to the supplier or contractor. Organizations can use open-source information to monitor for indications of stolen information, poor development and quality control practices, information spillage, or counterfeits. In some cases, it may be appropriate or required to share assessment and review results with other organizations in accordance with any applicable rules, policies, or inter-organizational agreements or contracts.

Enhancements:
(1) SUPPLIER ASSESSMENTS AND REVIEWS | TESTING AND ANALYSIS
Employ [Selection (one or more): organizational analysis; independent third-party analysis; organizational testing; independent third-party testing] of the following supply chain elements, processes, and actors associated with the system, system component, or system service: [Assignment: organization-defined supply chain elements, processes, and actors].
Discussion: Relationships between entities and procedures within the supply chain, including development and delivery, are considered. Supply chain elements include organizations, entities, or tools that are used for the research and development, design, manufacturing, acquisition, delivery, integration, operations, maintenance, and disposal of systems, system components, or system services. Supply chain processes include supply chain risk management programs; SCRM strategies and implementation plans; personnel and physical security programs; hardware, software, and firmware development processes; configuration management tools, techniques, and measures to maintain provenance; shipping and handling procedures; and programs, processes, or procedures associated with the production and distribution of supply chain elements. Supply chain actors are individuals with specific roles and responsibilities in the supply chain. The evidence generated and collected during analyses and testing of supply chain elements, processes, and actors is documented and used to inform organizational risk management activities and decisions.
Related Controls: CA-8, SI-4.
References: [FASCB], [14 CFR 201], [ISO 13873], [ISO 27036], [FIPS 140-3], [FIPS 180-4], [FIPS 186-4], [FIPS 202], [SP 800-30], [SP 800-161], [IR 7622], [IR 8272].

title: ACQUISITION STRATEGIES, TOOLS, AND METHODS
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Contracts and procurement records include explicit clauses addressing supply chain risk mitigation (e.g., tamper‐evident packaging, verified vendor lists, blind or filtered buys).
• Evidence of a formal supply chain risk assessment process that informs procurement strategies is available through assessment reports, audit trails, or vendor risk scoring data.
• Procurement systems and contract management tools show operational integration of acquisition strategies with automated alerts or review checkpoints for risk mitigation.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may produce documentation or policy statements that mention mitigations without evidence of integration into actual procurement workflows.
• Superficial contract language may exist without corresponding enforcement mechanisms or review of vendor performance against supply chain security standards.
• Compliance may be faked by using off-the-shelf contract tools that do not enforce tailored acquisition strategies or by failing to periodically update risk assessments in response to evolving threats.

VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Procurement Policy and Contract Clause Review
WHY: Ensures that supply chain risk mitigations such as tamper‐evident packaging, controlled distribution, and filtered buys are explicitly required in acquisition contracts.
HOW: Review a sample set of current contracts and procurement policies. Interview procurement officials to confirm the operational use of these clauses and compare against referenced policy documentation.
FAIL: Absence of specific language or clauses addressing supply chain risks in procurement documents, or failure to demonstrate how these measures are enforced.

CHECK: Supply Chain Risk Assessment Integration
WHY: Demonstrates that acquisition strategies are driven by risk-based decisions and that assessments actively inform contracting methods and vendor selection.
HOW: Examine risk assessment reports and documentation showing how results are mapped to acquisition strategies. Validate that risk mitigation actions are triggered in the procurement process.
FAIL: Lack of updated risk assessment reports or evidence that risk findings are not linked to procurement strategy decisions.

CHECK: Supplier and Vendor Evaluation Process
WHY: Verifies that only trusted suppliers and vendors who meet predefined supply chain risk criteria are engaged through the acquisition process.
HOW: Audit records of supplier evaluations, validated vendor lists, and logs from contract management tools. Check evidence of periodic re-assessment of vendors for compliance with supply chain security standards.
FAIL: Evidence of contracting with unvetted or non-compliant vendors, or absence of automated controls that flag or filter high-risk suppliers.

CHECK: Implementation of Technical Controls
WHY: Confirms that practical, technical measures (e.g., tamper‐evident packaging and controlled distribution channels) are applied during the acquisition process.
HOW: Conduct field inspections of sampled procured components, review shipping and handling logs, and verify that packaging meets tamper‐evident standards. Check integrated systems that track distribution channel compliance.
FAIL: Lack of evidence or verification process for tamper‐evident packaging, or on-site inspections revealing non-compliant packaging or processes.

CHECK: Process Automation and Audit Logging
WHY: Operational controls should provide automated alerts and audit logs to verify that supply chain risk mitigations are effective in real-time procurement activities.
HOW: Review system logs from procurement and contract management tools for automated alerts linked to supply chain risk events, and check reports on process anomalies related to vendor selection and order fulfillment.
FAIL: No automated logging or alert system in place, or logs that fail to capture critical supply chain risk events.

EXPERT TIPS
• Leverage automated contract management systems to periodically flag and report on contracts lacking supply chain risk specific language.
• Verify that risk assessments are recurrent and reflect current threat landscapes; a dated risk report might expose reliance on obsolete controls.
• During vendor evaluations, look for third-party certifications or prior audit reports as a strong indicator of a trusted supply chain partner.
• Always correlate technical evidence (e.g., tamper‐evident features) with process documentation; gaps between the two may indicate superficial compliance.
• Prioritize continuous monitoring evidence (automated logs, real-time alerts) as these are less prone to falsification compared to static documentation.
content:
Statement:
Employ the following acquisition strategies, contract tools, and procurement methods to protect against, identify, and mitigate supply chain risks: [Assignment: organization‐defined acquisition strategies, contract tools, and procurement methods].

Guidance:
The use of the acquisition process provides an important vehicle to protect the supply chain. There are many useful tools and techniques available, including obscuring the end use of a system or system component, using blind or filtered buys, requiring tamper‐evident packaging, or using trusted or controlled distribution. The results from a supply chain risk assessment can guide and inform the strategies, tools, and methods that are most applicable to the situation. Tools and techniques may provide protections against unauthorized production, theft, tampering, insertion of counterfeits, insertion of malicious software or backdoors, and poor development practices throughout the system development life cycle. title: PROVENANCE
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence includes documented provenance baselines maintained as part of the system’s configuration management records. Look for artifacts such as timestamped change logs, signed handovers, system inventories that list unique component identifiers (serial numbers, SWID tags, RFID labels), and chain-of-custody documents generated at each lifecycle stage.
• Technical artifacts include automated audit logs that track changes to configurations and provenance records, digital certificates or cryptographic signatures on firmware/software, and supplier declarations or attestation reports. 
• Operational patterns indicating an active control are regular validation of provenance records during system audits, integration of provenance monitoring into continuous configuration monitoring, and observable remediation when provenance discrepancies are noted.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Documentation may be fabricated or kept static without proper periodic updates. Just having a policy or a manual log without external validation or automation is a common evasion.
• Surface-level implementations include using generic labels that aren’t tied to cryptographic or immutable audit logs, thereby failing to provide a reliable chain-of-custody.
• Gaps often occur when organizations do not integrate provenance verification into their automated monitoring systems, making it easier to manually alter records without triggering alarms.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Existence of a Provenance Baseline Document & Change Logs
   WHY: Demonstrates that a historically accurate, timestamped record of system/component origins, updates, and ownership transfers is maintained.
   HOW: Request an export of system configuration records and change logs from your change management tool; verify these records are digitally signed or include immutable timestamps, and that they reflect lifecycle events from development to operations.
   FAIL: No digital timestamps, missing records, or reliance solely on manual, unsynchronized logs indicates an insufficient provenance process.

2. CHECK: Verification of Unique Component Identification
   WHY: Unique identifiers (e.g., serial numbers, SWID tags, RFID labels) are needed to track the provenance and confirm system components have not been replaced or tampered with.
   HOW: Cross-reference physical inspections (or automated scans) of components with inventory databases; verify that each critical component is uniquely identifiable and that records match supplier documentation.
   FAIL: Inconsistent or missing component identifiers, or mismatches between physical labels and system records, signal a gap in the provenance process.

3. CHECK: Validation Mechanisms for Authenticity & Integrity
   WHY: The use of cryptographic controls and tamper-evident measures ensures prevents unauthorized modifications and verifies that components/software have not been altered.
   HOW: Use automated tools to perform cryptographic signature verifications on firmware/software components; inspect audit logs for non-repudiation evidence; review any side-channel analysis or physical unclonable functions (PUFs) records if applicable.
   FAIL: Inability to verify digital signatures, absence of tamper-evident evidence, or incomplete cryptographic records indicates a failure in authenticating provenance.

4. CHECK: Integration of Supply Chain Provenance into Operational Monitoring
   WHY: Consistent, automated monitoring of provenance throughout the supply chain indicates that the organization actively manages and tracks component origins and transfers.
   HOW: Verify that provenance data (such as supplier declarations, tagging records, and inventory updates) is integrated into a centralized monitoring system with alarm thresholds for discrepancies; perform a simulated chain-of-custody audit.
   FAIL: Isolated databases or manual reconciliation processes with long intervals between updates suggest that the monitoring of supply chain provenance is ineffective.

EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Focus on evidence from automated tools (version control logs, cryptographic validation reports, and continuous monitoring dashboards) rather than solely on policy documentation.
• Critical Indicator: Discrepancies between the digital provenance data and physical labels are a major red flag indicating potential tampering.
• Detail to Note: Ensure that all provenance records are not only maintained but are also periodically audited against independent sources (e.g., supplier attestations, independent supply chain assessments) to prevent forgery or superficial compliance.
• Verify the integration of provenance validation into incident response procedures, as timely detection of provenance alterations is as important as routine record keeping.

This practical methodology provides a layered verification approach that focuses on observable, technical, and operational evidence of the control's effectiveness, minimizing the risk of compliance being just a paper exercise.
content:
Control Statement
Document, monitor, and maintain valid provenance of the following systems, system components, and associated data: [Assignment: organization-defined systems, system components, and associated data].

Discussion
Every system and system component has a point of origin and may be changed throughout its existence. Provenance is the chronology of the origin, development, ownership, location, and changes to a system or system component and associated data. It may also include personnel and processes used to interact with or make modifications to the system, component, or associated data. Organizations consider developing procedures (see SR-1) for allocating responsibilities for the creation, maintenance, and monitoring of provenance for systems and system components; transferring provenance documentation and responsibility between organizations; and preventing and monitoring for unauthorized changes to the provenance records. Organizations have methods to document, monitor, and maintain valid provenance baselines for systems, system components, and related data. These actions help track, assess, and document any changes to the provenance, including changes in supply chain elements or configuration, and help ensure non-repudiation of provenance information and the provenance change records. Provenance considerations are addressed throughout the system development life cycle and incorporated into contracts and other arrangements, as appropriate.

## Related Controls
CM-8, MA-2, MA-6, RA-9, SA-3, SA-8, SI-4.


Enhancements:
PROVENANCE | IDENTITY – Establish and maintain unique identification of the following supply chain elements, processes, and personnel associated with the identified system and critical system components: [Assignment: organization‐defined supply chain elements, processes, and person associated with organization‐defined systems and critical system components]. Discussion: Knowing who and what is in the supply chains of organizations is critical to gaining visibility into supply chain activities. Without reasonable visibility into supply chain elements, processes, and personnel, it is very difficult for organizations to understand and manage risk and reduce their susceptibility to adverse events. Related Controls: IA-2, IA-8, PE-16.
PROVENANCE | TRACK AND TRACE – Establish and maintain unique identification of the following systems and critical system components for tracking through the supply chain: [Assignment: organization‐defined systems and critical system components]. Discussion: Tracking the unique identification of systems and system components during development and transport activities provides a foundational identity structure for the establishment and maintenance of provenance. For example, system components may be labeled using serial numbers or tagged using radio-frequency identification tags. Labels and tags can help provide better visibility into the provenance of a system or system component. A system or system component may have more than one unique identifier. Identification methods are sufficient to support a forensic investigation after a supply chain compromise or event. Related Controls: IA-2, IA-8, PE-16, PL-2.
PROVENANCE | VALIDATE AS GENUINE AND NOT ALTERED – Employ the following controls to validate that the system or system component received is genuine and has not been altered: [Assignment: organization‐defined controls]. Discussion: For many systems and system components, especially hardware, there are technical means to determine if the items are genuine or have been altered, including optical and nanotechnology tagging, physically unclonable functions, side‐channel analysis. Related Controls: IA-2, IA-8, PE-16.
PROVENANCE SUPPLY CHAIN INTEGRITY — PEDIGREE – Employ [Assignment: organization‐defined controls] and conduct [Assignment: organization‐defined analysis] to ensure the integrity of the system and system components by validating the internal composition and provenance of critical or mission‐essential technologies, products, and services. Discussion: Authoritative information regarding the internal composition of system components and the provenance of technology, products, and services provides a strong basis for trust. The validation of the internal composition and provenance can be achieved by various evidentiary artifacts or records that manufacturers and suppliers produce during the research and development, design, manufacturing, acquisition, delivery, integration, operations and maintenance, and disposal of technology, products, and services. Evidentiary artifacts include, but are not limited to, software identification (SWID) tags, software component inventory, the manufacturers’ declarations of platform attributes, and measurements that are tightly bound to the hardware itself. Related Controls: RA-3.

title: SUPPLY CHAIN CONTROLS AND PROCESSES
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for SR-3 Supply Chain Controls and Processes:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   - Visible Evidence: Look for formal, up-to-date supply chain risk management process documentation that describes the methods for identifying and addressing supply chain weaknesses. This includes approved security plans, supply chain risk management plans, and documented procedures that tie risk assessments to operational activities.
   - Technical Artifacts: Check revision histories, configuration management logs, and automated monitoring tools that list known supply chain elements (hardware, software, firmware, etc.), vendors, and risk scoring. Artifact trails like incident reports, change logs, and audit trails offer tangible evidence of process activity.
   - Operational Patterns: Observe active coordination between supply chain personnel and security teams (e.g., meeting minutes, email communications, periodic risk review sessions). Evidence that vulnerabilities are periodically re-assessed and that responses (remediation actions, incident assessments) are executed as events occur is a strong indicator.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   - Faking Compliance: Organizations sometimes produce a supply chain risk management document without embedding the process in daily operations. Reviews may show a policy that hasn’t been updated or practiced, with no supporting evidence of actual risk assessments.
   - Surface-Level Implementations: Generic or overly broad policies that merely regurgitate standard templates can be a red flag. In practice, checking against actual vendor logs and incident alerts often reveals gaps.
   - Technical Gaps: A common issue is the lack of automated integration between procurement, configuration, and risk management systems. This leads to reliance on manual reporting and poor traceability, which is not effective for meeting the control’s intent.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   • CHECK: Supply Chain Process Documentation
     WHY: A current, detailed supply chain risk management process shows that the organization has proactively identified addressing risks in the supply chain.
     HOW: Review the security and privacy plans, supply chain risk management plan, or other designated documents. Interview responsible supply chain personnel to confirm the process is understood and actively applied. Look for revision history and date stamps that indicate recent and periodic updates.
     FAIL: Missing, outdated, or generic documents without evidence that the processes are actively maintained and relevant to current supply chain components.

   • CHECK: Operational Integration of Supply Chain Controls
     WHY: Integration of controls into systems (e.g., configuration management, automated monitoring) demonstrates operational effectiveness rather than a mere documented process.
     HOW: Inspect IT infrastructure and configuration management systems for records indicating supply chain risk assessments, risk mitigation activities, or configuration changes based on supply chain reviews. Verify logs from automated tools that track supply chain-related incidents.
     FAIL: Lack of automated evidence, isolated documentation without system-based correlation, or logs showing infrequent or no supply chain risk-related events.

   • CHECK: Coordination Between Supply Chain and Security Personnel
     WHY: Effective coordination is crucial for translating documented policies into proactive risk management activities.
     HOW: Interview key personnel from both the security and supply chain teams. Review meeting minutes or communication logs that detail joint reviews, risk discussions, or coordinated incident responses related to supply chain events.
     FAIL: Evidence of siloed operations, missing or inconsistent communication records, and no clear assignment of responsibilities.

   • CHECK: Incident and Change Management Records Related to Supply Chain Events
     WHY: Tracking changes and incidents shows that the organization is not only monitoring risks but is also reactive to supply chain-related events.
     HOW: Look for incident tickets, change logs, and remediation action records specifically flagged as supply chain related. Confirm that risk events are followed by documented corrective actions with timelines and accountability.
     FAIL: Absence of change logs, lack of evidence that incidents trigger risk reviews and adjustments, or a backlog of unresolved supply chain incidents.

   • CHECK: Automated Tools and Continuous Monitoring
     WHY: Real-time or periodic automated monitoring indicates that supply chain risks are actively tracked and managed in operational systems.
     HOW: Query security information and event management (SIEM) systems for alerts pertaining to supply chain components. Validate that data from procurement and inventory systems ties back to risk ratings and that these inputs trigger corrective actions.
     FAIL: No alerts or logs related to supply chain risk, isolated manual reviews with no automated backing, or tools that exist only in theory without active deployment.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   - Verification Shortcuts: In interviews, ask for examples of recent supply chain incidents to verify that processes aren’t just on paper. Request a walk-through of a recent risk mitigation action.
   - Key Indicators of Poor Implementation: Look for missing or mismatched time stamps in documents and logs, a lack of integration between risk management and IT systems, or overly generic policies that cover a broad spectrum of risks without specificity.
   - Details That Matter: Confirm that risk assessments include specific supply chain elements (vendors, hardware, software, firmware) and that remediation timelines are documented and adhered to. Real-time monitoring evidence often stands as the most difficult to fake compared to purely documented policies.

This multi-layered validation approach ensures both the existence and operational effectiveness of the supply chain controls and processes, addressing SR-3 comprehensively.
content:
Statement:
a. Establish a process or processes to identify and address weaknesses or deficiencies in the supply chain elements and processes of [Assignment: organization‐defined system or systems component] in coordination with [Assignment: organization‐defined supply chain personnel];

b. Employ the following controls to protect against supply chain risks to the system, system component, or system service and to limit the harm or consequences from supply chain‐related events: [Assignment: organization‐defined supply chain controls]; and

c. Document the selected and implemented supply chain processes and controls in [Selection: security and privacy plans; supply chain risk management plan; [Assignment: organization‐defined document]].

Guidance:
Supply chain elements include organizations, entities, or tools employed for the research and development, design, manufacturing, acquisition, delivery, integration, operations and maintenance, and disposal of systems and system components. Supply chain processes include hardware, software, and firmware development processes; shipping and handling procedures; personnel security and physical security programs; configuration management tools, techniques, and measures to maintain provenance; or other programs, processes, or procedures associated with the development, acquisition, maintenance and disposal of systems and system components. Supply chain elements and processes may be provided by organizations, system integrators, or external providers. Weaknesses or deficiencies in supply chain elements or processes represent potential vulnerabilities that can be exploited by adversaries to cause harm to the organization and affect its ability to carry out its core missions or business functions. Supply chain personnel are individuals with roles and responsibilities in the supply chain.

title: SUPPLY CHAIN RISK MANAGEMENT PLAN
methodology:
Below is a detailed methodology for validating the implementation of a Supply Chain Risk Management (SCRM) Plan in line with control SR-2.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • The existence of a documented SCRM plan that outlines risk management activities across R&D, design, manufacturing, acquisition, delivery, integration, operations, maintenance, and disposal.
   • Visible review logs or version history indicating periodic updates and modifications that address evolving threats and changes in the environment.
   • Access controls and audit trails protecting the SCRM plan from unauthorized disclosure or tampering (e.g., restricted file permissions, digital signatures, or configuration management systems).

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations might create a generic document that lists required elements but fails to tailor plans to specific systems or components, leaving gaps in actual risk management activities.
   • Surface-level implementations can include static documents with outdated information that are not actually kept current or validated against current supply chain risks.
   • Technical protections may be nominal (e.g., password-protected files without logging or change management), meaning the plan could be easily modified without detection.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify the existence of a comprehensive, documented supply chain risk management plan.
   WHY: A physical or digital document with clear scope, requirements, and tailored risk mitigations proves that the organization has formally addressed supply chain risks.
   HOW: - Locate the SCRM plan in the organization’s secure repository.
         - Confirm that it covers research and development through disposal phases.
         - Interview responsible personnel and review table-of-contents to check for lifecycle coverage.
   FAIL: Absence of a tailored plan or a generic document that does not reference specific systems or recent supply chain issues.

   CHECK: Confirm that the plan is regularly reviewed and updated at the defined frequency (or when environmental changes dictate).
   WHY: Regular updates ensure that the plan remains relevant in response to threat evolutions, operational changes, or supply chain disruptions.
   HOW: - Review change management logs, version histories, or timestamps that indicate periodic updates.
         - Interview the SCRM manager about the schedule and triggers for plan updates.
         - Compare the plan’s review cycle with documented organizational standards for review frequency.
   FAIL: Outdated documentation with no evidence of review or updates since the plan’s initial draft.

   CHECK: Examine technical and operational safeguards protecting the SCRM plan from unauthorized disclosure/modification.
   WHY: Controls on confidentiality and integrity of the plan ensure that the plan’s contents remain trustworthy and unaltered.
   HOW: - Verify file system controls such as access permissions, encryption, or document management policies.
         - Check audit logs or change notification systems that track access and changes to the plan.
         - Interview IT/security personnel responsible for maintaining the SCRM plan repository.
   FAIL: Evidence that the plan is stored with weak access controls (e.g., open network shares without logging or controls) or that there is no audit trail for modifications.

   CHECK: Assess operational integration of the SCRM plan into overall risk management and supply chain processes.
   WHY: The plan’s effectiveness is measured by its integration into day-to-day operations and by ongoing monitoring responses.
   HOW: - Review meeting minutes or incident response logs that reference actions taken due to findings in the SCRM plan.
         - Validate that personnel follow documented processes by reviewing sample communications or incident records.
         - Confirm that there are metrics/KPIs in place related to supply chain risk management, and that these are reviewed periodically.
   FAIL: Disconnected or siloed plan that is not reflected in operational practices, with little or no evidence of real-world application.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcuts: Directly request the version history and change logs from the document management system to quickly assess the review/update cadence.
   • Critical Indicators: Look for explicit risk identification at each stage of the system lifecycle; missing sections for specific lifecycle phases can be a red flag.
   • Details that Matter: Emphasis should be placed on how the plan is protected digitally – weak access logs or lack of encryption are common pointers to a superficial implementation.
   • Common Pitfall: Avoid relying solely on policy documents; focus on the evidence of active risk monitoring practices and timely updates reflective of current threats.

Following these steps will help ensure that the SCRM plan is robust, current, and resilient against unauthorized modifications, thereby providing real operational effectiveness against supply chain risks.
content:
Statement:
a. Develop a plan for managing supply chain risks associated with the research and development, design, manufacturing, acquisition, delivery, integration, operations and maintenance, and disposal of the following systems, system components or system services: [Assignment: organization‐defined systems, system components, or system services];

b. Review and update the supply chain risk management plan [Assignment: organization‐defined frequency] or as required, to address threat, organizational or environmental changes; and

c. Protect the supply chain risk management plan from unauthorized disclosure and modification.

Guidance:
The dependence on products, systems, and services from external providers, as well as the nature of the relationships with those providers, present an increasing level of risk to an organization. Threat actions that may increase security or privacy risks include unauthorized production, the insertion or use of counterfeits, tampering, theft, insertion of malicious software and hardware, and poor manufacturing and development practices in the supply chain. Supply chain risks can be endemic or systemic within a system element or component, a system, an organization, a sector, or the Nation. Managing supply chain risk is a complex, multifaceted undertaking that requires a coordinated effort across an organization to build trust relationships and communicate with internal and external stakeholders. Supply risk management (SCRM) activities include identifying and assessing risks, determining appropriate risk response actions, developing SCRM plans to document response actions, and monitoring performance against plans. The SCRM plan (at the system-level) is implementation specific, providing policy implementation, requirements, constraints and implications. It can either stand-alone, or be incorporated into system security and privacy plans. The SCRM plan addresses managing, implementation, and monitoring of SCRM controls and the development/sustainment of systems across the SDLC to support mission and business functions. Because supply chains can differ significantly across and within organizations, SCRM plans are tailored to the individual program, organizational, and operational contexts. Tailored SCRM plans provide the basis for determining whether a technology, service, system component, or system is fit for purpose, and as such, the controls need to be tailored accordingly. Tailored SCRM plans help organizations focus their resources on the most critical mission and business functions based on mission and business requirements and their risk environment.

title: POLICY AND PROCEDURES
methodology:
Below is a practical methodology to validate the implementation of SR-1 (Policy and Procedures) for supply chain risk management:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: Look for a formally approved supply chain risk management policy document that clearly states purpose, scope, roles/responsibilities, and references to legal and regulatory requirements. It should be prominently disseminated to all relevant personnel via intranet or policy management systems. 
   • Technical Artifacts: Version-controlled policy and procedure documents (e.g., in a document management repository) with timestamps, revision histories, meeting minutes for review sessions, and logs of policy acknowledgments by assigned roles. 
   • Operational Patterns: Regular updates triggered after audits, security incidents, or regulatory changes; evidence that a designated official manages distribution and that training or awareness sessions are conducted.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Superficial Documentation: Organizations may produce a generic or outdated policy document without evidence that it’s integrated within daily practices or aligned with regulatory changes. 
   • Lacking Enforcement: A designated official might be named on paper, but there may be no records of periodic reviews or actionable meeting outcomes.
   • Incomplete Dissemination: Policies may only be accessible to senior management rather than all assigned personnel, or they may be embedded in a larger document without clear visibility.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify the existence of a current, formally approved supply chain risk management policy and procedure set.
   WHY: Ensures that the organization has an official guide that governs the supply chain risk management program.
   HOW: Access the document management system to retrieve the latest version; verify that the document includes purpose, scope, roles, and regulatory references; confirm with key personnel that the document has been disseminated.
   FAIL: No accessible document, obsolete version, or lack of formal approval signatures or revision histories.

   CHECK: Confirm that a designated official is responsible for the management and dissemination of the policy.
   WHY: A clear assignment of responsibilities is critical to ensure ongoing management and updates.
   HOW: Review organizational charts, appointment letters, or records in the policy management system that identify the responsible official; interview staff to validate awareness of this role.
   FAIL: No documentation or ambiguous assignment, or if interviews reveal unawareness of the designated responsibility.

   CHECK: Inspect records of policy reviews and updates (including frequency and event-triggered changes).
   WHY: Regular review and update are necessary to align policies with evolving risks and regulatory changes.
   HOW: Look for meeting minutes, revision logs, or compliance audit findings that document review sessions and subsequent policy updates; verify dates against the organization-defined review frequency.
   FAIL: Missing or infrequent review records, dated documents that do not reflect recent events, or absence of triggered updates after incidents.

   CHECK: Validate that the policies and procedures are disseminated and accessible to all relevant personnel.
   WHY: Dissemination is critical to ensure that everyone in scope is aware of and can follow the policy.
