In situations where malicious code cannot be detected by detection methods or technologies, organizations rely on other types of controls, including security coding practices, procurement management and control, trusted procurement processes, and monitoring practices to ensure that software does not perform functions other than the functions intended. Organizations may determine that, in response to the detection of malicious code, different actions may be warranted. For example, organizations can define actions in response to malicious code detection during periodic scans, the detection of malicious downloads, or the detection of maliciousness when attempting to open or execute files.

Enhancements:
MALICIOUS CODE PROTECTION | CENTRAL MANAGEMENT

[Withdrawn: Incorporated into PL-9.]
MALICIOUS CODE PROTECTION | AUTOMATIC UPDATES

[Withdrawn: Incorporated into SI-3.]
MALICIOUS CODE PROTECTION | NON-PRIVILEGED USERS

[Withdrawn: Incorporated into AC-6(10).]
MALICIOUS CODE PROTECTION | UPDATES ONLY BY PRIVILEGED USERS

Update malicious code protection mechanisms only when directed by a privileged user.

Discussion: Protection mechanisms for malicious code are typically categorized as security-related software and, as such, are only updated by organizational personnel with appropriate access privileges.

Related Controls: CM-5.
MALICIOUS CODE PROTECTION | PORTABLE STORAGE DEVICES
MALICIOUS CODE PROTECTION | TESTING AND VERIFICATION  
(a) Test malicious code protection mechanisms [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] by introducing known benign code into the system; and  
(b) Verify that the detection of the code and the associated incident reporting occur.  
Discussion: None.  
Related Controls: CA-2, CA-7, RA-5.
MALICIOUS CODE PROTECTION | NONSIGNATURE-BASED DETECTION  
[Withdrawn: Incorporated into SI-3.]
MALICIOUS CODE PROTECTION | DETECT UNAUTHORIZED COMMANDS  
(a) Detect the following unauthorized operating system commands through the kernel application programming interface on [Assignment: organization-defined system hardware components]: [Assignment: organization-defined unauthorized operating system commands]; and  
(b) [Selection (one or more): issue a warning; audit the command execution; prevent the execution of the command].  
Discussion: Detecting unauthorized commands can be applied to critical interfaces other than kernel-based interfaces, including interfaces with virtual machines and privileged applications. Unauthorized operating system commands include commands for kernel functions from system processes that are not trusted to initiate such commands as well as commands for kernel functions that are suspicious even though commands of that type are detected by a combination of command types, command classes, or specific instances of commands. Organizations can also define hardware components by component type, component location in the network, or a combination thereof. Organizations may select different actions for different types, classes, or instances of malicious commands.
MALICIOUS CODE PROTECTION | AUTHENTICATE REMOTE COMMANDS  
[Withdrawn: Moved to AC-17(10).]
MALICIOUS CODE PROTECTION | MALICIOUS CODE ANALYSIS  
(a) Employ the following tools and techniques to analyze the characteristics and behavior of malicious code: [Assignment: organization-defined tools and techniques]; and  
(b) Incorporate the results from malicious code analysis into organizational incident response and flaw remediation processes.  
Discussion: The use of malicious code analysis tools provides organizations with a more in-depth understanding of adversary tradecraft (i.e., tactics, techniques, and procedures) and the functionality and purpose of specific instances of malicious code. Understanding the characteristics of malicious code facilitates effective organizational responses to current and future threats. Organizations can conduct malicious code analyses by employing reverse engineering techniques or by monitoring the behavior of executing code.  
Related Controls: None.

References: [SP 800-83], [SP 800-125B], [SP 800-177].

title: FLAW REMEDIATION
methodology:
Validation Methodology for SI-2: Flaw Remediation

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Evidence of a defined process that identifies, reports, and corrects system flaws (e.g., ticketing systems or change management systems with flaw remediation records).
• Technical artifacts such as patch management dashboards, automated update logs, and configuration management records linking identified flaws to remediation actions.
• Operational patterns where security-relevant software/firmware updates are routinely tested (via separate testing records or staging environments) and integrated into baseline configurations.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may merely produce flowchart documents or policies without evidence of active, routine flaw remediation.
• Surface-level implementations include manual patching without automated logging or having outdated configuration management systems where remediation actions aren’t traceable.
• Technical gaps often occur when critical updates are installed without proper testing or when automated mechanisms are configured but not actively monitored.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Verify the automated patch management and update logs showing installation timestamps and patch status.
WHY: Automated evidence confirms that patches are applied within the organization-defined time period, reducing the risk of overlooked vulnerabilities.
HOW: Retrieve logs or dashboard screenshots from the patch management tool; compare update dates/timestamps against release dates and defined remediation SLAs.
FAIL: Absence of timestamps; outdated automated reports; or patch records that require excessive manual reconciliation.

CHECK: Examine system configuration management records and change management tickets for linking flaw identification with remediation actions.
WHY: This demonstrates that flaw remediation is incorporated into the operational configuration management process and is subject to tracking and accountability.
HOW: Inspect the configuration repository or change management system to find records of flaw identification, risk assessment, testing, and patch deployment. Confirm that each record includes details such as flaw ID, remediation steps, and authorizations.
FAIL: Incomplete records where flaws are identified but lack evidence of testing or remediation; or entries that do not reference update deployments.

CHECK: Assess testing documentation and evidence for software/firmware updates (when required) to evaluate potential side effects.
WHY: Testing ensures that updates do not introduce new vulnerabilities or operational disruptions, proving that remediation actions are safe and effective.
HOW: Review testing protocols, test logs, and results from pilot updates. Interview the system or change managers to confirm that testing precedes installations where applicable.
FAIL: Lack of documented test procedures or evidence; updates applied without any recorded testing; or inconsistencies between test results and production updates.

CHECK: Validate the existence and operation of an automated mechanism (if implemented) for periodic flaw remediation status monitoring.
WHY: Automated checks provide continuous assurance that security-relevant updates are current, offering observable, tamper-resistant evidence.
HOW: Examine automated reporting dashboards or audit logs that list vulnerability statuses across system components. Confirm that the frequency of these reports aligns with organizational definitions.
FAIL: Missing or non-functioning automated reports; or manually generated and potentially inconsistent reports that do not reflect real-time data.

CHECK: Review performance metrics and remediation benchmarks (e.g., time-to-remediate measures) to confirm timely flaw remediation.
WHY: Performance metrics are critical for evaluating operational effectiveness; they ensure that remediation actions meet the organization-defined benchmarks and risk tolerance.
HOW: Inspect dashboard analytics, reports, or key performance indicator (KPI) summaries that detail the average remediation times and any exceptions or delays identified.
FAIL: Absence of metrics or significant delays between flaw identification and remediation that exceed organizational benchmarks.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Use automated tools to capture and cross-reference patch deployment logs with vulnerability scan results – these are difficult to fake if properly integrated.
• Confirm that remediation actions leave detailed 'audit trails' in the configuration management database; missing audit trails are a red flag.
• Look for out-of-band patches or ad hoc manual interventions without supporting documentation, as they often bypass formal testing requirements.
• Interview stakeholders (e.g., system administrators, change managers) to verify that policies are not merely documented, but practiced on a day-to-day basis.
• Emphasize evidence of continuous monitoring and performance trend data – these are critical as they validate that the process remains effective over time.

This methodology focuses on observable, automated, and objective evidence rather than simply reviewing policies or procedures, ensuring that flaw remediation is effectively implemented and continuously maintained.
content:
Statement:
a. Identify, report, and correct system flaws;  b. Test software and firmware updates related to flaw remediation for effectiveness and potential side effects before installation;  c. Install security-relevant software and firmware updates within [Assignment: organization-defined time period] of the release of the updates; and  d. Incorporate flaw remediation into the organizational configuration management process.

Guidance:
The need to remediate system flaws applies to all types of software and firmware. Organizations identify systems affected by software flaws, including potential vulnerabilities resulting from those flaws, and report this information to designated organizational personnel with information security and privacy responsibilities. Security-relevant updates include patches, service packs, and malicious code signatures. Organizations also address flaws discovered during assessments, continuous monitoring, incident response activities, and system error handling. By incorporating flaw remediation into configuration management processes, required remediation actions can be tracked and verified. Organization-defined time periods for updating security-relevant software and firmware may vary based on a variety of risk factors, including the security category of the system, the criticality of the update (i.e., severity of the vulnerability related to the discovered flaw), the organizational risk tolerance, the mission supported by the system, or the threat environment. Some types of flaw remediation may require more testing than others. Organizations determine the type of testing needed for the specific type of flaw remediation activity considered during change management. In some situations, organizations may determine that the testing of software or firmware updates is not necessary or practical, such as when implementing simple malicious code signature updates. In testing decisions, organizations consider whether security-relevant software or firmware updates are obtained from authorized sources with appropriate digital signatures.

Enhancements:
(1) FLAW REMEDIATION | CENTRAL MANAGEMENT  [Withdrawn: Incorporated into PL-9]
(2) FLAW REMEDIATION | AUTOMATED FLAW REMEDIATION STATUS  Determine if system components have applicable security-relevant software and firmware updates installed using [Assignment: organization-defined automated mechanisms] [Assignment: organization-defined frequency].  Discussion: Automated mechanisms can track and determine the status of known flaws for system components.  Related Controls: CA-7, SI-4.
(3) FLAW REMEDIATION | TIME TO REMEDIATE FLAWS AND BENCHMARKS FOR CORRECTIVE ACTIONS  (a) Measure the time between flaw identification and flaw remediation; and  (b) Establish the following benchmarks for taking corrective actions: [Assignment: organization-defined benchmarks].  Discussion: Organizations determine the time it takes on average to correct system flaws after such flaws have been identified and subsequently establish organizational benchmarks.
(4) FLAW REMEDIATION | AUTOMATED PATCH MANAGEMENT TOOLS Employ automated patch management tools to facilitate flaw remediation to the following system components: [Assignment: organization-defined system components]. Discussion: Using automated tools to support patch management helps to ensure the timeliness and completeness of system patching operations. Related Controls: None.
(5) FLAW REMEDIATION | AUTOMATIC SOFTWARE AND FIRMWARE UPDATES Install [Assignment: organization-defined security-relevant software and firmware updates] automatically to [Assignment: organization-defined system components]. Discussion: Due to system integrity and availability concerns, organizations consider the methodology used to carry out automatic updates. Organizations balance the need to ensure that the updates are installed as soon as possible with the need to maintain configuration management and control with any mission or operational impacts that automatic updates might impose. Related Controls: None.
(6) FLAW REMEDIATION | REMOVAL OF PREVIOUS VERSIONS OF SOFTWARE AND FIRMWARE Remove previous versions of [Assignment: organization-defined software and firmware components] after updated versions have been installed. Discussion: Previous versions of software or firmware components that are not removed from the system after updates have been installed may be exploited by adversaries. Some products may automatically remove previous versions of software and firmware from the system. Related Controls: None.

title: POLICY AND PROCEDURES
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Existence of a formal, published system and information integrity policy document that is easily accessible (e.g., via an internal portal or intranet). 
• Documented procedures that detail how the policies are operationalized, including review logs and approval signatures. 
• Evidence of regular dissemination (e.g., emails, intranet announcements) and version control that reflects updates after defined triggers (e.g., audits, incidents, regulatory changes).

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may merely compile a policy document without active dissemination or practical training, making the policy a paper exercise. 
• Creating superficial procedures that just restate the policy without actionable steps, or failing to update them in a timely manner. 
• Designating a responsible individual on paper without clear evidence of active role execution (e.g., absence of communication or review records).

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Verify the existence and accessibility of the system and information integrity policy document and its associated procedures.
   WHY: Directly confirms that a formal, documented control exists and is available to relevant personnel.
   HOW: Inspect the shared document repository or intranet where the policy is published; check document metadata (date, version) and confirm that dissemination logs (e.g., email distributions) exist.
   FAIL: No central repository, outdated documents, or absence of evidence of distribution.

2. CHECK: Validate that the policy explicitly addresses purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, inter-department coordination, and compliance.
   WHY: Ensures the policy covers all required components and is not just a high-level statement without details.
   HOW: Review the policy content against a checklist of required elements; interview key personnel to verify their understanding of roles and responsibilities.
   FAIL: Policy omits critical elements or personnel are unclear about their assigned responsibilities.

3. CHECK: Confirm that documented procedures exist to implement the policy controls and are followed during operations.
   WHY: Demonstrates active operationalization of the policy and indicates that the policy is not merely theoretical.
   HOW: Examine sample procedures detailing routine tasks (e.g., patch management, integrity monitoring); review log entries or automated task reports that correlate with the procedures.
   FAIL: Procedures are missing, incomplete, or there is no evidence of procedures being carried out in practice.

4. CHECK: Identify and verify the designated official responsible for managing the policy and procedures.
   WHY: Accountability is crucial; verifying a designated official confirms that there is oversight and management commitment.
   HOW: Check the organizational chart, appointment letters, or email communications that officially designate the responsible individual; interview stakeholders to confirm interaction with the designated official.
   FAIL: No evidence of a designated official or evidence shows the responsibility has not been actively assumed.

5. CHECK: Review the documentation for regular review and update processes of the policy and procedures.
   WHY: Indicates that the organization actively maintains the efficacy of the controls in response to changes or events.
   HOW: Examine revision histories, change logs, or meeting minutes documenting reviews and updates triggered by assessments, audits, incidents, or regulatory changes.
   FAIL: Outdated documents with no revision history or lack of review meeting records.

EXPERT TIPS
• Look for version control and timestamp metadata in published policies; this is a quick indicator of currency and review regularity. 
• If evidence of dissemination (e.g., emails, training records) is missing, probe further to see if training sessions include policy walk-throughs, which are less easily faked. 
• Be cautious of policies that list responsibilities without clear evidence (e.g., interview data) that the designated official actually fulfills the role. 
• Confirm that the triggers for policy review (e.g., audit findings or security incidents) are reflected in the policy’s change logs or meeting minutes; absence of such evidence is a red flag.

This comprehensive methodology emphasizes direct, automatable checks, operational evidence, and real-world effectiveness to ensure that the SI-1 control is not only documented but actively implemented throughout the organization.
content:
Statement:
a. Develop, document, and disseminate to [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles]:

1. [Selection (one or more): Organization-level; Mission/business process-level; System-level] system and information integrity policy that:

(a) Addresses purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, coordination among organizational entities, and compliance; and

(b) Is consistent with applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines; and

2. Procedures to facilitate the implementation of the system and information integrity policy and the associated system and information integrity controls; and

b. Designate an [Assignment: organization-defined official] to manage the development, documentation, and dissemination of the system and information integrity policy and procedures; and

c. Review and update the current system and information integrity:

1. Policy [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] and following [Assignment: organization-defined events]; and

2. Procedures [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] and following [Assignment: organization-defined events].

Guidance:
System and information integrity policy and procedures address the controls in the SI family that are implemented within systems and organizations. The risk management strategy is an important factor in establishing such policies and procedures. Policies and procedures contribute to security and privacy assurance. Therefore, it is important that security and privacy programs collaborate on the development of system and information integrity policy and procedures. Security and privacy program policies and procedures at the organization level are preferable, in general, and may obviate the need for mission- or system-specific policies and procedures. Procedures can be established for security and privacy programs, for mission or business processes, and for systems, if needed. Procedures describe how the policies or controls are implemented and can be directed at the individual or role that is the object of the procedure. Procedures can be documented in system security and privacy plans or in one or more separate documents. Events that may precipitate an update to system and information integrity policy and procedures include assessment or audit findings, security incidents or breaches, or changes in applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines. Simply restating controls does not constitute an organizational policy or procedure.

title: HARDWARE-BASED PROTECTION
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Visible evidence of physical hardware write-protect mechanisms (e.g., jumpers, switches, or dedicated ports) installed on critical firmware components.
• Technical artifacts such as photographs of hardware boards, inventory lists, or system configuration documentation indicating the presence of write-protect switches.
• Operational patterns where firmware update logs or change control records clearly show manual disablement and re-enablement events tied to authorized personnel.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations might mark a system as compliant by simply having a documented policy without ensuring the hardware mechanism is actually present or activated.
• Superficial compliance may include configuring software flags that mimic hardware-based write-protection rather than implementing genuine physical protection.
• Often, the procedures for disabling write protection are formalized on paper but not practically enforced, with no evidence of proper controls during maintenance operations.

VALIDATION STEPS:
1. CHECK: Inspect Hardware Write-Protect Mechanism
   WHY: Physical, hardware-based switches (e.g., jumpers, switches) ensure that firmware components are protected against unauthorized modifications.
   HOW: Conduct a physical examination of servers and network devices to locate evidenced hardware switches; review system diagrams and inventory to validate that designated firmware components have write-protect mechanisms.
   FAIL: Absence of visible hardware components (e.g., missing jumpers or switches) or documentation failing to correlate with actual hardware setups.

2. CHECK: Evaluate Firmware Update Logs and Change Records
   WHY: Consistent, documented logs of manual disablement and subsequent re-enablement demonstrate that the controlled procedure is actively followed.
   HOW: Request and analyze logs or audit trails that capture the disablement/re-enablement events; verify timestamps and associated authorized user details match documented procedures.
   FAIL: Lack of logs, absence of recorded manual interventions, or mismatches between user identities in logs and authorized personnel lists.

3. CHECK: Validate Authorized Personnel and Procedure Training
   WHY: Ensuring only authorized individuals are permitted to disable write protection confirms control over firmware modifications.
   HOW: Interview sample authorized personnel and review training records or access control lists; confirm that documented procedures are known and followed, and compare with logs of firmware maintenance events.
   FAIL: Discrepancies in personnel lists, missing training evidence, or instances where unauthorized users appear to have performed firmware updates.

4. CHECK: Review Detailed Procedures for Disabling/Enabling Write-Protect
   WHY: Clear, operational procedures ensure consistency and security during firmware modifications, preventing accidental or malicious changes.
   HOW: Examine operation manuals and maintenance procedures; compare with actual maintenance records and interview maintenance staff to verify they adhere to the documented manual process.
   FAIL: Procedures are either outdated, not followed in practice, or missing key steps (such as re-enabling the protection) after firmware updates.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Verification shortcuts include correlating physical inspections with photographic evidence and cross-checking firmware update logs against ticketing systems to ensure synchronization.
• Critical indicators of poor implementation include missing physical write-protect mechanisms, unlogged maintenance events, or a mismatch between authorized personnel and recorded operations.
• Details that matter most are the tangible, physical artifacts (the hardware switches) and the integrity of the operational logs demonstrating that authorized individuals are following a systematically enforced process.
content:
Statement:
a. Employ hardware-based, write-protect for [Assignment: organization-defined system firmware components]; and
b. Implement specific procedures for [Assignment: organization-defined authorized individuals] to manually disable hardware write-protect for firmware modifications and re-enable the write-protect prior to returning to operational mode.

title: SOFTWARE-ENFORCED SEPARATION AND POLICY ENFORCEMENT
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Visible evidence includes runtime boundaries where security domains are isolated by software mechanisms. Security and audit logs should distinctly show enforcement decisions when inter-domain actions are attempted.
• Technical artifacts include configuration files, enforcement modules or middleware code segments, domain policy definitions, and automated test outputs proving that cross-domain actions are blocked.
• Operational patterns: regular logging of access denial events, periodic reviews of separation checks during system scans, and consistent alerts during any attempted inter-domain breaches.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations may declare policy enforcement in documentation without configuring actual enforcement in the software, or may rely on network perimeter segmentation instead of true software-enabled enforcement.
• Superficial implementations often involve generic access control lists that do not map to true domain separation boundaries.
• Technical gaps often include missing runtime code checks or improper logging that fails to capture policy enforcement events, making it easier for attackers to use undocumented channels.

VALIDATION STEPS:
CHECK 1: Verify Enforcement of Domain Separation Boundaries
WHY: This proves that software actively distinguishes and isolates security domains rather than relying solely on administrative policy.
HOW: Review system code or configuration management settings to confirm that access control logic distinctly enforces boundaries. Test access between domains using controlled inputs and observe enforcement responses.
FAIL: If tests allow cross-domain access or if configuration files do not reflect domain-specific logic, the control has likely failed.

CHECK 2: Audit Logging and Monitoring for Policy Enforcement
WHY: Proper logs provide evidence of real-time enforcement and alert on breaches, demonstrating operational effectiveness.
HOW: Access and review audit logs to identify events where attempted cross-domain actions were denied. Verify integration with security monitoring tools that correlate these events.
FAIL: Lack of detailed logging or the absence of alerts on domain boundary crossings indicates an ineffective implementation.

CHECK 3: Automated Penetration Testing for Separation Weaknesses
WHY: Automated tests simulate attack vectors that try to bypass domain separation, ensuring the software’s enforcement mechanisms are robust under stress.
HOW: Execute scripted penetration tests specifically targeting cross-domain access. Analyze automated reports for any bypasses, misconfigurations, or unauthorized access paths.
FAIL: Discovery of vulnerabilities where automated tests succeed in breaching domain separation implies a failure to enforce policies adequately.

CHECK 4: Configuration and Alignment of Security Domain Definitions
WHY: Valid domain definition ensures that policy enforcement logic is aligned with approved security principles and operational boundaries.
HOW: Examine system architecture diagrams, configuration repositories, and policy files to confirm that defined security domains match the actual runtime enforcement mechanisms.
FAIL: Mismatches between documented domain boundaries and operational configurations or software behavior indicate a breakdown in enforced separation.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Shortcut: Use automated configuration management tools to quickly compare policy definitions with runtime settings.
• Critical indicator: Frequent, unlogged cross-domain access events are a strong sign of poor enforcement.
• Attention to detail: Ensure that the domain separation code covers all possible entry points, including API endpoints, middleware components, and backend services, as omissions are common sources of vulnerability.
content:
Statement:
Implement software-enforced separation and policy enforcement mechanisms between [Assignment: organization-defined security domains].

Guidance:
System owners may require additional strength of mechanism to ensure domain separation and policy enforcement for specific types of threats and environments of operation.

title: HARDWARE-ENFORCED SEPARATION AND POLICY ENFORCEMENT
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Visible Evidence: Inspect system BIOS/UEFI screens and boot logs to confirm that hardware-assisted virtualization features (such as Intel VT-x, AMD-V, or ARM TrustZone) are active. Look for dedicated hardware security modules (HSM) or security co-processors enforcing separation between domains.
• Technical Artifacts: Retrieve firmware/BIOS configuration files, hypervisor (or embedded security module) configuration and logs demonstrating that hardware-enforced isolation is in use. Verify settings and registers (via CPUID or platform-specific tools) that confirm hardware separation.
• Operational Patterns: Observe that operations across security domains do not share common OS-level processes or memory. Check that cross-domain activities are strictly mediated (and logged) by hardware mechanisms.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Fake Compliance via Software-only Options: Organizations may claim enforcement by using virtualization software or OS-based isolation while neglecting the hardware features that provide true separation.
• Superficial Configuration Changes: Simply enabling a feature in the BIOS without actively configuring its operational policies or without testing/hardening the mechanism.
• Overreliance on Documentation: Relying on policy and design documents that state separation exists without verifying that the hardware-enforced components are enabled and actively protecting domain boundaries.

VALIDATION STEPS:

CHECK: Review Firmware/BIOS Settings for Hardware Virtualization
WHY: Confirms that the underlying hardware-level separation mechanisms (e.g., Intel VT-x, AMD-V) are enabled and active.
HOW: Access BIOS/UEFI configuration screens or exported configuration files; use platform tools (e.g., CPUID, dmidecode) to verify that virtualization extensions are enabled.
FAIL: If virtualization extensions or other relevant hardware features are disabled or missing, then the separation is not truly hardware-enforced.

CHECK: Inspect Hypervisor/Hardware Security Module Configurations
WHY: Verifies that domain separation is being actively enforced by hardware-supported security modules rather than by software layers alone.
HOW: Examine hypervisor policies and system logs for settings that enforce domain isolation; assess configurations in HSMs or secure co-processors that manage access controls between security domains.
FAIL: Discovery of reliance solely on OS-level access controls or missing hardware-related isolation policies indicates non-compliance with the control.

CHECK: Perform Controlled Cross-Domain Access Testing
WHY: Demonstrates that hardware-level policy enforcement effectively prevents unauthorized inter-domain communication.
HOW: Execute targeted penetration tests where attempts are made to breach separation boundaries (e.g., memory access across domains) and monitor the hardware's response, using both automated tools and test scripts designed for hardware-isolation scenarios.
FAIL: If the test shows that unauthorized access between domains is possible (or only prevented by secondary software mechanisms), it indicates that robust hardware-enforced separation is not in place.

CHECK: Analyze System and Security Audit Logs for Hardware-Level Enforcement Events
WHY: Ensures that enforcement mechanisms are not only configured but are actively monitored with evidence of real-time hardware-triggered events.
HOW: Review log files from BIOS/UEFI, hypervisor, and hardware modules for events related to domain access attempts and policy enforcement; verify that alerts or anomalies are recorded as expected.
FAIL: An absence of logs or inconsistent hardware event timestamps may indicate that either logging is insufficient or that hardware-enforced separation mechanisms are not fully operational.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Shortcut Verification: Use automated tools that query hardware registers and hypervisor configurations to quickly confirm that hardware-based features are active. Tools like CPUID readers, dmidecode, or vendor-specific firmware audit tools can save time over manual inspection.
• Critical Indicators: Pay special attention to whether the configuration reflects true isolation – for example, ensuring that cross-domain shared memory is strictly disabled and that hardware interrupts are independently managed.
• Detail Matters: Look for evidence that separation is enforced not just at boot-time but continuously. Real-world incidents, hardware-fuse activations, or error logs consistent with enforced policy breaches are highly indicative of active controls.
• Verification Depth: Do not rely on documentation; always correlate documented policies with real-time configurations and operational logs from hardware components.

By following these checks, auditors can verify that hardware-enforced separation and policy enforcement mechanisms are implemented effectively, going beyond documentation to ensure robust physical separation between defined security domains.
content:
Statement:
Implement hardware-enforced separation and policy enforcement mechanisms between [Assignment: organization-defined security domains].

Guidance:
System owners may require additional strength of mechanism and robustness to ensure domain separation and policy enforcement for specific types of threats and environments of operation. Hardware-enforced separation and policy enforcement provide greater strength of mechanism than software-enforced separation and policy enforcement.

title: SENSOR RELOCATION
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• CHECK: Verify that sensors or monitoring capabilities are configured to change physical or logical location in response to defined conditions.
  WHY: This behavior indicates that sensor relocation is actively implemented rather than being a one-time static placement.
  HOW: Review network configuration files, sensor management dashboards, and automated orchestration scripts that trigger relocation events; check system logs for relocation events with condition triggers such as threat alerts or scheduled randomization events.
  FAIL: Absence of automated logs or scripts indicating relocation, or if sensors remain permanently at one location despite configuration suggesting dynamic behavior.

• CHECK: Identify operational correlations between threat intelligence inputs and sensor relocation events.
  WHY: Successful dynamic relocation should be aligned with changing threat landscapes or internal risk signals.
  HOW: Cross-reference threat intelligence alerts with timestamps from sensor relocation logs; verify that location assignments in the monitoring system change in response to these alerts.
  FAIL: Sensor relocation events occurring independent of any relevant threat signals or without clear correlation can indicate a superficial setup without real integration.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may create documentation and policies that describe sensor relocation without actual implementation, using static or rarely updated configurations.
• Some may implement superficial toggles in the software interface that appear dynamic but do not actively change sensor locations in the underlying infrastructure.
• Inadequate integration with threat detection sources might result in relocation events that are triggered manually or on an ad hoc basis, undermining automation and timely response.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Review automated relocation scripts or orchestration tools.
   WHY: These artifacts provide observable evidence that sensor relocation is implemented and operational.
   HOW: Inspect code repositories for scripts configured to change sensor assignments and audit change management logs for automated relocation events.
   FAIL: Lack of such scripts or reliance solely on manual processes indicates a weak implementation.

2. CHECK: Inspect configuration management databases (CMDB) for records of sensor endpoint updates and relocations.
   WHY: A CMDB should reflect dynamically updated sensor locations if the control is active.
   HOW: Query the CMDB for sensor location changes over time and correlate with defined conditions or threat events.
   FAIL: Static entries or inconsistent updates indicate non-functional or simulated sensor relocation.

3. CHECK: Analyze live sensor network traffic and monitoring data for evidence of sensor reassignments.
   WHY: Active log data and real-time events serve as robust evidence of the sensor relocation mechanism.
   HOW: Utilize network monitoring and log analysis tools (e.g., SIEM) to search for events tagged with sensor relocation operations; validate event details against expected condition triggers.
   FAIL: Uniform traffic logs with no indications of sensor location changes suggest that relocation may not be truly operational.

4. CHECK: Confirm integration between threat intelligence platforms and sensor relocation triggers.
   WHY: Effective relocation should be triggered by accurate, timely threat intelligence inputs.
   HOW: Verify configuration settings in both the threat intelligence feed and sensor management system to ensure that triggers translate into location reassignments; review recent events for proper cause-effect relationships.
   FAIL: Discrepancies between threat events and sensor relocations suggest broken or superficial integration.

EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Focus on verifying real-time log entries and configuration management system updates; these are harder to fake than static policies or manuals.
• Critical Indicator: The absence of direct correlation between threat alerts and sensor relocation events often points to a process that was implemented for compliance only, rather than operational effectiveness.
• Remember: The effectiveness of sensor relocation is best gauged by its responsiveness to actual, live threat conditions, not just simulated or preprogrammed events. Pay extra attention to timestamp correlations and automation details during audits.
content:
Statement:
Relocate [Assignment: organization-defined sensors and monitoring capabilities] to [Assignment: organization-defined locations] under the following conditions or circumstances: [Assignment: organization-defined conditions or circumstances].

Guidance:
Adversaries may take various paths and use different approaches as they move laterally through an organization (including its systems) to reach their target or as they attempt to exfiltrate information from the organization. The organization often only has a limited set of monitoring and detection capabilities, and they may be focused on the critical or likely infiltration or exfiltration paths. By using communications paths that the organization typically does not monitor, the adversary can increase its chances of achieving its desired goals. By relocating its sensors or monitoring capabilities to new locations, the organization can impede the adversary’s ability to achieve its goals. The relocation of the sensors or monitoring capabilities might be done based on threat information that the organization has acquired or randomly to confuse the adversary and make its lateral transition through the system or organization more challenging.

Enhancements:
(1) SENSOR RELOCATION | DYNAMIC RELOCATION OF SENSORS OR MONITORING CAPABILITIES
Dynamically relocate [Assignment: organization-defined sensors and monitoring capabilities] to [Assignment: organization-defined locations] under the following conditions or circumstances: [Assignment: organization-defined conditions or circumstances].
Discussion: None.
Related Controls: None.
References: [SP 800-160-2].

title: ALTERNATE COMMUNICATIONS PATHS
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Visible evidence includes design documents and network diagrams explicitly identifying alternate communication paths for command and control operations. These artifacts often show redundant pathways (e.g., backup satellite links, secondary networks) and escalation procedures.
• Technical artifacts such as configuration files, system logs, and monitoring dashboards that record test runs, failover events or real-time switchovers to alternate paths.
• Operational patterns include periodic testing (e.g., drills or simulations) of alternate communication paths, and recorded incident reports that log the activation and performance of these paths during disruptions.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations may simply maintain a paper-based policy or diagram without operationalizing or regularly testing the alternate communications pathways.
• Surface-level implementations include setting up redundant hardware without verifying proper network integration or clear escalation paths.
• Technical gaps may involve failure to update alternate path configurations when primary communications infrastructure changes, or inadequate monitoring that misses degradation/failure in the backup paths.

VALIDATION STEPS:
1. CHECK: Verify that updated network diagrams and configuration documentation explicitly list alternate communications channels.
   WHY: This demonstrates that alternate paths have been planned and documented as part of the organization’s command and control strategy.
   HOW: Review architectural diagrams, change management records, and system configuration files; cross-check with incident response plans.
   FAIL: Absence of detailed diagrams or outdated documentation that does not reflect current network setups.

2. CHECK: Assess logs and monitoring dashboards for evidence of periodic testing or activation events of alternate communications paths.
   WHY: Real operational tests or activations indicate that these paths are not only theoretical but actively maintained.
   HOW: Query system logs for test events (e.g., simulated failovers), interviews with operations personnel, and evidence of drill exercises.
   FAIL: No records or logs evidencing regular tests or activation events, or tests that are incomplete or not meaningful.

3. CHECK: Confirm via interviews and incident records that alternative decision makers and their authority limits are clearly defined and operational.
   WHY: Ensuring command and control continuity requires that backup leadership roles are well-understood and that their actions during an incident are authorized.
   HOW: Interview key personnel in operations and incident response, review organizational charts and escalation protocols, check after-action reports from communications incidents.
   FAIL: Lack of clear designation or training of alternate decision makers or incident records that fail to reference alternate paths.

4. CHECK: Evaluate technical integrations between primary and alternate communications paths (e.g., seamless routing configurations and automated failover settings).
   WHY: The ability to switch or route through an alternate path automatically or with minimal manual intervention is crucial for effective incident response.
   HOW: Perform technical testing (e.g., simulated cut-over exercises, scripted failover tests), inspect configuration settings in routing/switch devices.
   FAIL: Manual, error-prone routing change procedures that do not seamlessly integrate alternate paths, or misconfigurations that delay response.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Verification shortcuts include focusing on recent drill reports and incident post-mortems – these documents typically record the robustness (or weakness) of alternate communication processes.
• Critical indicators of poor implementation include a single point of failure in the network design and evidence that backup roles were never activated during tests.
• Always correlate documentation with real-world testing events; policies without periodic exercises tend to be a sign of superficial compliance. The details that matter most are the frequency and completeness of live failover tests and the proper integration of alternate networks in the overall system architecture.
content:
Statement:
Establish [Assignment: organization-defined alternate communications paths] for system operations organizational command and control.

Guidance:
An incident, whether adversarial- or nonadversarial-based, can disrupt established communications paths used for system operations and organizational command and control. Alternate communications paths reduce the risk of all communications paths being affected by the same incident. To compound the problem, the inability of organizational officials to obtain timely information about disruptions or to provide timely direction to operational elements after a communications path incident, can impact the ability of the organization to respond to such incidents in a timely manner. Establishing alternate communications paths for command and control purposes, including designating alternative decision makers if primary decision makers are unavailable and establishing the extent and limitations of their actions, can greatly facilitate the organization’s ability to continue to operate and take appropriate actions during an incident.

title: CROSS DOMAIN POLICY ENFORCEMENT
methodology:
Control: SC-46 – CROSS DOMAIN POLICY ENFORCEMENT

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence includes dedicated hardware appliances (e.g., cross domain gateways) or explicitly segmented network zones with enforced firewalls or routers that segregate security domains.
• Technical artifacts such as configuration files for firewalls/gateways, network diagrams showing physical cabling and segregation, and cross domain communication policies that enforce interface restrictions.
• Operational patterns include active monitoring and logging of cross domain data flows, regular automated checks against policy rules, and maintenance of physical separation evidence (e.g., labeled ports, distinct routing paths).

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may list a gateway in documentation but use reconfigured or legacy hardware that permits traffic flows outside defined boundaries.
• Superficial segmentation (e.g., use of VLANs on a single physical switch without true physical separation) can be mistaken for enforcement, even though it doesn’t preclude covert channels.
• Relying solely on static documentation or periodic manual inspections rather than continuous, automated enforcement and logging can lead to compliance on paper only.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
• CHECK: Examine the configuration of the cross domain gateway or firewall(s) to confirm no logical paths are available between interfaces.
  WHY: Ensures that policy enforcement mechanisms are actively blocking unauthorized data flows between security domains.
  HOW: Collect configuration files and audit logs from the device; use automated configuration management tools to verify that rules enforce isolation (e.g., rule sets explicitly deny cross interface traffic).
  FAIL: Presence of permissive rules or undocumented exceptions that allow potential logical bypasses.

• CHECK: Verify network architecture and physical cabling diagrams (if physical enforcement is used) to ensure proper physical separation between domains.
  WHY: Documentation and physical evidence confirm that there are no covert data channels via shared physical infrastructure.
  HOW: Review updated network topology diagrams; inspect labeled ports and physical interfaces; compare against the security control baseline documentation.
  FAIL: Inconsistent or outdated diagrams, evidence of shared infrastructure where isolation is required.

• CHECK: Review system and security logs for evidence of enforced cross domain traffic monitoring and anomaly detection.
  WHY: Active monitoring indicates that the enforcement mechanism is operational and that attempts to bypass the control are caught in real time.
  HOW: Use SIEM tools to query logs for cross domain traffic; look for alerts triggered by policy violations; verify that log retention and alerting processes are in place.
  FAIL: Sparse or absent logging entries concerning cross domain communication, indicating uncontrolled or unmonitored traffic flows.

• CHECK: Test for potential covert channels between domains by simulating cross domain requests or traffic using a controlled test.
  WHY: A controlled test can reveal whether policy enforcement truly blocks unauthorized communication, beyond documented configurations.
  HOW: Set up a test scenario where controlled data requests are sent across domains; use network analysis tools to detect if traffic is able to traverse enforced boundaries; compare network traceroutes and firewall logs for discrepancies.
  FAIL: Successful transmissions that bypass the intended enforcement mechanism, or lack of corresponding blocking alerts in monitoring systems.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Always cross-reference hardware and software configurations with live network traffic and physical infrastructure checks. Documentation alone can be engineered to appear compliant.
• Pay attention to legacy systems; even if new devices enforce strong segmentation, older components could still allow bypass paths.
• Automation is key. Utilize continuous configuration management and network monitoring tools to generate real-time assessments rather than relying on periodic manual reviews.
• Validate that any cross domain gateways have undergone recent vulnerability assessments, ensuring that their configurations haven’t been altered to introduce hidden channels.
• Critical detail: If logical policy enforcement is used, ensure that there is no inadvertent bridging between interfaces through misconfigured routing or intermediary devices.

By following these practical validation steps, auditors can move beyond theoretical compliance and genuinely confirm the operational effectiveness of Cross Domain Policy Enforcement.
content:
Statement:
Implement a policy enforcement mechanism [Selection: physically; logically] between the physical and/or network interfaces for the connecting security domains.

Guidance:
For logical policy enforcement mechanisms, organizations avoid creating a logical path between interfaces to prevent the ability to bypass the policy enforcement mechanism. For physical policy enforcement mechanisms, the robustness of physical isolation afforded by the physical implementation of policy enforcement to preclude the presence of logical covert channels penetrating the security domain may be needed. Contact ncdsmo@nsa.gov for more information.

title: SYSTEM TIME SYNCHRONIZATION
methodology:
For SC-45 SYSTEM TIME SYNCHRONIZATION, the following methodology focuses on verifying that the organization is not only declaring a policy for time synchronization but is also actively enforcing it across systems and components with observable artifacts. 

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Check for running time synchronization services (e.g., NTP, chrony, or Windows Time Service) on endpoint systems. Look for configuration files that list authoritative and secondary time sources, and system logs showing periodic synchronization events. 
• Technical Artifacts: Hardware and OS-level logs that record time adjustments (e.g., /var/log/syslog, /var/log/messages, or event viewer logs on Windows), configuration files (e.g., /etc/ntp.conf, /etc/chrony.conf, Windows registry entries) showing defined NTP servers, and scheduled tasks or daemons. 
• Operational Patterns: Consistent and periodic clock adjustments aligning with authoritative sources, low drift rates across interconnected systems, and alerts that trigger when time discrepancies exceed defined thresholds. 

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Superficial Configurations: Organizations may simply list external NTP servers in policies or on paper, but fail to ensure daemon functionality or proper monitoring of synchronization status. 
• Misconfigured Parameters: Time server configurations that do not reflect the required authoritative or secondary time sources, using outdated or internal non-authoritative servers. 
• Inconsistent Monitoring: Logging and alerting mechanisms might not be active or configured to trigger when synchronization fails, making it appear compliant while silently drifting. 

3. VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Verify active time synchronization service across systems.
WHY: A running NTP/chrony or equivalent service that regularly contacts external authoritative sources indicates actual enforcement of time synchronization.
HOW: Use system commands (e.g., 'service ntpd status', 'systemctl status chronyd', or checking Windows Time Service status) and review process lists. 
FAIL: The service is not running, disabled, or misconfigured, indicating potential non-synchronization.

CHECK: Inspect configuration files for defined authoritative and secondary time sources.
WHY: Configuration files like /etc/ntp.conf or /etc/chrony.conf should explicitly list primary and secondary servers, ensuring redundancy and accuracy in time sync.
HOW: Access and review configuration files, verifying that servers are configured with proper parameters (e.g., server address, poll interval, max allowable drift).
FAIL: Missing, commented out, or incorrectly set server entries that do not point to trusted external time authorities.

CHECK: Analyze system logs for recorded time synchronization events and time correction measures.
WHY: Logs showing periodic synchronization or adjustments indicate active synchronization. Look for entries that indicate correction when clocks deviate beyond allowed thresholds.
HOW: Query logs (using grep or log analysis tools) for keywords such as “sync”, “time correction”, or “offset” and verify the timestamps for regular activities.
FAIL: Absence of synchronization records or too infrequent/irregular events may signal issues with timeliness of updates.

CHECK: Test operational effectiveness by introducing controlled time drift on a test system and monitor if synchronization occurs within the defined period.
WHY: This helps verify that the mechanism is actively enforcing synchronization when the drift exceeds the threshold.
HOW: On a non-production system, artificially adjust the system clock and then check if the synchronization service re-aligns the clock quickly as per organization-defined parameters.
FAIL: If the external time service does not re-synchronize the clock within the defined period, it may indicate misconfiguration or a non-responsive service.

CHECK: Validate that monitoring tools and alerts are configured to report synchronization failures.
WHY: Automated alerts help ensure that any deviation beyond acceptable deviation levels is promptly detected and corrected.
HOW: Check monitoring dashboards, review alert policies, and simulate failure conditions to see if an alert is triggered.
FAIL: Lack of generated alerts or notification mechanisms when synchronization drift is detected.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Verification Shortcuts: For Unix/Linux systems, running a simple command such as ‘ntpq -p’ (for NTP) or ‘chronyc tracking’ provides immediate insight into synchronization status and server responses. 
• Critical Indicators: Look at the offset values and delay in synchronization reports. Offsets consistently in tens or hundreds of milliseconds could reveal issues if more granular time is needed. 
• Key Details: Ensure that both configuration and runtime evidence exist. Merely listing NTP servers in a policy without evidence in logs or configuration files can be a red flag. 
• Redundancy Check: Confirm that there is an effective switch to a secondary time source, especially for geographically distributed installations, which is critical for uninterrupted operations.

This methodology emphasizes operational checks, continuous monitoring, and dynamic testing to ensure that SYSTEM TIME SYNCHRONIZATION is not only documented but fully functional in live environments and resilient against common compliance evasions.
content:
Statement:
Synchronize system clocks within and between systems and system components.

Guidance:
Time synchronization of system clocks is essential for the correct execution of many system services, including identification and authentication processes that involve certificates and time-of-day restrictions as part of access control. Denial of service or failure to deny expired credentials may result without properly synchronized clocks within and between systems and system components. Time is commonly expressed in Coordinated Universal Time (UTC), a modern continuation of Greenwich Mean Time (GMT), or local time with an offset from UTC. The granularity of time measurements refers to the degree of synchronization between system clocks and reference clocks, such as clocks synchronizing within hundreds of milliseconds or tens of milliseconds. Organizations may define different time granularities for system components. Time service can be critical to other security capabilities—such as access control and identification and authentication—depending on the nature of the mechanisms used to support the capabilities.

Enhancements:
(1) SYSTEM TIME SYNCHRONIZATION | SYNCHRONIZATION WITH AUTHORITY TIME SOURCE
(a) Compare the internal system clocks [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] with [Assignment: organization-defined authoritative time source]; and
(b) Synchronize the internal system clocks to the authoritative time source when the time difference is greater than [Assignment: organization-defined time period]. Discussion: Synchronization of internal system clocks with an authoritative source provides uniformity of time stamps for systems with multiple system clocks and systems connected over a network. Related Controls: None.
(2) SYSTEM TIME SYNCHRONIZATION | SECONDARY AUTHORITY TIME SOURCE (a) Identify a secondary authoritative time source that is in a different geographic region than the primary authoritative time source; and (b) Synchronize the internal system clocks to the secondary authoritative time source if the primary authoritative time source is unavailable. Discussion: It may be necessary to employ geolocation information to determine that the secondary authoritative time source is in a different geographic region. References: [IETF 5905].

title: DETONATION CHAMBERS
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Visible, isolated environments configured specifically as detonation chambers (sandbox or virtualized environment) in email gateways or endpoint security architectures.
• Technical artifacts such as system logs showing file submissions, analysis reports, alerts from dynamic execution analyses, and isolation network configurations indicating that the environment is isolated from production networks.
• Operational patterns including periodic automated analysis of suspicious files, integration with threat intelligence feeds, and system-generated verdicts on potential malicious behavior that trigger containment or further investigation.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations sometimes fake compliance with a basic sandbox that doesn’t fully isolate execution environments, risking exposure of production systems.
• Superficial logging where automated reports exist but are not actively monitored or integrated with incident response processes.
• Using outdated or misconfigured virtualized environments that lack the level of isolation required, allowing malware to exhibit benign behavior rather than its real harmful intent.

VALIDATION STEPS:

CHECK: Verify the presence and configuration of the detonation chamber environment.
WHY: A properly deployed and isolated sandbox environment is essential for safely executing and analyzing suspicious files without risk to production environments.
HOW: Check system configuration settings (via management console or configuration files) to confirm that a designated detonation chamber/sandbox exists and is set to operate in isolation. Verify via network diagrams and isolation configurations that the detonation chamber is separate from the production network.
FAIL: Absence of a dedicated sandbox environment or evidence that the detonation chamber is running within production systems.

CHECK: Validate operational integration between the detonation chamber and threat detection/response workflows.
WHY: Real-time operational integration ensures that any malicious activity detected is swiftly followed by appropriate incident response actions.
HOW: Trigger a controlled test (using a known benign file that mimics suspicious characteristics) to check if the file is automatically routed to the detonation chamber. Review logs and integration records that confirm the file was analyzed, and that a report was generated and fed into the incident response process.
FAIL: No log entries indicating the file was routed to a sandbox, or the lack of integration with alerting and incident response workflows.

CHECK: Inspect system and audit logs for evidence of dynamic execution analysis events.
WHY: Detailed logs are crucial for confirming that suspicious files are executed in isolation and that their behavior is analyzed.
HOW: Audit log repositories for records showing submissions to the detonation chamber, analysis outcomes, and remediation actions. Look for time-stamped records that correlate file submissions with analysis results.
FAIL: Incomplete or missing logs that make it impossible to trace sandbox analysis events, or logs that only list an execution attempt without further analysis details.

CHECK: Confirm isolation controls and network segmentation for the detonation chamber are actively enforced.
WHY: Ensuring that the execution environment remains isolated prevents any malicious code from affecting live systems.
HOW: Review network architecture documents and verify via configuration management tools that the detonation chamber network segment is logically and/or physically separated. Use network scanning tools to ensure that the detonation chamber does not have unnecessary access to production systems.
FAIL: Evidence that the detonation chamber environment is not properly segmented or can reach production systems directly, raising risk of lateral movement.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Verification Shortcut: Request a live demo or screen capture from the security operations center showing the detonation chamber in action to quickly confirm its active status and isolation properties.
• Critical Indicator: Look for continuous and detailed integration of sandbox analysis logs into threat intelligence systems; a stagnant or low-volume log may indicate that suspicious samples are bypassing proper analysis.
• Focus on the technical configurations (e.g., virtual network settings, firewall rules) rather than solely relying on policy documents. Any evidence that the environment is misconfigured or inadequately segregated is a strong indicator of poor implementation.
• Check for periodic testing or red teaming exercises that validate the detonation chamber’s efficacy, as static configurations might hide latent misconfigurations.

This methodology focuses on obtaining automated and observable evidence of the detonation chamber’s existence, its proper configuration in isolation, and its active integration with incident response processes, all of which help ensure the control’s operational effectiveness.
content:
Statement:
Employ a detonation chamber capability within [Assignment: organization-defined system, system component, or location].

Guidance:
Detonation chambers, also known as dynamic execution environments, allow organizations to open email attachments, execute untrusted or suspicious applications, and execute Universal Resource Locator requests in the safety of an isolated environment or a virtualized sandbox. Protected and isolated execution environments provide a means of determining whether the associated attachments or applications contain malicious code. While related to the concept of deception nets, the employment of detonation chambers is not intended to maintain a long-term environment in which adversaries can operate and their actions can be observed. Rather, detonation chambers are intended to quickly identify malicious code and either reduce the likelihood that the code is propagated to user environments or prevent such propagation completely.

title: USAGE RESTRICTIONS
methodology:
Control ID: SC-43 – USAGE RESTRICTIONS

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Documentation that defines usage restrictions and guidelines for system components (e.g., mobile code, mobile devices, wireless peripherals, printers). These guidelines should clearly list authorized components and any limitations on their use.
• Configuration settings or automated scripts enforcing restrictions on hardware and software components – for instance, whitelists for peripheral devices or mobile code execution environments.
• Operational logs and audit trails showing that system activities (e.g., device connections, mobile code execution) are actively monitored and that only authorized uses are permitted.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Superficial documentation that states policies on usage restrictions without corresponding technical enforcement controls.
• Static inventories that list approved devices but omit dynamic, runtime verification mechanisms (e.g., real-time logging and alerting on non-compliant usage).
• Reliance solely on endpoint configuration which can be manually overridden – lacking automated monitoring or periodic audits.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Validate that a documented list of authorized system components and usage restrictions exists and ties directly to the system inventory.
WHY: Demonstrates that restrictions are clearly defined and relevant to the components in use.
HOW: Review asset management databases and policy documents to confirm components and usage guidelines match system configurations; cross-reference with hardware admission logs.
FAIL: Missing or outdated documentation; discrepancies between inventory list and actual system components in operation.

CHECK: Examine system configurations and enforcement mechanisms (e.g., network access controls, mobile code execution restrictions) that operationalize usage restrictions.
WHY: Automated configuration settings are less prone to manipulation than self-reported policies.
HOW: Inspect configuration files, automated scripts, or access control lists on firewalls and endpoint protection systems; verify alerting and blocking rules.
FAIL: Lack of automated controls or enforcement mechanisms that can be manually bypassed, indicating only a paper control exists.

CHECK: Review operational and audit logs to verify that the use of system components is monitored and unauthorized usage is detected and blocked.
WHY: Logs prove that restrictions are enforced during runtime and non-compliant events trigger responses.
HOW: Use SIEM tools or direct log queries to check for alerts related to unauthorized device usage, mobile code execution, or peripheral connection events; validate incident response actions if abnormalities are noted.
FAIL: Sparse or missing logs; evidence that audit trails are not reviewed or discrepancies between configuration policies and operational enforcement.

CHECK: Conduct interviews with system administrators and security officers regarding how usage restrictions are implemented, monitored, and maintained in day-to-day operations.
WHY: Direct interviews can confirm whether controls are enforced operationally and not just documented on paper.
HOW: Prepare targeted questions on enforcement strategies, exception handling, and recent incidents related to unauthorized usage; corroborate interview findings with technical evidence.
FAIL: Inconsistent responses or an overreliance on procedural descriptions without supporting technical data.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Always correlate documented policies with real-time system data; static lists without dynamic enforcement can be easily out-of-date or circumvented.
• Prioritize evidence showing both configuration effectiveness (e.g., automated controls) and active monitoring (e.g., logs, SIEM alerts).
• Be critical of claims that policies are followed if system logs or audit records lack indications that the measures are continually enforced.
• Check for regular updates to usage restrictions and enforcement settings; outdated controls are a prime indicator of poor operational security posture.

Following these steps ensures a practical verification of SC-43, highlighting not only the presence of usage restrictions but also their continuous and effective operational enforcement.
content:
Statement:
a. Establish usage restrictions and implementation guidelines for the following system components: [Assignment: organization-defined components]; and
b. Authorize, monitor, and control the use of such components within the system.

Guidance:
Usage restrictions apply to all system components including but not limited to mobile code, mobile devices, wireless access, and wired and wireless peripheral components (e.g., copiers, printers, scanners, optical devices, and other similar technologies). The usage restrictions and implementation guidelines are based on the potential for system components to cause damage to the system and help to ensure that only authorized system use occurs.

title: SENSOR CAPABILITY AND DATA
methodology:
Control ID: SC-42 – SENSOR CAPABILITY AND DATA

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Devices (e.g., mobile phones, tablets) in designated areas should display user-visible notifications when sensor functions (e.g., GPS, microphone, camera) are activated. There should be signage or system overlays warning users when sensors are active.
• Technical Artifacts: Configuration files and management console settings should document approved sensor configurations and remote activation exceptions; automated logging and access records must show that sensor data is only transmitted to authorized endpoints.
• Operational Patterns: Regular audits of sensor activity logs, observed patterns of sensor activation (both remote and manual), and system alerts indicate active monitoring of sensor usage.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Fake Compliance: Organizations may simply deploy a banner or static notice on devices rather than dynamically linking sensor activation events to the display. Relying solely on policy documents without enforcing technical controls is common.
• Surface-Level Implementations: Configuring sensors without restricting remote activation options; logging sensor activation events without proper review of whether data flows to unauthorized recipients.
• Technical Gaps: Lack of integration between sensor activation events and real-time alerting mechanisms, or configuration exceptions not updated in response to changing risk profiles.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK 1: Verify that devices in controlled areas display a visible, real-time sensor activation notice when sensors (e.g., camera, GPS, microphone) are enabled.
WHY: This ensures that sensor use is explicitly indicated to the user group, preventing covert activation.
HOW: Observe a sample of mobile devices in designated secure areas; trigger sensor activation and confirm that a user-visible indicator (pop-up or LED) appears.
FAIL: Absence of any activation notice or presence of only static disclaimers unrelated to real-time sensor status.

CHECK 2: Inspect system configurations for sensor settings to ensure remote activation is prohibited unless explicitly allowed under pre-defined exceptions.
WHY: Proper configuration confirms that only authorized sensor activations occur, reducing the risk of covert data collection.
HOW: Review technical configuration files and centralized management consoles for sensor settings; validate against the organization-defined exceptions list.
FAIL: Presence of ‘default enabled’ remote activation without condition checks, or configurations that resemble a policy only without enforced technical limits.

CHECK 3: Review audit logs and network records to verify that sensor-collected data is transmitted solely to authorized endpoints.
WHY: Ensuring data is directed only toward authorized recipients confirms that sensor data is used for approved purposes and minimizing potential abuse.
HOW: Use automated log analysis tools to filter sensor data transmissions; compare endpoint addresses against the list of authorized roles/entities.
FAIL: Discovering instances where sensor data is sent to non-approved locations or shows evidence of unauthorized third-party access.

CHECK 4: Validate that sensor configurations employ data minimization techniques (e.g., obfuscation of non-essential details) to protect privacy.
WHY: Minimization limits exposure of unnecessary personal information at the sensor’s collection point, which mitigates privacy risks.
HOW: Inspect device configuration guidelines; test sensor outputs to ensure that non-required data (e.g., detailed images, full audio) is appropriately minimized or obscured during collection.
FAIL: Raw, high-fidelity sensor data is collected/transmitted without any signs of minimization or filtering controls.

CHECK 5: Confirm that periodic audits and user training records exist and reflect active efforts to monitor and address improper sensor use.
WHY: Audits and training are strong operational indicators that sensor control measures are actively managed beyond mere technical configurations.
HOW: Request and review audit reports, training logs, and incident reports related to sensor activations over the past period; verify that actions taken align with documented controls.
FAIL: No evidence of regular audits, outdated training records, or lack of incident reports regarding sensor misuse.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Verification Shortcuts: Directly trigger sensor functions on a test device in a controlled area to instantly check for proper real-time notifications and system logging. Automated scripts can simulate sensor activation events.
• Critical Indicators: Watch for gaps in log timestamps or irregular sensor configuration settings that may indicate bypassed technical controls. A discrepancy between shown sensor use and audit trails is a red flag.
• Key Details: Ensure that any exceptions for remote activation are narrow and fully documented. Check that sensor data flows consistently match the approved list of authorized endpoints or roles, and that supporting training and audit programs are current.

Overall, it is essential that both technical controls and operational practices work seamlessly to enforce authorized sensor use and transparent disclosure to affected users. This layered approach minimizes risks of covert data collection and unauthorized sensor data transmissions.
content:
Statement:
a. Prohibit [Selection (one or more): the use of devices possessing [Assignment: organization-defined environmental sensing capabilities] in [Assignment: organization-defined facilities, areas, or systems]; the remote activation of environmental sensing capabilities on organizational systems or system components with the following exceptions: [Assignment: organization-defined exceptions where remote activation of sensors is allowed]]; and  b. Provide an explicit indication of sensor use to [Assignment: organization-defined group of users].

Guidance:
Sensor capability and data applies to types of systems or system components characterized as mobile devices, such as cellular telephones, smart phones, and tablets. Mobile devices often include sensors that can collect and record data regarding the environment where the system is in use. Sensors that are embedded within mobile devices include microphones, cameras, Global Positioning System (GPS) mechanisms, and accelerometers. While the sensors on mobiles devices provide an important function, if activated covertly, such devices can potentially provide a means for adversaries to learn valuable information about individuals and organizations. For example, remotely activating the GPS function on a mobile device could provide an adversary with the ability to track the movements of an individual. Organizations may prohibit individuals from bringing cellular telephones or digital cameras into certain designated facilities or controlled areas within facilities where classified information is stored or sensitive conversations are taking place.

Enhancements:
Verify that the system is configured so that data or information collected by the [Assignment: organization-defined sensors] is only reported to authorized individuals or roles. Discussion: In situations where sensors are activated by authorized individuals, it is still possible that the data or information collected by the sensors will be sent to unauthorized entities. Related Controls: None.
(2) SENSOR CAPABILITY AND DATA | AUTHORIZED USE Employ the following measures so that data or information collected by [Assignment: organization-defined sensors] is only used for authorized purposes: [Assignment: organization-defined measures]. Discussion: Information collected by sensors for a specific authorized purpose could be misused for some unauthorized purpose. For example, GPS sensors that are used to support traffic navigation could be misused to track the movements of individuals. Measures to mitigate such activities include additional training to help ensure that authorized individuals do not abuse their authority and, in the case where sensor data is maintained by external parties, contractual restrictions on the use of such data. Related Controls: PT-2.
(4) SENSOR CAPABILITY AND DATA | NOTICE OF COLLECTION Employ the following measures to facilitate an individual’s awareness that personally identifiable information is being collected by [Assignment: organization-defined sensors]: [Assignment: organization-defined measures]. Discussion: Awareness that organizational sensors are collecting data enables individuals to more effectively engage in managing their privacy. Measures can include conventional written notices and sensor configurations that make individuals directly or indirectly aware through other devices that the sensor is collecting information. The usability and efficacy of the notice are important considerations. Related Controls: PT-1, PT-4, PT-5.
