(5) SENSOR CAPABILITY AND DATA | COLLECTION MINIMIZATION Employ [Assignment: organization-defined sensors] that are configured to minimize the collection of information about individuals that is not needed. Discussion: Although policies to control for authorized use can be applied to information once it is collected, minimizing the collection of information that is not needed mitigates privacy risk at the system entry point and mitigates the risk of policy control failures. Sensor configurations include the obscuring of human features, such as blurring or pixelating flesh tones. Related Controls: SA-8, SI-12. References: [OMB A-130], [SP 800-124].

title: PORT AND I/O DEVICE ACCESS
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Physically: Inspect server and workstation chassis for missing or physically disabled ports. Look for tamper-evident seals, label stickers, or signage that indicate I/O devices (like USB or CD/DVD drives) have been removed or disabled.
• Logically: Check BIOS/UEFI configurations where ports have been disabled, and observe system logs from endpoint management solutions that note device policy enforcement. Verify system scans (e.g., via configuration management tools) report devices as disabled.
• Operational: Repeated automated scans or audits showing no active drivers for unauthorized I/O devices and consistent reporting from vulnerability management tools that port controls are set as defined by policy.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations might simply disable device drivers or use software policies that can be easily reverted by users with administrative privileges, rather than physically removing the port.
• Faking compliance by only applying settings on a subset of systems or not updating BIOS-level settings across all endpoints leads to gaps in enforcement.
• Temporary implementation: Systems might enforce controls initially but then revert through auto-updates or misconfigurations, offering a superficial compliance check without sustained security.

VALIDATION STEPS:
CHECK: Verify physical port/I/O device removal by inspecting a sample of systems
WHY: Physical removal or disabling is the most robust method and shows hands-on enforcement of policy
HOW: Perform an inventory inspection (physical audit) of a random selection of systems in data centers and offices; check for removal of USB, Thunderbolt, and optional unused optical drives and verify presence of tamper labels or permanent modifications
FAIL: Finding intact ports or attached I/O devices that can be re-enabled easily indicates non-compliance

CHECK: Review BIOS/UEFI configurations on a sample set of systems
WHY: BIOS-level settings are less likely to be bypassed or reset, validating the control’s technical enforcement
HOW: Randomly select endpoints and examine BIOS/UEFI settings to confirm that all nonessential ports have been disabled per organization policy (check for disabled USB ports, etc.)
FAIL: BIOS settings are found to allow enabled ports or can be easily altered without proper security measures

CHECK: Query and analyze endpoint management logs for device control policies
WHY: Operational logs provide automated, continuous evidence of enforcement and potential alert on policy violations
HOW: Collect logs and automated reports from endpoint management systems and review configuration reports showing that I/O device access is blocked or disabled. Look for audit events around device driver changes or device insertion attempts.
FAIL: Inconsistencies or missing log entries regarding enforcement or evidence of drivers re-enabling indicate a potential gap

CHECK: Test by attempting to connect unauthorized devices
WHY: Hands-on tests (red team style) verify that disabled ports or devices cannot be reactivated by internal users or attackers
HOW: In a controlled environment, try attaching a USB device or other peripheral and then observe if the device is recognized by the system. Document any incident where the device bypasses controls.
FAIL: Successful detection of the device by the system or insufficient mitigation measures indicates poor enforcement

EXPERT TIPS:
• Shortcut: Use network-based asset management systems to quickly flag systems with a policy non-compliance trend in port configurations.
• Critical indicator: Consistency across BIOS settings, OS-level configurations, and physical port states is key. Partial implementations are a strong risk indicator.
• Detail matters: Emphasize permanency in disabling (physical removal or BIOS lock-down) rather than temporary (software-disable or policy-based) measures; verify that emergency or fallback configurations do not inadvertently re-enable ports in recovery situations.
content:
Statement:
[Selection: Physically; Logically] disable or remove [Assignment: organization-defined connection ports or input/output devices] on the following systems or system components: [Assignment: organization-defined systems or system components].

Guidance:
Connection ports include Universal Serial Bus (USB), Thunderbolt, and Firewire (IEEE 1394). Input/output (I/O) devices include compact discs and digital versatile disc drives. Disabling or removing such connection ports and I/O devices helps prevent the exfiltration of information from systems and the introduction of malicious code from those ports or devices. Physically disabling or removing ports and/or devices is the stronger action.

title: WIRELESS LINK PROTECTION
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence includes wireless network devices (access points, controllers) that are configured with robust encryption and channel/hop management settings. Technical artifacts such as device configuration files, firmware logs, and audit records verify that organizational policies for wireless link protection are enforced. Operational patterns include routine wireless spectrum scans, incident logs that capture attempted signal parameter manipulations, and regularly updated firmware addressing vulnerabilities.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may publish policy documents and screenshots of settings that appear compliant, yet fail to enforce encryption end-to-end. For instance, default settings with weak ciphers may be left unchanged, or certain link segments (e.g., internal-only segments) might be configured without sufficient protection. Superficial implementations can include misconfigured antennas or unmanaged hardware, leaving signals prone to side-channel analysis.

VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Configuration Verification
WHY: Confirm that wireless devices enforce strong encryption and robust signal parameter protection as defined by the organization.
HOW: Access device management interfaces, retrieve configuration settings, and verify use of approved protocols (e.g., WPA2-Enterprise/WPA3) and settings (e.g., dynamic frequency selection, channel hopping).
FAIL: Evidence that devices are using default, outdated, or weak encryption settings, or misconfigured parameters that do not adhere to the policy.

CHECK: Physical and Signal Leakage Assessment
WHY: Ensures that wireless signals are contained within authorized areas and physical antenna placements limit exposure.
HOW: Conduct a site walk and perform wireless spectrum analysis using a network analyzer tool to check for signal leakage beyond controlled boundaries and assess the physical security of antenna placements.
FAIL: Detection of wireless signals in unauthorized areas or physical setups that allow signal spillover which could be exploited.

CHECK: Monitoring and Log Analysis
WHY: Operational effectiveness is shown via consistent monitoring for signal parameter attack attempts.
HOW: Review SIEM logs and wireless IDS/IPS records for alerts related to abnormal signal behavior, parameter anomalies, or interference attacks. Confirm that log retention and alert thresholds match the organization’s defined security posture.
FAIL: Lack of relevant logging or absence of automated alerts following potential signal parameter attack attempts.

CHECK: Firmware and Software Update Verification
WHY: Updated firmware/software is critical for mitigating known vulnerabilities and ensuring protections are current.
HOW: Check version numbers against vendor recommendations and verify that recent security patches addressing wireless link vulnerabilities have been applied. Review change management records for wireless devices.
FAIL: Devices running outdated firmware or missing critical updates that address signal parameter attacks.

CHECK: Personnel Interviews and Walk-Throughs
WHY: Direct inquiries with network staff can reveal how policies are practically implemented and monitored.
HOW: Conduct interviews with network/security personnel to confirm that wireless scanning, threat assessment routines, and incident response procedures are active. Cross-check with operational procedures and incident reports.
FAIL: Inconsistent or superficial responses that suggest a disconnect between documented policies and active practices.

EXPERT TIPS
• Verification Shortcut: Use a wireless network analyzer to perform a quick spectrum scan to check for rogue signals or weak encryption indicators that might not be obvious from documentation alone.
• Critical Details: Pay special attention to antenna orientation, power levels, and physical barriers; these can often be superficially adjusted without real security impact.
• Red Flag: Absence of regular log reviews or inconsistent firmware update records may indicate that the control is not being actively managed even if documented policies exist.
• Overall, prioritize automated, observable evidence (such as current configuration exports, real-time logs, and wireless scans) over static policies to ensure the control is genuinely effective.
content:

Protect external and internal [Assignment: organization-defined wireless links] from the following signal parameter attacks: [Assignment: organization-defined types of signal parameter attacks or references to sources for such attacks].

Guidance:
Wireless link protection applies to internal and external wireless communication links that may be visible to individuals who are not authorized system users. Adversaries can

title: PROCESS ISOLATION
methodology:
Control: SC-39 PROCESS ISOLATION

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• CHECK: Verify that each process runs within its own dedicated address space as defined by the operating system kernel configuration.
  WHY: Separate address spaces indicate that the system enforces strict memory boundaries, limiting unauthorized access between processes.
  HOW: Identify system configuration files (e.g., kernel boot parameters, memory management configs), and review process listings with tools like “ps” and memory mapping tools. Also look for evidence in system logs indicating address space allocation per process.
  FAIL: A single shared or overlapping address space or absence of memory protection mechanisms suggests the controls are not effective.

• CHECK: Assess the use of sandboxing or virtualization technologies to enforce process isolation.
  WHY: Use of these technologies confirms that the system logically enforces distinct execution domains beyond basic OS separation.
  HOW: Examine configuration files or management consoles for sandbox environments, containerization tools (Docker, LXC) or hypervisor settings that show separate virtualization instances. Validate through automated system queries such as analyzing VM IDs and container namespaces.
  FAIL: Discovery of processes running with full host privileges or evidence that sandboxing is disabled or improperly configured indicates non-compliance.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may document process isolation policies without implementing underlying hardware memory management protections, relying solely on software-level isolation. 
• Some systems might allow inter-process communication that bypasses isolation boundaries (e.g., shared memory segments not strictly controlled).
• Failing to enforce isolation at the thread level in a multi-threaded environment might technically be compliant with process isolation but miss the granular requirements outlined in enhancements.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
• CHECK: Inspect the operating system’s memory management configuration (e.g., using dmesg, /proc/self/maps, or similar interfaces) to ensure that distinct address spaces are created at process launch.
  WHY: This is direct evidence that the system architecture enforces isolation at the hardware and OS levels.
  HOW: Run commands that reveal memory mappings and verify that no overlapping segments exist among isolated processes. Compare configurations with hardware separation features enabled (e.g., Intel VT-x/AMD-V) if available.
  FAIL: If evidence shows shared memory segments where isolation should be enforced or if memory mapping analysis reveals improper segmentation, then process isolation is likely compromised.

• CHECK: Validate that sandboxing or virtualization techniques are actively used for executing untrusted code or applications.
  WHY: Active sandboxing indicates that additional isolation measures are implemented to minimize risk from potentially malicious processes.
  HOW: Review live system process queries, control panel outputs, or run automated scripts that detect container environments and verify distinct execution domains. Interview system administrators if needed to confirm practice vs. policy.
  FAIL: Lack of any sandboxed or containerized execution examples, or instances where untrusted code runs on the host without isolation, signal a bypass of process isolation controls.

• CHECK: Test the separation mechanisms by attempting controlled inter-process interactions that should be blocked by the isolation boundaries.
  WHY: Real-world tests can reveal weaknesses that documentation may not cover, ensuring that the control is effective in practice.
  HOW: Conduct a penetration test where one process attempts to access another’s memory space; successful defenses (segmentation faults, access violations) confirm proper isolation.
  FAIL: If the test results in unauthorized access or modifications between process memory spaces, this is a clear indicator of failure.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• TIP: Automated scripts that analyze system logs and memory maps can quickly spot anomalies in address space allocation – leverage these for continuous monitoring.
• TIP: Focus on areas with legacy or custom configurations. Often, custom-built solutions may follow outdated practices that bypass true process isolation.
• TIP: Special attention should be paid to enhancements such as hardware-assisted memory separation. Failure to activate these features in supported environments is a common gap.
• TIP: Verify both process and thread isolation in multi-threaded platforms. While OS-level process isolation might be in place, threads within the same process need separate guards if required by design.

This comprehensive methodology ensures that verification goes beyond mere policy existence and truly inspects the operational mechanics of process isolation in the environment.
content:
Statement:
Maintain a separate execution domain for each executing system process.

Guidance:
Systems can maintain separate execution domains for each executing process by assigning each process a separate address space. Each system process has a distinct address space so that communication between processes is performed in a manner controlled through the security functions, and one process cannot modify the executing code of another process. Maintaining separate execution domains for executing processes can be achieved, for example, by implementing separate address spaces. Process isolation technologies, including sandboxing or virtualization, logically separate software and firmware from other software, firmware, and data. Process isolation helps limit the access of potentially untrusted software to other system resources. The capability to maintain separate execution domains is available in commercial operating systems that employ multi-state processor technologies.

Enhancements:
PROCESS ISOLATION | HARDWARE SEPARATION
Implement hardware separation mechanisms to facilitate process isolation.
Discussion: Hardware-based separation of system processes is generally less susceptible to compromise than software-based separation, thus providing greater assurance that the separation will be enforced. Hardware separation mechanisms include hardware memory management.
Related Controls: None.
PROCESS ISOLATION | SEPARATE EXECUTION DOMAIN PER THREAD
Maintain a separate execution domain for each thread in [Assignment: organization-defined multi-threaded processing].
Discussion: None.
Related Controls: None.

title: OPERATIONS SECURITY
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Documented OPSEC processes that reference the five-step procedure (identification, analysis of threats, analysis of vulnerabilities, risk assessment, and countermeasure implementation).
• Risk analysis reports, threat and vulnerability assessments, and records of applied countermeasures available as technical artifacts.
• Operational evidence such as logs showing periodic review meetings, update cycles, and integration of OPSEC into the System Development Life Cycle (SDLC).

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations might produce high-level policies and generic training documentation without demonstrating active OPSEC processes across projects.
• Over-reliance on static documentation that isn’t updated, thus faking an active process without real operational follow‐through.
• Fake compliance by showing only snapshots of historical risk analyses without evidence of continuous threat monitoring or current vulnerability assessments.

VALIDATION STEPS:
CHECK: Review recent and dated risk analysis reports and OPSEC documentation that explicitly follow the five-step process.
WHY: This demonstrates that the organization not only has a policy but actively identifies, analyzes, and mitigates risks over the SDLC.
HOW: Request samples of risk assessments, threat/vulnerability analysis reports, and review change logs for updates. Interview team members involved in OPSEC activities.
FAIL: Missing or outdated analyses, or reports that lack any link to ongoing operations or threat updates.

CHECK: Validate that operational countermeasures are active by examining system logs and incident response records.
WHY: Actual incident logs and SIEM alerts confirm that countermeasures aren’t just documented but are effective in real-time operations.
HOW: Access configuration management and monitoring logs. Verify that countermeasure mechanisms (like access controls, anomaly detections, and alerting) are triggered and logged accordingly.
FAIL: Absence of updated logs or evidence that countermeasures are only theoretical or have never been used in practice.

CHECK: Conduct interviews with process owners and operational staff to verify the integration of OPSEC activities within the SDLC.
WHY: This ensures that the risk, threat, and vulnerability assessments are communicated and acted upon, leading to improvements in both security posture and operational controls.
HOW: Prepare targeted questions about the OPSEC process, review training records, and cross-check integration of threat analysis in project documentation.
FAIL: Inconsistent or superficial responses indicating that the OPSEC process is not effectively integrated into daily operations.

CHECK: Inspect evidence of continuous monitoring and update procedures, such as automated SIEM alerts and regular review meetings.
WHY: Operational effectiveness depends on real-time monitoring and rapid response to emerging threats.
HOW: Examine SIEM configurations, verify that alerts for anomalies in operations security are active, and review meeting minutes or update logs showing periodic reviews.
FAIL: Alerts not properly configured, infrequent monitoring, or gap periods where no updates were performed, suggesting a lapse in active control.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Shortcut: Focus on SIEM alerts and automated logs which are hard to fake and provide clear evidence of active monitoring.
• Critical Indicator: Recent updates to risk analysis documents and a proactive record of incident responses – static documents without current follow‐up are red flags.
• Key Detail: Validate tailored training sessions and clear demonstration of how threat intelligence and vulnerability data have been integrated into operational decisions.
• Remember: Policies alone aren’t enough; look for evidence of a live, evolving process that adjusts to emerging threats in real time.
content:
Statement:
Employ the following operations security controls to protect key organizational information throughout the system development life cycle: [Assignment: organization-defined operations security controls].

Guidance:
Operations security (OPSEC) is a systematic process by which potential adversaries can be denied information about the capabilities and intentions of organizations by identifying, controlling, and protecting generally unclassified information that specifically relates to the planning and execution of sensitive organizational activities. The OPSEC process involves five steps: identification of critical information, analysis of threats, analysis of vulnerabilities, assessment of risks, and the application of appropriate countermeasures. OPSEC controls are

title: OUT-OF-BAND CHANNELS
methodology:
Control ID: SC-37 – OUT-OF-BAND CHANNELS

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Look for written procedures, operational manuals, or standard operating procedures (SOPs) that detail the use of out‐of‐band channels (e.g., separate network maps, physical delivery logs, and designated courier services). Check for physical delivery receipts, proof of identification at receipt (if using courier services), and segregated communication equipment in secured areas.
• Technical Artifacts: Verify network documentation showing physically distinct pathways for sensitive transmissions, separate interfaces or dedicated hardware for out-of-band communications, and system logs that register out‐of‐band transactions. Identify audit logs and alerting mechanisms that are independent of regular traffic channels.
• Operational Patterns: Observe recurring transactions that exclusively use non‐standard paths, such as backup key management information being sent via designated out-of-band paths. Audit trails should reflect separation between operational traffic and sensitive data movement.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Faking Compliance: Organizations might produce out-of-date or incomplete documentation that nominally references out‐of‐band channels without evidence of current operational use. They may also point to network diagrams or policy documents that do not match what is actually in use.
• Surface-Level Implementations: Implementing a separate email account or VPN as an out‐of‐band channel that still runs on the same physical infrastructure as the production network can be a superficial fix. Relying solely on “paper policies” without demonstrating physical or technical separation also fails in practice.
• Technical Gaps: Missing evidence of real-time logging, absence of audits, or failure to provide verifiable proof of separate channels (e.g., lack of dedicated hardware or documented physical paths) can indicate a gap.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK 1: Verify Out-Of-Band Channel Documentation
• WHY: Valid recent procedures and network diagrams that detail segregated channels prove planned control existence and adherence.
• HOW: Obtain and review current SOPs, network topology diagrams, and system configuration guides that explicitly mark out-of-band channels. Interview responsible personnel to confirm regular operation of these channels.
• FAIL: If documentation is outdated, vague, or if personnel cannot clearly explain the channel’s structure and usage.

CHECK 2: Confirm Physical and Network Separation
• WHY: Demonstrable physical and technical separation ensures that these channels are not co-mingled with routine operational traffic, thus reducing exposure to common threats.
• HOW: Inspect physical network setups, secure areas where out-of-band devices are housed, and verify independent network routing through network management tools. Look for dedicated hardware (e.g., separate modems, routers or communication interfaces) used exclusively for out-of-band communication.
• FAIL: Evidence indicates shared or logically aggregated pathways with the main production network, or if network maps fail to clearly delineate separate channels.

CHECK 3: Validate Transmission Evidence
• WHY: Artifacts such as delivery logs, secure transmission receipts, or audit trails confirm that out-of-band channels are actively used for the designated information and confirm recipient identity compliance.
• HOW: Inspect logs (digital timestamps, courier service receipts, or manual sign-off forms) that capture the actual use of out-of-band channels for secure delivery. Cross-reference these logs with secure transaction databases and review key transmissions (e.g., cryptographic key alerts, maintenance notifications).
• FAIL: Absence of clear audit trails, missing or inconsistent receipts, or evidence that all transmissions occur via in-band channels.

CHECK 4: Test Operational Functionality
• WHY: Live tests or scenario-based exercises demonstrate the effectiveness and readiness of the out-of-band channels under operational conditions.
• HOW: Arrange a controlled test scenario where a secure message or item is transmitted via the designated out-of-band channel. Monitor end-to-end execution including physical receipt, identification verification, and log generation. Validate that the process meets documented procedures.
• FAIL: If the test exercise fails to follow the documented process, or if tracking and logging are inconsistent with requirements.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Verification Shortcuts: Ask for a recent incident or test report that specifically used the out-of-band channel. Often, real operational examples or simulated exercises provide more credible evidence than static documentation.
• Critical Indicators: Focus on evidence of real-time logging and independent audit trails. Confirm that physical separation is not merely assumed but demonstrable through hardware inspection or separate network management interfaces.
• Details That Matter: Pay attention to the granularity of recipient authentication (e.g., physical checks like government-issued IDs at courier receipt) and verify that there is a clear separation in both documentation and everyday practice. Inconsistent or missing timestamps, vague procedural instructions, or vague network maps are red flags for poor implementation.

This methodology ensures that verification of SC-37 is driven by observable, operational evidence that goes beyond paper policies, checking both technical artifacts and practical execution in real-world conditions.
content:
Statement:
Employ the following out-of-band channels for the physical delivery or electronic transmission of [Assignment: organization-defined information, system components, or devices] to [Assignment: organization-defined individuals or systems]: [Assignment: organization-defined out-of-band channels].

Guidance:
Out-of-band channels include local, non-network accesses to systems; network paths physically separate from network paths used for operational traffic; or non-electronic paths, such as the U.S. Postal Service. The use of out-of-band channels is contrasted with the use of in-band channels (i.e., the same channels) that carry routine operational traffic. Out-of-band channels do not have the same vulnerability or exposure as in-band channels. Therefore, the confidentiality, integrity, or availability compromises of in-band channels will not compromise or adversely affect the out-of-band channels. Organizations may employ out-of-band channels in the delivery or transmission of organizational items, including authenticators and credentials; cryptographic key management information; system and data backups; configuration management changes for hardware, firmware, or software; security updates; maintenance information; and malicious code protection updates.

Enhancements:
OUT-OF-BAND CHANNELS | ENSURE DELIVERY AND TRANSMISSION
Employ [Assignment: organization-defined controls] to ensure that only [Assignment: organization-defined individuals or systems] receive the following information, system components, or devices: [Assignment: organization-defined information, system components, or devices].
Discussion: Techniques employed by organizations to ensure that only designated systems or individuals receive certain information, system components, or devices include sending authenticators via an approved courier service but requiring recipients to show some form of government-issued photographic identification as a condition of receipt. Related Controls: None.
References: [SP 800-57-1], [SP 800-57-2], [SP 800-57-3].

title: DISTRIBUTED PROCESSING AND STORAGE
methodology:
Control SC-36 – DISTRIBUTED PROCESSING AND STORAGE Validation Methodology

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Evidence: Look for system/network diagrams, architecture reviews, or configuration documents that indicate components are intentionally spread across multiple physical locations or logical domains. 
• Technical Artifacts: Replication configuration files, synchronization scripts/settings, monitoring logs from polling tools, and redundancy verification records (for example, proofs of parallel data processing, duplicate systems with active load balancing).
• Operational Patterns: Regular polling reports that identify fault tolerance and error corrections across distributed nodes, and logs showing synchronization of duplicate systems.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Faked Compliance: Organizations might simply document a distributed approach without implementing true physical/logical separation, e.g., running multiple virtual instances on the same physical hardware. 
• Surface-Level Implementations: Using backup or redundant systems that are not synchronized in real time, or merely declaring ‘high availability’ in policy without evidence of isolated fault domains.
• Technical Gaps: Lack of active polling implementations or errant polling that does not cover all distributed components, thereby leaving silent downtime or compromise undetected.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Verify physical/logical separation of processing and storage components
WHY: Ensures that components are not just logically duplicated but are truly isolated to prevent single points of failure or compromise.
HOW: Interview system administrators, examine network diagrams, and physically verify server locations or virtualization host configurations. Use asset management systems to cross-check against declared distribution.
FAIL: All processing components are hosted on the same server cluster or physical location without evidence of isolation.

CHECK: Examine synchronization mechanisms between duplicate systems
WHY: Real-time or near real-time synchronization is critical for operational resilience in distributed architectures
HOW: Review system logs demonstrating synchronization events, inspect configuration files, and observe the use of synchronization software or scripts across distributed components. Check settings for time stamps and log entries showing consistent updates.
FAIL: Absence of logs or visible synchronization processes; configuration files lacking parameters for time-based replication or consistency checks.

CHECK: Assess the effectiveness of polling techniques for fault/error detection
WHY: Polling techniques are meant to rapidly identify faults, errors, or potential compromises in distributed environments.
HOW: Evaluate the performance and configuration of the polling tool deployed. Validate polling reports against known fault events. Look for automated alerts or ticketing entries that trigger on discrepancies between distributed data outcomes.
FAIL: Infrequent, outdated, or missing polling reports; manual intervention required for error detection, indicating a lack of robust automated surveillance.

CHECK: Validate redundancy actions and response plans when discrepancies are found
WHY: Ensuring that out-of-sync or compromised components are quickly remediated is a sign of an operationalized and secure distributed processing strategy.
HOW: Review incident response logs and compare them with polling alerts. Interview operations to confirm adherence to defined mitigation actions. Check evidence of automated failover or manual intervention logs especially after fault detection events.
FAIL: No documented evidence of remedial actions following polling alerts; discrepancies in replication or synchronization are left unaddressed.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Verification Shortcut: Cross-reference polling tool logs with network performance monitoring systems for a second layer of automated evidence. If discrepancies exist in both, it likely indicates poor implementation.
• Critical Indicators: Look for consistency in timestamp logs across distributed nodes, which confirm reliable and prompt synchronization; irregularities here often signal configuration errors or latent faults.
• Important Details: Verify that duplicate components are not merely virtual clones hosted on a single physical machine. Physical separation or logically partitioned domains (with independent administrative control) are crucial. Also pay attention to how and when polling tools flag issues, as delays or false negatives indicate undermine resilience.

This comprehensive methodology focuses on validating whether distributed processing and storage are genuinely resistant to faults and adversarial actions, ensuring the decentralized architecture truly supports redundancy, synchronization, and rapid error detection.
content:
Statement:
Distribute the following processing and storage components across multiple [Selection: physical locations; logical domains]: [Assignment: organization-defined processing and storage components].

Guidance:
Distributing processing and storage across multiple physical locations or logical domains provides a degree of redundancy or overlap for organizations. The redundancy and overlap increase the work factor of adversaries to adversely impact organizational operations, assets, and individuals. The use of distributed processing and storage does not assume a single primary processing or storage location. Therefore, it allows for parallel processing and storage. SC-36 and CP-9(6) require the duplication of systems or system components in distributed locations. The synchronization of duplicated and redundant services and data helps to ensure that information contained in the distributed locations can be used in the mission or business functions of organizations, as needed.

Enhancements:
DISTRIBUTED PROCESSING AND STORAGE | POLLING TECHNIQUES
(a) Employ polling techniques to identify potential faults, errors, or compromises to the following processing and storage components: [Assignment: organization-defined distributed processing and storage components]; and
(b) Take the following actions in response to identified faults, errors, or compromises: [Assignment: organization-defined actions].
Discussion: Distributed processing and/or storage may be used to reduce opportunities for adversaries to compromise the confidentiality, integrity, or availability of organizational information and systems. However, the distribution of processing and storage components compares the processing results and/or storage content from the distributed components and subsequently votes on the outcomes. Polling identifies potential faults, compromises, or errors in the distributed processing and storage components.
Related Controls: SI-4.
DISTRIBUTED PROCESSING AND STORAGE | SYNCHRONIZATION
Synchronize the following duplicate systems or system components: [Assignment: organization-defined duplicate systems or system components].

title: EXTERNAL MALICIOUS CODE IDENTIFICATION
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Existence of dedicated scanning systems that continuously probe external networks for malicious code and malicious websites.
• Evident use of isolation measures such as virtualization, where scanning actions are performed in a contained environment separate from production systems.
• Operational logs and alerts showing active scans, updates from threat intelligence feeds, and successful detections of known external threats.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Relying solely on passive filtering (e.g., web proxies or firewalls) instead of active network probing, which may not truly qualify as external malicious code identification.
• Implementing the required systems only as a documented policy, without operational evidence of regular scans.
• Failing to enforce proper isolation; for instance, scanning processes running on production systems rather than dedicated virtualized sandboxes.

VALIDATION STEPS:
CHECK: Confirm active external scanning processes are in place.
WHY: Continuous probing and scanning is essential to proactively detect malicious code on external websites.
HOW: Review security management consoles or SIEM dashboards for scheduled or real-time external scanning jobs; check network logs for traffic patterns that match out-bound scanning to known risky domains.
FAIL: No visible logs or configuration entries indicating active external scanning functions.

CHECK: Verify that isolation measures (preferably virtualization) are employed during scanning operations.
WHY: Isolation is key to preventing the accidental infection of operational systems if malicious code is encountered.
HOW: Examine system architecture diagrams or configurations to ensure scanning operations run in sandboxed environments; verify that detected malicious code analysis occurs on virtualized systems.
FAIL: Scans are executed on live production networks or there’s no clear evidence of sandboxing/virtualization.

CHECK: Assess integration and regular updating of threat intelligence feeds that support scanning criteria.
WHY: Up-to-date threat signatures and intelligence ensure that the system recognizes emerging external malicious code threats.
HOW: Inspect configuration settings and logs showing connections to threat intelligence feeds, including update timestamps and refresh cycles; verify alerting for newly detected threats.
FAIL: Configuration logs reveal stale intelligence data, or feeds have not been updated in a timely manner.

CHECK: Review incident logs for detection and response relating to external malicious code.
WHY: Real-world detections and corresponding alerts indicate that the control is functioning effectively.
HOW: Analyze alert logs and incident reports that detail events of detected external malicious code; cross-reference with known threat events from external scans.
FAIL: Incident logs do not reflect any detections, or the alert response does not correspond with the scanning system’s operational windows.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Focus on testing dynamic, real-time feeds and verify that the scanning system reacts to current threat intelligence rather than outdated static rules.
• Cross-check logs and alert incidents with network traffic captures for external scanning activity; automated correlation is often more reliable than relying on policy documents alone.
• When possible, simulate a controlled benign threat (using industry-standard test payloads) in an isolated environment to validate detection without risking production assets.
• Pay close attention to the isolation mechanisms — their misconfiguration is often a telltale sign that the environment may not be fully secured against lateral movement in case of accidental exposure.

This validation methodology leverages both technical evidence and operational evidence to ensure that the external malicious code identification control is active, effective, and not simply a paper implementation.
content:
Statement:
Include system components that proactively seek to identify network-based malicious code or malicious websites.

Guidance:
External malicious code identification differs from decoys in SC-26 in that the components actively probe networks, including the Internet, in search of malicious code contained on external websites. Like decoys, the use of external malicious code identification techniques requires some supporting isolation measures to ensure that any malicious code discovered during the search and subsequently executed does not infect organizational systems. Virtualization is a common technique for achieving such isolation.

title: NON-MODIFIABLE EXECUTABLE PROGRAMS
methodology:
Control: SC-34 NON-MODIFIABLE EXECUTABLE PROGRAMS

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Evidence that system components (operating environments and designated applications) are loaded from hardware-enforced, read-only media (e.g., firmware-configured boot devices, immutable disk partitions, or one-time programmable ROM).
• Artifacts include boot logs referencing read-only media, validated hash values (golden images) stored off-system, digital certificates confirming integrity, and system BIOS/UEFI configurations that enforce boot from non‐writable media.
• Operational patterns: Systems that by design do not accept runtime modifications to operating system or critical applications, with process isolation and audit logs indicating no reprogramming events.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may produce paperwork claiming that read-only media is used while failing to enforce hardware write-protection (e.g., using software policies only).
• Evidence of read-only media might be faked through labels or directory permissions without underlying hardware enforcement, such as partitions that are ‘mounted’ as read-only but can be remounted.
• Failure to control all designated system components (e.g., neglecting certain firmware or application portions) allows attackers to substitute modified code, even if some media remain read-only.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK 1: Verify Boot Media Hardware Enforcement
WHY: Confirms that the operating environment is physically tethered to non-modifiable, read-only hardware, mitigating risk of unauthorized changes.
HOW:
 • Inspect BIOS/UEFI settings to confirm boot order privileges the hardware-enforced, read-only device.
 • Examine boot logs or system event logs for messages indicating boot from read-only media.
 • Physically verify that the media (e.g., DVD-R, fixed ROM) is installed and cannot be updated in system configuration.
FAIL: BIOS settings show boot from writable media, or boot log events contradict the use of read-only devices.

CHECK 2: Validate Integrity of Executable Code Using Cryptographic Hashes
WHY: Ensures that the executables loaded from read-only media match the approved, unaltered code base.
HOW:
 • Obtain the golden hash values from a secure source.
 • Use automated vulnerability/scanner tools or manual hash verification (e.g., SHA-256) on the operating system and critical application images in the designated read-only storage.
 • Compare the calculated hashes with the approved values.
FAIL: Discrepancies between the calculated hash and approved golden values, or inability to perform hash validation due to misconfiguration.

CHECK 3: Examine Media Write-Protection Mechanisms and Access Controls
WHY: Direct verification that once the executable code is written, mechanisms prevent rewriting or tampering.
HOW:
 • Assess system configuration to confirm hardware-level protection (e.g., physical write-protection tabs for optical media, jumper settings on ROM boards) are enabled.
 • Review access control policies for any interfaces that could potentially reprogram or modify the media.
 • Verify, via automated tool or manual inspection, that no software processes can override the hardware settings.
FAIL: Discovering any active, unauthorized write interfaces, evidence of reprogramming capability, or misconfigured hardware settings that allow rewriting.

CHECK 4: Review and Test for Persistence of Non-Modifiable Storage Across Restarts
WHY: Verifies that no unintended persistence mechanisms default to writable states across component restarts or power cycles.
HOW:
 • Initiate a system restart or power cycle; then check system boot logs and system configuration to ensure that all designated components continue to reference the read-only media.
 • Use automated scripts to record media mount options and confirm they remain read-only over multiple cycles.
FAIL: Media settings revert to writable, or logs indicate any switch to persistent writable storage after restart.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Directly query the system’s BIOS/UEFI using command-line utilities (e.g., dmidecode on Linux) to quickly confirm boot device type and configuration.
• Indicator: Discrepancies between documented hardware read-only settings and live system settings (especially after updates or maintenance) are critical red flags.
• Tip: Focus on verifying physical and hardware-enforced protections rather than relying solely on logical OS-level read-only mounts, as these can be more easily bypassed.
• Double-check firmware and hardware revision numbers against known secure versions to ensure non-modifiable conditions were indeed applied at the time of manufacture or provisioning.

This methodology balances technical verification with practical on-site and automated checks to ensure that non-modifiable executable programs are not vulnerable to tampering or unauthorized modification.
content:
Statement:
For [Assignment: organization-defined system components], load and execute:

a. The operating environment from hardware-enforced, read-only media; and

b. The following applications from hardware-enforced, read-only media: [Assignment: organization-defined applications].

Guidance:
The operating environment for a system contains the code that hosts applications, including operating systems, executives, or virtual machine monitors (i.e., hypervisors). It can also include certain applications that run directly on hardware platforms. Hardware-enforced, (DVD-R) disk drives as well as one-time, programmable, read-only memory. The use of non-modifiable storage ensures the integrity of software from the point of creation of the read-only image. The use of reprogrammable, read-only memory can be accepted as read-only media provided that integrity can be adequately protected from the point of initial writing to the insertion of the memory into the system, and there are reliable hardware protections against reprogramming the memory while installed in organizational systems.

Enhancements:
NON-MODIFIABLE EXECUTABLE PROGRAMS | NO WRITABLE STORAGE
Employ [Assignment: organization-defined system components] with no writable storage that is persistent across component restart or power on/off.
Discussion: Disallowing writable storage eliminates the possibility of malicious code insertion via persistent, writable storage within the designated system components. The restriction applies to fixed and removable storage, with the latter being addressed either directly or as specific restrictions imposed through access controls for mobile devices.
Related Controls: AC-19, MP-7.
NON-MODIFIABLE EXECUTABLE PROGRAMS | INTEGRITY PROTECTION ON READ-ONLY MEDIA
Protect the integrity of information prior to storage on read-only media and control the media after such information has been recorded onto the media.
Discussion: Controls prevent the substitution of media into systems or the reprogramming of programmable read-only media prior to installation into the systems. Integrity protection controls include a combination of prevention, detection, and response.
Related Controls: CM-3, CM-5, CM-9, MP-2, MP-4, MP-5, SC-28, SI-3.
NON-MODIFIABLE EXECUTABLE PROGRAMS | HARDWARE-BASED PROTECTION [Withdrawn: Moved to SC-51.]

title: SYSTEM PARTITIONING
methodology:
Control SC-32 SYSTEM PARTITIONING – Validation Methodology

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Architectural Separation: Look for up-to-date network diagrams and system inventories that clearly show system components allocated into distinct physical or logical domains. Evidence may include network topology diagrams, VLAN configurations, and physical rack allocations.
• Technical Artifacts: Check firewall configurations, router ACLs, and traffic filtering rules confirming that partition boundaries are enforced. Log files and network flow records that monitor inter-domain communications also serve as strong evidence.
• Operational Patterns: Identify routine review of partition boundaries, patch management schedules based on group assignments, and alerts for unauthorized cross-domain traffic indicating an active control.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Document-Only Approaches: Organizations may maintain partitioning policies and diagrams that aren’t reflected in actual configurations. This paper-only approach can give a false sense of security.
• Virtual Separation Pitfalls: Implementations that “virtually” separate system components on the same physical host or network segment without robust segmentation (e.g., using weak VLAN segmentation or misconfigured inter-VLAN routing) may be insufficient.
• Misconfigured Managed Interfaces: Surface-level firewall rules or ACLs may appear compliant but might allow unintended communication channels between domains, undermining the isolation.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Verify Physical and Logical Separation in Network Diagrams
WHY: Accurate diagrams that depict separate physical racks, rooms, or logical segments confirm that the intended partitioning design is implemented.
HOW: Request the latest network and system architecture diagrams. Cross-check these diagrams with asset inventories and physical site inspections if possible.
FAIL: Diagrams are outdated, incomplete, or inconsistent with the actual physical/logical system layout.

CHECK: Inspect Configuration of Firewalls and Router ACLs
WHY: Properly configured network devices enforce isolation by restricting inter-domain communication, thereby operationalizing the partitioning.
HOW: Extract and review configuration files from firewalls, routers, and switches. Use automated configuration scanning tools to validate that access control lists limit traffic between defined domains.
FAIL: Configurations reveal permissive rules that allow cross-domain access or lack explicit segmentation controls.

CHECK: Review Traffic Flow and Log Data
WHY: Regular traffic monitoring logs and anomaly alerts provide observable evidence that partition boundaries are respected in practice.
HOW: Analyze network flow logs and intrusion detection system alerts. Look for evidence of unintended cross-domain communications or policy violations between partitioned segments.
FAIL: Log analysis shows regular instances of unauthorized or unexpected communications between partitions.

CHECK: Validate Enforcement of Privileged Function Separation (Enhanced)
WHY: For domains where privileged functions are concerned, physical separation reduces the risk of a single point of failure.
HOW: Confirm that systems handling critical or privileged operations are housed in separate physical environments. This can include inspecting operational procedures, physical site access logs, or environment-specific configuration settings.
FAIL: Privileged modules are co-located with general operations or operational evidence suggests that separation isn’t enforced in practice.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Verification Shortcut: Use network scanning tools (e.g., Nmap) from within each partition to attempt cross-connectivity; inability to connect is a strong sign of proper isolation.
• Critical Indicator: Discrepancies between documented partitioning and actual configurations/logs are a red flag. Always favor data from automated monitoring tools over manual policy descriptions.
• Key Detail: Ensure that all managed interfaces (including remote management) strictly enforce cross-domain restrictions; misconfigured interfaces are a common point of failure.
• Cross-Validation: Compare network diagrams, configuration files, and traffic logs – any inconsistency can indicate that the control is only partially implemented or frequently bypassed.

This step-by-step methodology emphasizes practical, operational checks to ensure that system partitioning is not just a documented policy but is actively enforced and maintained in the live environment.
content:
Statement:
Partition the system into [Assignment: organization-defined system components] residing in separate [Selection: physical; logical] domains or environments based on [Assignment: organization-defined circumstances for physical or logical separation of components].

Guidance:
System partitioning is part of a defense-in-depth protection strategy. Organizations determine the degree of physical separation of system components. Physical separation options include physically distinct components in separate racks in the same room, critical components in separate rooms, and geographical separation of critical components. Security categorization can guide the selection of candidates for domain partitioning. Managed interfaces restrict or prohibit network access and information flow among partitioned system components.

Enhancements:
SYSTEM PARTITIONING | SEPARATE PHYSICAL DOMAINS FOR PRIVILEGED FUNCTIONS
Partition privileged functions into separate physical domains.
Discussion: Privileged functions that operate in a single physical domain may represent a single point of failure if that domain becomes compromised or experiences a denial of service.
Related Controls: None.
References: [FIPS 199], [IR 8179].

title: COVERT CHANNEL ANALYSIS
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Existence of a documented covert channel analysis report that details identified storage and/or timing channels with corresponding risk assessments.
   • Technical artifacts such as simulation outputs, network traffic logs, and measurement reports that quantify the maximum bandwidth of the covert channels identified in both test and operational environments.
   • Operational patterns showing that channels have been periodically tested for exploitability, with mitigation measures in place (e.g., reduced bandwidth settings) and retrospective logs of any covert channel tests.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations may simply produce a static analysis report without real dynamic testing in operational environments, leading to over-reliance on assumptions rather than measured evidence.
   • Falsified or out-of-date simulation outputs that do not represent current system configurations or production load conditions.
   • Superficial documentation that claims analysis was performed, but without detailed technical artifacts or observable operational adjustments (e.g., bandwidth mitigation) applied consistently across environments.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   • CHECK: Verify the existence of a detailed covert channel analysis report.
         WHY: The report should detail the specific communication paths with potential for covert channels, including both storage and timing types, thereby providing evidence that analysis was conducted.
         HOW: Review the analysis documentation for methodology, identified channels, simulation results, and risk assessments. Cross-check with system architecture diagrams.
         FAIL: Absence of a formal report or a report that only contains generic assessments without system-specific details.

   • CHECK: Confirm that the maximum bandwidth for each identified covert channel has been measured in an operational environment.
         WHY: Measured bandwidth (versus theoretical estimates) provides reliable evidence that the organization understands the risk and has quantified it under realistic conditions.
         HOW: Inspect measurement logs, automated test results, and monitoring dashboards that record covert channel bandwidth metrics during live or simulated production conditions.
         FAIL: Evidence limited to lab or development environment measurements without validation under real operational conditions.

   • CHECK: Validate that exploitability tests have been conducted on a subset of identified covert channels.
         WHY: Testing for exploitability moves the process beyond static analysis to dynamic validation that the channels cannot be leveraged for an unauthorized information flow.
         HOW: Examine testing records, penetration test reports, or run controlled tests to attempt data exfiltration using the identified channels, and check that any exploitable pathways are documented with remediation actions.
         FAIL: Lack of testing evidence or tests that are only theoretical, with no practical attempts to simulate an exploit.

   • CHECK: Assess the operational monitoring and logging to detect any anomalous behavior that may indicate covert channel exploitation.
         WHY: Continuous monitoring ensures that if a covert channel becomes active or its characteristics change, it is promptly identified and addressed.
         HOW: Review network monitoring dashboards, system logs, and incident response records for events tied to unusual data timing or storage patterns that align with covert channel activity.
         FAIL: No monitoring or inconsistent logging data that fails to capture covert communication attempts.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut by reviewing outputs from specialized covert channel analysis tools; consistency across multiple independent tests in production offers strong evidence of operational effectiveness.
   • Critical indicators of poor implementation include reliance on one-off lab results, outdated testing methods, or wide gaps between documented analysis and current production metrics.
   • Pay attention to the granularity of the measurement data – coarse measurements may hide transient spikes in covert channel bandwidth that are exploitable.
   • Ensure that testing methodologies are repeated periodically to capture changes in system behavior over time, especially after major system updates or configuration changes.
content:
Statement:
a. Perform a covert channel analysis to identify those aspects of communications within the system that are potential avenues for covert [Selection (one or more): storage; timing] channels; and
b. Estimate the maximum bandwidth of those channels.

Guidance:
Developers are in the best position to identify potential areas within systems that might lead to covert channels. Covert channel analysis is a meaningful activity when there is the potential for unauthorized information flows across security domains, such as in the case of systems that contain export-controlled information and have connections to external networks (i.e., networks that are not controlled by organizations). Covert channel analysis is also useful for multilevel secure systems, multiple security level systems, and cross-domain systems.

Enhancements:
COVERT CHANNEL ANALYSIS | TEST COVERT CHANNELS FOR EXPLOITABILITY
Test a subset of the identified covert channels to determine the channels that are exploitable.
Discussion: None.
Related Controls: None.
COVERT CHANNEL ANALYSIS | MAXIMUM BANDWIDTH
Reduce the maximum bandwidth for identified covert [Selection (one or more): storage; timing] channels to [Assignment: organization-defined values].
Discussion: The complete elimination of covert channels, especially covert timing channels, is usually not possible without significant performance impacts.
Related Controls: None.
COVERT CHANNEL ANALYSIS | MEASURE BANDWIDTH IN OPERATIONAL ENVIRONMENTS
Measure the bandwidth of [Assignment: organization-defined subset of identified covert channel(s)] in the operational environment of the system.
Discussion: Measuring covert channel bandwidth in specified operational environments helps organizations determine how much information can be covertly leaked before such leakage adversely affects mission or business functions. Covert channel bandwidth may be significantly different when measured in settings that are independent of the specific environments of operation, including laboratories or system development environments.
Related Controls: None.

title: CONCEALMENT AND MISDIRECTION
methodology:
Below is a detailed methodology to validate the implementation of SC-30 (Concealment and Misdirection) controls:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Look for system configurations that actively change system identities, IP addresses, or processing/storage locations according to a schedule or triggered by events. Check for visible decoy systems (honeypots/deception nets) on network diagrams and in network traffic logs.
• Technical Artifacts: Review system logs showing periodic changes (e.g., virtual machine migrations, randomized processing assignments), router or firewall configurations that hide true endpoints, and audit trails indicating the publication of misleading security information on deceptive systems. Automation scripts that rotate or change system configurations are also key indicators.
• Operational Patterns: Identify recurring shifts in system endpoints, randomized processes applied to system resource allocation, and evidence of configured decoy services. Notice deviation from static configurations in network topology over repeated assessments.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Faked Documentation: Organizations may simply produce static policies or diagrams that claim dynamic behaviors without evidence of operational change. Relying on policy documents rather than logged operations is a common evasion.
• Surface-Level Implementation: Deploying a one-time configuration change or isolated instances (e.g., a single honeypot left untouched) without continuous or randomized adjustments does not to truly implement misdirection techniques.
• Technical Gaps: Missing automation logs, unsynchronized changes across multiple system components, or using misleading information that is easily detectable by an experienced adversary.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Verify that system processing or storage relocation is occurring.
WHY: Active, periodic, or randomized changes in system component locations increase adversary uncertainty by breaking static targeting assumptions.
HOW: Review automation logs/schedules for virtual machine migrations or location changes; interview operational staff and check SIEM data for multiple relocation events over defined time periods.
FAIL: Absence of scheduled or randomized relocation events; static server and storage location data during the review period.

CHECK: Validate the existence and operation of decoy systems or deceptive information services.
WHY: Decoy systems and misleading security information directly contribute to misdirection by confusing adversaries about the true system architecture.
HOW: Scan the network for the presence of honeypots or deception nets; verify that these systems are regularly updated with realistic, yet misleading, security state information; check for integration with incident monitoring tools.
FAIL: Lack of decoy system artifacts or deceptive misinformation channels, or if decoy components are misconfigured and easily identifiable.

CHECK: Confirm that concealment configurations (e.g., router or firewall adjustments) are actively hiding true system endpoints.
WHY: Properly configured concealment mechanisms prevent adversaries from easily mapping the network and identifying critical assets.
HOW: Review router/firewall configuration backups and active configuration files for rules that alter packet flows or perform network address translation in a non-standard way; cross-reference with network discovery scans.
FAIL: Static, traditional network configurations lacking dynamic obfuscation rules; easily mapped network topology with no evidence of misdirection techniques.

CHECK: Examine if randomness is introduced into operational processes affecting system communications or asset identification.
WHY: Randomization increases uncertainty, making it difficult for adversaries to predict system responses or target valuable assets.
HOW: Assess if there are automated scripts or controls that apply randomness to at least one of the following: IP address allocation, service endpoints, or processing/storage location schedules; review logs and change management records for randomization artifacts.
FAIL: Absence of randomization scripts or controls, or evidence that changes occur at regular, predictable intervals (i.e., deterministic behavior rather than truly random).

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Focus on automation logs and SIEM event trails. If changes (like migrations or configuration rotations) are visible there and are not documented only in static policies, the control is more likely in actual effect.
• Critical Indicator: Look for mismatches between known static configurations (from baseline network maps) versus dynamically updated configurations accessible in runtime logs. Consistency in dynamic changes is a positive sign.
• Pitfall: Beware of controls that are “check-box” compliant on paper but not verified by active monitoring. Ensure that deception nets and relocation events are not simply theoretical or done on an ad hoc basis.
• Detail Matters: Documentation of timing and randomness parameters is important – ensure that processes are truly randomized and not following predictable patterns.

This methodology emphasizes verifying real-time, automated, and observable behaviors rather than relying solely on policies and static configuration documents.
content:
Statement:
Employ the following concealment and misdirection techniques for [Assignment: organization-defined systems] | [Assignment: organization-defined time periods] to confuse and mislead adversaries: [Assignment: organization-defined concealment and misdirection techniques].

Guidance:
Concealment and misdirection techniques can significantly reduce the targeting capabilities of adversaries (i.e., window of opportunity and available attack surface) to initiate and complete attacks. For example, virtualization techniques provide organizations with the ability to disguise systems, potentially reducing the likelihood of successful attacks without the cost of having multiple platforms. The increased use of concealment and misdirection techniques and methods—including randomness, uncertainty, and virtualization—may sufficiently confuse and mislead adversaries and subsequently increase the risk of discovery and/or exposing tradecraft. Concealment and misdirection techniques may provide additional time to perform core mission and business functions. The implementation of concealment and misdirection techniques may add to the complexity and management overhead required for the system.

Enhancements:
(1) CONCEALMENT AND MISDIRECTION | VIRTUALIZATION TECHNIQUES  [Withdrawn: Incorporated into SC-29(1).]
(2) CONCEALMENT AND MISDIRECTION | RANDOMNESS  Employ [Assignment: organization-defined techniques] to introduce randomness into organizational operations and assets.
Discussion: Randomness introduces increased levels of uncertainty for adversaries regarding the actions that organizations take to defend their systems against attacks. Such actions may impede the ability of adversaries to correctly target information resources of organizations that support critical missions or business functions. Uncertainty may also cause adversaries to hesitate before initiating or continuing attacks. Misdirection techniques that involve ...
(3) CONCEALMENT AND MISDIRECTION | CHANGE PROCESSING AND STORAGE LOCATIONS  Change the location of [Assignment: organization-defined processing and/or storage]  [Selection: [Assignment: organization-defined time frequency]; at random time intervals]].
Discussion: Adversaries target critical mission and business functions and the systems that support those mission and business functions while also trying to minimize the exposure of their existence and tradecraft. The static, homogeneous, and deterministic nature of organizational systems targeted by adversaries makes such systems more susceptible to attacks with less adversary cost and effort to be successful. Changing processing and storage locations (also referred to as moving target defense) addresses the advanced persistent threat using techniques such as virtualization, distributed processing, and replication. This enables organizations to relocate the system components (i.e., processing, storage) that support critical mission and business functions. Changing the locations of processing activities and/or storage sites introduces a degree of uncertainty into the targeting activities of adversaries. The targeting uncertainty increases the work factor of adversaries and makes compromises or breaches of the organizational systems more difficult and time-consuming.
Related Controls: None.
(4) CONCEALMENT AND MISDIRECTION | MISLEADING INFORMATION  Employ realistic, but misleading information in [Assignment: organization-defined system components] about its security state or posture.
Discussion: Employing misleading information is intended to confuse potential adversaries regarding the nature and extent of controls deployed by organizations. Thus, adversaries may employ incorrect and ineffective attack techniques. One technique for misleading adversaries is for organizations to place misleading information regarding the specific controls deployed in external systems that are known to be targeted by adversaries. Another technique is the use of deception nets that mimic actual aspects of organizational systems but use, for example, out-of-date software configurations.
Related Controls: None.
(5) CONCEALMENT AND MISDIRECTION | CONCEALMENT OF SYSTEM COMPONENTS  Employ the following techniques to hide or conceal [Assignment: organization-defined system components]: [Assignment: organization-defined techniques].
Discussion: By hiding, disguising, or concealing critical system components, organizations may be able to decrease the probability that adversaries target and successfully compromise those assets. Potential means to hide, disguise, or conceal system components include the configuration of routers or the use of encryption or virtualization techniques.
Related Controls: None.

title: HETEROGENEITY
methodology:
Below is the expert validation methodology for SC-29 (Heterogeneity):

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: 
     - System architecture diagrams and network inventories that list components by vendor, operating system, and application type.
     - Procurement records or asset inventories that clearly indicate a mix of technology providers and diverse software stacks.
   • Technical Artifacts:
     - Automated configuration management databases (CMDB) showing detailed technology attributes for each component.
     - Logs from vulnerability management systems that highlight disparate patch cycles and vulnerability distributions across different technologies.
   • Operational Patterns:
     - Periodic maintenance and update routines that show a range of disparate remediation efforts across different platforms.
     - Active change management records that document why and how different technologies were purposefully deployed.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Fake Compliance Tactics:
     - Presenting documents with claimed diversity without corresponding evidence in inventory or operations (i.e., a superficial cross-vendor checklist).
     - Resorting to regulatory-compliant architecture diagrams that are not in sync with actual production configurations.
   • Surface-Level Implementations:
     - Deploying components with nominal vendor differences but using similar underlying codebases or libraries that maintain a common vulnerability footprint.
     - Using identical management and patching processes due to a common control system, thereby negating potential benefits of heterogeneity.
   • Technical Gaps:
     - Lack of correlation between documentation and actual production systems evidenced by discrepancies in the CMDB.
     - Failures in observing differences operationally, such as identical access controls or misconfigured firewalls across technologies.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   
   CHECK: Review System Inventories & Architecture Diagrams
   WHY: Verifies that the system components are sourced from multiple technology providers and are not part of a single vendor monoculture.
   HOW: Inspect architecture diagrams, network inventories, and CMDB entries to identify vendor names, operating systems, and application types. Look for evidence of diversity in critical components based on procurement or configuration details.
   FAIL: If a review reveals that most critical components share the same vendor, operating system, or underlying technology stack.
   
   CHECK: Correlate Procurement and Contractual Records
   WHY: Demonstrates that decisions to deploy diverse technology components are deliberate and reflected in procurement documents.
   HOW: Examine procurement records, vendor contracts, or RFP documents that cite multiple vendors for providing similar system components.
   FAIL: If records show a reliance on a single supplier or minimal diversity, contradicting the intended heterogeneous approach.
   
   CHECK: Analyze Vulnerability Management and Patch Cycle Logs
   WHY: Differences in vulnerability profiles and patching cycles across components indicate operational diversity and independent management.
   HOW: Review automated vulnerability scans and patch management logs to verify that diverse technology stacks are managed separately and exhibit varied vulnerabilities/patching timelines.
   FAIL: If vulnerability reports indicate uniform update and patch procedures across components or if a vulnerability in one supplier leads to simultaneous issues across the system.
   
   CHECK: Interview Technical Operations Staff
   WHY: Provides context on how heterogeneity is practiced beyond static configurations and confirms process adherence.
   HOW: Talk with system architects, network engineers, and operations staff to discuss how diverse technologies are actually integrated, managed, and monitored throughout the system lifecycle.
   FAIL: If staff feedback indicates that documented diversity is not reflected in day-to-day operations or that a single technology dominates despite claims of heterogeneity.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Verification Shortcuts: Use automated configuration management tools to query the CMDB for vendor fields and operating system diversity—this provides a quick overview of technology mix.
   • Critical Indicators of Poor Implementation: Uniform patch cycles across components and a single vendor as a primary supplier are red flags indicating inadequate heterogeneity.
   • Details That Matter: Ensure the diversity is not superficial; look at both the hardware/software and the management/operational processes surrounding each component. Paying attention to real incident response and vulnerability mitigation practices is key in assessing true heterogeneity.

This methodology not only checks for the existence of policies but more importantly, validates that the operational implementation of heterogeneous deployment is both deliberate and effective in reducing potential single points of failure.
content:
Statement:
Employ a diverse set of information technologies for the following system components in the implementation of the system: [Assignment: organization-defined system components].

Guidance:
Increasing the diversity of information technologies within organizational systems reduces the impact of potential exploitations or compromises of specific technologies. Such diversity protects against common mode failures, including those failures induced by supply chain attacks. Diversity in information technologies also reduces the likelihood that the means

title: PROTECTION OF INFORMATION AT REST
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• CHECK: Identify active cryptographic solutions on storage systems (e.g., full-disk encryption, database field encryption, encryption at file-level).
  WHY: Visible configuration and system logs showing encryption algorithms in use prove that protection of information at rest is active.
  HOW: Query system configurations, review encryption management dashboards, and inspect logs from encryption services; verify cryptographic settings against organizational standards.
  FAIL: Missing or default encryption settings, absence of logs showing encryption processes, or evidence that encryption is inconsistently applied.

• CHECK: Examine evidence of offline storage controls for sensitive information marked by the organization.
  WHY: Offline storage systems should have restricted network access and physical security measures, demonstrating adherence to the guidance of removing data from online access.
  HOW: Review physical access logs, off-network storage inventory, and backup procedures to ensure designated information is stored offline in secure locations.
  FAIL: Presence of sensitive data on network-connected devices or improper indexing that indicates online exposure.

• CHECK: Validate cryptographic key management practices including safeguarded storage (e.g., hardware-protected key stores like TPMs).
  WHY: Proper key management is critical; the presence of a key management infrastructure and safe-housing for keys is a clear indicator of effective cryptographic protection.
  HOW: Inspect key management logs, key storage configurations, and secure enclave or TPM configuration details; assess key rotation and backup procedures.
  FAIL: Discovery of keys stored in unprotected files, lack of key rotation, or absence of hardware-protected key storage.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations may provide outdated or superficial configuration screenshots without showing real-time, operational evidence.
• Documentation that cites policies without actual enforcement or inspection of live system settings can be faked.
• Partial encryption implementations where only a segment of the sensitive data is protected while other portions remain unencrypted, or encryption keys are stored alongside data in clear text.

VALIDATION STEPS:
1. Automated scan for encryption configurations: Execute scripts or use security tools to query storage systems (servers, databases, cloud storage) for encryption settings and verify compliance.
2. Manual review of system configuration files: Check audit trails and configuration management databases for cryptographic settings and verify that encryption is applied uniformly to all defined information at rest.
3. Key management inspection: Login to the key management system or hardware security module to verify key lifecycle management, protection mechanisms (e.g., TPM, HSM), and restricted access policies.
4. Offline storage verification: Conduct a physical and network segmentation review of systems designated for offline storage to ensure that backup data is not accessible from the main operational network.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Prioritize real-time system queries and automated scans rather than relying solely on policy documents or static screenshots.
• Verify a sample of systems manually to spot misconfigurations that automated tools might overlook—especially around encryption key storage and revocation.
• Look for anomalies in system logs that might indicate intermittent or inconsistent application of the encryption controls (e.g., log entries showing encryption module errors).
