• Confirm with system owners that offline storage protocols (air-gapping or physical isolation) are in active use and not just theoretical procedures.
• When assessing cryptographic implementations, ensure the algorithms in use meet current strength recommendations and that the key rotation policies are enforced consistently.
content:
Statement:
Protect the [Selection (one or more): confidentiality; integrity] of the following information at rest: [Assignment: organization-defined information at rest].

Guidance:
Information at rest refers to information that is not in process or transit and is stored on system components such as hard drives, SAN devices, or databases. The focus is on the state of the information rather than the storage device.

Enhancements:
PROTECTION OF INFORMATION AT REST | CRYPTOGRAPHIC PROTECTION
Implement cryptographic mechanisms to prevent unauthorized disclosure and modification of the following information at rest on [Assignment: organization-defined system components or media]: [Assignment: organization-defined information].
Discussion: The selection of cryptographic mechanisms is based on the need to protect the confidentiality and integrity of organizational information. The strength of mechanisms is commensurate with the security category or classification of the information. Organizations have the flexibility to encrypt information on system components or media or encrypt data structures, including files, records, or fields.
Related Controls: AC-19, SC-12, SC-13.
PROTECTION OF INFORMATION AT REST | OFFLINE STORAGE
Remove the following information from online storage and store offline in a secure location: [Assignment: organization-defined information].
Discussion: Removing organizational information from online storage to offline storage eliminates the possibility of individuals gaining unauthorized access to the information through a network. Therefore, organizations may choose to move information to offline storage in lieu of protecting such information in online storage.
Related Controls: None.
PROTECTION OF INFORMATION AT REST | CRYPTOGRAPHIC KEYS
Provide protected storage for cryptographic keys [Selection: [Assignment: organization-defined safeguards]; hardware-protected key store].
Discussion: A Trusted Platform Module (TPM) is an example of a hardware-protected data store that can be used to protect cryptographic keys.
Related Controls: SC-12, SC-13.

title: PLATFORM-INDEPENDENT APPLICATIONS
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• There is a documented and maintained inventory of applications that explicitly lists platform independent solutions (such as those built on Java, Python, web-based frameworks, or containerized microservices). 
• Application deployment artifacts (configuration files, container orchestration scripts, and automated deployment pipelines) demonstrate the absence of hard-coded, platform-specific dependencies. 
• Operational evidence is provided by successful deployments and operation on multiple hardware/OS environments, and cross-platform test results can be observed in continuous integration/continuous deployment (CI/CD) dashboards.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may claim platform independence in policy or documentation without verifying that the code actually avoids platform-specific APIs or configurations. 
• Some may deploy applications in a containerized or virtualized environment as a superficial nod to platform independence while the underlying application logic remains tied to specific OS features. 
• Surface-level compliance might be faked by maintaining an inventory list with platform independent labels but not performing ongoing cross-platform validation.

VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Verify that the application inventory explicitly identifies platform independent applications.
WHY: A maintained inventory shows that the organization has acknowledged and reviewed its technology stack for cross-platform capabilities.
HOW: Review the configuration management database, application catalog, or system architecture diagrams for references to platform independent implementation (e.g., mentions of container orchestration, cross-platform frameworks).
FAIL: No such inventory exists or the list includes applications that are clearly built on platform-specific frameworks.

CHECK: Assess deployment records and configuration artifacts for evidence of multi-platform support.
WHY: Deployment artifacts, such as container orchestration scripts or multi-environment deployment pipelines, directly indicate that the applications are designed to work across varied platforms.
HOW: Examine CI/CD logs, container configurations, and deployment automation scripts to confirm that applications have been deployed and tested on more than one operating system or platform environment.
FAIL: Artifacts reveal deployments tailored for only one specific operating system, or there is no evidence of tests on diverse platforms.

CHECK: Conduct a code-level review for platform independence patterns.
WHY: Code reviews or automated static analysis identifying use of standardized, cross-platform libraries (and the absence of hard-coded OS-specific calls) provide technical proof of the platform independence design.
HOW: Use code scanning tools to detect usage of platform independent frameworks (for example, Java Virtual Machine-based languages, .NET Core, or web standards) and verify that there are no dependencies on proprietary or OS-specific APIs.
FAIL: Code analysis shows dependencies on platform-specific APIs or libraries that would limit cross-platform functionality.

CHECK: Interview development and operations teams regarding cross-platform testing and confirmation of portability during deployment.
WHY: Direct confirmation from those responsible for the systems can reveal whether proper cross-platform testing is done consistently, beyond just documented claims.
HOW: Discuss testing procedures, obtain test case evidence from CI pipelines, or review meeting minutes and incident logs that pertain to multi-platform deployments.
FAIL: Team members are unable to provide evidence of cross-platform testing, or cross-platform test cases are absent from the CI/CD process.

EXPERT TIPS
• A shortcut is to request recent deployment and test reports from the CI/CD tool; multiple platform logs (e.g., Linux, Windows, containerized environments) are a strong indicator of true platform independence. 
• Pay extra attention to code repositories for any residue of OS-specific hacks or commented notes suggesting platform barriers. 
• Look beyond policy documents—real, operational evidence (like production logs from diverse platform environments) matters most. 
• When in doubt, attempt to replicate the deployment in a different environment as a spot-check to confirm true platform independence.
content:
Statement:
Include within organizational systems the following platform independent applications: [Assignment: organization-defined platform-independent applications].

Guidance:
Platforms are combinations of hardware, firmware, and software used to execute applications. Platform-independent applications promote portability and enhance availability in case systems are under attack.

title: DECOYS
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Visible evidence includes dedicated network segments or systems labeled as decoys/honeypots distinct from production systems. Administrators often display clear indicators (such as specialized banners or network names) identifying these assets as intentional decoys.
• Technical artifacts include configuration files for honeypot software (e.g., Kippo, Dionaea), network diagrams showing isolated decoy zones, and logs/alerts from intrusion detection systems that highlight activity on these decoy systems.
• Operational patterns involve consistent triggering of alerts from non-critical systems, detailed analysis records of attacker behaviors on the decoys, and documented incident response activities that reference decoy alerts.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations might deploy decoy systems in name only without proper isolation or realistic configuration, thereby generating superficial logs that do not reflect genuine attacker interaction.
• Some may simulate decoy activity by generating bogus logs or automated alerts without actual integration into the network traffic flow.
• Misconfigurations such as decoys that are inadvertently reachable as stepping stones to production environments or too easily identifiable by experienced attackers can compromise effective decoy usage.

VALIDATION STEPS:
CHECK: Verify Decoy Systems in Architectural Diagrams
WHY: Ensures that decoy systems are properly planned as part of the network architecture and are isolated from production assets.
HOW: Review network diagrams and system inventories to confirm presence and isolation of decoy systems. Validate that decoys are placed in separate segments and have distinct addressing.
FAIL: Absence of decoy systems in documentation or discovery of decoys within production segments indicates a failed implementation.

CHECK: Inspect Decoy Configuration and Isolation Settings
WHY: Proper decoy configuration is critical to both attract adversaries and to prevent misuse as stepping stones into operational systems.
HOW: Examine configuration files, firewall rules, and segmentation controls for decoy systems. Look for explicit isolation and monitoring configurations that prevent lateral movement.
FAIL: Misconfigured or inadequately isolated decoy settings, such as decoys sharing identical credentials or network spaces with production systems, signal non-compliance.

CHECK: Analyze Decoy System Logs and Incident Alerts
WHY: Operational effectiveness is proven when decoys generate actionable alerts and capture attack attempts.
HOW: Analyze logs from intrusion detection/monitoring systems for activity on decoy systems. Verify that alerts from decoy systems have been properly investigated and correlated with potential attack patterns.
FAIL: Lack of meaningful logs, alerts that are routinely ignored, or absence of documented response actions following decoy alerts indicate ineffective deployment.

CHECK: Evaluate Periodic Testing and Drills of Decoy Effectiveness
WHY: Regular testing demonstrates that decoys are actively maintained, updated, and capable of capturing real-world attack behaviors.
HOW: Review records of scheduled security exercises or red team assessments that include decoy systems. Validate that outcomes and lessons learned are documented and improvements tracked.
FAIL: Absence of testing records or evidence showing decoys are static and never subjected to simulated attacks suggests a potential compliance gap.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Shortcut: Log analytics tools can be invaluable for automating the validation of decoy activity – look for recurrent patterns that align with attacker behavior rather than benign test signals.
• Critical Indicator: If decoy systems are not generating alert volumes similar to what would be expected under attack conditions, it likely reflects poor configuration or ineffective lure design.
• Detail: Ensure decoy systems have realistic configurations (operating system fingerprints, open ports, and services that mimic genuine targets) that cannot be easily identified as fake by sophisticated adversaries.
• Note: Cross-verify incident response actions taken after decoy triggers to ensure that the decoys are not only capturing data but also effectively contributing to threat intelligence and response.

This methodology focuses on validating the real-world operational effectiveness of decoy systems and ensuring they are more than just box-ticking measures.
content:
Statement:
Include components within organizational systems specifically designed to be the target of malicious attacks for detecting, deflecting, and analyzing such attacks.

Guidance:
Decoys, such as honeypots, honeynets, or deception nets, are established to attract adversaries and deflect attacks away from operational systems. They require isolation measures and may require legal consultation.

Enhancements:
DECOYS | DETECTION OF MALICIOUS CODE [Withdrawn: Incorporated into SC-35.]

title: THIN NODES
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for SC-25, THIN NODES, which focuses on verifying that system components run with minimal functionality and data storage as required:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • The system exhibits a lean software stack with only essential services and drivers. This is evident in the absence of unnecessary background processes and installed packages.
   • Technical evidence includes system configuration files, package manifests, and runtime process listings that show only core components are active.
   • Operational patterns include periodic system audits and automated scans confirming the non-existence of extraneous modules or user-installed utilities beyond the minimal set.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations might simply disable visible services without removing underlying packages or libraries that could be exploited.
   • There is a risk of maintaining default or legacy software images that are never pruned for unnecessary features, leading to superficial minimalism.
   • Sometimes, audit logs or configuration repositories are tampered with or set up only to meet documentation requirements rather than true operational minimalism.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify Installed Components
   WHY: Demonstrates that only the necessary software and libraries are present on the system, reducing the attack surface.
   HOW: Evaluate the package list (for example, via rpm -qa on RHEL or dpkg -l on Debian), compare against an approved minimal list defined in the baseline configuration.
   FAIL: The presence of extra, non-essential packages, drivers, or services that are not in the approved baseline.

   CHECK: Assess Running Processes and Services
   WHY: An active process list that contains only necessary services indicates that no redundant functionalities are active concurrently.
   HOW: Use commands like ps or systemctl list-units to verify active processes. Correlate running services against the documented minimal configuration.
   FAIL: Discovery of non-critical or extraneous background processes, which may indicate that minimal functionality has not been enforced.

   CHECK: Inspect System Hardening Configurations
   WHY: Hardening guides typically require removal or disabling of unneeded functionality. Effective configuration files (such as in /etc, registry entries, etc.) will reflect minimal installation.
   HOW: Review configuration files and group policies; check baseline hardening documents against actual settings (e.g., firewall rules, enabled modules) using automated compliance scanners when available.
   FAIL: Configurations containing legacy or non-essential modules, or deviations from the minimal configuration baseline.

   CHECK: Evaluate Audit and Update Mechanisms
   WHY: Continuous monitoring ensures that the system maintains its minimal functionality state over time.
   HOW: Validate that there is an automated process (e.g., via a configuration management tool or scheduled audits) which reviews and remediates deviations from the thin node standard. Look for audit logs that record system component changes.
   FAIL: Absence of automated audit logs or evidence of remediation, suggesting the system may drift from its minimal state.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Start with a trusted baseline image: if the system originated from a hardened, minimal image provided by the vendor or your hardening team, verify that it has not been altered.
   • Use automation: Tools like Chef, Puppet, or custom scripts can very efficiently track installed software and service states. Automated diff comparisons against a sanctioned baseline can rapidly flag deviations.
   • Remain cautious of policy-only artifacts: Documentation might be adjusted to look compliant but if system scans and logs show additional components, then the control is ineffective.
   • Focus on dynamic evidence: Look at real-time process listings and active configuration settings rather than solely documented settings, as these provide stronger assurance that the control is operational.
   • Make sure to trace any deviations back to documented exceptions or change controls. Unexplained extras are critical indicators of poor implementation.

This approach ensures that the control is verified with practical, technical evidence and that compliance cannot be easily faked by merely altering documentation. It focuses on the actual operational health of the node and its minimal functionality posture.
content:
Statement:
Employ minimal functionality and information storage on the following system components: [Assignment: organization-defined system components].

title: FAIL IN KNOWN STATE
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   - Visible evidence includes system configuration files clearly documenting a defined healthy state (the 'known state') to which the system auto-resets in the event of a failure. Screenshots and console outputs from system dashboards showing state transitions during simulated failures serve as proof.
   - Technical artifacts include log files that record failure events with a corresponding action to revert or power-down in a controlled manner, automated test scripts that trigger failover, and archived state data that confirms preservation of system state information.
   - Operational patterns: Regularly scheduled tests or drills (e.g., failover tests) that demonstrate the system consistently reaching its predefined known state after failures.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   - Organizations sometimes provide documentation claiming that a known state has been defined, without actual evidence from system configurations or logs.
   - Superficial implementations may configure a default restart without checking that the state is indeed secure or ‘known’ in practice, leading to random or insecure residual states.
   - Technical gaps are often seen where tests are never triggered or where logs exist but do not correlate with real-world failures; sometimes, only front-end alerts exist with no real background recovery procedures.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify the system’s configuration management records for a clearly defined known state.
   WHY: A documented, approved baseline that the system reverts to is critical for controlled recovery and continuity of operations.
   HOW: Examine configuration management databases or system policy files; look for versioned baseline configurations that define the known safe state; cross-check with change management logs.
   FAIL: If no baseline is defined or if the documented known state does not match the operational configuration.

   CHECK: Review and analyze failure logs to confirm automatic transition to the known state.
   WHY: Logs that capture failure events and subsequent state transitions provide evidence of operational effectiveness.
   HOW: Use log analysis tools to search for entries that record failure events; validate the timestamps and subsequent actions (e.g., invoked scripts, reboot events) leading to a known state; verify that preserved system state information is archived.
   FAIL: If logs do not record any controlled recovery actions or if state information is not preserved.

   CHECK: Conduct or review results of simulated failure testing in a controlled environment.
   WHY: Regular test runs confirm that the failover mechanism works as intended and that the system indeed enters the defined known state.
   HOW: Trigger a benign failure scenario (ideally in a test or sandbox environment) to see if the system reverts to the known state; capture and review the automated responses.
   FAIL: If the simulation results indicate that the system does not revert to the predefined configuration or if unintended behaviors occur upon failure.

   CHECK: Confirm that preserved state information includes sufficient details (for instance, configuration snapshots and status indicators) used for recovery and post-incident analysis.
   WHY: Retaining complete state information ensures that any incident can be thoroughly reviewed to mitigate future risks.
   HOW: Examine the backup routines and state preservation logs; verify that all key state indicators are archived as per the organization-defined policy.
   FAIL: If the state information is incomplete, outdated, or missing important data fields.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   - Verification shortcuts: Look for automated alerts in your SIEM that are triggered upon failure events; these alerts should correlate with a rollback to a known state. The presence of these alerts with corresponding log evidence is a strong indicator of proper implementation.
   - Critical indicators of poor implementation: A lack of regular failover simulation tests, incomplete documentation in system baselines, or a mismatch between the documented state and what is observed in logs.
   - Details that matter: Ensure that the defined known state isn’t merely a ‘safe mode’ but is fully audited and aligned with current system configurations; cross-check with change logs to confirm that state definitions are up-to-date and actively maintained.

This methodology focuses on obtaining automated, observable evidence from system logs, configuration management tools, and simulations, thereby verifying that the fail in known state control is truly effective rather than being just a documented compliance measure.
content:
Statement:
Fail to a [Assignment: organization-defined known system state] for the following failures on the indicated components while preserving [Assignment: organization-defined system state information] in failure.

Guidance:
Failure in a known state prevents loss of confidentiality, integrity, or availability of information during system/component failures, facilitating system restart with less disruption.

title: SESSION AUTHENTICITY
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for SC-23 (SESSION AUTHENTICITY), incorporating both the core requirements and specified enhancements:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   - Visible Evidence: Actual session tokens issued by the system appear to be uniquely generated (e.g., long, random strings) with no visible patterns. Upon logout, browser cookies or tokens are removed or invalidated.
   - Technical Artifacts: System and application logs showing session creation and termination events; configuration files that specify allowed certificate authorities for TLS sessions; automated scripts or reports that assess entropy of issued session identifiers.
   - Operational Patterns: Session identifiers are revoked or invalidated promptly upon user logout. Periodic audits (or automated routines) confirm that only system‐generated, random tokens are issued. An infrastructure review confirms that only pre-approved Certificate Authorities are trusted for session encryption.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   - Organizations may fake compliance by advertising session management policies in documentation without implementing proper technical controls. For example, reusing static or predictable session identifiers, or merely clearing session data on the client-side rather than invalidating session tokens server-side.
   - Surface-Level Implementations: Relying solely on front-end logout procedures while leaving back-end session identifiers active, or implementing token reuse checks only superficially without verifying randomness.
   - Technical Gaps: Not enforcing ingredient randomness in session identifiers, thereby allowing short or predictable tokens which adversaries can guess; failure to fully enforce allowed certificate authority restrictions when validating TLS connections.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   
   CHECK: Examine session token values for randomness and uniqueness.
   WHY: True compliance is evidenced by tokens that cannot be predicted or reused; system-generated randomness ensures the authenticity of sessions.
   HOW: Capture several session tokens from active sessions; use entropy calculation tools or randomness checks; review coding standards and token generation libraries for adherence to randomness requirements.
   FAIL: Discovery of predictable patterns, static tokens, or reuse of session identifiers indicates failed implementation.
   
   CHECK: Test session termination integrity by performing a logout and attempting token reuse.
   WHY: Invalidation upon logout ensures that even if an attacker intercepts a session token, it cannot be reused, which is critical for session authenticity.
   HOW: Log in to an application, note the session token, perform a logout, then attempt to access the system with the same token; verify via logs and response codes if the session remains active or is properly terminated.
   FAIL: If the session remains valid or token reuse is successful, the session invalidation mechanism is compromised.
   
   CHECK: Verify TLS configuration to ensure sessions are established using only allowed certificate authorities.
   WHY: Proper verification of TLS certificates maintains the integrity and authenticity of session communications, ensuring only trusted CAs are used.
   HOW: Check server configuration files and TLS handshake details (via tools like OpenSSL or browser certificate viewers) for allowed certificate authority lists as specified by the organization.
   FAIL: Presence of certificates from non-authorized CAs or misconfigured trust settings.
   
   CHECK: Audit system logs for events marking session creation and termination.
   WHY: Consistent logging of session events demonstrates that session authenticity controls (generation, invalidation, and termination) are actively monitored and enforced.
   HOW: Review log management and analysis systems for entries that detail session ID issuance, logout, or session expiration. Correlate a sample of entries with active sessions.
   FAIL: Inconsistent or missing log entries around session termination or creation indicate weaker operational controls.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   - Shortcut: Automate token analysis by integrating entropy testing in routine security scans. Look for tokens that do not change significantly between sessions.
   - Indicator: A red flag is the reuse of session tokens across multiple sessions or the existence of tokens that are too short (indicating low randomness). Always validate against the defined randomness criteria.
   - Detail: In real-world environments, verifying that session termination is enforced server-side (and not just client-side) is critical. Use network monitoring or proxy tools to confirm that logged-out tokens are rejected by the server immediately.
   - Tip: Cross-reference TLS handshake details with internal approved certificate authority lists. Mismatches here can be a significant trust failure, undermining session authenticity.

This methodology focuses on operational effectiveness with concrete, observable evidence while minimizing reliance on purely documentation-based claims. Regular validation, automated checks, and practical testing against session reuse and TLS misconfiguration are key to ensuring robust session authenticity.
content:
Statement:
Protect the authenticity of communications sessions.

Guidance:
Protecting session authenticity addresses communications protection at the session level, ensuring confidence at both ends of sessions regarding identity and validity of transmitted information.

Enhancements:
SESSION AUTHENTICITY | INVALIDATE SESSION IDENTIFIERS AT LOGOUT: Invalidate session identifiers upon user logout or other session termination. Discussion: Curtailed ability of adversaries to capture and reuse session IDs.
SESSION AUTHENTICITY | USER-INITIATED LOGOUTS AND MESSAGE DISPLAYS [Withdrawn: Incorporated into AC-12(1).]
SESSION AUTHENTICITY | UNIQUE SYSTEM-GENERATED SESSION IDENTIFIERS: Generate a unique session identifier for each session with [Assignment: organization-defined randomness requirements] and recognize only system-generated identifiers. Discussion: Protects against brute-force attacks.
SESSION AUTHENTICITY | UNIQUE SESSION IDENTIFIERS WITH RANDOMIZATION [Withdrawn: Incorporated into SC-23(a).]
SESSION AUTHENTICITY | ALLOWED CERTIFICATE AUTHORITIES: Only allow the use of [Assignment: organization-defined certificate authorities] for verifying protected sessions. Discussion: TLS certificates facilitate secure sessions.

title: ARCHITECTURE AND PROVISIONING FOR NAME/ADDRESS RESOLUTION SERVICE
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Visible evidence includes network diagrams showing multiple DNS server deployments with geographic diversity and clear delineation of roles (internal vs external). 
• Technical artifacts involve DNS configuration files, geolocation data for IP addresses, access control lists detailing DNS administrative roles, and system logs showing failover events and administrative changes.
• Operational patterns include routine failover testing, monitoring logs for DNS queries/responses under load, and regular reviews of role-based access permissions to the DNS management systems.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations may claim geographic distribution on paper but deploy servers in the same physical data center or region. 
• Documentation might show role separation while the actual administrative credentials and access rights are not technically segregated.
• Surface-level tests (e.g., ping tests) might not reveal if internal vs external roles are properly enforced or if failover mechanisms function in real-world conditions.

VALIDATION STEPS:
CHECK: Verify Geographic Separation of DNS Servers
WHY: Ensures fault tolerance in case of a regional outage, proving that the primary and secondary DNS services are deployed in distinct physical or cloud locations.
HOW: Access DNS server configuration details, use network tools (e.g., traceroute, WHOIS) to validate geographic diversity; confirm distinct IP ranges and hosting locations.
FAIL: Both DNS servers are found within the same data center or geographic region.

CHECK: Validate Role Separation and Access Controls
WHY: Ensures internal and external roles are separated, minimizing the risk of unauthorized changes and limiting exposure in case of a breach.
HOW: Review access control lists and DNS management logs; verify that administrative accounts are segregated and that changes by external roles are independently approved and monitored.
FAIL: A single administrative account or group has unchecked control over the DNS configuration without clear role-based separation.

CHECK: Test Fault Tolerance Through Controlled Failover
WHY: Operational testing of failover capability proves that the setup is genuinely resilient under disruption.
HOW: Perform a scheduled failover test by temporarily disabling the primary DNS server (with prior notice to operations) and observe if the secondary DNS seamlessly handles the queries; review logs for correct redirection.
FAIL: End users experience downtime or significant delays, or the logs indicate failure of the secondary DNS to properly resolve queries during the test.

CHECK: Cross-Verify Network Documentation Against Observable Data
WHY: Verifies that the documented architecture aligns with the real deployment, ensuring that the physical and logical configurations support the stated fault tolerance and role separation.
HOW: Compare network diagrams and documented deployment configurations with actual DNS server configurations, IP geolocation data, and firewall rules; use network scanning tools for cross-verification.
FAIL: Discrepancies are found between the published documentation and real-world configuration details (e.g., undocumented servers, misconfigured firewall rules, or overlapping administrative roles).

EXPERT TIPS:
• Quickly check the DNS SOA and NS records externally to ensure that servers are served from visibly different IP ranges and hosts.
• Focus on audit logs for administrative changes rather than relying solely on configuration files, as logs provide a chronological record of access and modifications.
• A critical indicator of poor implementation is if role separation is documented in policy but not enforced at the technical level, evidenced by a lack of strict access controls or overlapping administrative privileges.
• Always validate failover not just by availability but by performance metrics; look for latency or incomplete responses during failover scenarios.
content:
Statement:
Ensure the systems that collectively provide name/address resolution service for an organization are fault-tolerant and implement internal and external role separation.

Guidance:
Systems providing name and address resolution include DNS servers. Organizations deploy at least two geographically separated DNS servers (primary and secondary) and specify access roles.

title: SECURE NAME/ADDRESS RESOLUTION SERVICE (RECURSIVE OR CACHING RESOLVER)
methodology:
Below is the detailed validation methodology for SC-21 (Secure Name/Address Resolution Service):

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Evidence of properly configured DNS resolvers (recursive or caching) that support DNSSEC is visible in system configuration files (e.g., named.conf, resolv.conf with DNSSEC options enabled) and secured communication channels (DoT/DoH settings).
   • Technical artifacts include logs from DNS servers indicating DNSSEC validation events, output from DNS query tools (for example, use of dig +dnssec showing RRSIG records), and network configuration documentation showing use of encrypted DNS channels.
   • Operational patterns such as regular DNS queries that result in DNSSEC-validated responses, log events flagging DNSSEC signature checks and failures, and fallback/transitions to authenticated channels all indicate that the control is active.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations may falsely claim DNSSEC is enabled based solely on documentation without properly configuring the resolver or by having a DNS server that isn’t actually performing DNSSEC validation.
   • Some implementations will superficially enable DNSSEC flags without ensuring that key rollovers, trust anchor updates, or proper signature validation are operational, which leads to a false sense of compliance.
   • Technical gaps may include misconfigured or outdated trust anchors, lack of integration with upstream authoritative sources, or not deploying encrypted DNS (DoT/DoH) channels resulting in reliance on insecure transport.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify that the DNS resolver is configured to perform DNSSEC validation.
   WHY: This ensures that name/address resolution is authenticated and that responses include valid DNSSEC signatures, protecting against man-in-the-middle attacks and cache poisoning.
   HOW: Manually run DNS queries (e.g., using the command 'dig +dnssec example.com') and verify that the response includes RRSIG records; review configuration files for DNSSEC options (like 'dnssec-enable yes' in BIND) and check that trust anchors are correctly configured.
   FAIL: Absence of RRSIG records in the query response, or misconfigured/disabled DNSSEC settings in configuration files, indicates a failure of this control.

   CHECK: Confirm that DNS queries are transmitted over authenticated channels (e.g., DNS over TLS (DoT) or DNS over HTTPS (DoH)).
   WHY: Using secure channels ensures that the resolution requests and responses cannot be tampered with en route, which is critical for data integrity and origin authentication.
   HOW: Inspect client and network configurations for settings that enforce encrypted DNS resolution; check firewall and proxy logs to ensure use of appropriate protocols; use network monitoring tools to verify that DNS traffic is encrypted.
   FAIL: Evidence of plain-text DNS traffic being allowed or configured indicates that this control is not effectively implemented.

   CHECK: Check operational logs for entries that show DNSSEC validation events (both successes and failures).
   WHY: Real-time log data serves as an operational indicator that DNSSEC is actively enforced and that any anomalies are captured.
   HOW: Access DNS server logs or SIEM to review DNSSEC events; look for log entries that denote successful RRSIG validation or errors/fallback events during DNSSEC processing.
   FAIL: A lack of log entries related to DNSSEC events, or continuous error log entries without remediation, suggests poor operational enforcement of this control.

   CHECK: Validate that the resolver’s trust anchor configurations are up to date and manage key rollovers properly.
   WHY: Outdated or misconfigured trust anchors can lead to false negatives and weaken the security posture of DNS resolution.
   HOW: Review the current trust anchor list on the resolver and verify against trusted sources (such as IANA/ICANN published anchors); check for alerts or error messages regarding key rollovers.
   FAIL: Discrepancies between the configured trust anchors and the current trusted anchors, or evidence of unresolved key rollover events, indicate non-compliance.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Verification Shortcut: Use automated tools (like DNSSEC scanning utilities) that can continuously validate and report on DNSSEC status; these tools help correlate configuration with actual operational behavior.
   • Critical Indicator: A properly configured environment should not only display DNSSEC signed records but also actively log validation events. Absence of logging is a red flag.
   • Details that Matter: Focus on both the configuration and the operational logs. Even if documentation claims compliance, failing to see the DNS traffic encrypted or missing validation logs suggests that the control may be implemented only on paper.
   • Don’t Rely Solely on Documentation: Manual testing and real-time monitoring provide the best evidence that the DNS resolution service is secure and that data origin/authentication checks are effectively enforced.

This methodology emphasizes practical, observable evidence and steps that are difficult to fake, ensuring that the deployment of secure name/address resolution services meets the required compliance standards.
content:
Statement:
Request and perform data origin authentication and data integrity verification on the name/address resolution responses the system receives from authoritative sources.

Guidance:
Each client of name resolution services either performs this validation on its own or uses authenticated channels to trusted validation providers. Systems providing local name/address resolution include recursive resolving or caching DNS servers. DNS client resolvers either validate DNSSEC signatures or use authenticated channels.

title: SECURE NAME/ADDRESS RESOLUTION SERVICE (AUTHORITATIVE SOURCE)
methodology:
Below is the practical validation methodology for verifying the SC-20 control – Secure Name/Address Resolution Service (Authoritative Source).

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible evidence: Authoritative DNS responses include cryptographic artifacts. Look for DNS records such as DNSKEY, RRSIG, and DS records in responses from the DNS server. 
   • Technical artifacts: Presence of DNSSEC-related records (e.g., RRSIG records attached to resource record sets) and configured chain‐of‐trust linking child and parent zones.
   • Operational patterns: Regular monitoring of DNS responses for signature validity, active alerts when DNSSEC signatures are expired or invalid, and periodic automated chain-of-trust verifications.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations may simply enable DNSSEC on paper, but fail to correctly implement a complete chain-of-trust (e.g., misconfigured DS records or expired signatures).
   • Some may include the DNSSEC-related records in responses but not update keys regularly, leading to operational gaps over time.
   • Surface-level documentation or configuration settings without periodic operational testing can give a false sense of security.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   • CHECK: Query the authoritative server for a domain’s DNS records with DNSSEC enabled.
     WHY: Presence and validity of DNSKEY and RRSIG records confirm that origin authentication and integrity are in effect.
     HOW: Run a command like ‘dig +dnssec example.com’ and inspect the output for DNSKEY and RRSIG records, ensuring the response status confirms a valid DNSSEC chain. Use automated tools (e.g., DNSViz, Verisign DNSSEC debugger) to parse results.
     FAIL: Absence of RRSIG records, a ‘bogus’ status in the DNSSEC validation, or missing DNSKEY records indicate failure.

   • CHECK: Verify the chain-of-trust between a parent and child domain by comparing DS records from the parent zone with DNSKEY records from the child zone.
     WHY: A consistent chain-of-trust confirms that both parent and child zones are actively participating in secure resolution services.
     HOW: Use an online DNSSEC chain-of-trust verification tool or perform manual queries (e.g., ‘dig +dnssec childdomain.example.com’, then ‘dig childdomain.example.com ds’) to confirm that DS records are available and consistent with the child zone’s DNSKEY records.
     FAIL: Discrepancy between DS and DNSKEY records or the absence of DS records in the parent zone breaks the expected chain-of-trust.

   • CHECK: Review operational logs from the DNS servers for entries related to DNSSEC validation events, key rollovers, and signature expirations.
     WHY: Active monitoring and logging demonstrate that DNSSEC mechanisms are not only configured, but also maintained and operational.
     HOW: Access and filter DNS server logs on events like signature validation failures or key updates, and verify that alerts are generated for anomalous DNSSEC events.
     FAIL: A lack of relevant log entries, missing alerts, or persistent validation failures would indicate that the operational oversight is weak or ineffective.

   • CHECK (Optional – for child zones with secure resolution): Confirm that the DNS configuration for subordinate zones properly indicates the security status and supports DNSSEC.
     WHY: This ensures that child subspaces within a distributed, hierarchical namespace are delegated with the necessary trust information.
     HOW: Perform DNS queries against the child zones and validate that they return appropriate DNSSEC artifacts, then verify delegation using tools or manual DS record lookups.
     FAIL: Missing DNSSEC artifacts in child zone responses or unclear delegation records show that the secure resolution service isn’t fully implemented.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: Use automated DNSSEC validation tools to quickly ascertain the integrity of the DNSSEC chain – these tools often flag misconfigurations that could be missed by manual inspection.
   • Critical Indicator: Consistent and timely key rollovers with updated logs are a strong indicator of an actively maintained DNSSEC implementation. Stale keys or repeated validation errors provide a red flag.
   • Attention to Detail: Ensure that every level of the hierarchical namespace (root, parent, child zones) is being validated. Gaps in the chain-of-trust, especially at delegation points, are a common oversight.
   • Automation Advantage: Integrate periodic DNSSEC checks into your vulnerability and configuration management systems to catch misconfigurations before they lead to service degradation or security incidents.

This methodology emphasizes hands-on checks using observable, automated, and operationally relevant evidence, rather than relying solely on configuration documents.
content:
Statement:
a. Provide additional data origin authentication and integrity verification artifacts along with the authoritative name resolution data the system returns in response to external name/address resolution queries; and
b. Provide the means to indicate the security status of child zones and (if the child supports secure resolution services) to enable verification of a chain of trust among parent and child domains, when operating as part of a distributed, hierarchical namespace.

Guidance:
Providing authoritative source information enables external clients to obtain origin authentication and integrity verification assurances for the host/service name to network address resolution information. Systems include DNS servers and use DNSSEC digital signatures and cryptographic keys.

Enhancements:
SECURE NAME/ADDRESS RESOLUTION SERVICE (AUTHORITATIVE SOURCE) | CHILD SUBSPACES [Withdrawn: Incorporated into SC-20.]
SECURE NAME/ADDRESS RESOLUTION SERVICE (AUTHORITATIVE SOURCE) | DATA ORIGIN AND INTEGRITY: Provide data origin and integrity protection artifacts for internal name/address resolution queries. Discussion: None.

title: VOICE OVER INTERNET PROTOCOL
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Evidence that VoIP systems are treated as any other IP-based technology, with proper security controls (encryption in transit, authentication, and segmentation).
• Technical artifacts such as configuration files that show usage of secure protocols (e.g., TLS for signaling, SRTP for media) on VoIP components and network diagrams indicating segregation of VoIP traffic.
• Operational patterns including consistent use of call detail records (CDRs), regular monitoring of VoIP traffic for anomalies, and adherence to baseline secure configurations for endpoints.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations might simply point to the existence of VoIP systems without verifying that they have been configured securely (e.g., leaving SIP traffic unencrypted).
• Surface-level compliance may be faked if security reports or configuration snapshots are produced only for a subset of VoIP components, while insecure endpoints remain in operation.
• Firms may rely solely on documentation or policies rather than verifying that all deployed VoIP endpoints and network segments are hardened and actively monitored.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Verify Secure Signaling & Media Encryption
   WHY: Confirm that sensitive VoIP communications are protected from interception and manipulation.
   HOW: Review device and server configuration files (e.g., SIP server settings) to ensure that TLS (for signaling) and SRTP (for media) are enabled, and validate through packet captures that traffic is encrypted.
   FAIL: Identification of plain text SIP messages or media flows in network packet captures.

2. CHECK: Validate Endpoint Hardening & Secure Configuration
   WHY: Secure endpoints are the primary contact points in VoIP systems and must be hardened to reduce vulnerabilities.
   HOW: Inspect VoIP endpoint settings and hardening checklists, ensuring default credentials are changed and latest security updates are applied, and review endpoint logs for any deviations from the secure baseline.
   FAIL: Evidence of default configurations, outdated firmware, or misconfigurations that expose VoIP endpoints to threats.

3. CHECK: Assess Network Segmentation and Firewall Rules for VoIP Traffic
   WHY: Proper segmentation limits attack surface and prevents lateral movement from compromised VoIP systems.
   HOW: Examine network diagrams and firewall rule sets to verify that VoIP traffic is isolated in a dedicated VLAN or secure zone, with strict access controls between VoIP and non-VoIP networks.
   FAIL: VoIP traffic is found traversing unsegregated networks, access controls are too permissive, or segmentation is absent.

4. CHECK: Review Monitoring and Alerting for VoIP-related Events
   WHY: Continuous monitoring and rapid detection of abnormal behavior are key to operational security of VoIP systems.
   HOW: Validate that call detail records (CDRs) and system logs are being collected and regularly reviewed; check that anomaly detection systems or SIEM rules are in place and active for suspicious VoIP call patterns.
   FAIL: Missing or incomplete monitoring data, or absence of automated alerts tied to unusual VoIP activities.

EXPERT TIPS
• Focus on implementation details over policy artifacts. A secure VoIP deployment is demonstrated by active encrypted traffic and monitored, segmented networks—documentation alone is insufficient.
• When performing packet analysis, pay particular attention to SIP and RTP flows. Even a few unencrypted messages can indicate a misconfiguration.
• Validate a sample of endpoints across different segments to uncover overlooked insecure deployments.
• Use automated configuration compliance tools to regularly verify settings on VoIP components; manual checks are costlier and less reliable over time.
• Critical details such as encryption methods and segmentation boundaries make all the difference in real-world security—overlooking these can lead to exposures regardless of written policies.

This methodology ensures that the VoIP technologies are not only configured per best practices but are actively managed to mitigate risks associated with their deployment, even though the control has been withdrawn as a separate entity.
content:
Guidance:
Withdrawn: Technology-specific; addressed as any other technology or protocol.

title: MOBILE CODE
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Defined Lists and Policies: Look for documented lists of acceptable and unacceptable mobile code along with technical requirements for acquisition, development, and use. Check that mobile code policies include conditions for digital signing, confined execution, download prevention, and administrative alerts.
• System Configurations and Logs: Verify that mobile code is executed only in pre-configured, isolated environments (e.g., virtual machines or containers). Check system configuration files and runtime logs for entries detailing digital signature verifications, mobile code execution environments, and automated blocking/quarantine actions.
• Active Monitoring and Alerting: Evidence of continuous monitoring exists if log aggregation systems show alerts/quarantine events when unacceptable mobile code activity is detected, including prompt notifications to administrators.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Superficial Policy Documentation: Organizations may create policies on paper without actual enforcement within system configurations or active monitoring mechanisms.
• Incomplete Implementation of Digital Signature Verification: Simply requiring a signature without validating the trust chain can be faked if the underlying verification mechanisms or trusted certificate lists aren’t properly implemented.
• Unmonitored Confined Environments: Setting up virtualized or container environments for mobile code execution but neglecting to monitor or log execution events fails in practice.
• Loophole Allowances: Default settings might allow downloads or automatic execution for certain user groups, effectively bypassing mobile code restrictions.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Verification of Mobile Code Policy and Acceptable Code Lists
   WHY: A defined and approved list of acceptable/unacceptable mobile code underpins operational enforcement.
   HOW: Review official policy documents and system configuration files to check if specific mobile code types, digital signature requirements, and confined execution criteria are published and adhered to. Interview relevant stakeholders and examine control matrices.
   FAIL: Absence of documented lists or discrepancies between policies and system configurations indicate non-compliance.

2. CHECK: Digital Signature Verification of Mobile Code
   WHY: Ensuring that only mobile code with a trusted digital signature is executed proves control over code authenticity.
   HOW: Inspect execution logs and security software configurations to confirm that mobile code is checked for digital signatures against a trusted certificate authority. Test by submitting both signed and unsigned code samples on a test environment to observe if unsigned code is rejected or quarantined.
   FAIL: Execution of unsigned mobile code or absence of signature validation logic signals a control failure.

3. CHECK: Enforcement of Execution in Confined Environments
   WHY: Allowing mobile code only in isolated environments limits risk of compromise spreading to critical systems.
   HOW: Validate that mobile code runs exclusively inside designated virtual machines or containers. Identify process execution logs, configuration files, and runtime environment settings that enforce confinement. Use automated tools to simulate mobile code execution and capture contextual details of the running environment.
   FAIL: Evidence of mobile code executing outside a virtualized or confined environment indicates entire control breakdown.

4. CHECK: Automated Blocking/Alerting Mechanisms for Unacceptable Mobile Code
   WHY: Timely detection and prevention of unacceptable mobile code reduce risk; active alerts and quarantines show operational monitoring.
   HOW: Trigger tests with code samples defined as unacceptable (based on organizational criteria) and verify that the system alerts administrators, blocks downloads, or quarantines the code. Review centralized security logs and alert records for mobile code events and administrative notifications.
   FAIL: Absence of logging, failure to block downloads, or lack of notifications when unacceptable mobile code is tested revealed weak implementation.

5. CHECK: Regular Log Review and Incident Response Correlation
   WHY: Consistent monitoring and review ensure that mobile code incidents are detected and corrective actions enforced over time.
   HOW: Evaluate automated log review systems, SIEM configurations and incident response documentation to verify that mobile code-related events trigger proper follow-up actions. Look for regular sampling of alerts and verification of responsive actions within the documented response timeline.
   FAIL: Missing or outdated event monitoring records or failure to correlate mobile code events with incident response activities is a red flag.

EXPERT TIPS
• Quick Shortcut: Begin by searching for configuration files and logs that explicitly mention 'mobile code', 'digital signature', and 'confined execution' as these provide immediate insights into operational enforcement.
• Critical Details: Pay close attention to the consistency between policy language and technical enforcement. Weak linking between documented procedures and runtime controls is often an indicator of poor implementation.
• Testing in a Controlled Environment: Simulate code execution with both acceptable and unacceptable mobile code samples to verify if automated defenses activate appropriately – practical testing usually unearths hidden gaps.
• Observe Change Logs: Frequent modifications or patches related to mobile code management can indicate reactive rather than proactive updates, so review the history to gauge longevity and stability of the control implementation.
content:
Statement:
a. Define acceptable and unacceptable mobile code and mobile code technologies; and
b. Authorize, monitor, and control the use of mobile code within the system.

Guidance:
Mobile code includes any program, application, or content that can be transmitted across a network and executed on a remote system. It addresses actions to prevent unacceptable mobile code, including requiring mobile code to be digitally signed by a trusted source.

Enhancements:
MOBILE CODE | IDENTIFY UNACCEPTABLE CODE AND TAKE CORRECTIVE ACTIONS: Identify [Assignment: organization-defined unacceptable mobile code] and take [Assignment: organization-defined corrective actions]. Discussion: Corrective actions include blocking, quarantine, or alerting administrators.
MOBILE CODE | ACQUISITION, DEVELOPMENT, AND USE: Verify that the acquisition, development, and use of mobile code meets [Assignment: organization-defined mobile code requirements]. Discussion: None.
MOBILE CODE | PREVENT DOWNLOADING AND EXECUTION: Prevent the download and execution of [Assignment: organization-defined unacceptable mobile code]. Discussion: None.
MOBILE CODE | PREVENT AUTOMATIC EXECUTION: Prevent the automatic execution of mobile code in [Assignment: organization-defined software applications] and enforce [Assignment: organization-defined actions] prior to executing the code. Discussion: Includes prompting users before opening attachments or clicking links.
MOBILE CODE | ALLOW EXECUTION ONLY IN CONFINED ENVIRONMENTS: Allow execution of permitted mobile code only in confined virtual machine environments.

title: PUBLIC KEY INFRASTRUCTURE CERTIFICATES
methodology:
Below is an expert methodology for validating the implementation of PKI Certificates per SC-17.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: An up-to-date certificate inventory with details of issuers, issuance dates, and certificate policies. Public-facing and internal systems clearly documenting certificate chains, including trust anchor listings.
   • Technical Artifacts: System configuration files showing trust stores (e.g., in browsers, operating systems, application servers) that list only approved CAs, certificate logs from management systems, automated audit reports from vulnerability scanners that capture certificate details.
   • Operational Patterns: Regular certificate renewal and revocation activities; processes and automated scripts that periodically compare active trust anchors against an approved list; centralized PKI management that logs certificate issuance and validation.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Fake Compliance: Organizations may create a certificate inventory document or policy without actively enforcing certificate issuance policies. They might simply include the approved trust anchors in policy documents without actual enforcement in deployed trust stores.
   • Surface-Level Implementations: Configurations that import a wide range of CA roots by default (i.e., using browser defaults or OS defaults) without customization to include only approved anchors; self-signed certificates mistakenly used without proper oversight.
   • Technical Gaps: Lack of automated checks or centralized management that monitors changes in the trust store; failure to monitor certificate lifecycles leading to expired or non-compliant certificates still operational in the environment.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify Certificate Issuance and Inventory
   WHY: Ensures that public key certificates are generated or sourced strictly under the organizationally defined certificate policies from approved providers.
   HOW: - Review automated logs or reports from the certificate management system for issuer details.
         - Validate that the certificate chain includes only certificates issued by approved CAs as per the organizational certificate policy.
         - Manually sample certificate details (e.g., issuer, subject, validity period) from critical systems.
   FAIL: Certificates showing issuers not on the approved list or discrepancies between reported and actual certificate attributes.

   CHECK: Inspect Trust Store Configurations
   WHY: Critical to ensure only approved trust anchors are present, reducing the risk of rogue or compromised CAs being trusted.
   HOW: - Extract trust store entries from key systems (servers, desktops, network appliances) using automated scripts or manual configuration file reviews.
         - Compare the list of trust anchors against the organization-defined approved list.
         - Verify that updates to trust stores are logged and managed via formal change control.
   FAIL: Discovering trust stores with unapproved or extraneous CA entries, or evidence that trust store modifications occur without oversight.

   CHECK: Assess Certificate Lifecycle Management
   WHY: Continuous renewal, replacement, and revocation are indicators of an active and compliant PKI deployment.
   HOW: - Examine certificate management system logs to verify that certificates are renewed before expiration and revoked as necessary.
         - Check for the presence of automated alerts and remediation for expiring or compromised certificates.
         - Validate processes for issuing and revoking certificates against the documented certificate policy.
   FAIL: Evidence of expired or non-revoked certificates remaining active, or lifecycle processes that are only documented without operational evidence.

   CHECK: Validate Automated Monitoring & Alerting
   WHY: Automated monitoring provides ongoing assurance that only compliant certificates are in use and that deviations are quickly identified.
   HOW: - Review outputs from vulnerability scanners or automated certificate inventory tools that flag non-compliant certificates.
         - Confirm that alerts related to certificate anomalies (e.g., unapproved issuers or mismatches in the trust store) are generated and responded to in a timely manner.
   FAIL: Absence of automated monitoring outputs or lack of documented responses to certificate-related alerts.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Verification Shortcuts: Use automated tools (e.g., certificate inventory scanners or command-line utilities) to quickly extract certificate chain information from public-facing endpoints; leverage centralized PKI management dashboards.
   • Critical Indicators: Pay special attention to certificate issuer details and trust store contents. Any deviation from the approved list is a red flag. Ensure certificate lifecycle activities (renewal, revocation) appear regularly in logs.
   • Details Matter Most: Verify not only that policies exist but that system configurations actively enforce them. Look for evidence such as automated change logs, timestamped configuration snapshots, and real-time monitoring alerts. Avoid relying solely on policy documents; observe production evidence.

By following these practical steps, you can effectively validate that the public key infrastructure certificates environment complies with SC-17 requirements and is operationally effective.
content:
Statement:
a. Issue public key certificates under [Assignment: organization-defined certificate policy] or obtain public key certificates from an approved service provider; and
b. Include only approved trust anchors in trust stores or certificate stores managed by the organization.

Guidance:
Public key infrastructure (PKI) certificates are certificates with visibility external to organizational systems and related to internal operations such as application-specific time services. In cryptographic systems with a hierarchical structure, a trust anchor is an authoritative source for which trust is assumed.

title: TRANSMISSION OF SECURITY AND PRIVACY ATTRIBUTES
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Visible evidence includes system logs, configuration files, and network traffic captures that show security and privacy attributes being attached to data payloads exchanged between systems and components. 
• Technical artifacts include cryptographic binding signatures, metadata headers in API calls, and integrity verification schemes in application or middleware configurations.
• Operational patterns include regular verification logs, automated integrity checks, and alert generation when attribute integrity is compromised or spoofing is detected.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations might simply include static attribute values in documentation or configuration files without dynamically binding them to each transmission.
• Superficial implementations may rely solely on label insertion without cryptographic binding, making it trivial for an adversary to alter attributes without detection.
• Gaps often appear when anti-spoofing mechanisms are omitted or when integrity checks are not enforced across all transmission channels.

VALIDATION STEPS:
CHECK: Verify that transmitted messages include dynamic security and privacy attributes (e.g., as part of message headers or payload metadata).
WHY: This indicates that the organization is actively associating defined attributes with each transmission rather than statically including them.
HOW: Use network traffic analyzers (e.g., Wireshark) to capture and inspect actual data flows between systems; look for attribute fields that correlate with the defined organizational policy.
FAIL: No evidence of attribute fields or only static, non-dynamic attributes that are not cryptographically bound.

CHECK: Ensure that cryptographic binding is implemented, binding security attributes to the transmitted information.
WHY: Cryptographic binding makes it difficult for adversaries to modify attributes without detection, thus ensuring integrity.
HOW: Review system configurations and code that implement integrity verification, then test by tampering with a transmitted attribute in a controlled environment to see if the binding mechanism detects the anomaly.
FAIL: Transmissions where attributes can be modified independently or lack associated cryptographic proofs.

CHECK: Validate implementation of anti-spoofing mechanisms that protect against falsification of security attributes.
WHY: Anti-spoofing is critical to prevent unauthorized entities from injecting or replicating trusted attribute values.
HOW: Audit network devices and applications to verify that they check for valid cryptographic signatures or tokens, and perform penetration testing targeting spoofing of attribute fields.
FAIL: Devices or applications allowing modification of attribute data without triggering alerts or without verifying cryptographic tokens.

CHECK: Confirm that integrity verification mechanisms are used to protect the security and privacy attributes during transit.
WHY: This ensures that any tampering in transit is detected in real-time, safeguarding the policy enforcement mechanisms.
HOW: Review runtime logs for integrity check failures, and simulate transmission tampering in a test environment to confirm that the integrity verification process logs an error or rejects the tampered message.
FAIL: Lack of integrity check logs or a failure to reject messages with altered attributes.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Shortcut verification by focusing on system components that handle sensitive data exchanges; inspect their runtime logs and configuration settings for evidence of cryptographic binding and anti-spoofing.
• Prioritize checking integration points (APIs, middleware) where attribute binding is critical—these are the most common attack vectors if improperly implemented.
• Look for inconsistencies between documented attribute handling procedures and actual system behavior, which is a typical indicator that the control is not operating as intended.
• When possible, automate tests to simulate attribute tampering and verify that integrity mechanisms consistently flag discrepancies.

By following these steps, auditors can confirm not only that security and privacy attributes are present, but also that they are robustly safeguarded during transmission, fulfilling both organizational policy and technical rigor.
content:
Statement:
Associate [Assignment: organization-defined security and privacy attributes] with information exchanged between systems and between system components.

Guidance:
Security and privacy attributes can be explicitly or implicitly associated with the information contained in organizational systems or system components. Attributes are abstractions that represent the basic properties or characteristics of an entity with respect to protecting information or managing personally identifiable information. They are used to implement access control and information flow control policies; reflect special dissemination, management, or distribution instructions; or support other aspects of security and privacy policies.

Enhancements:
TRANSMISSION OF SECURITY AND PRIVACY ATTRIBUTES | INTEGRITY VERIFICATION: Verify the integrity of transmitted security and privacy attributes. Discussion: Ensures attributes have not been modified in an unauthorized manner.
TRANSMISSION OF SECURITY AND PRIVACY ATTRIBUTES | ANTI-SPOOFING MECHANISMS: Implement anti-spoofing mechanisms to prevent adversaries from falsifying the security attributes. Discussion: Prevents the alteration of attributes leading organizations to believe an insufficient level of security is in place.
TRANSMISSION OF SECURITY AND PRIVACY ATTRIBUTES | CRYPTOGRAPHIC BINDING: Implement [Assignment: organization-defined mechanisms or techniques] to bind security and privacy attributes to transmitted information. Discussion: Cryptographic mechanisms provide strong binding to transmitted information.

title: COLLABORATIVE COMPUTING DEVICES AND APPLICATIONS
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Visible configuration settings or system logs proving that remote activation is disabled on collaborative computing devices by default, with documented exceptions.
• Screens or status indicators on devices (projectors, cameras, white boards) that signal active operation when in use by nearby individuals.
• Evidence that devices, in secure environments or SCIFs, have physical or logical disconnect options enabled and observable.
• In online meeting platforms, a clear display of current participant lists to alert users.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations might simply update policy documents without configuring system settings, claiming remote activation prohibition without technical enforcement.
• Fake configuration settings in management consoles that are not actually applied or monitored in real-time.
• Overreliance on user notifications that are easily bypassed or disabled, thus missing an explicit physical indicator of active use.
• Inconsistent implementation of disconnect mechanisms where the control exists only on paper, not in operational environments.

VALIDATION STEPS:
CHECK: Verify that remote activation commands for collaborative computing devices (e.g., cameras, mics, white boards) are blocked unless exceptions are explicitly defined.
WHY: This automated configuration check confirms that the device cannot be remotely activated contrary to policy, minimizing unauthorized access.
HOW: Review system logs, device configurations, and network access control lists; test remote activation commands from an authorized endpoint to confirm they are blocked or trigger an alert. 
FAIL: Successful remote activation without detecting exceptions, or inconsistent enforcement across similar devices.

CHECK: Confirm that devices display an explicit indication of use when active.
WHY: Provides real-time physical feedback to on-site users, ensuring that activation is visible and not surreptitious.
HOW: Observe devices during a controlled activation process; check for LED indicators, on-screen messages, or audible cues that confirm active status.
FAIL: Absence of any physical or logical indication of device activation during operation.

CHECK: Validate that physical or logical disconnect mechanisms are available and user-friendly on collaborative devices.
WHY: Ensures that participants can easily disable collaboration features to prevent unauthorized follow-on access or eavesdropping.
HOW: Execute user tests where participants intentionally disconnect the device; review user interface design and documented procedures for disconnect, verifying minimal steps are required.
FAIL: Discovery of overly complex procedures or absence of disconnect options, or if the disconnect function is either disabled or hidden.

CHECK: For online meetings and teleconferencing apps, confirm that explicit indication of current participants is available and clearly visible.
WHY: Prevents unauthorized user participation by ensuring that all active participants are clearly displayed.
HOW: Join an active session and observe the participant list; verify that the list updates in real time when outbound or inbound communications occur.
FAIL: Participant presence information is hidden, outdated, or only available via non-intuitive navigation paths.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Use automated tools to scan device configurations and network logs to detect remote activation commands, rather than relying solely on manual checks.
• A common indicator of poor implementation is when the explicit user activation indicator can be easily disabled or ignored by end users.
• Ensure that a baseline configuration is enforced across all collaborative devices; manual exceptions are a red flag if not centrally managed.
• During testing, simulate both normal users and potential adversaries to validate that the control does not merely exist in documentation. In practice, even a slight misconfiguration that allows remote activation without proper indication is a critical vulnerability.
• Always correlate physical inspection with technical logs to ensure the operational controls are not just theoretical.
content:
Statement:
a. Prohibit remote activation of collaborative computing devices and applications with the following exceptions: [Assignment: organization-defined exceptions where remote activation is to be allowed]; and
b. Provide an explicit indication of use to users physically present at the devices.

Guidance:
Collaborative computing devices and applications include remote meeting devices and applications, networked white boards, cameras, and microphones. The explicit indication of use includes signals to users when collaborative computing devices and applications are activated.

Enhancements:
COLLABORATIVE COMPUTING DEVICES | PHYSICAL OR LOGICAL DISCONNECT: Provide [Selection (one or more): physical; logical] disconnect of collaborative computing devices in a manner that supports ease of use. Discussion: Failing to disconnect from collaborative computing devices can result in subsequent compromises of organizational information. Providing easy methods to disconnect ensures that participants carry out the disconnect activity without complex procedures.
COLLABORATIVE COMPUTING DEVICES | BLOCKING INBOUND AND OUTBOUND COMMUNICATIONS TRAFFIC: [Withdrawn: Incorporated into SC-7.]
COLLABORATIVE COMPUTING DEVICES | DISABLING AND REMOVAL IN SECURE WORK AREAS: Disable or remove collaborative computing devices and applications from [Assignment: organization-defined systems or system components] in [Assignment: organization-defined secure work areas]. Discussion: Failing to disable or remove them can result in compromises including eavesdropping. A Sensitive Compartmented Information Facility (SCIF) is an example of a secure work area.
COLLABORATIVE COMPUTING DEVICES | EXPLICITLY INDICATE CURRENT PARTICIPANTS: Provide an explicit indication of current participants in [Assignment: organization-defined online meetings and teleconferences]. Discussion: This prevents unauthorized individuals from participating without explicit knowledge.

title: PUBLIC ACCESS PROTECTIONS
methodology:
CONTROL: SC-14 PUBLIC ACCESS PROTECTIONS

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Public-facing systems deployed in segregated network segments (e.g., DMZs) with clearly defined firewall rules and access control lists (ACLs). Screenshots or live views of network diagrams and active firewall configurations can serve as evidence. 
• Technical Artifacts: Firewall and router configuration files, access control logs, IDS/IPS reports, and system audit logs that clearly capture traffic to and from public access endpoints. 
• Operational Patterns: Recurrent patch management, penetration testing results, and periodic vulnerability assessments that specifically target public access services, demonstrating an ongoing operational effort to protect publicly accessible systems.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Superficial Compliance: Organizations may simply produce documentation or outdated network diagrams that depict a DMZ or segmented architecture without actual enforcement at the technical level. 
• Ineffective Implementations: Using default firewall configurations or unmonitored ACLs that exist only on paper without active monitoring and enforcement. 
• Missed Technical Updates: Failing to integrate real-time log correlation or intrusion detection for public endpoints, thereby leaving blind spots that can be easily exploited.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Validate DMZ and network segmentation for public access systems
WHY: Ensures that publicly accessible systems are isolated from sensitive internal resources, reducing lateral movement risks in the event of a breach.
HOW: Review firewall and router configuration files for rules that explicitly segregate DMZ zones; cross-check with up-to-date network diagrams and perform a network scan to verify that services are not accessible from non-designated network segments.
FAIL: Public systems are directly accessible from internal or overly broad network zones without proper segmentation.

CHECK: Verify that access control lists (ACLs) and firewall rules are actively filtering traffic to public endpoints
WHY: Prevents unauthorized access and minimizes exposure to potential external threats.
HOW: Use automated scanning tools (e.g., port scanners) to validate that only allowed ports and services are reachable from outside; review real-time firewall logs and change management records to confirm that rules are maintained and updated.
FAIL: Discovery of open or unnecessary ports and services that do not match approved ACL entries; misconfigured or static rules not aligned with current security policies.

CHECK: Confirm activation and proper configuration of IDS/IPS focused on public access services
WHY: Helps detect and alert on potential intrusion attempts targeting public endpoints in real-time.
HOW: Review IDS/IPS configuration settings, correlation logs, and incident response reports for events related to public access probes; if available, simulate benign intrusion attempts to ensure alerts are triggered and properly logged.
FAIL: Absence of IDS/IPS data for traffic to public systems or evidence that alerts are ignored, indicating either misconfiguration or disabled monitoring.

CHECK: Assess patch management and vulnerability assessment practices for public server exposures
WHY: Ensures that systems exposed to public networks are regularly updated and vulnerabilities are remediated, minimizing the risk of exploitation.
HOW: Examine automated vulnerability scan reports, patch management logs, and remediation timelines for public-facing servers; verify that critical vulnerabilities are addressed within organizational SLAs.
FAIL: Outdated systems, repeated vulnerabilities across scans, or evidence of deferred critical patches for public access systems.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Leverage automated scanning and log aggregation tools to obtain live evidence of network segmentation and active IDS/IPS monitoring, reducing reliance on potentially outdated documentation. 
• Critical Indicators: Pay close attention to any discrepancies between documented network segmentation and live network scans; inconsistent firewall rules or unmonitored access logs are early signs of poor enforcement. 
• Practical Detail: Verify that remediation actions for public systems are not only documented but also reflected in active configurations and regular vulnerability assessments; the absence of timely updates often signals superficial compliance rather than robust protection.
• Remember: Public access protections are dynamic; periodic re-validation (especially following network changes) is essential to ensure continued operational effectiveness.
content:
Guidance:
[Withdrawn: Incorporated into AC-2, AC-3, AC-5, AC-6, SI-3, SI-4, SI-5, SI-7, and SI-10.]

title: CRYPTOGRAPHIC PROTECTION
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for SC-13: CRYPTOGRAPHIC PROTECTION.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
