HOW: Examine maintenance logs, audit results, and periodic testing documentation for boundary protection devices. Confirm that reviews occur on a scheduled basis and include verification of connection integrity.
FAIL: Missing, outdated, or infrequent review records raise concerns that the control could be neglected in practice.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Verification Shortcut: Directly execute a controlled connectivity test from a classified system to an external IP using network scanning tools. A blocked attempt indicates an effective boundary protection device in place.
• Critical Indicator: Look for instances of misconfigured or default firewall rules; they are a common sign that the control might be set up on paper but ineffective in practice.
• Detail Focus: Ensure that both physical and logical network segregation are consistent across documentation and actual configurations. A disparity between the two is a frequent sign of technical debt or oversight.
• Best Practice: Validate that logs are not only generated but are actively monitored and reviewed by a dedicated security team, confirming real-time enforcement.

This methodology prioritizes automated, observable evidence and hands-on testing to confirm that classified national security systems are properly shielded from direct connections to external networks.
content:
Statement:
Prohibit the direct connection of a classified national security system to an external network without the use of [Assignment: organization-defined boundary protection device].

Guidance:
A direct connection is a dedicated physical or virtual connection between two or more systems. Organizations typically do not have complete control over external networks, including the Internet. Boundary protection devices (e.g., firewalls, gateways, and routers) mediate communications and information flows between classified national security systems and external networks.

title: UNCLASSIFIED NATIONAL SECURITY SYSTEM CONNECTIONS
methodology:
Control ID: SC-7(25) – UNCLASSIFIED NATIONAL SECURITY SYSTEM CONNECTIONS

This methodology validates that unclassified national security systems do not directly connect to an external network, and that all such connections are mediated by an approved, organization-defined boundary protection device.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Network architecture and topology diagrams explicitly showing the unclassified national security systems connected exclusively via firewalls, gateways, or routers. Physical or virtual schematics that highlight boundary devices isolated between internal systems and external networks.
• Technical Artifacts: Configurations and change logs from boundary protection devices, firewall rule sets, router ACLs, and network segmentation policies. Network flow logs (e.g., syslog, SNMP alerts) that capture mediation of traffic between systems.
• Operational Patterns: Continuous logging and monitoring of traffic flows that enforce clear demarcation, with no observed bypasses; periodic audits and penetration tests that confirm no direct connections exist.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Faked Compliance: Organizations might create network diagrams that appear compliant while actual configurations on devices (e.g., misconfigured firewalls or improperly segmented routers) allow direct connectivity.
• Surface-Level Implementations: Simply ‘labeling’ a device as a boundary protection device in documentation or relying solely on policy texts without verifying active monitoring and proper rule enforcement.
• Frequent Gaps: Overlooked virtual bypasses such as unsecured VPN tunnels or improperly configured redundant paths that provide direct connectivity even though a boundary device is nominally present.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Review and verify network architecture diagrams.
WHY: Diagrams should clearly depict that unclassified national security systems are isolated from direct external connectivity by showing boundary devices in-line.
HOW: Request the latest approved network diagrams and physically inspect or interview network admins; trace network paths from unclassified systems to external networks.
FAIL: If diagrams reveal direct, unmediated connections or omit boundary protection devices between the systems and external networks.

CHECK: Examine boundary protection device configurations (e.g., firewall, router ACLs, gateway settings).
WHY: Proper configurations are critical to ensure that all external traffic is filtered or mediated appropriately.
HOW: Collect configuration backups and change logs; review the enforcement rules for traffic entering/exiting the unclassified national security system subnet; cross-check against organizational standards.
FAIL: If configurations are overly permissive, missing key filtering rules, or if rule sets are static and not aligned with actual network segments.

CHECK: Conduct network scanning and traffic analysis from both internal and external perspectives.
WHY: Automated scans can reveal unauthorized open ports or direct connectivity routes that bypass boundary devices.
HOW: Use trusted scanning tools (e.g., NMAP) to simulate both external and internal access attempts; verify that attempts to reach unclassified national security systems must pass through the boundary device.
FAIL: If scanning identifies open paths directly connecting external networks to unclassified systems, bypassing the boundary protection device.

CHECK: Analyze logs and real-time monitoring data from boundary protection devices.
WHY: Consistent log entries, alerts, and flow data confirm that traffic is being mediated and that no unauthorized direct connections occur.
HOW: Review syslog, SNMP alerts, or security information and event management (SIEM) data for evidence of enforced traffic policies and any incidents of direct connectivity attempts.
FAIL: If logs indicate any inconsistencies, unexplained bypass routes, or if significant periods exist where monitoring was inactive or misconfigured.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: For quick verification, corroborate network diagrams with live device configuration and traffic logs – disparate data sources reduce the chance of faked compliance.
• Critical Indicator: The integrity and real-time nature of log data is paramount; stale or incomplete logs often signal poor operational control.
• Detail Focus: Pay special attention to any redundancies in network routing (e.g., VPN tunnels, alternate paths) that might inadvertently allow direct connectivity despite a boundary device being in place.
• Cross-Validation: Interview network/system administrators alongside reviewing technical artifacts; anomalies in recollection or contradicting evidence across sources are a red flag.
• Automated Support: Use continuous automated monitoring tools integrated with SIEM to proactively detect deviations from the established boundaries.

By following these steps, auditors can verify that the organization's unclassified national security systems are consistently protected from direct external connections, ensuring that all traffic is appropriately mediated through organizationally defined boundary protection devices.
content:
Statement:
Prohibit the direct connection of [Assignment: organization-defined unclassified national security system] to an external network without the use of [Assignment: organization-defined boundary protection device].

Guidance:
A direct connection is a dedicated physical or virtual connection between two or more systems. Organizations typically do not have complete control over external networks, including the Internet. Boundary protection devices (e.g., firewalls, gateways, and routers) mediate communications and information flows between unclassified national security systems and external networks.

title: PERSONALLY IDENTIFIABLE INFORMATION
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
   • Evidence of defined processing rules: Look for configuration files, system documentation, or code modules that explicitly apply organization-defined processing rules to PII. These rules might be visible in data transformation scripts or API middleware.
   • Active monitoring at interfaces: Inspect network logs, intrusion detection system (IDS) alerts, and SIEM dashboards that show real-time monitoring of external interfaces and key internal boundaries for PII processing activities.
   • Exception documentation: Verify that there is a maintained, time-stamped log or database of documented processing exceptions. This log should detail reasons for exceptions and have change history that indicates periodic review and cleanup.
   • Operational patterns: Regular review meetings, automated exception clearance processes, and audit trail analyses that evidence systematic monitoring and prompt removal of unsupported exceptions.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
   • Superficial documentation: Organizations may have a policy that defines processing rules without implementing or integrating them into the technical environment.
   • Bulk logging without enforcement: Systems might log exceptions to processing rules arbitrarily without ensuring they reflect actual processing activities.
   • Outdated review processes: Exception logs may be maintained but rarely reviewed or updated, meaning that removed or outdated exceptions continue to exist.
   • Interface monitoring gaps: Monitoring might only be performed at the perimeter without actually tracking internal data flows where processing deviations occur.

3. VALIDATION STEPS:
   CHECK: Validate that organization-defined processing rules for PII exist and are actively applied in system components.
   WHY: This confirms that processing of PII is governed by explicit rules rather than ad hoc practices.
   HOW: Review configuration files, source code, or middleware settings that implement these rules; verify that they are integrated with data processing workflows.
   FAIL: No trace of PII processing checks in system configurations or rule enforcement in source code.

   CHECK: Assess evidence of monitoring at external interfaces and key internal boundaries.
   WHY: Continuous monitoring ensures that deviations from permitted PII processing are quickly detected.
   HOW: Examine SIEM dashboards, audit logs, and IDS/IPS configurations to ensure they cover both external access points and internal data movement.
   FAIL: Monitoring artifacts are incomplete, not covering either external or internal points, or are outdated.

   CHECK: Inspect the documented processing exceptions log.
   WHY: Documenting exceptions with a clear rationale ensures that non-standard PII processing events are tracked and managed.
   HOW: Request access to the exception logs, verify that each entry includes a timestamp, reason for exception, and planned review date; confirm that there’s historical evidence of periodic reviews and removals.
   FAIL: Exceptions are undocumented, lack review history, or documentation appears to be created solely to satisfy compliance without operational follow-up.

   CHECK: Verify the review process for processing exceptions is operational and periodically executed.
   WHY: Regular reviews guarantee that unsupported or obsolete exceptions are identified and removed, maintaining control over PII processing.
   HOW: Interview operational teams, review meeting minutes, and look for automated review scripts or tools; confirm that past review cycles have led to remedial actions.
   FAIL: There is no regular review process in place, or evidence shows that exceptions have never been reassessed since their documentation.

4. EXPERT TIPS:
   • Shortcut: Instead of solely reviewing documentation, directly query the logging systems and SIEM; automated queries can quickly reveal if an interface is being monitored as required.
   • Critical indicator: Check for integration between the exception management system and change control workflows. A lack of proactive exception removal is a red flag.
   • Important detail: Ensure that system code or configurations are checked for enforcement of rules—if the rule logic isn’t in the runtime environment, then merely having documented policies won’t suffice.
   • Real-world focus: Pay extra attention to the timeliness and relevance of exception documentation. If reviews haven’t been carried out routinely or if changes in application logic aren’t updated in exception logs, this control is effectively weakened.
   • Validate cross-boundary monitoring: Ensure that logging covers both edge (external interface) and core (internal boundaries) to catch any bypass attempts where PII might flow unmonitored.
content:
Statement:
For systems that process personally identifiable information: (a) Apply the following processing rules to data elements of personally identifiable information: [Assignment: organization-defined processing rules]; (b) Monitor for permitted processing at the external interfaces to the system and at key internal boundaries within the system; (c) Document each processing exception; and (d) Review and remove exceptions that are no longer supported.

Guidance:
Managing the processing of personally identifiable information is an important aspect of protecting an individual’s privacy. Applying, monitoring for, and documenting exceptions to processing rules ensure that personally identifiable information is processed only in accordance with established privacy requirements.

title: DISABLE SENDER FEEDBACK ON PROTOCOL VALIDATION FAILURE
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Evidence of implemented measures includes configuration files or management consoles where sender feedback on protocol validation errors is explicitly disabled.
• Technical artifacts such as audit logs or error handling logs that only record internal error details without relaying them to the sender demonstrate compliance.
• Operational patterns include tests where malformed requests are issued and the recipient system does not send out detailed error messages to the requester.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may simply change error message text without truly disabling the feedback mechanism, meaning error details could still be inadvertently exposed.
• A surface-level implementation might involve conditional suppression for some errors but not across all protocols or endpoints.
• Technical gaps often include legacy systems, third-party integrations, or incorrectly scoped server configurations that do not uniformly disable sender feedback.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Review configuration settings for major protocol handlers (e.g., SMTP servers, web servers, API gateways) to confirm that error responses do not disclose protocol validation details.
   WHY: Proper configuration ensures critical error details are not leaked to potential adversaries, limiting information disclosure.
   HOW: Access server configuration files or management interfaces and verify that error message settings are set to provide minimal or generic messages.
   FAIL: Discovery of settings that provide detailed error messages or references to documentation visible to external requesters.

2. CHECK: Conduct active testing by sending intentionally malformed requests to trigger protocol validation failures.
   WHY: Real operational behavior confirms that the system does not provide detailed error feedback that could be exploited.
   HOW: Use automated testing tools to generate malformed requests (for example, a deliberately corrupted SMTP or API request) and monitor both the sender’s response and internal logs to assess what is returned.
   FAIL: If the sender receives detailed feedback on the nature of the error.

3. CHECK: Examine audit logs or network traffic captures to ensure error messages remain internal and no excessive information is sent out in responses.
   WHY: Observable logging and network traces provide solid evidence of controls in action and help reveal any bypass paths.
   HOW: Utilize a SIEM or network monitoring solution to capture error handling interactions and verify that responses are generic or fully suppressed.
   FAIL: Log entries or network captures that show error details are echoed back to the sender.

EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Focus on high-risk endpoints where protocol errors are more likely to lead to actionable feedback. Often, misconfigurations exist on legacy systems or edge services.
• Critical indicator: Pay attention to support for custom error handling – ensuring that generic error messages are used consistently across all protocols prevents inadvertent leaks.
• Note: Even if documentation states that feedback is disabled, always verify with real-world tests as automated tools are effective at uncovering any discrepancies between intended configuration and operational reality.
content:
Statement:
Disable feedback to senders on protocol format validation failure.

Guidance:
Disabling feedback to senders when there is a failure in protocol validation format prevents adversaries from obtaining information that would otherwise be unavailable.

title: SEPARATE SUBNETS FOR CONNECTING TO DIFFERENT SECURITY DOMAINS
methodology:
Control SC-7(22) – SEPARATE SUBNETS FOR CONNECTING TO DIFFERENT SECURITY DOMAINS

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence: Network diagrams showing distinct subnets dedicated to different security domains; documented IP segmentation schemas. 
• Technical artifacts: Configurations from routing devices (routers, Layer 3 switches), VLAN configurations, access control lists (ACLs), and firewall rules that enforce subnet separation. 
• Operational patterns: Logged traffic flows, routing logs, and anomaly detection alerts that confirm traffic isolation between subnets corresponding to different security levels.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Superficial implementations: Organizations might simply label ports or segments in documentation without actual enforced network separation. 
• Faked compliance: Relying solely on policy documentation or outdated network diagrams rather than current, active configurations. 
• Frequently missed technical gaps: Misconfigured routing rules or firewall exceptions that inadvertently allow cross-domain traffic between subnets, which can be overlooked if routine checks focus only on documentation.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Verify Network Architecture Diagrams
WHY: Up-to-date diagrams showing clearly delineated subnets for separate security domains prove that network segmentation is planned and maintained.
HOW: Interview network admins and review the current, version-controlled network diagrams; compare against live configuration exports from network devices.
FAIL: Diagrams are outdated or vague, with no clear separation of different security domains.

CHECK: Inspect Router and Firewall Rules
WHY: Properly configured ACLs, routing policies, and firewall rules physically enforce the separate subnet structure.
HOW: Extract and review configuration files from routers, firewalls, and Layer 3 switches; check for explicit denies or routing policies that prevent unauthorized cross-domain communication.
FAIL: Configuration files show “catch-all” rules that allow unrestricted communication or lack explicit segmentation controls.

CHECK: Perform Network Scans and Traffic Analysis
WHY: Active scanning will reveal whether the subnets are truly isolated by identifying any unexpected open ports or routes between subnets designated for different security domains.
HOW: Use tools to perform network mapping and traffic flow tests between subnets; monitor logs and network traffic to verify no cross-boundary leakage exists.
FAIL: Scanning results indicate unexpected connectivity between subnets, or traffic analysis reveals frequent unauthorized cross-domain communication.

CHECK: Verify Change Management and Audit Logs
WHY: Consistent documentation of changes to network segmentation indicates an operational commitment to maintaining separate security domains.
HOW: Audit change management systems and logs for configuration updates around subnet segmentation, ensuring that modifications are tracked, approved, and tested.
FAIL: Lack of recent change logs or uncontrolled modifications that bypass established segmentation policies.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Directly pull live configuration from network devices using automation scripts; compare against documented subnet segmentation. 
• Critical indicators: Look for inconsistencies between the approved network segmentation design and actual implemented routing or ACL rules. 
• Practical detail: Ensure that periodic rescans and automated network audits are in place, as static documentation can be quickly outdated, and real-time monitoring is key to verifying operational effectiveness.

Overall, focus on verifying that technical configurations and real-time traffic behaviors confirm effective logical separation of networks to enforce security boundaries as intended.
content:
Statement:
Implement separate network addresses to connect to systems in different security domains.

Guidance:
The decomposition of systems into subnetworks (i.e., subnets) helps to provide the appropriate level of protection for network connections to different security domains that contain information with different security categories or classification levels.

title: ISOLATION OF SYSTEM COMPONENTS
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• CHECK: Determine if network segments or firewalled zones exist between different mission-critical components.
  WHY: Visible network segmentation, evidenced by defined subnets and firewall rules, directly shows that isolation of components is enforced.
  HOW: Inspect network diagrams, firewall rule sets, and router configurations that list separate VLANs or subnets for distinct functions. Validate that cross-zone logs indicate controlled traffic flows.
  FAIL: Absence of defined network boundaries, flat network architecture, or missing firewall configuration details indicates a failed isolation control.

• CHECK: Review the deployment of cross-domain solutions (e.g., proxies or gateways) specifically managing the data flow between separate domains.
  WHY: Deployments of cross-domain systems prove that boundaries are not only conceptual but enforced with real technical controls limiting data leakage.
  HOW: Assess system configurations of cross-domain security devices; verify their logging mechanisms, traffic filtering rules, and secure management practices.
  FAIL: If cross-domain solutions are not clearly configured, or if traffic monitoring is disabled/missing, the isolation is not effectively enforced.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations might simply apply generic firewall rules without tailoring them to distinct business functions, creating gaps in truly isolating sensitive components.
• Relying on documentation or self-attestation without demonstrable network logs or real-time monitoring evidence can mask non-implementation.
• Superficial segmentation implemented on paper (e.g., in diagrams) that is not fully enforced at the network level can lead to bypasses via misconfigured devices.

VALIDATION STEPS:
1. CHECK: Verify active firewall and router configuration settings that enforce distinct subnet segmentation for each mission or business function.
   WHY: Automated configuration exports and live network traffic tests provide solid evidence that these controls are not merely documented.
   HOW: Obtain configuration backup files or access the network controller to list rules, VLAN assignments, and routing policies; cross-check these assumptions with live traffic analysis.
   FAIL: If configurations are generic or rules allow unauthorized cross-traffic, the check fails.

2. CHECK: Examine logs from cross-domain devices to ensure that communication flows are restricted and monitored as designed.
   WHY: Consistent logging and real-time alerts indicate that boundary protection mechanisms are actively monitoring isolated components.
   HOW: Review system logs, SIEM alerts, and audit reports showing allowed and blocked cross-domain interactions; perform test transmissions and check for expected block/allow responses.
   FAIL: Lack of logs, errors in logs, or configuration misalignment with policy criteria suggest non-compliance.

3. CHECK: Perform a network scan and simulated attack to test if isolation boundaries can be bypassed or if ports remain closed as documented.
   WHY: Penetration testing and network scanning reveal the operational integrity of isolation controls beyond what documentation states.
   HOW: Use automated vulnerability scanners and port scanners targeted at the demilitarized zones (DMZ) and cross-domain interfaces; document any open ports or unauthorized services.
   FAIL: Discovery of unintended open paths or unauthorized connectivity indicates failure in boundary enforcement.

4. CHECK: Validate that network segmentation is consistently applied and monitored over time by comparing snapshots of network configurations and change logs.
   WHY: Consistency over time demonstrates that the organization is maintaining consistent operational controls rather than relying on periodic or patchy implementations.
   HOW: Analyze historical configuration change logs, network monitoring system (NMS) reports, and compare against documented segmentation criteria.
   FAIL: Inconsistencies or frequent, untracked changes in network boundaries indicate a lapse in maintaining isolation.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Shortcuts: Directly logging into firewalls and using automated tools to pull configuration snapshots can quickly verify segmentation without relying solely on manual documentation.
• Critical Indicators: Look for default settings or broad access rules that often signal lazy configuration approaches; detailed traffic logs showing blocked attempts are key listing indicators of proper isolation.
• Important Details: Focus on whether segmentation covers all relevant high-risk components; misconfigurations in niche systems (e.g., management ports) can be a red flag even if main network segments appear compliant.
• Practical Check: Correlate network scanning results with firewall logs to ensure there are no undocumented pathways or exceptions.
content:
Statement:
Employ boundary protection mechanisms to isolate [Assignment: organization-defined system components] supporting [Assignment: organization-defined missions and/or business functions].

Guidance:
Organizations can isolate system components that perform different mission or business functions. Such isolation limits unauthorized information flows among system components and provides the opportunity to deploy greater levels of protection for selected system components. Isolating system components provides enhanced protection that limits the potential harm from hostile cyber-attacks and errors. The degree of isolation varies depending upon the mechanisms chosen. Boundary protection mechanisms include routers, gateways, and firewalls that separate system components into physically separate networks or subnetworks; cross-domain devices.

title: DYNAMIC ISOLATION AND SEGREGATION
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Evidence of dynamic isolation is visible in real-time event logs from automated network segmentation devices—such as firewalls, software-defined networking (SDN) controllers, or intrusion detection systems (IDS)—that indicate triggered isolation events in response to security incidents.
• Technical artifacts include configuration files and automation scripts that govern dynamic isolation, along with audit trails of network isolation actions. Network monitoring tools (e.g., packet captures) showing disrupted traffic flows to/from isolated components also serve as proof.
• Operational patterns include recurring, time-correlated isolation events in automated logs, and documented integration between threat detection systems and dynamic segmentation controls.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations sometimes claim dynamic isolation compliance by maintaining static segmentation rules that are manually tweaked, rather than truly dynamic, automated responses.
• Fake compliance may be shown through superficial logging where timestamps and events are simulated or reported after the fact, rather than directly generated by the isolation mechanisms.
• Configurations that simply echo policy documents without active monitoring or without integration with actual threat feeds can indicate a technical gap between policy and real-time function.

VALIDATION STEPS:
1. CHECK: Verify automated network segmentation logs for isolation events.
   WHY: Authentic, timely log entries demonstrate that isolation is triggered in response to specific security events.
   HOW: Review firewall/IDS/SDN controller logs. Correlate timestamps of dynamic isolation events with incident responses or threat detections to ensure the automation is active.
   FAIL: Absence of automated log entries or reliance solely on manual intervention logs suggests dynamic isolation is not effectively implemented.

2. CHECK: Inspect and validate configuration settings of dynamic isolation mechanisms (e.g., firewalls, SDN controllers).
   WHY: Properly configured systems are essential to enact real-time isolation and segregation.
   HOW: Access the configuration interfaces to verify that rules or scripts for isolation are present, automated, and tied to actual threat conditions rather than static settings. Check for integration with threat intelligence feeds.
   FAIL: Configuration settings that are static, outdated, or not linked to automated isolation triggers indicate a superficial implementation.

3. CHECK: Conduct a controlled simulation of an isolation trigger.
   WHY: A simulated threat or anomaly test confirms that the dynamic isolation functions correctly in real time.
   HOW: In coordination with the organization, initiate a benign simulation (such as a controlled misbehavior or fake threat event) and monitor to see if designated components are automatically isolated per the dynamic policies. Capture logs and network traffic data during the exercise.
   FAIL: No response or delayed actions, with system components remaining interconnected during the test, demonstrates a failure in dynamic isolation capability.

4. CHECK: Analyze network traffic via packet captures before and after a simulated isolation.
   WHY: Comparing network flows confirms that isolation effectively disrupts actual communication between isolated and trusted components.
   HOW: Use network monitoring tools to capture packets; verify that post-trigger, communication to/from the targeted components is blocked or rerouted. Validate that security policies enforce isolation in traffic routing.
   FAIL: Unchanged traffic patterns or continued open communication channels indicate that segregation is not actively enforced.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Rely on automated, timestamped logs directly from network devices rather than solely on reviewed policy documents to detect genuine isolation events.
• Low-latency response and immediate log entries are critical—long response times or inconsistent logs can be indicators of a poor implementation.
• Verify the integration between threat intelligence feeds and segmentation rules; isolated systems that are incorrectly configured to ignore updated threat data do not provide robust isolation.
• Look out for inconsistencies between simulated test results and actual incident logs, which may suggest logs have been post-edited or fabricated.
• Cross-check network monitoring results (e.g., packet captures) with automated log data to validate that the isolation is both triggered and technically enforced on the network.

This validation methodology ensures that dynamic isolation and segregation are actively protecting the system rather than merely existing as printed policies or static, manually maintained segmentation. It emphasizes real-time automated evidence, operational effectiveness, and integration with threat responses, making sure the control is not only in place but also fully functional in practice.
content:
Statement:
Provide the capability to dynamically isolate [Assignment: organization-defined system components] from other system components.

Guidance:
The capability to dynamically isolate certain internal system components is useful when it is necessary to partition or separate system components of questionable origin from components that possess greater trustworthiness. Component isolation reduces the attack surface of organizational systems. Isolating selected system components can also limit the damage from successful attacks when such attacks occur.

title: BLOCK COMMUNICATION FROM NON-ORGANIZATIONALLY CONFIGURED HOSTS
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Evidence that the organization has defined a list of approved communication clients and corresponding network policies is visible in firewall/NAC configurations and centralized management consoles.
• Technical artifacts include live firewall rule dumps, NAC configuration files, and SIEM logs demonstrating blocked traffic from endpoints not in the approved list.
• Operational patterns: Network scans and logs show repeated blocking of non-organizational endpoints, and periodic reviews reflect active enforcement of these rules.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations might simply document approved client lists without implementing robust traffic filtering at the network perimeter.
• Some may set up rules that appear to block traffic but only filter by IP ranges, missing application-level checks, thus letting unauthorized applications communicate over allowed channels.
• There may be gaps in endpoint management, allowing users to bypass controls using VPNs or tunneling software, or misconfigured devices that are not centrally managed.

VALIDATION STEPS:
1. CHECK: Validate Firewall/NAC Configuration Rules
   WHY: Confirm the network enforces rules that block traffic from non-organizationally configured hosts.
   HOW: Retrieve firewall and NAC configuration reports; verify that rules exist specifically targeting unauthorized communication client traffic (e.g., instant messaging, videoconferencing not managed by IT). Use automated scripts or network management tools to query live configurations.
   FAIL: Absence of explicit rules for non-approved clients, or presence of overly generic rules that do not distinguish between end user and managed devices.

2. CHECK: Analyze SIEM/Log Data for Blocked Traffic
   WHY: Operational evidence is demonstrated through logged events showing blocked inbound/outbound traffic from unauthorized endpoints.
   HOW: Use a SIEM tool to filter logs for events tagged as blocked communications; confirm that events correlate with attempts originating from endpoints not marked as organization-configured. Examine timestamps and source endpoints to ensure consistency.
   FAIL: Lack of log entries for blocked traffic or logs only showing 'allowed' events without verifying that non-approved clients are being filtered.

3. CHECK: Conduct Controlled Testing
   WHY: Practical tests validate that non-authorized communication clients are indeed blocked in a real-world scenario.
   HOW: Set up a test using a machine that is not centrally managed, install an independently configured communication client (e.g., a third-party IM or conferencing tool), and attempt to establish a connection. Monitor network response and logging for a block event.
   FAIL: Successful connection establishment by a non-approved client, or inconsistent blocking across tests.

4. CHECK: Review Endpoint Management and Configuration Enforcement
   WHY: Ensures that endpoints are correctly configured as organizationally approved, which is necessary for the control to function properly.
   HOW: Audit a sample of endpoints to verify they are enrolled in the centralized management system and have approved security configurations. Cross-reference these endpoints with firewall/NAC policies.
   FAIL: Discovery of endpoints that are not enrolled or misconfigured; evidence of unapproved software installed that is allowed communication without police intervention.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Shortcut: Use automated network scanning tools that simulate traffic from known unauthorized hosts to quickly gauge if blocking is active.
• Critical indicator: Inconsistent or absent logging is a red flag, as it may mean the control is in place only on paper.
• Watch for misconfigurations where firewall/NAC devices have outdated rule sets or are not synchronized with endpoint management databases.
• Confirmation by correlating independent data sources (configuration files, SIEM logs, real traffic tests) is crucial, as documentation alone can be faked.
content:
Statement:
Block inbound and outbound communications traffic between [Assignment: organization-defined communication clients] that are independently configured by end users and external service providers.

Guidance:
Communication clients independently configured by end users and external service providers include instant messaging clients and video conferencing software and applications. Traffic blocking does not apply to communication clients that are configured by organizations to perform authorized functions.

title: FAIL SECURE
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• CHECK: Verify that boundary protection devices (firewalls, routers, etc.) have configurations explicitly set to operate in a fail secure mode.
   WHY: This confirms that the system is designed to maintain secure state even if the device fails, ensuring no open or unprotected access is granted.
   HOW: Review device configuration files or management console settings to identify parameters (e.g., default-deny rules, safe shutdown states) that enforce fail secure behavior. Check operational logs for documented failure events indicating that the secure state was maintained.
   FAIL: If configurations default to open access or if logs show that failure events resulted in an insecure state, the control is not implemented properly.

• CHECK: Assess whether redundancy and automated monitoring tools are in place to detect and respond to boundary device failures.
   WHY: Redundancy and monitoring support the fail secure posture by automatically identifying and mitigating failures, reducing the window during which an insecure state might occur.
   HOW: Confirm via system monitoring dashboards and configuration management logs that alerts are triggered on failures and that redundant devices seamlessly take over operations. Validate that these tools are integrated with incident response protocols.
   FAIL: The absence of real-time alerts, redundant failovers, or any monitoring evidence of proactive responses to device failure indicates a gap in the implementation.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations may merely document the intent to fail secure without enforcing the settings on actual devices. Shallow configurations (using defaults that do not explicitly define secure shutdown actions) can be used to indicate compliance falsely.
• Some may rely solely on vendor statements rather than practical internal tests. Inadequate monitoring—such as logging only administrative actions rather than actual fail secure events—can hide non-compliance.

VALIDATION STEPS:
1. CHECK: Device Configuration Settings for Fail Secure Mode
   WHY: Configuration settings are the primary technical artifact proving that devices are prepared to fail securely.
   HOW: Access firewall/router configuration through the management interface or CLI, search for parameters that determine secure shutdown or default-deny behaviors, and corroborate against system specifications.
   FAIL: Absence of specific fail secure settings, or presence of open configurations that would allow unrestricted traffic upon failure, indicates non-compliance.

2. CHECK: Simulation of a Boundary Device Failure
   WHY: Simulated failures demonstrate operational effectiveness, moving beyond theoretical or policy-only compliance.
   HOW: In a controlled lab or production pre-test environment, intentionally disable or disrupt a boundary protection device and observe whether the system maintains secure connectivity (e.g., does not default to an open state) using real-time monitoring and log capture.
   FAIL: If the simulation results in unfiltered or unauthorized connection attempts, the fail secure behavior is not functioning as intended.

3. CHECK: Review of Automated Monitoring and Alert Logs
   WHY: Automated logging is an observable artifact that continuously verifies the operational state and timely response to failures.
   HOW: Extract and analyze logs from both the protection devices and the centralized monitoring system for entries correlating with boundary failures. Confirm that alerts or incident tickets were generated and that follow-up actions were documented.
   FAIL: Missing or improperly triggered log entries during known failure events point to incomplete or faked compliance.

4. CHECK: Redundancy and Failover Mechanisms Validation
   WHY: The presence of redundant devices or failover procedures confirms that there is an additional layer of security to maintain the secure state during failures.
   HOW: Cross-check network diagrams and incident response runbooks to ensure that alternative secure devices are active during boundary device failure events. Validate through interviews with network administrators and view historical maintenance logs.
   FAIL: Inadequate documentation or absence of failover behavior in historical logs is a sign of potential risk.

EXPERT TIPS:
• When examining device configurations, focus on actual enforced parameters rather than generic 'secure by default' claims. Real-world configurations often include hard-coded rules that cannot be easily overridden.
• In simulation tests, ensure the test environment mimics production conditions as closely as possible – test both complete shutdown and degraded performance scenarios.
• Pay attention to the quality and granularity of monitoring logs; superficial entries that do not detail the nature of the event or the subsequent system state may be a red flag.
• Confirm that redundancy isn’t just a physical backup but is also operationally verified via periodic, documented failover tests.
• Always compare the technical findings with the incident response procedures to ensure alignment between documented policies and real-time practice.

This methodology provides a comprehensive approach to validating that systems appropriately enforce a fail secure posture in the event of boundary protection device failures, ensuring that security properties remain intact regardless of operational disruptions.
content:
Statement:
Prevent systems from entering unsecure states in the event of an operational failure of a boundary protection device.

Guidance:
Fail secure is a condition achieved by employing mechanisms to ensure that in the event of operational failures of boundary protection devices at managed interfaces, systems do not enter into unsecure states where intended security properties no longer hold. Managed interfaces include routers, firewalls, and application gateways that reside on protected subnetworks (commonly referred to as demilitarized zones). Failures of boundary protection devices cannot lead to or cause information external to the devices to enter the devices nor can failures permit unauthorized information releases.

title: AUTOMATED ENFORCEMENT OF PROTOCOL FORMATS
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Review network device configurations, logs from deep packet inspection (DPI) firewalls or XML gateways showing active protocol validation rules in real-time. Screenshots of active rules or configurations that enforce specific protocol formats are key signs.
• Technical Artifacts: Configuration files, system logs extracts indicating blocked or flagged packets that do not adhere to protocol format. Automated alerts showing rejections based on malformed protocols are concrete evidence.
• Operational Patterns: Regular monitoring reports indicating consistent enforcement, with documented events where non-compliant packets were detected and dropped or flagged. Patterns of auto remediation mechanisms during traffic analysis are also indicative.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Superficial Configurations: Simply having a policy or documentation about protocol enforcement with default or sample settings that are not actively monitoring traffic.
• Log Fudging: Manipulated or insufficient logging that shows compliance on paper without active enforcement. Process payments where manual overrides or soft exceptions have been used frequently.
• Incomplete DPI Deployment: Using a firewall or XML gateway in a passive mode or with outdated signatures/formats; thereby, enforcement appears in configuration but isn’t actually active in traffic flows.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Verify Active DPI/Protocol Validation Device Configuration
WHY: Confirm the enforcement mechanism is actually configured to inspect and enforce protocol formats.
HOW: Access configuration management interface of the DPI firewall or XML gateway; check that rule sets and protocol format rules (e.g., regex, XML schema validation) are active and applying to live traffic.
FAIL: If rule sets are missing, default configurations are in place, or the system is in monitoring mode only without enforcement actions.

CHECK: Analyze System Logs for Protocol Format Violations
WHY: Logs directly show enforcement in action by capturing dropped or flagged packets.
HOW: Perform a log review on the device; search for entries that indicate detection of protocol format violations, rejections or alerts triggered by malformed protocol content. Use automation tools to filter events that specifically correspond to protocol mismatch errors.
FAIL: Lack of relevant log entries, or logs only showing generic blockage without explicit mention of protocol enforcement.

CHECK: Generate Test Traffic with Malformed Protocols
WHY: Testing provides evidence that enforcement mechanism actively evaluates and rejects non-compliant traffic.
HOW: Craft controlled test packets or malformed XML documents that do not conform to the specified protocol. Send the test traffic through the device and verify from logs or alert systems that the system blocks or flags the traffic.
FAIL: If the system accepts the malformed packets or if there’s no corresponding alert or log entry indicating detection and enforcement.

CHECK: Validate Automated Alerting and Remediation Actions
WHY: Automated responses indicate that the enforcement is not merely passive but integrated into broader security operations.
HOW: Verify that when a protocol format violation is detected, an automated alert is raised and, if applicable, automated remediation (like dropping the packet, session termination, or quarantine) is triggered. Check configuration details and incident response logs.
FAIL: Absence of automated responses, or if manual intervention is consistently required to address policy violations.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Verification Shortcut: Check if the device dashboard displays real-time traffic statistics and enforcement logs. A sudden spike in blocked/alerted events is a good sign that the enforcement is working.
• Critical Indicator of Poor Implementation: If the enforcement rules are outdated or only cover a subset of expected traffic (e.g., only HTTP but not HTTPS or other protocols), this indicates a significant gap.
• Detail Matters: Ensure that the configuration isn’t just logging events but actively rejecting malformed traffic. Also, check that exceptions or bypass rules are tightly controlled and audited.
• Automated Testing: Regular use of automated test tools for protocol compliance can pre-empt configuration drifts and simulate attacks, ensuring continuous integrity of enforcement.
• Cross-Validation: Validate that enforcement rules on devices align with broader security policies and that they’re periodically updated to reflect evolving protocol standards.
content:
Statement:
Enforce adherence to protocol formats.

Guidance:
System components that enforce protocol formats include deep packet inspection firewalls and XML gateways. The components verify adherence to protocol.

title: PREVENT DISCOVERY OF SYSTEM COMPONENTS
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for NIST SC-7(16) – PREVENT DISCOVERY OF SYSTEM COMPONENTS.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • The network design documentation and configuration files show that managed interfaces are not registered in public DNS, and network address translation (NAT) is actively used.
   • External and internal DNS zone file reviews confirm the absence of IP addresses linked to managed interfaces. Configuration management databases (CMDB) and change logs indicate periodic address changes.
   • Operational network scans initiated from untrusted network segments do not reveal the managed interface IPs, indicating that discovery tools cannot enumerate these addresses.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations might simply disable DNS lookups for some segments while inadvertently leaving other avenues open (e.g., SNMP or undocumented IPv6 pointers). 
   • Superficial implementations may rely on internal documentation stating compliance without actually configuring firewalls or NAT correctly. 
   • Static IP addresses present in legacy systems or overlooked configuration files may be visible, nullifying the control’s intent.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   • CHECK: Verify DNS Zone Configuration
         WHY: Ensures that managed interface IP addresses are not published to DNS.
         HOW: Audit both internal/external DNS zone files and test with DNS queries from an external network; confirm that no records exist for managed interfaces.
         FAIL: Discovery of any A/AAAA records referencing the managed interfaces indicates non-compliance.

   • CHECK: Evaluate NAT and Firewall Configuration
         WHY: NAT and firewall rules should obscure internal IP addresses from external scanning.
         HOW: Review network architecture diagrams and NAT/firewall configurations; perform external network scans to check if managed system components are detectable.
         FAIL: Visible IP addresses during scans or misconfigured NAT/firewall rules are a sign of failure.

   • CHECK: Conduct External Penetration Testing/Scanning
         WHY: Active testing validates that common discovery tools do not reveal interfaces.
         HOW: Launch controlled, external network scans using tools like Nmap from an off-network environment; assess if managed interfaces remain hidden.
         FAIL: If scanning returns expected IP responses or banner information related to managed systems, the control is not effectively implemented.

   • CHECK: Review Change Management Logs
         WHY: Periodic changes to network addresses should be tracked to maintain dynamic obscurity.
         HOW: Audit configuration management systems and change logs to verify that scheduled periodic IP changes occur; corroborate with intercepted network observation.
         FAIL: Static address records or absent logging of address changes indicate a breakdown in process.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: Use automated scanners from multiple external vantage points. Discrepancies in discovered vs. documented addresses are immediate red flags.
   • Critical Indicator: Check for any legacy system configurations that have not been included in centralized NAT or DNS management, as these are common oversights.
   • Detail Matter: Ensure IPv6 configurations are reviewed with equal rigor—often, IPv6 pointer records or misconfigurations can reveal managed interfaces inadvertently.

This methodology combines both technical artifact reviews and active operational checking to ensure that managed interface components remain undiscoverable to unauthorized entities.
content:
Statement:
Prevent the discovery of specific system components that represent a managed interface.

Guidance:
Preventing the discovery of system components representing a managed interface helps protect network addresses of those components from discovery through common tools and techniques used to identify devices on networks. Network addresses are not available for discovery and require prior knowledge for access. Preventing the discovery of components and devices can be accomplished by not publishing network addresses, using network address translation, or not entering the addresses in domain name systems. Another prevention technique is to periodically change network addresses.

title: NETWORKED PRIVILEGED ACCESSES
methodology:
Control SC-7(15) is about ensuring that networked, privileged accesses are always routed through a dedicated, managed interface. This is critical to maintaining strong access control and enabling detailed auditing. Below is a detailed validation methodology:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • There is a distinct, dedicated management network or interface clearly used solely for privileged access sessions. This is evident via network diagrams, configuration files of network devices (e.g., firewalls, routers) and access policy configurations.
   • Technical artifacts include configuration snapshots from network devices showing ACLs or firewall rules that restrict privileged access to the dedicated interface, and detailed logs from a centralized audit system that records access events from that interface.
   • Operational patterns include routine use of the dedicated interface for privileged work, with no direct remote access allowed on general-purpose production networks. Regular audits of access logs and interface usage metrics also indicate that this dedicated path is actively monitored.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations sometimes declare compliance by simply relying on policy documentation without verifying that alternate access methods (e.g., standard production or user networks) are not used for privileged sessions.
   • Some environments may only restrict access in documentation or theoretical network diagrams while failing to implement segment-specific routing and monitoring in practice.
   • Technical gaps frequently include incomplete firewall rules or misconfigurations that allow privileged sessions to spill over to non-dedicated interfaces, and audit logs being insufficiently granular or not segregated by access type.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   • CHECK: Verify that all privileged access requests (e.g., SSH, RDP admin sessions) are routed via a dedicated network interface.
     WHY: This demonstrates that access is being controlled using separate paths that can be strictly audited and monitored.
     HOW: Review network device configurations (firewall ACLs, router routing tables) to confirm that IP routes for privileged traffic are designated on the dedicated management network. Use network scanning tools to check for open ports outside this dedicated path on critical systems.
     FAIL: If privileged access sessions are observed on standard production interfaces or if network configurations do not distinctly separate privileged interface traffic.

   • CHECK: Validate that audit trails for privileged sessions are recorded separately with sufficient detail (time stamps, sources, commands, etc.).
     WHY: Independent and granular logging ensures that any unauthorized access can be detected and correlated to the dedicated interface usage.
     HOW: Inspect audit logs from centralized logging systems to verify that logs for privileged accesses are being captured with clear indicators that they came via the management interface. Correlate log entries with the IP ranges assigned to the dedicated interface.
     FAIL: Incomplete logs, absence of IP source information, or audit logs that do not reflect dedicated interface usage indicate a failing implementation.

   • CHECK: Examine and test firewall and ACL configurations that enforce the segregation of privileged access.
     WHY: Ensuring the integrity of the dedicated access path requires that firewalls and ACLs are correctly configured and enforced.
     HOW: Use configuration reviews and automated policy compliance tools to check that only dedicated management interface networks have permission to access administrative ports of critical systems. Attempt controlled access tests to confirm that attempts from non-designated networks are rejected.
     FAIL: Successful access attempts from non-designated networks or misconfigurations in firewall rules exposing privileged services to broader networks significantly point to a gap in implementation.

   • CHECK: Confirm that network diagrams and system configurations reflect operational realities by reconciling documented designs with observed network traffic flows.
     WHY: Documentation that accurately mirrors live operations is hard to fake and helps ensure the dedicated interface is truly isolated for privileged accesses.
     HOW: Interview system/network administrators and review change management records along with real-time network monitoring data (for instance, via SIEM tools) to ensure the documented dedicated interface is actively used for privileged access sessions.
     FAIL: A disconnect between documentation and actual network flows is a red flag indicative of poor implementation.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Leverage automated configuration management and monitoring tools to continuously validate that only dedicated IP ranges are used for privileged sessions. This prevents lapses when new systems are added.
   • Look for anomalies in the logs that could indicate attempts to bypass the dedicated interface (e.g., connections targeting administrative ports over the general network). This is a critical indicator of potential security gaps.
   • In practice, prioritize evidence from live network traffic captures and real-time access logs over static policy documents. This helps uncover misconfigurations that might not be captured in routine documentation.
   • Always cross-check firewall and routing configurations with real-world traffic patterns. A common pitfall is outdated diagrams or configs that might not reflect recent changes, thus verification through active testing is essential.

This methodology combines in-depth technical validation with practical, observable evidence, ensuring that the dedicated, managed interface for networked, privileged accesses isn’t just a theoretical policy but an effectively enforced control.
content:
Statement:
Route networked, privileged accesses through a dedicated, managed interface for purposes of access control and auditing.

Guidance:
Privileged access provides greater accessibility to system functions, including security functions. Adversaries attempt to gain privileged access to systems through remote access to cause adverse mission or business impacts, such as exfiltrating information or bringing down a critical system capability. Routing networked, privileged access requests through a dedicated, managed interface further restricts privileged access requests for increased access control and auditing.

title: PROTECT AGAINST UNAUTHORIZED PHYSICAL CONNECTIONS
methodology:
Control: SC-7(14) – PROTECT AGAINST UNAUTHORIZED PHYSICAL CONNECTIONS

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Clearly labeled and physically separated cable trays, connection frames, and patch panels specifically marked for their managed interfaces. Access-controlled equipment rooms, wiring closets, or cable distribution areas with secure entry points and signage.
• Technical Artifacts: Updated physical network diagrams showing segregation, inventories of cable management and interface equipment, and logs from physical access control systems (badge readers, CCTV recordings). Additionally, configuration documentation that includes physical security measures for managed interfaces.
• Operational Patterns: Routine physical inspections and periodic audits of data center layouts; consistent enforcement of controlled access policies; calibration and review of access control lists and entry logs.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Superficial Documentation: Organizations may produce neat network diagrams and policies claiming segregation while actual physical infrastructure remains unsegregated or shared.
• Minimal Signage/Labeling: Installing labels without physically separating cable infrastructure, merely relying on markings or low-impact measures.
• Outdated or Infrequent Reviews: Maintaining policies and access logs that aren’t regularly updated or monitored for unauthorized changes.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Inspect Physical Cable Management Areas
WHY: Actual physical separation and clear labeling on cable trays, patch panels, and wiring closets prove that the infrastructure is explicitly designed to enforce separation.
HOW: Conduct an on-site visual inspection of equipment rooms, cable trays and patch panels; verify that each managed interface has a designated area with tamper-resistant labeling and physical barriers. Review updated diagrams for consistency.
FAIL: Inadequate or missing labeling, mixing of cables from different security categories, or accessible trays/patch panels in unprotected areas.

CHECK: Review Physical Access Control Mechanisms
WHY: Restricted and monitored physical access (e.g., badge access, CCTV coverage) confirms that only authorized personnel can connect to managed interfaces.
HOW: Examine access logs, test entry controls at equipment rooms or wiring closets, and interview staff responsible for physical security to ensure proper enforcement.
FAIL: Absence of accessible logs, unlocked or unmonitored access points, or evidence of unauthorized personnel entering these areas.

CHECK: Validate Network and Physical Layout Documentation
WHY: Updated, accurate documentation ensures that there is an acknowledged control process and that separation requirements are met.
HOW: Compare current physical network diagrams and inventory lists with actual installations during a walkthrough; check for documented reviews and updates.
FAIL: Contradictions between documentation and observed installations, outdated diagrams, or missing records of periodic reviews.

CHECK: Audit Incident and Maintenance Logs
WHY: Regular logging of incidents and maintenance related to physical connections indicates active monitoring and a corrective process.
HOW: Review logs to verify that issues around physical access have been recorded, investigated, and resolved; confirm that maintenance procedures include checks on physical security measures.
FAIL: Non-existent or superficial logs, recurring issues without corrective actions, or lack of evidence for regular maintenance checks.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Verification Shortcut: Begin with a walkthrough of the data center or wiring closets. Verifying physical infrastructure in person often reveals overlooked lapses like loose labels or exposed cable trays that documentation will not disclose.
• Critical Indicator: The presence (or absence) of tamper-proof measures on patch panels and the frequency of access control audits. Frequent breaches or unauthorized access attempts suggest poor implementation.
• Detail That Matters: Always check that any “managed interface” implied by documentation actually has dedicated, segregated physical infrastructure. Superficial labeling or shared equipment can undermine the control’s effectiveness.
• Pro Tip: Use a combination of digital (access logs and CCTV) and physical inspection methods to validate that controls aren’t just in place on paper but are actively preventing unauthorized physical connections.

These steps provide a practical approach to verifying that unauthorized physical connections are effectively protected through both physical segregation and operational controls.
content:
Statement:
Protect against unauthorized physical connections at [Assignment: organization-defined managed interfaces].

Guidance:
Systems that operate at different security categories or classification levels may share common physical and environmental controls, since they may share space within the same facilities. In practice, it is possible that these separate systems may share common equipment rooms, wiring closets, and cable distribution paths. Protection against unauthorized physical connections can be achieved by using clearly identified and physically separated cable trays, connection frames, and patch panels for each side of managed interfaces with physical access controls that enforce limited authorized access to these items.

title: ISOLATION OF SECURITY TOOLS, MECHANISMS, AND SUPPORT COMPONENTS
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence includes network diagrams and physical layouts clearly showing that security tools (e.g., IDS, SIEM, forensic systems) are located in dedicated subnetworks physically separated from operational or business networks.
• Technical artifacts demonstrating compliance include firewall configuration files, routing tables, and VLAN/subnetting configurations that enforce isolation with managed interfaces between the security and other internal systems.
• Operational patterns include logs from firewall devices and network monitoring systems that indicate controlled and limited traffic flows between isolated networks.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations might simply document the existence of segregated networks on paper without proper implementation, relying on policies rather than enforced technical controls.
• Surface-level implementations include virtual segmentation that lacks proper physical separation or managed interfaces, making it easier for an adversary to pivot.
• Frequently missed gaps include inadequate configuration of managed interfaces, leaving unnecessary open channels or misconfigured firewall rules that do not truly restrict traffic between segments.

VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Verify network architecture diagrams and physical topology maps.
WHY: Confirm that security tools and related support components are placed in dedicated, physically separate subnetworks.
HOW: Review current network diagrams, data center layouts, and physical cabling maps; compare these with the inventory of security tool deployments.
FAIL: Diagrams show integrated networks or fail to denote physical separation of security mechanisms.

CHECK: Assess firewall and router configurations for managed interfaces between security subnetworks and other internal systems.
WHY: Demonstrates that communication between the separate networks is controlled and monitored.
HOW: Inspect configuration files, filter rules, and access control lists on network devices; verify that only authorized traffic is permitted across interfaces.
FAIL: Absence of managed interfaces or overly permissive network rules that allow unrestricted traffic flows.

CHECK: Evaluate the segmentation through endpoint testing and network scanning.
WHY: Provides operational evidence that security tool networks are isolated in practice, not just on paper.
HOW: Attempt controlled network scans from non-security components to security subnetwork IP ranges; verify that connections are blocked or require strict authentication.
FAIL: Successful connectivity indicating improper segmentation or exposure of security tools to other network segments.

CHECK: Review system logs and change management records for evidence of regular monitoring and testing of the isolation controls.
WHY: Confirms continuous operational oversight and adherence to the isolation principle.
HOW: Examine logs from intrusion detection systems, firewall monitoring, and audit trails for changes to network segmentation configurations.
FAIL: Lack of monitoring logs or outdated records that suggest neglected enforcement of isolation controls.

EXPERT TIPS
• Always verify that documentation matches real-time configurations through direct inspection of network devices rather than relying solely on policy documents.
• Look for discrepancies between documented separation and actual network traffic logs; automated scanning results that show cross-network traffic are a red flag.
• A well-implemented isolation scheme should not allow any bypass method from non-security networks—if any such connectivity is detected, review firewall and interface policy settings immediately.
• Cross-check penetration test reports with implemented configurations to validate that threat vectors are effectively mitigated by the isolated architecture.
content:
Statement:
Isolate [Assignment: organization-defined information security tools, mechanisms, and support components] from other internal system components by implementing physically separate subnetworks with managed interfaces to other components of the system.

Guidance:
Physically separate subnetworks with managed interfaces are useful in isolating computer network defenses from critical operational processing networks to prevent adversaries from discovering the analysis and forensics techniques employed by organizations.

title: HOST-BASED PROTECTION
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Visible Evidence: You should see active host-based firewall processes or services running on each system component (server, workstation, mobile device).
• Technical Artifacts: Firewall configuration files (such as iptables, Windows Firewall policy store), logs of firewall activity, and records from centralized management that reflect the current host-based boundary protection settings.
• Operational Patterns: Regularly updated and centrally monitored firewall rules, automated alerts for unauthorized changes, and evidence of periodic audits of firewall settings.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Fake Compliance: Organizations may simply deploy default firewall software without customizing rules to protect against specific threats or boundary breaches.
• Surface-Level Implementations: Enabling a firewall service without properly configuring inbound/outbound rule sets, or simply installing the software without ensuring it’s active across all zones/hosts.
• Technical Gaps: Missing validation that the firewall protects all interfaces, lack of correlation between policy documents and live configuration, or relying solely on manual configuration without automated monitoring.

VALIDATION STEPS:
1. CHECK: Verify that a host-based firewall is enabled and running on each system component.
   WHY: Active firewalls are essential evidence that the control is in place and defending the host against malicious network communications.
   HOW: Use operating system commands (e.g., ‘netsh advfirewall show allprofiles’ on Windows, ‘iptables -L’ or ‘firewalld --state’ on Linux) and validate via central management consoles if available.
   FAIL: Absence of running firewall services or disabled/incorrectly configured firewall rules.

2. CHECK: Review firewall configuration files and policies for organization-defined rule sets.
   WHY: Properly configured firewall rules prove that the protection mechanism is tailored to organizational requirements, not just left at defaults.
   HOW: Inspect configuration files (e.g., /etc/iptables/rules.v4, group policy objects on Windows) and verify rule definitions against organizational baseline requirements.
   FAIL: Default configurations, incomplete rule sets, or configurations that do not match defined security requirements.

3. CHECK: Validate the integration of firewall logs with centralized monitoring or SIEM systems.
   WHY: Central monitoring ensures ongoing oversight and response capability, confirming the operational effectiveness of the control.
   HOW: Examine log exports, SIEM alerts, or management console dashboards that capture real-time firewall events and policy changes.
   FAIL: Lack of log forwarding, absence of periodic review, or misconfigured logging policies that ignore crucial traffic events.

4. CHECK: Test host-based firewall effectiveness through simulated network scans or penetration tests.
   WHY: Active testing demonstrates that the firewall effectively blocks unauthorized traffic and is not merely present, but functionally enforcing rules.
   HOW: Conduct vulnerability scans and penetration tests aimed at firewall rule evasion, verifying that unauthorized ports and services are inaccessible.
   FAIL: Discovering open ports that should be blocked according to organizational policy, or ease of bypassing firewall protections.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Verification Shortcuts: Use centralized endpoint management tools to quickly snapshot the firewall status across multiple system components. Automated reporting can reveal non-compliant hosts.
• Indicators of Poor Implementation: Repeated manual exceptions in configurations, outdated rule sets, or logs that show consistent rule modifications without proper change control.
• Critical Details: Focus on verifying that every network interface is covered by active firewall rules, ensuring no gaps exist (especially on remote or virtual interfaces), and cross-check alignment between documented policies and live configurations.
This methodology ensures that host-based boundary protection is not only documented but operates effectively and is verifiable through automated and operational evidence.
content:
Statement:
Implement [Assignment: organization-defined host-based boundary protection mechanisms] at [Assignment: organization-defined system components].

Guidance:
Host-based boundary protection mechanism includes host-based firewalls. System components that employ host-based boundary protection mechanisms include servers, workstations, notebook computers, and mobile devices.

title: RESTRICT INCOMING COMMUNICATIONS TRAFFIC
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• CHECK: Verify that firewall/router ACL configurations are in place to restrict traffic to authorized source/destination pairs.
  WHY: Active, properly configured network devices provide clear evidence that only approved communications are allowed.
  HOW: Review current firewall and router configuration files or management console outputs; confirm that ACLs or rule sets explicitly list the authorized IP address ranges or subnets for both sources and destinations.
  FAIL: If configurations are absent, over-broad (e.g., permitting entire networks without filtering), or only documented without corresponding active settings, this check has failed.

• CHECK: Corroborate presence of logs that reflect enforcement of these controls.
  WHY: Automated log entries showing drops or blocks on unauthorized communication attempts demonstrate operational control effectiveness.
  HOW: Analyze firewall, router, or IDS/IPS logs for entries indicating blocked or rejected unauthorized source/destination traffic; cross-reference timestamps and source addresses with defined policy rules.
  FAIL: A lack of logging or logs that indicate allowed unauthorized traffic suggest ineffective control enforcement.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may provide static documentation listing authorized sources/destinations yet fail to maintain or update actual device configurations.
• Some implementations use overly permissive firewall rules that superficially comply with policy but do not effectively restrict spoofed or misaligned address traffic.
• Reliance solely on policy documentation without continuous monitoring or logging can lead to situations where the controls are not effectively enforced.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Obtain and review current firewall and router configuration exports to verify specific rules that allow only designated authorized source and destination IP pairs.
   WHY: Ensures that the live configuration aligns with the control requirements.
   HOW: Use configuration management tools or direct CLI access to list current rules; verify that each rule references authorized IP ranges as per security policy.
   FAIL: Presence of generic rules (e.g., permit any source to any destination) or absence of criteria-based filtering indicates non-compliance.

2. CHECK: Examine automated system logs related to incoming traffic to validate rule enforcement.
   WHY: Real-time logs prove that the control is actively being enforced and that unauthorized attempts are being blocked.
   HOW: Query firewall/IDS logs for rejected or dropped connection attempts; compare these entries against the approved list of IPs to ensure only unauthorized sources are being blocked.
   FAIL: Logs that show traffic from unauthorized addresses being accepted or lack evidence of traffic filtering indicate a failure in control implementation.

3. CHECK: Perform an external network scan or controlled penetration test to simulate unauthorized traffic toward the protected destination.
   WHY: Practical testing confirms that the configured rules effectively block unwanted traffic and can’t be bypassed by spoofing.
   HOW: Use an approved scanning tool or red team exercise to send traffic from non-authorized IP ranges to authorized destinations; observe responses and check logs for blocked attempts.
   FAIL: Successful communication from unauthorized sources or lack of detected alerts on the network perimeter signals a failure in applying the control.

4. CHECK: Validate change control logs for firewall/ACL rule modifications to ensure that updates follow policy and are consistently applied.
   WHY: Regular review of change records shows that any modifications are intentional, approved, and documented as part of continuous security monitoring.
   HOW: Review change management systems or audit logs for rule updates; confirm that changes meet the defined standards for authorized sources/destinations.
   FAIL: Inconsistent change records or undocumented modifications suggest weak enforcement and potential lapses in security posture.

EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Use automated tools (e.g., firewall configuration audit tools) to quickly compare live settings against predefined authorized lists.
• Critical Indicator: The presence of any log entries that report allowed communication from non-whitelisted IPs provides immediate evidence of a misconfigured control.
• Remember: Regularly scheduled external penetration tests and vulnerability scans are essential to uncover potential bypass strategies that internal audits might not reveal. Robust logging and real-time monitoring are key to continuous control effectiveness.
content:
Statement:
Only allow incoming communications from [Assignment: organization-defined authorized sources] to be routed to [Assignment: organization-defined authorized destinations].

Guidance:
General source address validation techniques are applied to restrict the use of illegal and unallocated source addresses as well as source addresses that should only be used within the system. The restriction of incoming communications traffic provides determinations that source and destination address pairs represent authorized or allowed communications. Determinations can be based on several factors, including the presence of such address pairs in lists of authorized or allowed communications, the absence of such address pairs in lists of unauthorized or disallowed pairs, or meeting more general rules for authorized or allowed source and destination pairs. Strong authentication of network addresses is not possible without the use of explicit security protocols, and thus, addresses can often be spoofed. Further, identity-based incoming traffic restriction methods can be employed, including router access control lists and firewall rules.

title: PREVENT EXFILTRATION
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
