• Evidence includes active data loss prevention (DLP) alerts, deep packet inspection (DPI)/XML gateway logs, and network traffic analysis reports that document attempts or blocks of outbound data transfers.
• Artifacts such as configuration files and runtime logs from firewalls, gateway devices, and endpoint monitoring that show protocol enforcement and anomaly detection in place.
• Regular tests and penetration test reports that simulate exfiltration attempts and verify blocking mechanisms.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations might rely solely on policy documentation or scheduled tests, failing to show evidence of continuous active monitoring.
• Some may configure DPI or DLP tools in a passive mode where logs exist but blocking actions are not enforced, or fake alert data by tweaking thresholds.
• Superficial implementations where configurations exist on paper, but actual network and endpoint traffic isn’t consistently analyzed, allowing manual exfiltration bypasses.

VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Verify active network monitoring and DLP/DPI logs
WHY: Active logs that record blocked or flagged outbound traffic confirm that mechanisms preventing exfiltration are operational.
HOW: Access and review logs from firewalls, DPI devices, and DLP systems for events indicating detection/blocking of anomalous outbound sessions (e.g., alerts on packets that deviate from standard protocol behaviors, attempts to use disallowed ports or protocols).
FAIL: Absence of logs, or no evidence of active event interception during defined operational periods, indicates potential gaps.

CHECK: Assess configuration settings on deep packet inspection (DPI) devices and XML gateways
WHY: Properly configured devices enforcing strict protocol adherence are critical in preventing data exfiltration through non-approved means.
HOW: Audit configuration files to verify that rules for protocol structure enforcement are enabled, and cross-check with recent operational logs to ensure these rules are active and effective.
FAIL: Configuration settings that are outdated or set to only log anomalies without actively blocking exfiltration attempts suggest non-compliance.

CHECK: Review exfiltration test records and simulated penetration test outcomes
WHY: Regular exercise of the control through simulated tests ensures both the functional and operational effectiveness of data exfiltration prevention measures.
HOW: Examine test schedules, methodology documents, and results from exfiltration-specific penetration tests. Verify that tests cover both technical prevention and operational response (e.g., beaconing detection, protocol anomaly analysis).
FAIL: Lack of recent tests, incomplete test records, or evidence that tests are superficial and do not mimic real exfiltration scenarios indicates a potential bypass.

CHECK: Evaluate outbound traffic analytics for deviations and anomaly detection
WHY: Continuous traffic analysis that detects deviations from expected volume or types of traffic is essential for identifying unintentional or intentional exfiltration attempts.
HOW: Audit network monitoring tools for analytics reports showing traffic baselines, and evidence of alerts triggered by unusual traffic patterns (e.g., spikes during off-hours or non-standard protocols used in outbound traffic).
FAIL: If analytics do not show defined baselines or alerts for atypical traffic, it may indicate that the control is not tuned correctly or is easily bypassed.

EXPERT TIPS
• Use correlation: Cross-reference firewall/DPI/DLP logs with network flow data to validate that detected anomalies correspond with blocking actions.
• Focus on anomaly detection: Pay particular attention to deviations from normal traffic patterns during non-peak hours – these are common avenues for unnoticed exfiltration.
• Validate testing frequency and comprehensiveness: Even if tests are scheduled, ensure that they simulate a range of realistic exfiltration techniques (e.g., using steganography or protocol header manipulations).
• Check for alert fatigue: An abnormal absence of security alerts may indicate thresholds are set too high or monitoring is not properly calibrated.

The above methodology focuses on observable, technical evidence and operational testing. It aims to verify that prevention measures are not only documented but actively blocking and alerting on potential exfiltration attempts – and that the organization tests these measures at an organization-defined frequency.
content:
Statement:
(a) Prevent the exfiltration of information; and (b) Conduct exfiltration tests [Assignment: organization-defined frequency].

Guidance:
Prevention of exfiltration applies to both the intentional and unintentional exfiltration of information. Techniques used to prevent the exfiltration of information from systems may be implemented at internal endpoints, external boundaries, and across managed interfaces and include adherence to protocol formats, monitoring for beaconing activity from systems, disconnecting external network interfaces except when explicitly needed, employing traffic profile analysis to detect deviations from the volume and types of traffic expected, call backs to command and control centers, conducting penetration testing, monitoring for steganography, disassembling and reassembling packet headers, and using data loss and data leakage prevention tools. Devices that enforce strict adherence to protocol formats include deep packet inspection firewalls and Extensible Markup Language (XML) gateways.

title: RESTRICT THREATENING OUTGOING COMMUNICATIONS TRAFFIC
methodology:
Below is a structured methodology to validate control SC-7(9): RESTRICT THREATENING OUTGOING COMMUNICATIONS TRAFFIC

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible evidence includes active network security appliances (firewalls, IDS/IPS) with defined outbound filtering rules geared to block suspicious traffic. 
   • Technical artifacts include detailed logs from network devices that record both the denial of outbound connections and the associated internal user identities. 
   • Operational patterns like real-time alerting and automated blocking of outgoing connections tied to threat signatures indicate this control is active.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations might generate superficial logs or configure generic outbound filters that do not specifically correlate internal user identity with denied traffic. 
   • Some setups rely solely on policy documentation without actual monitoring or effective rule implementation. 
   • A gap may be observed where the IDS/IPS flags outbound threats but the integration with audit logging for internal user identity is absent or incomplete.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK 1: Verify Outbound Threat Detection and Denial Configuration
WHY: Ensures that systems are actively detecting and blocking traffic that threatens external targets based on defined threat signatures or anomalous behaviors.
HOW: Review the configuration of network security appliances (firewalls, IDS/IPS) to confirm they include outbound filtering rules for known threat indicators. Validate that the device logs report instances where specific outbound traffic was denied.
FAIL: If outbound filtering rules are missing, misconfigured, or device logs show no alerts for suspicious traffic, this is a strong indicator of non-compliance.

CHECK 2: Confirm Audit Logging Captures Internal User Identities
WHY: This confirms that the control is not only preventing malicious outbound traffic but also auditing the identity of the internal user associated with attempted communications.
HOW: Access system and network audit logs (e.g., syslog, firewall logs) and trace entries where outbound communications were denied. Verify that these logs include user identifiers, IP addresses, or session identifiers linking the attempt to a specific internal source.
FAIL: Failure to capture or properly correlate user identities with blocked traffic events (e.g., generic system entries or missing user fields) suggests the control is not being fully implemented.

CHECK 3: Simulate Threatening Outbound Traffic
WHY: Testing with simulated threat activity can demonstrate the control’s operational effectiveness in real-time enforcement.
HOW: Conduct a controlled test by initiating an outbound connection that mimics known threat behavior (using a testing tool or red teaming approach). Verify that the system denies the traffic and simultaneously logs the test event with the proper user context.
FAIL: If the simulated threat is not blocked, or if the blocking event is not logged with accurate user detail, this is an operational gap in the control.

CHECK 4: Correlate Automated Alerts with Operational Response
WHY: Ensures that alerting systems not only detect threatening outbound communications but also trigger appropriate operational responses.
HOW: Evaluate the incident response workflow by reviewing automated alert mechanisms triggered by outbound threat events. Check that these alerts include actionable details like the user identity and that follow-up actions (like escalation or investigation) are documented.
FAIL: Ineffective or generic alert messages, a lack of follow-up actions, or discrepancies in the alert logs indicate an incomplete deployment of this control.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: Use SIEM dashboards to correlate outbound block events with user logins instantly. Look for anomalies such as events with missing user data. 
   • Critical Indicator: Incomplete user attribution in the logs is one of the strongest signals of poor implementation. 
   • Details Matter: Ensure that log retention periods meet operational needs and that time-synchronized logging across devices is in place to facilitate correlation.

Following these steps provides both technical and operational evidence of the active enforcement of control SC-7(9), ensuring that outgoing communications which pose threats are detected, blocked, and accurately logged with internal user context.
content:
Statement:
Detect and deny outgoing communications traffic posing a threat to external systems; and Audit the identity of internal users associated with denied communications.

Guidance:
Detecting outgoing communications traffic from internal actions that may pose threats to external systems is known as extrusion detection. Extrusion detection is carried out within the system at managed interfaces. Extrusion detection includes the analysis of.

title: ROUTE TRAFFIC TO AUTHENTICATED PROXY SERVERS
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
- CHECK: Confirm that all outgoing internal communications designated for external networks are being redirected through proxy servers that require authentication.
- WHY: If internal communications intended for external networks are forced to route via authenticated proxies, it demonstrates that the organization is effectively limiting direct connections and potentially securing external traffic.
- HOW: Verify network diagrams and routing configurations on firewalls and routers to ensure that policies redirect traffic, review authenticated proxy configuration settings, and inspect proxy logs for evidence of session authentications from internal hosts.
- FAIL: Direct outbound connections from internal hosts to external networks which bypass any proxy authentication indicate non-compliance.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
- Organizations might configure proxy servers for only a subset of traffic or rely solely on documentation and internal policies without enforcing rules in network devices.
- Superficial implementations include proxies that do not enforce authentication properly (e.g., default or weak credentials) or where traffic bypass rules exist, negating the proxy’s filtering benefits.
- A common technical gap is the absence of consistent traffic logging or incomplete integration between routing/firewall policies and proxy server settings.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Inspect firewall and router configuration rules.
   WHY: Ensures that traffic intended for external networks is automatically redirected to proxy servers.
   HOW: Log into firewall management, review outgoing ACLs (access control lists) and NAT rules; verify that rules mandate routing traffic via the proxy’s managed interface.
   FAIL: Any rule that permits direct connectivity to external networks without proxy forwarding indicates non-compliance.

2. CHECK: Examine the proxy servers’ configuration for enforced authentication mechanisms.
   WHY: Verifies that the proxy servers require authentication and that credentials are being used to initiate sessions.
   HOW: Access the proxy server’s management interface to review authentication settings; inspect logs for successful authentication events correlated with internal IP addresses.
   FAIL: If the proxy configuration allows access without proper authentication or if logs do not show authenticated sessions, implementation is lacking.

3. CHECK: Validate system logs for evidence of authenticated proxy sessions and traffic flow from internal to external endpoints.
   WHY: Provides observable, automated evidence that internal traffic is indeed handled by the proxy and authenticated properly.
   HOW: Collect logs from network monitoring tools and the proxy servers; correlate session start times, authentication successes, and session terminations to ensure that all external-bound traffic involves an authenticated proxy session.
   FAIL: Absence of such correlated logs or presence of sessions that do not require authentication indicates gaps in control implementation.

4. CHECK: Perform network traffic analysis using tools like traceroute or packet capture.
   WHY: Operational tests can confirm that traffic from internal systems is not bypassing the proxy infrastructure.
   HOW: Initiate a traceroute from an internal system to an external resource; verify that intermediary hops include the authenticated proxy server’s IP address, and inspect packet captures for authentication handshakes.
   FAIL: A traceroute that shows direct routing to an external IP, bypassing the proxy infrastructure, is a red flag.

EXPERT TIPS
- Short Verification: Use automated network monitoring tools that continuously verify proxy session authentication and route enforcement. Alert on any instances of direct external communications.
- Critical Indicators: Ensure that no exceptions or bypass rules exist in firewall/NAT configurations; missing or sporadic proxy log entries often indicate configuration drift or rule circumvention.
- Detail Focus: Pay special attention to the authentication mechanism details (e.g., certificate validation, two-factor authentication) on the proxy, as superficial setups are easily mimicked but fail under scrutiny.
- Extra Check: Review incident response records or network audit trails in case of anomalies to determine if any traffic ever circumvented proxy controls.

This methodology provides actionable, practical checks that emphasize observable evidence and operational behavior over mere documentation, ensuring that the control is properly implemented in the environment.
content:
Statement:
Route [Assignment: organization-defined internal communications traffic] to [Assignment: organization-defined external networks] through authenticated proxy servers at managed interfaces.

Guidance:
External networks are networks outside of organizational control. A proxy server is a server (i.e., system or application) that acts as an intermediary for clients requesting system resources from non-organizational or other organizational servers. System resources that may be requested include files, connections, web pages, or services. Client requests established through a connection to a proxy server are assessed to manage complexity and provide additional protection by limiting direct connectivity. Web content filtering devices are one of the most common proxy servers that provide access to the Internet. Proxy servers can support the logging of Transmission Control Protocol sessions and the blocking of specific Uniform Resource Locators, Internet Protocol addresses, and domain names. Web proxies can be configured with organization-defined lists of authorized and unauthorized websites. Note that proxy servers may inhibit the use of virtual private networks (VPNs) and create the potential for "man-in-the-middle" attacks (depending on the implementation).

title: SPLIT TUNNELING FOR REMOTE DEVICES
methodology:
Control SC-7(7): Split Tunneling for Remote Devices – Validation Methodology

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Examine VPN configuration screens and management consoles that clearly indicate disabled split tunneling options. Look for screenshots, system configuration management dashboards, or automated policy enforcement logs that confirm only secure, provisioned tunnels are in use.
• Technical Artifacts: Gather configuration files, registry settings, and scripts that lock down client VPN settings (e.g., group policy objects or mobile device management profiles). Audit logs from VPN concentrators and network appliances that reference allowed IP ranges for tunneled traffic serve as hard evidence.
• Operational Patterns: Monitor network flows and connection logs to ensure that all traffic from remote devices follows the designated secured VPN paths with no evidence of dual-homing or unauthorized external network access.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Faking Compliance: Organizations may present policy documentation or static VPN configuration printouts that claim split tunneling is disabled, but actual client configurations may be user-modifiable or inconsistent across endpoints.
• Surface-Level Implementations: Merely disabling user control without hard enforcement on the VPN gateway may allow technically split tunneling (e.g., users manually reconfiguring client devices on non-managed networks).
• Technical Gaps: Overlooked platform differences (like mismatches between Windows and macOS client configurations) or misconfigured safeguarded split tunnels (e.g., insufficiently restricted IP ranges) can create vulnerabilities.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Verify VPN Client Configuration Enforcement
WHY: Confirming that client VPN configurations are enforced and not user-alterable prevents unauthorized split tunneling.
HOW: Remotely connect to a sample endpoint, inspect the VPN client settings (via MDM or local configuration panels), and attempt to modify tunnel routing settings. Compare with centrally managed policies.
FAIL: If the settings are configurable by a user or revert to defaults allowing split tunneling.

CHECK: Inspect VPN Gateway Configuration and Policy Enforcement
WHY: Analyzing the VPN gateway ensures that network traffic is restricted to approved, secured channels despite any local device configurations.
HOW: Log into the VPN concentrator or gateway management console and review policies related to allowed traffic routes. Look for rules that enforce tunnel exclusivity and approved destination IP ranges.
FAIL: If rules are absent, too broad, or if logs indicate sessions where traffic bypassed the intended secure tunnel.

CHECK: Analyze Network Traffic and Flow Logs
WHY: Real operational traffic analysis provides concrete evidence that remote devices are not accessing external networks via unauthorized channels.
HOW: Use network monitoring tools (e.g., SIEM or packet capture systems) to analyze remote user traffic patterns. Verify that traffic goes exclusively through the secure VPN tunnel and check for anomalies indicative of split tunneling.
FAIL: Discovery of traffic flows with dual-path access or communications with unexpected external IP addresses.

CHECK: Confirm Automated Enforcement and Alerting Mechanisms
WHY: Automation reduces the window for misconfigurations and provides timely alerts on any deviations from enforced split tunneling policies.
HOW: Review alerting logs and system integration settings in your incident management tool. Trigger simulated events (or perform penetration tests) to see if unauthorized split tunneling attempts are automatically detected and blocked.
FAIL: Lack of automated alerts or the ability to bypass restrictions during simulated test conditions.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut Verification: Log onto a known remote device as a test user and immediately inspect the VPN client configuration panel for any visible options for split tunneling – if any such options appear active, it’s a red flag.
• Critical Indicator: Inconsistent application of settings across device types and operating systems is a major red flag. Always validate across a sample range of devices.
• Detail Matters: Pay close attention to the granular rules on the VPN gateway; the specific allowed IP addresses and protocols are key in verifying that any provisioned split tunnel is truly secure and limited only to approved destinations.
• Additional Insight: Consider running a network traceroute from a remote device to a known external address to ensure that it follows the secure tunnel path rather than attempting a direct route.

This methodology emphasizes observable, technical evidence and operational confirmation rather than relying solely on policy documentation. By following these steps, you ensure that split tunneling, when allowed, is securely provisioned and that unauthorized dual-path network access is reliably prevented.
content:
Statement:
Prevent split tunneling for remote devices connecting to organizational systems unless the split tunnel is securely provisioned using [Assignment: organization-defined safeguards].

Guidance:
Split tunneling is the process of allowing a remote user or device to establish a non-remote connection with a system and simultaneously communicate via some other connection to a resource in an external network. This method of network access enables a user to access remote devices and simultaneously, access uncontrolled networks. Split tunneling might be desirable by remote users to communicate with local system resources, such as printers or file servers. However, split tunneling can facilitate unauthorized external connections, making the system vulnerable to attack and to exfiltration of organizational information. Split tunneling can be prevented by disabling configuration settings that allow such capability in remote devices and by preventing those configuration settings from being configurable by users. Prevention can also be achieved by the detection of split tunneling (or configuration settings that allow split tunneling) in the remote device, and by prohibiting the connection if the remote device is using split tunneling. A virtual private network (VPN) can be used to securely provision a split tunnel. A securely provisioned VPN includes locking connectivity to exclusive, managed, and neutral environments, or to a specific set of pre-approved addresses, with user control.

title: RESPONSE TO RECOGNIZED FAILURES
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Visible evidence includes active monitoring systems that generate alerts when failures are recognized. Screen displays or dashboards showing real‐time incident logs with timestamped entries can provide this evidence.
• Technical artifacts include automated alert logs, incident response system records, and change management logs that correlate recognized failures with timely remediation actions.
• Operational patterns such as periodic failure simulations, post-incident reviews, and documented follow-up actions indicate that the response mechanism is functioning in practice.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations may simply ensure that alerts are generated without a corresponding, effective incident response process. For example, setting up alerts without any follow-up or remediation actions is a surface-level implementation.
• Reliance solely on documentation or policy without observable evidence in system logs or remediation actions. 
• Failure to correlate recognized failures with remedial changes in production environments, resulting in gap between recorded events and actual resolution.

VALIDATION STEPS:
1. CHECK: Verify that automated monitoring and alerting systems are actively logging recognized failures.
   WHY: Active alerts and logs demonstrate that recognized failures are detected and captured in a timely manner.
   HOW: Review monitoring dashboards and incident logs; check that timestamps and severity levels are recorded. Use automated queries to extract recent failure events.
   FAIL: Absence of recent logs of failures or lack of automation in logging these incidents.

2. CHECK: Confirm incident response processes by reviewing sample incident reports and remediation actions related to recognized failures.
   WHY: Evidence of documented responses shows that the control is not just theoretical but operationally active.
   HOW: Interview incident response teams and review incident reports for timelines, identified failures, and corresponding fix actions. Check for sign-offs in the change management system.
   FAIL: Incomplete or outdated incident reports, or evidence that corrective actions were not executed timely.

3. CHECK: Inspect records of simulation exercises or failure drills that test the recognition and response processes.
   WHY: Regular testing indicates continuous readiness and demonstrates operational effectiveness under failure scenarios.
   HOW: Obtain test plans, simulation logs, and after-action reports; verify exercise frequency and lessons learned are integrated into process improvements.
   FAIL: Lack of recent exercises, no simulation records, or drills that show significant delays in response actions.

4. CHECK: Evaluate change management and patching logs to ensure that recognized failures trigger timely remediation measures.
   WHY: Correlating alerts with subsequent remediation activities confirms that recognized failures lead to effective response and mitigation.
   HOW: Cross-check timeline data between detected failure events and subsequent patch or configuration update records in the change management system.
   FAIL: No correlation between failure logs and remediation actions; significant delays between recognition and response hint at process deficiencies.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Focus on cross-verifying alert logs with incident response records – ensuring that each recognized failure has an associated remedial action.
• Leverage centralized logging and automated dashboards to reduce reliance on manual records, which can be more easily faked.
• Look for evidence of continual improvement practices: updates to response procedures after incidents, and periodic testing that reveals lessons learned.
• A critical indicator of poor implementation is when failure simulations are not aligned with actual incident data – suggesting that improvements in the response process may be only on paper.

Note: Although SC-7(6) has been withdrawn and incorporated into SC-718, these checks remain valid for ensuring that the control of responding to recognized failures is effectively implemented.
content:
Statement:
[Withdrawn: Incorporated into SC-718.]

title: BOUNDARY PROTECTION
methodology:
Control ID: SC-7 – BOUNDARY PROTECTION

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Deployed firewall devices, routers, IDS/IPS appliances, and network segmentation architectures (e.g., DMZs). Network diagrams should clearly segregate public-accessible systems from internal ones.
• Technical Artifacts: Configuration files of boundary devices (firewalls, routers, VPN concentrators), continuously generated logs showing traffic filtering and monitoring, and documented traffic flow policies including approved exceptions.
• Operational Patterns: Regular monitoring of inbound/outbound traffic, automated alerts for anomalous activity (such as spoofing attempts), and periodic reviews of exceptions and route announcements (BGP, DNS, etc.).

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Fake Compliance: Relying solely on policy documents or static network diagrams without verifying live device configurations; using outdated or non-operational logging setups.
• Surface-Level Implementations: Simple segmentation labels on diagrams without ensuring actual logical/physical network separation; having firewalls configured with broad rules that do not enforce deny-by-default principles.
• Technical Gaps: Lack of proper filtering for control plane traffic (BGP, DNS management protocols), incomplete documentation of exceptions, and failure to monitor critical boundary interfaces in real time.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Review Configuration of Boundary Devices
WHY: Valid configurations on firewalls, routers, and IDS/IPS establish that traffic is being managed per the defined security architecture.
HOW: Retrieve and analyze current configuration files for rule sets that enforce a deny-all permit-by-exception approach; verify that managed interfaces have explicit traffic flow policies. Use automated configuration management tools (e.g., NIST CSF automated validators) to compare against compliance baselines.
FAIL: Absence of explicit deny-all rules, overly permissive configurations, or outdated rule sets indicate a failure in proper boundary protection.

CHECK: Verify Existence and Isolation of DMZ/Subnetwork
WHY: Effective network segmentation via physically or logically separated subnetworks minimizes the risk of lateral movement from externally accessible systems to internal resources.
HOW: Analyze network diagrams and conduct traceroute/ping tests to ensure that DMZ servers are on separate VLANs or subnets, distinct from core internal networks. Validate device configurations that enforce segmentation.
FAIL: Discovery that DMZ systems share the same subnetwork or routing domain as internal systems, or lack of clear segmentation controls.

CHECK: Examine Monitoring Logs and Alerting Mechanisms
WHY: Active and automated monitoring confirms that boundary protection devices are operationally effective and are detecting anomalous traffic (e.g., spoofed addresses or unauthorized control plane messages).
HOW: Access and review logs from firewall, IDS/IPS, and network management systems looking for evidence of denied traffic. Ensure that alerts for unexpected traffic patterns are configured and test fired.
FAIL: Sparse or nonexistent logs, missing alert configurations, or alert thresholds that have not been met because controls are not actively filtering or monitoring traffic.

CHECK: Assess Control Plane Traffic Protections
WHY: Protecting control plane traffic (BGP, DNS, management protocols) from unauthorized access is crucial to ensure stability and security of routing and network management.
HOW: Inspect configurations and network monitoring records for filters applied to control plane protocols. Confirm that routing protocols (BGP) and DNS traffic have specific rules and that any exceptions are documented. Review published information (where applicable) that enables remote detection of unauthorized control plane traffic.
FAIL: Presence of open or undocumented control plane pathways, or logs indicating frequent unauthorized control plane traffic that is not adequately filtered.

CHECK: Evaluate Exception Management and Review Process
WHY: A formal process for documenting and periodically reviewing exceptions ensures that boundary controls remain tight and only justified exceptions exist temporarily.
HOW: Request evidence of recent exception records, review meeting minutes or review logs that capture exception lifecycle management. Look for automation in exception tracking where exceptions are timestamped and linked to valid business cases.
FAIL: Exceptions that are outdated without review, undocumented deviations from traffic policies, or a process that relies solely on ad hoc handoffs without periodic auditing.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Use automated configuration scanners that cross-check firewall and routing policies against established compliance baselines. Look for mismatches in rule definitions that could indicate broad permit exceptions.
• Critical Indicator: The absence of up-to-date and granular logs for boundary devices is almost always a red flag. Ensure remote management interfaces are visible in real-time monitoring platforms.
• Important Detail: Even when DMZs or segregated networks exist on paper, verifying live traffic flow and actual physical/logical separations is essential. Tools that simulate attack traffic can sometimes reveal misconfigured segmentation.
• Best Practice: Confirm that policies for control plane traffic are not only documented but are continuously monitored with alerts enabled for any unusual behavior. This dynamic verification tends to expose weaknesses that static reviews might miss.

These practical steps, when executed holistically, help verify that boundary protection is not only documented but is an active, monitored, and effective element of the overall security posture.
content:
Statement:
a. Monitor and control communications at the external managed interfaces to the system and at key internal managed interfaces within the system;
b. Implement subnetworks for publicly accessible system components that are [Selection: physically; logically] separated from internal organizational networks; and
c. Connect to external networks or systems only through managed boundary protection devices arranged in accordance with an organizational security and privacy architecture.

Guidance:
Managed interfaces include gateways, routers, firewalls, guards, network-based malicious code analysis, virtualization systems, or encrypted tunnels implemented within a security architecture. Subnetworks that are physically or logically separated from internal networks are referred to as demilitarized zones or DMZs. Restricting or prohibiting interfaces within organizational systems includes restricting external web traffic to designated web servers within managed interfaces, prohibiting external traffic that appears to be spoofing internal addresses, and prohibiting internal traffic that appears to be spoofing external addresses. [SP 800-189] provides additional information on source address validation techniques to prevent ingress and egress of traffic with spoofed addresses. Commercial telecommunications services are provided by network components and consolidated management systems shared by customers. These services may also include third-party-provided access lines and other service elements. Boundary protection may be implemented as a common control for all or part of an organizational network such that the boundary to be protected is greater than a system-specific boundary (i.e., an authorization boundary).

Enhancements:
BOUNDARY PROTECTION | PHYSICALLY SEPARATED SUBNETWORKS
[Withdrawn: Incorporated into SC-7.]
BOUNDARY PROTECTION | PUBLIC ACCESS
[Withdrawn: Incorporated into SC-7.]
BOUNDARY PROTECTION | ACCESS POINTS
Limit the number of external network connections to the system.
Discussion: Limiting the number of external network connections facilitates monitoring of inbound and outbound communications traffic. The Trusted Internet Connection [DHS TIC] initiative is an example of a federal guideline that requires limits on the number of external network connections. Limiting the number of external network connections to the system is important during transition periods from older to newer technologies (e.g., transitioning from IPv4 to IPv6 network protocols). Such transitions may require implementing the older and newer technologies simultaneously during the transition period and thus increase the number of access points to the system.
BOUNDARY PROTECTION | EXTERNAL TELECOMMUNICATIONS SERVICES
(a) Implement a managed interface for each external telecommunication service;
(b) Establish a traffic flow policy for each managed interface;
(c) Protect the confidentiality and integrity of the information being transmitted across each interface;
(d) Document each exception to the traffic flow policy with a supporting mission or business need and duration of that need;
(e) Review exceptions to the traffic flow policy [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] and remove exceptions that are no longer supported by an explicit mission or business need;
(f) Prevent unauthorized exchange of control plane traffic with external networks;
(g) Publish information to enable remote networks to detect unauthorized control plane traffic from internal networks; and
(h) Filter unauthorized control plane traffic from external networks.
Discussion: External telecommunications services can provide data and/or voice communications services. Examples of control plane traffic include Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) routing, Domain Name System (DNS), and management protocols. See [SP 800-189] for additional information on the use of the resource public key infrastructure (RPKI) to protect BGP routes and detect unauthorized BGP announcements.
BOUNDARY PROTECTION | DENY BY DEFAULT — ALLOW BY EXCEPTION
Deny network communications traffic by default and allow network communications traffic by exception [Selection (one or more): at managed interfaces; for [Assignment: organization-defined systems]]. 
Discussion: Denying by default and allowing by exception applies to inbound and outbound network communications traffic. A deny-all, permit-by-exception network communications traffic policy ensures that only those system connections that are essential and approved are allowed.

title: RESOURCE AVAILABILITY
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Look for operational dashboards, system configuration interfaces, or monitoring tools that clearly show resource allocation settings (such as CPU/memory quotas or priority classifications) in real-time. Screenshots, live dashboards, and system configuration files can all be evidence.
• Technical Artifacts: Configuration files for resource schedulers (e.g., cgroups in Linux, Windows Job Objects), logs showing quota enforcement, and system audit logs that capture resource limits hit or adjusted. Additionally, use of orchestration platforms (such as Kubernetes resource limits/requests) can support compliance.
• Operational Patterns: Regular monitoring of resource usage, documented performance baselines for high-priority processes, and automated alerts when quotas or priority thresholds are breached. Periodic reports from automated resource management tools also indicate active enforcement.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Faked Compliance: Organizations may simply document policies on resource availability without showing integration into running systems. They could provide screenshots of control panels that are not actively enforced or out-of-date configurations.
• Surface-level Implementations: Default settings that are not tuned to actual operational loads, or policies that exist only in management documentation without corresponding change in scheduler settings. Relying on OS defaults without custom resource allocation can give a false impression.
• Common Gaps: Lack of integration between monitoring systems and resource allocation configurations; failure to periodically review and adjust quotas when business needs change; missing logs or absent automated enforcement reports.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Verify that resource allocation configurations (e.g., CPU/memory quotas and process priorities) are implemented in the system’s scheduler or hypervisor settings.
   WHY: These configurations provide the tangible enforcement mechanism for ensuring resource availability as per priority needs.
   HOW: Retrieve configuration files, query system APIs or dashboard settings (for example, check Linux cgroups settings or Windows System resource manager configs) and validate consistency with policy.
   FAIL: Absence of custom resource control settings or presence of default/unmodified OS configurations.

2. CHECK: Inspect system and audit logs for evidence of automated enforcement and alerts related to quota breaches or priority adjustments.
   WHY: Operational logs that capture resource throttling or prioritization actions validate that the controls are actively managed in response to events.
   HOW: Pull audit logs from resource management tools or operating system event logs for relevant events over a defined time period and verify that alerts/actions correspond with quotas or priority violations.
   FAIL: Sparse or missing logs indicating enforcement actions, or logs that only show administrative overrides without automated events.

3. CHECK: Perform a controlled test by simulating resource-intensive processes and observing if high-priority processes retain expected performance levels while lower-priority processes are throttled.
   WHY: A real-time test confirms that the control is not just configured but also operationally effective in preserving resource availability.
   HOW: Execute a test where multiple processes are launched; monitor system performance metrics (like CPU/memory usage) and verify that high-priority processes remain unaffected by lower-priority load.
   FAIL: If high-priority processes suffer performance degradation or if there is no distinction in treatment between processes.

4. CHECK: Review automated reporting and monitoring tools for regular health and compliance reports on resource allocations and quota utilization.
   WHY: Consistent and automated reporting indicates that the control is integrated into the operational framework and monitored on an ongoing basis.
   HOW: Access reports from the central monitoring/dashboard system, check historical trends against defined thresholds, and verify the presence of automated alerts.
   FAIL: Infrequent or manually generated reports, or absence of threshold/alert mechanisms on resource usage.

EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Directly query system APIs (e.g., using “systemctl” on Linux for cgroups or Windows PowerShell scripts for resource allocations) to pull real-time configuration data rather than relying solely on static documentation.
• Critical Indicators: Non-updated configurations or absence of log entries during peak load times are key red flags. Ensure dynamic adjustments and historical consistency in resource availability reports.
• Details Matter: Verify that controls are not only present but tailored to the organization-defined resource needs. Look for evidence of periodic reviews and adjustments in resource allocation strategies rather than one-time configurations.
content:
Statement:
Protect the availability of resources by allocating [Assignment: organization-defined resources] by [Selection (one or more): priority; quota; [Assignment: organization-defined controls]].

Guidance:
Priority protection prevents lower-priority processes from delaying or interfering with the system that services higher-priority processes. Quotas prevent users or processes from obtaining more than predetermined amounts of resources.

title: DENIAL-OF-SERVICE PROTECTION
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Visible evidence includes active network boundary devices (e.g., firewalls, IDS/IPS) with live filtering rules specific to denial-of-service (DoS) events, monitoring dashboards showing traffic anomalies, and redundancy/load balancing configurations in production.
• Technical artifacts include configuration files from firewalls and load balancers, SIEM logs evidencing DoS attack detections, performance graphs and stress-test reports, and documented capacity planning measures.
• Operational patterns reveal automatic detection and alerting on abnormal traffic, routine log reviews, and periodic testing of DoS protection controls.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations may simply adopt a policy statement without updating firewall rule sets or monitoring thresholds, creating a compliance paper trail that lacks real, functioning technical controls.
• Superficial measures such as increasing bandwidth without proper filtering or anomaly detection might mask vulnerabilities.
• Reliance solely on documentation or scheduled tests without live monitoring can give a false sense of security.

VALIDATION STEPS:

CHECK: Verify filtering and blocking rules on boundary protection devices (firewalls, IDS/IPS).
WHY: Active and correctly configured filtering rules are a primary defense against inbound DoS attack traffic.
HOW: Inspect firewall/IDS configuration files, review rule sets against the organization-defined DoS events, and cross-check live logs for evidence of filtering actions; if possible, simulate benign traffic spikes to observe rule behavior.
FAIL: Missing, outdated, or misconfigured filtering rules; absence of corresponding log entries when attack signatures are simulated.

CHECK: Validate load balancing, capacity management, and redundancy measures.
WHY: These measures mitigate the impact of DoS attacks by ensuring that no single system is overwhelmed and that alternative pathways are available.
HOW: Review network capacity planning documents alongside real-time performance graphs, analyze load balancer logs, and examine results from recent stress tests. Confirm that these configurations match the defined parameters for handling DoS events.
FAIL: Lack of documented redundancy measures, absence of automated load balancing, or poor performance under artificially induced high-traffic conditions.

CHECK: Verify the deployment and effectiveness of DoS detection and monitoring tools.
WHY: Proactive monitoring is essential for the early identification and mitigation of DoS attacks.
HOW: Examine SIEM dashboards and logs for alerts related to DoS events, review configurations of the monitoring tools to ensure thresholds are set according to organization-defined DoS types, and check incident reports for follow-up actions on alerted events.
FAIL: Incomplete or unmonitored logging, misconfigured alert thresholds, or absence of historical alert data related to DoS incidents.

CHECK: Audit controls restricting the ability of internal/external actors from launching DoS attacks.
WHY: Effective controls should not only defend against incoming DoS attacks but also prevent your systems from being used as staging points for outbound attacks.
HOW: Review outbound filtering rules, inspect egress traffic logs, and interview security personnel regarding testing or simulations conducted for insider threat scenarios. Validate that policies are enforced by automated controls.
FAIL: Loosely enforced egress monitoring, misconfigured internal controls allowing unrestricted outbound traffic, or absence of audit trails for attempted misuse of system resources.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Shortcut: Leverage automated network analysis tools to verify that real-time traffic anomalies correlate with the activation of filtering, load balancing, and alert generation.
• Critical Indicator: Recurring alerts without remediation or frequent policy exceptions in firewall configurations can indicate poor implementation and monitoring.
• Note: Emphasize the review of live system logs and real-time performance data over static documentation. If possible, observe a live simulation test to validate the control’s effective response.
content:
Statement:
a.  [Selection: Protect against; Limit] the effects of the following types of denial-of-service events: [Assignment: organization-defined types of denial-of-service events]; and
 
b.  Employ the following controls to achieve the denial-of-service objective: [Assignment: organization-defined controls by type of denial-of-service event].

Guidance:
Denial-of-service events may occur due to a variety of internal and external causes, such as an attack by an adversary or a lack of planning to support organizational needs with respect to capacity and bandwidth. Such attacks can occur across a wide range of network protocols (e.g., IPv4, IPv6). A variety of technologies are available to limit or eliminate the origination and effects of denial-of-service events. For example, boundary protection devices can filter certain types of packets to protect system components on internal networks from being directly affected by or the source of denial-of-service attacks. Employing increased network capacity and bandwidth combined with service redundancy also reduces the susceptibility to denial-of-service events.

Enhancements:
DENIAL-OF-SERVICE PROTECTION | RESTRICT ABILITY TO ATTACK OTHER SYSTEMS
Restrict the ability of individuals to launch the following denial-of-service attacks against other systems: [Assignment: organization-defined denial-of-service attacks].
 
Discussion: Restricting the ability of individuals to launch denial-of-service attacks requires the mechanisms commonly used for such attacks to be unavailable. Individuals of concern include hostile insiders or external adversaries who have breached or compromised the system and are using it to launch a denial-of-service attack. Organizations can restrict the ability of individuals to connect and transmit arbitrary information on the transport medium (i.e., wired networks, wireless networks, spoofed Internet protocol packets). Organizations can also limit the ability of individuals to use excessive system resources. Protection against individuals having the ability to launch denial-of-service attacks may be implemented on specific systems or boundary devices that prohibit egress to potential target systems.
DENIAL-OF-SERVICE PROTECTION | CAPACITY, BANDWIDTH, AND REDUNDANCY
Manage capacity, bandwidth, or other redundancy to limit the effects of information flooding denial-of-service attacks.
 
Discussion: Managing capacity ensures that sufficient capacity is available to counter flooding attacks. Managing capacity includes establishing selected usage priorities, quotas, partitioning, or load balancing.
DENIAL-OF-SERVICE PROTECTION | DETECTION AND MONITORING
(a) Employ the following monitoring tools to detect indicators of denial-of-service attacks against, or launched from, the system: [Assignment: organization-defined monitoring tools]; and
(b) Monitor the following system resources to determine if sufficient resources exist to prevent effective denial-of-service attacks: [Assignment: organization-defined system resources].

title: INFORMATION IN SHARED SYSTEM RESOURCES
methodology:
Control SC-4 focuses on preventing unauthorized and unintended information transfer via shared system resources, ensuring that any residual data from previous users, roles, or processes is not available to subsequent entities. Below is a complete validation methodology:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: Look for system configuration settings that mandate memory and storage sanitization on release. Verify that system screens, audit dashboards, or administrative consoles show active enforcement of object reuse policies.
   • Technical Artifacts: Check for automated logs and reports indicating successful clearing of shared buffers, caches, and memory segments. Validate that the sanitization routines are triggered automatically, documented in system manifests or configuration files (e.g., secure zeroization routines). Evidence of cryptographic key regeneration for encrypted resources also supports compliance.
   • Operational Patterns: Observe periodic system reinitialization triggered by process lifecycle events, periodic memory audits, or automated notifications when shared resources are reallocated. Repeated execution of cleanup scripts or kernel-level sanitization tasks is a strong indicator.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Faked Compliance: Organizations may show documentation or policy statements about sanitization without demonstrating that the cleaning routines are actively enforced at runtime.
   • Surface-Level Implementations: Relying solely on encryption without actively reinitializing or clearing resource memory can leave residual data accessible if keys or states persist across sessions.
   • Technical Gaps: Overlooking non-volatile storage areas, caches, or transient memory segments often leads to failures. Some organizations implement sanitization only for high-level processes and neglect shared lower-level buffers used by kernel or virtual memory.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Validate that shared system memory buffers and caches are sanitized upon process termination or reuse.
   WHY: This demonstrates that residual data is actively removed, preventing unauthorized access by subsequent processes/users.
   HOW: Review system or kernel configuration files and security logs to confirm that sanitization routines (e.g., zeroization, reinitialization scripts) are executed. Interview administrators and verify automation triggers that purge residual data.
   FAIL: Absence of automated logs or evidence that buffers/caches are cleared, coupled with lingering data in test environments using memory dump analysis.

   CHECK: Verify encryption key regeneration and sanitization for encrypted shared resources.
   WHY: Ensuring encryption keys are not reused or left in memory conditions protects against residual data exposure from encrypted artifacts.
   HOW: Review cryptographic module configurations and audit logs for key lifecycle management. Test shared resource accesses before and after key regeneration to ensure that prior encrypted data is not recoverable.
   FAIL: Static encryption setups that leave keys or data in memory across sessions, or lack of automated key lifecycle management logs.

   CHECK: Assess the implementation of system resource reuse policies in multilevel or periods processing environments.
   WHY: This is critical when processing switches between different information classification levels, ensuring that hardware reuse does not leak residual classified data.
   HOW: Evaluate approved procedures, inspect system logs for level-switch operations, and verify that sanitization procedures are applied when hardware components or shared resources transition between different classification contexts.
   FAIL: Evidence of level transitions without associated sanitization events or anomalous logs indicating reuse of resources across classification boundaries.

   (Optional) CHECK: Perform a controlled test by simulating termination of a process that utilizes shared resources and then initiating a new process. Monitor for any residual data exposure.
   WHY: Real-time validation that the implemented controls are actively cleansing data between process sessions.
   HOW: Use forensic memory analysis tools to capture system memory before and after termination; compare data remnants in shared areas.
   FAIL: Discovery of residual data correlating with the terminated process indicates ineffective sanitization routines.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Verification Shortcuts: Review system and audit logs over a sustained period to detect any anomalies where resource cleanup might have failed. Automated log correlation tools can quickly pinpoint deviations.
   • Critical Indicators: Focus on areas where system resource reuse is common (e.g., shared memory segments in multi-tenant environments) and inspect any anomalies in memory dumps or timing logs during level transitions.
   • Important Details: The actual automation of sanitization routines and dynamic key management are essential. Over-reliance on policy or manual cleanup often signals weak enforcement, so prioritize empirical tests over documentation.

In summary, these checks verify operational effectiveness by examining real-time logs, configuration settings, and dynamic memory management, ensuring that shared system resources are truly sanitized between uses and that encrypted resources are managed securely. This practical approach minimizes the possibility of residual data being accessed by unauthorized parties.
content:
Statement:
Prevent unauthorized and unintended information transfer via shared system resources.

Guidance:
Preventing unauthorized and unintended information transfer via shared system resources stops information produced by the actions of prior users or roles (or the actions of processes acting on behalf of prior users or roles) from being available to current users or roles (or current processes acting on behalf of current users or roles) that obtain access to shared system resources after those resources have been released back to the system. Information in shared system resources also applies to encrypted representations of information. In other contexts, control of information in shared system resources is referred to as object reuse and residual information protection. Information in shared system resources does not address information remanence, which refers to the residual representation of data that has been nominally deleted; covert channels (including storage and timing channels), where shared system resources are manipulated to violate information flow restrictions; or components within systems for which there are only single users or roles.

Enhancements:
INFORMATION IN SHARED SYSTEM RESOURCES | SECURITY LEVELS [Withdrawn: Incorporated into SC-4.]
INFORMATION IN SHARED SYSTEM RESOURCES | MULTILEVEL OR PERIODS PROCESSING Prevent unauthorized information transfer via shared resources in accordance with [Assignment: organization-defined procedures] when system processing explicitly switches between different information classification levels or security categories. Discussion: Changes in processing levels can occur during multilevel or periods processing with information at different classification levels or security categories. It can also occur during serial reuse of hardware components at different classification levels. Organization-defined procedures can include approved sanitization processes for electronically stored information.

title: SECURITY FUNCTION ISOLATION
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Visible evidence includes clear system architecture diagrams or configuration screens that delineate security functions (e.g., security kernel, dedicated modules) from nonsecurity functions.
• Technical artifacts such as hardware configuration settings (processor rings, mode registers), code segmentation metadata, file system permissions on security-critical components, and access control lists.
• Operational patterns like strict privilege separation in execution logs and system call traces that show only authorized modules accessing security functionality, along with evidence of minimal nonsecurity code within the security boundary.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Relying solely on documentation or policy declarations without hard enforcement in the underlying system: e.g., indicating separation in architecture documents, while the actual implementation has loosely coupled modules integrated together.
• Faking compliance by using generic file system permissions or relying on virtual segmentation that does not enforce hardware or kernel-level separation.
• Implementing visible separation only in the design phase while permitting runtime interactions between modules that should be isolated.

VALIDATION STEPS:
1. CHECK: Verify system architecture diagrams and configuration settings for clearly defined isolation boundaries between security and nonsecurity functions.
   WHY: These diagrams and settings provide a blueprint demonstrating that an architectural separation has been planned and implemented.
   HOW: Examine system technical architecture documentation; inspect configuration screens in system management consoles; and review any system security policies. Also, cross-check against operational logs showing enforced boundaries.
   FAIL: Absence of clear documentation, diagrams, or evidence that configuration enforces separate partitions, or if operational logs indicate frequent cross-boundary interactions.

2. CHECK: Assess hardware-enforced isolation mechanisms (e.g., processor rings, distinct execution modes).
   WHY: Hardware separation (processor rings/modes) is difficult to fake and guarantees that critical security code operates with higher privileges than nonsecurity functions.
   HOW: Access the system’s hardware configuration details using system diagnostics or secure boot logs; verify that the security kernel runs in a distinct, higher-privilege ring than nonsecurity tasks through inspection of low-level system settings and registers.
   FAIL: Uniform privilege levels across modules or absence/incorrect configuration of hardware rings that should enforce isolation.

3. CHECK: Test file system and address space protections for security function code.
   WHY: File system protections and memory isolation prevent unauthorized modifications and access to security functions.
   HOW: Review file and directory permissions specifically for files containing security functions; attempt controlled read/write tests from non-authorized accounts; use automated vulnerability scanners to check for misconfigurations.
   FAIL: Overly permissive file permissions, or evidence that non-privileged processes can access or modify security function code.

4. CHECK: Review logging and monitoring evidence for enforcement of access control between modules.
   WHY: Continuous monitoring reveals whether the isolation boundary is actively enforced and alerts are generated when nonsecurity functions attempt unauthorized interactions with security functions.
   HOW: Analyze access logs, intrusion detection system reports, and audit trails for any events indicating breaches of the isolation boundary; confirm that alerts are triggered on unauthorized attempts.
   FAIL: Lack of logging detail, absence of alerts for suspected cross-boundary interactions, or indications that nonsecurity functions are inadvertently given access to security functions.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Shortcut: Focus on dynamic, automated evidence like real-time access logs and system call traces rather than relying solely on static documentation.
• Critical indicator: The presence of hardware-level isolation (processor rings/modes) is one of the strongest assurances that security functions are truly isolated.
• Detail matters: Ensure that file system permissions specifically target security modules, and that any maintenance or emergency access procedures do not inadvertently disable the isolation boundary.
• Be wary of systems that claim segregation only on paper – verify by testing with controlled exploits to confirm that separation is functionally enforced under real-world conditions.

This methodology emphasizes verifying operational effectiveness through observable, automated checks and live system behavior instead of relying on superficial documents or laboratory settings.
content:
## Control
Isolate security functions from nonsecurity functions.

## Discussion
Security functions are isolated from nonsecurity functions by means of an isolation boundary implemented within a system via partitions and domains. The isolation boundary controls access to and protects the integrity of the hardware, software, and firmware that perform system security functions. Systems implement code separation in many ways, such as through the provision of security kernels via processor rings or processor modes. For non-kernel code, security function isolation is often achieved through file system protections that protect the code on disk and address space protections that protect executing code. Systems can restrict access to security functions using access control mechanisms and by implementing least privilege capabilities. 

While the ideal is for all code within the defined security function isolation boundary to only contain security-relevant code, it is sometimes necessary to include nonsecurity functions as an exception. The isolation of security functions from nonsecurity functions can be achieved by applying the systems security engineering design principles in SA-8, including SA-8(1), SA-8(3), SA-8(4), SA-8(10), SA-8(12), SA-8(13), SA-8(14), and SA-8(18).

Enhancements:
SECURITY FUNCTION ISOLATION | HARDWARE SEPARATION
Employ hardware separation mechanisms to implement security function isolation. Discussion: Hardware separation mechanisms include hardware ring architectures that are implemented within microprocessors and hardware-enforced address segmentation used to support logically distinct storage objects with separate attributes (i.e., readable, writable). Related Controls: None.
SECURITY FUNCTION ISOLATION | ACCESS AND FLOW CONTROL FUNCTIONS
Isolate security functions enforcing access and information flow control from nonsecurity functions and from other security functions. Discussion: Security function isolation occurs because of implementation. The functions must still be scanned and monitored. Security functions that are potentially isolated from access and flow control enforcement functions include auditing, intrusion detection, and malicious code protection functions. Related Controls: None.
SECURITY FUNCTION ISOLATION | MINIMIZE NONSECURITY FUNCTIONALITY
Minimize the number of nonsecurity functions included within the isolation boundary containing security functions. Discussion: Where it is not feasible to achieve strict isolation of nonsecurity functions from security functions, it is necessary to take actions to minimize nonsecurity-relevant functions within the security function boundary. Nonsecurity functions contained within the isolation boundary are considered security-relevant because errors or malicious code in the software can directly impact the security functions of systems. The fundamental design objective is that the specific portions of systems that provide information security are of minimal size and complexity. Minimizing the number of nonsecurity functions in the security-relevant boundary also allows designers and implementers to focus only on those functions which are necessary to provide the desired security capability (typically access enforcement). By minimizing the nonsecurity functions within the isolation boundaries, the amount of code that is trusted to enforce security policies is significantly reduced, thus contributing to understandability. Related Controls: None.
SECURITY FUNCTION ISOLATION | MODULE COUPLING AND COHESIVENESS
Implement security functions as largely independent modules that maximize internal cohesiveness within modules and minimize coupling between modules. Discussion: The reduction of inter-module interactions helps to constrain security functions and manage complexity. The concepts of coupling and cohesion are important with respect to modularity in software design. Coupling refers to the dependencies that one module has on other modules. Cohesion refers to the relationship between functions within a module. Best practices in software engineering and systems security engineering rely on layering.
SECURITY FUNCTION ISOLATION | LAYERED STRUCTURES
Implement security functions as a layered structure minimizing interactions between layers of the design and avoiding any dependence by lower layers on the functionality or correctness of higher layers. Discussion: The implementation of layered structures with minimized interactions among security functions and non-looping layers (i.e., lower-layer functions do not depend on higher-layer functions) enables the isolation of security functions and the management of complexity. Related Controls: None.

title: SEPARATION OF SYSTEM AND USER FUNCTIONALITY
methodology:
Control: SC-2, SEPARATION OF SYSTEM AND USER FUNCTIONALITY

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: Administrative consoles, separate login portals, or distinct network addresses for management interfaces vs. standard user interfaces.
   • Technical Artifacts: Network diagrams showing segmented management networks, authentication configurations that clearly separate admin vs. user credentials, VM configurations that allocate distinct virtual machines for management functions, and firewall rules enforcing compartmentalization.
   • Operational Patterns: Regular alerts or logs verifying that only privileged accounts access management interfaces, use of multi-factor authentication for system management accounts, and audit trails demonstrating successful isolation between user and administrative sessions.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Fake Compliance: Organizations might simply relabel management interfaces without applying real isolation; using the same IP range or login credentials for both user and admin functions.
   • Surface-Level Implementations: Soft segmentation by logical labeling in UI rather than enforcing technical network segmentation or using the same server instance with different user roles but insufficient separation.
   • Technical Gaps: Missing or improperly configured firewall/ACL rules; inadequate logging of management interface access; and lack of multi-factor authentication distinct from standard user logins.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify network segmentation between user facing systems and system management interfaces.
   WHY: Ensures that management functions are isolated and not accessible via the same network paths as user functions.
   HOW: Scan network boundaries and review network diagrams; test reachability of management interfaces from user segments using automated network scanners.
   FAIL: If management interfaces are reachable from user segments or if network diagrams show no physical/logical separation.

   CHECK: Confirm that administrative interfaces require separate and elevated authentication (e.g., multi-factor authentication) distinct from user authentication.
   WHY: Provides evidence that a different and higher level of security is enforced for system management functions, mitigating risk of privilege escalation.
   HOW: Validate authentication configurations on management consoles; review login procedures and authentication logs for administrative actions.
   FAIL: If admin and user authentications use identical processes or credentials, or if MFA is missing on management endpoints.

   CHECK: Inspect configuration and access control lists (ACLs)/firewall rules which enforce separation of system and user functionalities.
   WHY: Enforced technical controls are necessary for limiting management interfaces to authorized users only.
   HOW: Review firewall rules, ACLs, and access logs to verify that only specific administrative networks or IP addresses can access management interfaces.
   FAIL: If ACLs/firewall rules are overly permissive and allow general user network access to management functionality.

   CHECK: Evaluate documentation and configurations for virtualization or dedicated hardware implementing management functions separately.
   WHY: Demonstrates that the separation is implemented by using technical means such as separate operating system instances or virtual machines.
   HOW: Review virtualization platform configurations, audit logs, and system diagrams from administration consoles ensuring that no overlap with user interfaces exists.
   FAIL: If system management functions share the same VM or physical hardware without clear boundaries.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Verification Shortcuts: Use network scanning tools like nmap to quickly identify which IP addresses host system management ports and confirm they are not accessible from user zones.
   • Critical Indicators: Look at detailed firewall and access logs — frequent access attempts from unexpected IP ranges to management interfaces can indicate poor isolation.
   • Important Details: Ensure that web administrative interfaces are isolated on separate domains with dedicated authentication; mere URL alterations or subdirectories are insufficient.
   • Additional Observations: Verify regular reviews and audits of network segmentation and interface access controls to ensure ongoing compliance with SC-2.

This methodology emphasizes practical, automated verifications and analysis of configuration, logs, and network design rather than mere policy review, crucial for ensuring robust separation of system management and user functionalities.
content:
Statement:
Separate user functionality, including user interface services, from system management functionality.

Guidance:
System management functionality includes functions that are necessary to administer databases, network components, workstations, or servers. These functions typically require privileged user access. The separation of user functions from system management functions is physical or logical. Organizations may separate system management functions from user functions by using different computers, instances of operating systems, central processing units, or network addresses; by employing virtualization techniques; or some combination of these or other methods. Separation of system management functions from user functions includes web administrative interfaces that employ separate authentication methods for users of any other system resources. Separation of system and user functions may include isolating administrative interfaces on different domains and with additional access controls. The separation of system and user functionality can be achieved by applying the systems security engineering design principles in SA-8, including SA-8(1), SA-8(3), SA-8(4), SA-8(10), SA-8(12), SA-8(13), SA-8(14), and SA-8(18).

title: POLICY AND PROCEDURES
methodology:
Below is a complete methodology to validate the implementation of SC-1 (Policy and Procedures) in a practical audit:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • A formally documented system and communications protection policy exists – published on the intranet or management repository, complete with clearly defined purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, and coordination mechanisms.
   • Evidence of policy dissemination, such as training records, distribution lists, and acknowledgment receipts from organization-defined personnel or roles.
   • Documented procedures that not only restate the policy but also outline step-by-step implementation for the controls (e.g., instructions in security plans or specific system configuration guides).
   • A designated official (or role) managing policy development, updates, and communication, with records of their responsibilities and periodic review activities.
   • Review and update logs (version control, change management records) triggered by defined events (e.g., audit findings, law changes, or security incidents).

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations may fob off compliance by simply having a generic security policy that barely mentions communications or system protection without detailed role-specific procedures.
   • Some may produce outdated documents without evidence of periodic review or linking policy changes to triggering events.
   • Relying solely on high-level policy documents without an integrated set of procedures or evidence that employees have been briefed or trained.
   • Claims of a designated official exist on paper, but no active role or log of review actions is present.
   • Policies which are only stored on a file server with no evidence of controlled distribution or active enforcement.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify the existence of an officially approved system and communications protection policy document.
     WHY: An approved and published document demonstrates management commitment and provides the baseline for protective controls.
     HOW: Access the policy repository, review the document header for approval dates, authorized signatories, and reference to applicable laws and guidelines. Check automated distribution via an internal portal.
     FAIL: A missing or outdated document; no official review or sign-off marker.

   CHECK: Confirm the presence and currency of documented procedures linked to the policy.
     WHY: Well-documented procedures indicate the practical steps that bridge policy into operational activities.
     HOW: Locate procedures in the security and privacy plans; verify that they detail implementation procedures and list responsibilities. Cross-reference change logs for review frequency and event-triggered updates.
     FAIL: Procedures are vague, missing, or do not reference key implementation steps.

   CHECK: Inspect evidence of policy dissemination and personnel training/onboarding practices.
     WHY: Demonstrates that the controlled information is not just documented but actually communicated to the appropriate personnel.
     HOW: Review training records, email acknowledgments, or automated system logs that capture policy access; interview a sample of employees in key roles.
     FAIL: No record of training sessions, reports of personnel unaware of the policy, or absence of acknowledgment receipts.

   CHECK: Validate the role and activities of the designated official responsible for managing the policy.
     WHY: Regular oversight ensures that policy and procedures are continuously updated and aligned with regulatory changes and operational events.
     HOW: Interview the designated official, review their meeting minutes, task logs, and incident response follow-ups that refer to policy reviews.
     FAIL: The designated official’s role is undocumented, inactive, or shows no evidence of policy update activities.

   CHECK: Examine version control/change management logs for the policy and procedures repository.
     WHY: An operational change management process shows proactive policy maintenance and response to events (e.g., audit findings, security incidents).
     HOW: Review system logs, change management software, or document management systems for timestamps that align with review cycles and triggered events.
     FAIL: Missing or static version history, no recorded updates despite changes in internal/external conditions.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcuts: Start with the document repository and change logs because they offer automated evidence of reviews and updates that can't be easily faked.
   • Indicators of Poor Implementation: Outdated approval dates, lack of specific dissemination evidence, and procedures that merely repeat policy text without operational guidance.
   • Details that Matter Most: The linkage between the policy, procedures, and actual training/operational evidence; active management by the designated official; and clear change triggers evidenced by a disciplined version control process.
   • Be cautious of red flags such as generic security policies which do not specifically articulate communications protection; these typically indicate a paper exercise with little operational enforcement.
   • Validate by cross-referencing interviews with document evidence – discrepancies between claimed practices and actual updates are a crucial indicator of non-compliance.
content:
Statement:
a.  Develop, document, and disseminate to [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles]:  
   1.  [Selection (one or more): Organization-level; Mission/business process-level; System-level] system and communications protection policy that:  
     (a)  Addresses purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, coordination among organizational entities, and compliance; and  
     (b)  Is consistent with applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines; and  
   2.  Procedures to facilitate the implementation of the system and communications protection policy and the associated system and communications protection controls;  

b.  Designate an [Assignment: organization-defined official] to manage the development, documentation, and dissemination of the system and communications protection policy and procedures; and  

c.  Review and update the current system and communications protection:
