   1.  Policy [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] and following [Assignment: organization-defined events]; and  
   2.  Procedures [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] and following [Assignment: organization-defined events].

Guidance:
System and communications protection policy and procedures address the controls in the SC family that are implemented within systems and organizations. The risk management strategy is an important factor in establishing such policies and procedures. Policies and procedures contribute to security and privacy assurance. Therefore, it is important that security and privacy programs collaborate on the development of system and communications protection policy and procedures. Security and privacy program policies and procedures at the organization level are preferable, in general, and may obviate the need for mission- or system-specific policies and procedures. The policy can be included as part of the general security and privacy policy or can be established for security and privacy programs, for mission or business processes, and for systems, if needed. Procedures describe how the policies or controls are implemented and can be directed at the individual or role that is the object of the procedure. Procedures can be documented in system security and privacy plans or in one or more separate documents. Events that may precipitate an update to system and communications protection policy and procedures include assessment or audit findings, security incidents or breaches, or changes in applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines. Simply restating controls does not constitute an organizational policy or procedure.

title: SPECIALIZATION
methodology:
Control SA-23 (SPECIALIZATION) requires that organizations employ design, modification, augmentation, or reconfiguration on critical systems to boost their trustworthiness. Below is a practical methodology for validating this control:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Documentation showing approved architecture modifications, design review artifacts, change management records detailing specialized enhancements, and diagrams highlighting augmented security components.
• Technical Artifacts: Source code changes or configuration files that include additional authentication, non-repudiation modules, or specialized security logic; logs from automated security tools verifying the applied modifications; and version control history indicating targeted changes on mission-essential systems.
• Operational Patterns: Routine use of the enhanced configurations in security-critical processes (e.g., supplemental authentication processes or added audit trails in critical systems) and evidence of periodic review or testing specifically related to these specialized features.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Faked Compliance: Organizations might provide static documentation or policy statements without demonstrating any hands-on modifications. They might also showcase outdated change logs or superficial design documents that don’t reflect current system configurations.
• Surface-Level Implementations: Simply attaching a security add-on without integrating it into a broader trust framework; patching systems without periodic confirmation of functionality; or relying heavily on off-the-shelf solutions without custom tailoring to mission essential functions.
• Frequently Missed Technical Gaps: Incomplete reviews of how augmentation impacts the overall security posture, lack of simulated attack tests on the modified components, or inadequate logging to verify ongoing operational effectiveness.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK 1: Verify that mission-essential systems have documented design enhancements
WHY: Documentation such as design reviews, change management records, and updated architectural diagrams confirm that enhancements have been purposefully applied.
HOW: Inspect change logs, system architecture documents, and design review meeting minutes; compare pre- and post-enhancement versions.
FAIL: Absence of updated design artifacts or design documents that fail to address specialized enhancements.

CHECK 2: Review technical artifacts showing actual modifications or additions
WHY: Code repositories, configuration files, or automated deployment scripts that include specialized security functions indicate real-world implementation.
HOW: Access version control systems, examine commit messages for security enhancement tags, and review configuration management databases (CMDB) for proof of specialized settings.
FAIL: No evidence of targeted modifications in change history or configuration files remaining unchanged since baseline implementations.

CHECK 3: Validate operational effectiveness via security logs and monitoring
WHY: Active monitoring logs demonstrating the utilization of added security functions (e.g., supplemental authentication attempts, non-repudiation logs) confirm the control’s operational status.
HOW: Analyze security incident logs and audit logs; verify that enhanced components are producing expected outcomes in real-world usage.
FAIL: Lack of expected log entries or monitoring reports that indicate the absence or malfunction of specialized functions.

CHECK 4: Perform independent testing or review of enhanced security functions
WHY: Independent testing (e.g., penetration testing or simulated attack scenarios) confirms that the specialized enhancements are not merely documented but are effective in strengthening trustworthiness.
HOW: Examine third-party assessments, recent penetration test results, or perform targeted review sessions with the system owners emphasizing the specialized enhancements.
FAIL: Testing results that indicate the enhancements are bypassable, non-operational, or ineffective in mitigating identified threats.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut Verification: Quickly cross-reference design and change-management documentation with runtime logs; inconsistencies often expose superficial implementation.
• Critical Indicators: Focus on the correlation between documented design changes and live system behavior; any discrepancy suggests that the specialized functions are not effectively deployed.
• Detail Focus: Look closely at commit messages and logs for references to security-specific jargon (e.g., non-repudiation, additional authentication) and confirm that they match operational data. 
• Practical Advice: Engage with system operators to verify that they actively rely on these specialized functions in day-to-day operations, ensuring the change is not just theoretical.

This multi-pronged approach emphasizes both documentary and operational evidence, making it challenging to merely fake compliance and ensuring that the system enhancement truly bolsters trustworthiness.
content:
Statement:
Employ [Selection (one or more): design; modification; augmentation; reconfiguration] on [Assignment: organization-defined systems or system components] supporting mission-essential services or functions to increase the trustworthiness in those systems or components.

Guidance:
It is often necessary for a system or system component that supports mission-essential services or functions to be enhanced to maximize the trustworthiness of the resource. Sometimes this enhancement is done at the design level. In other instances, it is done post-design, either through modifications of the system in question or by augmenting the system with additional components. For example, supplemental authentication or non-repudiation functions may be added to the system to enhance the identity of critical resources to other resources that depend on the organization-defined resources.

title: UNSUPPORTED SYSTEM COMPONENTS
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• An up-to-date asset inventory or configuration management database (CMDB) that clearly marks system components with their support status, including dates when support is no longer available.
• Evidence of established policies and procedures detailing the process for replacing or providing support for unsupported components, including centralized tracking of vendor support life cycle.
• Operational patterns such as periodic reviews and scanning reports that flag deprecated or unsupported components, plus documented isolation measures (e.g., network segmentation) where replacement is not immediately feasible.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Relying solely on written policies or management assertions without showing current, automated evidence (e.g., outdated spreadsheets or one-off audits).
• Using manual asset inventories that have not been updated regularly, leading to misclassification of components as supported when they are not.
• Implementing superficial network segmentation or relying on compensating controls that are not rigorously tested, resulting in unsupported components remaining exposed.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Review the asset inventory/CMDB for entries marked as unsupported components.
   WHY: This shows that the organization actively tracks components that require replacement or alternative support, ensuring risks are identified.
   HOW: Extract a current list of all components, then cross-reference with vendor end-of-life announcements or support notifications. Verify that unsupported components are flagged and have documented remediation actions.
   FAIL: An asset inventory that does not clearly mark unsupported items or lacks evidence of regular updates implies non-compliance.

2. CHECK: Verify patch management and vulnerability scanning reports for unsupported components.
   WHY: Automated scanning provides operational evidence that unsupported components have not been left on production systems without additional controls.
   HOW: Review the output of vulnerability management tools and patching logs to ensure that systems with unsupported components are either isolated or compensated with alternative support measures. Confirm that any unsupported component is flagged for isolation or risk mitigation.
   FAIL: Absence of unscheduled alerts or lack of documented exceptions indicates potential lapses in monitoring or enforcement.

3. CHECK: Examine evidence of alternative support measures such as in-house patch development or external vendor contracts.
   WHY: Demonstrates that the organization is actively mitigating the risks when replacement is not an option.
   HOW: Request documented evidence of support contracts, service-level agreements (SLAs), or records from in-house development teams showing ongoing maintenance of unsupported components. Look for reviews and testing reports that validate these alternative measures.
   FAIL: No evidence of such arrangements or reliance on unsupported components without any compensatory controls indicates non-compliance.

4. CHECK: Assess network architecture and segmentation controls related to unsupported system components.
   WHY: Proper isolation reduces risk exposure if an unsupported component is exploited.
   HOW: Review architecture diagrams, firewall policies, and network segmentation controls to verify that unsupported components are not directly exposed to public or uncontrolled networks. Perform a targeted network scan to ensure isolation measures are active.
   FAIL: Unsupported components found on flat or publicly accessible network segments reveal weak isolation controls.

EXPERT TIPS
• Focus on integrated, automated monitoring tools that update the support status of system components in near-real time; manual inventories can easily become outdated.
• Pay particular attention to systems serving critical mission functions: they must either be replaced or have robust compensatory controls, otherwise they represent a high-value target.
• Check for periodic reviews or automated alerts from patch management systems that flag unsupported components shortly after vendor notifications are received; delays can hint at broader process weaknesses.
• Validate that isolation controls (e.g., segmentation, access controls) are not just theoretical; perform scripted network tests to ensure they are effectively in place and not easily bypassed.
content:
Statement:
a. Replace system components when support for the components is no longer available from the developer, vendor, or manufacturer; or

b. Provide the following options for alternative sources for continued support for unsupported components Selection (one or more in-house support; Assignment: organization-defined support from external providers).

Guidance:
Support for system components includes software patches, firmware updates, replacement parts, and maintenance contracts. An example of unsupported components includes when vendors no longer provide critical software patches or product updates, which can result in an opportunity for adversaries to exploit weaknesses in the installed components. Exceptions to replacing unsupported system components include systems that provide critical mission or business capabilities where newer technologies are not available or where the systems are so isolated that installing replacement components is not an option.

Alternative sources for support address the need to provide continued support for system components that are no longer supported by the original manufacturers, developers, or vendors when such components remain essential to organizational mission and business functions. If necessary, organizations can establish in-house support by developing customized patches for critical software components or, alternatively, obtain the services of external providers who provide ongoing support for the designated unsupported components through contractual relationships. Such contractual relationships can include open-source software value-added vendors. The increased risk of using unsupported system components can be mitigated, for example, by prohibiting the connection of such components to public or uncontrolled networks, or implementing other forms of isolation.

Enhancements:
DEVELOPER SCREENING | VALIDATION OF SCREENING
[Withdrawn: Incorporated into SA-21.]

title: DEVELOPER SCREENING
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• CHECK: Verify that a centralized registry exists listing each external developer with documented access authorizations linked to their government duty assignments and personnel screening records.
  WHY: A centralized registry with updated authorization and screening details is a tangible indicator that the organization is tracking developer credentials and enforcing screening procedures.
  HOW: Review the registry for records that map developers to their system access roles and assign official government duty levels; inspect evidence of periodic reviews and updates in account management logs.
  FAIL: Missing, outdated, or incomplete records in the registry, or records that are not linked to access control systems.

• CHECK: Examine electronic access provisioning systems for integration with verified government duty records and additional screening criteria.
  WHY: Automated systems that control access must enforce appropriate authorizations and ensure that only properly screened developers are granted access.
  HOW: Inspect access control logs and system configurations; verify that access requests for external developers require valid government duty documentation; perform test queries to check that unverified developers are blocked.
  FAIL: Manual overrides, evidence of backdoor provisioning, or lack of integration between personnel screening data and access systems.

• CHECK: Validate that periodic audits and system reviews include checks for proper alignment between a developer’s authorized duties and their current system access rights.
  WHY: Routine audits help detect discrepancies and enforce continuous compliance with the defined screening criteria.
  HOW: Review audit reports and sampling of developer access records over time; interview administrators for evidence of active remediation processes when discrepancies are found.
  FAIL: Absence of regular audit reports, static or infrequently updated records, or failure to remediate mismatches between roles and access rights.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations sometimes rely solely on static documentation (e.g., HR certifications, outdated personnel records) that may not reflect current access status.
• Misinterpretation or minimal interpretation of 'government duties' may allow individuals to claim superficial compliance without proper vetting.
• Over-reliance on external attestation letters without integration into technical access control mechanisms can create gaps that are easy to bypass.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. Automated System Check: Query the centralized access registry and provisioning system to extract all external developer accounts and cross-check against approved government duty assignments and additional screening criteria. 
2. Log Inspection: Extract and review logs for access provisioning events to ensure only developers with valid and updated screening records receive system access.
3. Audit Report Analysis: Examine recent internal audit reports to confirm that periodic reviews include verification of the alignment between developer screening and actual access rights.
4. Manual Verification: Interview system administrators or security officers to confirm that real-time monitoring exists and that non-compliance is escalated promptly.

EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Focus on cross-referencing developer accounts with dated access logs to quickly identify anomalies or outdated authorizations.
• Critical Indicator: Look for discrepancies where access rights remain active despite revoked or expired screening credentials.
• Detail Matters: The tight integration between human resource records, access control systems, and audit logs is essential; a gap in any one area indicates weak compliance.
• Tip: Ensure that the system checks are dynamic and that manual verifications (e.g., random spot checks) are performed regularly to detect faked compliance that isn’t reflected in real-time access logs.
content:
Statement:
Require that the developer of [Assignment: organization-defined system, system component, or system service]:  a. Has appropriate access authorizations as determined by assigned [Assignment: organization-defined official government duties]; and  b. Satisfies the following additional personnel screening criteria: [Assignment: organization-defined additional personnel screening criteria].

Guidance:
Developer screening is directed at external developers. Internal developer screening is addressed by PS-3. Because the system, system component, or system service may be used in critical activities essential to the national or economic security interests of the United States, organizations have a strong interest in ensuring that developers are trustworthy.

title: CUSTOMIZED DEVELOPMENT OF CRITICAL COMPONENTS
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• CHECK: Verify that custom-developed repositories or directories exist for critical system components as defined by organizational risk assessments.
  WHY: The presence of dedicated repositories and custom build pipelines demonstrates that the organization has reimplemented security-critical components rather than simply reusing off-the-shelf solutions.
  HOW: Look for multiple committed code revisions, commit logs referencing security enhancements, and build pipelines validated by CI/CD tools that produce custom artifacts for components flagged as critical.
  FAIL: No evidence of custom code directories or commits specifically for the identified critical system components; reliance solely on external vendor code with superficial policy statements.

• CHECK: Examine the development environment attached to these components to confirm the integration of security testing and static/dynamic analysis tools.
  WHY: Operational security testing is a key indicator that custom components are rigorously evaluated against common attacks and vulnerabilities.
  HOW: Inspect automated testing reports, tool integration in the CI/CD pipeline, and evidence of routine security scans (e.g., code analysis, fuzz testing) on the critical component codebase.
  FAIL: Absence of automated test logs or security tool integration, or reliance solely on manual testing documentation that can be easily faked.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Documentation-Only Compliance: Organizations might provide internal policy documents stating that components are custom developed without reflecting tangible evidence in the code repository.
• False Reimplementation: Repackaging or minimally modifying an off-the-shelf component to meet the control without fundamentally altering the design to mitigate known threats is common.
• Overreliance on Guidelines: Demonstrations might only include architectural diagrams or high-level design documents without the actual underlying implementation details.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Inventory and reconcile a list of organization-defined critical system components with the implemented components.
   WHY: It confirms that all components deemed vulnerable by risk assessments have undergone a reimplementation or custom development process.
   HOW: Interview the development team, review risk assessment documents, and trace these components in the system architecture documentation to source code repositories.
   FAIL: Components appear in production that were not subject to the custom development effort or lack traceability back to documented critical components.

2. CHECK: Review version control records and commit histories for evidence of dedicated efforts to reimplement security-critical functionality.
   WHY: Detailed commit histories with security-related annotations (such as commit messages indicating threat mitigation) provide undeniable evidence of the custom development process.
   HOW: Search commit logs for keywords (e.g., “custom development,” “security hardening,” “reimplementation”), review branch histories, and verify timestamps that correlate with risk review cycles.
   FAIL: Sparse commit messages, generic change logs, or evidence that the repository has imported bulk vendor code with no modification.

3. CHECK: Validate that there is an integrated security testing regime in the build/deployment pipeline for these components.
   WHY: Effective custom development should include built-in security tests to ensure that reimplemented components meet the intended security improvements.
   HOW: Analyze CI/CD pipeline configurations, review automated testing frameworks integrated with the build process, and inspect security test reports and vulnerability scan results.
   FAIL: The testing pipeline shows no specialized steps for the critical components or relies solely on standard generic tests unrelated to the custom development effort.

4. CHECK: Verify that detailed design and implementation documentation exists that clearly differentiates these components from standard vendor-supplied versions.
   WHY: Comprehensive documentation provides insight into design decisions and highlights the specific security enhancements made during custom development.
   HOW: Request architectural design documents, conduct interviews with development leads, and compare the custom documentation against vendor documentation to highlight differences.
   FAIL: Documentation is generic, borrowed from vendor guidelines, or is missing entirely for the custom-developed components.

EXPERT TIPS
• Consider automating repository and CI/CD pipeline audits using integrated scripts to flag security-specific commit messages and testing logs.
• Look for recurring indicators such as regular security scans, consistent update intervals, and detailed commit notes – these are often the most reliable signals of genuine custom development efforts.
• Focus on the integration in production systems – if custom components are bypassed during deployment or if there are ad hoc patches after initial implementation, the control may be weak.
• Documentation can be faked easily; always corroborate design claims with observable code changes and automated logs.
• Critical virtual indicators include artifact signing logs and build pipeline records that ensure the integrity of the custom-developed components before production deployment.
content:
Statement:
Reimplement or custom develop the following critical system components: [Assignment: organization-defined critical system components].

Guidance:
Organizations determine that certain system components likely cannot be trusted due to specific threats to and vulnerabilities in those components for which there are no viable security controls to adequately mitigate risk. Reimplementation or custom development of such components may satisfy requirements for higher assurance and is carried out by initiating changes to system components (including hardware, software, and firmware) such that the standard attacks by adversaries are less likely to succeed. In situations where no alternative sourcing is available and organizations choose not to reimplement or custom develop critical system components, additional controls can be employed. Controls include enhanced auditing, restrictions on source code and system utility access, and protection from deletion of system and application files.

title: COMPONENT AUTHENTICITY
methodology:
SA-19 COMPONENT AUTHENTICITY – VALIDATION METHODOLOGY

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Look for physical labeling, packaging, and tamper-proof seals on critical hardware components. Authenticity certificates or manufacturer documentation should be attached to shipment records, repair logs, and disposal manifests.
• Technical Artifacts: Check for scan logs from anti-counterfeit scanning tools, digital signatures on component firmware/software, and audit trails from configuration management systems that record component service, repair, and disposal actions.
• Operational Patterns: Observe that the organization routinely verifies component origin during procurement, uses automated scans at the point of receiving parts, and logs all maintenance, repair, and disposal activities with traceable evidence.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Faked Compliance: Organizations might simply archive vendor certificates or upload sample scan logs without connecting them to actual procurement events. They may rely on paper trails that aren’t linked to live inventory.
• Surface-Level Implementations: Using generic supply-chain verification policies without integrating automated anti-counterfeit technology can be a bypass. Recording disposal events without enforcing secure disposal procedures is another common gap.
• Technical Gaps: Missing integration between component service/repair records and the actual inventory system allows counterfeit components to slip through. Inadequate logging (or filtering) of scanning device outputs can lead to superficial compliance that fails operationally.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Inventory & Procurement Verification
• WHY: Genuine components should come with verifiable supply chain documentation that is tied to inventory records.
• HOW: Review a sample of procurement records and cross-check vendor certificates, shipment manifests, and invoices with observed physical asset labels. Confirm that component serial numbers and authenticity details match those provided by the vendor.
• FAIL: Inability to map procurement records to physical components, missing or mismatched certificates, or unexplained gaps in the chain of custody.

CHECK: Anti-Counterfeit Scanning Evidence
• WHY: Automated scanning processes are critical to catch counterfeit parts in real time.
• HOW: Obtain and analyze logs from anti-counterfeit scanning tools, checking for timestamps, component IDs, and any alerts triggered during scanning. Validate that these scanning processes are integrated with alerting and response mechanisms.
• FAIL: Absence of detailed scan logs, reliance on manual checks with no audit trail, or repeated overrides without documented justifications.

CHECK: Configuration Control for Component Service & Repair
• WHY: Ensuring that service and repair activities maintain traceability and authenticity is vital to component integrity.
• HOW: Inspect configuration management and service repair logs for components undergoing repair. Verify that records include details on the vendor, process, and returned parts’ authenticity post-service.
• FAIL: Missing or outdated service records, untraceable repair events, or evidence that components were serviced without verifying their original authenticity.

CHECK: Secure Component Disposal Process
• WHY: Proper disposal protects against reintroduction or tampering with counterfeit components.
• HOW: Review disposal logs and physical records of decommissioned components. Confirm that disposal procedures enforce a secure chain of custody and are aligned with documented standards.
• FAIL: Incomplete disposal records, evidence of components reentering the inventory post-disposal, or absence of procedural checks during disposal.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Verification Shortcuts: Assess traceability from procurement to integration into the operational environment by sampling on-site inventories and matching physical labels with digital records. Automated correlation of procurement and scan logs tends to be a strong indicator.
• Critical Indicators: Look for consistent linkage between scanned logs and procurement records. Lack of integration or isolated manual procedures often signals a high risk of counterfeit components slipping through.
• Details Matter: Emphasize the validity and timeliness of certificates, the robustness of chain-of-custody records, and the integration of automated scan results. These small details often differentiate a nominal control from one that is truly effective in preventing counterfeit integrations.
content:
Statement:
[Withdrawn: Moved to SR-11.]



Enhancements:
COMPONENT AUTHENTICITY | ANTI-COUNTERFEIT TRAINING [Withdrawn: Moved to SR-11(1).]
COMPONENT AUTHENTICITY | CONFIGURATION CONTROL FOR COMPONENT SERVICE AND REPAIR [Withdrawn: Moved to SR-11(2).]
COMPONENT AUTHENTICITY | COMPONENT DISPOSAL [Withdrawn: Moved to SR-12.]
COMPONENT AUTHENTICITY | ANTI-COUNTERFEIT SCANNING [Withdrawn: Moved to SR-11(3).]

title: TAMPER RESISTANCE AND DETECTION
methodology:
Control SA-18 (Tamper Resistance and Detection) has been withdrawn and merged into SR-9; however, many organizations retain mechanisms designed to resist and detect tampering. The following methodology provides a practical validation approach:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • CHECK: Verify that physical and logical tamper detection mechanisms are active.
     WHY: Evidence of tamper resistance measures (e.g., chassis intrusions, firmware integrity checks, cryptographic signing of system components) indicates the organization has implemented layered detection.
     HOW: Look for hard evidence such as sensor logs (e.g., chassis intrusion logs), system event logs that record tamper detection events, and firmware or BIOS logs that record modifications or alerts. Additionally, review configurations of integrity-check tools (e.g., Tripwire, AIDE) that monitor system files.
     FAIL: Absence of any tamper logs, disabled or misconfigured integrity monitoring, or purely documented policies without real-time alerts.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations may simply produce outdated documentation or static screenshots to claim compliance without ensuring operational monitoring. 
   • Superficial sensor installation without integration into monitoring systems (e.g., sensors that provide no alerting mechanism or feed into security operations) is a common bypass.
   • Over-reliance on policy wording instead of active, enforced detection measures is also a telltale sign.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   • CHECK 1: Verify tamper detection logging. 
     WHY: Automated logs are difficult to fake and provide real-time evidence of tampering events.
     HOW: Access system and device logs (operating system logs, firmware logs, hardware sensor logs) and confirm that tamper events (e.g., chassis access, unusual system modifications) are recorded and timestamped. Automate log retrieval where possible.
     FAIL: No tamper-related entries, outdated log records, or logs showing that detection mechanisms are disabled.

   • CHECK 2: Validate integrity monitoring tool configuration.
     WHY: Tools like file integrity checkers (Tripwire, AIDE) can expose unauthorized modifications if properly configured.
     HOW: Review the configuration and schedules of these monitoring tools; verify that integrity baseline files exist and that the system automatically scans, comparing against trusted baselines, with alerts raised for discrepancies.
     FAIL: Non-existent or misconfigured tools; integrity baselines that have not been frequently updated or that show gaps in monitoring coverage.

   • CHECK 3: Confirm physical tamper sensor functionality. 
     WHY: Physical sensors (e.g., chassis intrusion detection mechanisms) are direct indicators of tampering attempts at the hardware level.
     HOW: Physically inspect critical systems for tamper evidence (e.g., sealed enclosures, cable tamper detection), validate sensor configurations via management interfaces, and review sensor activation logs. Where possible, simulate a tamper event in a controlled manner and confirm that an alert is generated.
     FAIL: Sensors missing, disabled, or without corresponding log evidence, or if simulation tests do not trigger alerts.

   • CHECK 4 (Optional): Assess system design documentation for integrated tamper detection in hardware/firmware lifecycle. 
     WHY: This helps validate that tamper resistance was considered during multiple phases of the system development life cycle.
     HOW: Review technical design documents, build procedures, and firmware update processes to verify embedding of tamper resistance features (e.g., cryptographic signing, secure boot routines). Focus on evidence rather than policy statements.
     FAIL: Documentation that does not reflect actual implemented technical controls or is outdated relative to current configurations.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: Begin by obtaining and reviewing system log samples for anomalies—automated log collectors or SIEM tools provide a high-level view of tamper events that are hard to fake.
   • Critical Indicator: Look for integration between physical tamper sensors and alerting mechanisms; if physical and logical tamper detection are not synchronized (e.g., sensor alerts do not trigger SIEM notifications), the overall efficacy is compromised.
   • Detail Matter: Ensure that integrity monitoring baselines are maintained on secure, write-protected media and that updates occur in a controlled, verifiable manner.
   • Remember: Even if the control has been withdrawn and merged into SR-9, evidence of operational tamper resistance (active monitoring, effective sensor configuration, timely logging, and real alerting) is key to a robust security posture.

This comprehensive methodology focuses on real-time, automated evidence of tamper resistance and detection, ensuring that implementation is operational rather than only theoretical.
content:
Statement:
[Withdrawn: Moved to SR-9.]



Enhancements:
TAMPER RESISTANCE AND DETECTION | MULTIPLE PHASES OF SYSTEM DEVELOPMENT LIFE CYCLE [Withdrawn: Moved to SR-9(1).]

title: DEVELOPER SECURITY AND PRIVACY ARCHITECTURE AND DESIGN
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for SA-17 – Developer Security and Privacy Architecture and Design:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Existence of a detailed design specification and security/privacy architecture documentation that aligns with the overall enterprise architecture. This documentation should clearly allocate security and privacy controls among physical and logical components.
   • Artifacts demonstrating formal or informal policy models and top-level specifications, including models of interfaces to security-relevant hardware, software, and firmware (e.g., formal proofs, diagrams, and interface specifications).
   • Evidence of a structured development process that incorporates security testing structures, correspondence checks between architecture and implementation, and adherence to principles like least privilege and conceptually simple design.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations may produce high-level, generic documentation that is not tightly coupled to the actual implementation details. This can be faked by offering outdated or “placeholder” documents.
   • Superficial compliance in the form of checklists or templates that make no real effort to map security controls to the deployed components.
   • Failure to integrate the design model with iterative code reviews and continuous automated testing that validates the architecture’s consistency with implementation.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify that a design specification exists which explicitly describes security and privacy functionality and how controls are allocated among system components.
   WHY: This specification is the foundation that proves the security architecture was designed in alignment with organizational policies and enterprise architecture.
   HOW: Inspect the documented design artifacts (e.g., system design documents, diagrams, and architecture models) and confirm they incorporate detailed mappings of security controls to system components. Interview developers or architects about how these documents guide implementations.
   FAIL: Absence of detailed design artifacts or evidence that the documentation is outdated, generic, or not specific enough to the system’s components.

   CHECK: Confirm the presence of formal or informal policy models along with a top-level specification that covers security-relevant hardware, software, and firmware interfaces.
   WHY: These models explicitly demonstrate how security mechanisms interact and provide evidence of internal consistency and complete description of relevant components.
   HOW: Review any formal proofs, modeling artifacts, or demonstrations (even if only informal) that show the correlation between the policy model and the implemented controls. Validate that the interface specifications include exceptions, error messages, and effects.
   FAIL: No evidence that any formal/informal model exists, or the provided models lack detail regarding interfaces to security-relevant elements or do not reflect the actual implemented details.

   CHECK: Check that the design documentation includes rationale and determination of security-relevant hardware, software, and firmware definitions and how those support least privilege and conceptually simple design.
   WHY: This confirms that the development has been structured to reduce complexity and limit permissions in line with best security practices.
   HOW: Evaluate the design documents to determine if there’s a clear definition and rationale for security-relevant components. Cross-reference with implementation reviews and code repository interviews to ensure controls are properly applied.
   FAIL: A design that is overly complex, lacks clear rationale for security-relevant components, or assigns broad privileges without a traceable documentation link.

   CHECK: Validate evidence that the architecture supports structured testing by reviewing if the design specifies clear testable controls and corresponding test cases.
   WHY: Evidence of structured testing ensures that the architecture’s claims are operationally verified against real-world deployment and scenarios.
   HOW: Request and review testing plans, test case documentation, and automated test results that correlate with the architectural design. Observe if the tests cover key security functions and validate interface conditions.
   FAIL: Lack of specific test cases that validate the documented controls or evidence that testing is ad-hoc or incomplete, thus breaking the assurance that the design is comprehensively verified.

   CHECK: Assess the consistency between the documented top-level specification and actual implementation through selective code reviews or simulated interface testing.
   WHY: This ensures that the developed system faithfully implements the documented security architecture and that the formal/informal correspondence is maintained.
   HOW: Perform targeted code reviews focusing on modules or components labeled as security-relevant, and compare these with the documented formal top-level specifications. Interview developers for insight into any deviations and corrective measures.
   FAIL: Significant deviations or inconsistencies between the design documentation and observed code behavior, or evidence of undocumented changes that affect security-relevant functionalities.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: Focus first on the artifacts produced during early design reviews – these often highlight the intended security posture and mapping. Also, cross-check these with automated test outputs, an effective way to verify operational enforcement.
   • Critical Indicator: If design documents exist only as templates and are rarely referenced during code reviews or testing phases, it is a red flag. Similarly, audits showing discrepancies between model and implementation are strong indicators of poor implementation.
   • Details Matter: Ensure that the mapping between high-level security requirements and their practical implementation is explicitly defined. Overlapping or ambiguous responsibilities in the design are common pitfalls.
   • Remember: Automated evidence (version-controlled documentation changes, integrated test dashboards, and code review records) is harder to fake than paper policies. Leverage these sources for robust assurance.

By following these steps, you can validate that the developer’s security and privacy architecture and design not only exist on paper but are operationally effective and integrated into the actual development process.
content:
Statement:
Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to produce a design specification and security and privacy architecture that:  a. is consistent with the organization’s security and privacy architecture that is an integral part of the organization’s enterprise architecture;  b. accurately and completely describes the required security and privacy functionality, and the allocation of controls among physical and logical components; and  c. expresses how individual security and privacy functions, mechanisms, and services work together to provide required security and privacy capabilities and a unified approach to protection.

Guidance:
Developer security and privacy architecture and design are directed at external developers, although they could also be applied to internal (in-house) development. In contrast, PL-8 is directed at internal developers to ensure that organizations develop a security and privacy protection.

Enhancements:
DEVELOPER SECURITY AND PRIVACY ARCHITECTURE AND DESIGN | FORMAL POLICY MODEL: Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to: (a) Produce, as an integral part of the development process, a formal policy model describing the [Assignment: organization-defined elements of organizational security and privacy policy] to be enforced; and (b) Prove that the formal policy model is internally consistent and sufficient to enforce the defined elements of the organizational security and privacy policy when implemented. Discussion: Formal models describe specific behaviors or security and privacy policies using formal languages, thus enabling the correctness of those behaviors and policies to be formally proven. Not all components of systems can be modeled. Generally, formal specifications are scoped to the behaviors or policies of interest, such as nondiscretionary access control policies. Further, developers must select the formal modeling language and approach based on the nature of the behaviors and policies to be described and the available tools. Related Controls: AC-3, AC-4, AC-25.
DEVELOPER SECURITY AND PRIVACY ARCHITECTURE AND DESIGN | SECURITY-RELEVANT COMPONENTS: Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to: (a) Define security-relevant hardware, software, and firmware; and (b) Provide a rationale that the definition for security-relevant hardware, software, and firmware is complete. Discussion: The security-relevant hardware, software, and firmware represent the portion of the system, component, or service that is trusted to perform correctly to maintain required security properties. Related Controls: AC-25, SA-5.
DEVELOPER SECURITY AND PRIVACY ARCHITECTURE AND DESIGN | FORMAL CORRESPONDENCE: Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to: (a) Produce, as an integral part of the development process, a formal top-level specification that specifies the interfaces to security-relevant hardware, software, and firmware in terms of exceptions, error messages, and effects; (b) Show via proof to the extent feasible with additional informal demonstration as necessary, that the formal top-level specification is consistent with the formal policy model; (c) Show via informal demonstration, that the formal top-level specification completely covers the interfaces to security-relevant hardware, software, and firmware; (d) Show that the formal top-level specification is an accurate description of the implemented security-relevant hardware, software, and firmware; and (e) Describe the security-relevant hardware, software, and firmware mechanisms not addressed in the formal top-level specification but strictly internal to the security-relevant hardware, software, and firmware. Discussion: Correspondence is an important part of the assurance gained through modeling. It demonstrates that the implementation is an accurate transformation of the model, and that any additional code or implementation details that are present have no impact on the behaviors or policies being modeled. Related Controls: AC-3 AC-4 AC-25 SA-4 SA-5.
DEVELOPER SECURITY AND PRIVACY ARCHITECTURE AND DESIGN | INFORMAL CORRESPONDENCE: Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to: (a) Produce, as an integral part of the development process, an informal descriptive top-level specification that specifies the interfaces to security-relevant hardware, software, and firmware in terms of exceptions, error messages, and effects; (b) Show via [Selection: informal demonstration; convincing argument with formal methods as feasible] that the descriptive top-level specification is consistent with the formal policy model; (c) Show via informal demonstration, that the descriptive top-level specification completely covers the interfaces to security-relevant hardware, and firmware; (d) Show that the descriptive top-level specification is an accurate description of the interfaces to security-relevant hardware, software, and firmware; and (e) Describe the security-relevant hardware, software, and firmware mechanisms not addressed in the descriptive top-level specification but strictly internal to the security-relevant hardware, software, and firmware. Discussion: Correspondence is an important part of the assurance gained through modeling. It demonstrates that the implementation is an accurate transformation of the model, and that additional code or implementation detail has no impact on the behaviors or policies being modeled. Related Controls: AC-3 AC-4 AC-25 SA-4 SA-5.
DEVELOPER SECURITY AND PRIVACY ARCHITECTURE AND DESIGN | CONCEPTUALLY SIMPLE DESIGN: Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to: (a) Design and structure the security-relevant hardware, software, and firmware to use a complete, conceptually simple protection mechanism with precisely defined semantics; and (b) Internally structure the security-relevant hardware, software, and firmware with specific regard for this mechanism. Discussion: The principle of reduced complexity states that the system design is as simple and small as possible (see SA-8(7)). A small and simple design is easier to understand and analyze and is also less prone to error (see AC-25, SA-8(131)). The principle of reduced complexity applies to any aspect of a system, but it has particular importance for security due to the various analyses performed to obtain evidence about the emergent security property of the system. Related Controls: AC-25, SA-8, SC-3.
DEVELOPER SECURITY AND PRIVACY ARCHITECTURE AND DESIGN | STRUCTURE FOR TESTING: Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to structure security-relevant hardware, software, and firmware to facilitate testing. Discussion: Applying the security design principles in [SP 800-160-1] promotes complete, consistent, and comprehensive testing and evaluation of systems, system components, and services. The thoroughness of such testing contributes to the evidence produced to generate an effective assurance case or argument as to the trustworthiness of the system, system component, or service. Related Controls: SA-5, SA-11.
DEVELOPER SECURITY AND PRIVACY ARCHITECTURE AND DESIGN | STRUCTURE FOR LEAST PRIVILEGE: Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to structure security-relevant hardware, software, and firmware to facilitate controlling access with least privilege. Discussion: The principle of least privilege states that each component is allocated sufficient privileges to accomplish its specified functions but no more (see SA-8(14)). Applying the principle of least privilege limits the scope of the component’s actions, which has two desirable effects. Related Controls: AC-5, AC-6, SA-8.

title: DEVELOPER-PROVIDED TRAINING
methodology:
CHECK: Verify the developer-provided training materials repository
WHY: A centralized repository showing training materials (presentations, videos, handouts, etc.) confirms that material was provided, versioned, and is currently available to staff
HOW: Review the repository or content management system for artifacts tagged as developer training; check version history and content for alignment with system security and privacy functions; verify that materials include concrete instructions on security operations
FAIL: Absence of a centralized repository or only showing outdated versions and generic training materials unrelated to specific security functions

CHECK: Inspect training attendance logs and records
WHY: Verified attendance or participation records (system logs from online training platforms, sign-in sheets, video conference recordings) prove that personnel received the training
HOW: Obtain automated logs or records from the training delivery system; correlate participant identifiers with system developer groups; confirm that attendance records exist for both in-house and externally provided training sessions
FAIL: Missing or incomplete attendance records, records solely based on self-certification without automated logging, or sporadic sessions with very limited attendance

CHECK: Evaluate training assessment and follow-up evidence
WHY: Assessments and feedback reports provide evidence that training was not simply performed but also understood and incorporated by the team
HOW: Request anonymized test scores, quiz results, or follow-up survey data; review improvement plans or remediation sessions scheduled after initial assessments
FAIL: Lack of any formal assessment, standardized testing, or surveys to gauge training effectiveness; reliance solely on statements of completion without follow-up evaluation

CHECK: Conduct interviews and workflow reviews
WHY: Direct interviews with system developers and training coordinators confirm the practical use of training materials and awareness of security/privacy practices
HOW: Interview a sample of system developers and training administrators to verify that the training addresses operational security controls, discuss recent training sessions, and check if training materials are referenced in daily workflows
FAIL: Testimonies indicating that training was perfunctory or only existed on paper, or that developers are unaware of specific security controls

EXPERT TIP: Automate log collection and verification where possible
WHY: Automated evidence reduces the risk of manipulated records and ensures a factual basis for verifying training participation and content updates
HOW: Leverage system monitoring tools that track material access (e.g., LMS logs, digital signatures on training documents, and periodic scheduled training sessions) and correlate with employee accounts
FAIL: Manual-only logs or absence of integration between training delivery systems and developer accounts, indicating a higher risk of forgery or incomplete training records
content:
Statement:
Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to provide the following training on the correct use and operation of the implemented security and privacy functions, controls, and/or mechanisms: [Assignment: organization-defined training].

Guidance:
Developer-provided training applies to external and internal (in-house) developers. Training personnel is essential to ensuring the effectiveness of the controls implemented within organizational systems. Types of training include web-based and computer-based training, classroom-style training, and hands-on training (including micro-training). Organizations can also request training materials from developers to conduct in-house training or offer self-training to organizational personnel. Organizations determine the type of training necessary and may require different types of training for different security and privacy functions, controls, and mechanisms.

title: DEVELOPMENT PROCESS, STANDARDS, AND TOOLS
methodology:
Below is a practical methodology for validating implementation of nist-sa-15 (Development Process, Standards, and Tools):

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Documented development process that explicitly integrates security and privacy requirements. Look for artifacts such as process handbooks, version-controlled process documentation (e.g., Git repositories with change logs) and records of scheduled reviews.
   • Detailed records of standards, tools, and tool option configurations. Evidence includes configuration files, build system logs that note specific tool versions and options, and documented change control procedures tied to tool configurations.
   • Operational evidence that teams perform ongoing quality metric reviews, criticality analyses, and automated vulnerability scans. This includes security and privacy tracking tools’ logs, meeting minutes from risk reviews, and threat modeling analysis outputs.
   • Archival records of released/delivered system components along with related incident response plans and quality metric evidence.
   • Active use of automated vulnerability assessments and a demonstrated process of incorporating threat and vulnerability data into development improvements.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations may provide high-level policies or process documents without actual evidence of implementation. For example, a document may state that security requirements were reviewed, but automated evidence (e.g., tool logs, formal change records) is not maintained.
   • Superficial or outdated configuration records for development tools that fail to reflect the current state of the production environment.
   • Fake reviews where meeting minutes or periodic reports are generated only as a paperwork exercise, without corresponding logs or outputs from actual tool runs or criticality analyses.
   • Using a generic incident response plan without demonstrating integration into the development and deployment cycle (i.e., no testing logs or exercises conducted).

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Validate the Documented Development Process with Security & Privacy Integration
   WHY: Ensures that security and privacy considerations are embedded from the start in the development lifecycle.
   HOW: • Inspect the official development process documentation to confirm inclusion of explicit security/privacy requirements and change control mechanisms. • Examine version control history (e.g., Git commits) for evidence of process and tool configuration updates over time.
   FAIL: If the documentation is missing detailed security and privacy requirements or if version control logs do not show controlled changes.

   CHECK: Review Standards, Tools, and Tool Option Documentation
   WHY: Ensures adherence to defined tools and configurations crucial for maintaining supply chain integrity and process repeatability.
   HOW: • Review the configuration files, build scripts, and tool-setting documents to verify that they list the exact tool versions, options, and configurations being used. • Validate these artifacts against the current production setup.
   FAIL: If there are discrepancies between what is documented and what is actually observed in the build environments or if detailed configurations are missing.

   CHECK: Assess Security and Privacy Tracking Tools Usage & Criticality Analysis
   WHY: Proper tracking and analysis indicate active management of security vulnerabilities and risk prioritization during development.
   HOW: • Check records (e.g., from a vulnerability management or issue-tracking system) for regular entries, analyses, or tickets linked to security/privacy aspects. • Verify the existence of periodic criticality analyses and that their outputs have influenced development decisions.
   FAIL: If tracking data is sparse, generated only on an ad-hoc basis, or if criticality analysis reports are absent or not actioned.

   CHECK: Verify Quality Metrics & Attack Surface Reduction Efforts
   WHY: Quality metrics and attack surface reduction measures, such as elimination of non-essential code paths or vulnerable APIs, ensure the quality of delivered components.
   HOW: • Inspect evidence (test reports, continuous integration logs, quality gate checklists) that demonstrate defined quality metrics (e.g., zero compiler warnings, test coverage) are met. • Review design documentation for evidence of attack surface reduction techniques (like API minimization, use of layered defenses).
   FAIL: If quality metric evidence is missing, incomplete, or if there is no clear link between quality reviews and actual development outcomes.

   CHECK: Examine Incident Response & Archival Practices
   WHY: A robust incident response plan and proper archiving underpin the organization’s ability to respond to security events and maintain traceability of system components.
   HOW: • Review the incident response plan provided by developers, including test records or exercise simulations. • Check archival records for system components and development artifacts (e.g., source code snapshots, configuration baselines) stored and preserved per the process.
   FAIL: If the incident response plan isn’t test-driven or if archival evidence is incomplete or not well correlated with delivered releases.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Use automated tooling to extract repository metadata and cross-check it against documented processes (e.g., verifying commit timestamps and signatures that indicate controlled changes).
   • Look for immutable logs in version control or configuration management systems—this is a strong sign of integrity in the development process.
   • Interview developers or process owners to confirm that reviews and threat modeling sessions are conducted as scheduled, looking beyond static documents.
   • Pay attention to the consistency and granularity of artifacts: superficial evidence (e.g., generic screenshots or summary reports) may signal that the process is not fully implemented in practice.
   • Cross-reference tool-generated vulnerability reports and incident response test logs to confirm alignment with critical security and privacy requirements.

This methodology emphasizes observable, automated, and operational evidence over mere policy documents, ensuring that the development process, standards, and tools are effectively implemented and maintained in line with security and privacy mandates.
content:
Statement:
a. Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to follow a documented development process that:
   1. Explicitly addresses security and privacy requirements;
   2. Identifies the standards and tools used in the development process;
   3. Documents the specific tool options and tool configurations used in the development process; and
   4. Documents, manages, and ensures the integrity of changes to the process and/or tools used in development; and
b. Review the development process, standards, tools, tool options, and tool configurations [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] to determine if the process, standards, tools, tool options and tool configurations selected and employed can satisfy the following security and privacy requirements: [Assignment: organization-defined security and privacy requirements].

Guidance:
Development tools include programming languages and computer-aided design systems. Reviews of development processes include the use of maturity models to determine the potential effectiveness of such processes. Maintaining the integrity of changes to tools and processes facilitates effective supply chain risk assessment and mitigation. Such integrity requires continual control throughout the system development life cycle to track authorized changes and prevent unauthorized changes.

Enhancements:
DEVELOPMENT PROCESS, STANDARDS, AND TOOLS | QUALITY METRICS
Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to:
(a) Define quality metrics at the beginning of the development process; and
(b) Provide evidence of meeting the quality metrics [Selection (one or more): [Assignment: organization-defined frequency]; [Assignment: organization-defined program review milestones]; upon delivery].
Discussion: Organizations use quality metrics to establish acceptable levels of system quality. Metrics can include quality gates, which are collections of completion criteria or sufficiency standards that represent the satisfactory execution of specific phases of the system development project. For example, a quality gate may require the elimination of all compiler warnings or a determination that such warnings have no impact on the effectiveness of required security or privacy capabilities. During the execution phases of development projects, quality gates provide clear, unambiguous indications of progress. Other metrics apply to the entire development project. Metrics can include defining the severity thresholds of vulnerabilities in accordance with organizational risk tolerance, such as requiring no known vulnerabilities in the delivered system with a Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS) severity of medium or high.

Related Controls: None.

## (2) DEVELOPMENT PROCESS, STANDARDS, AND TOOLS | SECURITY AND PRIVACY TRACKING TOOLS

Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to select and employ security and privacy tracking tools for use during the development process.

Discussion: System development teams select and deploy security and privacy tracking tools, including vulnerability or work item tracking systems that facilitate assignment, sorting, filtering, and tracking of completed work items or tasks associated with development processes.

Related Controls: SA-11.

## (3) DEVELOPMENT PROCESS, STANDARDS, AND TOOLS | CRITICALITY ANALYSIS

Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to perform a criticality analysis:

(a) At the following decision points in the system development life cycle: [Assignment: organization-defined decision points in the system development life cycle]; and
(b) At the following level of rigor: [Assignment: organization-defined breadth and depth of criticality analysis].

Discussion: Criticality analysis performed by the developer provides input to the criticality analysis performed by organizations. Developer input is essential to organizational criticality analysis because organizations may not have access to detailed design documentation for system components that are developed as commercial off-the-shelf products. Such design documentation includes functional specifications, high-level designs, low-level designs, source code, and hardware schematics. Criticality analysis is important for organizational systems that are designated as high value assets. High value assets can be moderate- or high-impact systems due to heightened adversarial interest or potential adverse effects on the federal enterprise. Developer input is especially important when organizations conduct supply chain criticality analyses.

## (4) DEVELOPMENT PROCESS, STANDARDS, AND TOOLS | THREAT MODELING AND VULNERABILITY ANALYSIS

[Withdrawn: Incorporated into SA-11(2).]

## (5) DEVELOPMENT PROCESS, STANDARDS, AND TOOLS | ATTACK SURFACE REDUCTION

Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to reduce attack surfaces to [Assignment: organization-defined thresholds].

Discussion: Attack surface reduction is closely aligned with threat and vulnerability analyses and system architecture and design. Attack surface reduction is a means of reducing risk to organizations by giving attackers less opportunity to exploit weaknesses or deficiencies (i.e., potential vulnerabilities) within systems, system components, and system services. Attack surface reduction includes implementing the concept of layered defenses, applying the principles of least privilege and least functionality, applying secure software development practices, deprecating unsafe functions, reducing entry points available to unauthorized users, reducing the amount of code that executes, and eliminating application programming interfaces (APIs) that are vulnerable to attacks.

Related Controls: AC-6, CM-7, RA-3, SA-11.

## (6) DEVELOPMENT PROCESS, STANDARDS, AND TOOLS | CONTINUOUS IMPROVEMENT

Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to implement an explicit process to continuously improve the development process.

Discussion: Developers of systems, system components, and system services consider the effectiveness and efficiency of their development processes for meeting quality objectives and addressing security and privacy capabilities in current threat environments.

Related Controls: None.

## (7) DEVELOPMENT PROCESS, STANDARDS, AND TOOLS | AUTOMATED VULNERABILITY ANALYSIS

Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] to:

(a) Perform an automated vulnerability analysis using [Assignment: organization-defined tools];
(b) Determine the exploitation potential for discovered vulnerabilities;
(c) Determine potential risk mitigations for delivered vulnerabilities; and
(d) Deliver the outputs of the tools and results of the analysis to [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles].

Discussion: Automated tools can be more effective at analyzing exploitable weaknesses or deficiencies in large and complex systems, prioritizing vulnerabilities by severity, and providing recommendations for risk mitigations.

Related Controls: RA-5, SA-11.

## (8) DEVELOPMENT PROCESS, STANDARDS, AND TOOLS | REUSE OF THREAT AND VULNERABILITY INFORMATION

Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to use threat modeling and vulnerability analyses from similar systems, components, or services to inform the current development process.

Discussion: Analysis of vulnerabilities found in similar software applications can inform potential design and implementation issues for systems under development. Similar systems or system components may exist within developer organizations. Vulnerability information is available from a variety of public and private sector sources, including the NIST National Vulnerability Database.

Related Controls: None.

## (9) DEVELOPMENT PROCESS, STANDARDS, AND TOOLS | USE OF LIVE DATA

[Withdrawn: Incorporated into SA-3(2).]

## (10) DEVELOPMENT PROCESS, STANDARDS, AND TOOLS | INCIDENT RESPONSE PLAN

Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to provide, implement, and test an incident response plan.

Discussion: The incident response plan provided by developers may provide information not readily available to organizations and be incorporated into organizational incident response plans. Developer information may also be extremely helpful, such as when organizations respond to vulnerabilities in commercial off-the-shelf products.

Related Controls: IR-8.

## (11) DEVELOPMENT PROCESS, STANDARDS, AND TOOLS | ARCHIVE SYSTEM OR COMPONENT

Require the developer of the system or system component to archive the system or component to be released or delivered together with the corresponding evidence supporting the final security and privacy review.

Discussion: Archiving system or system components requires the developer to retain key development artifacts, including hardware specifications, source code, object code, and relevant documentation from the development process that can provide a readily available configuration baseline for system and component upgrades or modifications.

Related Controls: CM-2.

## (12) DEVELOPMENT PROCESS, STANDARDS, AND TOOLS | MINIMIZE PERSONALLY IDENTIFIABLE INFORMATION

Require the developer of the system or system component to minimize the use of personally identifiable information in development and test environments.

Discussion: Organizations can minimize the risk to an individual's privacy by using techniques such as de-identification or synthetic data. Limiting the use of personally identifiable information in development and test environments helps reduce the level of privacy risk created by a system.

Related Controls: PM-25, SA-3, SA-8.

References: [SP 800-160-1], [IR 8179].

title: CRITICALITY ANALYSIS
methodology:
Control ID: SA-14 – CRITICALITY ANALYSIS (Withdrawn – Incorporated into RA-9)

Even though this control has been withdrawn and incorporated into RA-9 (and its enhancement into SA-20), organizations that still reference SA-14 often demonstrate analogous practices as part of their overall risk and sourcing management strategy. The following methodology outlines practical steps to verify that the spirit of Criticality Analysis – namely, identifying components whose failure or loss (due to lack of viable alternative sourcing) could jeopardize the system – is actively maintained.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible evidence may include regularly updated system architectures or dependency maps where critical components are clearly flagged and risk‐rated based on sourcing alternatives.
   • Technical artifacts might encompass internal reports, risk assessment logs, or automated inventory tools that track vendor dependencies and service/component criticality.
   • Operational patterns include scheduled risk analyses, periodic reviews of third-party vendor status, and integrated sourcing alternatives discussions in change management meetings.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations might simply repackage old criticality assessments without verifying current system configurations or vendor landscapes.
   • Superficial compliance is often faked by producing a one-time document or checklist that is never updated to reflect system or vendor changes.
   • Frequently, technical gaps include a lack of integration with real-time monitoring systems or missing routine validations with operational teams, causing the analysis to remain static and not reflect current risk exposures.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   • CHECK: Existence of a Current Critical Component Listing
       WHY: A documented and updated list of system components (with emphasis on those lacking alternative sourcing) proves that the organization has identified areas vulnerable to single vendor dependency or potential supply chain risk.
       HOW: Request to view the latest system dependency maps, risk assessment reports, or automated asset inventories. Interview technical personnel to verify that critical components are flagged and reviewed routinely.
       FAIL: No evidence of an updated list or if the list exists but is outdated or appears to be copied from previous cycles without current verification.

   • CHECK: Record of Periodic Criticality and Sourcing Analyses
       WHY: Demonstrates that the organization is not performing a one-off analysis, but instead integrates regular assessments into its operational risk management.
       HOW: Check scheduling records (e.g., calendar invites, task management logs, or recurrent risk assessment reports) that indicate when analyses were performed. Verify with operations or risk management teams that these assessments are part of the established workflow.
       FAIL: Absence of recurring assessments, or review evidence that fails to tie analysis outcomes to remediation efforts or procurement updates.

   • CHECK: Engagement with Vendor/Procurement Documentation
       WHY: Active vendor management and sourcing strategy is essential to confirm that alternatives are considered and validated in the face of identified critical components.
       HOW: Examine procurement records, vendor risk assessments, and communications confirming that contingency plans or alternative sourcing strategies are in place for the most critical components. Speak with the procurement or vendor management teams for corroboration.
       FAIL: Missing procurement logs, lack of documented vendor risk assessment processes, or evidence that alternative sourcing strategies have not been explored or updated.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Verification Shortcut: Ask for a recent incident or near-miss report related to a single-sourcing issue to quickly gauge whether the criticality analysis is more than just documentation. The presence of a post-incident analysis involving sourcing alternatives is a strong indicator of active management.
   • Critical Indicator: Pay attention to how the organization links the technical dependency mappings with business continuity and procurement discussions. If these areas operate in siloes, it may indicate superficial implementation.
   • Detail Focus: Ensure that the evidence shows not only that a list or schedule exists but also that there are clear remediation or escalation paths defined when a critical component is found to have no viable alternative sourcing. This operational linkage is key to genuine control effectiveness.

By focusing on these tangible checks and cross-validating operational evidence with documented processes, you can confidently assess whether the legacy intent of SA-14 (criticality analysis) continues in practice under RA-9/SA-20.

[Withdrawn: Incorporated into RA-9 ]



Enhancements:
