title: TRUSTWORTHINESS
methodology:
Although SA-13 (Trustworthiness) has been withdrawn and its intent incorporated into SA-8, auditors still need to verify that the elements related to trustworthiness are robustly implemented as part of the overall security architecture. Below is a practical validation methodology addressing this legacy control’s intent:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Evidence often comes in the form of active integration of trustworthiness assessments into overall system security reviews (as now part of SA-8). Look for components that ensure system integrity, supply chain verification, and runtime trust assessments.
• Technical artifacts include automated logs showing code signing validations, runtime attestation events, vulnerability scan output that references trust metrics, and documented integration of trust metrics within the security architecture review processes.
• Operational patterns might include routine verification of trusted configurations, continuous monitoring systems that cross-validate software integrity, and anomaly detection alerts tied to supply chain or trusted code irregularities.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may claim compliance by merely referencing outdated SA-13 documentation without showing integration into current SA-8 practices.
• Superficial implementations might include having a policy that mentions trustworthiness without clear or automated processes for verifying code integrity and supply chain security.
• Technical gaps occur when there is no integration between the trustworthiness control testing (such as automated attestation and trusted source validations) and other critical security controls.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
• CHECK: Verify the existence and integration of trustworthiness controls within the SA-8 framework.
  WHY: This ensures that the trust and integrity aspects formerly described in SA-13 are being actively managed as part of the system’s overall security architecture.
  HOW: Review the latest security architecture documentation and interview security personnel to confirm that trusted computing and supply chain validations are integrated within existing processes.
  FAIL: No evidence that these evaluations have been migrated into operational security reviews or that they are enforced via technical mechanisms.

• CHECK: Review automated logging and monitoring systems for events related to runtime trust verifications, code signing validations, or supply chain integrity checks.
  WHY: Automated logs provide observable, non-documentary evidence that trustworthiness measures are effectively enforced.
  HOW: Request a sample of system logs and event correlation dashboards; verify that they include entries specific to trust verification events (e.g., integrity checks, certificate validations, anomaly alerts).
  FAIL: Absence of relevant log entries or dashboards that focus on trust or integrity validation events.

• CHECK: Test for periodic independent evaluations (e.g., vulnerability scans, code audits, or penetration tests) that specifically assess trust metrics and code integrity.
  WHY: Regular independent assessments are essential to validate that trustworthiness isn’t just documented but operationally enforced.
  HOW: Examine the most recent test reports and evidence of remediation for any identified shortcomings related to trust and integrity, and confirm these evaluations are part of the continuous validation process.
  FAIL: Lack of periodic assessment reports, or reports that do not address trust-related metrics in both design and operations.

• CHECK: Confirm the integration of trusted platform modules (TPMs), secure boot processes, or similar hardware/software trust anchors where applicable.
  WHY: Such integrations offer a tangible measure of an environment’s commitment to maintaining trustworthiness.
  HOW: Assess system architecture diagrams and configuration settings that validate the presence of these security features, and verify their operational status.
  FAIL: Discrepancy between documented architecture and observable configuration, or evidence that these features are disabled/unmonitored.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: If the security architecture review (now part of SA-8) does not reference trustworthiness-specific mechanisms (like integrity verifications or secure boot processes), this is a red flag.
• Critical Indicator: Look for discrepancies between policy and actual system logs or audit trail data. Genuine implementation will show live evidence (e.g., timestamps, system alerts) of trustworthiness checks.
• Detail Matter: Authentic automation of trust assessments (via tools that validate code signatures or supply chain components) is far more reliable than self-declared compliance in policy documents.

By focusing on these checks, auditors can confidently determine whether the intent of SA-13 (trustworthiness) has been truly absorbed into the current security controls under SA-8, ensuring robust operational effectiveness.
content:
Statement:
[Withdrawn: Incorporated into SA-8.]

title: SUPPLY CHAIN PROTECTION
methodology:
Below is a practical methodology to validate the now‐withdrawn SA-12 (Supply Chain Protection) control (noting that its functions have been migrated into the SR family and other controls). The methodology focuses on demonstrating that the organization is effectively protecting its supply chain even though SA-12 as a stand-alone control is no longer active. The approach emphasizes visible operational evidence, technical artifacts, and continuous practices that are hard to fake.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: A live supply chain risk management (SCRM) process including real-time monitoring, audit logs, and inter-organizational agreements with vendors. Evidence of vendor risk assessments and supply chain due diligence documentation can be found in procurement and IT operations records.
• Technical Artifacts: Logs from procurement systems, asset management tools, and integrated risk management dashboards. These artifacts should show periodic supplier reviews, security validations for incoming shipments of hardware/software, and traceable certification of components.
• Operational Patterns: Regularly scheduled reviews of supplier security posture, updated vendor lists with risk ratings, and documented assessments using external intelligence feeds. This includes active remediation or escalation procedures when vulnerabilities or risks in the supply chain are detected.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Surface-Level Implementations: Organizations might publish policies and guidelines on supply chain protection without demonstrating actual ongoing review or detailed evidence of enforcement. Relying solely on documentation rather than operational evidence is common.
• Faked Compliance: Some organizations might create static, out-of-date vendor assessments or only perform periodic reviews as a checkbox exercise. Outward claims of ‘trusted’ vendor lists without linked artifacts (e.g., audit logs or supplier performance metrics) can be deceptive.
• Technical Gaps: Lack of automated logging of procurement activities, absence of integration between procurement and security logging systems, and failure to validate component authenticity beyond paper trails. Often, inter-organizational agreements are updated on paper but not actively enforced through systems.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
• CHECK: Verify that a documented, regularly reviewed supply chain risk management (SCRM) process is in active use.
  WHY: An operational SCRM process demonstrates that the organization is continuously evaluating supplier risk and protecting critical components.
  HOW: Interview procurement and IT security team members; review meeting minutes or logs showing scheduled assessments; and inspect real-time dashboards or reporting systems for supplier risk ratings.
  FAIL: Evidence shows only static policies without actual documented execution, or there’s no current meeting/activity log reflecting ongoing risk management.

• CHECK: Inspect audit logs from procurement and asset management systems for records that validate component authenticity and supplier performance.
  WHY: Reliable and automated logs are difficult to fake and indicate active monitoring of supply chain transactions and validations.
  HOW: Extract and review logs from procurement systems; identify entries for supplier validation, delivery verification, and component authenticity checks; check if these logs correlate with risk assessments or automated alerts.
  FAIL: Logs are missing, sparse, or show only manual, infrequent interventions with no integration into active risk management tools.

• CHECK: Validate that inter-organizational agreements or supplier contracts include specific supply chain security requirements.
  WHY: Enforceable agreements indicate that suppliers are contractually obligated to adhere to security standards, and any breach can trigger risk mitigation processes.
  HOW: Review a sample of vendor contracts or agreements; verify the inclusion of security requirements and monitor if contractual obligations are tied to periodic supplier reviews or compliance check-ins.
  FAIL: Contracts and agreements lack explicit security terms or are outdated, indicating that the organization may not truly enforce supply chain security with its suppliers.

• CHECK: Confirm that external threat intelligence and all-source intelligence are integrated into the supply chain risk assessment process.
  WHY: Leveraging external intelligence ensures that emerging supply chain threats are promptly detected and mitigated, providing a dynamic protective measure.
  HOW: Check for integration of threat feeds into the risk management system; review processes or automated alerts triggered by external threat discoveries related to suppliers or components.
  FAIL: No evidence of external intelligence usage; risk assessments remain reactive and based solely on internal reviews.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Short Verification Shortcut: Ask for “live” demonstrations of the supply chain dashboard and request a walkthrough of recent supplier risk cases. This on-the-spot demo can quickly reveal if the process is active or merely on paper.
• Critical Indicators of Poor Implementation: Outdated contracts, minimal log evidence, and infrequent or scheduled (rather than continuous) reviews are strong indicators that the supply chain protection mechanism is not effectively supported.
• Details that Matter Most: Emphasize correlating procurement system logs with vendor risk assessment updates. Look for automated alerts and cross-check them with documented follow-up actions. The existence of dynamic, automated processes is a solid indicator of real control effectiveness.

In summary, even though SA-12 has been withdrawn, assessing the implementation of the migrated supply chain protection measures across the SR and related controls reveals whether the organization actively minimizes supply chain risks rather than merely documenting policies.

title: DEVELOPER TESTING AND EVALUATION
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible test and evaluation artifacts such as an up‐to‐date and version-controlled security and privacy assessment plan with clearly logged scheduled tests.
• Automation logs, build pipeline integration, and reports from unit, integration, system, regression, static and dynamic code analysis tools that show regular execution of the tests.
• Incident and flaw remediation tickets or records that document issue detection, tracking, and resolution.
• Independent verification records or audit logs, if applicable, validating that testing and evaluation have been executed as defined.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may generate a policy document and a test plan that appears thorough on paper but lack automated evidence of consistent execution.
• Producing outdated or incomplete test reports—evidence may be simulated or manually created without linkage to actual code commits and CI/CD pipelines.
• Flaw remediation processes that exist only as a tick‐box exercise rather than showing a clear timeline of detection, investigation, and correction.
• Over-reliance on static documentation without demonstrable support from operational logs and tool outputs.

VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Verify the existence and version history of the security and privacy assessment plan
WHY: An up‑to‑date plan in source control with change-tracking proves that a structured process is in place and actively maintained.
HOW: Access the version control repository or centralized document management system and check for current date stamps, recent updates, and audit log history for the assessment plan.
FAIL: No evidence of a maintained plan, outdated documents without version history, or lack of visible updates in the tool chain.

CHECK: Inspect automated testing and evaluation logs (unit, integration, system, or regression tests)
WHY: Regular execution of tests via automated pipelines demonstrates operational effectiveness and routine assessments rather than just documented policy.
HOW: Review CI/CD logs and artifact repositories for evidence of scheduled tests running at organization-defined frequencies, including automated alerts and results summaries from static/dynamic code analysis tools.
FAIL: Infrequent or missing testing logs, inconsistencies between planned frequency and actual execution, or records showing tests were skipped.

CHECK: Validate recorded evidence of flaw remediation and the follow-up process
WHY: Demonstrating that detected flaws are documented, tracked, and remedied in a timely manner proves the remediation process is active and verifiable.
HOW: Audit ticketing and issue tracking systems to correlate test findings with remediation tickets; review timestamps, developer notes, and resolution evidence linked to identified flaws.
FAIL: Flaw tickets lacking proper details, no evidence of timely resolution, or remediation actions that do not correspond to actual test results.

CHECK: Review outputs from static and dynamic analysis tools
WHY: These outputs provide concrete evidence that automated security testing is integrated into the development process and reflect the depth of testing and evaluation.
HOW: Locate and examine reports from static code analysis, dynamic code analysis, penetration tests, or fuzz testing tools; review defect density metrics and follow-up remediation logs.
FAIL: Lack of such reports, reports indicating numerous unaddressed issues (high false positives), or evidence that tool outputs are not prioritized in remediation decisions.

CHECK: Confirm the inclusion of independent verification (if required) in the testing process
WHY: Independent verification provides an extra layer of assurance that the security assessment and flaw remediation processes are not solely self-reported, enhancing credibility.
HOW: Check for independent audit records, third-party reviews, or internal independent verification reports that cross-check the efficacy of the developer testing and evaluation plan.
FAIL: Absence of any audit or independent verification documentation, or evidence that such reviews were not conducted regularly.

EXPERT TIPS
• Short-cut verification by using a traceability matrix that links test cases to security controls ensures that all required controls are covered by automated and manual tests.
• Focus on artifact timestamps and integration with the CI/CD pipeline to avoid accepting static policy documents as evidence of active testing.
• Critical indicators of poor implementation include a high density of unresolved flaws, missing automated test logs, or independent reviews that raise concerns about remediation processes.
• Evaluate consistency: check that the frequency and depth of testing in operational logs match the organization's stated plan, as discrepancies suggest that the implementation might be superficial.
content:
Statement:
Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service, at all post-design stages of the system development life cycle, to:

a. Develop and implement a plan for ongoing security and privacy control assessments;

b. Perform [Selection (one or more): unit; integration; system; regression] testing/evaluation [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] at [Assignment: organization-defined depth and coverage];

c. Produce evidence of the execution of the assessment plan and the results of the testing and evaluation;

d. Implement a verifiable flaw remediation process; and

e. Correct flaws identified during testing and evaluation.

Guidance:
Developer testing and evaluation confirms that the required controls are implemented correctly, operating as intended, enforcing the desired security and privacy policies, and meeting established security and privacy requirements. Security properties of systems and the privacy of individuals may be affected by the interconnection of system components or changes to those components. The interconnections or changes—including upgrading or replacing applications, operating systems, and firmware—may adversely affect previously implemented controls. Ongoing assessment during development allows for additional types of testing and evaluation that developers can conduct to reduce or eliminate potential flaws. Testing custom software applications may require approaches such as manual code review, security architecture review, and penetration testing, as well as static analysis, dynamic analysis, binary analysis, or a hybrid of the three analysis approaches.

Developers can use the analysis approaches, along with security instrumentation and fuzzing, in a variety of tools and in source code reviews. The security and privacy assessment plans include the specific activities that developers plan to carry out, including the types of analyses, testing, evaluation, and reviews of software and firmware components; the degree of rigor to be applied; the frequency of the ongoing testing and evaluation; and the types of artifacts produced during those processes. The depth of testing and evaluation refers to the rigor and level of detail associated with the assessment process. The coverage of testing and evaluation refers to the scope (i.e., number and type) of the artifacts included in the assessment process. Contracts specify the acceptance criteria for security and privacy assessment plans, flaw remediation processes, and the evidence that the plans and processes have been diligently applied. Methods for reviewing and protecting assessment plans, evidence, and documentation are commensurate with the security category or classification level of the system. Contracts may specify protection requirements for documentation.

Enhancements:
DEVELOPER TESTING AND EVALUATION | STATIC CODE ANALYSIS
Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to employ static code analysis tools to identify common flaws and document the results of the analysis.
Discussion: Static code analysis provides a technology and methodology for security reviews and includes checking for weaknesses in the code as well as for the incorporation of libraries or other included code with known vulnerabilities or that are out-of-date and not supported. Static code analysis can be used to identify vulnerabilities and enforce secure coding practices. It is most effective when used early in the development process, when each code change can automatically be scanned for potential weaknesses. Static code analysis can provide clear remediation guidance and identify defects for developers to fix. Evidence of the correct implementation of static analysis can include aggregate defect density for critical defect types, evidence that defects were inspected by developers or security professionals, and evidence that defects were remediated. A high density of ignored findings, commonly referred to as false positives, indicates a potential problem with the analysis process or the analysis tool. In such cases, organizations weight the validity of the evidence against evidence from other sources.
Related Controls: None.
DEVELOPER TESTING AND EVALUATION | THREAT MODELING AND VULNERABILITY ANALYSES
Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to perform threat modeling and vulnerability analyses during development and the subsequent testing and evaluation of the system, component, or service that:
(a) Uses the following contextual information: [Assignment: organization-defined information concerning impact levels, environment of operations, known or assumed threats, and acceptable risk levels];
(b) Employs the following tools and methods: [Assignment: organization-defined tools and methods];
(c) Conducts the modeling and analyses at the following level of rigor: [Assignment: organization-defined breadth and depth of modeling and analyses]; and
(d) Produces evidence that meets the following acceptance criteria: [Assignment: organization-defined acceptance criteria].
Discussion: Systems, system components, and system services may deviate significantly from the functional and design specifications created during the requirements and design stages of the system development life cycle. Therefore, updates to threat modeling and vulnerability analyses of those systems, system components, and system services during development and prior to delivery are critical to the effective operation of those systems, components, and services. Threat modeling and vulnerability analyses at this stage of the system development life cycle ensure that design and implementation changes have been accounted for and that vulnerabilities created because of those changes have been reviewed and mitigated.
Related controls: PM-15, RA-3, RA-5.
DEVELOPER TESTING AND EVALUATION | INDEPENDENT VERIFICATION OF ASSESSMENT PLANS AND EVIDENCE
(a) Require an independent agent satisfying [Assignment: organization-defined independence criteria] to verify the correct implementation of the developer security and privacy assessment plans and the evidence produced during testing and evaluation; and
DEVELOPER TESTING AND EVALUATION | MANUAL CODE REVIEWS
Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to perform a manual code review of [Assignment: organization-defined specific code] using the following processes, procedures, and/or techniques: [Assignment: organization-defined processes, procedures, and/or techniques].
Discussion: Manual code reviews are usually reserved for the critical software and firmware components of systems. Manual code reviews are effective at identifying weaknesses that require knowledge of the application’s requirements or context that, in most cases, is unavailable to automated analytic tools and techniques, such as static and dynamic analysis. The benefits of manual code review include the ability to verify access control matrices against application controls and review detailed aspects of cryptographic implementations and controls.
Related Controls: None.
DEVELOPER TESTING AND EVALUATION | PENETRATION TESTING
Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to perform penetration testing: (a) At the following level of effort: [Assignment: organization-defined breadth and depth of testing]; (b) Under the following constraints: [Assignment: organization-defined constraints].
Discussion: Penetration testing is an assessment methodology in which assessors, using all available information technology product or system documentation and working under specific constraints, attempt to circumvent the implemented security features of information technology products and systems. Useful information for assessors who conduct penetration testing includes product and system design specifications, source code, and administrator and operator manuals. Penetration testing can include white-box, gray-box, or black-box testing with analyses performed by skilled professionals who simulate adversary actions. The objective of penetration testing is to discover vulnerabilities in systems, system components, and services that result from implementation errors, configuration faults, or other operational weaknesses or deficiencies. Penetration tests can be performed in conjunction with automated and manual code reviews to provide a greater level of analysis than would ordinarily be possible. When user session information and other personally identifiable information is captured or recorded during penetration testing, such information is handled appropriately to protect privacy.
Related Controls: CA-8, PM-14, MM-25, PT-2, SA-3, SI-2, SI-6.
DEVELOPER TESTING AND EVALUATION | ATTACK SURFACE REVIEWS
Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to perform attack surface reviews.
Discussion: Attack surfaces of systems and system components are exposed areas that make those systems more vulnerable to attacks. Attack surfaces include any accessible areas where weaknesses or deficiencies in the hardware, software, and firmware components provide opportunities for adversaries to exploit vulnerabilities. Attack surface reviews ensure that developers analyze the design and implementation changes to systems and when user session information and other personally identifiable information is captured or recorded during attack surface reviews, such information is handled appropriately to protect privacy.
Related Controls: None.
DEVELOPER TESTING AND EVALUATION | VERIFY SCOPE OF TESTING AND EVALUATION
Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to verify that the scope of testing and evaluation provides complete coverage of the required controls at the following level of rigor: [Assignment: organization-defined breadth and depth of testing and evaluation].
Discussion: Verifying that testing and evaluation provides complete coverage of required controls can be accomplished by a variety of analytic techniques ranging from informal to formal. Each of these techniques provides an increasing level of assurance that corresponds to the degree of formality of the analysis. Rigorously demonstrating control coverage at the highest levels of assurance can be achieved using formal modeling and analysis techniques, including correlation between control implementation and corresponding test cases.
Related Controls: SA-15.
DEVELOPER TESTING AND EVALUATION | DYNAMIC CODE ANALYSIS
Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to employ dynamic code analysis tools to identify common flaws and document the results of the analysis.
Discussion: Dynamic code analysis provides runtime verification of software programs using tools capable of monitoring programs for memory corruption, user privilege issues, and other potential security problems. Dynamic code analysis employs runtime tools to ensure functional correctness consequently performs in the way it was designed. A type of dynamic analysis, known as fuzz testing, induces program failures by deliberately introducing malformed or random data into software programs. Fuzz testing strategies are derived from the intended use of applications and the functional and design specifications for the applications. To understand the scope of dynamic code analysis and the assurance provided, organizations may also consider conducting code coverage analysis and/or concordance analysis.
Related Controls: None.
DEVELOPER TESTING AND EVALUATION | INTERACTIVE APPLICATION SECURITY TESTING
Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to employ interactive application security testing tools to identify flaws and document the results of the analysis.
Discussion: Interactive (also known as instrumentation-based) application security testing is a method of detecting vulnerabilities by observing applications as they run during testing. The use of instrumentation relies on direct measurements of the actual running applications and uses access to the code, user interaction, libraries, frameworks, backend connections, and configurations to directly measure control effectiveness. When combined with analysis techniques, interactive application security testing can identify a broad range of potential vulnerabilities and confirm control effectiveness.
Related Controls: None.

title: DEVELOPER CONFIGURATION MANAGEMENT
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for SA-10 Developer Configuration Management:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   - Visible evidence: A robust version control system (VCS) that records all changes (commits, merges) including secure one-way hash records, signed commit metadata, and time-stamped audit logs.
   - Technical artifacts: Change management tickets linked to source code commits; automated build systems that execute trusted generation comparisons; integrity verification logs for software, firmware, and hardware components; a maintained repository of master copies.
   - Operational patterns: Regular automated integrity checks, periodic reconciliation of master copies versus deployed versions, and routine reviews by security/privacy representatives to ensure only approved configuration changes are implemented.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   - Organizations sometimes rely on documentation or manual sign-offs without integrating automated tools or preserving detailed version history making it hard to verify the authenticity of changes.
   - Faking compliance by uploading generic audit reports or screenshots from the VCS that do not demonstrate linked change tickets or detailed commit trails.
   - Overlooking integration of trusted generation tools; for instance, an organization might perform build processes without automating comparisons between newly generated versions and previous trusted builds.
   - Ignoring the security and privacy oversight role, simply listing them on paper without demonstrating their active involvement in the configuration management process.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify version control system logs and commit histories for configuration items.
   WHY: Detailed logs with timestamps, commit IDs, one-way hash verifications, and documented approvals provide tangible evidence that configuration management is active and changes are traceable.
   HOW: Review the VCS (e.g., Git, SVN) for a sample of security-relevant files, checking for commit messages referencing change tickets, integrity hashes, and approvals. Ensure non-approved or ad hoc changes are not present.
   FAIL: Absence of detailed commit records, missing integrity verification data (e.g., hashes), or changes that cannot be linked to documented change requests indicate poor control.

   CHECK: Review the change management records for developer-initiated changes.
   WHY: Documented change requests and approval workflows ensure that only organization-approved changes are implemented, maintaining the integrity of configuration items.
   HOW: Select a sample of changes from the past review period and trace each change from the change management system (ticket system) to the actual VCS commit. Confirm that these changes were authorized and documented.
   FAIL: If commits exist without ticket references or if approval records are inconsistent or missing, this indicates a bypass of proper change control.

   CHECK: Validate the use and operation of automated integrity verification mechanisms (software/firmware/hardware).
   WHY: Automated integrity checks (using secure one-way hashes or similar verifications) help detect unauthorized modifications, preventing tampering.
   HOW: Examine build pipelines/logs that trigger integrity verification for new builds. Confirm that verification reports are logged and any discrepancies cause alerts or build failures.
   FAIL: The absence of automated logs or manual checks that are inconsistent, or verification that does not use secure hash methods, indicates ineffective enforcement.

   CHECK: Assess the trusted generation and mapping integrity processes for version control.
   WHY: Maintaining a verified map between current master copies (both in development and operational environments) ensures consistency and prevents unauthorized modifications.
   HOW: Verify that documentation or tooling exists for comparing newly generated versions of security-relevant code/descriptions with previous trusted versions. Interview the configuration management personnel and review tool outputs to confirm correct mapping.
   FAIL: Lack of any comparison tool logs or inability to correlate current builds with master build data signals a gap in integrity maintenance.

   CHECK: Confirm inclusion and active involvement of organization-defined security and privacy representatives in the configuration management process.
   WHY: Their oversight is critical for ensuring that configuration changes align with security policies and that integrity verification processes are not bypassed.
   HOW: Interview the designated security and privacy representatives and review meeting minutes or sign-off records on configuration management decisions. Check that they have reviewed recent configuration changes.
   FAIL: If representatives are listed without demonstrable evidence of reviews, or if their input is missing from recent change records, it suggests a nominal rather than an operational control.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   - Shortcut: Focus on automated tools’ outputs and cross-reference with manual change logs; this dual evidence minimizes the risk of superficial compliance.
   - Critical Indicator: Look for evidence that security-critical components (source code for security-related functionality, firmware) are treated differently with higher levels of scrutiny and automated checks.
   - Pitfall: Don’t be satisfied with the mere presence of a configuration management policy. Confirm that the process is integrated into the daily workflow of developers and is enforced by automated systems.
   - Detail: The linkage between change tickets and commits, plus secure hashing and timestamping, are key technical indicators that the developer configuration management process is truly operational and resistant to bypass attempts.

This methodology emphasizes a move away from paper-only compliance and ensures that technical artifacts and operational patterns are verifiable, minimizing risks of superficial or fake implementations.
content:
Statement:
Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to:
a. Perform configuration management during system, component, or service [Selection (one or more): design; development; implementation; operation; disposal];
b. Document, manage, and control the integrity of changes to [Assignment: organization-defined configuration items under configuration management];
c. Implement only organization-approved changes to the system, component, or service.

Guidance:
Organizations consider the quality and completeness of configuration management activities conducted by developers as direct evidence of applying effective security controls. Controls include protecting the master copies of material used to generate security-relevant portions of the system hardware, software, and firmware from unauthorized modification or destruction. Maintaining the integrity of changes to the system, system component, or system service requires strict configuration control throughout the system development life cycle to track authorized changes and prevent unauthorized changes.

The configuration items that are placed under configuration management include the formal model; the functional, high-level, and low-level design specifications; other design data; implementation documentation; source code and hardware schematics; the current running version of the object code; tools for comparing new versions of security-relevant hardware descriptions and source code with previous versions; and test fixtures and documentation. Depending on the mission and business needs of organizations and the nature of the contractual relationships in place, developers may provide configuration management support during the operations and maintenance stage of the system development life cycle.

Enhancements:
DEVELOPER CONFIGURATION MANAGEMENT | SOFTWARE AND FIRMWARE INTEGRITY VERIFICATION
Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to enable integrity verification of software and firmware components.
Discussion: Software and firmware integrity verification allows organizations to detect unauthorized changes to software and firmware components using developer-provided tools, technologies, and mechanisms. The integrity checking mechanisms can also address counterfeiting of software and firmware components. Organizations verify the integrity of software and firmware components, for example, through secure one-way hashes provided by developers. Delivered software and firmware components also include any updates to such components.
Related Controls: SI-7, SR-11.
DEVELOPER CONFIGURATION MANAGEMENT | ALTERNATIVE CONFIGURATION MANAGEMENT PROCESSES
Provide an alternate configuration management process using organizational personnel in the absence of a dedicated developer configuration management team.
Discussion: Alternate configuration management processes may be required when organizations use commercial off-the-shelf information technology products. Alternate configuration management processes include organizational personnel who review and approve proposed changes to systems, system components, and system services and conduct security and privacy impact analyses prior to the implementation of changes to systems, components, or services.
Related Controls: None.
DEVELOPER CONFIGURATION MANAGEMENT | HARDWARE INTEGRITY VERIFICATION
Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to enable integrity verification of hardware components.
Discussion: Hardware integrity verification allows organizations to detect unauthorized changes to hardware components using developer-provided tools, techniques, methods, and mechanisms. Organizations may verify the integrity of hardware components with hard-to-copy labels, verifiable serial numbers provided by developers, and by requiring the use of anti-tamper technologies. Delivered hardware components also include hardware and firmware updates to such components.
Related Controls: SI-7.
DEVELOPER CONFIGURATION MANAGEMENT | TRUSTED GENERATION
Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to employ tools for comparing newly generated versions of security-relevant hardware descriptions, source code, and object code with previous versions.
Discussion: The trusted generation of descriptions, source code, and object code addresses authorized changes to hardware, software, and firmware components between versions during development. The focus is on the efficacy of the configuration management process by the developer to ensure that newly generated versions of security-relevant hardware descriptions, source code, and object code continue to enforce the security policy for the system, system component, or system service.
Related Controls: None.
DEVELOPER CONFIGURATION MANAGEMENT | MAPPING INTEGRITY FOR VERSION CONTROL
Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to maintain the integrity of the relationship between the master build data describing the current version of security-relevant hardware, software, and firmware and the on-site master copy of the data for the current version.
Discussion: Mapping integrity for version control addresses changes to hardware, software, and firmware components during both initial development and system development life cycle updates. Maintaining the integrity between the master copies of security-relevant hardware, software, and firmware (including designs, hardware drawings, source code) and the equivalent data in master copies in operational environments is essential to ensuring the availability of organizational systems that support critical mission and business functions.
Related Controls: None.
DEVELOPER CONFIGURATION MANAGEMENT | TRUSTED DISTRIBUTION
Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to execute procured uses for ensuring that security-relevant hardware, software, and firmware updates distributed to the organization are exactly as specified by the master copies.
Discussion: The trusted distribution of security-relevant hardware, software, and firmware updates helps to ensure that the updates are correct representations of the master copies maintained by the developer and have not been tampered with during distribution.
Related Controls: None.
DEVELOPER CONFIGURATION MANAGEMENT | SECURITY AND PRIVACY REPRESENTATIVES
Require [Assignment: organization-defined security and privacy representatives] to be included in the [Assignment: organization-defined configuration management and control process].
Discussion: Information security and privacy representatives can include system security officers, senior agency information security officers, senior agency officials for privacy, and system privacy officers. Representation by personnel with information security and privacy.

title: EXTERNAL SYSTEM SERVICES
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• CHECK: Verification of contractual agreements, SLAs, or interagency agreements that include specific security and privacy requirements for external systems.
  WHY: These documents prove that external providers are contractually bound to meet defined security standards and controls.
  HOW: Review a sample of current agreements to confirm inclusion of organization-defined security controls; check for supporting evidence such as signed amendments or oversight communication logs.
  FAIL: Absence or vagueness in contractual language regarding security controls, or agreements simply referencing vendor policies without meeting internal standards.

• CHECK: Documentation and evidence of organizational oversight processes, including defined user roles and responsibilities for managing external service providers.
  WHY: This confirms that the organization has clear accountability and procedures to manage external service risks.
  HOW: Examine organizational charts, role descriptions, and meeting minutes or escalations that detail oversight of external systems; compare outlined processes with actual operational practices.
  FAIL: Lack of documented oversight efforts, undefined roles, or evidence that responsibility for managing external risks is not regularly enforced.

• CHECK: Operational evidence of ongoing monitoring of external service provider compliance, such as regular reports, audit logs, or monitoring dashboards.
  WHY: Ongoing monitoring is essential to ensure that providers continuously meet the agreed-upon security and privacy requirements.
  HOW: Validate that monitoring tools are actively collecting data; check sample incident records or compliance reports submitted by external providers, and verify review cycles via system logs or audit outcomes.
  FAIL: Reliance solely on annual reviews, absent or outdated monitoring logs, or reports that are not tied to active enforcement measures.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Many organizations simply attach a compliance contract without following up with enforcement: contracts may list security requirements but lack operational monitoring evidence.
• Security controls may be self-attested or recast in reports from external providers without independent validation.
• Surface-level implementation such as scheduling periodic reviews without capturing real-time security incidents or deviations.

VALIDATION STEPS:
1. CHECK: Contract/Service-Level Agreement Review
   WHY: Demonstrates that external providers are contractually obligated to meet security requirements.
   HOW: Randomly select several contracts and agreements to verify inclusion of all organization-defined controls, references to oversight responsibilities, and clear remediation processes.
   FAIL: Contracts missing detailed security control requirements or oversight provisions.

2. CHECK: Oversight Documentation
   WHY: Confirms the defined roles and responsibilities for managing external service relationships.
   HOW: Review documented oversight policies, user role assignments, and meeting records that discuss external service performance and risk assessments.
   FAIL: Lack of formal documentation or evidence that roles/responsibilities are actively used in vendor management.

3. CHECK: Monitoring and Reporting Mechanisms
   WHY: Provides tangible evidence that external provider compliance is actively monitored on an ongoing basis.
   HOW: Inspect automated monitoring dashboards, review logs of security incidents related to external services, and verify that compliance reports are generated, reviewed, and acted upon at agreed intervals.
   FAIL: Missing or static monitoring dashboards, outdated logs, or lack of regular review cycles.

4. CHECK: Audit Trails and Independent Assessments
   WHY: Independent audits verify actual compliance and effectiveness beyond self-attested reports.
   HOW: Obtain copies of recent independent audit reports or external assessments that review the performance of external service providers against the contractual security requirements.
   FAIL: Absence of independent audit reports or evidence that audits are not conducted regularly.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Shortcut: Use dashboard tools or SIEM systems that automatically aggregate compliance and monitoring data; these tools are hard to fake and provide operational insights.
• Critical Indicator: Look for discrepancies between contract terms and operational implementation; contracts without active oversight and reporting often signal a superficial implementation.
• Detail Matters: Check that the oversight documentation clearly reflects real-time, dynamic monitoring rather than static, annual checklists.
• Insider Hint: Cross-reference incident reports with contractual remedial actions to ensure that notifications and responses are timely and effective.

This methodology emphasizes not just the existence of policies and agreements but verifies through multiple layers of automated and operational evidence that the external service controls are actively enforced and monitored.
content:
Statement:
a. Require that providers of external system services comply with organizational security and privacy requirements and employ the following controls: [Assignment: organization-defined controls];
b. Define and document organizational oversight and user roles and responsibilities with regard to external system services; and
c. Employ the following processes, methods, and techniques to monitor control compliance by external service providers on an ongoing basis: [Assignment: organization-defined processes, methods, and techniques].

Guidance:
External system services are provided by an external provider, and the organization has no direct control over the implementation of the required controls or the assessment of control effectiveness. Organizations establish relationships with external service providers in a variety of ways, including through business partnerships, contracts, interagency agreements, lines of business arrangements, licensing agreements, joint ventures, and supply chain exchanges. The responsibility for managing risks from the use of external system services remains with authorizing officials. For services external to organizations, a chain of trust requires that organizations establish and retain a certain level of confidence that each provider in the consumer-provider relationship provides adequate protection for the services rendered. The extent and nature of this chain of trust are based on relationships between organizations and the external providers. Organizations document the basis for the trust relationships so that the relationships can be monitored. External system services documentation includes government, consumer providers, and user security roles and responsibilities, and service-level agreements. Service-level agreements define the expectations of performance for implemented controls, describe measurable outcomes, and identify remedies and response requirements for identified instances of noncompliance.

title: SECURITY AND PRIVACY ENGINEERING PRINCIPLES
methodology:
Below is a comprehensive methodology for validating the application of systems security and privacy engineering principles (SA-8) throughout the system lifecycle:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible evidence includes documented design blueprints, architecture review records, and system diagrams that explicitly incorporate principles such as clear abstractions, least common mechanism, modularity and layering, and partially ordered dependencies.
   • Technical artifacts include system design documents, threat modeling outputs, configuration files, and code base architecture reviews that demonstrate how these principles are embedded in design and implementation.
   • Operational patterns include regular design walkthroughs, integration testing that validates layer separations, and evidence of developer training and reviews focused on secure design principles.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations may simply produce high-level documentation without corresponding, practical implementations in code or configuration. For instance, an architectural diagram might claim layered protections without verifying that actual system boundaries are enforced.
   • Surface-level implementations include checklist-based reviews where design documents mention security principles but lack operational follow-through (e.g., not integrating threat modeling into iterative development).
   • Technical gaps often include ambiguous responsibility for enforcing least common mechanism – for example, sharing system resources among modules without proper isolation or audit trail mechanisms.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   • CHECK: Architectural Design Documentation Review
     WHY: Valid design documents that articulate clear abstractions, separation of duties and modular boundaries indicate that the system is built using the defined security principles.
     HOW: Examine system architecture documents, diagrams, and design review minutes. Validate that principles such as modularity, layering, and least common mechanism are explicitly addressed. Interview system architects for elaboration.
     FAIL: Absence of explicit reference to security engineering principles or reliance on generic diagrams without detail on how each principle is applied.

   • CHECK: Code and Configuration Analysis for Isolation Mechanisms
     WHY: Reviewing code and configuration for evidence of enforced component boundaries (e.g., different modules accessing system resources independently) demonstrates operational application of least common mechanism and modularity.
     HOW: Analyze the source code repositories and configuration files to identify strictly partitioned modules. Look for audit logs and code comments that explain the use of independent mechanisms rather than shared libraries for crucial functions.
     FAIL: Shared or monolithic code components that do not clearly delineate responsibilities or resources, with no audit trail demonstrating proper isolation.

   • CHECK: Threat Modeling and Risk Assessment Evidence
     WHY: Regular threat modeling sessions that incorporate these principles (layering, partially ordered dependencies) serve as live evidence that the organization is actively engaging the principles throughout the lifecycle.
     HOW: Review threat models, risk assessments, and mitigation plans. Confirm that these documents address inter-component communication, dependency mapping, and resource isolation. Validate that findings led to design adjustments.
     FAIL: Outdated or generic threat models that fail to specifically incorporate or test for adherence to systems security and privacy engineering principles.

   • CHECK: Developer Training and Compliance Assessment
     WHY: Training materials and records demonstrate that developers understand and apply these principles in practice, ensuring consistency between design and implementation.
     HOW: Request evidence of secure systems engineering training sessions, developer workshops, and post-training assessments. Check that training materials contain dedicated segments on clear abstractions, modularity, and least common mechanism.
     FAIL: Lack of targeted training or absence of assessment records, indicating that developers may not be effectively applying these principles in coding or design reviews.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: Start by scanning architectural and design documentation. If these do not clearly articulate principles like modularity and layering, then it is a strong indicator that implementation may be superficial, even if policies exist.
   • Critical Indicator: The presence of iterative threat modeling sessions that evolve with system upgrades is a robust sign that security principles are dynamically integrated into the SDLC.
   • Detail Alert: Ensure that enforcement is not just at the design stage. Validate that deployment configurations, runtime isolation, and access control implementations are consistent with the declared principles.
   • Remember: Look for evidence that not only documents these principles but also demonstrates corrective actions when deviations or vulnerabilities are identified.

By following these validation steps, auditors can ensure that security and privacy engineering principles are not only well-documented but effectively translated into the secure and resilient operation of the system.
content:
Statement:
Apply the following systems security and privacy engineering principles in the specification, design, development, implementation, and modification of the system and system components: [Assignment: organization-defined systems security and privacy engineering principles].

Guidance:
Systems security and privacy engineering principles are closely related to and implemented throughout the system development life cycle (see SA-3). Organizations can apply systems security and privacy engineering principles to new systems under development or to systems undergoing upgrades. For existing systems, organizations apply systems security and privacy engineering principles to system upgrades and modifications to the extent feasible, given the current state of hardware, software, and firmware components within those systems.

The application of systems security and privacy engineering principles helps organizations develop trustworthy, secure, and resilient systems and reduces the susceptibility to disruptions, hazards, threats, and the creation of privacy problems for individuals. Examples of system security engineering principles include: developing layered protections; establishing security and privacy policies, architecture, and controls as the foundation for design and development; incorporating security and privacy requirements into the system development life cycle; delineating physical and logical security boundaries; ensuring that developers are trained on how to build secure software; tailoring controls to meet organizational needs; and performing threat modeling to identify use cases, threat agents, attack vectors and patterns, design patterns, and compensating controls needed to mitigate risk.

Organizations that apply systems security and privacy engineering concepts and principles can facilitate the development of trustworthy, secure systems, system components, and system services; reduce risk to acceptable levels; and make informed risk management decisions. System security engineering principles can also be used to protect against certain supply chain risks, including incorporating tamper-resistant hardware into a design.

Enhancements:
(1) SECURITY AND PRIVACY ENGINEERING PRINCIPLES | CLEAR ABSTRACTIONS
Implement the security design principle of clear abstractions. Discussion: The principle of clear abstractions states that a system has simple, well-defined interfaces and functions that provide a consistent and intuitive view of the data and how the data is managed. The clarity, simplicity, necessity, and sufficiency of the system interfaces—
(2) SECURITY AND PRIVACY ENGINEERING PRINCIPLES | LEAST COMMON MECHANISM
Implement the security design principle of least common mechanism in [Assignment: organization-defined systems or system components].
Discussion: The principle of least common mechanism states that the amount of mechanism common to more than one user and depended on by all users is minimized [POPEK74]. Mechanism minimization implies that different components of a system refrain from using the same mechanism to access a system resource. Every shared mechanism (especially a mechanism involving shared variables) represents a potential information path between users and is designed with care to ensure that it does not unintentionally compromise security [SALTZER75]. Implementing the principle of least common mechanism helps to reduce the adverse consequences of sharing the system state among different programs. A single program that corrupts a shared state (including shared variables) has the potential to corrupt other programs that are dependent on the state. The principle of least common mechanism also supports the principle of simplicity of design and addresses the issue of covert storage channels [LAMPSON73]. Related Controls: None.
(3) SECURITY AND PRIVACY ENGINEERING PRINCIPLES | MODULARITY AND LAYERING
Implement the security design principle of modularity and layering in [Assignment: organization-defined systems or system components].
Discussion: The principles of modularity and layering are fundamental across system engineering disciplines. Modularity and layering derived from functional decomposition are effective in managing system complexity by making it possible to comprehend the structure of the system. Modular decomposition, or refinement in system design, is challenging and resists general statements of principle. Modularity serves to isolate functions and related data structures into well-defined logical units. Layering allows the relationships of these units to be better understood so that dependencies are clear and undesired complexity can be avoided. The security design principle of modularity extends functional modularity to include considerations based on trust, trustworthiness, privilege, and security policy. Security-informed modular decomposition includes the allocation of policies to systems in a network, separation of system applications into processes with distinct address spaces, allocation of system policies to layers, and separation of processes into subjects with distinct privileges based on hardware-supported privilege domains. Related Controls: SC-2, SC-3.
(4) SECURITY AND PRIVACY ENGINEERING PRINCIPLES | PARTIALLY ORDERED DEPENDENCIES
Implement the security design principle of partially ordered dependencies in [Assignment: organization-defined systems or system components].
Discussion: The principle of partially ordered dependencies states that the synchronization, calling, and other dependencies in the system are partially ordered. A fundamental concept in system design is layering, whereby the system is organized into well-defined, functionally 
CHAPTER THREE PAGE 258

title: SYSTEM DOCUMENTATION
methodology:
Below is a comprehensive methodology for validating implementation of SA-5 (System Documentation) controls.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Comprehensive administrator documentation is visibly stored in a centralized repository or configuration management system. This documentation details secure configuration, installation, and operational procedures, and includes sections on effective use and maintenance of security mechanisms and known vulnerabilities related to administrative functions.
• User documentation is readily accessible and clearly explains how users can interact securely with the system, use the security and privacy functions, and understand their responsibilities for maintaining security and privacy.
• Evidence exists of documented attempts (e.g., tickets, logs, emails) to obtain or recreate documentation when vendor or original documentation isn’t available. There is also evidence of distribution records to designated personnel or roles.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may produce generic documentation that superficially meets policy requirements without covering all the detailed topics (e.g., missing sections on known vulnerabilities or secure recovery procedures).
• Static documentation that is outdated or not linked to the current system configuration may be presented as compliant, despite not reflecting current operational practices.
• Documentation distribution claims without audit logs or verifiable distribution lists can be used to fake compliance.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Verify the administrator documentation covers secure configuration, installation, operation procedures, effective use/maintenance of security functions, and known vulnerabilities.
WHY: Comprehensive documentation for administrators ensures that system controls and configurations are understood, enabling proper security management.
HOW: Review the stored documentation repository (e.g., internal wiki or document management system) for sections addressing secure configuration, operational procedures, vulnerability management, and maintenance instructions; interview system administrators for confirmation.
FAIL: Missing or skeletal sections on installation, configuration, operation, or vulnerability details indicates a failure to meet control requirements.

CHECK: Verify that user documentation includes content on using security and privacy functions, methods for secure system interaction, and clear user responsibilities.
WHY: Well-targeted user documentation is critical to ensure that end users utilize the system securely and maintain overall system integrity.
HOW: Inspect a sample of user documentation distributed to staff; check for clear instructions and responsibilities, and correlate with training records or distribution logs.
FAIL: Absence of detailed instructions on security functions or unclear user responsibilities signals non-compliance.

CHECK: Confirm documented procedures and logs related to attempts to acquire system documentation when unavailable.
WHY: Tracking of procurement or recreation efforts proves that the organization has responded to gaps in vendor-provided documentation.
HOW: Examine ticketing systems, email trails, or incident logs where attempts to obtain documentation were recorded, including any mitigation documentation actions taken.
FAIL: Lack of any records on efforts to obtain or recreate documentation indicates a failure in managing documentation gaps.

CHECK: Validate distribution procedures for the documentation to organization-defined personnel or roles.
WHY: Ensuring that the respective staff have timely access to correct documentation ensures adherence to secure operational practices.
HOW: Check distribution lists, release announcements, or training session records that confirm the correct distribution channels and a version-control mechanism in place.
FAIL: If documentation is only available on a central server without logs demonstrating proper distribution, or if targeted staff are unaware of its existence, this indicates poor implementation.

CHECK: Check that documentation is up-to-date and aligns with current system configurations and known vulnerabilities.
WHY: Currency and alignment with the live system are crucial for ensuring that documentation reflects the actual security posture.
HOW: Compare recent system configuration changes (e.g., from version control or change management records) with the current documentation, and review audit logs for documentation update frequency.
FAIL: Outdated or mismatching documentation relative to the actual system configuration signifies a failure.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Verification shortcuts: Ask administrators and end-users during interviews for concrete examples of using the documentation during a recent incident or configuration change. This often reveals how well the documentation is maintained and used.
• Critical Indicator: Pay extra attention to sections detailing known vulnerabilities – an area where poor documentation can lead to significant risk if not updated or thorough.
• Detail Focus: Ensure the documentation has appropriate version control and distribution logs. Without these, it’s difficult to assert that the right personnel have the correct information in a timely manner.
• Automated Evidence: Utilize document management systems that log access and updates, as these logs are less susceptible to fabrication compared to standalone spreadsheets or email confirmations.
• Check for integration: Look for evidence of the documentation being referenced within incident response or change management processes, indicating its role in operational security.

This methodology emphasizes practical checks based on observable, autonomous evidence rather than relying solely on stated policies or theoretical documentation.
content:
Statement:
a. Obtain or develop administrator documentation for the system, system component, or system service that describes:

1. Secure configuration, installation, and operation of the system, component, or service;

2. Effective use and maintenance of security and privacy functions and mechanisms; and

3. Known vulnerabilities regarding configuration and use of administrative or privileged functions;

b. Obtain or develop user documentation for the system, system component, or system service that describes:

1. User-accessible security and privacy functions and mechanisms and how to effectively use those functions and mechanisms;

2. Methods for user interaction, which enables individuals to use the system, component, or service in a more secure manner and protect individual privacy; and

3. User responsibilities in maintaining the security of the system, component, or service and privacy of individuals;

c. Document attempts to obtain system, system component, or system service documentation when such documentation is either unavailable or nonexistent and take [Assignment: organization-defined actions] in response; and

d. Distribute documentation to [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles].

Guidance:
System documentation helps personnel understand the implementation and operation of controls. Organizations consider establishing specific measures to determine the quality and completeness of the content provided. System documentation may be used to support the management of supply chain risk, incident response, and other functions. Personnel or roles that require documentation include system owners, system security officers, and system administrators. Attempts to obtain documentation include contacting manufacturers or suppliers and conducting web-based searches. The inability to obtain documentation may occur due to the age of the system or component or the lack of support from developers and contractors. When documentation cannot be obtained, organizations may need to recreate the documentation if it is essential to the implementation or operation of the controls. The protection provided by the documentation is commensurate with the security category or classification of the system. Documentation that addresses system vulnerabilities may require an increased level of protection. Secure operation of the system includes initially starting the system and resuming secure system operation after a lapse in system operation.

Enhancements:
(1) SYSTEM DOCUMENTATION | FUNCTIONAL PROPERTIES OF SECURITY CONTROLS [Withdrawn: Incorporated into SA-4(1).]
(2) SYSTEM DOCUMENTATION | SECURITY-RELEVANT EXTERNAL SYSTEM INTERFACES [Withdrawn: Incorporated into SA-4(2).]
(3) SYSTEM DOCUMENTATION | HIGH-LEVEL DESIGN [Withdrawn: Incorporated into SA-4(2).]
(4) SYSTEM DOCUMENTATION | LOW-LEVEL DESIGN [Withdrawn: Incorporated into SA-4(2).]
(5) SYSTEM DOCUMENTATION | SOURCE CODE [Withdrawn: Incorporated into SA-4(2).]

title: ACQUISITION PROCESS
methodology:
Below is a complete validation methodology for SA-4, Acquisition Process, which ensures that acquisition contracts incorporate tailored, enforceable security and privacy requirements throughout the system lifecycle.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
 • Visible Evidence: Actual acquisition contracts and associated addenda that explicitly include security and privacy functional requirements, strength of mechanism requirements, assurance requirements, and documentation requirements. Look for language that references either standardized or organization-defined contract clauses.
 • Technical Artifacts: Signed contracts, change orders, and technical documentation such as high-level design documents, low-level design outputs, configurations for security settings (e.g., default security configurations, STIGs, or USGCB compliance documents), and continuous monitoring plans that are integrated into an organization’s security program.
 • Operational Patterns: Regular reviews or audits of acquisition-related documentation; active supplier management including periodic reassessments of vendor security documentation; and integration of acceptance criteria and continuous monitoring plans as part of the procurement lifecycle.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
 • Faked Compliance: Organizations sometimes insert generic contract language copied from previous engagements without ensuring it aligns with the specific system’s security/privacy needs. They may show policies without evidence that the contract requirements are actively enforced during vendor development and integration.
 • Surface-Level Implementations: Inclusion of templated documentation that is not updated or tailored to reflect the technical reality of the system. For example, generic continuous monitoring plans that are never integrated within the security monitoring tools or incomplete details in the design documentation (e.g., missing specifics of control implementation).
 • Frequently Missed Technical Gaps: Inadequate linkage between contract clauses and operational security control testing, absence of detailed evidence (such as up-to-date design and implementation info), and failure to verify that default configurations and acceptance criteria are enforced in live systems.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK 1: Review Acquisition Contracts for Specific Security & Privacy Clauses
• WHY: Ensures that the contract explicitly requires security and privacy functional, strength, assurance, and documentation requirements.
• HOW: Select a sample of current acquisition contracts; verify that they include detailed sections or references to organization-defined (or standardized) security and privacy clauses covering items (a) – (i) of SA-4.
• FAIL: If the sample contract contains only generic legal language without technical details or fails to reference the needed security & privacy requirements.

CHECK 2: Validate Integration of Continuous Monitoring Plans
• WHY: Demonstrates that the acquisitions process includes planning and operational oversight for control effectiveness over time.
• HOW: Request a copy of the developer’s continuous monitoring plan; check if it is synchronized with the organizational continuous monitoring program and if it addresses changes in control implementation.
• FAIL: If the continuous monitoring plan is absent, purely theoretical, or not integrated into the overall security monitoring program.

CHECK 3: Verify Documentation of Design & Implementation Information
• WHY: Confirms the acquisition process mandates sufficient technical detail in the design/implementation artifacts (e.g., high-level and low-level design documents, schematics, source code references) to enforce security controls.
• HOW: Review the developer-provided documentation, ensuring it contains specific details on security configuration, system development lifecycle methods, and acceptance criteria. Verify artifacts against the contract requirements.
• FAIL: If documentation is overly generic, missing detailed system partitioning, or failing to specify security-relevant interfaces and configurations.

CHECK 4: Assess Supplier Use of Approved Technologies and Configurations
• WHY: Ensures that only government off-the-shelf (GOTS)/commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) technologies meeting approved standards (e.g., NSA-approved, NIAP, FIPS-validated if applicable) are deployed.
• HOW: Examine procurement records and technical evaluations; verify that suppliers have provided evidence of meeting approved criteria and that security configurations (e.g., default password changes, STIGs, USGCB) are enforced in the system.
• FAIL: If there is no evidence of supplier adherence to approved criteria, or if the products in use haven’t been validated against required protection profiles.

CHECK 5: Confirm Allocation of Responsibilities within the Contract
• WHY: Demonstrates clear assignment of responsibilities between the organization and the vendor regarding information security, privacy, and supply chain risk management.
• HOW: Inspect contract documentation for clear statements detailing party responsibilities, addressing accountability for security documentation protection and implementation of controls.
• FAIL: If roles and responsibilities are vague, omitted, or merely referenced in a generic manner without clear assignment.

4. EXPERT TIPS
 • Shortcut: When reviewing contracts, use keyword searches (e.g., “security functional requirements”, “continuous monitoring”) to quickly locate relevant sections.
 • Key Indicator of Poor Implementation: A contract that merely references a generic policy or standard without detailing technical, operational, and evaluation requirements; this often signals a checkbox exercise rather than active enforcement.
 • Critical Detail: Ensure that all contract language is not only present on paper but that subsequent technical artifacts and monitored operational processes reflect these commitments. Look for evidence of periodic updates in the design and continuous monitoring documentation.
 • Insider Note: Cross-check the acquisition contract details with the actual vendor-provided deliverables during system development reviews to validate that contracts are being operationalized as intended.

This methodology emphasizes a blend of automated evidence review (through sample contract verification, artifact checks, monitoring logs) and manual interviews/document analysis, focusing on real-world enforcement and integration of the specified security and privacy requirements into the acquisition process.
content:
Statement:
Include the following requirements, descriptions, and criteria, explicitly or by reference, using [Selection (one or more): standardized contract language; [Assignment: organization-defined contract language]] in the acquisition contract for the system, system component, or system service:

a. Security and privacy functional requirements;
b. Strength of mechanism requirements;
c. Security and privacy assurance requirements;
d. Controls needed to satisfy the security and privacy requirements.

e. Security and privacy documentation requirements;
f. Requirements for protecting security and privacy documentation;
g. Description of the system development environment and environment in which the system is intended to operate;
h. Allocation of responsibility or identification of parties responsible for information security, privacy, and supply chain risk management; and

i. Acceptance criteria.

Guidance:
Security and privacy functional requirements are typically derived from the high-level security and privacy requirements described in SA-2. The derived requirements include security and privacy capabilities, functions, and mechanisms. Strength requirements associated with such capabilities, functions, and mechanisms include degree of correctness, completeness, resistance to tampering or bypass, and resistance to direct attack. Assurance requirements include development processes, procedures, and methodologies as well as the evidence from development and assessment activities that provide grounds for confidence that the required functionality is implemented and possesses the required strength of mechanism. [SP 800-160-1] describes the process of requirements engineering as part of the system development life cycle.

Controls can be viewed as descriptions of the safeguards and protection capabilities appropriate for achieving the particular security and privacy objectives of the organization and for reflecting the security and privacy requirements of stakeholders. Controls are selected and implemented in order to satisfy system requirements and include developer and organizational responsibilities. Controls can include technical, administrative, and physical aspects. In some cases, the selection and implementation of a control may necessitate additional specification by the organization in the form of derived requirements or instantiated control parameter values. The derived requirements and control parameter values may be necessary to provide the appropriate level of implementation detail for controls within the system development life cycle.

Security and privacy documentation requirements address all stages of the system development life cycle. Documentation provides user and administrator guidance for the implementation and operation of controls. The level of detail required in such documentation is based on the security categorization or classification level of the system and the degree to which organizations depend on the capabilities, functions, or mechanisms to meet risk response expectations. Requirements can include mandated configuration settings that specify allowed functions, ports, protocols, and services. Acceptance criteria for systems, system components, and system services are defined in the same manner as the criteria for any organizational acquisition or procurement.

Enhancements:
(1) ACQUISITION PROCESS | FUNCTIONAL PROPERTIES OF CONTROLS
Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to provide a description of the functional properties of the controls to be implemented. 
Discussion: Functional properties of security and privacy controls describe the functionality (i.e., security or privacy capability, functions, or mechanisms) visible at the interfaces of the controls and specifically exclude functionality and data structures internal to the operation of the controls. 
Related Controls: None.
(2) ACQUISITION PROCESS | DESIGN AND IMPLEMENTATION INFORMATION FOR CONTROLS
Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to provide design and implementation information for the controls that includes: [Selection (one or more): security-relevant external system interfaces; high-level design; low-level design; source code or hardware schematics; [Assignment: organization-defined design and implementation information]] at [Assignment: organization-defined level of detail]. 
Discussion: Organizations may require different levels of detail in the documentation for the design and implementation of controls in organizational systems, system components, or system services based on mission and business requirements, requirements for resiliency and trustworthiness, and requirements for analysis and testing.
