Systems can be partitioned into multiple subsystems. Each subsystem within the system can consist of one or more modules. The high-level design for the system is expressed in terms of subsystems and the interfaces between subsystems providing security-relevant functionality. The low-level design for the system is expressed in terms of modules and the interfaces between modules providing security-relevant functionality. Design and implementation documentation can include manufacturer, version, serial number, verification, installation, software bundles used, date of purchase or download, and the vendor or download source. Source code and hardware schematics are referred to as the implementation representation of the system. 
Related Controls: None.
(3) ACQUISITION PROCESS | DEVELOPMENT METHODS, TECHNIQUES, AND PRACTICES
Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to demonstrate the use of a system development life cycle process that includes: (a) [Assignment: organization-defined systems engineering methods]; (b) [Assignment: organization-defined Selection (one or more): systems security; privacy] engineering methods]; and (c) [Assignment: organization-defined software development methods; testing, evaluation, assessment, verification, and validation methods; and quality control processes]. 
Discussion: Following a system development life cycle that includes state-of-the-practice software development methods, systems engineering methods, systems security and privacy engineering methods, and quality control processes helps to reduce the number and severity of latent errors within systems, system components, and system services. Reducing the number and severity of such errors reduces the number of vulnerabilities in those systems, components, and services. Transparency in the methods and techniques that developers select and implement for systems engineering, systems security and privacy engineering, software development, component and system assessments, and quality control processes provides an increased level of assurance in the trustworthiness of the system, system component, or system service being acquired. 
Related Controls: None.
(4) ACQUISITION PROCESS | ASSIGNMENT OF COMPONENTS TO SYSTEMS
(5) ACQUISITION PROCESS | SYSTEM, COMPONENT, AND SERVICE CONFIGURATIONS
Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to: (a) Deliver the system, component, or service with [Assignment: organization-defined security configurations] implemented; and (b) Use the configurations as the default for any subsequent system, component, or service reinstallation or upgrade.
Discussion: Examples of security configurations include the U.S. Government Configuration Baseline (USGCB), Security Technical Implementation Guides (STIGs), and any limitations on functions, ports, protocols, and services. Security characteristics can include requiring that default passwords have been changed. 
Related Controls: None.
(6) ACQUISITION PROCESS | USE OF INFORMATION ASSURANCE PRODUCTS
(a) Employ only government off-the-shelf or commercial off-the-shelf information assurance and information assurance-enabled information technology products that compose an NSA-approved solution to protect classified information when the networks used to transmit the information are at a lower classification level than the information being transmitted; and (b) Ensure that these products have been evaluated and/or validated by NSA or in accordance with NSA-approved procedures.
Discussion: Commercial off-the-shelf IA or IA-enabled information technology products used to protect classified information by cryptographic means may be required to use NSA-approved key management. See [NSA CSFC].  
Related Controls: SC-8, SC-12, SC-13.
(7) ACQUISITION PROCESS | NIAP-APPROVED PROTECTION PROFILES
(a) Limit the use of commercially provided information assurance and information assurance-enabled information technology products to those products that have been successfully evaluated against a National Information Assurance partnership (NIAP)-approved Protection Profile for a specific technology type, if such a profile exists; and (b) Require, if no NIAP-approved Protection Profile exists for a specific technology type but a commercially provided information technology product relies on cryptographic functionality to enforce its security policy, that the cryptographic module is FIPS-validated or NSA-approved.
Discussion: See [NIAP CCEVS] for additional information on NIAP. See [NIST CMVP] for additional information on FIPS-validated cryptographic modules.  
Related Controls: IA-7, SC-12, SC-13.
(8) ACQUISITION PROCESS | CONTINUOUS MONITORING PLAN FOR CONTROLS
Require the developer of the system, system component, or system service to produce a plan for continuous monitoring of control effectiveness that is consistent with the continuous monitoring program of the organization.
Discussion: The objective of continuous monitoring plans is to determine if the planned, required, and deployed controls within the system, system component, or system service continue to be effective over time based on the inevitable changes that occur. Developer continuous monitoring plans include a sufficient level of detail such that the information can be incorporated into continuous monitoring programs implemented by organizations. Continuous monitoring plans can include the types of control assessment and monitoring.

title: SYSTEM DEVELOPMENT LIFE CYCLE
methodology:
Below is the practical validation methodology for verifying the implementation of SA-3: SYSTEM DEVELOPMENT LIFE CYCLE.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Existence of a formal, organization-defined SDLC process document that incorporates both information security and privacy requirements. This process should detail phases (concept, design, implementation, testing, deployment, and maintenance) and display integrated risk management activities.
   • Evidence that roles and responsibilities, including those for security and privacy, are clearly defined in project charters, staffing documents, or role descriptions used in SDLC activities.
   • Artifacts such as version-controlled design documents, secure coding standards, change management logs, and test plans indicating security/ privacy checkpoints.
   • Observable operational patterns such as reviews during phase transitions, documented security risk assessments at project milestones, and audit logs showing consistent adherence to the SDLC process.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations sometimes produce a generic SDLC document as a policy that is not reflected in actual day-to-day practices. Simply having a documented SDLC does not prove its effective integration of security/privacy safeguards.
   • Falsified role assignment – e.g., listing roles on an organizational chart without tracking the actual involvement of qualified personnel in development reviews or risk analysis sessions.
   • Surface-level integration of risk management that is not tied to the project workstreams – evidence might be cursory meeting logs without concrete evidence of applied technical requirement changes or security test results.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify that the SDLC process documentation includes security and privacy checkpoints.
   WHY: Strong evidence that the control incorporates security and privacy from inception through post-deployment.
   HOW: Review approved SDLC process documents; cross-check with project-specific risk assessments and secure coding guidelines; confirm that test and evaluation phases have documented security controls.
   FAIL: Absence of security/privacy checkpoints or reliance solely on generic development handbooks not tailored to security/privacy needs.

   CHECK: Confirm that roles and responsibilities for information security and privacy are clearly defined and assigned in key SDLC phases.
   WHY: Ensures accountability and that qualified personnel oversee security/privacy integration.
   HOW: Interview SDLC project leads; examine staffing documents, role descriptions, and meeting minutes to verify involvement of security/privacy experts in each development phase.
   FAIL: If roles are missing, unclear, or if no evidence shows involvement of security/privacy specialists (e.g., no meeting records or test reports showing their oversight).

   CHECK: Validate that the risk management process is integrated into the SDLC activities.
   WHY: This demonstrates that risks are being identified, evaluated, and mitigated throughout all phases of development.
   HOW: Analyze risk assessment reports, decisions logs, and design review meeting minutes to ensure that identified risks have corresponding mitigations integrated into the design and testing phases.
   FAIL: The process shows risk assessments as a one-off activity, not integrated into the design and testing phases, or lacks updates after design changes.

   CHECK: Examine version history and change management logs to confirm that security and privacy requirements are maintained over the lifecycle of systems development.
   WHY: A robust versioning and change control process reflects continuous monitoring and commitment to an integrated practice.
   HOW: Inspect configuration management systems to see version changes, security patching actions, and review the audit trail for SDLC milestones.
   FAIL: Absence of change logs or inconsistent updating which could indicate that security/privacy requirements are not reliably maintained.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: Zero in on critical phase transitions (e.g., from design to development and from testing to production) – these are the junctures where integrated security measures should be evident in meeting minutes and change requests.
   • Critical Indicator: Lack of specific security test results or a separate risk register updated through all project phases signals a weak implementation.
   • Note: When interviewing staff, ask for specific examples of when their input on security/privacy risk led to a change in design; vague responses can be a red flag.
   • Reminder: Ensure that automation is used where possible – look for integration of security testing (e.g., static analysis tools, dynamic testing reports) within the CI/CD pipeline as evidence of routine functional compliance with security/privacy requirements.
   • Also consider the enhanced requirements such as managing preproduction environments and controls around use of live data. Verify that these specialized practices are reflected in test environment documentation and that relevant access controls are applied analogous to production environments.

This methodology emphasizes operational effectiveness – evidence is drawn from continuous practice rather than mere policy existence, reducing the likelihood of superficial compliance.
content:
Statement:
a. Acquire, develop, and manage the system using [Assignment: organization-defined system development life cycle] that incorporates information security and privacy considerations;
b. Define and document information security and privacy roles and responsibilities throughout the system development life cycle;
c. Identify individuals having information security and privacy roles and responsibilities; and
d. Integrate the organizational information security and privacy risk management process into system development life cycle activities.

Guidance:
A system development life cycle process provides the foundation for the successful development, implementation, and operation of organizational systems. The integration of security and privacy considerations early in the system development life cycle is a foundational principle of systems security engineering and privacy engineering. To apply the required controls within the system development life cycle requires a basic understanding of information security and privacy, threats, vulnerabilities, adverse impacts, and risk to critical mission and business functions. The security engineering principles in SA-8 help individuals properly design, code, and test systems and system components. Organizations include qualified personnel (e.g., senior agency information security officers, senior agency officials for privacy, security and privacy architects, and security and privacy engineers) in system development life cycle processes to ensure that established security and privacy requirements are incorporated into organizational systems. Role-based security and privacy training programs can ensure that individuals with security and privacy roles and responsibilities have the experience, skills, and expertise to conduct assigned system development life cycle activities. The effective integration of security and privacy requirements into enterprise architecture also helps to ensure that important security and privacy considerations are addressed throughout the system life cycle and that those considerations are directly related to organizational mission and business processes. This process also facilitates the integration of the information security and privacy architectures into the enterprise architecture, consistent with the risk management strategy of the organization. Because the system development life cycle involves multiple organizations (e.g., external suppliers, developers, integrators, service providers), acquisition

Enhancements:
(1) SYSTEM DEVELOPMENT LIFE CYCLE | MANAGE PREPRODUCTION ENVIRONMENT
Protect system preproduction environments commensurate with risk throughout the system development life cycle for the system, system component, or system service. 
Discussion: The preproduction environment includes development, test, and integration environments. The program protection planning processes established by the Department of Defense are examples of managing the preproduction environment for defense contractors. Criticality analysis and the application of controls on developers also contribute to a more secure system development environment.
Related Controls: CM-2, CM-4, RA-3, RA-9, SA-4.
(2) SYSTEM DEVELOPMENT LIFE CYCLE | USE OF LIVE OR OPERATIONAL DATA
(a) Approve, document, and control the use of live data in preproduction environments for the system, system component, or system service; and 
(b) Protect preproduction environments for the system, system component, or system service at the same impact or classification level as any live data in use within the preproduction environments.
Discussion: Live data is also referred to as operational data. The use of live or operational data in production (i.e., development, test, and integration) environments can result in significant risks to organizations. In addition, the use of personally identifiable information in testing, research, and training increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure or misuse of such information. Therefore, it is important for the organization to manage any additional risks that may result from the use of live or operational data. Organizations can minimize such risks by using test or dummy data during the design, development, and testing of systems, system components, and system services.
Related Controls: PM-25, RA-3.
(3) SYSTEM DEVELOPMENT LIFE CYCLE | TECHNOLOGY REFRESH
Plan for and implement a technology refresh schedule for the system throughout the system development life cycle.
Discussion: Technology refresh planning may encompass hardware, software, firmware, processes, personnel skill sets, suppliers, service providers, and facilities. The use of obsolete or nearing obsolete technology may increase the security and privacy risks associated with unsupported components, counterfeit or repurposed components, components unable to implement security or privacy requirements, slow or inoperable components, components from untrusted sources, inadvertent personnel error, or increased complexity. Technology refreshes typically occur during the operations and maintenance stage of the system development life cycle.
Related Controls: MA-6.

title: ALLOCATION OF RESOURCES
methodology:
Below is the practical validation methodology for control SA-2 – Allocation of Resources.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: Look for explicit budget line items or budget documents that include a designated amount for information security and privacy. Organizational financial plans and capital investment documentation should explicitly reference IT security and privacy cost centers. 
   • Technical Artifacts: Review automated records from financial management systems and approval logs for capital planning. Check that system acquisition and maintenance budgets include clearly delineated security allocations.
   • Operational Patterns: Observe periodic financial reviews where the information security budget is monitored and adjusted. Look for integration between operational risk assessments and resource planning reviews.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Faked Compliance: Organizations might include generic lines in documentation that mention security funding but without enforceable traceability to specific controls or investments.
   • Surface-Level Implementations: Using outdated or one-time allocations rather than updating for emerging threats. Documentation might exist without active linkage to ongoing system development or acquisition lifecycle.
   • Technical Gaps: Budget approval systems might list security funding without applying rigorous follow-up reviews or aligning allocations to dynamic risks like supply chain threats.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Budget Line Item for Information Security/Privacy
   WHY: A distinct, approved line item in budgeting documents demonstrates an active financial commitment to protecting systems.
   HOW: Review recent capital investment records and financial planning documents for a clearly identified security/ privacy budget; verify in the financial management system the allocation is updated periodically.
   FAIL: Absence of any discrete budget entry for security or vague wording that does not specify security-related investments.

   CHECK: Integration of Security Requirements in Capital Planning
   WHY: Integration ensures that security funding is linked to system acquisition and sustainment, reflecting an operational view of risk.
   HOW: Interview capital planners and inspect documentation demonstrating how security requirements and risks were assessed and converted into dynamic resource allocations as part of the life cycle.
   FAIL: Evidence that security requirements are an afterthought or are merely mentioned in general risk assessments without a direct connection to funding decisions.

   CHECK: Traceability between Security Funding and System/System Service Plans
   WHY: This demonstrates the control’s intent to link planned missions and business processes to the technical means of protection, ensuring investments are driven by strategic risk analysis.
   HOW: Test a sample of system/service acquisition projects. Correlate documented security requirements (from business plans) to allocated resources in project or program planning documentation.
   FAIL: Inconsistencies or missing traceability between mission-based security requirements and their resource allotments; lack of supporting artifacts tying risk data to actual funding.

   CHECK: Periodic Review Mechanisms for Security Funding
   WHY: Regular reviews ensure that allocated resources stay in alignment with changing security and privacy requirements across the system development life cycle.
   HOW: Examine meeting minutes, review schedules, or audit trails that verify periodic capital planning review sessions including adjustments to security funding. Verify that review frequencies match organizational policy.
   FAIL: No evidence of regular reviews or adjustments; static allocations failing to take into account changes in threat environment or business objectives.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: Request sample budget approval logs with timestamps – a rapid way to verify both the existence and periodic update of the security funding line item.
   • Critical Indicator: If the funding for security is lumped together with general IT spending without clear segregation, this often indicates a superficial compliance posture.
   • Experience Insight: Look beyond policy documents – confirm that interviews with financial and operational managers reflect a nuanced understanding of how security funding adapts to evolving technical risks (especially supply chain risks).
   • Details Matter: Attention should be paid to the frequency of updates and the practical alignment with system life cycle phases. A robust process will demonstrate iterative reviews, not a one-off allocation.
content:
Statement:
a. Determine the high-level information security and privacy requirements for the system or system service in mission and business process planning;
b. Determine, document, and allocate the resources required to protect the system or system service as part of the organizational capital planning and investment control process; and
c. Establish a discrete line item for information security and privacy in organizational programming and budgeting documentation.

Guidance:
Resource allocation for information security and privacy includes funding for system and services acquisition, sustainment, and supply chain-related risks throughout the system development life cycle.

title: POLICY AND PROCEDURES
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: A formally published and version-controlled document outlining the organization’s system and services acquisition policy and procedures, distributed to relevant personnel. Meeting minutes or announcements that demonstrate dissemination and training sessions.
• Technical Artifacts: Electronic repositories (e.g., intranet sites, document management systems) hosting current, approved policy documents with revision histories. Audit logs or policy management tool records showing review and update frequencies.
• Operational Patterns: Regular updates triggered by defined events (e.g., post-audit, regulatory changes) and evidence of inter-departmental coordination, often seen in consistent notifications and training refreshers.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Faked Compliance: Organizations that create a policy document only to satisfy an audit requirement, without proper dissemination or operational enforcement.
• Superficial Implementation: Policies that are generic and not tailored to the organization’s operations, or procedures that are boilerplate and lack specificity regarding roles and responsibilities.
• Technical Gaps: Lack of verifiable records that demonstrate dynamic review and update processes or missing automated mechanisms to alert pertinent personnel of changes.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Verify Existence and Distribution of Policy Documents
   WHY: The presence of a formally documented policy that is accessible and version-controlled confirms that the organization has established the baseline for system and services acquisition.
   HOW: Inspect the document repository or intranet for the acquisition policy; review version history and distribution lists; confirm that targeted personnel have acknowledged receipt.
   FAIL: Absence of a clearly defined, version-controlled policy document or evidence that it has not been shared with key roles.

2. CHECK: Validate Designation of a Responsible Official
   WHY: A designated official ensures accountability and oversight of policy development and updates, demonstrating management commitment.
   HOW: Interview or review the organizational chart and RACI matrix to identify the person responsible; cross-check against documented meeting minutes or official memos appointing this role.
   FAIL: Missing or ambiguous assignment of responsibility in both documentation and operational records.

3. CHECK: Confirm Scheduled Reviews and Update Triggers
   WHY: Consistent reviews tied to specific frequencies or events (e.g., audit findings, incidents) demonstrate that the policy remains current and effective.
   HOW: Examine the review logs or change management records to see the documented dates and reasons for recent reviews; verify that changes in regulation or incidents triggered timely updates.
   FAIL: No documented schedule, log, or evidence that reviews are conducted, or dates are outdated beyond their defined frequency.

4. CHECK: Evaluate Implementation of Procedures Complementing the Policy
   WHY: Procedures that detail how the policy is operationalized ensure that the requirements are not just documented policies but are actively enforced.
   HOW: Review the supplementary procedure documents or sections in the security plan; confirm training records or walkthroughs that demonstrate how these procedures are applied in practice.
   FAIL: Absence of clear, actionable procedures or evidence of their implementation, indicating a disconnect between documented policy and operational practice.

EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Ask for automated dashboards or policy management tools that log document access, acknowledgments, and updates – these are hard to fake and reflect ongoing oversight.
• Critical Indicator: Ensure the designated official is actively engaged; a non-responsive point of contact is a red flag for poor implementation.
• Detail Matters: Look for integration of policy review with related processes such as risk assessments and incident responses. The evidence should indicate that policy updates are reactive to real operational needs (e.g., post-incident reviews).
• Verification Shortcut: During interviews, ask for a recent example where a regulatory change or security incident resulted in an immediate policy review and update to validate the ‘living’ nature of the process.

By systematically combining the assessment of documentation, automated records, and direct verification of operational practices, you can validate that the SA-1 control not only exists on paper but is actively managed and enforced in practice.
content:
Statement:
a. Develop, document, and disseminate to [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles]:
   1. [Selection (one or more)]: Organization-level; Mission/business process-level; System-level] system and services acquisition policy that:
      (a) Addresses purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, coordination among organizational entities, and compliance; and
      (b) Is consistent with applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines; and
   2. Procedures to facilitate the implementation of the system and services acquisition policy and the associated system and services acquisition controls;

b. Designate an [Assignment: organization-defined official] to manage the development, documentation, and dissemination of the system and services acquisition policy and procedures; and

c. Review and update the current system and services acquisition:
   1. Policy [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] and following [Assignment: organization-defined events]; and
   2. Procedures [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] and following [Assignment: organization-defined events].

Guidance:
Discussion: System and services acquisition policy and procedures address the controls in the SA family that are implemented within systems and organizations. The risk management strategy is an important factor in establishing such policies and procedures. Policies and procedures contribute to security and privacy assurance. Therefore, it is important that security and privacy programs collaborate on the development of system and services acquisition policy and procedures. Security and privacy program policies and procedures at the organization level are preferable, in general, and may obviate the need for mission- or system-specific policies and procedures. The policy can be included as part of the general security and privacy policy or be established for security and privacy programs, for mission or business processes, and for systems, if needed. Procedures describe how the policies or controls are implemented and can be directed at the individual or role that is the object of the procedure. Procedures can be documented in system security and privacy plans or in one or more separate documents. Events that may precipitate an update to system and services acquisition policy and procedures include assessment or audit findings, security incidents or breaches, or changes in laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines. Simply restating controls does not constitute an organizational policy or procedure.

title: THREAT HUNTING
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Evidence of an active threat hunting program is shown by documented hunt missions, regular scheduled hunts with defined objectives, and post-mortem analyses on findings that led to remediation actions. Look for dashboards that track threat intelligence feeds, indicators of compromise (IOCs), and anomaly detection trends in logging tools.
• Technical artifacts include system logs from SIEM tools, automated hunt queries/scheduled scripts, and response artifacts detailing proactive investigations. Correlation between hunt alerts and incident tickets also demonstrates that threat hunting is not just a policy but an active process.
• Operational patterns include periodic threat hunting reports, evidence of red-team or adversary simulation results used as baselines, and documented escalation procedures once a threat is detected.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may claim a threat hunting program exists by merely documenting generic procedures without the actual operational evidence of hunts in the live environment.
• Surface-level implementations might include one-off threat hunting exercises that are not integrated into continuous security monitoring. Fake dashboards or automated tools that are installed but not actively configured can be used to simulate compliance.
• Technical gaps often missed include regular updates to threat models, integration of threat intelligence into hunts, and clear linkage between hunt outcomes and remediation actions.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Verify that threat hunting sessions are scheduled and documented with defined objectives and outcomes.
   WHY: Consistently scheduled and documented hunts demonstrate proactive efforts rather than sporadic or simulated activity.
   HOW: Review operational logs, meeting minutes, and threat hunt dashboards; check if post-hunt reports include actionable discoveries and follow-up action items.
   FAIL: Lack of session logs, missing post-hunt analysis, or only static policy documents with no evidence of continuous execution.

2. CHECK: Examine SIEM and security tool logs for evidence of active threat hunting queries and anomaly detections.
   WHY: Automated logging of threat hunting queries shows that threat hunters are actively interrogating the environment.
   HOW: Access SIEM query histories, search for repetitive threat hunting scripts, and correlate alerts with hunt sessions; review change logs for updates in search queries reflecting evolving threat intelligence.
   FAIL: Absence of regularly updated queries or lack of historical data indicating proactive searches.

3. CHECK: Review incident response and remediation reports that stem directly from findings during threat hunting exercises.
   WHY: This proves that threat hunting activities are effectively moving the organization from detection to remediation, closing the loop on proactive security measures.
   HOW: Correlate threat hunting reports with incident tickets in the ticketing system; verify that identified threats led to documented remediation actions or changes in security controls.
   FAIL: If threat hunting reports do not match any subsequent remediation actions or if there is no linkage between the hunts and incident ticketing system.

4. CHECK: Inspect integration of threat intelligence with threat hunting tools and processes.
   WHY: Effective threat hunting relies on current threat intelligence feeds to prioritize genuine risks rather than noise.
   HOW: Validate that external threat intelligence (e.g., from commercial sources or open-source communities) is integrated into the hunt process and that choices of hunt targets are based on recent threat indicators.
   FAIL: Outdated threat feeds, isolated threat hunting reports that do not reference actual threat intelligence, or a disconnect between threat intelligence alerts and hunt focus areas.

EXPERT TIPS
• Verification Shortcut: Look for a “Threat Hunting Command Center” view in security dashboards; if it’s active and updated, it’s usually a good sign. Also, verify that the threat hunting team is engaged in periodic review calls or debriefs.
• Critical Indicator: A poor implementation is signaled by threat hunt reports that continuously flag benign events or where the same alerts are repeated without corrective action.
• Key Detail: Always check for evidence that lessons learned from threat hunts are fed back into updates for threat models and hunting playbooks; this cross-validation indicates a mature, continuously improving program.
content:
Statement:
.

title: CRITICALITY ANALYSIS
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for Control RA-9 – CRITICALITY ANALYSIS:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Documentation showing a formal criticality analysis process performed during the architecture and design phases. This includes diagrams of system functions mapped to hardware, software, and firmware components, along with traceability to mission outcomes.
   • Artifacts such as system architecture diagrams, requirements traceability matrices, meeting minutes, and change requests that document reviews of the criticality impact from component failures or system modifications.
   • Observable operational patterns including periodic reviews of critical components in risk management meetings, updates to risk assessment registers, and integration with supply chain risk management processes.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations might simply maintain an outdated list of ‘critical’ components as a checkbox exercise, without revalidating the list against current mission requirements or technical changes.
   • Developers may produce analysis documents that were never actively used in decision-making, or rely solely on static policies without dynamic integration into the system development lifecycle.
   • Superficial mappings that do not deeply trace organizational missions to specific system components, failing to account for interdependencies, outsourced services, or changing threat models.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   
   CHECK: Validate that updated criticality analysis documentation exists for each defined system and component.
   WHY: Ensures that there is a formal process that identifies critical functions and components, aligned with current organizational missions and risk scenarios.
   HOW: Interview system engineers, review system design documents or architectural diagrams, trace requirements from mission needs to system components, and verify that the analysis was updated at the decision points in the SDLC.
   FAIL: Absence of recent documentation or analysis that does not clearly map mission functions to specific system components, or relies solely on outdated templates.

   CHECK: Verify that the criticality analysis process is integrated into the system development lifecycle (SDLC).
   WHY: Integration in SDLC shows that criticality analysis influences design modifications and protection measures, such as redundancy or alternate routing to reduce risks.
   HOW: Check project records for early-stage architectural reviews, look for change logs where criticality considerations led to design adjustments, and confirm that analysis criteria are part of the formal design and development process.
   FAIL: Evidence that the criticality analysis is a one-time exercise or is performed post-deployment, indicating it does not proactively influence system design.

   CHECK: Confirm traceability from organizational mission requirements to specific system components.
   WHY: Traceability ensures that the identification of critical system components is not superficial and that each component’s role in supporting a mission has been clearly analyzed.
   HOW: Review requirements traceability matrices and system architecture documents; verify that each mission-critical function has clearly identified associated components with supporting risk impact assessments.
   FAIL: Lack of mapping between mission requirements and technical components, or isolated documentation in which functions and components are listed without cross-referencing their relationship.

   CHECK: Evaluate the periodic review process for criticality analysis outputs.
   WHY: Regular reviews reinforce operational effectiveness by ensuring that changes to the system or threat landscape are promptly reflected in the criticality analysis.
   HOW: Look for evidence of scheduled review cycles in meeting minutes, risk management reports, or change control documentation; interview risk management or systems engineering teams about review practices.
   FAIL: Absence of a periodic review schedule, or evidence that the analysis is not revisited when system modifications or threat updates occur.

   CHECK: Assess the linkage between the criticality analysis and supply chain risk management practices.
   WHY: This confirms that criticality analysis is used to inform protective measures not only within the organization’s own processes but also in managing external dependencies and subcontractor services.
   HOW: Review documentation that shows how the outcomes of the criticality analysis have influenced procurement, contract requirements, or vendor risk assessments; verify that critical components identified influence supply chain criteria.
   FAIL: No evidence of integration between internal criticality analysis outputs and supply chain risk management practices, or if supplier evaluations do not consider the criticality of components they provide.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Look beyond policy documentation – active artifacts like updated diagrams, dynamic traceability matrices, and review records provide more reliable evidence of implementation.
   • Critical indicators of poor implementation include static lists that do not reflect recent system changes or review cycles; these are major red flags when verifying genuine compliance.
   • Focus on the quality and currency of evidence—evidence that indicates a reactive, rather than proactive, implementation often signals that the criticality analysis did not effectively guide system design modifications.
   • When interviewing system engineers or risk managers, probe for details on specific instances where criticality analysis directly influenced design changes or mitigated risks. This provides context beyond static documentation.

This methodology emphasizes automated and observable evidence that critically evaluates the effectiveness of the control implementation and reduces the risk of superficial compliance.
content:
Statement:
Identify critical system components and functions by performing a criticality analysis for [Assignment: organization-defined systems, system components, or system services] at [Assignment: organization-defined decision points in the system development life cycle].

Guidance:
Discussion: Not all system components, functions, or services necessarily require significant protections. For example, critical analysis is a key tenant of supply chain risk management and informs the prioritization of protection activities. The identification of critical system components and functions considers applicable laws, executive orders, regulations, directives, policies, standards, system functionality requirements, system and component interfaces, and system and component dependencies. Systems engineers conduct a functional decomposition of a system to identify mission-critical functions and components. The functional decomposition includes the identification of organizational missions supported by the system and relates specific functions to perform those missions, and traceability to the hardware, software, and firmware components that implement those functions, including when the functions are shared by many components within and external to the system. The operational environment of a system or a system component may impact the criticality, including the connections to and dependencies on cyber-physical systems, devices, system-of-systems, and outsourced IT services. System components that allow unmediated access to critical system components or functions are considered critical due to the inherent vulnerabilities that such components create. Component and function criticality are assessed in terms of the impact of a component or function failure on the organizational missions that are supported by the system that contains the components and functions. Criticality analysis is performed when an architecture or design is being developed, modified, or upgraded. If such analysis is performed early in the system development life cycle, organizations may be able to modify the system design to reduce the critical nature of these components and functions, such as by adding redundancy or alternate paths into the system design. Criticality analysis can also influence the protection measures required by development contractors. In addition to criticality analysis for systems, system components, and system services, criticality analysis of information is an important consideration. Such analysis is conducted as part of security categorization in RA-2.

title: PRIVACY IMPACT ASSESSMENTS
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Documented Privacy Impact Assessments (PIAs) archived as living documents with version control showing updates over time.
• Evidence of PIAs being completed, reviewed, and approved before the development or procurement of any IT systems processing PII.
• Artifacts such as sign-off sheets, meeting minutes, and change logs from cross-functional teams (IT, legal, privacy officers) demonstrating early and ongoing privacy risk evaluation.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may produce generic, boilerplate PIA documents that are not frequently updated or tailored to the specific system or data involved.
• PIAs conducted solely as a checkbox exercise after technology deployment rather than before procurement or design.
• Lack of audit trails or collaborative review records, where a single person’s signature stands in for thorough multi-departmental input.
• Documentation that exists only on paper (or as a PDF snapshot) without corresponding evidence in system development lifecycle records or change management logs.

VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Verify that a current, version-controlled PIA document exists prior to system or technology procurement.
WHY: It demonstrates that privacy risks were evaluated before committing to technology that processes PII.
HOW: Review secure repositories or project management platforms for approved PIA documents with timestamps that predate IT procurement or system implementation, and check for digital signatures from key stakeholders.
FAIL: Absence of a PIA document before procurement, static documents with no updates, or evidence that the PIA was completed post-deployment.

CHECK: Confirm that PIAs are living documents updated in response to changes in technology or business practices throughout the system’s lifecycle.
WHY: Continuous updates are essential to ensure that emerging privacy risks are consistently evaluated and mitigated.
HOW: Check version histories, change logs, or audit trails in document management systems, and interview the privacy officer to confirm that updates occur in response to significant changes.
FAIL: Single, unchanging PIAs with no recorded updates or no evidence of an established review cycle.

CHECK: Validate that cross-functional teams (including privacy officers, program managers, IT/security experts, and counsel) were involved in the PIA process prior to system deployment.
WHY: Comprehensive evaluation requires collaborative input to adequately assess privacy risks.
HOW: Review meeting minutes, email correspondence, or collaborative platform logs that detail stakeholder involvement and collective decision-making.
FAIL: Lack of records showing multi-stakeholder collaboration, or documentation that the PIA was solely authored by a single individual without review.

CHECK: Check that procurement records or project initiation documents reference completed PIAs, confirming that assessments are a prerequisite for IT investments and new PII data collections.
WHY: Embedding PIA completion into procurement or initiation processes ensures that privacy considerations are factored in early.
HOW: Inspect procurement checklists, project initiation templates, or system requirements documents that include mandatory PIA sign-offs and approvals.
FAIL: Missing references to PIAs in procurement documents or evidence that PIAs were retrofitted after system procurement.

EXPERT TIPS
• Rapidly identify compliance issues by focusing on the time stamps of PIA documents relative to IT system milestones and procurement approvals.
• Pay special attention to change logs in document management systems, as a static document is a strong indicator of non-continuous risk management.
• Interview key stakeholders for real-life examples of how privacy risks were mitigated during system design. A lack of detailed narrative around risk mitigation suggests superficial compliance.
• Ensure that PIA documentation integrates with broader system lifecycle and change management records, as disjoint processes are a sign of potential gaps in operational implementation.
content:
Statement:
Conduct privacy impact assessments for systems, programs, or other activities before:
a. Developing or procuring information technology that processes personally identifiable information; and
b. Initiating a new collection of personally identifiable information that:
   1. Will be processed using information technology; and
   2. Includes personally identifiable information permitting the physical or virtual (online) contacting of a specific individual, if identical questions have been posed to, or identical reporting requirements imposed on, ten or more individuals, other than agencies, instrumentalities, or employees of the federal government.

Guidance:
Discussion: A privacy impact assessment is an analysis of how personally identifiable information is handled to ensure that handling conforms to applicable privacy requirements, determine the privacy risks associated with an information system or activity, and evaluate ways to mitigate privacy risks. A privacy impact assessment is both an analysis and a formal document that details the process and the outcome of the analysis.

Organizations conduct and develop a privacy impact assessment with sufficient clarity and specificity to demonstrate that the organization fully considered privacy and incorporated appropriate privacy protections from the earliest stages of the organization’s activity and throughout the information life cycle. In order to conduct a meaningful privacy impact assessment, the organization’s senior agency official for privacy works closely with program managers, system owners, information technology experts, security officials, counsel, and other relevant organization personnel. Moreover, a privacy impact assessment is not a time-restricted activity that is limited to a particular milestone or stage of the information system or personally identifiable information life cycles. Rather, the privacy analysis continues throughout the system and personally identifiable information life cycles. Accordingly, a privacy impact assessment is a living document that organizations update whenever changes to the information technology, changes to the organization’s practices, or other factors alter the privacy risks associated with the use of such information technology.

title: RISK RESPONSE
methodology:
Control ID: RA-7 – RISK RESPONSE

This control requires that an organization responds systematically to security and privacy assessment findings in line with its risk tolerance. Below is a practical validation methodology:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Updated risk registers, formal risk response plans, approved risk acceptance forms, and remediation action logs that clearly link findings from security/privacy assessments, monitoring, and audits.
• Technical Artifacts: Automated dashboards showing real-time updates to risk items, integrated ticketing systems for risk mitigation actions, and documented plan-of-action and milestone (POA&M) records for risks that cannot be immediately mitigated.
• Operational Patterns: Regular risk response meetings with documented minutes, periodic sign-offs by risk owners, and cross-referencing of audit reports with remediation activities.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Policy Overkill Without Action: Organizations might have overly generic policies or risk registers that are never updated or tied to real events.
• Superficial Documentation: Producing risk response documents that are not aligned with actual system vulnerabilities or audit findings.
• Incomplete Artifact Integration: Using separate systems for risk registers and remediation actions can result in gaps where responses are recorded in one place but not acted upon or monitored.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Verify that the risk register is regularly updated with entries reflecting findings from security and privacy assessments.
WHY: An updated risk register demonstrates that the organization is actively monitoring and categorizing threats in relation to its risk tolerance.
HOW: Review the risk register to ensure entries have recent time-stamps that correspond with the latest assessment and audit reports. Confirm that each entry includes mitigation steps, ownership, and management sign-off.
FAIL: A risk register with outdated entries, missing details, or entries that do not correlate with recent assessment findings indicates non-compliance.

CHECK: Validate that risks which cannot be immediately mitigated are followed by documented plan-of-action and milestone (POA&M) entries.
WHY: Documented POA&M entries provide evidence that risk acceptance or mitigation decisions are formally tracked and actioned over time.
HOW: Examine the POA&M records in your project management/ticketing system to find clear links between assessment findings and planned remediation activities. Ensure each risk has defined milestones and a management approval trail.
FAIL: Absence of connected POA&M records or evidence that risk items are left unresolved without appropriate escalation signals a breakdown in the response process.

CHECK: Confirm evidence of periodic risk response reviews and management approvals.
WHY: Regular reviews and approvals ensure that risk responses remain aligned with the organization’s risk tolerance and that emerging threats are appropriately managed.
HOW: Check meeting minutes, management review logs, or automated audit trails that record periodic risk response meetings, including attendance, actions taken, and sign-offs by risk owners and senior management.
FAIL: Missing or inconsistent review meeting records, or lack of sign-offs, suggest that risk response processes are not being monitored effectively.

CHECK: Cross-reference audit and monitoring reports against remediation action logs and risk registers.
WHY: This step confirms that the organization is not merely documenting risk responses but is also tying them back to concrete security findings and improvements.
HOW: Identify several audit reports and verify that each identified risk item is tracked in the risk register and that corresponding remediation actions have been initiated. Use an automated search in the ticketing system to find links between reported findings and follow-up activities.
FAIL: Discrepancies between audit findings and registered remediation actions, or missing links between these documents, indicate that the risk response process is not fully operational.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut Verification: Focus on live system dashboards and automated logs. These offer more reliable evidence than static documents which can be easily backdated.
• Critical Detail: Look for clear ownership, time stamps, and interconnection among assessment findings, risk register updates, and POA&M items. Lack of these details is a red flag.
• Real-World Behavior: Interview risk owners and review recent management meeting minutes to confirm that responses to risks are not just theoretical but are actively influencing day-to-day operations.
• Check Consistency: Ensure that any risk acceptance decisions are accompanied by documented rationale and periodic reviews to account for any changes in the threat landscape.

This methodology prioritizes verifiable, dynamic evidence over static documentation, ensuring that the organization’s risk response is both operational and aligned with its actual risk tolerance.
content:
Statement:
Respond to findings from security and privacy assessments, monitoring, and audits in accordance with organizational risk tolerance.

Guidance:
Discussion: Organizations have many options for responding to risk including mitigating risk by implementing new controls or strengthening existing controls, accepting risk with appropriate justification or rationale, sharing or transferring risk, or avoiding risk. The risk tolerance of the organization influences risk response decisions and actions. Risk response addresses the need to determine an appropriate response to risk before generating a plan of action and milestones entry. For example, the response may be to accept risk or reject risk, or it may be possible to mitigate the risk immediately so that a plan of action and milestones entry is not needed. However, if the risk response is to mitigate the risk, and the mitigation cannot be completed immediately, a plan of action and milestones entry is generated.

title: TECHNICAL SURVEILLANCE COUNTERMEASURES SURVEY
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence includes documented records showing that technical surveillance countermeasures surveys (TSCM) have been performed at designated locations, at planned frequencies, or after specific events.
• Technical artifacts may include survey reports with details on devices detected or not detected, certificates or credentials of the personnel performing the survey, and electronic records from scanning tools used during the survey.
• Operational patterns include a recurring process of scheduling and executing surveys; follow‐up actions based on survey findings; and integration of survey outcomes into risk assessment reports.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may fake compliance by simply producing generic reports that lack detailed methodology or fail to demonstrate actual technical survey activities.
• Surface-level implementations include relying solely on third-party certifications without evidence of on-site, real-time actions or using out-of-date survey reports.
• Technical gaps frequently missed include the absence of evidence regarding the qualifications of personnel, a missing chain-of-custody for survey data, and a lack of actionable follow-up steps implemented after survey results.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Documented Evidence of Scheduled Surveys
   WHY: Regularly scheduled and event-triggered TSCM surveys ensure surveillance risks are actively managed.
   HOW: Verify the calendar or schedule records aligned with the organization-defined frequency and events; review internal notifications and meeting minutes that annotate survey outcomes and follow-up actions.
   FAIL: Absence of a schedule, outdated planning documents, or no records correlating survey events with organizational risk assessments.

2. CHECK: Detailed Survey Reports and Artifacts
   WHY: Detailed survey reports, including scope, methods, and findings, demonstrate a true technical examination rather than merely ticking a policy box.
   HOW: Review a sample of survey reports, ensuring they include technical signatures (device map, equipment logs, detection records) and that they were performed by qualified personnel. Confirm that they reflect both visual inspections and electronic/hardware assessments.
   FAIL: Generic or overly-summarized reports lacking technical detail, or reports that do not reference the methodology and tools used.

3. CHECK: Verification of Personnel Qualifications
   WHY: Surveys must be conducted by qualified individuals to be effective; checking credentials ensures that surveys were conducted properly.
   HOW: Cross-reference survey report sign-offs with personnel credentials or certifications in TSCM techniques. Check that records exist proving training or certification in technical surveillance countermeasures.
   FAIL: Missing or unverifiable qualifications for those who signed/approved survey reports.

4. CHECK: Integration of Survey Findings into Risk Management
   WHY: Effective implementation means that survey findings inform risk assessments and corrective actions.
   HOW: Verify that follow-up actions or remediation steps are documented in risk management logs or cybersecurity risk assessment reports. Look for evidence of communication of findings to relevant stakeholders.
   FAIL: Survey findings not referenced or acted upon in risk assessment documentation or if remediation actions are absent/incomplete.

5. CHECK: Use of Technical Tools and Methodologies
   WHY: Dependence on specialized technical scanning tools and methodologies provides an objective layer of detection that supports survey credibility.
   HOW: Ask for evidence of technical tool use (e.g., scanner logs, detection thresholds used). Validate that the survey methodology includes both hardware and software scanning, and review configurations or tool reports.
   FAIL: Lack of technical logs or methodologies, or reliance solely on physical inspection without supporting technical evidence.

EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Ask to see a live demonstration or recent tool output during the review session to confirm that the surveys include technical verification measures.
• Critical Indicator: Any survey report lacking detailed device detection logs or vague remedial recommendations should raise immediate concern regarding the depth of the assessment.
• Key Detail: Ensure surveys cover both internal and external facets of the facility, as adversaries may exploit any overlooked entry points.
• Note: Cross-check survey results with any incident reports or threat intelligence bulletins; discrepancies here often indicate that the surveys might be superficial.

This methodology focuses on verifying that TSCM surveys are not just documented procedures, but active, effective operations integrated into the organization’s broader security posture.
content:
Statement:
Employ a technical surveillance countermeasures survey at [Assignment: organization-defined locations] [Selection (one or more): [Assignment: organization-defined frequency]; when the following events or indicators occur: [Assignment: organization-defined events or indicators]].

Guidance:
Discussion: A technical surveillance countermeasures survey is a service provided by qualified personnel to detect the presence of technical surveillance devices and hazards and to identify technical security weaknesses that could be used in the conduct of a technical penetration of the surveyed facility. Technical surveillance countermeasures surveys also provide evaluations of the technical security posture of organizations and facilities and include visual, electronic, and physical examinations of surveyed facilities, internally and externally. The surveys also provide useful input for risk assessments and information regarding organizational exposure to potential adversaries.

title: VULNERABILITY MONITORING AND SCANNING
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for RA-5 Vulnerability Monitoring and Scanning.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: Automated scanning tools scheduled to run at defined intervals. Real-time dashboards and alerts populated by vulnerability scanners, updated system inventories, and vulnerability tracking dashboards are visible.
   • Technical Artifacts: Logs from vulnerability scanning tools (including tool configuration files that reference CVE/OVAL standards), scan reports with timestamped results, remediation ticket logs, configuration management records showing tool updates, and integration logs showing interoperability between tools.
   • Operational Patterns: Routine and ad hoc scans triggered by new vulnerability disclosures, consistent update of scanning definitions and checklists, documented exception handling and remediation cycles, and periodic reviews of historical audit logs for exploitation evidence.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Fake Compliance: Organizations might provide out-of-date, static reports or screenshots without corresponding tool logs. They may simply run scans on paper without actual automated tool integration.
   • Surface-Level Implementations: Running scans in a test environment only, using outdated vulnerability definitions, or scheduling scans only to fulfill compliance checkboxes, without integrating results into a broader risk management process. 
   • Technical Gaps: Lack of updating scanning tools with current vulnerabilities; failure to correlate scan outputs with remediation ticket systems; incomplete coverage (e.g., not scanning all critical components or embedded software); and failure to continuously update the scanning criteria when new vulnerability types are discovered.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   
   CHECK: Verify automated vulnerability scanning schedules and tool configurations.
   WHY: Ensures that scanning is conducted with the defined frequency and that the tool is configured with current vulnerability signatures (CVE, OVAL) to catch known issues.
   HOW: Inspect scheduler settings (e.g., CRON jobs or task scheduler configurations) and review recent scan reports from the tool’s log files. Confirm that tool update mechanisms (for CVE/OVAL data) are active.
   FAIL: Missing or outdated scanner schedules, tool logs indicating scans are not being executed, or failure to update asset vulnerability definitions.
   
   CHECK: Check integration between vulnerability scanner outputs and vulnerability management/remediation workflows.
   WHY: Demonstrates that identified vulnerabilities are being actively processed and remediated as part of an operational program.
   HOW: Review remediation ticketing systems for automatically created vulnerability tickets and trace remediation progress. Interview operational personnel to confirm that scan reports feed directly into incident or change management tools.
   FAIL: No correlation between scan reports and remediation tickets, delays in response times, or evidence that scan outputs are archived without actionable follow-up.
   
   CHECK: Confirm that interface and interoperability standards (e.g., SCAP, CVSS) are used in the scanning tools and reports.
   WHY: Validates that the tools are employing industry standards in enumerating vulnerabilities, enabling accurate impact measurement and consistent checklists which is critical for cross-tool correlation.
   HOW: Verify tool configuration documents and scan report formats for references to SCAP, CVSS scores, or OVAL definitions. Assess whether the reported vulnerabilities match industry databases (e.g., NVD).
   FAIL: Scan reports lacking standardized information, discrepancies in scoring or labeling of vulnerabilities, or tools configured with proprietary formats that cannot be correlated with standard databases.
   
   CHECK: Assess the process for updating the list of vulnerabilities to be scanned (including post-new vulnerability discovery updates).
   WHY: Ensures that the vulnerability scanner’s definitions and targets are actively updated in response to emerging threats.
   HOW: Check change logs in vulnerability management tools for recent updates to vulnerability definitions and evidence of modifying scan targets after a new vulnerability announcement. Interview technical staff responsible for managing these updates.
   FAIL: Absence of recent updates to scanning parameters, or evidence that new vulnerabilities are not being incorporated into scanning routines.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Verification Shortcuts: Use automated queries in tool dashboards to list recent scan executions and cross-reference timestamps with known vulnerability disclosure dates. This helps shortcut manual log reviews.
   • Critical Indicators: Consistently zero or overly sanitized vulnerability results can indicate a misconfiguration or a process running in a non-production environment. Lack of integration between scanning results and remediation tracking systems is also a red flag.
   • Details That Matter: Confirm that tool update schedules are tightly managed, and check that dashboard alerts and email notifications are active. Also, verify that operational personnel understand the bridging process between scanner output and remediation, as this is key to operational effectiveness.
   • Common Pitfalls: Beware of environments where scan reports are extensive but remediation tickets are sparse. Ensure that vulnerability scanning is not only scheduled but also dynamically adjusted based on emerging threats.

This methodology focuses on verifying visible, operational evidence of an active vulnerability monitoring and scanning program that cannot be easily faked solely through documentation.
content:
Statement:
a. Monitor and scan for vulnerabilities in the system and hosted applications [Assignment: organization-defined frequency and/or randomly in accordance with organization-defined process] and when new vulnerabilities potentially affecting the system are identified and reported;

b. Employ vulnerability monitoring tools and techniques that facilitate interoperability among tools and automate parts of the vulnerability management process by using standards for:
   1. Enumerating platforms, software flaws, and improper configurations;
   2. Formatting checklists and test procedures; and
   3. Measuring vulnerability impact;

c. Analyze vulnerability scan reports and results from vulnerability monitoring;

d. Remediate legitimate vulnerabilities [Assignment: organization-defined response times] in accordance with an organizational assessment of risk;

e. Share information obtained from the vulnerability monitoring process and control assessments with [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles] to help eliminate similar vulnerabilities in other systems; and

f. Employ vulnerability monitoring tools that include the capability to readily update the vulnerabilities to be scanned.

Guidance:
Discussion: Security categorization of information and systems guides the frequency and comprehensiveness of vulnerability monitoring (including scans). Organizations determine the required vulnerability monitoring for system components, ensuring that the potential sources of vulnerabilities—such as infrastructure components (e.g., switches, routers, guards, sensors), networked printers, scanners, and copiers—are not overlooked. The capability to readily update vulnerability monitoring tools as new vulnerabilities are discovered and announced and as new scanning methods are developed helps to ensure that new vulnerabilities are not missed by employed vulnerability monitoring tools. Vulnerability monitoring and analyses for custom software may require additional approaches, such as static analysis, dynamic analysis, binary analysis, or a hybrid of the three approaches. Organizations can use these analysis approaches in source code reviews and in a variety of tools, including web-based application scanners, static analysis tools, and binary analyzers.

Vulnerability monitoring includes scanning for patch levels; scanning for functions, ports, protocols, and services that should not be accessible to users or devices; and scanning for flow control mechanisms that are improperly configured or operating incorrectly. Vulnerability monitoring may also include continuous vulnerability monitoring tools that use instrumentation to continuously analyze components. Instrumentation-based tools may improve accuracy and may be run throughout an organization without scanning. Vulnerability monitoring tools that facilitate interoperability include tools that are Security Content Automated Protocol (SCAP)-validated. Thus, organizations consider using scanning tools that express vulnerabilities in the Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (CVE) naming convention and that employ the Open Vulnerability Assessment Language (OVAL) to determine the presence of vulnerabilities. Sources for vulnerability information include the Common Weakness Enumeration (CWE) listing and the National Vulnerability Database (NVD).

Control assessments, such as red team exercises, provide additional sources of potential vulnerabilities for which to scan. Organizations also consider using scanning tools that express vulnerability impact by the Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS). Vulnerability monitoring includes a channel and process for receiving reports of security vulnerabilities from the public at-large. Vulnerability disclosure programs can be as simple as publishing a monitored email address or web form that can receive reports, including notification authorizing good-faith research and disclosure of security vulnerabilities. Organizations generally expect that such research is happening with or without their authorization and can use public vulnerability disclosure channels to increase the likelihood that discovered vulnerabilities are reported directly to the organization for remediation. Organizations may also employ the use of financial incentives (also known as 'bug bounties') to further encourage external security researchers to report discovered vulnerabilities. Bug bounty programs can be tailored to the organization’s needs.

Enhancements:
 (1) VULNERABILITY MONITORING AND SCANNING | UPDATE TOOL CAPABILITY [Withdrawn: Incorporated into RA-5.]
 (2) VULNERABILITY MONITORING AND SCANNING | UPDATE VULNERABILITIES TO BE SCANNED
Update the system vulnerabilities to be scanned [Selection (one or more): [Assignment: organization-defined frequency; prior to a new scan; when new vulnerabilities are identified and reported].
Discussion: Due to the complexity of modern software, systems, and other factors, new vulnerabilities are discovered on a regular basis. It is important that newly discovered vulnerabilities are added to the list of vulnerabilities to be scanned to ensure that the organization can take steps to mitigate those vulnerabilities in a timely manner.
Related Controls: SI-5.
 (3) VULNERABILITY MONITORING AND SCANNING | BREADTH AND DEPTH OF COVERAGE
Define the breadth and depth of vulnerability scanning coverage.
Discussion: The breadth of vulnerability scanning coverage can be expressed as a percentage of components within the system, by the particular types of systems, by the criticality of systems, or by the number of vulnerabilities to be checked. Conversely, the depth of vulnerability scanning coverage can be expressed as the level of the system design that the organization intends to monitor (e.g., component, module, subsystem, element).
 (4) VULNERABILITY MONITORING AND SCANNING | DISCOVERABLE INFORMATION
Determine information about the system that is discoverable and take [Assignment: organization-defined corrective actions].
Discussion: Discoverable information includes information that adversaries could obtain without compromising or breaching the system, such as by collecting information that the system is exposing or by conducting extensive web searches. Corrective actions include notifying appropriate organizational personnel, removing designated information, or changing the system to make the designated information less relevant or attractive to adversaries. This enhancement excludes intentionally discoverable information that may be part of a decoy capability (e.g., honeypots, honeynets, or deception nets) deployed by the organization.
Related Controls: AU-13, SC-26.
 (5) VULNERABILITY MONITORING AND SCANNING | PRIVILEGED ACCESS
Implement privileged access authorization to [Assignment: organization-defined system components] for [Assignment: organization-defined vulnerability scanning activities].
Discussion: In certain situations, the nature of the vulnerability scanning may be more intrusive, or the system component that is the subject of the scanning may contain classified or controlled unclassified information, such as personally identifiable information. Privileged access authorization to selected system components facilitates more thorough vulnerability scanning and protects the sensitive nature of such scanning.
Related Controls: None.
 (6) VULNERABILITY MONITORING AND SCANNING | AUTOMATED TREND ANALYSES
Compare the results of multiple vulnerability scans using [Assignment: organization-defined automated mechanisms].
Discussion: Using automated mechanisms to analyze multiple vulnerability scans over time can help determine trends in system vulnerabilities and identify patterns of attack.
Related Controls: None.
 (7) VULNERABILITY MONITORING AND SCANNING | AUTOMATED DETECTION AND NOTIFICATION OF UNAUTHORIZED COMPONENTS
[Withdrawn: Incorporated into CM-8.]
 (8) VULNERABILITY MONITORING AND SCANNING | REVIEW HISTORIC AUDIT LOGS
Review historic audit logs to determine if a vulnerability identified in a [Assignment: organization-defined system] has been previously exploited within an [Assignment: organization-defined time period].
Discussion: Reviewing historic audit logs to determine if a recently detected vulnerability in a system has been previously exploited by an adversary can provide important information for forensic analyses. Such analyses can help identify, for example, the extent of a previous intrusion, the trade craft employed during the attack, organizational information exfiltrated or modified, mission or business capabilities affected, and the duration of the attack.
Related Controls: AU-6, AU-11.
 (9) VULNERABILITY MONITORING AND SCANNING | PENETRATION TESTING AND ANALYSES
[Withdrawn: Incorporated into CA-8]
 (10) VULNERABILITY MONITORING AND SCANNING | CORRELATE SCANNING INFORMATION
Correlate the output from vulnerability scanning tools to determine the presence of multi-vulnerability and multi-hop attack vectors.
Discussion: An attack vector is a path or means by which an adversary can gain access to a system in order to deliver malicious code or exfiltrate information. Organizations can use attack trees to show how hostile activities by adversaries interact and combine to produce adverse impacts or negative consequences to systems and organizations. Such information, together with correlated data from vulnerability scanning tools, can provide greater clarity regarding multi-vulnerability and multi-hop attack vectors. The correlation of vulnerability scanning information is especially important when organizations are transitioning from older technologies to newer technologies (e.g., transitioning from IPv4 to IPv6 network protocols). During such transitions, some system operations may inadvertently be unmanaged and create opportunities for adversary exploitation.
Related Controls: None. References: [ISO 29147], [SP 800-40], [SP 800-53A], [SP 800-70], [SP 800-115], [SP 800-126], [IR 7781], [IR 8011-4], [IR 8023].
 (11) VULNERABILITY MONITORING AND SCANNING | PUBLIC DISCLOSURE PROGRAM
Establish a public reporting channel for receiving reports of vulnerabilities in organizational systems and system components.
Discussion: The reporting channel is publicly discoverable and grants language authorizing good-faith research and the disclosure of vulnerabilities to the organization. The organization does not condition its authorization on an expectation of indefinite non-disclosure to the public by the reporting entity but may request a specific time period to properly remediate the vulnerability.
Related Controls: None. References: None.

title: RISK ASSESSMENT
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Evidence may include a regularly updated risk assessment report that details identified threats, vulnerabilities, likelihood, impact assessments, and risk outcomes. Visual indicators might include a risk register maintained in a central repository, automated scans correlating with risk identification, and integration of risk data in security dashboards.
• Technical artifacts such as change logs, system vulnerability scan reports, incident tracking systems, and the linkage between risk findings and remediation ticketing systems indicate active risk management.
• Operational patterns include scheduled risk assessment reviews, updated risk scores post system modifications, and documented dissemination to assigned roles.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may claim compliance by having a dated static risk document without evidence of continuous updates, resulting in a mere paper exercise.
• Superficial implementations like storing risk assessments only in internal emails or on a shared drive without proper version control, or risk assessments that do not tie into operational mitigations.
• Often missed technical gap: not integrating risk assessment results with automated monitoring systems and incident response processes.

VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Validate that a formal risk assessment report exists covering threat and vulnerability identification, likelihood, and impact assessment across identified domains.
WHY: The report evidences a systematic approach to risk identification and quantification as mandated.
HOW: Interview risk management personnel; review the most recent risk assessment report; correlate findings with changes in asset inventory and system logs.
FAIL: No report is available or the report is outdated and does not reflect current threat intelligence.

CHECK: Confirm integration of system-level risk assessments with organizational and mission/business process reviews.
WHY: It demonstrates that risk management decisions are not siloed and are applied across various enterprise levels.
HOW: Check meeting minutes of risk council discussions; review integrated risk dashboards; validate communications (automated notification/email reports) to defined organizational roles.
FAIL: Absence of evidence linking system-level assessments to higher-level risk decisions or uncoordinated risk management actions.

CHECK: Evaluate evidence of periodic risk assessment reviews and updates, particularly in response to system or environment changes.
WHY: Regular review and update are essential for dynamic risk landscapes and are a core expectation in continuous risk management.
HOW: Examine audit logs or configuration management records showing risk assessment schedule compliance; verify that the review frequency matches organizational policy; check triggered reviews post significant changes.
FAIL: No audit trail or system alerts exist for periodic reviews and update responses, or reviews occur ad hoc rather than systematically.
