   FAIL: Equipment offline or non-functional, missing recent logs, or evidence of disabled alerts.

2. CHECK: Review automated physical access log records along with audit trail timestamps.
   WHY: Automated logs that are regularly updated and retained are key artifacts demonstrating continuous monitoring.
   HOW: Request sample logs from access control systems and cross-reference recorded events with known time-based activities (e.g., off-hour access attempts) and sensor/alarm data.
   FAIL: Sparse, outdated, or tampered log entries; large gaps in logging or inconsistencies with physical observations.

3. CHECK: Confirm integration between physical monitoring systems and incident response processes.
   WHY: Effective coordination ensures that detected incidents trigger immediate, appropriate responses as required by the control implementation.
   HOW: Examine incident response records that reference physical access breaches; interview security personnel and verify documented incident drill exercises.
   FAIL: Lack of coordinated incident records, isolated systems that do not communicate with the incident response team, or absence of drills.

4. CHECK: Validate scheduled reviews of physical access logs and surveillance data.
   WHY: Regular review underpins proactive identification of anomalous activities and prompt response to physical security incidents.
   HOW: Assess review logs, meeting minutes, or automated report output that indicate the frequency and outcomes of physical access log reviews.
   FAIL: No evidence of periodic reviews, unaddressed anomalies in review records, or review cycles that do not match the defined frequency.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Shortcut: Ask security personnel for a live demonstration of system alerts and video playback to quickly gauge real-time functionality.
• Critical indicators: Look for inconsistencies between physical observations (e.g., presence of cameras) and their corresponding digital logs; large gaps are telltale signs of poor maintenance.
• Valuable details: Pay close attention to synchronization between sensor events and incident response actions, as delays or disconnects typically signal flawed integration. This control’s effectiveness is not simply in having equipment but ensuring it’s actively managed and produces actionable intelligence.
content:
Statement:
a. Monitor physical access to the facility where the system resides to detect and respond to physical security incidents;

b. Review physical access logs [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] and upon occurrence of [Assignment: organization-defined events or potential indications of events]; and

c. Coordinate results of reviews and investigations with the organizational incident response capability.

Guidance:
Physical access monitoring includes publicly accessible areas within organizational systems. Examples of physical access monitoring include the employment of guards, video surveillance equipment (i.e., cameras), and sensor devices. Reviewing physical access logs can help identify suspicious activity, anomalous events, or potential threats. The reviews can be supported by audit logging controls, such as AU-2, if the access logs are part of an automated system. Organizational incident response capabilities include investigations of physical security incidents and responses to the incidents. Incidents include security violations or suspicious physical access activities. Suspicious physical access activities include accesses outside of normal work hours, repeated accesses to areas not normally accessed, accesses for unusual lengths of time, and out-of-sequence accesses.

Enhancements:
MONITORING PHYSICAL ACCESS | INTRUSION ALARMS AND SURVEILLANCE EQUIPMENT
Monitor physical access to the facility where the system resides using physical intrusion alarms and surveillance equipment.
Discussion: Physical intrusion alarms can be employed to alert security personnel when unauthorized access to the facility is attempted. Alarm systems work in conjunction with physical barriers, physical access control systems, and security guards by triggering a response when these other forms of security have been compromised or breached.
Related Controls: None.
MONITORING PHYSICAL ACCESS | AUTOMATED INTRUSION RECOGNITION AND RESPONSES
Recognize [Assignment: organization-defined classes or types of intrusions] and initiate [Assignment: organization-defined response actions] using [Assignment: organization-defined automated mechanisms].
Discussion: Response actions can include notifying selected organizational personnel or law enforcement personnel. Automated mechanisms implemented to initiate response actions include system alert notifications, email and text messages, and activating door locking mechanisms. Physical access monitoring can be coordinated with intrusion detection mechanisms.
Related Controls: None.

title: ACCESS CONTROL FOR OUTPUT DEVICES
methodology:
CONTROL ID: PE-5 – ACCESS CONTROL FOR OUTPUT DEVICES

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence, such as output devices (printers, monitors, copiers) being located within physically secured areas (e.g., locked rooms, rooms with card reader or keypad access) 
• Technical artifacts, including access control logs for secured areas and device authentication records if individual identity is linked to output release
• Operational patterns: Regular audits of room access logs, monitoring camera feeds to verify that only authorized personnel approach the devices, and periodic physical patrols or security checks confirming that output devices remain in secured zones

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations sometimes fudge compliance by simply placing output devices in areas labeled as secure without evidence of enforced access control (e.g., dummy locks or ineffective card readers)
• Surface-level implementations might involve posting a sign that access is restricted without any actual technical or physical enforcement
• A common technical gap is the lack of integrated logging or authentication on devices that claim to tie output release to an individual identity, making it difficult to verify who retrieved the output

3. VALIDATION STEPS
• CHECK: Physical Location and Access Controls
  WHY: Verifying that output devices are in areas with environmental physical safeguards ensures that unauthorized persons cannot easily approach them
  HOW: Inspect secured areas where output devices are located to confirm locked door status, review the presence of card readers or keypads, and monitor entry logs
  FAIL: Devices are found in open or unsupervised areas or the access mechanism is purely decorative with no actual controlled entry

• CHECK: Access and Authentication Mechanisms on Devices
  WHY: Devices with built-in authentication ensure that outputs (like print jobs) are released only to individuals who can verify their identity
  HOW: Access a sample device (e.g., a printer or copier) to confirm that user authentication (PIN, badge, or other personal credentials) is required to release outputs; review device configuration settings
  FAIL: Device outputs are automatically released without any individual user authentication or identity logging

• CHECK: Log and Monitoring Evidence
  WHY: Consistent logging provides evidence that access to output devices is monitored and records of who retrieves output are maintained, enhancing accountability
  HOW: Retrieve and analyze logs from secured areas (e.g., door access logs, device authentication logs) and cross-reference with user schedules or expected output retrieval events
  FAIL: Logs are missing, incomplete, or indicate unauthorized attempts, suggesting inadequate enforcement of access control

• CHECK: Physical Security Monitoring
  WHY: Active monitoring (e.g., CCTV near output devices) offers a secondary control to deter unauthorized access and capture any bypass attempts in real time
  HOW: Verify that surveillance cameras cover the output device areas; review recent footage or monitoring reports to verify regular checks are performed
  FAIL: Areas containing output devices lack active monitoring, or the monitoring system is outdated or frequently inoperative

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Verification Shortcut: Directly request and review a sample of access logs and video surveillance records from secured areas. This tangible evidence is less likely to be faked compared to policy documents.
• Critical Indicator: If user authentication is optional on devices that claim to support individual ID linking, suspect a superficial deployment. Ensure that authentication is enforced at every output release
• Detail that Matters: Check the integration between physical access control systems (e.g., door card readers) and output device management; a lack of correlation may indicate an area ripe for bypass
• Do not rely solely on visual confirmations or antiquated CCTV claims; always corroborate with system logs and, if possible, automated alerts that flag anomalous access patterns.

By following these validation steps, one can effectively verify that control PE-5 is not only documented but implemented in a practical, enforceable, and audit-ready manner.
content:
Statement:
Control physical access to output from [Assignment: organization-defined output devices] to prevent unauthorized individuals from obtaining the output.

Guidance:
Controlling physical access to output devices includes placing output devices in locked rooms or other secured areas with keypad or card reader access controls and allowing access to authorized individuals only, placing output devices in locations that can be monitored by personnel, installing monitor or screen filters, and using headphones. Examples of output devices include monitors, printers, scanners, audio devices, facsimile machines, and copiers.

Enhancements:
ACCESS CONTROL FOR OUTPUT DEVICES | ACCESS TO OUTPUT BY AUTHORIZED INDIVIDUALS [Withdrawn: Incorporated into PE-5].
ACCESS CONTROL FOR OUTPUT DEVICES | LINK TO INDIVIDUAL IDENTITY
Link individual identity to receipt of output from output devices.
Discussion: Methods for linking individual identity to the receipt of output from output devices include installing security functionality on facsimile machines, copiers, and printers. Such functionality allows organizations to implement authentication on output devices prior to the release of output to individuals.

title: ACCESS CONTROL FOR TRANSMISSION
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for PE-4 (Access Control for Transmission):

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Evidence of physical controls on system distribution and transmission lines such as locked wiring closets, cable trays within conduits, and secured distribution areas. 
   • Technical artifacts include facility access logs, sensor/tamper alert logs, and maintenance records of physical security devices (e.g., tamper sensors or locks).
   • Operational patterns include periodic physical inspections, automated alerts from wiretapping sensors, and routine maintenance verifying the integrity of locked or secured areas.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations may claim compliance through documentation (policies, procedures, floor plans) without actual physical security enforcement.
   • Visible installations (e.g., conduits and locked rooms) might be superficially implemented without regular audits or sensor monitoring.
   • Outsourced maintenance records or inconsistent sensor log reviews can make it appear controls are active when they are not reliably verified.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   • CHECK: Physical Inspection of Transmission Facilities
     WHY: Direct observation confirms that distribution lines reside in secured, locked areas or within protective conduits.
     HOW: Visit facilities to verify that wiring closets, server rooms, and conduits are visibly locked and access is controlled (through locks, key cards, etc.). Review floor plans and compare them with physical installations.
     FAIL: Open or poorly secured areas; absence of physical barriers or locked cabinets indicate non-compliance.

   • CHECK: Review and Analysis of Access Logs and Sensor Alerts
     WHY: Operational logs demonstrate active monitoring and prompt detection of unauthorized access or tampering events.
     HOW: Examine automated access control logs, sensor/tamper alert records, and incident reports. Verify that logs are maintained, regularly reviewed, and tied to physical access enforcement.
     FAIL: Missing or inconsistent logs, lack of periodic reviews or evidence that alerts are ignored point to ineffective controls.

   • CHECK: Verification of Maintenance and Audit Records
     WHY: Scheduled inspections and audits ensure that physical controls are not only implemented but are continuously effective.
     HOW: Check records of routine physical security audits, sensor calibrations, and maintenance activities. Interview personnel responsible for upkeep to gauge operational enforcement.
     FAIL: Outdated audit records, records that do not reflect recent changes, or a lack of scheduled inspections signal poor implementation.

   • CHECK: Evaluation of Tamper-Evident Measures
     WHY: The existence and functionality of tamper sensors and seals help in early detection of physical compromise.
     HOW: Physically inspect selected transmission lines for tamper-evident seals and sensor placements. Test sensor functionality (if possible) or review recent sensor test logs.
     FAIL: Lack of tamper-evident features or sensor testing logs suggest vulnerabilities in the physical control mechanism.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Focus on real-time operational data over static documentation. Automated sensor alerts, recent log entries, and verifiable physical inspections are key.
   • Pay special attention to high-risk areas such as exposed wiring closets or distribution hubs – these are common targets for bypass attempts.
   • Ask facility managers to explain recent incidents or maintenance activities; discrepancies often indicate that controls are more theoretical than operational.
   • Regular unannounced inspections can reveal gaps that scheduled audits may miss; use them as part of your evaluation methodology.

This structured approach emphasizes observable evidence and operational effectiveness to validate that access controls for transmission lines are genuinely in place and functioning as intended.
content:
Statement:
Control physical access to [Assignment: organization-defined system distribution and transmission lines] within organizational facilities using [Assignment: organization-defined security controls].

Guidance:
Security controls applied to system distribution and transmission lines prevent accidental damage, disruption, and physical tampering. Such controls may also be necessary to prevent eavesdropping or modification of unencrypted transmissions. Security controls used to control physical access to system distribution and transmission lines include disconnected or locked spaces, locked wiring closets, protection of cabling by conduit or cable trays, and wiretapping sensors.

title: PHYSICAL ACCESS CONTROL
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence includes controlled entry/exit points with functioning physical barriers, badge readers, video surveillance, and clearly marked signs indicating access control measures. Physical logs, either displayed on screens or maintained in a secure log room, are also an indicator. 
• Technical artifacts include timestamped digital or paper audit logs from access control systems, key management records, and electronic records of visitor escorts. CCTV footage and automated alarm logs are further artifacts.
• Operational patterns such as routine facility walkthroughs by security staff, periodic physical inspections, and randomized checks on access records highlight that the control is active.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations might fake compliance by generating retrospective log entries or using unsynchronized clocks on access control systems. Paper logs may be fabricated without cross-checks against digital records or video evidence.
• Temporary badge issuance not tied to comprehensive identity checks, or static surveillance cameras in inactive areas, often provide surface-level compliance but fail to secure physical assets in practice.
• Omitting regular audits on key management and not updating logs after visitor exits represent commonly missed technical operational gaps.

VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Verify that controlled entry points (doors, turnstiles, vestibules) are equipped with functioning physical access devices (e.g., badge readers) and that these devices log each access event.
WHY: This confirms that access authorizations are being actively enforced, aligning with requirements to verify and control ingress and egress.
HOW: Observe the installation at designated entry/exit points, request a live demonstration of badge entry, and inspect electronic logs for recent events. Correlate entries with CCTV records if available.
FAIL: Lack of functioning access devices, missing logs, or discrepancies between the system’s log timestamps and physical activity indicate non-compliance.

CHECK: Validate that physical access audit logs are being maintained and routinely reviewed.
WHY: Active audit logs demonstrate that the organization is tracking every entry and exit, which is crucial for accountability and forensic investigations.
HOW: Review a sample of audit logs covering a defined period. Verify that log entries include time stamps, personnel identities, and access points. Cross-check these records with visitor logs and CCTV footage.
FAIL: Incomplete, missing, or poorly maintained logs—such as gaps in time stamps or unexplained entries—can signal a superficial implementation.

CHECK: Inspect visitor management procedures, ensuring that visitors are properly escorted and that their movement is logged.
WHY: This check is critical because visitor management significantly mitigates the risk of unauthorized access in publicly accessible areas.
HOW: Interview security personnel about visitor escort policies; review visitor logs and observe visitor check-in areas during a facility walkthrough.
FAIL: Absence of visitor logs, no evidence of escort policies in practice, or unsecured areas designated for visitors indicates poor implementation.

CHECK: Examine the key and access device management system, including inventories and change frequency schedules.
WHY: Securing keys, combinations, and other physical access devices is essential to prevent unauthorized access and demonstrates compliance with periodic inventory and key change protocols.
HOW: Inspect the key control logs, verify the frequency of inventory checks, and review records for recent key or combination changes. Ask staff to demonstrate the secure storage mechanism.
FAIL: Outdated inventory records, infrequent key changes, or unsecured storage areas provide clear indicators that the control is suboptimal.

CHECK: Physically observe areas designated as publicly accessible and verify that they are appropriately separated from non-public areas by physical barriers or access-controlled zones.
WHY: This ensures that physical access is segmented and that areas with sensitive assets are not inadvertently reachable by unauthorized individuals.
HOW: Conduct a physical walk-through of the facility, noting the presence of physical barriers, locked doors, and controlled access signages at transition points.
FAIL: If publicly accessible zones provide direct access to restricted areas or lack proper physical barriers, the control implementation is inadequate.

EXPERT TIPS
• Use cross-correlation: Always verify audit logs against independent sources like CCTV footage and security shift records to detect tampering or discrepancies.
• Random sampling: Request unannounced walkthroughs or random audits of the access control systems and key management processes.
• Focus on operational effectiveness: Don’t rely solely on policy documents—look for spontaneous evidence of checks by staff (e.g., ongoing visitor surveillance or guard patrols).
• Check time synchronization: Ensure that all systems (access control devices, CCTV, and logging systems) are time-synced to prevent faking of chronological records.
• Key areas to flag: Missing visitor logs, irregular key inventory reports, and evidence of outdated access mechanisms are strong indicators of non-compliance.

This methodology provides a framework to verify that physical access controls are not only documented but are effectively implemented and operational in preventing unauthorized physical entry and ensuring accountability.
content:
Statement:
a. Enforce physical access authorizations at [Assignment: organization-defined entry and exit points to the facility where the system resides] by:

   1. Verifying individual access authorizations before granting access to the facility; and

   2. Controlling ingress and egress to the facility using [Selection (one or more): [Assignment: organization-defined physical access control systems or devices]; guards];

b. Maintain physical access audit logs for [Assignment: organization-defined entry or exit points];

c. Control access to areas within the facility designated as publicly accessible by implementing the following controls: [Assignment: organization-defined physical access controls];

d. Escort visitors and control visitor activity [Assignment: organization-defined circumstances requiring visitor escorts and control of visitor activity];

e. Secure keys, combinations, and other physical access devices;

f. Inventory [Assignment: organization-defined physical access devices] every [Assignment: organization-defined frequency]; and

g. Change combinations and keys [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] and/or when keys are lost, combinations are compromised, or when individuals possessing the keys or combinations are transferred or terminated.

Guidance:
Physical access control applies to employees and visitors. Individuals with permanent physical access authorizations are not considered visitors. Physical access controls for publicly accessible areas may include physical access control logs/records, guards, or physical access devices and barriers to prevent movement from publicly accessible areas to non-public areas. Organizations determine the types of guards needed, including professional security staff, system users, or administrative staff. Physical access control systems comply with applicable laws, executive orders, directives, policies, regulations, standards, and guidelines. Organizations have flexibility in the types of audit logs employed. Audit logs can be procedural, automated, or some combination thereof. Physical access points can include facility access points, interior access points to systems that require supplemental access controls, or both. Components of systems may be in areas designated as publicly accessible with organizations controlling access to the components.

Enhancements:
PHYSICAL ACCESS CONTROL | SYSTEM ACCESS
Enforce physical access authorizations to the system in addition to the physical access controls for the facility at [Assignment: organization-defined physical spaces containing one or more components of the system].
Discussion: Control of physical access to the system provides additional physical security for those areas within facilities where there is a concentration of system components.
Related Controls: None.
PHYSICAL ACCESS CONTROL | FACILITY AND SYSTEMS
Perform security checks [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] at the physical perimeter of the facility or system for exfiltration of information or removal of system components.
Discussion: Organizations determine the extent, frequency, and/or randomness of security checks to adequately mitigate risk associated with exfiltration.
Related Controls: AC-4, SC-7.
PHYSICAL ACCESS CONTROL | CONTINUOUS GUARDS
Employ guards to control [Assignment: organization-defined physical access points] to the facility where the system resides 24 hours per day, 7 days per week.
Discussion: Employing guards at selected physical access points to the facility provides a more rapid response capability for organizations. Guards also provide the opportunity for human surveillance in areas of the facility not covered by video surveillance.
Related Controls: CP-6, CP-7, PE-6.
PHYSICAL ACCESS CONTROL | LOCKABLE CASINGS
Use lockable physical casings to protect [Assignment: organization-defined system components] from unauthorized physical access.
Discussion: The greatest risk from the use of portable devices—such as smart phones, tablets, and notebook computers—is theft. Organizations can employ lockable, physical casings to reduce or eliminate the risk of equipment theft. Such casings come in a variety of sizes, from units that protect a single notebook computer to full cabinets that can protect multiple servers, computers, and peripherals. Lockable physical casings can be used in conjunction with cable locks or lockdown plates to prevent the theft of the locked casing containing the computer equipment.
Related Controls: None.
PHYSICAL ACCESS CONTROL | TAMPER PROTECTION
Employ [Assignment: organization-defined anti-tamper technologies] to [Selection (one or more): detect; prevent] physical tampering or alteration of [Assignment: organization-defined hardware components] within the system.
Discussion: Organizations can implement tamper detection and prevention at selected hardware components or implement tamper detection at some components and tamper prevention at other components. Detection and prevention activities can employ many types of anti-tamper technologies, including tamper-detection seals and anti-tamper coatings. Anti-tamper programs help to detect hardware alterations through counterfeiting and other supply chain-related risks.
Related Controls: SA-16, SR-9, SR-11.
PHYSICAL ACCESS CONTROL | FACILITY PENETRATION TESTING
[Withdrawn: Incorporated into CA-8].
PHYSICAL ACCESS CONTROL | PHYSICAL BARRIERS
Limit access using physical barriers.
Discussion: Physical barriers include bollards, concrete slabs, jersey walls, and hydraulic active vehicle barriers.
Related Controls: None.
PHYSICAL ACCESS CONTROL | ACCESS CONTROL VESTIBULES
Employ access control vestibules at [Assignment: organization-defined locations within the facility].

title: PHYSICAL ACCESS AUTHORIZATIONS
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Visible evidence includes an up‐to‐date, approved list of individuals with access to the facility, physical credentials (e.g., ID badges, smart cards) visibly in use, and records of periodic reviews and removals. 
• Technical artifacts include digital logs from badge systems that correlate with the access list, automated HR integrations that disable access on termination, and meeting minutes or change logs documenting regular authorized access reviews.
• Operational patterns include a routine process for issuing, reviewing, and revoking physical access credentials; defined approval workflows for access changes; and integration with HR termination events.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations might simply maintain a printed list on a bulletin board or in a locked file without ensuring synchronization with actual badge issuance systems.
• Temporary or one-off visitors’ credentials may be improperly documented or not audited, thereby faking compliance.
• Review meetings may be held on paper only or not at the stated frequency, and credential revocation may be delayed due to manual processes, leading to discrepancies between the authorized list and actual badge usage.

VALIDATION STEPS:
CHECK: Verify the current physical access authorization list against the badge issuance records.
WHY: This step demonstrates that only individuals with approved credentials are granted entry and that the list ties into the actual technical issuance controls.
HOW: Obtain the latest approved access list from the security or facility manager; retrieve the electronic badge issuance or access control system logs; compare entries to ensure consistency.
FAIL: Discrepancies such as names on the list without corresponding active badge records, or badges issued to individuals not on the list prove noncompliance.

CHECK: Evaluate the periodic review process for the access list.
WHY: Regular reviews are critical to ensuring continuous appropriateness of authorized access and timely revocation after role or employment changes.
HOW: Examine documentation of review frequency, interview the manager responsible for the review, and check for meeting minutes or change logs that evidence a current review cycle.
FAIL: Missing or outdated review documentation, or evidence that reviews are only conducted sporadically, indicates the process is not effectively enforced.

CHECK: Sample physical credentials issuance and revocation events with HR records.
WHY: Ensures that both issuance and removal of credentials match HR records and that departures or role changes are promptly reflected in access controls.
HOW: Select a random sample of individuals from the authorized list; verify their hiring, role, or termination dates in HR records; check corresponding access control logs for badge activation and deactivation events.
FAIL: If individuals who have left the organization or changed roles still have active credentials, it indicates a breakdown in the timely update of the physical access list.

CHECK: Verify controls around visitor access and unescorted access to restricted areas.
WHY: Ensuring two forms of identification for visitors or restricting unescorted access confirms that visitors and non-regular personnel are properly vetted before entry.
HOW: Review visitor logs and sign‐in/out records; confirm that visitors are required to provide two forms of identification or are escorted; inspect any technical reports from automated access systems showing identification factors (e.g., PIV cards, biometrics, PINs).
FAIL: Evidence of unescorted access by visitors, missing identification checks, or inconsistent enforcement of the required identification factors indicates non-compliance.

EXPERT TIPS:
• A quick shortcut is to run an automated query on the access control system to identify active badge records that have not been recently validated against the approved access list—this frequently highlights overdue removals.
• Pay extra attention to the integration between HR termination events and access deactivation: any lag can be a critical vulnerability.
• Physical spot-checks at entry points (e.g., badge scanning live view) can provide immediate evidence of whether the process is enforced, beyond what documentation suggests.
• Documentation alone is not enough; confirmed technical logs and on-the-ground observations are key indicators of actual operational compliance.
• Look for any patterns of repeated exceptions in the logs that might suggest manual overrides or bypasses in the access control system.
content:
Statement:
a. Develop, approve, and maintain a list of individuals with authorized access to the facility where the system resides;
b. Issue authorization credentials for facility access;
c. Review the access list detailing authorized facility access by individuals [Assignment: organization-defined frequency]; and
d. Remove individuals from the facility access list when access is no longer required.

Guidance:
Physical access authorizations apply to employees and visitors. Individuals with permanent physical access authorization credentials are not considered visitors. Authorization credentials include ID badges, identification cards, and smart cards. Organizations determine the strength of authorization credentials needed consistent with applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines. Physical access authorizations may not be necessary to access certain areas within facilities that are designated as publicly accessible.

Enhancements:
PHYSICAL ACCESS AUTHORIZATIONS | ACCESS BY POSITION OR ROLE
Authorize physical access to the facility where the system resides based on position or role.

Discussion: Role-based facility access includes access by authorized permanent and regular/routine maintenance personnel, duty officers, and emergency medical staff.

Related Controls: AC-2, AC-3, AC-6.
PHYSICAL ACCESS AUTHORIZATIONS | TWO FORMS OF IDENTIFICATION
Require two forms of identification from the following forms of identification for visitor access to the facility where the system resides: [Assignment: organization-defined list of acceptable forms of identification].

Discussion: Acceptable forms of identification include passports, REAL ID-compliant drivers’ licenses, and Personal Identity Verification (PIV) cards. For gaining access to facilities using automated mechanisms, organizations may use PIV cards, key cards, PINs, and biometrics.

Related Controls: IA-2, IA-4, IA-5.
PHYSICAL ACCESS AUTHORIZATIONS | RESTRICT UNESCORTED ACCESS
Restrict unescorted access to the facility where the system resides to personnel with [Selection (one or more): security clearances for all information contained within the system; formal access authorizations for all information contained within the system; need for access to all information contained within the system; [Assignment: organization-defined physical access authorizations]].

Discussion: Individuals without required security clearances, access approvals, or need to know are escorted by individuals with appropriate physical access authorizations to ensure that information is not exposed or otherwise compromised.

Related Controls: PS-2, PS-6.

title: POLICY AND PROCEDURES
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence includes a formally documented physical and environmental protection policy that details roles, responsibilities, management commitment, and coordination mechanisms. This document is usually stored in a controlled document repository and referenced through distributed training or announcements.
• Technical artifacts demonstrating compliance include version-controlled policy documents, meeting minutes from policy review sessions, distribution logs showing dissemination to defined personnel, and records of periodic reviews and updates triggered by events such as audit findings or regulatory changes.
• Operational patterns include regular policy update cycles, designated personnel handling policy management, and integration of these policies into broader security and privacy programs.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may claim compliance by simply uploading a generic policy that merely restates the high-level control without addressing the organization’s specific physical and environmental needs.
• Some entities might list a policy on an intranet site without evidence of dissemination, training, or periodic reviews.
• Superficial implementation can be observed when the designated official role is not clearly defined in practice or when actual procedural activities (e.g., update events following incidents) are not documented.

VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Verify the existence of a formally documented physical and environmental protection policy that addresses purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, and compliance requirements.
WHY: This ensures that the organization has a tailored and approved security framework in place.
HOW: Review the document repository for the latest version of the policy, confirm signatures and approval evidence, and match its content against the organization-defined scope.
FAIL: The absence of a document, outdated versions without clear approval signatures, or generic text that does not reflect organizational specifics.

CHECK: Validate evidence of policy dissemination and training records to relevant personnel or roles.
WHY: Demonstrates that the policy is not just documented but actively communicated to those responsible for its implementation.
HOW: Inspect distribution lists, training logs, email communications, or intranet postings that show the policy has been shared with the intended recipients.
FAIL: Lack of dissemination evidence or training records, or documents that indicate minimal or one-off sharing without ongoing reinforcement.

CHECK: Confirm that there is a designated official responsible for managing the development, dissemination, and maintenance of the policy and procedures.
WHY: Clear assignment of roles ensures accountability and proper governance of the security controls.
HOW: Look for a documented assignment, such as meeting minutes or official communications, that designate a specific individual or role with defined responsibilities.
FAIL: If no documentation exists to prove that a responsible party has been designated, or if the role appears to be nominal without operational follow-up.

CHECK: Review records of periodic reviews and updates triggered by defined events such as audit findings, incidents, or changes in legal/regulatory requirements.
WHY: Regular updates indicate that the policy is actively managed and remains relevant in changing environments.
HOW: Check logs or calendars showing scheduled reviews, meeting minutes discussing update events, and evidence of revisions in response to assessments or external changes.
FAIL: Absence of recent review records, outdated policy documents, or failure to document changes following triggering events.

EXPERT TIPS
• A quick verification shortcut is to ask the designated official for examples of recent updates and review cycles – hesitation or vague answers may indicate poor implementation.
• Check that the dissemination effort is not just one-off by reviewing longitudinal training records over several review periods.
• Look at the integration of the policy within other security incident reports or audit findings – effective policies are referenced and enforced across multiple artifacts.
• Pay attention to the detail in the policy regarding roles and responsibilities; generic policies that lack specificity often signal a paper-compliance issue.
content:
Statement:
a. Develop, document, and disseminate to [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles]:
   1. [Selection (one or more): Organization-level; Mission/business process-level; System-level] physical and environmental protection policy that:
      (a) Addresses purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, coordination among organizational entities, and compliance; and
      (b) Is consistent with applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines; and
   2. Procedures to facilitate the implementation of the physical and environmental protection policy and the associated physical and environmental protection controls;

b. Designate an [Assignment: organization-defined official] to manage the development, documentation, and dissemination of the physical and environmental protection policy and procedures; and

c. Review and update the current physical and environmental protection:
   1. Policy [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] and following [Assignment: organization-defined events]; and
   2. Procedures [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] and following [Assignment: organization-defined events].

Guidance:
Physical and environmental protection policy and procedures address the controls in the PE family that are implemented within systems and organizations. The risk management strategy is an important factor in establishing such policies and procedures. Policies and procedures contribute to security and privacy assurance. Therefore, it is important that security and privacy programs collaborate on the development of physical and environmental protection policy and procedures. Security and privacy program policies and procedures at the organization level are preferable, in general, and may obviate the need for mission- or system-specific policies and procedures. The policy can be included as part of the general security and privacy policy or can be established for security and privacy programs, for mission or business processes, and for systems, if needed. Procedures describe how the policies or controls are implemented and can be directed at the individual or role that is the object of the procedure. Procedures can be documented in system security and privacy plans or in one or more separate documents. Events that may precipitate an update to physical and environmental protection policy and procedures include assessment or audit findings, security incidents or breaches, or changes in applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines. Simply restating controls does not constitute an organizational policy or procedure.

title: MEDIA DOWNGRADING
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Documented media downgrading process: Look for published procedures that detail the downgrading steps, including the mechanisms, equipment, frequency of testing, and personnel assignments.
• Technical artifacts: Audit logs, equipment test reports, and evidence of redaction tools (e.g., software/sanitization tool usage reports) that indicate that media downgrading has been performed.
• Operational patterns: Regular, recurring downgrading activities that are logged and tied to specific media identifiers, along with periodic reviews or testing records to ensure consistency with the security classification of the media.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Superficial documentation: Organizations may publish policies without aligning them with operational procedures or demonstrating effective use of approved downgrading techniques.
• Incomplete artifact retention: Evidence might be limited to administrative checklists without proof of technical effectiveness (e.g., logs without corresponding test results).
• Outdated processes: Using legacy downgrading tools or not updating procedures to match the current classification levels can create gaps despite the existence of policy.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Verify the existence of an organization-defined media downgrading process that outlines specific techniques (both for digital and non-digital media) and includes frequency of equipment and process testing.
   WHY: A comprehensive, documented process shows that the organization has established controls that match the risk level of the information.
   HOW: Review internal procedural documents, guidelines, and training materials; verify process version and update dates; and conduct interviews with process owners.
   FAIL: Absence of detailed procedures or reliance only on high-level policies without implementation specifics.

2. CHECK: Examine logs and audit trails for media downgrading actions, ensuring they capture the identification of media, downgrading method used, operator details, and timestamps.
   WHY: Detailed logging provides tangible evidence that downgrading actions were performed and are traceable to specific events and individuals.
   HOW: Access system logs, review samples of downgrading actions, confirm that each log entry corresponds to the documented process, and verify tool-generated outputs.
   FAIL: Missing or incomplete logs, lack of operator identification, or mismatches between documentation and log entries.

3. CHECK: Test the downgrading mechanism on sample media to verify that downgraded content cannot be retrieved or reconstructed.
   WHY: Hands-on testing confirms the operational effectiveness of the downgrading process rather than relying solely on documentation.
   HOW: Utilize approved sanitization tools and techniques on a set of media containing sample classified data, then attempt recovery using forensic methods; review equipment test reports.
   FAIL: Recovery of any portion of the data after the downgrading action or evidence that the downgrading tool does not match current security requirements.

4. CHECK: Review the schedule and results of periodic tests of the downgrading equipment and procedures as defined by the organization.
   WHY: Regular testing ensures the downgrading process remains effective and in line with evolving classification standards and threats.
   HOW: Verify test schedules, examine recent test reports, and interview the security team responsible for test planning and execution.
   FAIL: Outdated test schedules, missing or inconsistent test results, or evidence that tests are not performed with the defined frequency.

EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Focus on the audit logs first – they are hard to fake and provide a time-stamped, operational record of actions taken.
• Critical indicator: Any trace of recoverable data or missing downgrading execution details in logs signals a breakdown in the control, regardless of policy existence.
• Detail matters: Ensure that the record includes the identity and authorization status of the personnel performing the downgrading; without this, accountability is lost.
• Mature processes not only document actions but tie them directly to classification levels – always cross-check the downgrading method with the security category of the media to confirm proper alignment.
content:
Statement:
a. Establish [Assignment: organization-defined system media downgrading process] that includes employing downgrading mechanisms with strength and integrity commensurate with the security category or classification of the information;
b. Verify that the system media downgrading process is commensurate with the security category and/or classification level of the information to be removed and the access authorizations of the potential recipients of the downgraded information;
c. Identify [Assignment: organization-defined system media requiring downgrading]; and
d. Downgrade the identified system media using the established process.

Guidance:
Media downgrading applies to digital and non-digital media subject to release outside of the organization, whether the media is considered removable or not. When applied to system media, the downgrading process removes information from the media, typically by security category or classification level, such that the information cannot be retrieved or reconstructed. Downgrading of media includes redacting information to enable wider release and distribution. Downgrading ensures that empty space on the media is devoid of information.

Enhancements:
MEDIA DOWNGRADING | DOCUMENTATION OF PROCESS
Document system media downgrading actions. Discussion: Organizations can document the media downgrading process by providing information, such as the downgrading technique employed, the identification number of the downgraded media, and the identity of the individual that authorized and/or performed the downgrading action.
MEDIA DOWNGRADING | EQUIPMENT TESTING
Test downgrading equipment and procedures [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] to ensure that downgrading actions are being achieved. Discussion: None. Related Controls: None.
MEDIA DOWNGRADING | CONTROLLED UNCLASSIFIED INFORMATION
Downgrade system media containing controlled unclassified information prior to public release. Discussion: The downgrading of controlled unclassified information uses approved sanitization tools, techniques, and procedures. Related Controls: None.
MEDIA DOWNGRADING | CLASSIFIED INFORMATION
Downgrade system media containing classified information prior to release to individuals without required access authorizations. Discussion: Downgrading of classified information uses approved sanitization tools, techniques, and procedures to transfer information confirmed to be unclassified from classified systems to unclassified media. Related Controls: None.

title: MEDIA USE
methodology:
Validation Methodology for MP-7 MEDIA USE

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence: Look for system configuration screens or management console indicators that have settings explicitly disabling or restricting the use of certain removable media (e.g., USB flash drives, external hard drives, etc.). Physical evidence might include locked or physically modified ports on workstations.
• Technical artifacts: Documentation of endpoint configurations (such as Group Policy Objects, mobile device management (MDM) settings, or other centralized configuration management outputs) that enforce media use restrictions. DLP (Data Loss Prevention) tool logs documenting attempted unauthorized media connections are also key evidence.
• Operational patterns: Look for consistent alerts/log events from automated monitoring systems when an unapproved media device is attached. Check that regular reviews or scans are logged and that endpoint configuration drift is minimal over time.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Fake compliance: Organizations might simply have an up-to-date policy document without actually enforcing technical controls. For example, a policy might exist but USB ports may not be physically disabled or centrally monitored.
• Superficial implementations: Outdated configuration profiles that no longer match current hardware or active endpoints, or DLP tools that are not effectively set-up, may provide a false sense of security without blocking actual usage.
• Technical gaps: Custom or unauthorized firmware modifications or hardware add-ons (e.g., USB port adapters) that bypass the restrictions, and endpoints that are misconfigured can undermine the enforcement.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
• CHECK: Verify Endpoint Configuration for Media Controls
  WHY: This ensures that technical policies restricting media use are actively applied at the device level.
  HOW: Use an automated scan through a configuration management tool or patch management system to query settings related to USB and external port enablement on a representative sample of systems. Check for configuration files or Group Policy settings that disable unauthorized media use.
  FAIL: Findings where endpoints allow connection of unauthorized media types or show default settings with no disabling/enforcement.

• CHECK: Review DLP and System Logs for Media Access Events
  WHY: DLP logs or system logs that document blocks or alerts provide tangible evidence that the system is actively monitoring and enforcing media use policies.
  HOW: Filter the logs for events related to the attempted use of removable media (e.g., USB flash drives) and then verify if events appear from systems that should have restrictions in place. Cross-reference event timestamps with known policy enforcement periods.
  FAIL: Lack of events or logs that indicate that media insertions are monitored and blocked; sparse or inconsistent logging may indicate poor enforcement.

• CHECK: Physical Inspection of Workstations
  WHY: Physical controls (e.g., port locks, cages) are essential to enforce restrictions, providing a tangible layer of protection that can’t be bypassed solely via software.
  HOW: Conduct random sampling of workstations and mobile devices to visually confirm the presence of physical deterrents or disabled port modifications. Correlate this with technical configuration records.
  FAIL: Discovery of workstations with accessible or unprotected ports that permit connection of external media devices.

• CHECK: Validate Identification & Registration of Portable Media
  WHY: Enforcing the requirement that portable storage devices must have an identifiable owner ensures accountability and traceability.
  HOW: Audit endpoints by reviewing device inventories and connection logs. Verify that any detected portable media are registered against the organization’s asset management system, checking for unique identifications or owner records.
  FAIL: Finding portable media in use on systems without a corresponding identifiable record or user asset registration.

• CHECK: Assess User Awareness & Training Records
  WHY: Effective enforcement requires that users are aware of these restrictions, reducing the likelihood of intentional or accidental policy breaches.
  HOW: Interview a sample of system users and review training records to confirm that media use policies and the risks associated with non-compliance have been communicated properly.
  FAIL: Widespread gaps in user awareness, inadequate or missing training records, or verbal interviews that do not reflect a clear understanding of the media use control.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Verification shortcuts: Focus on using automated configuration auditing tools that continuously verify endpoint configurations against the approved baseline. A centralized dashboard showing live status of media control settings can quickly highlight discrepancies.
• Critical indicators of poor implementation: Watch for endpoints with outdated configurations or missing physical controls. Also note if there’s any sign of firmware modifications that re-enable disabled ports.
• Details that matter: Ensure that DLP logs are tamper-proof (preferably centrally stored and backed-up) and that physical inspections align with technical settings. Do not rely solely on policy documents; always verify real-time system settings and user practices.
• Real-world check: Often, a misconfiguration on a minority of endpoints is enough for attackers to exploit; hence, sample widely and compare against a known good baseline regularly.
content:
Statement:
a. [Selection: Restrict; Prohibit] the use of [Assignment: organization-defined types of system media] on [Assignment: organization-defined systems or system components] using [Assignment: organization-defined controls]; and
b. Prohibit the use of portable storage devices in organizational systems when such devices have no identifiable owner.

Guidance:
System media includes both digital and non-digital media. Digital media includes diskettes, magnetic tapes, flash drives, compact discs, digital versatile discs, and removable hard disk drives. Non-digital media includes paper and microfilm. Media use protections also apply to mobile devices with information storage capabilities. In contrast to MP-2, which restricts user access to media, MP-7 restricts the use of certain types of media on systems, for example, restricting or prohibiting the use of flash drives or external hard disk drives. Organizations use technical and nontechnical controls to restrict the use of system media. Organizations may restrict the use of portable storage devices, for example, by using physical cages on workstations to prohibit access to certain external ports or disabling or removing the ability to insert, read, or.

title: MEDIA SANITIZATION
methodology:
Below is the practical validation methodology for MP-6 MEDIA SANITIZATION:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence includes documented sanitization logs, certificates from approved sanitization tools, and maintenance records for sanitization equipment. In physical environments, you may also see marked or sealed media awaiting disposal.
• Technical artifacts include automated logs that record the sanitization event (date/time, media identifier, method used), dual authorization change logs, and test reports from sanitization equipment. Configuration files from remote purging/wiping tools can also serve as evidence.
• Operational patterns indicating control activity include routine reviews of sanitization logs, periodic equipment testing, dual authorization records for sanitization actions, and incident response measures (e.g., verification tests of purged data).

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations can fake compliance by providing sanitized media certificates or logs that superficially indicate sanitization without actual verification of sanitization effectiveness. For example, performing a basic drive format without applying proper clearing or cryptographic erase techniques.
• Surface-level implementations may also include undocumented or ad-hoc sanitization actions that are not integrated into formal processes (for example, skipping dual authorization steps or not logging key sanitization parameters).
• Frequently missed technical gaps include failure to periodically test and validate equipment, not verifying remote purging commands via controlled tests, and neglecting verification of non-destructive sanitization techniques on portable storage devices.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Verify Sanitization Operation Logs and Dual Authorization Records
WHY: Authenticated, timestamped logs with dual authorization evidence prove that sanitization was performed according to documented organizational practices.
HOW: Review automated logs from sanitization tools to confirm that media have been processed, ensuring logs contain media IDs, sanitization methods used, operator IDs, date/time stamps, and dual authorization where applicable. Check that the records match the inventory of disposed or reused media.
FAIL: Evidence is missing, incomplete, or if logs show a single approval record where dual authorization is required, or if dates and media identifiers do not align with physical media tracking records.

CHECK: Inspect and Test Sanitization Equipment and Procedures
WHY: Regular testing and documented results from sanitization equipment confirm that the mechanisms in place are effective and appropriately maintained.
HOW: Review test schedules and maintenance records for sanitization hardware (including remote purge/wipe systems). If possible, observe a test event or verify test outcomes from external audits. Check that the equipment’s sanitization method matches the media classification and covers both destructive and nondestructive requirements where used.
FAIL: Missing test records or evidence of infrequent testing; discovered anomalies in equipment maintenance log or outdated equipment that fails to meet organizational standard sanitization levels.

CHECK: Confirm Remote Purging/Wiping Capabilities and Associated Logs
WHY: Evidence of remote sanitization capabilities confirms the organization can protect data on media in cases where devices are lost or unanticipatedly exposed.
HOW: Validate configuration files and logs for systems with remote purging/wiping functionality. Test these systems (in a controlled lab environment if possible) to trigger a remote purge event and verify that strong authentication is in place to mitigate unauthorized access.
FAIL: Lack of automated logging for remote purge commands, weak authentication configurations, or absence of test results showcasing functionality of remote purging.

CHECK: Review Documentation and Evidence for Media Sanitization Approvals and Tracking
WHY: Formal tracking documentation that includes review, approval, and verification actions (especially for classified and controlled unclassified information) shows robust process adherence.
HOW: Examine records that list personnel who performed and verified the sanitization. Look for details such as types of media sanitized, sanitization techniques used, and disposal actions taken. Verify these records against physical evidence (e.g., disposal manifests for destroyed media).
FAIL: Sparse or incomplete records that do not provide full traceability, missing signatures or dual authorization evidence, or mismatches between recorded actions and physical media tracking information.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Verification Shortcut: Prioritize automated logs and electronic certifications over manual records whenever possible since these are harder to fake and provide time-stamped, tamper-evident evidence.
• Critical Indicator: Dual authorization logs are essential. If the logs reveal that only one individual is consistently approving sanitization actions, then the control is likely not being executed as required.
• Detail Focus: Ensure that sanitization methods are appropriate to the media’s sensitivity levels; for instance, a simple file deletion or quick format isn’t sufficient for high-classification data. Check that media destruction certificates, equipment testing logs, and remote purging audit trails are recent, complete, and cover all classified media.
• Experience suggests that periodic validation exercises, including data recovery tests on supposedly sanitized media, provide practical insight into the sanitization’s real effectiveness.

Overall, these steps emphasize observable, automated evidence and results from actual operational activity over mere policy documentation, ensuring that the control is effectively and authentically implemented in practice.
content:
Statement:
a. Sanitize [Assignment: organization-defined system media] prior to disposal, release out of organizational control, or release for reuse (using [Assignment: organization-defined sanitization techniques and procedures]); and
b. Employ sanitization mechanisms with the strength and integrity commensurate with the security category or classification of the information.

Guidance:
Media sanitization applies to all digital and non-digital system media subject to disposal or reuse, whether or not the media is considered removable. Examples include digital media in scanners, copiers, printers, notebook computers, workstations, network components, mobile devices, and non-digital media (e.g., paper and microfilm). The sanitization process removes information from system media such that the information cannot be retrieved or reconstructed. Sanitization techniques—including clearing, purging, cryptographic erase, de-identification of personally identifiable information, and destruction—prevent the disclosure of information to unauthorized individuals when such media is reused or released for disposal. Organizations determine the appropriate sanitization methods, recognizing that destruction is sometimes necessary when other methods cannot be applied to media requiring sanitization.

Enhancements:
MEDIA SANITIZATION | REVIEW, APPROVE, TRACK, DOCUMENT, AND VERIFY
Discussion: Organizations review and approve media to be sanitized to ensure compliance with records retention policies. Tracking and documenting actions include listing personnel who reviewed and approved sanitization and disposal actions, types of media sanitized, files stored on the media, sanitization methods used, date and time of the sanitization actions, personnel who performed the sanitization, verification actions taken and personnel who performed the verification, and the disposal actions taken. Organizations verify that the sanitization of the media was effective prior to disposal. Related Controls: None.
MEDIA SANITIZATION | EQUIPMENT TESTING
Test sanitization equipment and procedures [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] to ensure that the intended sanitization is being achieved.
Discussion: Testing of sanitization equipment and procedures may be conducted by qualified and authorized external entities, including federal agencies or external service providers. Related Controls: None.
MEDIA SANITIZATION | NONDESTRUCTIVE TECHNIQUES
Apply non destructive sanitization techniques to portable storage devices prior to connecting such devices to the system under the following circumstances: [Assignment: organization-defined circumstances requiring sanitization of portable storage devices].
Discussion: Portable storage devices include external or removable hard disk drives (e.g., solid state, magnetic), optical discs, magnetic or optical tapes, flash memory devices, flash memory cards, and other external or removable disks. Portable storage devices can be obtained from untrustworthy sources and contain malicious code that can be inserted into or transferred to organizational systems through USB ports or other entry points. While scanning storage devices is recommended, sanitization provides additional assurance that such devices are free of malicious code. Organizations consider nondestructive sanitization of portable storage devices when the devices are purchased from manufacturers or vendors prior to initial use or when organizations cannot maintain a positive chain of custody for the devices. Related Controls: None.
MEDIA SANITIZATION | CONTROLLED UNCLASSIFIED INFORMATION
[Withdrawn: Incorporated into MP-6.]
MEDIA SANITIZATION | CLASSIFIED INFORMATION
Downgrade system media containing classified information prior to release to individuals without required access authorizations.
Discussion: Downgrading of classified information uses approved sanitization tools, techniques, and procedures to transfer information confirmed to be unclassified from classified systems to unclassified media. Related Controls: None.
MEDIA SANITIZATION | MEDIA DESTRUCTION
[Withdrawn: Incorporated into MP-6.]
MEDIA SANITIZATION | DUAL AUTHORIZATION
Enforce dual authorization for the sanitization of [Assignment: organization-defined system media].
Discussion: Organizations employ dual authorization to help ensure that system media sanitization cannot occur unless two technically qualified individuals conduct the designated task. Individuals who sanitize system media possess sufficient skills and expertise to determine if the proposed sanitization reflects applicable federal and organizational standards, policies, and procedures. Dual authorization also helps to ensure that sanitization occurs as intended, protecting against errors and false claims of having performed the sanitization actions. Dual authorization may also be known as two-person control. To reduce the risk of collusion, organizations consider rotating dual authorization duties to other individuals. Related Controls: AC-3, MP-2.
MEDIA SANITIZATION | REMOTE PURGING OR WIPING OF INFORMATION
Provide the capability to purge or wipe information from [Assignment: organization-defined systems or system components] [Selection: remotely; under the following conditions: [Assignment: organization-defined conditions]].
Discussion: Remote purging or wiping of information protects information on organizational members’ system components if systems or components are unanticipatedly lost. Individuals. Remote purge or wipe commands require strong authentication to help mitigate the risk of unauthorized individuals purging or wiping the system, component, or device. The purge or wipe function can be implemented in a variety of ways, including by overwriting data or information multiple times or by destroying the key necessary to decrypt encrypted data. Related Controls: None.

title: MEDIA TRANSPORT
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for the Media Transport (MP-5) control. This methodology focuses on verifying that system media being transported outside of controlled areas is properly protected, tracked, documented, and handled only by authorized personnel.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Look for physical security measures such as locked transport containers, tamper-evident seals on media devices, and visibly labeled chain-of-custody records or tags attached to media.
• Technical Artifacts: Review logs that capture media transport events, digital signatures for custodian hand-offs, and cryptographic records (e.g., encryption logs if applicable) embedded within file metadata or transport system interfaces.
• Operational Patterns: Observe processes where system media is only released via a documented, approved chain-of-custody, with clearly recorded transfer events between authorized individuals at every leg of the journey.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Faked Compliance: Some organizations may simply generate static documentation that mimics chain-of-custody records without actual evidence of physical or secure transport processes. For instance, templates filled out after the fact rather than real-time logging.
• Surface-level Implementations: Using generic physical locks or containers without ensuring that they are designed for sensitive media, or relying solely on paper logs that are not cross-verified against digital transport records.
• Frequent Technical Gaps: Lack of integration between transport logs and physical hand-off receipts, missing cryptographic evidence for digital media protection, and ineffective tracking when media passes through third-party carriers.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
a. CHECK: Verify chain-of-custody documentation at hand-off points.
   WHY: Real-time, signed documentation ensures that accountability is maintained and that only authorized personnel handle media during transport.
   HOW: Request a sample set of chain-of-custody records, compare physical media tags with documented records, and verify signatures or digital logs that indicate authorized hand-offs.
   FAIL: Absence of real-time, signed records or mismatches between media identifiers and log entries indicates non-compliance.

b. CHECK: Inspect physical security controls applied to transport containers.
   WHY: Using purpose-designed locked or tamper-evident containers offers direct physical protection for system media during transit.
   HOW: Conduct on-site inspections (or request recent photos/videos) of containers used during transport, verify that containers are locked and sealed, and confirm that seals show no sign of tampering.
   FAIL: Use of generic containers without tamper-evident features or evidence of broken seals/locks in records means controls may be insufficient.

c. CHECK: Review digital evidence/logs of transport events.
   WHY: Automated logs that track media movement provide non-repudiable evidence of accountability and demonstrate operational use of the control.
   HOW: Evaluate system logs, timestamps, and audit trails that record the media’s departure, custody transfers, and arrival. Cross-reference these entries with scheduled transport events and authorized courier lists.
   FAIL: Absence or gaps in logs, inconsistent timestamps, or records that do not map to actual physical transport events are indicators of potential non-compliance.

d. CHECK: Confirm that cryptographic protections (if used) are properly implemented during transit.
   WHY: If media is encrypted or digitally signed during transport, appropriate cryptographic protections help maintain confidentiality and integrity.
   HOW: Review cryptographic key management logs, configuration of encryption tools, and any digital signatures appended to the media before transit. Verify that cryptographic evidence aligns with transport logs.
   FAIL: Missing cryptographic records or evidence that encryption is applied only selectively may indicate a weak implementation.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut Verification: Request recent case studies or incident reports that reference transport issues. Well-maintained records should reveal regular times when the controls were exercised, along with any corrective actions.
• Critical Indicators: Look for discrepancies between physical evidence (e.g., tamper-evident seals) and digital records. This misalignment is a strong sign of post-facto documentation rather than effective control.
• Real World Details: Ensure custody details (names, times, signatures) are complete and verifiable. A common pitfall is generic authorization (e.g., lists of personnel without individual identifiers), which provides a false sense of security.
• Automated Evidence Priority: Prioritize automated logs and digital audit trails over static documentation. They are less prone to backdating or tampering and are a more reliable indicator that proper controls are actively managed.

By focusing on these practical checks – valid chain-of-custody, secure physical transport containers, complete digital logs, and, where applicable, cryptographic protection – you maximize the likelihood that the implemented controls are both effective and verifiable.
content:
Statement:
a. Protect and control [Assignment: organization-defined types of system media] during transport outside of controlled areas using [Assignment: organization-defined controls];
b. Maintain accountability for system media during transport outside of controlled areas; 
c. Document activities associated with the transport of system media; and
d. Restrict the activities associated with the transport of system media to authorized personnel.

Guidance:
System media includes digital and non-digital media. Digital media includes flash drives, diskettes, magnetic tapes, external or removable hard disk drives (e.g., solid state and magnetic), compact discs, and digital versatile discs. Non-digital media includes microfilm and paper. Controlled areas are spaces for which organizations provide physical or procedural controls to meet requirements established for protecting information and systems. Controls to protect media during transport include cryptography and locked containers. Cryptographic mechanisms can provide confidentiality and integrity protections depending on the mechanisms implemented. Activities associated with media transport include releasing media for transport, ensuring that media enters the appropriate transport processes, and the actual transport. Authorized transport and courier personnel may include individuals external to the organization. Maintaining accountability of media during transport includes restricting transport activities to authorized personnel and tracking and/or obtaining records of transport activities as the media moves through the transportation system to prevent and detect loss, destruction, or tampering. Organizations establish documentation requirements for activities associated with the transport of system media in accordance with organizational assessments of risk. Organizations maintain the flexibility to define record-keeping methods for the different types of media transport as part of a system of transport-related records.

Enhancements:
MEDIA TRANSPORT | PROTECTION OUTSIDE OF CONTROLLED AREAS
[Withdrawn: Incorporated into MP-5.]
MEDIA TRANSPORT | DOCUMENTATION OF ACTIVITIES
[Withdrawn: Incorporated into MP-5.]
MEDIA TRANSPORT | CUSTODIANS Employ an identified custodian during transport of system media outside of controlled areas.
Discussion: Identified custodians provide organizations with specific points of contact during transport to ensure that custodians facilitate individual accountability. Custodial responsibilities can be transferred from one individual to another if an unambiguous custodian is identified. Related Controls: None.
MEDIA TRANSPORT | CRYPTOGRAPHIC PROTECTION
[Withdrawn: Incorporated into SC-28(1).]

title: MEDIA STORAGE
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Physical evidence such as locked storage cabinets, secure media libraries, and controlled access areas (e.g., security doors with electronic access controls) that house digital and non-digital media.
• Technical artifacts including inventory logs, checkout/return records, and automated access control system audit logs (from card readers, biometric systems, etc.) showing access attempts and granted entries.
• Operational patterns that show routine inventory audits, regular reconciliation of media logs, and maintenance records for physical access control equipment.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations might list policies or procedures without enforcing proper physical security. For example, having an inventory without actual routine checks.
• Falsified documentation in absence of real-time logs from automated access systems. An organization might simulate accountabilities with phantom entries within inventory logs.
• Relying on outdated media storage practices where media is simply stored in an unlocked area, or using unsecured basic cabinets without any access logging.

VALIDATION STEPS:

CHECK 1: Verify physical security controls for media storage
WHY: Visible, robust physical controls (locked cabinets, controlled access rooms) provide direct evidence that media is physically protected.
HOW: Conduct a site tour focusing on areas where digital and non-digital media are stored. Check for mechanical locks, restricted entry signs, and correlate with access control records (e.g., electronic access system logs).
FAIL: If media storage areas are unlocked, lack visible physical barriers, or have no evidence of access restriction.

CHECK 2: Validate media inventory and tracking processes
WHY: An effective inventory system ensures that all media are accounted for and their location is tracked, reducing risk of unauthorized access or loss.
HOW: Review sample inventory logs, checkout/return records, and reconciliation reports. Interview staff to confirm routine inventory checks and verification of media possession.
FAIL: Inconsistent or missing logs, discrepancies between physical media counts and recorded inventory, or absence of a formal media tracking process.

CHECK 3: Assess automated access control mechanisms and audit logging
WHY: Automated systems (e.g. card readers, biometric systems) that log access attempts provide verifiable and non-repudiable evidence of controlled entry to media storage areas.
HOW: Review audit logs from the access control systems around media storage areas. Confirm that log entries correlate with physical entry events and check whether alerts or exceptions are investigated.
FAIL: If access control systems are missing, logs are not maintained or reviewed, or there’s evidence of unlogged manual overrides.

CHECK 4: Confirm media sanitization/destruction procedures
WHY: Ensuring that media is properly sanitized or destroyed when no longer required proves that risk of residual data exposure is reduced.
HOW: Examine records of media sanitization, approved destruction techniques, and equipment maintenance logs. Interview personnel responsible for media destruction to verify adherence to approved procedures.
FAIL: If there are no records of media sanitization, or if destruction equipment is outdated or not maintained, indicating potential non-compliance with secure media disposal.

EXPERT TIPS:
• A quick physical walkthrough of media storage locations reveals a lot. Unsecured or poorly maintained areas are red flags.
• Cross-verify digital audit logs against manual records to ensure consistency; discrepancies often indicate superficial implementations.
• Check for timely updates of media inventory records; outdated logs signal lapses in operational oversight.
• Pay attention to the integration of automated access control systems. Lack of automated logging can indicate reliance on easily forged paper logs, undermining authenticity.
content:
Statement:
a. Physically control and securely store [Assignment: organization-defined types of digital and/or non-digital media] within [Assignment: organization-defined controlled areas]; and
b. Protect system media types defined in MP-4a until the media are destroyed or sanitized using approved equipment, techniques, and procedures.

Guidance:
System media includes digital and non-digital media. Digital media includes flash drives, diskettes, magnetic tapes, external or removable hard disk drives (e.g., solid state, magnetic), compact discs, and digital versatile discs. Non-digital media includes paper and microfilm. Physically controlling stored media includes conducting inventories, ensuring procedures are in place to allow individuals to check out and return media to the library, and maintaining accountability for stored media. Secure storage includes a locked drawer, desk, or cabinet or a controlled media library. The type of media storage is commensurate with the security category or classification of the information on the media. Controlled areas are spaces that provide physical and procedural controls to meet the requirements established for protecting information and systems. Fewer controls may be needed for media that contains information determined by organizations to be in the public domain, publicly releasable, or have limited adverse impacts on organizations, operations, or individuals if accessed by other than authorized personnel.
