In these situations, physical access controls provide adequate protection.

Enhancements:
MEDIA STORAGE | CRYPTOGRAPHIC PROTECTION
[Withdrawn: Incorporated into SC-28(1).]
MEDIA STORAGE | AUTOMATED RESTRICTED ACCESS  Restrict access to media storage areas and log access attempts and access granted using [Assignment: organization-defined automated mechanisms].
Discussion: Automated mechanisms include keypads, biometric readers, or card readers on the external entries to media storage areas. Related Controls: AC-3, AU-2, AU-6, AU-9, AU-12, PE-3.

title: MEDIA MARKING
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Visibility of labels on physical media (e.g., external hard drives, tapes, paper documents) that include security markings such as distribution restrictions and handling caveats. 
• Digital media management systems that log details about media markings reflected in metadata, asset inventories, or file properties. 
• Operational patterns where media moving out of a controlled area are either remarked or logged as departing, with clear marking standards applied consistently.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations may create documentation stating that all media should be marked but fail to physically label the items in use. 
• Falsification of asset records or metadata updates without corresponding physical labeling. 
• Overreliance on automated systems without periodic manual verifications; for example, having controls defined only in system policies but not enforced during media handling.

VALIDATION STEPS:
1. CHECK: Review a sample of both digital and non-digital media stored within and outside controlled areas for visible markings.
   WHY: Physical labels or digital metadata accurately reflect the media's security classification and distribution limitations.
   HOW: Inspect actual media in various controlled and non-controlled zones; document screenshots or physical photos showing the markings; correlate with organizational standards.
   FAIL: Absence of markings, inconsistent labeling, or mismatches between the media’s location (e.g., outside controlled areas) and its label.

2. CHECK: Verify the asset management records and system logs for media marking entries.
   WHY: Automated records and logs provide traceable evidence that media have been processed and marked in accordance with established policies.
   HOW: Query digital asset inventories for fields reflecting marking attributes; cross-check a sample of assets with their physical labeling; examine logs for any deviations or exceptions.
   FAIL: Incomplete or inconsistent records where media entries do not show expected marking information or are not updated when media location changes.

3. CHECK: Conduct interviews with personnel responsible for handling sensitive media.
   WHY: First-hand operational insights ensure that the marking procedures are practiced consistently rather than merely documented on paper.
   HOW: Interview staff in IT asset management, records management, and physical security; ask for details on handling caveats and marking exceptions, especially regarding media that remain within controlled areas.
   FAIL: Personnel are unable to specify processes or demonstrate practical knowledge of media marking procedures or exemptions.

4. CHECK: Review periodic audit reports and spot-check compliance evaluations related to media handling.
   WHY: Regular audits provide an independent verification that marking policies are actively enforced and adhered to over time.
   HOW: Examine recent internal or external audit documents specifically referencing media marking; validate that findings, if any, have been remediated and that corrective actions have been implemented.
   FAIL: Audit reports that highlight repeated non-compliance or deviations from the defined media marking standards, indicating superficial implementation.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Shortcut: When performing visual inspections, focus on high-risk items such as removable digital media that could be easily transported outside controlled areas. 
• Critical Indicator: Consistent discrepancies between asset management records and actual media markings are a red flag for procedural bypasses. 
• Detail Matters: Verify not only that media are marked, but also that the markings reflect specific distribution limitations and handling caveats that match legal and regulatory expectations; look out for generic or vague labels that do not provide sufficient guidance.
• Automated Evidence: Use digital inventory tools that can tie media identifiers to their markings and locations, minimizing the chance of purely document-based compliance without operational backing.
content:
Statement:
a. Mark system media indicating the distribution limitations, handling caveats, and applicable security markings (if any) of the information; and
b. Exempt [Assignment: organization-defined types of system media] from marking if the media remain within [Assignment: organization-defined controlled areas].

Guidance:
Security marking refers to the application or use of human-readable security attributes. Digital media includes diskettes, magnetic tapes, external or removable hard disk drives (e.g., solid state, magnetic), flash drives, compact discs, and digital versatile discs. Non-digital media includes paper and microfilm. Controlled unclassified information is defined by the National Archives and Records Administration along with the appropriate safeguarding and dissemination requirements for such information and is codified in [32 CFR 2002]. Security markings are generally not required for media that contains information determined by organizations to be in the public domain or to be publicly releasable. Some organizations may require markings for public information indicating that the information is publicly releasable. System media marking reflects applicable laws, executive orders, directives, policies, regulations, standards, and guidelines.

title: MEDIA ACCESS
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence: Restricted access to media storage areas (both locked cabinets for physical media and access-controlled rooms for digital media handling). Signage indicating restricted areas and lists of authorized personnel displayed near sensitive storage areas.
• Technical artifacts: Access control lists (ACLs) in system configuration files, digital access logs showing user access to media-related services, and configuration of endpoint management systems to block unauthorized media device use. Inventory logs of physical media (e.g., flash drives, external hard disk drives, paper records) with designated custodians.
• Operational patterns: Periodic audit logs showing enforcement of restrictions, automated alerts for any unauthorized media access attempts, and clear separation between roles (e.g., data custodians vs. general staff) enforced through system rights.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Faking physical controls: Organizations might rely solely on policy statements or locked storage without evidence of enforced monitoring or regular audits.
• Surface-level technical controls: Simply installing endpoint management software without ensuring it’s active or updated. Misconfigured ACLs that don’t cover all media interfaces are examples of technically present but ineffective implementations.
• Missed integration: Failing to integrate physical media custody logs with digital access control monitoring, thereby creating gaps where manual transfers or off-system media handling aren’t tracked.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Verify physical security controls for media storage areas
   WHY: Locked cabinets, restricted room access, and visible signage indicate organizational enforcement of physical media restrictions.
   HOW: Inspect storage areas, interview custodians, and review visitor logs. Compare listed authorized personnel against individuals who have actual physical access.
   FAIL: Unsecured storage, absent or outdated signage, or lack of access logs is a red flag.

2. CHECK: Review digital access control configurations and logs
   WHY: System-level configurations (like ACLs) and access logs demonstrate that only authorized personnel can connect or use digital media resources.
   HOW: Audit system configuration files for media access points, check endpoint management settings, and analyze logs to verify that only authorized users engaged with media devices during the review period.
   FAIL: Presence of unauthorized access attempts, inactive logging, or misconfigurations that allow generic user profiles access to media ports.

3. CHECK: Confirm periodic review and audits of media access controls
   WHY: Regular audits and reviews ensure controls are active and responsive to new threats or personnel changes.
   HOW: Request and review audit reports, interview security teams about their review process, and verify that corrective actions were taken after previous audit findings regarding media access.
   FAIL: Absence of audit records or evidence that audits are conducted reveals potential non-compliance or outdated controls.

4. CHECK: Assess media inventory and custodianship records
   WHY: An up-to-date inventory with designated custodians supports accountability and traceability for both digital and physical media.
   HOW: Compare media inventory records with HR access lists and observe physical inventory if available. Check that updates occur regularly when new media is introduced or personnel changes occur.
   FAIL: Outdated, incomplete, or unsynchronized inventory records suggest gaps in media control processes.

EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: A quick walkthrough of physical storage areas combined with a spot check of digital logs can immediately reveal if controls are effectively enforced.
• Critical Indicator: Loose or shared access credentials, especially in digital logs, are a strong sign that proper restrictions are not upheld.
• Detail that Matters: Ensure that both digital and non-digital media are covered equally in reviews – often physical controls receive more attention while digital controls can be misconfigured or overlooked.
• Reminder: Cross-reference user lists against actual access logs. Discrepancies between authorized personnel and log entries are one of the most significant early warning indicators of poor implementation.
content:
Statement:
Restrict access to [Assignment: organization-defined types of digital and/or non-digital media] to [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles].

Guidance:
System media includes digital and non-digital media. Digital media includes flash drives, diskettes, magnetic tapes, external or removable hard disk drives (e.g., solid state, magnetic), compact discs, and digital versatile discs. Non-digital media includes paper and microfilm. Denying access to patient medical records in a community hospital unless the individuals seeking access to such records are authorized healthcare providers is an example of restricting access to non-digital media. Limiting access to the design specifications stored on compact discs in the media library to individuals on the system development team is an example of restricting access to digital media.

Enhancements:
MEDIA ACCESS | AUTOMATED RESTRICTED ACCESS
[Withdrawn: Incorporated into MP-4(2).]
MEDIA ACCESS | CRYPTOGRAPHIC PROTECTION
[Withdrawn: Incorporated into SC-28(1).]

title: POLICY AND PROCEDURES
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• A centrally stored, dated media protection policy document that clearly outlines purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, and references to compliance requirements, including applicable laws and regulations.
• Attached or linked procedures that detail how the media protection policy is implemented, including step-by-step operational instructions.
• Evidence of dissemination (e.g., training records, distribution lists, intranet posting logs) showing that organization-defined personnel or roles have received and acknowledged the policy and procedures.
• An organizational record or official designation (e.g., appointment memo, job description amendment) that assigns responsibility to a designated official for managing policy documentation and dissemination.
• Records of periodic review and updates (meeting minutes, version histories, audit findings) that document when reviews were conducted, trigger events, and subsequent policy or procedure modifications.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations might produce a generic or boilerplate document that only nominally mentions media protection without detailing enforcement or operational elements.
• Compliance may be faked by having outdated or unused documents in a repository, rather than actively disseminating or training staff on the content.
• Some organizations might rely solely on policy existence rather than verifying that procedures are in place and that roles listed actually execute the needed updates and reviews.
• Records of policy reviews could be superficial if no substantive changes are made to meet emerging threats or legislative updates.

VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Identify and review the media protection policy document to verify it includes sections covering purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, coordination, and compliance requirements.
WHY: A comprehensive policy is foundational evidence demonstrating that media protection is systematically governed and aligned with external mandates.
HOW: Obtain the current approved policy document from a central repository, check for signatures and version history, and confirm that key sections are present and up to date.
FAIL: The document is generic, incomplete, outdated, or missing explicit references to applicable laws and internal roles, indicating non-compliance.

CHECK: Confirm that detailed procedures exist which correlate directly to the policy's media protection controls and are actionable by assigned staff.
WHY: Procedures are essential to move the policy from paper to practice; their presence and usability indicate operational effectiveness.
HOW: Review procedure documents, verify they include step-by-step processes, responsibilities, and are referenced in the media protection policy. Interview relevant personnel to confirm their understanding and usage.
FAIL: Procedures are absent, lack detail, or exist only as high-level summaries that do not guide daily operations, suggesting ineffective implementation.

CHECK: Validate that an organization-defined official is designated responsible for media protection policy management.
WHY: Accountable leadership supports the proper maintenance, dissemination, and periodic review of the media protection policy and procedures.
HOW: Check organizational charts, appointment memos, or HR records confirming the designation, and if possible, interview the official or their supervisor regarding responsibilities.
FAIL: Absence of a clearly identified official, or documentation that denotes temporary or unofficial designation, can lead to management gaps.

CHECK: Examine records of policy and procedure review cycles to ensure updates occur at defined intervals or following specific triggering events like audit findings or legal changes.
WHY: Regular reviews indicate that the organization is actively managing and updating its media protection measures in response to evolving risks and regulations.
HOW: Look at review logs, meeting minutes, or system update documentation. Verify that review dates align with the defined frequency and captured events are appropriately noted.
FAIL: Lack of evidence of scheduled reviews or updates, or records that show reviews did not lead to adjustments when needed, signals a stagnant or ineffective control environment.

CHECK: Evaluate dissemination efforts and awareness among organization-defined personnel or roles to ensure the policy and procedures are communicated effectively.
WHY: Proper dissemination ensures that the individuals responsible for media protection are informed and capable of executing the requisite controls.
HOW: Inspect training logs, distribution records from internal communications systems, or conduct spot interviews with personnel to confirm awareness and understanding of the policy.
FAIL: Inadequate training records or personnel reporting a lack of awareness indicates failure in disseminating the necessary information.

EXPERT TIPS
• Always request artifact copies directly from central repositories rather than departmental attachments to avoid reliance on potentially outdated printed manuals.
• Use automated systems log reviews and version control histories as a starting point; cross-check with staff interviews to confirm practical awareness and application.
• Pay careful attention to the language in the policy—vague or generic terms may indicate a document crafted just to satisfy compliance checkboxes without ensuring operational effectiveness.
• Look for signs of reactive rather than proactive updates; effective controls are updated preemptively rather than only in response to incidents or audit findings.
• Ensure that both IT and business process perspectives are integrated, as media protection often involves both technological safeguards and procedural adherence by non-technical staff.
content:
Statement:
a. Develop, document, and disseminate to [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles]:
1. [Selection (one or more): Organization-level; Mission/business process-level; System-level] media protection policy that:
   (a) Addresses purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, coordination among organizational entities, and compliance; and
   (b) Is consistent with applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines; and
2. Procedures to facilitate the implementation of the media protection policy and the associated media protection controls;
b. Designate an [Assignment: organization-defined official] to manage the development, documentation, and dissemination of the media protection policy and procedures; and
c. Review and update the current media protection:
1. Policy [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] and following [Assignment: organization-defined events]; and
2. Procedures [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] and following [Assignment: organization-defined events].

Guidance:
Media protection policy and procedures address the controls in the MP family that are implemented within systems and organizations. The risk management strategy is an important factor in establishing such policies and procedures. Policies and procedures contribute to security and privacy assurance. Therefore, it is important that security and privacy programs collaborate on the development of media protection policy and procedures. Security and privacy program policies and procedures at the organization level are preferable, in general, and may obviate the need for mission- or system-specific policies and procedures. The policy can be included as part of the general security and privacy policy or be represented by multiple policies that reflect the complex nature of organizations. Procedures can be established for security and privacy programs, for mission or business processes, and for systems, if needed. Procedures describe how these policies or controls are implemented and can be directed at the individual or role that is the object of the procedure. Procedures can be documented in system security and privacy plans or in one or more separate documents. Events that may precipitate an update to media protection policy and procedures include assessment or audit findings, security incidents or breaches, or changes in applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines. Simply restating controls does not constitute an organizational policy or procedure.

title: FIELD MAINTENANCE
methodology:
Below is the validation methodology for control MA-7 FIELD MAINTENANCE:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: Maintenance work orders, service logs, and physical verification reports that explicitly list the facility location. Look for annotations or tags marking maintenance activities as taking place at trusted facilities rather than at local/field sites.
   • Technical Artifacts: Automated logs from maintenance scheduling systems, facility access records, and configuration management databases (CMDB) that identify maintenance events and link them to pre-approved trusted maintenance facilities.
   • Operational Patterns: Regular cross-checks in audit logs that confirm no maintenance actions are logged as having occurred at unauthorized locations. Consistent correlation between maintenance events and corresponding facility IDs that match an approved list.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Fake Compliance: Organizations might simply update maintenance policies and work orders with the approved facility list, yet continue to perform actual repairs locally without proper logging.
   • Surface-level Implementations: Reliance solely on documentation or paper logs without corresponding automated audit trails, making it easy to bypass enforcement checks.
   • Technical Gaps: Missing real-time monitoring of maintenance events; absence of geolocation tags or verification of facility access controls can allow local interventions to be overlooked.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   • CHECK: Verify that maintenance work orders and service tickets include a facility identifier that matches the organization’s approved list of trusted maintenance facilities.
     WHY: This confirms that each activity is being conducted at an authorized location.
     HOW: Audit work order databases or service ticket logs and cross-reference facility IDs with the trusted facility registry.
     FAIL: Records showing maintenance at local or ambiguous facilities not matching the approved list.
   
   • CHECK: Examine system logs and change control records for maintenance events, confirming that no field (on-site/local) maintenance actions are present.
     WHY: Automated logging reduces the potential for manual misreporting and indicates operational consistency.
     HOW: Use log aggregation and filtering tools to isolate maintenance events and validate the location metadata against trusted facility criteria.
     FAIL: Discovery of maintenance logs with local site indicators or missing location metadata.
   
   • CHECK: Interview maintenance personnel and management to corroborate that the procedures enforce exclusive use of trusted facilities for critical system maintenance.
     WHY: Personnel confirmation provides an operational context beyond electronic logs and highlights real-world practices.
     HOW: Conduct structured interviews/questions regarding maintenance scheduling, facility usage, and any exceptions to the rule.
     FAIL: Staff reporting ambiguity, local maintenance practices, or inconsistent adherence to designated facilities.
   
   • CHECK: Review configuration management databases (CMDB) and asset inventories to ensure that system components marked for restricted field maintenance are clearly documented with the designated trusted facility requirement.
     WHY: Asset management documentation should align with maintenance restrictions, reinforcing that maintenance events are pre-planned and controlled by facility.
     HOW: Audit CMDB records for critical systems and confirm that their maintenance procedures reference trusted facility locations.
     FAIL: Systems lacking clear facility designation for maintenance or showing a history of unapproved local repairs.
   
   • CHECK: Implement an automated alerting mechanism (if available) that flags maintenance events occurring outside approved facilities.
     WHY: Real-time alerts ensure that deviations are immediately noticed and investigated.
     HOW: Verify that the alerting system is configured correctly, then test by simulating an event from an unauthorized local maintenance attempt.
     FAIL: Absence of automated alerts or alerts that fail to trigger upon simulating unauthorized events.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Verification Shortcuts: Focus on integrated maintenance service management systems that autonomously enforce facility tagging; these systems often provide dashboards summarizing compliance.
   • Critical Indicators: Discrepancies in facility identifiers or missing geospatial data often signal that maintenance actions might be conducted in non-approved locations.
   • Remember: Documentation alone is insufficient if electronic logs and operational records do not align; always corroborate with both technical evidence and stakeholder interviews.
   • Beware: Overly generic or static policies without dynamic enforcement systems are a red flag—ensure that controls are actively monitored in real time.

This methodology emphasizes operational effectiveness and the critical importance of verifying that every maintenance action on deployed systems strictly adheres to the approved trusted facility requirement, minimizing the risk associated with ad hoc or improperly documented field maintenance.
content:
Statement:
Restrict or prohibit field maintenance on [Assignment: organization-defined systems or system components] to [Assignment: organization-defined trusted maintenance facilities].

Guidance:
Field maintenance is the type of maintenance conducted on a system or system component after the system or component has been deployed to a specific site (i.e., operational environment). In certain instances, field maintenance (i.e., local maintenance at the site) may not be executed with the same degree of rigor or with the same quality control checks as depot maintenance. For critical systems designated as such by the organization, it may be necessary to restrict or prohibit field maintenance at the local site and require that such maintenance be conducted in trusted facilities with additional controls.

title: TIMELY MAINTENANCE
methodology:
Control ID: MA-6 – TIMELY MAINTENANCE Validation Methodology

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: Contracts and service level agreements (SLAs) with maintenance vendors that specify response times and spare parts availability. Inventory records showing available spare parts and documented schedules (preventive and/or predictive) for maintenance tasks. Screenshots or live views of computerized maintenance management systems (CMMS) with maintenance histories and automated alerts.
   • Technical Artifacts: Logs from the CMMS indicating maintenance events (both preventive and predictive), automated trigger records from condition monitoring systems, and performance dashboards. Configuration files or system settings that define maintenance intervals and thresholds.
   • Operational Patterns: Regularly scheduled maintenance activities reflected in logs, upgraded system components following predictive monitoring alerts, and evidence that remediation actions are executed within defined time periods post-failure.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Faking Compliance: Organizations sometimes present outdated contracts or policies without operational enforcement. A printed policy might exist but without corresponding, actual CMMS logs or regularly executed maintenance actions.
   • Superficial Implementation: Simply having a maintenance schedule doc or a checklist that is not actually followed; presenting static reports that have been manually altered rather than automatically generated logs.
   • Overlooked Technical Gaps: Missing integration between predictive maintenance sensors and the maintenance management system; sparse or inconsistent log data that fails to capture true incident response times.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Review Maintenance Contract and SLA Documentation
   WHY: Valid contracts and SLAs confirm that the organization has formal agreements to obtain spare parts/support within the defined time period.
   HOW: Verify that current contracts and vendor SLAs explicitly state the response times and spare parts availability; cross-reference contract effective dates with maintenance activity logs.
   FAIL: Outdated or missing contracts, or contracts that lack clear response time commitments indicate a control failure.

   CHECK: Inspect Spare Parts Inventory Records
   WHY: Active inventory and the tracking of spare parts ensure rapid replacement when components fail.
   HOW: Validate inventory management logs and physical inventory counts; ensure that parts critical to system operation are logged and are at validated stock levels.
   FAIL: Discrepancies between documented inventory and actual physical inventory, or lack of tracking records.

   CHECK: Analyze Automated Maintenance Logs and CMMS Data
   WHY: Automated logs provide reliable, tamper-evident evidence that maintenance actions (preventive/predictive) are executed as scheduled.
   HOW: Extract a sampling of maintenance events from the CMMS; check dates, maintenance types (preventive/predictive), and correlate with system downtime records; review automated notification and trigger logs.
   FAIL: Inconsistent, missing, or manipulated log entries; manual corrections that are not supported by automated event triggers.

   CHECK: Verify Preventive and Predictive Maintenance Scheduling
   WHY: Clearly defined, operational maintenance schedules ensure equipment is inspected and serviced before failures escalate.
   HOW: Review maintenance scheduling records, physical inspections, and condition monitoring reports; confirm that preventive tasks are documented with dates and performed as per schedule, and predictive monitoring reports are integrated with maintenance triggers.
   FAIL: Lack of clear scheduling data or irregular intervals in maintenance actions that contradict the documented intervals.

   (Optional) CHECK: Evaluate Integration Between Condition Monitoring and CMMS
   WHY: Integration ensures that real-time equipment condition data results in automated maintenance alerts, minimizing delays.
   HOW: Test an alert from condition monitoring by reviewing the actual trigger process in the CMMS; verify that data flows from sensors to maintenance logs automatically.
   FAIL: Absence of integration or manual intervention required to trigger maintenance logs indicates a weak predictive maintenance process.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: Focus on the CMMS – effective integration and robust log records are the best real-world indicators that timely maintenance is in place. A well-configured CMMS with automated entries is much harder to fake.
   • Critical Indicator: Look at maintenance response times post-incident. If actual repair or replacement actions consistently exceed defined timeframes, the maintenance process is likely not operational.
   • Detail Matter: Verify the alignment of contract dates, automated alerts, and maintenance logs. Discrepancies in dates or missing logs are a red flag.
   • Practical Note: If preventive and predictive schedules aren’t yielding a reduction in incidental failures or downtime, the maintenance plan may need re-evaluation.

This methodology emphasizes observable, automated evidence over self-authored documentation to ensure the control is actively implemented and effective.
content:
Statement:
Obtain maintenance support and/or spare parts for [Assignment: organization-defined system components] within [Assignment: organization-defined time period] of failure.

Guidance:
Organizations specify the system components that result in increased risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation when the functionality provided by those components is not operational. Organizational actions to obtain maintenance support include having appropriate contracts in place.

Enhancements:
TIMELY MAINTENANCE | PREVENTIVE MAINTENANCE
Perform preventive maintenance on [Assignment: organization-defined system components] at [Assignment: organization-defined time intervals].
Discussion: Preventive maintenance includes proactive care and the servicing of system components to maintain organizational equipment and facilities in satisfactory operating condition. Such maintenance provides for the systematic inspection, tests, measurements, adjustments, parts replacement, detection, and correction of incipient failures either before they occur or before they develop into major defects. The primary goal of preventive maintenance is to avoid or mitigate the consequences of equipment failures. Preventive maintenance is designed to preserve and restore equipment reliability by replacing worn components before they fail. Methods of determining what preventive (or other) failure management policies to apply include original equipment manufacturer recommendations; statistical failure records; expert opinion; maintenance that has already been conducted on similar equipment; requirements of codes, laws, or regulations within a jurisdiction; or measured values and performance indications. Related Controls: None.
TIMELY MAINTENANCE | PREDICTIVE MAINTENANCE
Perform predictive maintenance on [Assignment: organization-defined system components] at [Assignment: organization-defined time intervals].
Discussion: Predictive maintenance evaluates the condition of equipment by performing periodic or continuous (online) equipment condition monitoring. The goal of predictive maintenance is to perform maintenance at a scheduled time when the maintenance activity is most cost-effective and before the equipment loses performance within a threshold. The predictive component of predictive maintenance stems from the objective of predicting the future trend of the equipment's condition. The predictive maintenance approach employs principles of statistical process control to determine at what point in the future maintenance activities will be appropriate. Most predictive maintenance inspections are performed while equipment is in service, thus minimizing disruption of normal system operations. Predictive maintenance can result in substantial cost savings and higher system reliability. Related Controls: None.
TIMELY MAINTENANCE | AUTOMATED SUPPORT FOR PREDICTIVE MAINTENANCE
Transfer predictive maintenance data to a maintenance management system using [Assignment: organization-defined automated mechanisms].
Discussion: A computerized maintenance management system maintains a database of information about the maintenance operations of organizations and automates the processing of equipment condition data to trigger maintenance planning, execution, and reporting. Related Controls: None. References: None.

title: INFORMATION SPILLAGE RESPONSE
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• CHECK: Examine the Incident Response (IR) plan to confirm that specific personnel or roles are assigned explicit responsibility for information spillage incidents.
  WHY: This shows that the organization has designated accountable individuals to manage spills, ensuring timely response and remediation.
  HOW: Review the IR plan for named roles, responsibilities, and distribution lists; inspect meeting logs or review sign‐offs indicating management approval and periodic reviews of the plan.
  FAIL: The absence of designated, named roles/responsibilities, or outdated review dates in the IR documentation.

• CHECK: Verify that there are metrics and dashboards capturing incident response performance and trends.
  WHY: Operational metrics indicate that the organization is actively monitoring and measuring the incident response capability.
  HOW: Request samples of incident reports/dashboards showing quantitative metrics (mean time to respond, spill classification counts), and inspect automated logging tools that capture these metrics.
  FAIL: No evidence of trend data or an ad hoc, unstructured reporting approach that lacks automated collection.

• CHECK: Confirm secure distribution methods and controlled updates of the IR plan.
  WHY: Ensuring that the IR plan is properly disseminated protects against unauthorized disclosure and ensures that all relevant personnel are working off current, secure documents.
  HOW: Check secure document repositories, access control logs for IR plan distribution; verify through interviews that personnel receive updates via dedicated, secure channels (non-associated with the spill).
  FAIL: A lack of documented distribution mechanisms or evidence that the IR plan is emailed or stored using unsecured methods.

• CHECK: Validate that incident notifications for spillage employ communication methods not associated with the spill itself.
  WHY: This reduces the risk of further contamination by isolating the spill from the channels used to alert responders.
  HOW: Review incident logs to confirm the use of alternative communication mediums (e.g., secure messaging apps, dedicated incident lines) for alerts; conduct practical tests or simulate incident notifications if permitted.
  FAIL: Notifications found in the same medium or channel that experienced the spill, or missing evidence of alternative, secure communication methods.

• CHECK: Assess procedures for updating the IR plan in response to organizational changes or lessons learned from spill incidents.
  WHY: This ensures continuous improvement in the IR capability and responsiveness to real-world challenges.
  HOW: Inspect post-incident reviews, change logs, or after-action reports demonstrating that the IR plan was updated following incidents or tests; verify records showing redistribution of the updated plan.
  FAIL: No indication of regular updates, stagnant IR plan documents, or missing documentation of lessons learned and subsequent plan modifications.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations might post a generic or outdated IR plan with placeholder roles and review dates to fake compliance.
• Some may claim that metrics are tracked, but only low-fidelity, manual logs exist rather than automated, verifiable dashboards or reports.
• Procedures for secure distribution and alternative communication methods might be documented but not actually enforced in operational practice; evidence may be simulated rather than real.
• Incident response training and plan updates might be scheduled on paper but not reflected in actual changes or tested in practical drills.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. Check the latest IR plan document for clearly identified responsible personnel, secure distribution lists, and scheduled reviews approved by management.
2. Retrieve and analyze incident response metrics from automated systems to verify that performance data is collected and trends are appropriately tracked.
3. Confirm through logs and interviews that IR plan updates are securely communicated using alternative channels (distinct from the medium experiencing spillage).
4. Review incident post-mortem reports to ensure that IR plan modifications are responsive to detected issues and that tests or exercises validate the efficacy of spillage response procedures.
5. Observe a drill or simulation, if available, to confirm that the communication paths used do not risk further contamination and that isolated systems are properly managed during a spill.

EXPERT TIPS
• Focus on evidence that operational systems (automated logs, dashboards) effectively measure incident response performance rather than relying solely on policy documents.
• Look for irreconcilable gaps such as undocumented ad hoc changes; these often indicate poor operational diligence even if the policy text appears comprehensive.
• Verify that alternate communication methods are not simply another system channel but have been designed to be physically and logically separate from potential contamination pathways.
• Prioritize interviews and real-time system validations to confirm that the IR plan isn’t just ‘signed off’ on a document shelf but actively maintained and practiced.
• Always cross-check document timestamps and revision logs with management meeting minutes to ensure that periodic reviews are genuine and not backdated fixes.
content:
Statement:
Respond to information spills by:  
  a. Assigning [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles] with responsibility for responding to information spills;  
  b. Provide metrics for measuring the incident response capability within the organization;  
  c. Defines the resources and management support needed to effectively maintain and mature an incident response capability;  
  d. Addresses the sharing of incident information;  
  e. Is reviewed and approved by [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles] [Assignment: organization-defined frequency]; and  
  f. Explicitly designates responsibility for incident response to [Assignment: organization-defined entities, personnel, or roles].  
  b. Distribute copies of the incident response plan to [Assignment: organization-defined incident response personnel (identified by name and/or by role) and organizational elements];  
  c. Update the incident response plan to address system and organizational changes or problems encountered during plan implementation, execution, or testing;  
  d. Communicate incident response plan changes to [Assignment: organization-defined incident response personnel (identified by name and/or by role) and organizational elements]; and  
  e. Protect the incident response plan from unauthorized disclosure and modification.  
  b. Identifying the specific information involved in the system contamination; c. Alerting [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles] of the information spill using a method of communication not associated with the spill; d. Isolating the contaminated system or system component; e. Eradicating the information from the contaminated system or component; f. Identifying other systems or system components that may have been subsequently contaminated; and g. Performing the following additional actions: [Assignment: organization-defined actions].

Guidance:
It is important that organizations develop and implement a coordinated approach to incident response. Organizational mission and business functions determine the structure of incident response capabilities. As part of the incident response capabilities, organizations consider the coordination and sharing of information with external organizations, including external service providers and other organizations involved in the supply chain. For incidents involving personally identifiable information (i.e., breaches), include a process to determine whether notice to oversight organizations or affected individuals is appropriate and provide that notice accordingly.

Information spillage refers to instances where information is placed on systems that are not authorized to process such information. Information spills occur when information that is thought to be a certain classification or impact level is transmitted to a system and subsequently is determined to be of a higher classification or impact level. At that point, corrective actions are required. The nature of the response is based on the classification or impact level of the spilled information, the security capabilities of the system, the specific nature of the contaminated storage media, and the access authorizations of individuals with authorized access to the contaminated system. The methods used to communicate information about the spill after the fact do not involve methods directly associated with the actual spill to minimize the risk of further spreading the contamination before such contamination is isolated and eradicated.

Enhancements:
INCIDENT RESPONSE PLAN | BREACHES: Include the following in the Incident Response Plan for breaches involving personally identifiable information: (a) A process to determine if notice to individuals or other organizations, including oversight organizations, is needed; (b) An assessment process to determine the extent of the harm, embarrassment, inconvenience, or unfairness to affected individuals and any mechanisms to mitigate such harms; and (c) Identification of applicable privacy requirements. Discussion: Organizations may be required by law, regulation, or policy to follow specific procedures relating to breaches, including notice to individuals, affected organizations, and oversight bodies; standards of harm; and mitigation or other specific requirements.
INFORMATION SPILLAGE RESPONSE | RESPONSIBLE PERSONNEL [Withdrawn: Incorporated into IR-9 -]
INFORMATION SPILLAGE RESPONSE | TRAINING [Assignment: organization-defined frequency]. Discussion: Organizations establish requirements for responding to information spillage incidents in incident response plans. Incident response training on a regular basis helps to ensure that organizational personnel understand their individual responsibilities and what specific actions to take when spillage incidents occur. Related Controls: AT-2, AT-3, CP-3, IR-2.
INFORMATION SPILLAGE RESPONSE | POST-SPILL OPERATIONS: Implement the following procedures to ensure that organizational personnel impacted by information spills can continue to carry out assigned tasks while contaminated systems are undergoing corrective actions: [Assignment: organization-defined procedures]. Discussion: Corrective actions for systems contaminated due to information spillages may be time-consuming. Personnel may not have access to the contaminated systems while corrective actions are being taken, which may potentially affect their ability to conduct organizational business. Related Controls: None.
INFORMATION SPILLAGE RESPONSE | EXPOSURE TO UNAUTHORIZED PERSONNEL: Employ the following controls for personnel exposed to information not within assigned access authorizations: [Assignment: organization-defined controls]. Discussion: Controls include ensuring that personnel who are exposed to spilled information are made aware of the laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines regarding the information and the restrictions imposed based on exposure to such information. Related Controls: None.

title: INCIDENT RESPONSE PLAN
methodology:
CONTROL: IR-8 INCIDENT RESPONSE PLAN

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• CHECK: Verify existence of a documented incident response plan, tailored to the organization’s mission, size, structure, and functions.
  WHY: A formally authored plan indicates the organization has taken steps to address incident response methodically.
  HOW: Review the incident response portfolio in the security documentation repository and match its content against organizational specifics.
  FAIL: No accessible, organization-specific incident response plan or generic template that does not align with business context.

• CHECK: Inspect the structure section of the plan that details the organization, roles, and reporting relationships within the incident response team.
  WHY: Clear role delineation and reporting lines are crucial for operational effectiveness during an incident.
  HOW: Validate that the plan includes an organizational chart or detailed narrative showing team roles and responsibilities, including integration into the overall governance structure.
  FAIL: Missing or vague descriptions of team structure or unclear roles, indicating potential breakdown during an incident.

• CHECK: Confirm that the plan defines reportable incidents and aligns them with the overall organizational risk management framework.
  WHY: Defining what constitutes a reportable incident and how it fits into the organization’s risk management is key to timely and effective responses.
  HOW: Review the section of the plan that defines incident categories and thresholds; corroborate with incident logs and risk assessments.
  FAIL: Reportable incident criteria are either too generic or not referenced to operational risk, leading to lack of clear triggers during events.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may simply produce a generic plan or use a vendor-provided template that does not reflect their unique operational environment.
• Documentation may exist solely on paper or as a file, with no evidence of implementation, testing, or team engagement
• Superficial definitions of incident types without linking to specific organizational threats leave gaps in effective response.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
• CHECK: Conduct a walk-through review with the incident response team, asking for role-specific details outlined in the plan.
  WHY: Interactive assessment provides real-world confirmation that the team understands and can operationalize the plan.
  HOW: Schedule an interview or tabletop exercise and directly query team members on their roles and responsibilities during an incident.
  FAIL: Team members are unable to provide detailed examples or reference the plan’s key elements during questioning.

• CHECK: Review audit logs or exercise reports to verify that the incident response plan has been tested or exercised in the last 12 months.
  WHY: Testing demonstrates operational readiness and continuous refinement of the plan.
  HOW: Request records from tabletop exercises, incident simulations, or real incident post-mortems that reference plan usage.
  FAIL: No exercises or after-action reviews can be found, suggesting that the plan is unvalidated in practice.

• CHECK: Validate integration of the incident response plan within the organizational risk management and business continuity frameworks.
  WHY: It is essential that the plan is not an isolated document but one that interacts with broader organizational processes.
  HOW: Review cross-references between the incident response plan and risk management/BCP documents; verify that incident reports are fed into risk assessment cycles.
  FAIL: Absence of cross-references or procedural overlaps indicates a siloed and ineffective incident response process.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• TIP: Don’t rely solely on the presence of documentation – look for evidence of training, actual incident handling, and continuous improvement in practice.
• TIP: Pay close attention to how the plan is updated; outdated incident response plans can lead to operational failures during new types of threats.
• TIP: Verify that the plan explicitly lists reportable incident scenarios with thresholds and responsibilities – vague definitions are a red flag.
• TIP: When interviewing staff, ask for specific examples of recent incidents and post-mortem analyses, which can validate the plan’s active usage.

This practical validation methodology emphasizes observable, operational evidence over mere existence of documentation and ensures that the incident response plan is both comprehensive and actively implemented.

title: INCIDENT RESPONSE ASSISTANCE
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Visible evidence includes operational help desk channels, a dedicated support website or portal, automated incident ticketing systems, and clearly documented procedures for accessing incident response assistance.
• Technical artifacts such as system logs showing incident ticket generation, escalation records, automated notifications (push/pull mechanisms), and integrations with external incident response services (e.g. forensics or consumer redress) serve as compliance evidence.
• Operational patterns include regular test calls to the assistance resource, incident drills showing active use of support services, and periodic reviews of incident assistance logs to trace request resolutions.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations may simply produce outdated documentation or screenshots of the help desk system without showing live integrations or actual ticket records.
• A surface-level implementation might involve a static FAQ webpage that does not provide dynamic or interactive support for incident reporting.
• Common gaps include failure to incorporate automated mechanisms for alerting users or to demonstrate real-time coordination with external providers.

VALIDATION STEPS:
CHECK: Verify that an active incident response assistance resource (e.g., help desk, ticketing system) is available and monitored.
WHY: This confirms the organization has established an operational resource for incident handling support.
HOW: Review real-time dashboard or incident ticket logs, observe a live demonstration of the support tool in action, and check alert distribution records.
FAIL: No current logs, inactive dashboard, or legacy systems that have not been updated for current operations.

CHECK: Confirm automated mechanisms (e.g., self-service portals, push notifications) are implemented and effectively disseminate incident response information.
WHY: Automated tools increase availability and thus effectiveness of the response assistance resource.
HOW: Test the automated system via a controlled simulation; check that notifications are dispatched, and review logs to confirm retrieval of assistance information.
FAIL: Manual-only processes or automated systems that are misconfigured and do not produce observable evidence in logs or system queries.

CHECK: Examine records of coordinated incident response exercises involving external providers.
WHY: Demonstrates integration of external expertise in incident handling, critical for handling sophisticated incidents.
HOW: Review meeting minutes, signed agreements, and incident drill reports that include external provider participation; verify that designated team members are pre-registered with external entities where applicable.
FAIL: Lack of documented interaction with external providers or absence of external contacts in incident drill records.

CHECK: Review how incident response assistance is provided to end users in an actual incident report or drill scenario.
WHY: Real incident traceability and user feedback validate that the support resource is actively used and effective.
HOW: Analyze post-incident reports, user feedback reports, and ticket response times. Perform interviews with end users who have recently used the assistance resource to confirm responsiveness.
FAIL: Inconsistencies between documented procedures and actual user experiences, prolonged response times, or absence of follow-up records.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Verification shortcuts include running live incident simulation tests and querying the automated system’s API for timestamped records.
• Critical indicators of poor implementation include evidence of stale data in logs, lack of integration between automated systems and human operators, and absence of external provider involvement during drills.
• Focus on observable, live system interactions rather than static documentation; details such as up-to-date agreements with external providers and evidence of automated push/pull notifications are strongest indicators of a properly implemented control.
• Interview frontline staff to verify that they understand the procedures for incident response assistance and have recently utilized these services, ensuring the control is effectively embedded in the operational environment.
content:
Statement:
Provide an incident response support resource, integral to the organizational incident response capability, that offers advice and assistance to users of the system for the handling and reporting of incidents.

Guidance:
Incident response support resources provided by organizations include help desks, assistance groups, automated ticketing systems to open and track incident response tickets, and access to forensics services or consumer redress services, when required.

Enhancements:
INCIDENT RESPONSE ASSISTANCE | AUTOMATION SUPPORT FOR AVAILABILITY OF INFORMATION AND SUPPORT: Increase the availability of incident response information and support using [Assignment: organization-defined automated mechanisms]. Discussion: Automated mechanisms can provide a push or pull capability for users to obtain incident response assistance. For example, individuals may have access to a website to query the assistance capability, or the assistance capability can proactively send incident response information to users (general distribution or targeted) as part of increasing understanding of current response capabilities and support. Related Controls: None.
INCIDENT RESPONSE ASSISTANCE | COORDINATION WITH EXTERNAL PROVIDERS: (a) Establish a direct, cooperative relationship between its incident response capability and external providers of system protection capability; and
(b) Identify organizational incident response team members to the external providers. Discussion: External providers of a system protection capability include the Computer Network Defense program within the U.S. Department of Defense. External providers help to protect, monitor, analyze, detect, and respond to unauthorized activity within organizational information systems and networks. It may be beneficial to have agreements in place with external providers to clarify the roles and responsibilities of each party before an incident occurs. Related Controls: None. References: OMB A-130, IR 7559.

title: INCIDENT REPORTING
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Evidence exists in automated logs and ticketing systems that capture and timestamp each incident report submission. Incident response records show staff notifications and subsequent actions taken within the organization-defined reporting time period.
• Technical artifacts include SIEM logs, email alert logs, and incident management system outputs that detail automated submissions to designated authorities. These outputs often record incident details and the system vulnerabilities associated with them.
• Operational patterns include regular drills or simulations with recorded time-to-notify metrics, periodic staff training sessions, and a clear record of communication with external supply chain partners and authorities.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations can fake compliance by maintaining static incident response policies and outdated documentation without correlating these with real operational data (i.e., historical incident reports).
• Surface-level implementations occur when only automated tools are enabled but staffing or process follow-through is lacking; for example, having auto-generated emails that are not actually monitored or acted upon.
• Frequently missed technical gaps involve the absence of chain-of-custody logs in the incident management system and not capturing vulnerabilities reported alongside incidents, especially in supply chain cases.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Verify Automated Incident Reporting Logs
   WHY: Automated logs are hard to fake and provide a clear timestamp of incident reports, ensuring adherence to the organizational time period for reporting.
   HOW: Inspect SIEM and incident management system logs for automated reports. Correlate timestamps with the defined reporting window. Validate that the notifications include the incident details and the designated reporting authority.
   FAIL: Missing, inconsistent, or outdated timestamps and lack of clear evidence that notifications reached the designated reporting authorities.

2. CHECK: Review Incident Report Samples and Associated Actions
   WHY: Real incident reports, along with follow-up actions, demonstrate that the incident reporting process is not only documented but actively enforced.
   HOW: Randomly select a sample of incident records from the incident management database. Cross-check if each report has follow-up actions, mitigation steps documented, and if system vulnerabilities (as applicable) were reported appropriately.
   FAIL: Incident records without follow-up actions, missing details, or absence of vulnerability reporting where required.

3. CHECK: Interview Key Personnel and Validate Training Records
   WHY: Personnel familiarity with timely incident reporting and understanding of their roles confirms practical implementation beyond written policy.
   HOW: Interview incident response team members and operational staff to validate their knowledge of the reporting time period and channels. Check training completion logs and drill exercise reports that simulate incident reporting.
   FAIL: Staff unable to describe reporting processes, outdated or absent training records, or lack of involvement in simulations.

4. CHECK: Confirm Communication with External Authorities and Supply Chain Partners
   WHY: Evidence of direct communication or automatically shared incident information with external entities ensures compliance with the requirement to report to designated authorities.
   HOW: Request evidence such as email receipts, confirmation logs, or external communication records shown in incident reports. Validate incident report distribution lists for authorities and supply chain partners.
   FAIL: Absence of outreach records or confirmation from external partners, or if notifications are only documented in internal logs without external traces.

EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut by correlating SIEM alerts with incident management system entries: a time gap beyond the defined period signals implementation gaps.
• Critical indicators of poor implementation include non-existent or poorly maintained communication logs, and out-of-date training records among incident response personnel.
• Always prioritize the review of live system logs and actual incident cases rather than just reviewing policy documents. A well-maintained incident database that includes chain-of-custody metadata is a strong sign of operational rigor.
• Don’t overlook the automatic mechanisms for vulnerability reporting; verify that these are integrated into the incident reporting process, as gaps here can lead to missed remediation actions.

By following these checks and validations, auditors can gain solid evidence that the INCIDENT REPORTING control is not just a documented process, but an actively enforced and operationally effective practice.
content:
Statement:
a. Require personnel to report suspected incidents to the organizational incident response capability within [Assignment: organization-defined time period]; and
b. Report incident information to [Assignment: organization-defined authorities].

Guidance:
The types of incidents reported, the content and timeliness of the reports, and the designated reporting authorities reflect applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines. Incident information can inform risk assessments, control effectiveness assessments, security requirements for acquisitions, and selection criteria for technology products.

Enhancements:
INCIDENT REPORTING | AUTOMATED REPORTING: Report incidents using [Assignment: organization-defined automated mechanisms]. Discussion: The recipients of incident reports are specified in IR-6b. Automated reporting mechanisms include email, posting on websites (with automatic updates), and automated incident response tools and programs. Related Discussions: IR-7.
INCIDENT REPORTING | VULNERABILITIES RELATED TO INCIDENTS: Report system vulnerabilities associated with reported incidents to [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles]. Discussion: Reported incidents that uncover system vulnerabilities are analyzed by organizational personnel including system owners, mission and business owners, senior agency information security officers, senior agency officials for privacy, authorizing officials, and the risk executive (function). The analysis can serve to prioritize and initiate mitigation actions to address the discovered system vulnerability. Related Controls: None.
INCIDENT REPORTING | SUPPLY CHAIN COORDINATION: Provide incident information to the provider of the product or service and other organizations involved in the supply chain or supply chain governance for systems or system components related to the incident. Discussion: Organizations involved in supply chain activities include product developers, system integrators, manufacturers, packagers, assemblers, distributors, vendors, and resellers. Entities that provide supply chain governance include the Federal Acquisition Security Council (FASC). Supply chain incidents include compromises or breaches that involve information technology products, system components, development processes or personnel, distribution processes, or warehousing facilities. Organizations determine the appropriate information to share and consider the value gained from informing external organizations about supply chain incidents, including the ability to improve processes or to identify the root cause of an incident. Related Controls: SR-8. References: [FASC18], [41 CFR 201], [USCERT IR], [SP 800-61].

title: INCIDENT MONITORING
methodology:
Control ID: IR-5 – INCIDENT MONITORING

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Visible evidence: An active and well-maintained incident tracking/record system that logs incidents with timestamps, statuses, and detailed incident descriptions.
• Technical artifacts: Database logs, event correlation records from automated ticketing systems, integrated SIEM outputs, and linked network alerts tied to incident reports. Dashboards showing incident metrics and trend analyses are strong visible proofs.
• Operational patterns: Consistent incident logging in real time; periodic reviews of incident data; use of automated tools to aggregate, analyze, and store incident records, ensuring chain-of-custody is maintained for forensic purposes.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Faking compliance: Organizations may simply produce static screenshots of dashboards or generate sample incident records without real operational validation.
• Surface-level implementations: Maintaining a policy document that states incidents are tracked, but not consistently using automated tools or keeping audit trails of actual incident data.
• Frequently missed technical gaps: Lack of integration between network monitoring tools and the incident management system, resulting in manual, error-prone logs; and absence of automated data collection that fails to capture all necessary forensic details.

3. VALIDATION STEPS:

CHECK: Verify that all incident reports are automatically time-stamped and include key metadata (incident ID, status, origin, reporter, etc.).
WHY: Automated logging with detailed metadata confirms active and systematic monitoring of incidents.
HOW: Access the incident tracking system and sample multiple logs; check if records include time stamps, unique identifiers, and status updates. Correlate with SIEM alerts if available.
FAIL: Records missing unique identifiers, timestamps, or details; evidence found only in policy documents without operational logs.

CHECK: Assess the integration of automated mechanisms for incident data collection (e.g., Computer Incident Response Centers or network monitoring instruments).
WHY: Automated mechanisms ensure timely, accurate capture and analysis of incident data, reducing reliance on manual input and enhancing forensic rigor.
HOW: Review configuration settings for automated SIEM alerts, test cross-referencing between network logs and incident records, and verify incident correlation in the incident management software.
FAIL: Lack of integration between monitoring tools and the incident ticket system or reliance solely on manual logs.

CHECK: Validate that periodic incident trend analysis and follow-up tracking are performed to identify and remediate systemic issues.
WHY: Regular trend analysis confirms that incidents are not only recorded but actively analyzed to improve security posture and incident response strategies.
HOW: Review automated reporting or dashboards that show historical incident trends, and interview incident responders regarding the process for trend reviews.
FAIL: Absence of historical trend data or no evidence that incident records are routinely reviewed and used for process improvements.

CHECK: Examine audit trails or chain-of-custody logs ensuring that changes or updates to incident records are tracked automatically.
WHY: Maintaining an audit trail is critical for forensic investigations and demonstrates real-time, tamper-evident logging of incident events.
HOW: Inspect the incident logging system for audit log entries that document modifications, user access, and record updates over time.
FAIL: No visible audit logs or audit trails, or records that can easily be altered without versioning.

4. EXPERT TIPS:
• Shortcut: Look for direct integration between SIEM systems and incident tracking databases; a unified system that automates alerts to incidents is a strong sign of effective implementation.
• Critical indicator: Inconsistent or incomplete incident records, especially missing timestamps or lack of audit trails, suggest superficial compliance.
• Detail matters: Verify that even low-severity incidents are tracked in the system. The absence of granular data even for minor events may indicate that the control is not fully implemented.
• A field test or query that extracts recent incidents and sampling the record history can quickly reveal if the automated tracking is thorough and operational.

By focusing on these practical checkpoints, you can validate that IR-5 is not just documented on paper but actively implemented and operationally effective in the organization’s security incident management processes.
content:
Statement:
Track and document incidents.

Guidance:
Documenting incidents includes maintaining records about each incident, the status of the incident, and other pertinent information necessary for forensics as well as evaluating incident details, trends, and handling. Incident information can be obtained from a variety of sources, including network monitoring, incident reports, incident response teams, user complaints, supply chain partners, audit monitoring, physical access monitoring, and user and administrator reports. IR-4 provides information on the types of incidents that are appropriate for monitoring.

Enhancements:
INCIDENT MONITORING | AUTOMATED TRACKING, DATA COLLECTION, AND ANALYSIS: Track incidents and collect and analyze incident information using [Assignment: organization-defined automated mechanisms]. Discussion: Automated mechanisms for tracking incidents and collecting and analyzing incident information include Computer Incident Response Centers or other electronic databases of incidents and network monitoring devices. Related Controls: None.

title: INCIDENT HANDLING
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for verifying the implementation of IR-4 Incident Handling controls in an organization:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence includes an operational incident response team and a SOC actively monitoring and reacting to events, as well as a well-documented incident response plan that is regularly exercised. 
• Technical artifacts include incident logs (from automated incident handling systems), system reconfiguration logs (e.g., firewall/IDS/IPS rule changes), forensic analysis reports, and correlation records between incident handling and contingency operations. 
• Operational patterns include routine incident simulation exercises, after-action reviews demonstrating lessons learned, coordinated updates to IR procedures, and evidence of cross-team communication with contingency planning and external organizations.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Some organizations might produce polished documentation and static process flowcharts without showing real-time logs or dynamic responses to incidents. 
• Surface-level implementations include incident response exercises that are scripted or scheduled without resembling live incident conditions, or using a tool that only simulates automation rather than providing genuine dynamic reconfiguration.
• Frequent gaps include lack of integration between incident data sources, inadequate coordination with continuity-of-operations plans, and an absence of automated triggering of incident response actions (e.g., dynamic disabling of compromised systems).

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Verify Full Lifecycle Incident Handling Capability
WHY: Incident management must cover preparation, detection & analysis, containment, eradication, and recovery to be effective.
HOW: Examine system logs and incident reports to confirm that all lifecycle phases are executed during actual or simulated incidents. Review evidence such as after-action reports from exercises that include these phases, and request to see incident ticket histories from incident management systems.
FAIL: Presence of incident logs or reports that only address detection without further phases, or evidence that incident response stops after initial detection.

CHECK: Assess Coordination with Contingency and External Entities
WHY: Effective incident handling requires coordinated activities with contingency planning, and, where applicable, with external organizations (e.g., law enforcement, supply chain partners) to provide a comprehensive response.
HOW: Review records and communications that demonstrate joint exercises or response drills between incident response teams and continuity planning groups. Check meeting minutes or integrated incident response reports that illustrate inter-department or inter-organizational engagement.
FAIL: Documents that present siloed operations (incident response alone) without cross-references to contingency planning or external coordination; lack of evidence in simulated or live incident scenarios.

CHECK: Validate Use of Automated Incident Handling Processes and Dynamic Reconfiguration
WHY: Automation and dynamic response are critical to promptly contain incidents and minimize damage.
HOW: Inspect automated system logs (e.g., intrusion detection system, automated incident management tools) for evidence of automated alerts, response actions (such as system disabling, ACL changes), or dynamic reconfiguration events triggered by security violations. Validate that these automated events correlate with identified risk thresholds or incident scenarios.
FAIL: No verifiable automated logs showing dynamic responses; manual-only processes flagged as ‘automation’ without corresponding system evidence; disparate timestamps that fail to show real-time actions.

CHECK: Confirm Integration of Lessons Learned into Updated Procedures and Training
WHY: Incorporating lessons learned is essential for continuous improvement in incident handling capabilities.
HOW: Check records of incident debriefs, update logs for the incident response plan, and training records that show periodic updates based on past incidents. Look for tangible changes (e.g., revised procedures, updated detection signatures) that are directly linked to previous incident outcomes.
FAIL: Static incident response documentation that hasn’t been revised, training records that do not reflect updates after incidents, or absence of after-action review documentation.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Focus on finding dynamic, timestamped evidence (system logs, automated tool reports, real-time reconfiguration records) rather than just process narratives or slide decks. 
• During audits, ask to join a simulated incident response exercise to see live coordination in action. 
• Check for red flags such as overly generic remediation procedures or isolated incident reports that lack coordinated follow-through with other business continuity processes.
• Be alert for incident response plans that are 'paper-based' and not embedded into operational systems—this often indicates poor integration of lessons learned and inadequate automation.

Following these steps will help verify that the Incident Handling control is not just documented, but actively and effectively implemented across the organization.
content:
Statement:
a. Implement an incident handling capability for incidents that is consistent with the incident response plan and includes preparation, detection and analysis, containment, eradication, and recovery;

b. Coordinate incident handling activities with contingency planning activities;

c. Incorporate lessons learned from ongoing incident handling activities into incident response procedures, training, and testing, and implement the resulting changes accordingly; and

d. Ensure the rigor, intensity, scope, and results of incident handling activities are comparable and predictable across the organization.

Guidance:
Organizations recognize that incident response capabilities are dependent on the capabilities of organizational systems and the mission and business processes being supported by those systems. Organizations consider incident response as part of the definition, design, and development of mission and business processes and systems. Incident-related information can be obtained from a variety of sources, including monitoring, audit and access monitoring, and network monitoring; user or administrator reports; and reported supply chain events. Suspected security incidents include the receipt of suspicious email communications that can contain malicious code. Suspected supply chain incidents include the insertion of counterfeit hardware or malicious code into organizational systems or system components. For federal agencies, an incident that involves personally identifiable information is considered a breach. A breach results in unauthorized disclosure, the loss of control, unauthorized acquisition, compromise, or a similar occurrence where a person other than an authorized user accesses or potentially accesses personally identifiable information or an authorized user accesses or potentially accesses such information for other than authorized purposes.

Enhancements:
INCIDENT HANDLING | AUTOMATED INCIDENT HANDLING PROCESSES: Support the incident handling process using [Assignment: organization-defined automated mechanisms]. Discussion: Automated mechanisms that support incident handling processes include online incident management systems and tools that support the collection of live response data, full network packet capture, and forensic analysis. Related Controls: None.
INCIDENT HANDLING | DYNAMIC RECONFIGURATION: Include the following types of dynamic reconfiguration for [Assignment: organization-defined system components] as part of the incident response capability: [Assignment: organization-defined types of dynamic reconfiguration]. Discussion: Dynamic reconfiguration includes changes to router rules, access control lists, intrusion detection or prevention system parameters, and filter rules for guards or firewalls. Organizations may perform dynamic reconfiguration of systems to stop attacks, misdirect attackers, and isolate components of systems, thus limiting the extent of the damage from breaches or compromises. Related Controls: AC-2, AC-4, CM-2.
INCIDENT HANDLING | CONTINUITY OF OPERATIONS: Identify [Assignment: organization-defined classes of incidents] and take the following actions in response to those incidents to ensure continuation of organizational mission and business functions: [Assignment: organization-defined actions to take in response to classes of incidents]. Discussion: Classes of incidents include malfunctions due to design or implementation errors and omissions, targeted malicious attacks, and untargeted malicious attacks. Incident response actions include orderly system degradation, system shutdown, fallback to manual mode or evacuation of alternative technology whereby the system operates differently, employing deceptive measures, alternate information flows, or operating in a mode that is reserved for when systems are under attack. Organizations consider whether continuity of operations requirements during an incident conflict with the capability to automatically disable the system as specified as part of IR-4(5). Related Controls: None.
