INCIDENT HANDLING | INFORMATION CORRELATION: Correlate incident information and individual incident responses to achieve an organization-wide perspective on incident awareness and response. Discussion: Sometimes, a threat event, such as a hostile cyber-attack, can only be observed by bringing together information from different sources, including various reports and reporting procedures established by organizations. Related Controls: None.
INCIDENT HANDLING | AUTOMATIC DISABLING OF SYSTEM: Implement a configurable capability to automatically disable the system if [Assignment: organization-defined security violations] are detected. Discussion: Organizations consider whether the capability to automatically disable the system conflicts with continuity of operations requirements specified as part of CP-2 or IR-4(3). Security violations include cyber-attacks that have compromised the integrity of the system or exfiltrated organizational information and serious errors in software programs that could adversely impact organizational missions or functions or jeopardize the safety of individuals. Related Controls: None.
INCIDENT HANDLING | INSIDER THREATS: Implement an incident handling capability for incidents involving insider threats. Discussion: Explicit focus on handling incidents involving insider threats provides additional emphasis on this type of threat and the need for specific incident handling capabilities to provide appropriate and timely responses.
INCIDENT HANDLING | INSIDER THREATS — INTRA-ORGANIZATION COORDINATION: Coordinate an incident handling capability for insider threats that includes the following organizational entities [Assignment: organization-defined entities]. Discussion: Incident handling for insider threat incidents (e.g., preparation, detection and analysis, containment, eradication, and recovery) requires coordination among many organizational entities, including mission or business owners, system owners, human resources offices, procurement offices, personnel offices, physical security officers, senior agency information security officer, operations personnel, risk executive (function), senior agency official for privacy, and legal counsel. In addition, organizations may require external support from federal, state, and local law enforcement agencies. Related Controls: None.
INCIDENT HANDLING | CORRELATION WITH EXTERNAL ORGANIZATIONS: Coordinate with [Assignment: organization-defined external organizations] to correlate and share [Assignment: organization-defined incident information] to achieve a cross-organization perspective on incident awareness and more effective incident responses. Discussion: The coordination of incident information with external organizations—including mission or business partners, military or coalition partners, customers, and developers—can provide significant benefits. Cross-organizational coordination can serve as an important risk management capability. This capability allows organizations to leverage information from a variety of sources to effectively respond to incidents and breaches that could potentially affect the organization’s operations, assets, and individuals. Related Controls: AU-16, PM-16.
INCIDENT HANDLING | DYNAMIC RESPONSE CAPABILITY: Employ [Assignment: organization-defined dynamic response capabilities] to respond to incidents. Discussion: The dynamic response capability addresses the timely deployment of new or replacement organizational capabilities in response to incidents. This includes capabilities implemented at the mission and business process level and at the system level. Related Controls: None.
INCIDENT HANDLING | SUPPLY CHAIN COORDINATION: Coordinate incident handling activities involving supply chain events with other organizations involved in the supply chain. Discussion: Organizations involved in supply chain activities include product developers, system integrators, manufacturers, packagers, assemblers, distributors, vendors, and resellers. Supply chain incidents can occur anywhere through or to the supply chain and include compromises or breaches that involve primary or sub-tier providers, information technology products, system components, development processes or personnel, and distribution processes or warehousing facilities. Organizations consider including processes for protecting and sharing incident information in information exchange agreements and their obligations for reporting incidents to government oversight bodies (e.g., Federal Acquisition Security Council). Related Controls: CA-3, MA-2, SA-9, SR-8.
INCIDENT HANDLING | INTEGRATED INCIDENT RESPONSE TEAM: Establish and maintain an integrated incident response team that can be deployed to any location identified by the organization in [Assignment: organization-defined time period].
INCIDENT HANDLING | MALICIOUS CODE AND FORENSIC ANALYSIS: Discussion: When conducted carefully in an isolated environment, analysis of malicious code and other residual artifacts of a security incident or breach can give the organization insight into adversary tactics, techniques, and procedures. It can also indicate the identity or some characteristics of the adversary. In addition, malicious code analysis can help the organization develop responses to future incidents. Related Controls: None.
INCIDENT HANDLING | BEHAVIOR ANALYSIS: Analyze anomalous or suspected adversarial behavior in or related to [Assignment: organization-defined environments or resources]. Discussion: If the organization maintains a deception environment, an analysis of behaviors in that environment, including resources targeted by the adversary and timing of the incident or event, can provide insight into adversarial tactics, techniques, and procedures. External to a deception environment, the analysis of anomalous adversarial behavior (e.g., changes in system performance or usage patterns) or suspected behavior (e.g., changes in searches for the location of specific resources) can give the organization such insight. Related Controls: None.
INCIDENT HANDLING | SECURITY OPERATIONS CENTER: Establish and maintain a security operations center. Discussion: A security operations center (SOC) is the focal point for security operations and computer network defense for an organization. The purpose of the SOC is to defend and monitor an organization’s systems and networks (i.e., cyber infrastructure) on an ongoing basis. The SOC is also responsible for detecting, analyzing, and responding to cybersecurity incidents in a timely manner. The organization staffs the SOC with skilled technical and ...
INCIDENT HANDLING | PUBLIC RELATIONS AND REPUTATION REPAIR: (a) Manage public relations associated with an incident; and
(b) Employ measures to repair the reputation of the organization. Discussion: It is important for an organization to have a strategy in place for addressing incidents that have been brought to the attention of the general public, have cast the organization in a negative light, or have affected the organization’s constituents (e.g., partners, customers). Such publicity can be extremely harmful to the organization and affect its ability to carry out its mission and business functions. Taking proactive steps to repair the organization’s reputation is an essential aspect of reestablishing the trust and confidence of its constituents. Related Controls: None.

title: INCIDENT RESPONSE TESTING
methodology:
Incident Response Testing (IR-3) Verification Methodology

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Evidence of routine incident response tests such as tabletop exercises, walkthroughs, and simulated incidents is available. Examples include dated calendars, email notifications, meeting minutes, and test logs.
• Technical artifacts include automated test scripts, logs from simulation tools, and recorded metrics showing reaction times and outcomes.
• Operational patterns such as periodic test execution, coordinated exercises with both IT and business continuity teams, and documented post-test reviews indicate an active control.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations sometimes merely produce written test plans and schedules without actually executing the tests or capturing realistic results.
• Artifacts can be faked by documenting scheduled tests that never occurred or by using generic, non-specific scenarios in test plans.
• Automated test logs may be incomplete or manipulated, and coordination across different disciplines (disaster recovery, business continuity) may be overlooked in practice.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
• CHECK: Review the incident response test schedule and verify that tests (tabletop, simulation, automated) occurred at the organization-defined frequency.
  WHY: Regular execution demonstrates that the incident response capability is consistently exercised.
  HOW: Examine calendar events, meeting logs, and test reports with clear timestamps that match the defined schedule.
  FAIL: Lack of test logs, outdated records, or evidence of deviation from the scheduled frequency.

• CHECK: Inspect the documented test plans to ensure they include realistic incident scenarios and cover key aspects of the incident response process.
  WHY: Detailed test plans ensure that testing is comprehensive, realistic, and covers potential incident vectors.
  HOW: Review test plan documents, scenario scripts, and exercise documentation that reference expected escalation paths and roles.
  FAIL: Test plans with generic scenarios, missing action detail, or no connection to the incident response process.

• CHECK: Verify the use of automated tools or scripts in testing the incident response capability.
  WHY: Automated testing provides measurable, reproducible evidence of the control's effectiveness while reducing human error.
  HOW: Check automation logs, configuration files, and output from incident simulation tools; correlate test events with system-generated alerts.
  FAIL: Absence of automation logs or evidence that automated mechanisms are not in use or are misconfigured.

• CHECK: Confirm that incident response tests are coordinated with other related plans (e.g., disaster recovery, business continuity) and that cross-team collaboration is documented.
  WHY: Coordination across related plans increases overall resiliency and ensures a holistic approach to incident management.
  HOW: Review coordination meeting minutes, integrated test exercises, and follow-up reports that indicate multi-team participation.
  FAIL: Isolated test reports with no sign of interdepartmental coordination or integration with other continuity planning tests.

• CHECK: Evaluate post-test analysis reports for evidence of continuous improvement based on both qualitative and quantitative data gathered during tests.
  WHY: The effectiveness of incident response processes is best determined by learning from actual tests and adapting processes accordingly.
  HOW: Look for metrics trends, documented lessons learned, updated procedures, and evidence of corrective actions based on previous test shortcomings.
  FAIL: Absence of improvement recommendations, repetitive findings in successive test reports, or stale metrics indicating no process evolution.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Engage with staff who executed the tests and ask specific questions about incident handling steps to detect superficial compliance.
• Critical Indicator: Recurring issues or unaddressed findings in post-test reviews often signal poor implementation.
• Detail Matters: Ensure that test scenarios are tailored to current threat landscapes and include both qualitative feedback (e.g., team coordination) and quantitative metrics (e.g., response times).
• Look for Re-testing: A best practice is to see evidence of follow-up tests that confirm previous deficiencies have been addressed.

This methodology emphasizes practical evidence and operational effectiveness that goes beyond mere documentation, ensuring that the incident response testing control is robust, realistic, and continuously improving.
content:
Statement:
Test the effectiveness of the incident response capability for the system [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] using the following tests: [Assignment: organization-defined tests].

Guidance:
Organizations test incident response capabilities to determine their effectiveness and identify potential weaknesses or deficiencies. Incident response testing includes the use of checklists, walk-through or tabletop exercises, and simulations (parallel or full interrupt). Incident response testing can include a determination of the effects on organizational operations and assets and individuals due to incident response. The use of qualitative and quantitative data aids in determining the effectiveness of incident response processes.

Enhancements:
INCIDENT RESPONSE TESTING | AUTOMATED TESTING: Test the incident response capability using [Assignment: organization-defined automated mechanisms]. Discussion: Organizations use automated mechanisms to more thoroughly and effectively test incident response capabilities. This can be accomplished by providing more complete coverage of incident response issues, selecting realistic test scenarios and environments, and stressing the response capability. Related Controls: None.
INCIDENT RESPONSE TESTING | COORDINATION WITH RELATED PLANS: Coordinate incident response testing with organizational elements responsible for related plans. Discussion: Organizational plans related to incident response testing include business continuity plans, disaster recovery plans, continuity of operations plans, contingency plans, crisis communications plans, critical infrastructure plans, and occupant emergency plans. Related Controls: None.
INCIDENT RESPONSE TESTING | CONTINUOUS IMPROVEMENT: Use qualitative and quantitative data from testing to:
(a) Determine the effectiveness of incident response processes;
(b) Continuously improve incident response processes; and
(c) Provide incident response measures and metrics that are accurate, consistent, and in a reproducible format.

title: INCIDENT RESPONSE TRAINING
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
- CHECK: Review documented training records (including attendance logs, automated training system logs, and training certificates) that detail role-based incident response training sessions, including initial onboarding, recurring updates, and post-change sessions.
  WHY: These artifacts confirm that training was delivered as per role-specific requirements and at defined intervals (e.g., onboarding, periodic refreshers, and following system changes).
  HOW: Access the organization’s training management system, extract logs for incident response training sessions, and correlate training dates with employee role assignments and system change events.
  FAIL: Absence of time-stamped training records, certificates without corresponding logs, or evidence that training content was not updated following organizational or system changes.

- CHECK: Validate evidence of simulated event exercises and automated training environments used for incident response training.
  WHY: Simulation exercises and automated environments demonstrate practical, hands-on training and relevant real-life scenario practice, beyond mere policy reading.
  HOW: Review exercise logs, debrief reports, and system screenshots or video captures from automated training tools that provide incident response scenarios (including breach recognition and reporting drills).
  FAIL: Lack of documented simulation event records, missing debrief reports, or training modules that do not reflect realistic incident scenarios.

- CHECK: Interview a sample of system users and incident responders to test their recall and understanding of their incident response responsibilities.
  WHY: Observable user comprehension indicates effective training delivery and proper assimilation of procedures.
  HOW: Conduct brief interviews or targeted questionnaires focusing on what actions to take during an incident, key contacts, and how to report a breach.
  FAIL: Inability of personnel to clearly articulate their roles, responsibilities, or specific actions during simulated incidents.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
- Organizations might simply generate superficial training completion certificates or sign-in sheets without delivering the actual training content or ensuring role-appropriate depth. 
- Publishing training policies and schedules while failing to execute simulated exercises or update content based on system changes can create a false sense of compliance.
- The presence of outdated training materials or lack of correlation between training dates and role changes/system updates indicates a failure to maintain operational readiness.

VALIDATION STEPS:
1. CHECK the training management system for role-specific distributions: Extract evidence showing that users in different incident response roles (general staff, system administrators, incident responders) have attended tailored training sessions.
   WHY: This verifies that training is not generic, but customized according to job function.
   HOW: Use system export functions to correlate user roles with attended training logs, and check training content modules for role-specific topics.
   FAIL: Uniform training records for all staff regardless of role or absence of evidence for specialized content sessions.

2. CHECK automated training records and simulation exercise reports:
   WHY: Automated environments and simulation events provide a rich, measurable indication of training effectiveness.
   HOW: Examine logs, system screenshots, or automated audit trails that detail simulation dates, participant engagement levels, and post-event feedback or lessons learned.
   FAIL: Incomplete logs, missing simulation evidence, or training environment setups that are rarely used or poorly documented.

3. CHECK update logs and training review records:
   WHY: Incident response training content that is periodically reviewed, especially after system changes or incidents, signals active efforts to remain current and effective.
   HOW: Review change management and training update records to confirm that training content was revised following significant incidents or system updates.
   FAIL: Inconsistencies between system change records and training updates, or lack of any review schedule documentation.

4. CHECK direct validation interviews:
   WHY: Verbal confirmation and scenario-based questioning can reveal gaps in understanding that static records might miss.
   HOW: Conduct targeted interviews with various user groups; ask them to detail their incident reporting procedures, and gauge their readiness to take action during an incident.
   FAIL: Inconsistent or vague answers that suggest training was merely tick-box activity rather than effective learning.

EXPERT TIPS:
- Shortcut: Query the centralized training management system for automated alerts or flags that indicate overdue training; these often serve as quick indicators of lapses.
- Shortcut: Focus on cross-checking training logs with system change records—any gaps here often signal that training content is not being updated dynamically with the evolving threat landscape.
- Critical Detail: Ensure that simulation exercises are documented not only in terms of occurrence but also with performance metrics (e.g., response times, decision accuracy) to truly assess preparedness.
- Indicator of Poor Implementation: Over-reliance on document-based evidence (policies and signed sheets) without corresponding real-world drills or interactive training sessions should raise concerns about the actual readiness of the incident response team.

This comprehensive methodology emphasizes observable, automated evidence and hands-on exercises, ensuring that incident response training is not only in place but also operationally effective.
content:
Statement:
a. Provide incident response training to system users consistent with assigned roles and responsibilities:

1. Within [Assignment: organization-defined time period] of assuming an incident response role or responsibility or acquiring system access;

2. When required by system changes; and

3. [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] thereafter; and

b. Review and update incident response training content [Assignment: organization-defined events].

Guidance:
Incident response training is associated with the assigned roles and responsibilities of organizational personnel to ensure that the appropriate content and level of detail are included in such training. For example, users may only need to know who to call or how to recognize an incident; system administrators may require additional training on how to handle incidents; and incident responders may receive more specific training on forensics, data collection techniques, reporting, system recovery, and system restoration. Incident response training includes user training in identifying and reporting suspicious activities from external and internal sources. Incident response training for users may be provided as part of AT-2 or AT-3.

Enhancements:
INCIDENT RESPONSE TRAINING | SIMULATED EVENTS: Incorporate simulated events into incident response training to facilitate the required response by personnel in crisis situations. Discussion: Organizations establish requirements for responding to incidents in incident response plans. Incorporating simulated events into incident response training helps to ensure that personnel understand their individual responsibilities and what specific actions to take in crisis situations. Related Controls: None.
INCIDENT RESPONSE TRAINING | AUTOMATED TRAINING ENVIRONMENTS: Provide an incident response training environment using [Assignment: organization-defined automated mechanisms]. Discussion: Automated mechanisms can provide a more thorough and realistic incident response training environment. This can be accomplished, for example, by providing more complete coverage of incident response issues, selecting more realistic training scenarios and environments, and stressing the response capability. Related Controls: None.
INCIDENT RESPONSE TRAINING | BREACH: Provide incident response training on how to identify and respond to a breach, including the organization’s process for reporting a breach. Discussion: For federal agencies, an incident that involves personally identifiable information is considered a breach. A breach results in the loss of control, compromise, unauthorized disclosure, unauthorized acquisition, or a similar occurrence where a person other than an authorized user accesses or potentially accesses personally identifiable information or an authorized user accesses or potentially accesses such information for other than authorized purposes. Incident response training emphasizes the obligation of individuals to report both confirmed and suspected breaches involving information in any medium or form, including paper, oral, and electronic. Incident response training includes tabletop exercises that simulate a breach. See IR-21. Related Controls: None. References: [OMB M-17-12], [SP 800-50].

title: POLICY AND PROCEDURES
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• The organization maintains an officially approved Incident Response (IR) policy document that clearly states its purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, coordination mechanisms, and references to legal/regulatory requirements.
• There are detailed IR procedures that explain how the policy is operationalized – including incident identification, escalation, containment, eradication, and post-event analysis.
• Evident dissemination channels can be observed (e.g., intranet postings, email distributions, training records) that show the policy and procedures have been communicated to all defined personnel and roles.
• The appointment of a designated official (or role) for managing the IR documentation is documented in organizational charts or job descriptions, with evidence of active management.
• Review logs, change management records, or periodic meeting minutes demonstrating that the IR policy and procedures have been updated following predefined triggers (e.g., audit findings, security incidents, or regulatory changes).

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations might present a generic document as an IR policy without tailoring it to organizational specifics or current threats – essentially a boilerplate text that is not actively used.
• Relying solely on policy existence without any verifiable dissemination or training records may give a false sense of compliance.
• Outdated IR policy versions that have not been reviewed in line with change events, or documented reviews that are merely formalities rather than reflecting operational changes.
• Assigning a role nominally without actual accountability or evidence of the designated official’s involvement in incident response tasks.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Verify the availability of an officially approved Incident Response policy document.
   WHY: An approved and disseminated document provides upfront evidence that incident response is planned and governed by organizational rules.
   HOW: Review the central policy repository or intranet for the latest IR policy; check for management sign-off dates, version numbers, and distribution lists; confirm that the document clearly outlines purpose, roles, responsibilities, and legal/regulatory references.
   FAIL: The policy is missing, unsigned, or appears as a generic copy-paste without detailed operational elements.

2. CHECK: Examine the existence and content of detailed Incident Response procedures.
   WHY: Procedures translate policy into actionable steps and ensure the organization can respond effectively to incidents.
   HOW: Retrieve the IR procedures document and verify that it includes step-by-step instructions, clearly mapped roles, definitions of incident severities, escalation paths and includes contingencies for varied incident scenarios.
   FAIL: Procedures are absent, insufficiently detailed, or do not align with the stated policy objectives, indicating superficial documentation.

3. CHECK: Confirm that the designated incident response official is formally identified and actively engaged.
   WHY: Having a responsible official ensures that the IR policy and procedures are maintained, updated, and enforced.
   HOW: Inspect organizational charts, job descriptions, and meeting logs to locate the designated official; interview relevant staff or review email communications confirming their role in IR management.
   FAIL: There is no clear designation or the role appears inactive/non-participatory in documented IR activities.

4. CHECK: Validate that a review and update mechanism is in place for both the policy and procedures.
   WHY: Regular review ensures that the IR policy remains current with emerging threats, regulatory changes, and lessons learned from incidents.
   HOW: Request records of past review cycles, such as change logs, meeting minutes, or audit reports that indicate when and why the IR policy/procedures were updated; verify that the review frequency and triggers (e.g., after an incident or regulatory change) are documented and followed.
   FAIL: There is no documented schedule of reviews or evidence of updates following incident events or assessments over significant periods.

5. CHECK: Cross-check the IR policy and procedures against applicable legal, executive, and regulatory requirements.
   WHY: Ensuring compliance with external mandates is a core requirement, and integration of these requirements in the policy indicates thoroughness.
   HOW: Compare the policy’s content with current laws, directives, or executive orders relevant to incident response; validate that compliance obligations are addressed clearly within the policy.
   FAIL: Critical legal and regulatory references are missing or outdated, raising concerns about legal compliance.

EXPERT TIPS
• Verification Shortcut: Interview a sample of staff or conduct a simulated incident drill to see if the IR policy and procedures are followed in practice – observable operational readiness is a key indicator.
• Critical Indicator: Look for dated review cycles and documented updates; stagnation in documents signals poor implementation.
• Detail to Note: Check that the policy isn’t just a re-statement of control requirements but includes tailored organizational context and role-specific instructions.
• Quick Check: Verify document version control and distribution logs – digital repositories with automated auditing can confirm real-time compliance and dissemination.
• Practical Approach: If possible, request and review after-action reports from recent incident response exercises as these often provide evidence of how the policy and procedures are used in live situations.
content:
Statement:
a. Develop, document, and disseminate to [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles]:
1. [Selection (one or more): Organization-level; Mission/business process-level; System-level] incident response policy that:
   (a) Addresses purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, coordination among organizational entities, and compliance; and
   (b) Is consistent with applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines; and
2. Procedures to facilitate the implementation of the incident response policy and the associated incident response controls;

b. Designate an [Assignment: organization-defined official] to manage the development, documentation, and dissemination of the incident response policy and procedures; and

c. Review and update the current incident response:
1. Policy [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] and following [Assignment: organization-defined events]; and
2. Procedures [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] and following [Assignment: organization-defined events].

Guidance:
Incident response policy and procedures address the controls in the IR family that are implemented within systems and organizations. The risk management strategy is an important factor in establishing such policies and procedures. Policies and procedures contribute to security and privacy assurance. Therefore, it is important that security and privacy programs collaborate on the development of incident response policy and procedures. Security and privacy program policies and procedures at the organization level are preferable, in general, and may obviate the need for mission- or system-specific policies and procedures. The policy can be included as part of the general security and privacy policy or be represented by multiple policies that reflect the complex nature of organizations. Procedures can be established for security and privacy programs, for mission or business processes, and for systems, if needed. Procedures describe how the policies or controls are implemented and can be directed at the individual or role that is the object of the procedure. Procedures can be documented in system security and privacy plans or in one or more separate documents. Events that may precipitate an update to incident response policy and procedures include assessment or audit findings, security incidents or breaches, or changes in laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines. Simply restating controls does not constitute an organizational policy or procedure.

title: IDENTITY PROOFING
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for IA-12 (Identity Proofing) based on evidence of identity evidence collection, verification, and the use of sponsor/supervisor authorization:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Screenshots or printouts of registration workflows that include fields for identity evidence capture (e.g., scanned identity documents, biometric data) and supervisor/sponsor authorization signatures. In-person registration logs showing face-to-face verification sessions also serve as evidence.
• Technical Artifacts: Automated audit logs from the registration system that record events such as evidence submission, verification steps, and approval by designated registration authorities. Digital workflow records with metadata indicating which identity evidence was provided and its validation status. Records of integration with external identity proofing sources (for externally-proofed identities) are also relevant.
• Operational Patterns: Consistent use of a multi-step registration process where identity evidence is collected and cross-checked (either through in-person or validated remote methods), including mandatory supervisor or sponsor approval. Repeatable approval patterns that cannot be bypassed without verification and documentation.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Faking Compliance via Documentation: Organizations may produce policy documents or training materials that describe identity proofing without actually enforcing the verification of identity evidence during every account setup.
• Surface-Level Implementations: Relying solely on self-attested forms or digital forms without verifying physical documents or biometrics; accepting generic documents that are not cross-referenced against external authoritative sources.
• Technical Gaps: Skipping the in-person verification requirement or failing to integrate backend audit trails that capture the full process, such as submission of identity evidence, in-person encounters, or proper linkage between the identity and the account.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
• CHECK: Verification of Registration Workflows
  WHY: Ensures that the registration process mandates submission, review, and validation of identity evidence along with supervisor authorization.
  HOW: Obtain samples of user registration workflows and audit logs; verify that each account creation includes fields for identity evidence (documents/biometrics), supervisor or sponsor approval, and recorded in-person verification notes if required.
  FAIL: If the logs or workflows do not capture identity evidence details or show that supervisor/sponsor approval is missing or bypassed.

• CHECK: Audit Log and System Record Analysis
  WHY: Audit logs provide an automated, tamper-resistant record of identity proofing activities, confirming that technical control is actively enforced.
  HOW: Use automated queries to extract and review audit logs for identity evidence submission, validation results, and timestamps indicating when in-person or supervisor verifications occurred; compare with defined assurance level requirements.
  FAIL: Missing or incomplete audit records, or evidence of systematic bypassing of identity evidence validation steps.

• CHECK: Sample of Processed Accounts
  WHY: Sampling actual account creation events helps verify that identity proofing is operational rather than just documented.
  HOW: Randomly select a sample set of newly provisioned accounts. Interview registration authorities and cross-check the account creation records against supporting identity evidence (photocopies, biometrics, approval logs) and verify that in-person validations were documented when required.
  FAIL: Instances where accounts are created solely based on self-attested data or without proper identity evidence validations.

• CHECK: Integration with External Authority Proofing (if applicable)
  WHY: Ensures that externally proofed identities are accepted in accordance with defined risk levels and that transfer procedures are verified.
  HOW: Review configurations and workflows where identity evidence from external agencies is integrated. Verify that these identities meet the organizationally defined assurance levels and that the evidence presented is captured and reconciled with internal records.
  FAIL: Lack of integration evidence or the direct acceptance of externally proofed identities without checking evidence against defined assurance criteria.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut Verification: Leverage automated monitoring tools to flag any registration events lacking mandatory identity proofing fields or approval steps. A missing audit trail in a registration event is often a quick indicator of non-compliance.
• Critical Indicators: Watch out for consistently missing supervisor/sponsor approvals and any registrations with incomplete identity evidence documentation. These are red flags that the process is being bypassed or over-relied on self-attestation.
• Deep Dive: In interviews with registration personnel, ask for examples of how identity evidence was validated and verified; inconsistencies between policy and practice are common indicators of ineffective implementation.
• Prioritize Observability: Automated logs and real-time dashboards are far more reliable than manual records or periodic reports, so focus on verifying real-time monitoring and anomaly alerts.

This comprehensive methodology emphasizes operational effectiveness and the use of automated, verifiable evidence to prove that identity proofing is implemented as intended, making it significantly harder for superficial or mimicked compliance to go undetected.
content:
Statement:
a. Identity proof users that require accounts for logical access to systems based on appropriate identity assurance level requirements as specified in applicable standards and guidelines; b. Resolve user identities to a unique individual; and c. Collect, validate, and verify identity evidence.

Guidance:
Identity proofing is the process of collecting, validating, and verifying a user’s identity information for the purposes of establishing credentials for accessing a system. Identity proofing is intended to mitigate threats to the registration of users and the establishment of

Enhancements:
IDENTITY PROOFING | SUPERVISOR AUTHORIZATION
Require that the registration process to receive an account for logical access includes supervisor or sponsor authorization.

Discussion: Including supervisor or sponsor authorization as part of the registration process provides an additional level of scrutiny to ensure that the user's management chain is aware of the account, the account is essential to carry out organizational missions and functions and the user's privileges are appropriate for the anticipated responsibilities and authorities within the organization.

Related Controls: None.
IDENTITY PROOFING | IDENTITY EVIDENCE
Require evidence of individual identification be presented to the registration authority.

Discussion: Identity evidence, such as documentary evidence or a combination of documents and biometrics, reduces the likelihood of individuals using fraudulent identification to establish an identity or at least increases the work factor of potential adversaries. The forms of acceptable evidence are consistent with the risks to the systems, roles, and privileges associated with the user's account.

Related Controls: None.
IDENTITY PROOFING | IDENTITY EVIDENCE VALIDATION AND VERIFICATION
Require that the presented identity evidence be validated and verified through [Assignment: organizationally defined methods of validation and verification].

Discussion: Validation and verification of identity evidence increases the assurance that accounts and identifiers are being established for the correct user and authenticators are being bound to that user. Validation refers to the process of confirming that the evidence is genuine and authentic, and the data contained in the evidence is correct, current, and related to an individual. Verification confirms and establishes a linkage between the claimed identity and the actual existence of the user presenting the evidence. Acceptable methods for validating and verifying identity evidence are consistent with the risks to the systems, roles, and privileges associated with the users account.

Related Controls: None.
IDENTITY PROOFING | IN-PERSON VALIDATION AND VERIFICATION
Require that the validation and verification of identity evidence be conducted in person before a designated registration authority.

Discussion: In-person proofing reduces the likelihood of fraudulent credentials being issued because it requires the physical presence of individuals, the presentation of physical identity documents, and actual face-to-face interactions with designated registration authorities.

Related Controls: None.
IDENTITY PROOFING | ADDRESS CONFIRMATION
Related Controls: None.
Accept externally-proofed identities at [Assignment: organization-defined identity assurance level]. Discussion: To limit unnecessary re-proofing of identities, particularly of non-PIV users, organizations accept proofing conducted at a commensurate level of assurance by other agencies or organizations. Proofing is consistent with organizational security policy and the identity assurance level appropriate for the system, application, or information accessed. Accepting externally-proofed identities is a fundamental component of managing federated identities across agencies and organizations.

title: RE-AUTHENTICATION
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: Screenshots or live demonstrations showing that when a user's role or privileges change, the session is prompted for re-authentication. Evidence that the system forces a re-entry of credentials for elevated operations or periodic time-based sessions.
   • Technical Artifacts: Configuration files, system policies, or backend parameters that define triggers for re-authentication (e.g., after a fixed idle period, role changes, or access to privileged functions). Detailed audit logs capturing re-authentication events with timestamps linked to specific triggering events.
   • Operational Patterns: Regular log entries indicating re-authentication prompts at defined intervals or after specific events. Observations of how session tokens are invalidated upon security category changes, and how automated testing tools simulate events leading to re-authentication.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Fake Compliance: Organizations may simply display a policy stating that re-authentication is required without configuring systems accordingly—only implementing superficial login prompts during the initial access of an already authenticated session.
   • Surface-Level Implementations: Systems that only trigger re-authentication on login without dynamically enforcing it during role or credential changes, or for privileged actions.
   • Technical Gaps: Missing integration between session management and re-authentication triggers (e.g., session timeouts or privilege escalations not affecting active sessions) so that the control is referenced in documentation but not properly enforced in real time.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   • CHECK: Verify that a re-authentication prompt is triggered when a user’s role or authorizations change.
     WHY: This confirms that system configurations enforce a re-validation of credentials at critical moments beyond the initial login.
     HOW: Change a user’s role (or simulate a role escalation) and observe if the system forces re-authentication. Review audit logs for timestamps matching privilege change events and corresponding re-authentication events.
     FAIL: No re-authentication prompt is observed, and associated log entries for role changes lack any re-authentication events.

   • CHECK: Confirm the enforcement of re-authentication after a fixed time period or upon session inactivity.
     WHY: Demonstrates that the system does not maintain indefinitely active sessions and that timeout settings trigger credential re-entry.
     HOW: After logging in, leave the session idle past the defined inactivity threshold or timeout period and verify that the session requires re-authentication to resume. Check system configuration settings and audit logs for timeout triggers.
     FAIL: The session remains active without prompting re-authentication after the required period, or logs lack timeout-specific re-authentication entries.

   • CHECK: Validate that privileged operations trigger an immediate re-authentication challenge.
     WHY: Ensures that sensitive or privileged actions require the user to confirm identity again, reducing risk from session hijacking or misuse.
     HOW: Execute a task that requires elevated permissions (if accessible in a test environment) and verify that the system calls for a re-authentication step before the action is carried out. Confirm through both the UI behavior and logged events.
     FAIL: Privileged actions complete without demanding additional credentials or corresponding re-authentication log entries are missing.

   • CHECK: Inspect system and security logs for automated records that capture re-authentication events across various triggers.
     WHY: Correlating events across logs provides strong evidence that re-authentication controls are operational and not just theoretical.
     HOW: Use centralized log management tools (like SIEM) to search for events tagged with re-authentication actions (e.g., session re-keying, password re-entry prompts, forced logouts due to role changes). Verify frequency and timestamps correlate with expected control triggers.
     FAIL: Logs show sparse or no re-authentication events even during periods when policy events (role changes, timeouts) should occur, suggesting improper configuration.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Verification Shortcuts: Use automation scripts to simulate role changes and session timeouts, then review logs via centralized SIEM tools. This helps quickly identify if the re-authentication mechanism is actively enforced.
   • Critical Indicators: Pay close attention to edge cases like concurrent sessions and caching systems where re-authentication could be inadvertently bypassed. Ensure that re-auth events are triggered as per policy even under load.
   • Details that Matter: The granularity of audit logs (i.e., timestamp accuracy, user identifiers) is essential. Look for logs that clearly correlate triggering events with re-auth challenges. Additionally, review if any administrative overrides allow bypassing the control under specific conditions, posing a security risk.

This methodology emphasizes practical, observable evidence rather than mere documentation, ensuring that the re-authentication control not only exists on paper but is actually enforced in operational environments.
content:
Statement:
Require users to re-authenticate when [Assignment: organization-defined circumstances or situations requiring re-authentication].

Guidance:
In addition to the re-authentication requirements associated with device locks, organizations may require re-authentication of individuals in certain situations, including when roles, authorizations or credentials change, when security categories of systems change, when the execution of privileged functions occurs, after a fixed time period, or periodically.

title: ADAPTIVE AUTHENTICATION
methodology:
Adaptive Authentication (IA-10) Validation Methodology

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   - Visible Evidence: Logs or dashboards showing that users are challenged with additional authentication steps when predefined conditions are met. For example, alerts in the SIEM or access management tool that reference extra MFA events triggered by abnormal behavior.
   - Technical Artifacts: Configuration files or system policies specifying risk thresholds, supplemental authentication triggers, and integration points with the authentication system. Artifact examples include adaptive authentication rule sets and trigger logs showing extra MFA challenges.
   - Operational Patterns: Observed patterns where user sessions flagged as abnormal (unusual IP addresses, atypical access patterns, elevated data access rates) lead to real-time multi-factor or supplemental challenges being deployed.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   - Simulated Compliance: Organizations may only document the existence of adaptive authentication rules without actually enforcing them, or may enable static MFA challenges that don’t adapt based on risk indicators.
   - Superficial Implementations: Relying solely on policy documents without implementing dynamic triggers. For example, simply having a configuration file without integrated logging and runtime checks, or static challenge mechanisms that don’t scale with risk factors.
   - Technical Gaps: Incomplete integration between the monitoring systems (e.g., SIEM, user behavior analytics) and authentication systems. This gap leads to a situation where abnormal behavior isn’t effectively triggering supplemental authentication steps.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK 1: Verify Adaptive Challenge Log Entries
- WHY: Active logging of additional authentication challenges under suspicious circumstances proves the dynamic control is operational.
- HOW: 
  a. Query system or SIEM logs for entries that capture additional authentication events (e.g., supplementary MFA prompts) triggered by abnormal login patterns.
  b. Correlate these entries with events that meet the adaptive trigger thresholds (such as accessing systems from unusual IPs or at unusual times).
- FAIL: Lack of log entries tying supplemental authentication events to suspicious activity, or logs that only show standard MFA processes without any adaptive elements.

CHECK 2: Review Configuration Settings and Rule Sets
- WHY: Existence of clearly defined rules and thresholds in configuration files or system policies demonstrates that the adaptive logic is set to trigger as designed.
- HOW: 
  a. Inspect system configuration files, access management policies, or network device settings to locate adaptive authentication rules (e.g., triggered by abnormal access behaviors).
  b. Verify that these rules include parameters for what constitutes suspicious behavior (e.g., unusual network addresses, quantity of accessed records) and the associated supplemental authentication steps.
- FAIL: Rule sets or configuration files missing dynamic thresholds or only containing static authentication requirements without any risk-assessment triggers.

CHECK 3: Conduct a Controlled Suspicious Behavior Test
- WHY: Testing with simulated abnormal behavior validates the operational effectiveness of the adaptive authentication control in real-time.
- HOW: 
  a. Use a test account to simulate suspicious activities (e.g., logging in from an unexpected IP address, accessing high volumes of data, or attempting access outside typical hours).
  b. Observe if the system responds by prompting for additional authentication elements.
  c. Check network and SIEM logs to confirm that the triggered event correctly initiated a supplemental challenge.
- FAIL: If the test account does not receive an additional authentication prompt or if logs do not show any correlation between the simulated suspicious behavior and adaptive challenge events.

CHECK 4: Validate System Integration and Alerting
- WHY: Confirming the integration between monitoring systems (SIEM/user activity analytics) and the adaptive authentication mechanism ensures that risk signals are effectively enforced.
- HOW: 
  a. Review integration documentation and perform an end-to-end test to see how anomalous behavior is reported from the SIEM into the access management system.
  b. Check for automated alerts or dashboard counters that record adaptive authentication events.
- FAIL: Lack of integration between behavioral monitoring tools and the adaptive authentication system, or missing alert notifications when risk triggers occur.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   - Shortcut: Quickly review the SIEM dashboard for spikes or irregular patterns of multi-factor authentication challenges, which is an immediate indicator of adaptive authentication in action.
   - Critical Indicator: The correlation between user behavioral anomalies and the enforcement of supplemental authentication is paramount. Ensure that the logs not only show challenges but also detail the triggering event.
   - What Matters Most: The real-time responsiveness of the authentication system to behavioral triggers. Check both the configuration (what should happen) and the logged evidence (what did happen) to confirm true adaptive functionality.

By focusing on the above practical and operational checks, auditors can effectively assess whether adaptive authentication is not just documented, but is actively and correctly enforcing enhanced authentication measures under risk conditions.
content:
Statement:
Require individuals accessing the system to employ [Assignment: organization-defined supplemental authentication techniques or mechanisms] under specific [Assignment: organization-defined circumstances or situations].

Guidance:
Adversaries may compromise individual authentication mechanisms employed by organizations and subsequently attempt to impersonate legitimate users. To address this threat, organizations may employ specific techniques or mechanisms and establish protocols to assess suspicious behavior. Suspicious behavior may include accessing information that individuals do not typically access as part of their duties, roles, or responsibilities; accessing greater quantities of information than individuals would routinely access; or attempting to access information from suspicious network addresses. When pre-established conditions or triggers occur, organizations can require individuals to provide additional authentication information. Another potential use for adaptive authentication is to increase the strength of mechanism based on the number or types of records being accessed. Adaptive authentication does not replace and is not used to avoid the use of multi-factor authentication mechanisms but can augment implementations of multi-factor authentication.

title: SERVICE IDENTIFICATION AND AUTHENTICATION
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Unique identification tokens (certificates, digital signatures, provenance graphs) are visibly embedded within service configuration files and connection logs.
• Cryptographic artifacts (code signing certificates, service-specific keys) are stored and used during service communications.
• Operational logs demonstrate that services are only activated after a valid authentication exchange with external or internal services.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may reuse generic certificates across multiple services rather than generating unique identities per service, creating a facade of identification.
• Relying solely on documented policies without verifying that technical mechanisms (e.g., mutual TLS) are actively enforced.
• Instituting superficial checks (e.g., static information or self-signed certs with broadly distributed keys) that do not offer strong service-to-service authentication.

VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Verify that each service is configured with a unique digital certificate or cryptographic key used exclusively for authentication before communication.
WHY: Unique cryptographic identities prove that services are distinctly recognized and authenticated in transit, rather than using shared or generic credentials.
HOW: Inspect service configuration files, certificate repositories, and logs for use of unique digital certificates or keys; verify that mutual authentication (e.g., mutual TLS) is in place for service communications.
FAIL: Discovery of reused, generic, or missing certificates/keys in service configurations, or logging evidence of non-authenticated connections.

CHECK: Inspect logs and audit trails to confirm that authentication events occur prior to communication establishment.
WHY: Operational logs provide observable evidence that authentication procedures are enforced actively rather than just being documented.
HOW: Use automated log analysis tools to review connection initiation logs linking authentication events with service start-up; verify timestamp correlations to ensure the authentication precedes operational communication.
FAIL: Absence of pre-communication authentication records or logs showing communications established before proper authentication.

CHECK: Validate the separation of service identification/authentication decisions from the services performing actions based on those decisions, particularly in distributed architectures.
WHY: Separating decision logic reduces attack surface and showcases robust operational controls with independent verification of identities.
HOW: Review network topology diagrams, authentication proxy configurations, and audit logs to establish that a dedicated service or component performs identity validation before passing claims to other services.
FAIL: Findings where a single service both authenticates and acts upon its own identity claim, particularly if there’s no boundary between identity verification and functional service.

CHECK: Assess the use of electronic signatures or provenance metadata that trace the code or service origin.
WHY: These elements ensure integrity of the service's identity, confirming that the origin of service code or certificates is verified.
HOW: Inspect service binaries and deployment logs for attached electronic signatures or provenance data; use cryptographic checking tools to validate these signatures.
FAIL: Absence of such metadata or the presence of invalid/expired electronic signatures.

EXPERT TIPS
• Look for misconfigurations where services inadvertently bypass mutual authentication, such as fallback to non-secure protocols.
• Prioritize evidence from automated monitoring systems and cryptographic validation tools over manual document review since service logs and configurations are harder to fake.
• Consider that unique identifiers must not only exist, but their operational enforcement should be evident in real-time traffic and in periodic audits.
• Critical indicators of poor implementation include reused credentials, absence of timestamped authentication events, and reliance on vague policy documentation without technical backing.
• Use sample disruptions in test environments to prove that failure in proper authentication halts service communications, thereby verifying operational effectiveness.
content:
Statement:
Uniquely identify and authenticate [Assignment: organization-defined system services and applications] before establishing communications with devices, users, or other services or applications.

Guidance:
Services that may require identification and authentication include web applications using digital certificates or services or applications that query a database. Identification and authentication methods for system services and applications include information or code signing, provenance graphs, and electronic signatures that indicate the sources of services. Decisions regarding the validity of identification and authentication claims can be made by services separate from the services acting on those decisions. This can occur in distributed system architectures. In such situations, the identification and authentication decisions (instead of actual identifiers and authentication data) are provided to the services that need to act on those decisions.

Enhancements:
(1) SERVICE IDENTIFICATION AND AUTHENTICATION | INFORMATION EXCHANGE
[Withdrawn: Incorporated into IA-9]

title: IDENTIFICATION AND AUTHENTICATION (NON-ORGANIZATIONAL USERS)
methodology:
Below is a detailed validation methodology for IA-8 – Identification and Authentication (Non-Organizational Users).

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence includes system configurations that enforce unique identifiers and authentication methods (e.g., non-organizational user accounts are separated from internal accounts, and login events are logged with unique user IDs). 
• Technical artifacts include authentication server settings, system logs showing successful authentication events with non-org credentials, and configuration files listing accepted external authenticators (e.g., PIV, PIV-I or NIST-compliant tokens).
• Operational patterns such as periodic reconciliation of non-organizational user access, routine credential verification procedures, and automated alerts for suspicious authentication events indicate that the control is actively enforced.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may rely solely on policy or documentation stating that non-organizational users are uniquely identified, but without technical enforcement in system configurations or logs.
• Surface-level implementations include creating shared or generic non-org accounts that are not uniquely tied to a person or process, or accepting external authenticators without proper integration and runtime validation.
• Frequently missed technical gaps include failure to log non-org authentications or to maintain updated lists of approved external authenticators, making it difficult to verify if authentication mechanisms (like PIV/PIV-I credentials) are actually used.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK 1: Verify System Configuration for Unique Identifier Assignment
WHY: Ensuring each non-organizational user or process is assigned a unique identifier is a cornerstone for proper authentication and accountability.
HOW: Access system configuration files or identity management platforms; review settings that differentiate non-org user login mechanisms from organizational accounts and confirm that identifiers are non-generic. Use command-line queries or configuration management tools to extract these entries.
FAIL: Discovery of shared or generic credentials for non-org users or configuration files lacking unique identifier settings.

CHECK 2: Review Authentication Logs and Audit Trails
WHY: Logs showing distinct authentication events for non-organizational users indicate active enforcement of unique identity verification.
HOW: Collect and analyze authentication logs from an authentication server or SIEM tool. Look for entries that demonstrate unique user IDs, timestamps, and evidence of multi-factor checks (e.g., PIV/PIV-I authentications where applicable).
FAIL: Logs showing non-specific, aggregated, or missing non-org user authentication events, or absence of multi-factor authentication details.

CHECK 3: Validate Integration and Acceptance of NIST-Compliant External Authenticators
WHY: Acceptance of approved external authenticators such as PIV, PIV-I—or other NIST-compliant credentials—demonstrates compliance with defined external authentication requirements.
HOW: Review system configurations, certificate stores, or identity management policies to ensure only approved external authenticators are allowed; test by simulating an authentication attempt with an accepted external credential. Verify that systems reference the current list of NIST-compliant authenticators.
FAIL: Finding outdated policies, unapproved external credentials, or systems that accept credentials outside the verified list of authenticators.

CHECK 4: Confirm Policy Enforcement for Federated Authentication and Identity Mapping
WHY: Proper enforcement of federated identity controls (e.g., PIV-I acceptance or identifier disassociability techniques) is critical to protect privacy and ensure interoperability.
HOW: Inspect federation service configuration and identity mapping tables; validate that techniques (like cryptographic blinding, mapping tables) to disassociate identities are active. Review test cases where identities from external providers do not leak internally sensitive information.
FAIL: Evidence of direct, un-mapped identity attributes linking external authentication transactions to internal profiles, or missing mapping mechanisms.

CHECK 5: Cross-Check Documentation Against Real-World Implementation
WHY: Authentic controls are verified by direct evidence in system behavior rather than just documented controls.
HOW: Interview system administrators and perform spot-checks of system settings. Compare documented authentication procedures with live system responses (for example, by performing live tests using known non-organizational credentials and examining resultant logs and access behavior).
FAIL: Significant discrepancies between documented procedures and live system configurations or authentication behavior.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Verification shortcuts: Use centralized logging/monitoring tools (such as SIEM) to rapidly extract lists of non-organizational user login events and check for unique identifiers. Pay close attention to whether MFA, especially when PIV or PIV-I is expected, is recorded in logs.
• Critical indicators of poor implementation include generic non-org usernames, absent or incomplete audit trails, and reliance on static documentation without periodic technical reassessment.
• Details matter: Ensure that the list of accepted external authenticators is current and that identity mapping/disassociation techniques are properly configured, as these are often overlooked during periodic reviews.

Following these checks and steps provides a robust and practical method to verify that non-organizational users are uniquely identified and authenticated in compliance with IA-8.
content:
Statement:
Uniquely identify and authenticate non-organizational users or processes acting on behalf of non-organizational users.

Guidance:
Non-organizational users include system users other than organizational users explicitly covered by IA-2. Non-organizational users are uniquely identified and authenticated for accesses other than those explicitly identified and documented in AC-14. Identification and authentication of non-organizational users accessing federal systems may be required to protect federal, proprietary, or privacy-related information (with exceptions noted for national security systems). Organizations consider many factors—including security, privacy, scalability, and practicality—when balancing the need to ensure ease of use for access to federal information and systems with the need to protect and adequately mitigate risk.

Enhancements:
IDENTIFICATION AND AUTHENTICATION (NON-ORGANIZATIONAL USERS) | ACCEPTANCE OF PIV CREDENTIALS FROM OTHER AGENCIES
Accept and electronically verify Personal Identity Verification-compliant credentials from other federal agencies.

Discussion: Acceptance of Personal Identity Verification (PIV) credentials from other federal agencies applies to both logical and physical access control systems. PIV credentials are those credentials issued by federal agencies that conform to FIPS Publication 201 and supporting guidelines. The adequacy and reliability of PIV cards are addressed and authorized using [SP 800-79-2].

Related Controls: PE-3.
IDENTIFICATION AND AUTHENTICATION (NON-ORGANIZATIONAL USERS) | ACCEPTANCE OF EXTERNAL AUTHENTICATORS
(a) Accept only external authenticators that are NIST-compliant; and  
b) Document and maintain a list of accepted external authenticators.

Discussion: Acceptance of only NIST-compliant external authenticators applies to organizational systems that are accessible to the public (e.g., public-facing websites). External authenticators are issued by nonfederal government entities and are compliant with [SP 800-63B]. Approved external authenticators meet or exceed the minimum Federal Government-wide technical, security, privacy, and organizational maturity requirements. Meeting or exceeding Federal requirements allows Federal Government relying parties to trust external authenticators in connection with an authentication transaction at a specified authenticator assurance level.

Related Controls: None.
IDENTIFICATION AND AUTHENTICATION (NON-ORGANIZATIONAL USERS) | USE OF FICAM-APPROVED PRODUCTS
[Withdrew: Incorporated into IA-8(2).]
IDENTIFICATION AND AUTHENTICATION (NON-ORGANIZATIONAL USERS) | USE OF DEFINED PROFILES
Conform to the following profiles for identity management [Assignment: organization-defined identity management profiles].

Discussion: Organizations define profiles for identity management based on open identity management standards. To ensure that open identity management standards are viable, robust, reliable, sustainable, and interoperable as documented, the Federal Government assesses and scopes the standards and technology implementations against applicable laws, executive orders, directives, policies, regulations, standards, and guidelines.

Related Controls: None.
IDENTIFICATION AND AUTHENTICATION (NON-ORGANIZATIONAL USERS) | ACCEPTANCE OF PIV-I CREDENTIALS
Accept and verify federated or PKI credentials that meet [Assignment: organization-defined policy].

Discussion: Acceptance of PIV-I credentials can be implemented by PIV, PIV-I, and other commercial or external identity providers. The acceptance and verification of PIV-I credentials apply to both logical and physical access control systems. The acceptance and verification of PIV-I credentials address non-federal issuers of identity cards that desire to interoperate with United States Government PIV systems and that can be trusted by Federal Government-relying parties. The X.509 certificate policy for the Federal Bridge Certification Authority (FBCA) addresses PIV-I requirements. The PIV-I card is commensurate with the PIV credentials as defined in cited references. PIV-I credentials are the credentials issued by a PIV-I provider whose PIV-I certificate policy maps to the Federal Bridge PIV-I Certificate Policy. A PIV-I provider is cross-certified with the FBCA (directly or through another PKI bridge) with policies that have been mapped and approved as meeting the requirements of the PIV-I policies defined in the FBCA certificate policy.

Related Controls: None.
IDENTIFICATION AND AUTHENTICATION (NON-ORGANIZATIONAL USERS) | DISASSOCIABILITY
Implement the following measures to disassociate user attributes or identifier assertion relationships among individuals, credential service providers, and relying parties: [Assignment: organization-defined measures].

Discussion: Federated identity solutions can create increased privacy risks due to the tracking and profiling of individuals. Using identifier mapping tables or cryptographic techniques to blind credential service providers and relying parties from each other or to make identity attributes less visible to transmitting parties can reduce the privacy risks.

Related Controls: None.

title: CRYPTOGRAPHIC MODULE AUTHENTICATION
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Look for audit logs and system dashboards that display cryptographic module access events, including operator authentication attempts and successes. Check that cryptographic modules are distinctly labeled (e.g., FIPS-approved modules) and that they enforce operator authentication prior to role assumption.
• Technical Artifacts: Examine system configuration files and cryptographic module settings that define authorized user roles. Look for configuration artifacts such as key management server configurations, authentication tokens tied to roles, and secure boot logs indicating authentication steps. Monitor log files for entries showing time-stamped authentication events by the module.
• Operational Patterns: Regular review of authentication logs, periodic cryptographic module audits, and automated alerts for authentication failures underscore an active, well-integrated control.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Faking Compliance: Organizations sometimes implement superficial authentication mechanisms that only require a static password or use default credentials that remain unchanged, leading to minimal actual protection. They may also produce generic logs that do not tie authentication events to the unique identities of operators.
• Surface-Level Failures: Merely having a documented authentication policy without enforcing it within the cryptographic module can give a false sense of security. Relying exclusively on external system login events rather than module-specific authentication logs can also indicate a gap in proper implementation.
• Technical Gaps: Omitting role verification or neglecting to enforce multi-factor authentication (when required) can allow operators to assume roles without proper checks. Failure to integrate cryptographic module logs with centralized SIEM makes it difficult to verify real-time enforcement.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Verify that the cryptographic module enforces operator authentication with unique credentials and, when applicable, multi-factor authentication.
   WHY: This proves that the module is not only present but actively enforcing identity verification as per regulatory or policy requirements.
   HOW: Inspect the module’s configuration settings, review authentication logs for evidence of multi-factor steps, and test access with valid and invalid credentials.
   FAIL: If the module accepts default credentials, a simple password, or shows no evidence of multi-factor authentication features, this indicates a failure.

2. CHECK: Correlate the cryptographic module’s authentication logs with centralized logging/monitoring systems.
   WHY: This confirms the operational effectiveness of the authentication mechanisms, ensuring that all authentication events are captured, monitored, and reviewed regularly.
   HOW: Audit the logs from the module and verify correct integration with a SIEM or log management system. Look for time-synchronized entries that match expected authentication activity patterns.
   FAIL: Missing, unsynchronized, or generic log entries that fail to capture unique operator details suggest a gap in effective logging and monitoring.

3. CHECK: Test role-based access enforcement by attempting to access restricted cryptographic module functions with a user having lower privileges.
   WHY: This demonstrates that authentication not only verifies identity but also enforces role authorization, ensuring operators cannot perform unauthorized functions.
   HOW: Perform controlled tests where a non-privileged account attempts role-sensitive operations and verify that access attempts are denied. Check logs for rejection events.
   FAIL: If unauthorized roles can access functions or if log entries do not record such an attempt, it indicates poor role-based enforcement of authentication.

4. CHECK: Review the configuration management records for the cryptographic module to ensure that authentication configurations are regularly verified and updated.
   WHY: Regular reviews ensure that authentication mechanisms are maintained, secure, and compliant with evolving regulatory requirements.
   HOW: Examine change management documentation and configuration baselines to ensure updates correlate with periodic security reviews. Confirm by checking timestamps and audit reports.
   FAIL: Lack of documentation or evidence of outdated configurations could signal an operational risk.

EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Automated log analysis tools can quickly verify that all authentication events are tagged with unique operator IDs; any generic tags warrant deeper investigation.
• Critical Indicator: Consistently failed authentication attempts that are not flagged or investigated may indicate that the control is not correctly integrated.
• Detail Matters: Cross-reference the authentication module’s settings with the cryptographic module’s accepted list of roles; inconsistencies here are often a red flag for misconfigurations.
• Verify Integration: Ensure the cryptographic module’s authentication logs are integrated with enterprise-level monitoring to detect real-time anomalies, emphasizing operational effectiveness over mere policy documentation.

This methodology emphasizes observable, technical, and operational evidence over static documentation to ensure that the control is implemented authentically and effectively.
content:
Statement:
Implement mechanisms for authentication to a cryptographic module that meet the requirements of applicable laws, executive orders, directives, policies, regulations, standards, and guidelines for such authentication.

Guidance:
Authentication mechanisms may be required within a cryptographic module to authenticate an operator accessing the module and to verify that the operator is authorized to assume the requested role and perform services within that role.

title: AUTHENTICATION FEEDBACK
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence: During the authentication process, the password input field displays a series of asterisks or similar mask symbols. This behavior is observable through a live login demo or screenshots from various devices (desktop and mobile).
• Technical artifacts: The underlying HTML code for web applications should have the input field set with type='password', and mobile applications should invoke controlled input dialogs with obfuscation properties. Debugging tools and source code reviews can confirm this.
• Operational patterns: Consistency in feedback—each time a password is entered, no plaintext characters are shown, and any textual feedback is ephemeral, disappearing promptly if displayed at all.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Superficial compliance: Organizations may claim that the input is masked (e.g., showing asterisks) but might still inadvertently display hints (like the number of characters entered or timing delays that can be exploited).
• Incomplete implementations: Some systems provide proper masking in one interface (e.g., desktop) but not in others (e.g., mobile), or they rely on custom components that mimic masking without using standard secure mechanisms.
• Technical gaps: Error messages or authentication indicators that reveal too much information about the verification process (e.g., showing exactly which part of the input was incorrect) may also undermine proper mask implementation, even if the characters are physically obscured.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Verification of Input Field Masking
   WHY: Ensuring that as soon as a user types in their password, characters are replaced with asterisks (or similar) confirms that direct feedback is obscured.
   HOW: Perform a live test in the login interface on both desktop and mobile environments. Type a sample password and observe that every character is masked immediately with no plaintext exposure or detailed feedback.
   FAIL: If any portion of the password is displayed in clear text or if the system shows hints such as the number of characters typed.

2. CHECK: Front-End Code Inspection
   WHY: Reviewing the code behind the password field confirms that established best practices are followed (using input type='password').
   HOW: Use browser developer tools or mobile code inspection tools to check that the input field for passwords is correctly configured (e.g., in HTML, <input type="password">). Also, verify that any custom implementations do not inadvertently log or display the raw input.
   FAIL: The input field is improperly configured (e.g., set to type='text') or uses custom code that bypasses standard controls without proper masking mechanisms.

3. CHECK: Evaluation of Error Messaging
   WHY: Even if the input itself is masked, detailed authentication feedback can inadvertently provide hints to an attacker (e.g., indicating exactly how many characters are correct).
   HOW: Enter deliberately invalid credentials and observe the error messages. Confirm that the system only responds with a generic error message that does not imply which part of the input was wrong or provide timing/length hints.
   FAIL: Error messages indicate specifics such as the exact character count, parts of the credential that are correct, or other feedback that could aid an unauthorized individual.

4. CHECK: Cross-Platform Consistency Assessment
   WHY: Different devices may implement authentication input differently. Verifying across all platforms ensures that the control is uniformly effective regardless of device type or screen size.
