   HOW: Test the authentication process on multiple client types (e.g., desktop, mobile, tablet). Confirm that all interfaces use obscured feedback appropriately and that there are no discrepancies in behavior.
   FAIL: Inconsistencies in masking behavior across platforms, such as one interface displaying partial clear text or not obscuring feedback appropriately due to device limitations.

EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Direct interactive testing across devices is often more reliable than simply reviewing documentation or code. Use live sessions to monitor how the UI responds.
• Critical Indicator: Consistency in both visual and error feedback design. Even a small detail—such as a delay before the password is masked or an overly specific error message—can indicate a vulnerability.
• Details Matter: Check for any logging or debugging outputs that might accidentally capture unmasked input data. Ensure that both the front-end and back-end processes enforce obscuration throughout every stage of the authentication process.
• Experience Insight: Be wary of custom ‘enhancements’ to the basic password mask. Sometimes, additional features may introduce unintentional feedback loops or technical gaps that an attacker could exploit.

Overall, the effective implementation of IA-6 (Authentication Feedback) is evidenced by consistently obscured input and uniform error messaging without data leakage, validated through practical tests rather than solely relying on written policies.
content:
Statement:
Obscure feedback of authentication information during the authentication process to protect the information from possible exploitation and use by unauthorized individuals.

Guidance:
Authentication feedback from systems does not provide information that would allow unauthorized individuals to compromise authentication mechanisms. For some types of systems, such as desktops or notebooks with relatively large monitors, the threat (referred to as shoulder surfing) may be significant. For other types of systems, such as mobile devices with small displays, the threat may be less significant and is balanced against the increased likelihood of typographic input errors due to small keyboards. Thus, the means for obscuring authentication feedback is selected accordingly. Obscuring authentication feedback includes displaying asterisks when users type passwords into input devices or displaying feedback for a very limited time before obscuring it.

title: AUTHENTICATOR MANAGEMENT
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for IA-5 (Authenticator Management) that emphasizes observable, technical evidence of implementation rather than relying solely on documentation.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence includes system configuration screens showing that default authenticators (e.g., default passwords, pre-provisioned keys) have been replaced prior to deployment. Screenshots from administrative consoles, logs from authenticator issuance systems, and real-time monitoring of authentication events are key indicators.
• Technical artifacts include configuration files enforcing minimum strength criteria (e.g., password length, complexity), logs capturing the full lifecycle of authenticator issuance, change, revocation, and PKI certificate validation. Artifacts such as revised certificate chains and key binding audit trails are also used.
• Operational patterns include periodic password refresh events, automated rejection of commonly used or compromised passages, and proven responses to reported incidents (lost, compromised, or damaged authenticators) reflected in incident response logs.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may simply provide policy documentation or screenshots, but not actual evidence of enforced changes such as not replacing default credentials or not regularly refreshing passwords.
• Surface-level implementations include having a password policy written in documentation while the actual system configuration relies on weak hash functions, insecure channels for transmitting passwords, or retained default credentials.
• Technical gaps are often observed when revocation procedures or authenticator loss responses are not fully integrated into automated auditing systems. Some organizations hide behind manual processes that fail to cover all authenticators (e.g., group accounts) or ignore dynamic authentication events.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Verify Identity Proofing for Authenticator Distribution
WHY: Ensuring that initial authenticator distribution is tied to a verified identity underpins trust in any subsequent authentication.
HOW: Select a sample of authenticator issuance records; review audit logs or system alerts that capture verification of identity (for individuals, groups, services, or devices) during registration. Confirm that identity proofing steps (e.g., in-person verification or trusted external registration) are logged and cross-referenced with organizational identity management systems.
FAIL: Missing or incomplete logs, or lack of evidence that the identity of the receiver was verified prior to receiving the authenticator.

CHECK: Confirm Replacement of Default and Factory Credentials
WHY: Changing out default authorization credentials prevents attackers from using well-known values.
HOW: Inspect system configuration settings and installation checklists. Look for records that document changes to default credentials (e.g., factory-set passwords replaced) prior to first use; use automated configuration management tools to verify that no default values remain active.
FAIL: Discovery of factory-default credentials in system settings or audit logs, or absence of scheduled tasks/checks for replacement.

CHECK: Inspect Authenticator Strength Controls and Regular Refresh Processes
WHY: Strong authenticator mechanisms (passwords, certificates, biometrics) enforce resistance to attacks.
HOW: Query system parameters for password policy (minimum lengths, complexity rules, use of salted key derivation) and review configuration for expiration and refresh settings. For public key-based authentications, check PKI certificate management logs that include trust store updates and certificate revocation checks.
FAIL: Evidence of weak or static authenticator parameters, absence of automated refresh events, or untracked certificate validity status.

CHECK: Validate Revocation and Loss/Compromise Handling Procedures
WHY: A robust process for lost, compromised, or damaged authenticators minimizes exposure to ongoing risk.
HOW: Examine incident response logs and remediation tickets that show authenticators being revoked following reporting. Follow a chain-of-custody review for authenticator deactivation requests to ensure that procedures (including immediate disabling and reissue) were enforced.
FAIL: Lack of incident entries related to lost or compromised authenticators, or case studies showing delays in revocation; evidence indicating procedures exist only on paper.

CHECK: For Group/Role and Dynamic Credential Binding, Ensure Timely Credential Updates
WHY: When account memberships or roles change, failure to update shared credentials can leave the systems vulnerable to abuse.
HOW: Audit change management logs for group and role accounts; verify that authenticator changes (password resets or key reissue) occur immediately on role changes. Validate that there is dynamic binding evidence between user identity and authenticator (e.g., in centralized authentication logs or via automated directory services).
FAIL: Logs showing persistent use of outdated authenticators, or signs that group account credentials were not refreshed upon membership changes.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut Validation: Use automated configuration management and log analysis tools to quickly scan for default credential instances and verify regular password/credential refresh cycles without relying solely on manual review.
• Critical Indicators: Pay close attention to audit logs and incident response times—if a system’s logs do not clearly capture successful authenticator replacement or revocation events, the implementation might be superficial.
• Details Matter: Ensure that technical controls such as PKI trust store updates, dynamic binding processes, and enforcement of authenticators in transit (via cryptographically protected channels) are configured at the system level. Look for evidence that operational security controls (e.g., liveness detection for biometrics, password manager usage) are actively deployed rather than documented only.
• Red Flags: Lack of automated alerts on authentication failures, continued use of default or weak authenticator content, or any bypass between policy and technical implementation should signal a poor level of control implementation.

This methodology focuses on gathering observable, system-generated evidence to verify that IA-5 is operationally effective, ensuring that authenticator management not only exists in documentation but is also actively enforced and monitored in practice.
content:
Statement:
Manage system authenticators by:  
a. Verifying, as part of the initial authenticator distribution, the identity of the individual, group, role, service, or device receiving the authenticator;  
b. Establishing initial authenticator content for any authenticators issued by the organization;  
c. Ensuring that authenticators have sufficient strength of mechanism for their intended use;  
d. Establishing and implementing administrative procedures for initial authenticator distribution, for lost or compromised or damaged authenticators, and for revoking authenticators;  
e. Changing default authenticators prior to first use;  
f. Changing or refreshing authenticators [Assignment: organization-defined time period by authenticator type] or when [Assignment: organization-defined events] occur;  
g. Protecting authenticator content from unauthorized disclosure and modification;  
h. Requiring individuals to take, and having devices implement, specific controls to protect authenticators; and  
i. Changing authenticators for group or role accounts when membership to those accounts changes.

Guidance:
Authenticators include passwords, cryptographic devices, biometrics, certificates, one-time password devices, and ID badges. Device authenticators include certificates and passwords. Initial authenticator content is the actual content of the authenticator (e.g., the initial password). In contrast, the requirements for authenticator content contain specific criteria or characteristics (e.g., minimum password length). Developers may deliver system components with factory default authentication credentials (i.e., passwords) to allow for initial installation and configuration. Default authentication credentials are often well known, easily discoverable, and present a significant risk. The requirement to protect individual authenticators may be implemented via control PL-4 or PS-6 for authenticators in the possession of individuals and by controls AC-3, AC-6, and SC-28 for authenticators stored in organizational systems, including passwords stored in hashed or encrypted formats or files containing encrypted or hashed passwords accessible with administrator privileges.

Systems support authenticator management by organization-defined settings and restrictions for various authenticator characteristics (e.g., minimum password length, validation time window for) <page_number>138<page_number>.

Enhancements:
AUTHENTICATOR MANAGEMENT | PASSWORD-BASED AUTHENTICATION
For password-based authentication:
(a) Maintain a list of commonly-used, expected, or compromised passwords and update the list [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] and when organizational passwords are suspected to have been compromised directly or indirectly;
(b) Verify, when users create or update passwords, that the passwords are not found on the list of commonly-used, expected, or compromised passwords in IA-5(1)(a);
(c) Transmit passwords only over cryptographically-protected channels;
(d) Store passwords using an approved salted key derivation function, preferably using a keyed hash;
(e) Require immediate selection of a new password upon account recovery;
(f) Allow user selection of long passwords and passphrases, including spaces and all printable characters;
(g) Employ automated tools to assist the user in selecting strong password authenticators;
(h) Enforce the following composition and complexity rules: [Assignment: organization-defined composition and complexity rules].

Discussion: Password-based authentication applies to passwords regardless of whether they are used in single-factor or multi-factor authentication. Long passwords or passphrases are preferable over shorter passwords. Enforced composition rules provide marginal security benefits while decreasing usability. However, organizations may choose to establish certain rules for password generation (e.g., minimum character length for long passwords) under certain circumstances and can enforce this requirement in IA-5(1)(h). Account recovery can occur, for example, in situations when a password is forgotten. Cryptographically protected passwords include salted one-way cryptographic hashes of passwords. The list of commonly used, compromised, or expected passwords includes passwords obtained from previous breach corpuses, dictionary words, and repetitive or sequential characters. The list includes context-specific words, such as the name of the service, username, and derivatives thereof.
AUTHENTICATOR MANAGEMENT | PUBLIC KEY-BASED AUTHENTICATION
(a) For public key-based authentication:
(1) Ensure authorized access to the corresponding private key; and 
(2) Map the authenticated identity to the account of the individual or group; and
(b) When public key infrastructure (PKI) is used:
(1) Validate certificates by constructing and verifying a certification path to an accepted trust anchor, including checking certificate status information; and
(2) Implement a local cache of revocation data to support path discovery and validation.

Related Controls: IA-6.
AUTHENTICATOR MANAGEMENT | IN-PERSON OR TRUSTED EXTERNAL PARTY REGISTRATION [Withdrawn: Incorporated into IA-12(4).]
AUTHENTICATOR MANAGEMENT | AUTOMATED SUPPORT FOR PASSWORD STRENGTH [Withdrawn: Incorporated into IA-5(1).]
AUTHENTICATOR MANAGEMENT | CHANGE AUTHENTICATORS PRIOR TO DELIVERY
Require developers and installers of system components to provide unique authenticators or change default authenticators prior to delivery and installation.

Discussion: Changing authenticators prior to the delivery and installation of system components extends the requirement for organizations to change default authenticators upon system installation by requiring developers and/or installers to provide unique authenticators or change default authenticators for system components prior to delivery and/or installation. However, it typically does not apply to developers of commercial off-the-shelf information technology products. Requirements for unique authenticators can be included in acquisition documents prepared by organizations when procuring systems or system components.

Related Controls: None.
AUTHENTICATOR MANAGEMENT | PROTECTION OF AUTHENTICATORS
Protect authenticators commensurate with the security category of the information to which use of the authenticator permits access.

Discussion: For systems that contain multiple security categories of information without reliable physical or logical separation between categories, authenticators used to grant access to the systems are protected commensurate with the highest security category of information on the systems. Security categories of information are determined as part of the security categorization process.

Related Controls: RA-2.
AUTHENTICATOR MANAGEMENT | NO EMBEDDED UNENCRYPTED STATIC AUTHENTICATORS
Ensure that unencrypted static authenticators are not embedded in applications or other forms of static storage.

Discussion: In addition to applications, other forms of static storage include access scripts and function keys. Organizations exercise caution when determining whether embedded or stored authenticators are in encrypted or unencrypted form. If authenticators are used in the manner stored, then those representations are considered unencrypted authenticators.

Related Controls: None.
AUTHENTICATOR MANAGEMENT | MULTIPLE SYSTEM ACCOUNTS
Implement [Assignment: organization-defined security controls] to manage the risk of compromise due to individuals having accounts on multiple systems.

Discussion: When individuals have accounts on multiple systems and use the same authenticators such as passwords, there is the risk that a compromise of one account may lead to the compromise of other accounts. Alternative approaches include having different authenticators (passwords) on all systems, employing a single sign-on or federation mechanism, or using some form of one-time passwords on all systems. Organizations can also use rules of behavior (see PL-4) and access agreements (see PS-6) to mitigate the risk of multiple system accounts.

Related Controls: PS-6.
AUTHENTICATOR MANAGEMENT | FEDERATED CREDENTIAL MANAGEMENT
Use the following external organizations to federate credentials: [Assignment: organization-defined external organizations].

Discussion: Federation provides organizations with the capability to authenticate individuals and devices when conducting cross-organization activities involving the processing, storage, or transmission of information. Using a specific list of approved external organizations for authentication helps to ensure that those organizations are vetted and trusted.

Related Controls: AU-7, AU-16.
AUTHENTICATOR MANAGEMENT | DYNAMIC CREDENTIAL BINDING
Bind identities and authenticators dynamically using the following rules: [Assignment: organization-defined binding rules].

Discussion: Authentication requires some form of binding between an identity and the authenticator that is used to confirm the identity. In conventional approaches, binding is established by pre-provisioning both the identity and the authenticator to the system. For example, the binding between a username (i.e., identity) and a password (i.e., authenticator) is accomplished by provisioning the identity and authenticator as a pair in the system. New authentication techniques allow the binding between the identity and the authenticator to be implemented external to a system. For example, with smartcard credentials, the identity and authenticator are bound together on the smartcard. Using these credentials, systems can authenticate identities that have not been pre-provisioned, dynamically provisioning the identity after authentication. In these situations, organizations can anticipate the dynamic provisioning of identities. Pre-established trust relationships and mechanisms with appropriate authorities to validate identities and related credentials are essential.

Related Controls: AU-16, IA-5.
AUTHENTICATOR MANAGEMENT | HARDWARE TOKEN-BASED AUTHENTICATION
[Withdrawn: Incorporated into IA-2(1) and IA-2(2).]
AUTHENTICATOR MANAGEMENT | BIOMETRIC AUTHENTICATION PERFORMANCE
For biometric-based authentication, employ mechanisms that satisfy the following biometric quality requirements [Assignment: organization-defined biometric quality requirements].

Discussion: Unlike password-based authentication, which provides exact matches of user-input passwords to stored passwords, biometric authentication does not provide exact matches. Depending on the type of biometric and the type of collection mechanism, there is likely to be some divergence from the presented biometric and the stored biometric that serves as the basis for comparison. Matching performance is the rate at which a biometric algorithm correctly results in a match for a genuine user and rejects other users. Biometric performance requirements include the match rate, which reflects the accuracy of the biometric matching algorithm used by a system.

Related Controls: AC-7.
AUTHENTICATOR MANAGEMENT | EXPIRATION OF CACHED AUTHENTICATORS
Prohibit the use of cached authenticators after [Assignment: organization-defined time period].

Discussion: Cached authenticators are used to authenticate to the local machine when the network is not available. If cached authentication information is out of date, the validity of the authentication information may be questionable.

Related Controls: None.
AUTHENTICATOR MANAGEMENT | MANAGING CONTENT OF PKI TRUST STORES
For PKI-based authentication, employ an organization-wide methodology for managing the content of PKI trust stores installed across all platforms, including networks, operating systems, browsers, and applications.

Discussion: An organization-wide methodology for managing the content of PKI trust stores helps improve the accuracy and currency of PKI-based authentication credentials across the organization.

Related Controls: None.
AUTHENTICATOR MANAGEMENT | GSA-APPROVED PRODUCTS AND SERVICES
Use only General Services Administration-approved products and services for identity, credential, and access management.

Discussion: General Services Administration (GSA)-approved products and services are products and services that have been approved through the GSA conference program, where applicable, and posted to the GSA Approved Products List. GSA provides guidance for teams to design and build functional and secure systems that comply with Federal Identity, Credential, and Access Management (FICAM) policies, technologies, and implementation patterns.

Related Controls: None.
AUTHENTICATOR MANAGEMENT | IN-PERSON OR TRUSTED EXTERNAL PARTY AUTHENTICATOR ISSUANCE
Require that the issuance of [Assignment: organization-defined types of and/or specific authenticators] be conducted [Selection: in person; by a trusted external party] before [Assignment: organization-defined registration authority] with authorization by [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles].

Discussion: Issuing authenticators in person or by a trusted external party enhances and reinforces the trustworthiness of the identity proofing process.

Related Controls: IA-12.
AUTHENTICATOR MANAGEMENT | PRESENTATION ATTACK DETECTION FOR BIOMETRIC AUTHENTICATORS
Employ presentation attack detection mechanisms for biometric-based authentication.

Discussion: Biometric characteristics do not constitute secrets. Such characteristics can be obtained by online web accesses, taking a picture of someone with a camera phone to obtain facial images with or without their knowledge, lifting from objects that someone has touched (e.g., a latent fingerprint), or capturing a high-resolution image (e.g., an iris pattern). Presentation attack detection technologies including liveness detection can mitigate the risk of these types of attacks by making it difficult to produce artifacts intended to defeat the biometric sensor.

Related Controls: AC-7.
AUTHENTICATOR MANAGEMENT | PASSWORD MANAGERS
(a) Employ [Assignment: organization-defined password managers] to generate and manage passwords; and (b) Protect the passwords using [Assignment: organization-defined controls].

Discussion: For systems where static passwords are employed, it is often a challenge to ensure that the passwords are suitably complex and that the same passwords are not employed on multiple systems. A password manager is a solution to this problem as it automatically generates and stores strong and different passwords for various accounts. A potential risk of using password managers is that adversaries can target the collection of passwords generated by the password manager. Therefore, the collection of passwords requires protection including encrypting the passwords (see [IA-5(1)(d)] ) and storing the collection offline in a token.

Related Controls: None.

References: [FIPS 140-3], [FIPS 180-4], [FIPS 201-2], [FIPS 202], [SP 800-63-3], [SP 800-73-4], [SP 800-76-2], [SP 800-78-4], [IR TS259], [IR 7821], [IR 7849], [IR 7870], [IR 8040]

title: IDENTIFIER MANAGEMENT
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Evidence of a formal process where defined roles/personnel are authorized to assign identifiers (via approved procedures or system logs).
• Technical artifacts such as audit logs from the identity management system, incident tickets, or system configuration databases that record identifier creation, assignment, and prevention of reuse.
• Operational patterns including regular reviews of assigned identifiers, automatic checks in the provisioning systems, and alerts for identifier reissuance attempts that should have been blocked.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations might produce policy documentation without having operational enforcement (i.e., only paper controls with no automated evidence).
• Relying on manual forms or spreadsheets to track identifier assignment, leaving room for errors and reuse incidents that go unnoticed.
• Using shared system accounts hidden under rotational naming or generic accounts which effectively bypass individual accountability and identifier uniqueness.

VALIDATION STEPS:
1. CHECK: Verify the authorization process for identifier assignment.
   WHY: Ensures only approved personnel or roles can assign unique system identifiers.
   HOW: Request evidence from system configuration management tools, interview personnel in charge of identity provisioning, and inspect system logs showing authorized assignments with validation of the assigning role.
   FAIL: If there are no logs or clear records showing assignment source or if assignments are done via ad hoc methods lacking role-based authorization.

2. CHECK: Validate that the identifier assignment process selects unique identifiers for individuals, groups, roles, services, or devices.
   WHY: Confirms that technical mechanisms are in place to generate or enforce uniqueness, thereby avoiding duplicate identifiers.
   HOW: Review system configuration settings and logic rules within the identity management system, and test with a sample set of recently created accounts to ensure identifiers are not reused, checking against historical logs.
   FAIL: If multiple records show overlapping or reused identifiers beyond the organization-defined timeframe.

3. CHECK: Assess mechanisms preventing reuse of identifiers after de-provisioning.
   WHY: Preventing reuse safeguards against unauthorized access scenarios where ex-users might still be associated with previously assigned identifiers.
   HOW: Inspect the identity lifecycle management system’s retention policies and audit trails that track identifier status, and verify that deactivated identifiers are flagged in the system to inhibit reassignment.
   FAIL: If identifiers appear to have been reassigned to different accounts without an enforced cooldown period, or audit logs do not capture decommission events.

4. CHECK: For environments with public identifiers (e.g., email, chat IDs), confirm controls that prohibit system account identifiers from being used as public identifiers.
   WHY: Mitigates risks associated with external exposure of system account details.
   HOW: Review configuration policies in the communication platforms and compare account naming conventions against internal standards; verify that public identifiers are independently maintained from internal system identifiers.
   FAIL: If internal system account identifiers are found in public communications or documentation.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Use automated log analysis tools to quickly extract identifier assignment events; manual review of logs is prone to errors and omissions.
• Focus on real-time enforcement mechanisms over policy documents; effective identifier management is often hidden in system configurations and audit trails.
• Look for anomalies such as multiple assignments from the same originator or gaps in audit logs, which can be a strong indicator of an incomplete or manually overridden process.
• Validate that the process for revoking an identifier and its prevention of reuse is enforced automatically rather than through manual approval.

Overall, the review should emphasize the operational effectiveness of identifier management rather than the mere existence of policies. The automated, observable evidence tied to system logs, configuration settings, and maintenance records is critical in verifying compliance in a way that cannot be easily faked.
content:
Statement:
Manage system identifiers by:  
a. Receiving authorization from [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles] to assign individual, group, role, service, or device identifier;  
b. Selecting an identifier that identifies an individual, group, role, service, or device;  
c. Assigning the identifier to the intended individual, group, role, service, or device; and  
d. Preventing reuse of identifiers for [Assignment: organization-defined time period].

Guidance:
Common device identifiers include Media Access Control (MAC) addresses, Internet Protocol (IP) addresses, or device-unique token identifiers. The management of individual identifiers is not applicable to shared system accounts. Typically, individual identifiers are the usernames of the system accounts assigned to those individuals. In such instances, the account management activities of AC-2 use account names provided by IA-4. Identifier management also addresses individual identifiers not necessarily associated with system accounts. Preventing the reuse of identifiers implies preventing the assignment of previously used individual, group, role, service, or device identifiers to different individuals, groups, roles, services, or devices.

Enhancements:
(1) IDENTIFIER MANAGEMENT | PROHIBIT ACCOUNT IDENTIFIERS AS PUBLIC IDENTIFIERS
Prohibit the use of system account identifiers that are the same as public identifiers for individual accounts.
Discussion: Prohibiting account identifiers as public identifiers applies to any publicly disclosed account identifier used for communication such as, electronic mail and instant messaging.

title: DEVICE IDENTIFICATION AND AUTHENTICATION
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Devices display unique identifiers (e.g., MAC addresses, device certificates) embedded in configurations or through network access logs. 
• System configurations (e.g., 802.1x, EAP, RADIUS server configs) show enforcement of device authentication prior to network connectivity. 
• Operational evidence such as live connection logs and authentication transactions that capture bidirectional cryptographic exchanges.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Relying solely on static MAC filtering or IP addresses without integrating stronger cryptographic methods can give an illusion of control without true authentication. 
• Outdated configurations where device identifiers are not dynamically verified can be faked (e.g., devices mimicking known MAC/IP addresses). 
• Administrators sometimes bypass the more rigorous authentication for ease-of-use during testing or maintenance, leaving gaps in production.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Verify that network access devices (switches, WiFi controllers) are configured to enforce IEEE 802.1x authentication for connecting devices.
   WHY: 802.1x enforces unique device identification and cryptographic authentication and prevents unauthorized device connectivity.
   HOW: Retrieve configuration files from switches and wireless access points, inspect logs for authentication successes/failures, and trace session initiation requests to 802.1x servers.
   FAIL: Absence of 802.1x or related authentication logs, or devices connecting without any authentication challenge.

2. CHECK: Examine the logs on the RADIUS or authentication servers for evidence of cryptographic bidirectional authentication transactions.
   WHY: Authentic cryptographic exchanges show that both sides of a connection are verifying each other’s credentials, going beyond simple one-way trust.
   HOW: Query log repositories for entries that include mutual TLS or EAP-TLS handshake outcomes and check certificate validity details.
   FAIL: Logs that only confirm one-way authentication or missing cryptographic negotiation details are red flags.

3. CHECK: Confirm that unique, non-spoofable identifiers (e.g., device certificates, cryptographic tokens) are issued and enforced on devices.
   WHY: Unique credentials provided in enrollment and maintained through certificate authorities provide evidence against device impersonation.
   HOW: Review device enrollment procedures, inspect a sample of device certificates installed on endpoints, and check for certificate revocation lists within the authentication system.
   FAIL: Certificates or tokens that are generic, missing, or manually overridden signal a breakdown in proper device identification.

4. CHECK: Test remote and local network access controls on a sample of organization-defined devices by initiating connection attempts and monitoring the authentication process.
   WHY: Active testing simulates real-world scenarios of device connection, ensuring policies are enforced in operational environments.
   HOW: Perform connection attempts with both authorized and unauthorized devices, review the response from authentication servers, and verify that unauthorized attempts are blocked.
   FAIL: Unauthorized devices connect, or the authentication process does not require proper credential verification, indicating a control failure.

EXPERT TIPS
• Focus on live, automated authentication logs and real-time monitoring dashboards as they are harder to game compared to static documents. 
• Look for anomalies such as multiple devices using the same certificate or identifier—a critical sign of misconfigured device enrollment processes. 
• Ensure that both endpoints and network access devices have up-to-date firmware and configurations that enforce cryptographic bidirectional authentication; legacy equipment may only support weak or one-way authentication.
• Verification shortcuts: spot-check a few devices post-enrollment, request a demonstration of the authentication handshake in a lab setting, and correlate network traffic captures for TLS handshakes to quickly assess compliance.
• Always confirm that any “manual override” configurations are tightly controlled and audited, as these are common vectors for bypassing strict device authentication controls.
content:
Statement:
Uniquely identify and authenticate devices [Assignment: organization-defined devices and/or types of devices] before establishing a [Selection (one or more): local; remote; network] connection.

Guidance:
Devices that require unique device-to-device identification and authentication are included devices that are not owned by the organization. Systems use shared known information (e.g., Media Access Control [MAC], Transmission Control Protocol/Internet Protocol [TCP/IP] addresses) for device identification or organizational authentication solutions (e.g., Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE) 802.1x and Extensible Authentication Protocol (EAP), RADIUS server with EAP-Transport Layer Security (TLS) authentication, Kerberos) to identify and authenticate devices on local and wide area networks. Organizations determine the required strength of authentication mechanisms based on the security categories of systems and mission or business requirements. Because of the challenges of implementing device authentication on a large scale, organizations can restrict the application of the control to a limited number/type of devices based on mission or business needs.

Enhancements:
(1) DEVICE IDENTIFICATION AND AUTHENTICATION | CRYPTOGRAPHIC BIDIRECTIONAL AUTHENTICATION
Authenticate [Assignment: organization-defined devices and/or types of devices] before establishing [Selection (one or more): local; remote; network] connection using bidirectional authentication that is cryptographically based.
Discussion: A local connection is a connection with a device that communicates without the use of a network. A network connection is a connection with a device that communicates through a network. A remote connection is a connection with a device that communicates through an external network. Bidirectional authentication provides stronger protection to validate the identity of other devices for connections that are of greater risk.
Related Controls: SC-8, SC-12, SC-13.
(2) DEVICE IDENTIFICATION AND AUTHENTICATION | CRYPTOGRAPHIC BIDIRECTIONAL NETWORK AUTHENTICATION

title: IDENTIFICATION AND AUTHENTICATION (ORGANIZATIONAL USERS)
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for IA-2 (Identification and Authentication for Organizational Users).

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible evidence: Screenshots or dashboards of centralized authentication systems (e.g., Active Directory, LDAP) showing unique user accounts; enrollment records for multi-factor authentication (MFA) used by privileged and non-privileged users; system configuration files enforcing out-of-band authentication (if applicable).
   • Technical artifacts: Audit logs capturing each user’s login event with evidence of unique identification; records from MFA systems (hardware tokens, CAC/PIV logs) that tie factors to individual user IDs; configuration settings that detail individual authentication before group account usage.
   • Operational patterns: Regular, automated processing of authentication events (with event correlation), periodic reviews of account provisioning/deprovisioning, and audit trails confirming that group authenticators are preceded by individual verifications.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Faked compliance often occurs when organizations rely solely on policy documentation without actual enforcement in the systems. For example, using shared accounts across multiple users or enabling MFA only on paper without enforcing it on all system entry points.
   • Surface-level implementations include having a central repository for user IDs that is not actively integrated with authentication systems, or simply using password changes as “multifactor” without incorporating a secondary factor.
   • Technical gaps: Lack of proper logging, misconfigured systems that allow bypassing individual authentication for shared/group accounts, or systems that do not enforce separate device use for out-of-band MFA.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify that the authentication system (e.g., Active Directory or LDAP) enforces unique user identifiers.
     WHY: This proves that individual users are uniquely identified and that their logins can be audited for accountability.
     HOW: Query the directory for user account attributes (unique IDs, user names) and check integration with log management; verify that each entry is tied to an individual record and not a generic/shared account.
     FAIL: Discovery of multiple users with the same identifier or over-reliance on group accounts without individual identifiers.

   CHECK: Confirm that MFA is enforced for both privileged and non-privileged accounts (especially for remote or high-risk access).
     WHY: MFA provides additional assurance for user authentication, reducing risks from compromised credentials.
     HOW: Review system configuration files and policy settings to ensure MFA is active; examine logs for recorded MFA events, ensuring a secondary factor (e.g., hardware token, biometric, or CAC/PIV) was used.
     FAIL: Authentication logs that show only single-factor logins, or policy configurations that indicate MFA is optional or only sporadically applied.

   CHECK: Examine audit logs to ensure that any shared account usage is preceded by individual authentication before group-level access is granted.
     WHY: This ensures that accountability can be maintained even when group authenticators are used.
     HOW: Analyze time-stamped logs for login events; verify that prior to accessing a shared resource, there is evidence of individual user authentication (e.g., a unique session log for each user).
     FAIL: Logs that reveal direct group authentication without any preceding individual identity verification.

   CHECK: Inspect the configuration enforcing the use of a separate device for MFA when required (e.g., out-of-band tokens).
     WHY: Utilizing a separate device minimizes the risk of local compromise affecting the secondary authentication factor.
     HOW: Review configuration settings or policies for remote access and MFA; verify that records of authentication events indicate the use of a separate device (hardware tokens, separate mobile app, etc.).
     FAIL: Systems allowing multi-factor authentication to be completed entirely on the system being accessed or missing documentation/integration of a separate device.

   (Optional) CHECK: Validate that single sign-on (SSO) implementations retain traceability to individual users across applications.
     WHY: SSO should not obscure unique user identity, ensuring accountability across systems.
     HOW: Check SSO session logs and audit trails; confirm that session tokens are mapped back to individual user profiles within central authentication systems.
     FAIL: SSO logs or sessions that only record a generic or group identity without individual user mapping.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Verification shortcuts: Use centralized log aggregation and automated analytics to quickly correlate MFA events with individual logins. Tools that monitor Active Directory integrity can reveal shared accounts or unusual login patterns.
   • Indicators of poor implementation: Lack of integration between user directories and authentication logs, infrequent or incomplete log reviews, and configuration settings that allow a fallback to single-factor authentication when MFA should be enforced.
   • Critical details: Ensure that every authentication event is auditable and that any instance of group account usage includes a traceable individual authentication step. Pay special attention to systems with remote or network access, where the risk of credential compromise is higher.
   • Don’t rely solely on policy or documentation; automated evidence, system configuration files, and real-time logs provide the most compelling proof of operational effectiveness.

This methodology emphasizes practical, technical evidence over documentation alone and reduces reliance on controls that can be easily faked. Follow these steps to validate that your IA-2 implementation is robust and operational.
content:
Statement:
Uniquely identify and authenticate organizational users and associate that unique identification with processes acting on behalf of those users.

Guidance:
Organizations can satisfy the identification and authentication requirements by complying with the requirements in [HSPD 12]. Organizational users include employees or individuals who organizations consider to have an equivalent status to employees (e.g., contractors and guest researchers). Unique identification and authentication of users applies to all accesses other than those that are explicitly identified in AC-14 and that occur through the authorized use of group authenticators without individual authentication. Since processes execute on behalf of groups and roles, organizations may require unique identification of individuals in group accounts or for detailed accountability of individual activity.

Organizations employ passwords, physical authenticators, or biometrics to authenticate user identities or, in the case of multi-factor authentication, some combination thereof. Access to organizational systems is defined as either local access or network access. Local access is any access to organizational systems by users or processes acting on behalf of users, where access is obtained through direct connections without the use of networks. Network access is access to organizational systems by users (or processes acting on behalf of users) where access is obtained through network connections (i.e., non-local accesses). Remote access is a type of network access that involves communication through external networks. Internal networks include local area networks and wide area networks.

The use of encrypted virtual private networks for network connections between organization-controlled endpoints and non-organization-controlled endpoints may be treated as internal networks with respect to protecting the confidentiality and integrity of information traversing the network. Identification and authentication requirements for non-organizational users are detailed in IA-8.

Enhancements:
(1) IDENTIFICATION AND AUTHENTICATION (ORGANIZATIONAL USERS) | MULTI-FACTOR AUTHENTICATION TO PRIVILEGED ACCOUNTS
Implement multi-factor authentication for access to privileged accounts.
Discussion: Multi-factor authentication requires the use of two or more different factors to achieve authentication. The authentication factors are defined as follows: something you know (e.g., a personal identification number [PIN]), something you have (e.g., a physical authenticator such as a cryptographic private key), or something you are (e.g., a biometric). Multi-factor authentication solutions that feature physical authenticators include hardware authenticators that provide time-based or challenge-response outputs and smart cards such as the U.S. Government Personal Identity Verification (PIV) card or the Department of Defense (DoD) Common Access Card (CAC). In addition to authenticating users at the system level (i.e., at login), organizations may employ authentication mechanisms at the application level, at their discretion, to provide increased security. Regardless of the type of access (i.e., local, network, remote), privileged accounts are authenticated using multi-factor options appropriate for the level of risk. Organizations can add additional security measures, such as additional or more rigorous authentication mechanisms, for specific types of access.
Related Controls: AC-5, AC-6.
(2) IDENTIFICATION AND AUTHENTICATION (ORGANIZATIONAL USERS) | MULTI-FACTOR AUTHENTICATION TO NON-PRIVILEGED ACCOUNTS 
Implement multi-factor authentication for access to non-privileged accounts.
Discussion: Multi-factor authentication requires the use of two or more different factors to achieve authentication. The authentication factors are defined as follows: something you know (e.g., a personal identification number [PIN]), something you have (e.g., a physical authenticator such as a cryptographic private key), or something you are (e.g., a biometric). Multi-factor authentication solutions that feature physical authenticators include hardware authenticators that provide time-based or challenge-response outputs and smart cards such as the U.S. Government Personal Identity Verification card or the DoD Common Access Card. In addition to authenticating users at the system level, organizations may also employ authentication mechanisms at the application level, at their discretion, to provide increased information security. Regardless of the type of access (i.e., local, network, remote), non-privileged accounts are authenticated using multi-factor options appropriate for the level of risk. Organizations can provide additional security measures, such as additional or more rigorous authentication mechanisms, for specific types of access.
Related Controls: AC-5.
(3) IDENTIFICATION AND AUTHENTICATION (ORGANIZATIONAL USERS) | LOCAL ACCESS TO PRIVILEGED ACCOUNTS
(Withdrawn: Incorporated into IA-2(1).)
(4) IDENTIFICATION AND AUTHENTICATION (ORGANIZATIONAL USERS) | LOCAL ACCESS TO NON-PRIVILEGED ACCOUNTS
(5) IDENTIFICATION AND AUTHENTICATION (ORGANIZATIONAL USERS) | INDIVIDUAL AUTHENTICATION WITH GROUP AUTHENTICATION
When shared accounts or authenticators are employed, require users to be individually authenticated before granting access to the shared accounts or resources.

Discussion: Individual authentication prior to shared group authentication mitigates the risk of using group accounts or authenticators.

Related Controls: None.
(6) IDENTIFICATION AND AUTHENTICATION (ORGANIZATIONAL USERS) | ACCESS -- SEPARATE DEVICE
Implement multi-factor authentication for [Selection (one or more): local; network; remote] access to [Selection (one or more): privileged accounts; non-privileged accounts] such that:
(a) One of the factors is provided by a device separate from the system gaining access; and
(b) The device meets [Assignment: organization-defined strength of mechanism requirements].

Discussion: The purpose of requiring a device that is separate from the system to which the user is attempting to gain access for one of the factors during multi-factor authentication is to reduce the likelihood of compromising authenticators or credentials stored on the system. Adversaries may be able to compromise such authenticators or credentials and subsequently impersonate authorized users. Implementing one of the factors on a separate device (e.g., a hardware token), provides a greater strength of mechanism and an increased level of assurance in the authentication process.

Related Controls: AC-6.
(7) IDENTIFICATION AND AUTHENTICATION (ORGANIZATIONAL USERS) | NETWORK ACCESS TO NON-PRIVILEGED ACCOUNTS — SEPARATE DEVICE[Withdrawn: Incorporated into IA-26(.) ]
(8) IDENTIFICATION AND AUTHENTICATION (ORGANIZATIONAL USERS) | ACCESS TO ACCOUNTS — REPLAY RESISTANT
Implement replay-resistant authentication mechanisms for access to [Selection (one or more): privileged accounts; non-privileged accounts].

Discussion: Authentication processes resist replay attacks if it is impractical to achieve successful authentications by replaying previous authentication messages. Replay-resistant techniques include protocols that use nonces or challenges such as time synchronous or cryptographic authenticators.

Related Controls: None.
(9) IDENTIFICATION AND AUTHENTICATION (ORGANIZATIONAL USERS) | NETWORK ACCESS TO NON-PRIVILEGED ACCOUNTS — REPLAY RESISTANT[Withdrawn: Incorporated into IA-28(.) ]
(10) IDENTIFICATION AND AUTHENTICATION (ORGANIZATIONAL USERS) | SINGLE SIGN-ON
Provide a single sign-on capability for [Assignment: organization-defined system accounts and services].

Discussion: Single sign-on enables users to log in once and gain access to multiple systems. Organizations consider the operational efficiencies provided by single sign-on capabilities with the risk introduced by allowing access to multiple systems via a single authentication event. Single sign-on can present opportunities to improve system security, for example by providing the ability to add multi-factor authentication for applications and systems (existing and new) that may not be able to natively support multi-factor authentication.

Related Controls: None.
(11) IDENTIFICATION AND AUTHENTICATION (ORGANIZATIONAL USERS) | REMOTE ACCESS — SEPARATE DEVICE[Withdrawn: Incorporated into IA-26(.) ]
(12) IDENTIFICATION AND AUTHENTICATION (ORGANIZATIONAL USERS) | ACCEPTANCE OF PIV CREDENTIALS
Accept and electronically verify Personal Identity Verification-compliant credentials.

Discussion: Acceptance of Personal Identity Verification (PIV)-compliant credentials applies to organizations implementing local access control and physical access control systems. PIV-compliant credentials are those credentials issued by federal agencies that conform to FIPS Publication 201 and supporting guidance documents. The adequacy and reliability of PIV card issuers are authorized using [SP 800-79-2]. Acceptance of PIV-compliant credentials includes derived PIV credentials, the use of which is addressed in [SP 800-166]. The DOD Common Access Card (CAC) is an example of a PIV credential.

Related Controls: None.
(13) IDENTIFICATION AND AUTHENTICATION (ORGANIZATIONAL USERS) | OUT-OF-BAND AUTHENTICATION
Implement the following out-of-band authentication mechanisms under [Assignment: organization-defined conditions]; [Assignment: organization-defined out-of-band authentication].

title: POLICY AND PROCEDURES
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   - The organization has a formal, written document titled “Identification and Authentication Policy,” clearly addressing scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, legal compliance, and coordination among entities.
   - There are documented procedures, separate or appended to system security plans, describing how the policy is implemented. Look for version control, review dates, and evidence of dissemination (e.g., distribution lists, training completion records).
   - Evidence of an assigned official responsible for managing policy development and updates, such as an appointment memo or organizational chart with role responsibilities.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   - Organizations sometimes produce a policy document that merely restates control requirements from a template without meaningful tailoring or integration with actual operational roles and responsibilities.
   - Faking compliance by having an outdated document or one that is never formally disseminated or enforced; in other words, a paper-only exercise without active awareness among staff.
   - Relying solely on static documentation, while neglecting to log periodic reviews, updates triggered by events (e.g., incidents), or absence of evidence that changes have been implemented.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify the existence and currency of the written Identification and Authentication Policy and Procedures.
   WHY: A current document with a defined review cycle and version history signals active management and compliance with control requirements.
   HOW: Ask for the latest version of the policy, check the document header/footer for revision dates, and look for signatures or approval logs. Confirm the revision cycle corresponds with the organization-defined frequency and triggers.
   FAIL: An outdated document, no revision history, or policies that haven’t been updated following notable events indicate non-compliance.

   CHECK: Confirm assignment of a responsible official for managing the policy and procedures.
   WHY: Formal designation of an owner is critical to ensuring accountability and regular updates.
   HOW: Review organizational charts, role assignments, or appointment communications (e.g., memos), and validate through interviews or cross-checks with HR/IT governance documentation.
   FAIL: Absence of a designated owner or inability to verify the individual's role in policy management suggests a gap in control.

   CHECK: Assess evidence of effective dissemination and ongoing staff awareness of the IA policy.
   WHY: Dissemination not only means the document exists but that it is actively communicated and understood by relevant personnel, supporting real operational execution.
   HOW: Review training records, internal communications, or intranet postings; interview randomly selected personnel to confirm they are aware of and understand the policy's key points.
   FAIL: Lack of training records or staff unawareness indicates the procedure may be a formality rather than an operational mandate.

   CHECK: Review logs or meeting minutes documenting periodic reviews and updates triggered by assessments, audits, or security events.
   WHY: Evidence of a living policy that is regularly reviewed and adapted in response to internal or external changes shows operational effectiveness.
   HOW: Examine meeting minutes, change logs, or incident response reports to see if and when policy updates were made following significant events.
   FAIL: No record of reviews or unresponsiveness to changes in applicable laws, incidents, or audit findings indicates the process is not functioning as intended.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   - A quick verification shortcut is to check digital signature stamps or metadata in policy documents which should indicate both the review date and the designated responsible official.
   - Interviewing a few low-level staff or system owners can reveal whether the policy is just a document or a part of daily operations; genuine awareness across the organization is a strong indicator of implementation.
   - Prioritize evidence that shows a feedback loop (e.g., updated policies after incidents) to confirm that the process is dynamic and not static "check the box" compliance.
   - Always cross-reference the policy document with external audit notes or regulatory compliance reports to see if implementation aligns with actual organizational practice.
content:
Statement:
a. Develop, document, and disseminate to [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles]:

1. [Selection (one or more): Organization-level; Mission/business process-level; System-level] identification and authentication policy that:

(a) Addresses purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, coordination among organizational entities, and compliance; and  
(b) Is consistent with applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines; and  

2. Procedures to facilitate the implementation of the identification and authentication policy and the associated identification and authentication controls;  

b. Designate an [Assignment: organization-defined official] to manage the development, documentation, and dissemination of the identification and authentication policy and procedures; and  

c. Review and update the current identification and authentication:

1. Policy [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] and following [Assignment: organization-defined events]; and  
2. Procedures [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] and following [Assignment: organization-defined events].

Guidance:
Identification and authentication policy and procedures address the controls in the IA family that are implemented within systems and organizations. The risk management strategy is an important factor in establishing such policies and procedures. Policies and procedures contribute to security and privacy assurance. Therefore, it is important that security and privacy programs collaborate on the development of identification and authentication policy and procedures. Security and privacy program policies and procedures at the organization level are preferable, in general, and may obviate the need for mission- or system-specific policies and procedures. The policy can be included as part of the general security and privacy policy or be established for security and privacy programs, for mission or business processes, and for systems, if needed. Procedures describe how the policies or controls are implemented and can be directed at the individual or role that is the object of the procedure. Procedures can be documented in system security and privacy plans or in one or more separate documents. Events that may precipitate an update to identification and authentication policy and procedures include assessment or audit findings, security incidents or breaches, or changes in applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines. Simply restating controls does not constitute an organizational policy or procedure.

title: ALTERNATIVE SECURITY MECHANISMS
methodology:
Control CP-13 – Alternative Security Mechanisms – mandates that organizations have back-up or supplemental security controls ready to be activated as alternatives to primary controls when those primary controls become compromised or are otherwise unavailable. Below is a practical methodology to assess the implementation of this control.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
- Visible Evidence: Look for documented procedures and flowcharts that clearly designate alternative security mechanisms for critical functions (e.g., backup authentication methods, alternative encryption keys, contingency access methods). Check that these procedures are integrated into training and incident response documentation.
- Technical Artifacts: Evidence may include configuration files, automated scripts, and system logs that record activation of alternative security mechanisms (e.g., logs showing one-time pad usage, fallback multi-factor verification records, and alerts indicating a switch over to alternative methods during system failures).
- Operational Patterns: Regular testing and drills where the organization activates these alternatives and logs detailed performance data. Check for recorded incidents or scheduled tests where alternatives were successfully deployed.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
- Faked Compliance: Organizations might have documentation that describes alternative security controls without demonstrating evidence of actual deployment. Simply having policies and procedures does not confirm operational readiness.
- Surface-Level Implementations: Relying solely on manual processes (which can be easily bypassed) without automated checks, logging, or performance monitoring. Also, systems that lack regular testing or simulated activations may hide deficiencies.
- Frequent Gaps: Missing logs when alternatives are deployed, inadequate integration with the primary security system that leads to delayed switchovers, and failure to update alternative mechanism configurations post changes in the primary system.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
- CHECK: Verify the existence of documented procedures and configurations for alternative security mechanisms.
  WHY: This ensures the organization has planned fallback measures for critical security functions.
  HOW: Review system and security documentation; examine contingency and incident response plans; interview security administrators.
  FAIL: Lack of specific alternative mechanisms or outdated procedures that do not address current primary mechanisms.

- CHECK: Inspect logs and audit trails for evidence of activation or tests of alternative mechanisms.
  WHY: Automated and recorded evidence of alternative deployments demonstrates that mechanisms are operational and routinely tested for effectiveness.
  HOW: Query system logs, SIEM records, or audit trails for keywords such as "alternative mechanism activated", "fallback mode", or specific identifiers like one-time pad usage. Validate schedule-based test records.
  FAIL: No log entries or irregular logging indicating that tests/activations have not occurred as scheduled.

- CHECK: Conduct practical testing of activation procedures in a controlled environment (e.g., simulation or scheduled drill).
  WHY: Testing confirms the mechanisms work as intended and that personnel are trained for their deployment under duress.
  HOW: Coordinate with the incident response team to simulate a primary mechanism failure. Observe the timely switch-over to the alternative control and review performance metrics and error logging.
  FAIL: Failures in the test, delays in activation, errors in logs, or inability of staff to follow procedures correctly.

- CHECK: Review change control and update logs to assure alternative mechanisms are maintained alongside changes in the primary security controls.
  WHY: Ensures both primary and alternative mechanisms evolve in tandem, preventing gaps during updates.
  HOW: Audit configuration management records and change management systems; verify that security control updates include corresponding documentation or re-testing of alternative mechanisms.
  FAIL: Evidence that updates to primary controls were made without reviewing or updating fallback alternative mechanisms.

4. EXPERT TIPS
- Shortcut: Engage with the incident response team and mid-level administrators – they often can provide verbal or ad hoc evidence if the right practices are in place. Look for a consistent narrative and firsthand test reports on fallback activations.
- Critical Indicators: Pay close attention to log timestamps, error codes, and discrepancies between documented schedules and actual activation events. A lack of detailed records is a red flag.
- Key Details: Verify that alternative mechanisms are not just reactive policies but are embedded in operational processes with automated triggers where possible. Manual interventions should be minimal and well-documented.

By following these practical checks, auditors can ascertain that alternative security mechanisms are not only defined on paper but are actively integrated and effective within the organization’s overall cybersecurity framework.
content:
Statement:
Employ [Assignment: organization-defined alternative or supplemental security mechanisms] for satisfying [Assignment: organization-defined security functions] when the primary means of implementing the security function is unavailable or compromised.

Guidance:
Use of alternative security mechanisms supports system resiliency, contingency planning, and continuity of operations. To ensure mission and business continuity, organizations can implement alternative or supplemental security mechanisms. The mechanisms may be less effective than the primary mechanisms. However, having the capability to readily employ alternative or supplemental mechanisms enhances mission and business continuity that might otherwise be adversely impacted if operations had to be curtailed until the primary means of implementing the functions was restored. Given the cost and level of effort required to provide such alternative capabilities, the alternative or supplemental mechanisms are only applied to critical security capabilities provided by systems, system components, or system services. For example, an organization may issue one-time pads to senior executives, officials, and system administrators if multi-factor tokens—the standard means for achieving secure authentication—are compromised.

title: SAFE MODE
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Look for system logs and user interface alerts that document transitions into safe mode. Check if there are dashboard indicators or status banners that clearly indicate the system is operating under restricted conditions.
• Technical Artifacts: Identify configuration files or scripts that define criteria for entering safe mode. Investigate audit logs showing automated or manually triggered safe mode activations and subsequent reduction of functionality (e.g., only basic system processes running).
• Operational Patterns: Examine historical operational data for recurring automated safe mode events under specific conditions. Confirmation that the system periodically tests or simulates safe mode conditions in a controlled manner is also a strong signal.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Fake Compliance via Documentation: Organizations might have policies and documents describing safe mode, but without evidence of real-time activation logs or system-level restrictions, compliance may be superficial.
• Surface-level Implementations: Implementing safe mode without enforced technical restrictions (e.g., relying solely on an alert mechanism while keeping full functionality accessible) is a frequent shortcoming.
• Technical Gaps: A common pitfall is the lack of automated triggers to enter safe mode under predefined abnormal conditions, or having incomplete logging of the safe mode activation process that prevents verification of actual operations.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Verify Configuration Files or Policy Scripts
   WHY: These files should contain clear thresholds and function restrictions for safe mode activation.
   HOW: Inspect system configuration and policy scripts; look for parameters that specify conditions (e.g., temperature, network anomalies, power fluctuations) and corresponding restrictions on operations.
   FAIL: Absence of clearly defined conditions or missing safe mode restrictions in configuration documents.

2. CHECK: Review System Logs for Safe Mode Activation Events
   WHY: Log evidence of safe mode transitions confirms that the control is actively triggered when conditions are met.
   HOW: Query and review timestamps in system/event logs to confirm that safe mode was entered automatically or manually during tests or real incidents, and cross-reference with triggering conditions.
   FAIL: No log entries, mismatched timestamps, or vague log details lacking trigger context.

3. CHECK: Perform a Controlled Trigger Test
   WHY: A controlled test that simulates defined abnormal conditions will confirm operational effectiveness of safe mode activation and restrictions.
   HOW: Work with system operators to safely simulate one of the organization-defined conditions and observe if the system downgrades to the safe mode with the correct restricted operations; document the outcome.
   FAIL: Unable to trigger safe mode or if the system fails to properly restrict functions as defined.

4. CHECK: Inspect Audit Trails for Post-Activation Activity
   WHY: Audit trails demonstrate continuous monitoring of safe mode actions, ensuring that restrictions remain enforced during the condition.
   HOW: Analyze audit logs or security event monitoring tools to verify that only the allowed functions were performed once safe mode was activated, and that unusual activities were blocked or flagged.
   FAIL: Lack of comprehensive audit trails, or evidence of unauthorized functions being executed after safe mode activation.

EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut Verification: If the system offers a safe mode indicator on the operational dashboard, it provides immediate credibility; however, always cross-check against system logs.
• Critical Indicators: Pay attention to whether automated tests or simulation exercises are regularly conducted to validate that safe mode operations and restrictions work as intended.
• Key Details: The precision of the conditions defined in configuration and the accuracy of system logs are the most critical details; even minor misconfigurations can lead to ineffective safe mode responses.
• Additional Tip: Validate that safe mode restrictions do not simply mask full functionality, but actively enforce a reduced set of allowable operations – this is often a gap if testers only rely on policy documentation.

By following these practical and observable steps, one can ensure not only that the safe mode is properly defined but also that it is effective in a real operational environment.
content:
Statement:
When [Assignment: organization-defined conditions] are detected, enter a safe mode of operation with [Assignment: organization-defined restrictions of safe mode of operation].

Guidance:
For systems that support critical mission and business functions—including military operations, civilian space operations, nuclear power plant operations, and air traffic control operations (especially real-time operational environments)—organizations can identify certain conditions under which those systems revert to a predefined safe mode of operation. The safe mode of operation, which can be achieved either automatically or manually, restricts the operations that systems can execute when those conditions are encountered. Restriction includes allowing only selected functions to execute that can be carried out under limited power or with reduced communications bandwidth.

title: ALTERNATE COMMUNICATIONS PROTOCOLS
methodology:
Below is the expert validation methodology for CP-11: ALTERNATE COMMUNICATIONS PROTOCOLS:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible evidence includes operational alternate communications devices or systems actively configured to support continuity-of-operations. Examples are deployed alternate network configurations, secondary routing setups, or backup communication servers.
   • Technical artifacts such as system configuration files, network diagrams, and software logs that record switching events provide compliance evidence. Also, records in disaster recovery (DR) tools and monitoring systems that track protocol switchover events are key.
   • Operational patterns like recurring trigger events during scheduled tests or training exercises, and documented red team exercises demonstrating switchovers, indicate the control is active.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations sometimes provide documentation that declares the existence of alternate communications protocols without demonstrating actual configuration or operational testing.
   • Surface-level implementations may rely solely on high-level policy without integration into incident response or automated switching in live environments.
   • Frequently missed technical gaps include a lack of regular testing records, no live logs of protocol switchovers, and failure to review the impact on software applications or network operations during a switch.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify the configuration of alternate communications endpoints (e.g., backup routers, secondary communication channels) in the system configuration repositories.
   WHY: Active configuration settings showing defined alternate protocols confirm practical implementation rather than mere policy existence.
   HOW: Inspect configuration files, network diagrams, and device management consoles for hard-coded alternate protocol settings, and correlate with contingency plan instructions.
   FAIL: Absence of configured endpoints or reliance solely on documentation without technical configuration artifacts.
   
   CHECK: Review and inspect logs or event records that document testing or activation of alternate communications during drills or actual events.
   WHY: Logs that capture switchover events demonstrate that the protocols are not just defined but are used reliably in operational scenarios.
   HOW: Access system logs, DR testing records, or SIEM alerts that indicate the activation of alternate communication protocols during simulated or real outage conditions.
   FAIL: Missing logs or records indicating that actual testing or real-life events triggered the alternate communications protocol.
   
   CHECK: Evaluate contingency plan exercises and training records to confirm that personnel are both aware of and capable of initiating alternate communications protocols.
   WHY: Effective training and exercise records illustrate that the operational teams know how to deploy these protocols in a crisis, elevating practical resilience.
   HOW: Review incident response training logs, drill reports, and after-action reports that specifically refer to the activation and challenges encountered with alternate communications.
   FAIL: Lack of focused training records or evidence that the alternate protocols were referenced and tested in contingency exercises.
   
   CHECK: Conduct or review a live simulation where an alternate communications protocol is triggered, verifying that there is minimal disruption in system performance.
   WHY: A live simulation confirms the end-to-end functionality and interoperability of the alternate communications channels, proving resilience in practice.
   HOW: Either witness a scheduled simulation test or review simulation reports that detail performance metrics, protocol switch analysis, and any side effect mitigation steps taken during the test.
   FAIL: Simulation exercises that do not replicate real conditions or instances where switchover fails to complete successfully.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Verification Shortcut: Identify and query the DR/BCP system dashboard for any historical events detailing protocol switchover. Systems with automated alerting are less likely to be faked.
   • Critical Indicators: Pay close attention to how the alternate protocol impacts existing communications-dependent applications. Look for anomalies in application logs that could signal improper handling during the switchover.
   • Details that Matter: Ensure that the alternate protocol is not just theoretical. Verify through network path analysis and configuration reviews that the system supports dynamic updates, and confirm that the backup pathway has been stress-tested under real load conditions.
   • Be wary of controls that only exist on paper. Robust implementation should exhibit both technical configuration evidence and documented practical test outcomes.

This methodology provides concrete steps and practical evidence for assessing the operational effectiveness of alternate communications protocols, ensuring that the organization can actually employ them during continuity-of-operations scenarios.
content:
Statement:
Provide the capability to employ [Assignment: organization-defined alternative communications protocols] in support of maintaining continuity of operations.

Guidance:
Contingency plans and the contingency training or testing associated with those plans incorporate an alternate communications protocol capability as part of establishing resilience in organizational systems. Switching communications protocols may affect software applications and operational aspects of systems. Organizations assess the potential side effects of introducing alternate communications protocols prior to implementation.

title: SYSTEM RECOVERY AND RECONSTITUTION
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Documented contingency and reconstitution plans that detail recovery time objectives (RTOs) and recovery point objectives (RPOs), including clear timelines for reimaging and deactivation of interim capabilities.
• Automated system logs and test reports from periodic recovery exercises, including evidence of actual system restorations and reconstitutions from integrity-protected backups.
• Screenshots of configuration management systems showing controlled backups and restoration points, along with monitoring alert logs indicating restoration activities.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations sometimes rely solely on written policies or outdated test results rather than demonstrating current, active recovery procedures.
• Faking compliance by performing recovery drills in non-critical systems or isolated environments that do not fully replicate actual operational scenarios.
• Overlooking the deactivation of interim recovery configurations or failing to validate that backup components are adequately protected.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
• CHECK: Verify that the recovery and reconstitution plan is executed during live or simulated disruption events.
  WHY: It confirms the organization can actually restore systems within defined time frames and meet RTO/RPO objectives.
  HOW: Review logs and test reports from the most recent exercise; interview incident response teams; examine automated alerts and timeline metrics.
  FAIL: Lack of recent test records, incomplete logs, or recovery times exceeding defined targets.

• CHECK: Confirm that restoration activities (e.g., reimaging processes) use configuration-controlled and integrity-protected backups.
  WHY: This ensures that restored components come from a known, trusted state.
  HOW: Inspect backup and imaging procedures; check digital signatures/hashes on backup files; review change management records that verify the backup’s integrity.
  FAIL: Evidence of unverified backups or ad-hoc, undocumented restoration methods.

• CHECK: Assess evidence of deactivation of interim capabilities following recovery operations.
  WHY: The deactivation step is critical to returning to full operational capability and eliminating potential security gaps.
  HOW: Look for system configuration records showing the removal of temporary recovery configurations; interview system administrators to confirm procedures.
  FAIL: Interim recovery settings remain active after restoration efforts, suggesting incomplete reconstitution.

• CHECK: Review protection mechanisms for recovery and reconstitution components (hardware, firmware, and software) used during restoration.
  WHY: It ensures that tools critical to recovery are themselves secure against compromise.
  HOW: Conduct physical security checks, verify access control logs, and check configuration management systems for controlled access to backup media and recovery servers.
