  FAIL: Recoverable components are found on non-segregated networks or lack proper access restrictions.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Focus on automated recovery exercise logs: They are harder to fake than documentation and provide real-time evidence of successful operations.
• Critical indicators: Recovery time records, audit trails, and change management logs are the best indicators of true operational readiness.
• Verification shortcut: Ask for a recent incident or simulation debrief, then drill down on specific log times and configuration snapshots—if details are sketchy, recovery processes may be poorly implemented.
• Remember: The integration of recovery processes with continuous monitoring and reauthorization procedures is essential; isolated tests without these links indicate potential future vulnerabilities.
content:
Statement:
Provide for the recovery and reconstitution of the system to a known state within [Assignment: organization-defined time period consistent with recovery time and recovery point objectives] after a disruption, compromise, or failure.

Guidance:
Recovery is executing contingency plan activities to restore organizational mission and business functions. Reconstitution takes place following recovery and includes activities for returning systems to fully operational states. Recovery and reconstitution operations reflect mission and business priorities; recovery point, recovery time, and reconstitution objectives; and organizational metrics consistent with contingency plan requirements. Reconstitution includes the deactivation of interim system capabilities that may have been needed during recovery operations. Reconstitution also includes assessments of fully restored system capabilities, reestablishment of continuous monitoring activities, system reauthorization (if required), and activities to prepare the system and organization for future disruptions, breaches, compromises, or failures. Recovery and reconstitution capabilities can include automated mechanisms and manual procedures. Organizations establish recovery time and recovery point objectives as part of contingency planning.

Enhancements:
SYSTEM RECOVERY AND RECONSTITUTION | TRANSACTION RECOVERY
Discussion: Transaction-based systems include database management systems and transaction processing systems. Mechanisms supporting transaction recovery include transaction rollback and transaction journaling. Related Controls: None.
SYSTEM RECOVERY AND RECONSTITUTION | RESTORE WITHIN TIME PERIOD
Provide the capability to restore system components within [Assignment: organization-defined restoration time periods] from configuration-controlled and integrity-protected information representing a known, operational state for the components.
Discussion: Restoration of system components includes reimaging, which restores the components to known, operational states.
SYSTEM RECOVERY AND RECONSTITUTION | COMPONENT PROTECTION
Protect system components used for recovery and reconstitution.
Discussion: Protection of system recovery and reconstitution components (i.e., hardware, firmware, and software) includes physical and technical controls. Backup and restoration components used for recovery and reconstitution include router tables, compilers, and other system software. Related Controls: AC-3, AC-6, MP-2, MP-4, PE-3, PE-6.

title: SYSTEM BACKUP
methodology:
Below is the practical validation methodology for CP-9 System Backup:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   - Visible Evidence: Physical backup media (tapes, drives) labeled with dates, locations; backup schedules posted in operations centers; logs from backup software; screenshots of backup configuration settings in management consoles or dashboards.
   - Technical Artifacts: Detailed backup logs (including file integrity verifications with cryptographic hashes or digital signatures), automated backup reports, documented test restoration records, configuration files that specify backup frequency and retention, records of dual authorization events for deletions/destructions, network transfer logs for backup data to alternate sites.
   - Operational Patterns: Regular execution of backup jobs on defined schedules; periodic test restorations from a sample of backup data; regular offsite transfer or physically secured storage of backup media; routine audits verifying the cryptographic protection of backups.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   - Fake Compliance: Organizations may produce backup policies and schedule documents without demonstrating actual automated, functioning backup logs or restoration testing. They might show screenshots of configuration panels without evidence of historical backup success.
   - Surface-Level Implementations: Simply having a backup schedule or policy on file without evidence of operational backup, backup integrity tests, or offsite storage; poorly configured cron jobs that run but do not complete backups.
   - Technical Gaps: Omitting integrity verification (lack of cryptographic hashes or digital signatures), failure to physically segregate backup storage from production systems, and lacking documented dual authorization procedures resulting in single-person control over critical backup deletion or destruction.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   - CHECK: Verify the backup job logs and schedules.
     WHY: Consistent, automated logs provide evidence that backup operations are running per defined frequency.
     HOW: Extract and review logs from backup management tools; confirm timestamps align with organizational backup frequency; observe configuration snapshots.
     FAIL: Missing or inconsistent logs; backup jobs not aligned with configured schedules.

   - CHECK: Validate the integrity protection on backup data.
     WHY: Cryptographic signatures or hashes ensure that backup data has not been tampered with and remains reliable.
     HOW: Review backup files for associated integrity check files/signatures; confirm procedures for generating and verifying these artifacts; test a sample verification manually through cryptographic hash comparison.
     FAIL: Absence of cryptographic artifacts or failed integrity checks in samples.

   - CHECK: Review test restoration records and conduct a sample restoration exercise.
     WHY: Testing restore capability verifies operational effectiveness of backups and ensures recovery objectives are met.
     HOW: Examine logs and reports of recent restoration tests; if possible, perform a controlled restoration of a sample system function; verify that system-level, user-level, and documentation backups restore correctly.
     FAIL: No evidence of restoration tests, incomplete restoration of key components, or failure during sample restoration exercises.

   - CHECK: Confirm dual authorization for deletion or destruction of sensitive backup data.
     WHY: Dual authorization minimizes risks of accidental or malicious removal of backup data.
     HOW: Review procedural logs or system records that document dual authorization events; interview responsible personnel; check system configurations enforcing this control.
     FAIL: Single-party authorization events found or lack of any records demonstrating dual checks.

   - CHECK: Verify offsite or redundant backup storage measures.
     WHY: Separate storage provides physical and network resilience ensuring availability and security in case of primary site compromise.
     HOW: Inspect documented backup transfer logs; validate physical configuration of alternate storage (e.g., geographic separation, fire-rated containers, or secondary mirrored systems); evaluate audit trails of backup media transfers.
     FAIL: Backups only stored onsite or without the documented separation needed to meet resilience objectives.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   - Shortcut Verification: Ask directly for evidence of recent test restoration events and perform a quick “live” check of backup logs that are automatically generated by monitoring tools. A quick command-line verification on backup servers for recent timestamped backups is often revealing.
   - Critical Indicators: Look for anomalies in backup timestamps, gaps in log files, or absence of cryptographic verification artifacts. An operator disagreement on dual authorization records can be a red flag.
   - Details Matter: Ensure that backup schedules match recovery time and point objectives defined in contingency and disaster recovery plans; scrutinize the method and security mechanisms (e.g., encryption key management) used in backup data transfers.
   - Always Correlate: Combine evidence from technical artifacts with periodic operational tests. Relying solely on documented policies without active validation is a common pitfall.
   - Inquire about exceptions and deviations: If a system isn’t using dual authorization or offsite storage, get a risk-based rationale and temporary compensatory controls in place.

This multi-layered approach provides solid, non-fakeable evidence that CP-9 is not only implemented but operationally effective in ensuring the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of backup information.
content:
Statement:
a. Conduct backups of user-level information contained in [Assignment: organization-defined system components] [Assignment: organization-defined frequency consistent with recovery time and recovery point objectives];
b. Conduct backups of system-level information contained in the system [Assignment: organization-defined frequency consistent with recovery time and recovery point objectives];
c. Conduct backups of system documentation, including security- and privacy-related documentation [Assignment: organization-defined frequency consistent with recovery time and recovery point objectives]; and
d. Protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of backup information.

Guidance:
System-level information includes system state information, operating system software, middleware, application software, and licenses. User-level information includes information other than system-level information. Mechanisms employed to protect the integrity of system backups include digital signatures and cryptographic hashes. Protection of system backup information while in transit is addressed by MP-5 and SC-8. System backups reflect the requirements in contingency plans as well as other organizational requirements for backing up information. Organizations may be subject to laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, or policies with requirements regarding specific categories of information (e.g., personal health information). Organizational personnel consult with the senior agency official for privacy and legal counsel regarding such requirements.

Enhancements:
SYSTEM BACKUP | TESTING FOR RELIABILITY AND INTEGRITY Test backup information [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] to verify media reliability and information integrity.
Discussion: Organizations need assurance that backup information can be reliably retrieved. Reliability pertains to the systems and system components where the backup information is stored, the operations used to retrieve the information, and the integrity of the information being retrieved. Independent and specialized tests can be used for each of the aspects of reliability.
SYSTEM BACKUP | TEST RESTORATION USING SAMPLING Use a sample of backup information in the restoration of selected system functions as part of contingency plan testing.
Discussion: Organizations need assurance that system functions can be restored correctly and can support established organizational missions. To ensure that the selected system functions are thoroughly exercised during contingency plan testing, a sample of backup information is retrieved to determine whether the functions are operating as intended.
SYSTEM BACKUP | SEPARATE STORAGE FOR CRITICAL INFORMATION Store backup copies of [Assignment: organization-defined critical system software and other security-related information] in a separate facility or in a fire rated container that is not collocated with the operational system.
Discussion: Separate storage for critical information applies to all critical information regardless of the type of backup storage media. Critical system software includes operating systems, middleware, cryptographic key management systems, and intrusion detection systems. Security-related information includes inventories of system hardware, software, and firmware components. Alternate storage sites, including geographically distributed architectures, serve as separate storage facilities for organizations.
SYSTEM BACKUP | TRANSFER TO ALTERNATE STORAGE SITE Transfer system backup information to the alternate storage site [Assignment: organization-defined time period and transfer rate consistent with the recovery time and recovery point objectives].
Discussion: System backup information can be transferred to alternate storage sites either electronically or by the physical shipment of storage media. Related Controls: CP-7, MP-3, MP-4, MP-5.
SYSTEM BACKUP | REDUNDANT SECONDARY SYSTEM Conduct system backup by maintaining a redundant secondary system that is not collocated with the primary system and that can be activated without loss of information or disruption to operations.
Discussion: The effect of system backup can be achieved by maintaining a redundant secondary system that mirrors the primary system, including the replication of information. If this type of redundancy is in place and there is sufficient geographic separation between the two systems, the secondary system can also serve as the alternate processing site.
SYSTEM BACKUP | DUAL AUTHORIZATION FOR DELETION OR DESTRUCTION Enforce dual authorization for the deletion or destruction of [Assignment: organization-defined backup information].
Discussion: Dual authorization ensures that the deletion or destruction of backup information cannot occur unless two qualified individuals carry out the task. Individuals deleting or destroying backup information possess the skills or expertise to determine if the proposed deletion or destruction of information reflects organizational policies and procedures. Dual authorization may also be known as two-person control. To reduce the risk of collusion, organizations consider rotating dual authorization duties to other individuals.
SYSTEM BACKUP | CRYPTOGRAPHIC PROTECTION Implement cryptographic mechanisms to prevent unauthorized disclosure and modification of [Assignment: organization-defined backup information].
Discussion: The selection of cryptographic mechanisms is based on the need to protect the confidentiality and integrity of backup information. The strength of mechanisms selected is commensurate with the security category or classification of the information. Cryptographic protection applies to system backup information in storage at both primary and alternate locations. Organizations that implement cryptographic mechanisms to protect information at rest also consider cryptographic key management solutions.

title: TELECOMMUNICATIONS SERVICES
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • CHECK: Review of active alternate telecommunications service agreements and accompanying contingency plan documentation.
     WHY: Visible, current contracts and plans signal that the organization has formally arranged alternate channels for communications if primary circuits fail.
     HOW: Interview relevant stakeholders, review signed documents (contracts, SLAs, written contingency plans) to ensure they reference both primary and alternate providers, specified time periods, and rescue protocols.
     FAIL: Absence of documented contracts, outdated agreements, or plans that are missing critical details on alternate service activation.

   • CHECK: Evidence of routine failover tests and monitoring logs that include switching to alternate telecommunications.
     WHY: Operational logs and test results demonstrate that alternate services are not only documented but actively maintained and periodically verified in real scenarios.
     HOW: Access system logs, incident reports, and test schedules showing activation of alternate telecom channels during simulated outages or during actual events.
     FAIL: No testing reports, logs that do not reflect an alternate service trial, or indications that failover plans never move beyond theoretical documentation.

   • CHECK: Telecommunications Service Priority (TSP) enrollment and priority-of-service provisions for mission-critical lines.
     WHY: Enrollment in TSP and explicit priority provisioning indicates that the organization has additional measures to expedite service restoration under FCC rules, thereby reinforcing resilience.
     HOW: Verify FCC documentation, contracts, or government-issued confirmations that indicate TSP enrollment and priority-of-service clauses in the service agreements.
     FAIL: Lack of evidence for TSP enrollment or generic language in contracts that does not specify priority provisions.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations may simply have static documentation without active evidence of testing or validation of alternate telecom pathways. Contracts might be drafted on paper but not confirmed by logs or failover exercises.
   • Some may sign contracts that reference an alternate provider, yet the alternate service could share a single point of failure with the primary service (e.g., infrastructure sharing).
   • Surface-level compliance is sometimes faked by updating documentation without performing regular, rigorous operational tests; checklists might be ticked off without evidence of real-world effectiveness.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   • CHECK: Confirm that telecom service agreements explicitly detail alternate service provisions, specified timeframes for activation, and separation of physical paths to avoid single points of failure.
     WHY: Explicit contractual language is critical to ensure that alternate services can support essential functions in a crisis.
     HOW: Review and cross-reference contracts with contingency planning documents; look for clauses that discuss both availability and distinct physical/infrastructure routes.
     FAIL: Contracts or contingency policies that do not address separate physical routes or that rely solely on the same provider’s network.

   • CHECK: Validate evidence from recent contingency or failover tests demonstrating the operational switch to alternate telecommunications services.
     WHY: Testing is the real-world indicator that the provisions in place are effective and not just theoretical.
     HOW: Examine test logs, simulation reports, and after-action reviews for details of telecom service switchovers, measured recovery times, and any issues encountered.
     FAIL: No evidence of testing, outdated tests, or test outcomes that failed to complete threshold recovery objectives.

   • CHECK: Confirm TSP or similar FCC program enrollments and priority-of-service clauses in service agreements for both primary and alternate circuits.
     WHY: Enrollment ensures preferential restoration and gives confidence that high-priority communications are maintained under stress.
     HOW: Request and review documentation or communications from the telecom provider and FCC, confirming enrollment and clear priority instructions in service agreements.
     FAIL: Inability to locate enrollment records or contractual provisions lacking explicit priority language.

   • CHECK: Verify that the alternate telecommunications setup has been independently validated to ensure it does not inadvertently share a physical infrastructure with the primary service.
     WHY: Ensuring physical and logical separation prevents a single failure from compromising both primary and alternate channels.
     HOW: Review network diagrams, conduct interviews with telecom providers, and/or use external validation (such as infrastructure audits) to confirm separate provisioning paths.
     FAIL: Congruence in network paths or undisclosed shared infrastructure that could expose both services to the same outage.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: Start by requesting evidence of recent failover tests; these are hard-to-fake operational traces that signal real preparedness. Look for automated testing logs and timestamps.
   • Critical Indicator: The presence of detailed network path diagrams with clearly separated infrastructures for primary and alternate circuits is a strong indicator of robust implementation.
   • Key Detail: Verify that priority-of-service provisions are not only included in contracts but are actively communicated and maintained in real-time operations, as delays in TSP activation can critically impact restoration speeds.
   • Tip: Be wary of documentation-only evidence; insist on corroborative system logs, third-party confirmations, and tangible proof of cross-provider coordination in emergencies.
content:
Statement:
Establish alternate telecommunications services, including necessary agreements to permit the resumption of [Assignment: organization-defined system operations] for essential mission and business functions within [Assignment: organization-defined time period] when the primary telecommunications capabilities are unavailable at either the primary or alternate processing or storage sites.

Guidance:
Telecommunications services (for data and voice) for primary and alternate processing and storage sites are in scope for CP-8. Alternate telecommunications services reflect the continuity requirements in contingency plans to maintain essential mission and business functions despite the loss of primary telecommunications services. Organizations may specify different time periods for primary or alternate sites. Alternate telecommunications services include additional organizational or commercial ground-based circuits or lines, network-based approaches to telecommunications, or the use of satellites. Organizations consider factors such as availability, quality of service, and access when entering into alternate telecommunications agreements.

Enhancements:
TELECOMMUNICATIONS SERVICES | PRIORITY OF SERVICE PROVISIONS
(a) Develop primary and alternate telecommunications service agreements that contain priority-of-service provisions in accordance with availability requirements (including recovery time objectives); and
(b) Request Telecommunications Service Priority for all telecommunications services used for national security emergency preparedness if the primary and/or alternate telecommunications services are provided by a common carrier.
Discussion: Organizations consider the potential mission or business impact in situations where telecommunications service providers are servicing other organizations with similar priority of service provisions. Telecommunications Service Priority (TSP) is a Federal Communications Commission (FCC) program that directs telecommunications service providers to give preferential treatment to users enrolled in the program when they need to add new lines or have their lines restored following a disruption of service, regardless of the cause. The FCC sets the rules and policies for the TSP program, and the Department of Homeland Security manages the TSP program. The TSP program is always in effect and not contingent on a major disaster or attack taking place. Federal sponsorship is required to enroll in the TSP program. Related Controls: None.
TELECOMMUNICATIONS SERVICES | SINGLE POINTS OF FAILURE
Obtain alternate telecommunications services to reduce the likelihood of sharing a single point of failure with primary telecommunications services.
Discussion: In certain circumstances, telecommunications service providers or services may share the same physical infrastructure, which increases the vulnerability of a single failure point. It is important to have provider transparency for the actual physical transmission capability for telecommunication services.

title: ALTERNATE PROCESSING SITE
methodology:
Control CP-7 – ALTERNATE PROCESSING SITE Validation Methodology

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: An operational alternate processing site that is visibly equipped with hardware, network connectivity, and environmental controls similar to the primary site. On-site visit reports, photos, and infrastructure layouts can serve as physical evidence.
• Technical Artifacts: Up-to-date system configuration documents, infrastructure diagrams showing geographic separation, and test logs from recovery/resumption exercises. Active monitoring data indicating the readiness state of the alternate site can also be used.
• Operational Patterns: Regular and documented failover tests, incident response drills, and contractual agreements or SLA logs with providers. Evident maintenance of updated procedures for transition and resumption that mirror the primary site’s security controls.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Faked Documentation: Organizations sometimes have formalized documents or policies in place without actually preparing the site physically – for example, contracts exist but no periodic tests are run.
• Superficial Implementations: Declaring cloud services or remote facilities as alternate sites without ensuring that the essential security controls (physical, environmental, access) are equivalent to the primary site.
• Overlooked Agreement Details: Relying solely on signed agreements without verifying that service level agreements (SLAs) include priority-of-service provisions and that all participants have confirmed operational readiness.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
• CHECK: Documented and updated alternate processing site agreements and SLAs that include transfer and resumption timelines.
  WHY: This demonstrates that formal, legally binding steps have been taken to ensure functionalities are maintained as per recovery objectives.
  HOW: Review signed contracts, SLA documents, and amendment logs; verify that these documents specify recovery time objectives (RTO) and recovery point objectives (RPO) in line with organizational needs.
  FAIL: Documentation that is outdated, vague, or missing specific provisions for time-critical operations.

• CHECK: Physical or virtual evidence of site readiness (infrastructure, configurations, and environmental controls).
  WHY: The presence of a fully equipped site that mirrors primary controls is key evidence that the alternate site is not just a policy declaration.
  HOW: Conduct a site visit (or video walkthrough for cloud sites), examine configuration records, inspect maintenance logs for environmental conditions, and check access controls and physical security measures.
  FAIL: Significant discrepancies between the primary and alternate sites, unmaintained infrastructure, or lack of control consistency.

• CHECK: Routine and successfully executed failover and resumption tests.
  WHY: Regular testing provides operational proof that the alternate processing capability is practicable in a real event scenario.
  HOW: Review logs and technical reports from contingency drills; interview operations staff; verify issues identified in previous tests have been remedied.
  FAIL: Lack of recent or comprehensive test results, failed drills, or test evidence that does not align with documented recovery objectives.

• CHECK: Evidence of geographic or logical separation from the primary site to mitigate common threat vectors.
  WHY: Sufficient separation is critical to ensure that both sites are not affected by the same disruptive event.
  HOW: Review site location documentation, disaster risk assessments, and mapping of geographic separation; verify details against organizational threat models.
  FAIL: Insufficient separation that does not meet the risk criteria defined, or unclear separation boundaries in documentation.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut Validation: Rely on automation logs from monitoring systems that report the readiness state and alert statuses of the alternate site. An integrated dashboard that audits environmental controls, hardware status, and network connectivity can quickly reveal gaps.
• Critical Indicators: A well-maintained incident recovery test schedule combined with after-action reports is one of the most reliable operational indicators of true alternate site preparedness. Also, heavily scrutinize any ‘paper-only’ contingency plans.
• Key Details: Ensure that the contract terms stipulate actionable recovery timelines and that the alternate site's configurations truly replicate those at the primary site. Pay special attention to provisions around access controls and physical security protocols, as these can be easily overlooked if not explicitly tested.

This methodology focuses on active, demonstrable controls rather than documentation on paper, emphasizing operational effectiveness and tangible recovery preparations that can’t be easily faked.
content:
Statement:
a. Establish an alternate processing site, including necessary agreements to permit the transfer and resumption of [Assignment: organization-defined system operations] for essential mission and business functions within [Assignment: organization-defined time period consistent with recovery time and recovery point objectives] when the primary processing capabilities are unavailable;

b. Make available at the alternate processing site, the equipment and supplies required to transfer and resume operations or put contracts in place to support delivery to the site within the organization-defined time period for transfer and resumption; and

c. Provide controls at the alternate processing site that are equivalent to those at the primary site.

Guidance:
Alternate processing sites are geographically distinct from primary processing sites and provide processing capability if the primary processing site is not available. The alternate processing capability may be addressed using a physical processing site or other alternatives, such as failover to a cloud-based service provider or other internally or externally provided processing service. Geographically distributed architectures that support contingency requirements may also be considered alternate processing sites. Controls that are covered by alternate processing site agreements include the environmental conditions at alternate sites, access rules, physical and environmental protection requirements, and the coordination for the transfer and assignment of personnel. Requirements are allocated to alternate processing sites.

Enhancements:
ALTERNATE PROCESSING SITE | SEPARATION FROM PRIMARY SITE Identify an alternate processing site that is sufficiently separated from the primary processing site to reduce susceptibility to the same threats. Discussion: Threats that affect alternate processing sites are defined in organizational assessments of risk and include natural disasters, structural failures, hostile attacks, and errors of omission or commission. Organizations determine what is considered a sufficient degree of separation between primary and alternate processing sites based on the types of threats that are of concern. Related Controls: RA-3.
ALTERNATE PROCESSING SITE | ACCESSIBILITY Identify potential accessibility problems to alternate processing sites in the event of an area-wide disruption or disaster and outlines explicit mitigation actions. Discussion: Area-wide disruptions refer to those types of disruptions that are broad in geographic scope with such determinations made by organizations based on organizational assessments of risk. Related Controls: RA-3.
ALTERNATE PROCESSING SITE | PRIORITY OF SERVICE Develop alternate processing site agreements that contain priority-of-service provisions in accordance with availability requirements (including recovery time objectives). Discussion: Priority of service agreements refer to negotiated agreements with service providers that ensure that organizations receive priority treatment consistent with their availability requirements and the availability of information resources for logical alternate processing and/or at the physical alternate processing site. Organizations establish recovery time objectives as part of contingency planning. Related Controls: None.
ALTERNATE PROCESSING SITE | PREPARATION FOR USE Prepare the alternate processing site so that the site can serve as the operational site supporting essential mission and business functions. Discussion: Site preparation includes establishing configuration settings for systems at the alternate processing site consistent with the requirements for such settings at the primary site and ensuring that essential supplies and logistical considerations are in place. Related Controls: CM-2, CM-6, CP-4.
ALTERNATE PROCESSING SITE | INABILITY TO RETURN TO PRIMARY SITE Plan and prepare for circumstances that preclude returning to the primary processing site. Discussion: There may be situations that preclude an organization from returning to the primary processing site such as if a natural disaster (e.g., flood or hurricane) damaged or.

title: ALTERNATE STORAGE SITE
methodology:
Control: CP-6 – ALTERNATE STORAGE SITE

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: You should be able to review signed agreements with data center or storage providers for alternate storage. There should be documented evidence of separate physical facilities geographically distant enough to reduce the risk of a common disruption.
   • Technical Artifacts: Network diagrams, backup storage logs, or replication configuration files confirming that backup data is transferred and stored at a site separate from the primary data center.
   • Operational Patterns: Periodic backup runs, scheduled data transfers, environmental monitoring reports, and incident response testing demonstrating recovery from the alternate site.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Fake Compliance: Organizations may show documentation in form of contracts or physical pictures without verifying that active and operational backup processes occur at the remote location.
   • Surface-level Implementations: Configurations which replicate data to a secondary location that is not geographically or operationally separated to the extent needed (e.g., same metropolitan area), or paper-only contingency plans without active tests.
   • Technical Gaps: Failure to periodically test retrieval and restoration of backups from the alternate site that could hide potential operational deficiencies.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify the existence of signed agreements and operational contracts with an alternate storage provider.
   WHY: Active, binding agreements and detailed SLAs indicate that responsibilities, access controls, and physical and environmental protections are enforced.
   HOW: Interview facility managers and review contract documents and SLAs; verify what environmental and physical controls are outlined and test compliance of the alternate site's systems.
   FAIL: Absence of current contracts or reliance solely on internal policy documents without third-party evidence.

   CHECK: Examine network diagrams and data replication configuration settings.
   WHY: Detailed technical documentation and configuration data show that backup data is consistently and automatically replicated to an alternate site.
   HOW: Access and review network architecture diagrams, scheduled job logs, and replication/configuration files; confirm that the alternate site is geographically and network-wise separate.
   FAIL: Diagrams that depict the alternate site as an appended or co-located service, or replication configurations that do not align with geographic separation requirements.

   CHECK: Test the retrieval and restoration process from the alternate storage site.
   WHY: A hands-on restoration test is strong evidence that the alternate site is not only in place but also operational and effective in an actual recovery exercise.
   HOW: Conduct a simulated restoration exercise or inspect logs from recent recovery events; ensure that data can be retrieved intact and in a timely manner.
   FAIL: Inability to retrieve backup data, significant delays in restoration, or lack of documented tests indicating successful recoveries.

   CHECK: Review periodic testing and contingency exercise reports.
   WHY: Regularly scheduled tests and updates provide assurance that all elements of the alternate storage site are actively monitored and maintained.
   HOW: Request and review test reports, incident logs, and change management records that reflect routine drills and any incident remediation actions related to the alternate storage site.
   FAIL: No evidence of testing, outdated reports, or unaddressed discrepancies between test findings and implemented improvements.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Verification Shortcut: Inspect the backup process logs and monitoring alerts; these are often automated and difficult to fake. They provide a real-time look into whether data is going offsite as intended.
   • Critical Indicators: Pay careful attention to the geographic and physical separation details. Even if a remote site exists, nearby locations with similar environmental risks should raise red flags.
   • Detailing: Look for environmental controls at the alternate site (e.g., fire suppression, climate control, physical access logging) and confirm that they match or exceed the primary site’s standards. A gap here is a common oversight.
   • Operational Drift: Ensure that any updates to the primary system’s backup process are mirrored at the alternate site – discrepancies may suggest that the alternate storage site was set up as a one-off compliance tick rather than an operational necessity.
content:
Statement:
a. Establish an alternate storage site, including necessary agreements to permit the storage and retrieval of system backup information; and

b. Ensure that the alternate storage site provides controls equivalent to that of the primary site.

Guidance:
Alternate storage sites are geographically distinct from primary storage sites and maintain duplicate copies of information and data if the primary storage site is not available. Similarly, alternate processing sites provide processing capability if the primary processing site is not available. Geographically distributed architectures that support contingency requirements may be considered alternate storage sites. Items covered by alternate storage site agreements include environmental conditions at the alternate sites, access rules for systems and facilities, physical and environmental protection requirements, and coordination of delivery and retrieval of backup media. Alternate storage sites reflect the requirements in contingency plans so that organizations can maintain essential mission and business functions despite compromise, failure, or disruption in organizational systems.

Enhancements:
ALTERNATE STORAGE SITE | SEPARATION FROM PRIMARY SITE Identify an alternate storage site that is sufficiently separated from the primary storage site to reduce susceptibility to the same threats.
Discussion: Threats that affect alternate storage sites are defined in organizational risk assessments and include natural disasters, structural failures, hostile attacks, and errors of disruption in organizational systems.

title: CONTINGENCY PLAN UPDATE
methodology:
Below is the detailed validation methodology for the CP-5 Contingency Plan Update control.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • A maintained revision history in the contingency plan document that shows periodic review and updates.
   • Evidence of after-action or post-incident reviews influencing plan updates.
   • Scheduled review cycles and update logs within risk management or business continuity platforms demonstrating operational integration.
   • Records of simulation exercises, drills, or tests where the plan’s updated procedures were validated.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations may simply re-date an old plan without substantive changes to address evolving threats or organizational changes.
   • Focusing solely on policy documentation without correlating update events to actual system or process changes.
   • Bypassing real testing and relying on static or paper checks rather than simulated exercises that reveal gaps in the updated plan.
   • Limited audit trails that only capture administrative updates rather than operational implementation (i.e., no evidence of test results or updates after lessons learned).

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Review the revision history and update logs of the contingency plan.
   WHY: A detailed revision history confirms that the plan is periodically updated with new lessons from exercises or incidents.
   HOW: Access the master contingency plan, examine the revision log, and check for timestamps, rationale for changes, and evidence of review meetings.
   FAIL: An absent or superficial revision log with entries limited to superficial re-dates or missing context around changes.

   CHECK: Verify the schedule of reviews and updates, ensuring it aligns with policy requirements or risk assessments.
   WHY: A formal review schedule demonstrates proactive planning and compliance with update cycles.
   HOW: Look for documented review schedules or calendar invites, meeting minutes from review sessions, or system-generated reminders for updates.
   FAIL: A missing or outdated review schedule, or indications that the plan was not reviewed within the expected timeframe.

   CHECK: Validate evidence from exercises or simulated events that incorporate the updated plan procedures.
   WHY: Real-world exercises show that updates are operationally effective and tested.
   HOW: Request exercise reports, after-action reviews, and demonstration logs; interview incident response or business continuity teams about recent drills.
   FAIL: No test reports, or test documentation that doesn’t correlate with any recorded contingency plan updates.

   CHECK: Assess incident response records to determine if past incidents triggered targeted updates in the contingency plan.
   WHY: Actual incident responses that result in plan updates show a dynamic process taking lessons from real events.
   HOW: Review incident logs and associated follow-up documents (post-incident reports) and check correspondence with update logs.
   FAIL: Incident records exist without any follow-up actions in the contingency plan, or mismatches between incident lessons and plan changes.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Verification shortcuts: Focus on integrated systems that log changes automatically (e.g., configuration management databases), which are harder to fake than solely manual entries.
   • Critical indicators: Look for updates that coincide with major organizational changes or new technology deployments – these are typically reflected in substantial revision notes.
   • Details to prioritize: Verify that subsequent testing or simulation exercises occurred post-update. This step is a strong indicator that the updates were not merely paperwork changes.
   • Additional check: If possible, use automated systems to track document versioning and update frequencies alongside scheduled review cycles.

Overall, the control’s strength lies in the combination of documented updates and the operational evidence that those updates improve the organization's resilience. The methodology emphasizes cross-checking administrative records with dynamic testing and incident response outcomes to verify effective implementation.

title: CONTINGENCY PLAN TESTING
methodology:
Below is a practical methodology to validate CP-4 (Contingency Plan Testing) with concrete checks and expert tips:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Evidence that periodic contingency plan tests are performed (e.g., test schedule calendars, test execution logs, and post-test review meeting minutes).
• Artifacts such as after-action reports, corrective action tracking documents, and automated test system logs that detail test scenarios, results, and remedial activities.
• Operational signs include active engagement during tabletop exercises, simulation drills at alternate processing sites, and hands-on test participation by relevant personnel.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations might simply produce dated documents or test calendars without actual full exercise evidence or realistic simulations. In some cases, test results might be retroactively ‘generated’ for compliance without proper execution.
• Surface-level compliance may include a review-only walkthrough or limited checks that do not exercise the full recovery, reconstitution processes, or coordination with other related plans. 
• Frequently, evidence for automated testing or alternate site drills might be missing, incomplete, or not truly reflective of operational readiness.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
• CHECK: Verify the Contingency Test Execution Schedule
  WHY: A documented and adhered-to schedule demonstrates that periodic testing is planned and executed as required.
  HOW: Review the test calendars for dates and types of tests (tabletop, simulation, full recovery exercises), cross-check logs in test management systems, and interview the contingency planning team about recent test activities.
  FAIL: Absence of any formal schedule, outdated or non-adherence to the schedule, or reliance solely on retrospective documentation indicates non-compliance.

• CHECK: Examine Detailed Test Results & Corrective Action Records
  WHY: Detailed test reports and corrective action tracking help verify that tests were not only performed but also review deficiencies and proactive improvements.
  HOW: Inspect after-action reports showing test objectives, observations, identified weaknesses, and corrective action timelines. Verify that action items were assigned and resolved, and check for corroboration in change management systems.
  FAIL: Generic or missing test reports, lack of linkage between test findings and corrective actions, or evidence that corrective actions were never initiated indicate a failure.

• CHECK: Confirm Execution of Automated Testing & Alternate Site Exercises
  WHY: Automated testing and alternate site validation are critical to ensuring realistic hands-on assessments and readiness for an actual contingency event.
  HOW: Access automated testing logs, system outputs, and demonstration of test scripts. For alternate site exercises, review visit logs, simulation outcomes, and any documented evaluation of facility capabilities.
  FAIL: Incomplete log records, inconsistent automated results, or evidence that scheduled alternate site tests were canceled or superficially executed undermine the control.

• CHECK: Validate Coordination with Related Organizational Plans
  WHY: Coordination among related plans (business continuity, disaster recovery, etc.) confirms an integrated approach to contingency readiness and cross-functional testing.
  HOW: Look for meeting minutes, cross-departmental communications, or integrated test events that include representatives from all relevant planning areas. Interview key stakeholders to confirm collaboration.
  FAIL: Isolated contingency planning exercises without evidence of engagement from related operational teams indicate a siloed and ineffective approach.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Request a live demonstration of the automated testing tool or obtain a walkthrough of a recently executed exercise. Live or real-time evidence is hard to counterfeit.
• Critical Indicator: Pay close attention to the remediation trail—proper logs that show how deficiencies identified in tests were corrected and re-tested. Lack of follow-through is a major red flag.
• Detail Matters: Ensure that the tests are comprehensive (including full recovery and reconstitution) and not just a walkthrough. Verify that exercises challenge the operational readiness (e.g., involving unexpected interruptions and evaluation of communication channels).
• Interview personnel (e.g., DR coordinators, IT operations, and alternate site managers) to ensure that the documentation aligns with actual operational practices.

This practical methodology for CP-4 ensures that not only does the documentation exist, but that the contingency plan is actively tested, weaknesses are addressed, and readiness is maintained across all critical scenarios.
content:
Statement:
a. Test the contingency plan for the system [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] using the following tests to determine the effectiveness of the plan and the readiness to execute the plan: [Assignment: organization-defined tests].
b. Review the contingency plan test results; and
c. Initiate corrective actions, if needed.

Guidance:
Methods for testing contingency plans to determine the effectiveness of the plans and identify potential weaknesses include checklists, walk-through and tabletop exercises, simulations (parallel or full interrupt), and comprehensive exercises. Organizations conduct testing based on the requirements in contingency plans and include an evaluation of the impact on operations, assets, and individuals due to contingency scenarios.

Enhancements:
CONTINGENCY PLAN TESTING | COORDINATE WITH RELATED PLANS: Coordinate contingency plan testing with organizational elements responsible for related plans. Discussion: Plans related to contingency planning include Business Continuity Plans, Disaster Recovery Plans, Continuity of Operations Plans, Crisis Communications Plans, Critical Infrastructure Plans, Cyber Incident Response Plans, and Occupant Emergency Plans. Coordination does not require creating new organizational elements but does require cooperation with responsible entities. Related Controls: IR-8, PM-8.
CONTINGENCY PLAN TESTING | ALTERNATE PROCESSING SITE: Test the contingency plan at the alternate processing site: (a) To familiarize contingency personnel with the facility and available resources; and (b) To evaluate the capabilities of the alternate processing site to support contingency operations. Discussion: On-site visits provide insights into vulnerabilities and help refine the contingency plan. Related Controls: CP-7.
CONTINGENCY PLAN TESTING | AUTOMATED TESTING: Test the contingency plan using [Assignment: organization-defined automated mechanisms]. Discussion: Automated testing facilitates thorough coverage of contingency issues and realistic simulation of test scenarios. Related Controls: None.
CONTINGENCY PLAN TESTING | FULL RECOVERY AND RECONSTITUTION: Include a full recovery and reconstitution of the system to a known state as part of contingency plan testing. Discussion: Recovery involves executing contingency activities to restore mission functions, and reconstitution occurs after recovery. (Text continues...)

title: CONTINGENCY TRAINING
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for CP-3 (Contingency Training) that focuses on visible evidence, potential evasion tactics, concrete validation steps, and expert tips:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Training Records: Look for automated records (logs from Learning Management Systems) that show training sessions delivered, including dates, completion metrics, and role-specific content.
   • Updated Material: Confirm that the contingency training content includes details related to changes from system updates, plan tests, and simulated events, and that it’s periodically reviewed.
   • Simulated Exercises Evidence: Check for scheduled and conducted simulated contingency events, with documented attendance, after-action reports, and evaluation measures.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Blanket Training Policies: Organizations may claim they have contingency training policies but provide only generic, non-role-specific training that doesn’t align with responsibilities.
   • Outdated Content: Documents may exist showing training was done, but the content might not be updated when system changes or real incidents occur.
   • Incomplete Logging: Some organizations log training completions in spreadsheets or manual logs that can be easily manipulated, rather than using tamper-resistant systems.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify that new personnel assigned contingency roles receive training within the defined time period.
   WHY: Ensures that individuals are aware of their responsibilities before engaging in contingency operations.
   HOW: Review automated time-stamped training logs from the LMS; interview new hires on their training experiences.
   FAIL: Missing, delayed, or manual entries that lack a clear time stamp demonstrating on-time training delivery.

   CHECK: Examine the training content for evidence of updates after system changes or contingency events.
   WHY: Demonstrates the training remains relevant and aligned with current operational requirements.
   HOW: Compare version histories of training modules, look for annotations tied to system changes or incident lessons learned, and review update logs.
   FAIL: Outdated or generic content lacking any record of periodic review or event-driven update.

   CHECK: Confirm the presence and execution of simulated contingency events in the training records.
   WHY: Simulated events are critical for testing responses and ensuring practical readiness.
   HOW: Review after-action reports, event scheduling records, and participant feedback forms from simulated training exercises.
   FAIL: Evidence that simulated events were only planned on paper, or that no real exercises were executed.

   CHECK: Validate that training is role-specific and that personnel in different contingency roles receive appropriate content.
   WHY: Ensures the training is tailored to the specific duties and risk environments of each role.
   HOW: Inspect training curricula against role descriptions and interview role incumbents regarding the relevance of the training received.
   FAIL: One-size-fits-all training modules that do not reflect the unique needs and responsibilities of various roles.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Verification Shortcut: Request a sample of training records directly from an automated system rather than relying solely on policy documentation; a live demonstration can quickly reveal whether the training process is operational and automated.
   • Key Indicator: A lack of periodic updates in the training content (especially following significant system changes or incidents) is a red flag.
   • Critical Detail: Ensure that simulated events are not just theoretical exercises; real-life participation and follow-up reviews (documented improvement actions) are the gold standard for effective contingency training.
   • Automation Focus: Prioritize systems offering real-time, tamper-evident audit logs for training events over static documents.

This methodology emphasizes hands-on verification and process evidence to ensure that the contingency training not only exists as a policy artifact but is operationally effective and practically enforced.
content:
Statement:
a. Provide contingency training to system users consistent with assigned roles and responsibilities:
1. Within [Assignment: organization-defined time period] of assuming a contingency role or responsibility;
2. When required by system changes; and
3. [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] thereafter; and
b. Review and update contingency training content [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] and following [Assignment: organization-defined events].

Guidance:
Contingency training provided by organizations is linked to the assigned roles and responsibilities of organizational personnel to ensure that the appropriate content and level of detail is included in such training. It covers various scenarios depending on the role—from simple reporting instructions to detailed procedures for system administrators and organizational officials. Training updates may be driven by plan tests, incidents, or changes in regulations.

Enhancements:
CONTINGENCY TRAINING | SIMULATED EVENTS: Discussion: The use of simulated events creates an environment for personnel to experience actual threat events, including cyber-attacks that disable websites, ransomware attacks that encrypt organizational data on servers, hurricanes that damage or destroy organizational facilities, or hardware or software failures. Related Controls: None.
CONTINGENCY TRAINING | MECHANISMS USED IN TRAINING ENVIRONMENTS: Employ mechanisms used in operations to provide a more thorough and realistic contingency training environment. Discussion: Operational mechanisms refer to processes established to support a particular organizational mission or business objective. Actual mission processes, systems, and facilities may be used to generate simulated events and enhance training realism. Related Controls: None.

title: CONTINGENCY PLAN
methodology:
Below is the practical validation methodology for CP-2 (Contingency Plan):

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: A formally approved, version-controlled contingency plan document available on secure internal repositories. Distribution records, meeting minutes for review/approval sessions, and documented test/exercise reports support its existence.
   • Technical Artifacts: Metadata in configuration management systems (e.g., timestamps of updates, reviewer sign-offs), distribution lists (with contact details), drill or simulation logs, and incident response integration logs.
   • Operational Patterns: Regular, scheduled reviews with evidence of plan updates based on actual exercises, lessons‐learned reports, and evidence of coordinated contingency exercises integrated with incident handling.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Fake Compliance: Organizations might produce a static, outdated contingency plan solely for compliance reviews without executing or testing it. An outdated distribution list or incomplete role definitions can mask lack of operational activity.
   • Surface-Level Implementations: A contingency plan that exists only on paper, lacks actual testing or integration with incident response, or where distribution to key staff is merely claimed but not evidenced with logs or acknowledgments.
   • Technical Gaps: Missing change logs, absence of integration with capacity planning or alternate site operations, and non-reviewed or unsanctioned plans that do not reflect current organizational responsibilities.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Review the latest version of the contingency plan document in the secure repository.
   WHY: A current, version-controlled plan confirms that the organization has developed and maintains a plan with mission priorities, recovery objectives, roles/responsibilities, and restoration metrics.
   HOW: Access the internal compliance repository or configuration management system to locate the document. Verify that the document includes sections on mission/business functions, contingency roles (with contact info), restoration priorities, and metrics along with an approval stamp and review date.
   FAIL: If the document is dated, missing key sections (e.g., roles or metrics), or shows no evidence of periodic review or managerial approval.

   CHECK: Verify distribution records and conduct brief interviews with key contingency personnel.
   WHY: Evidence that the plan is distributed and understood by relevant staff proves it is operational rather than merely documented.
   HOW: Review distribution logs/email announcements and interview a sample of identified personnel to confirm they received the plan, understand their roles, and are aware of update procedures.
   FAIL: If personnel are unaware of the plan's contents, or no distribution records exist.

   CHECK: Examine logs or records for contingency plan testing exercises and lessons learned.
   WHY: Regular testing and incorporation of lessons learned demonstrate that the plan is actively maintained and integrated with incident handling activities.
   HOW: Request exercise records, post-mortem reports, or after-action reviews; check for updates in the plan following tests or actual incidents. Look for evidence of revised capacity planning or alternate site operations.
   FAIL: If testing logs are absent, or if post-exercise reviews do not result in actionable changes to the plan.

   CHECK: Confirm coordination between contingency planning and incident handling activities.
   WHY: Coordination ensures that the plan is not isolated and that it supports real-time incident response.
   HOW: Review incident response procedures and check for cross-references to the contingency plan. Interview incident response team members and review incident logs to see if contingency procedures were activated during an incident.
   FAIL: If there’s no clear evidence of interlinking procedures between incident handling and contingency planning.

   CHECK: Validate protection measures for the plan (ensuring it is not susceptible to unauthorized disclosure/modification).
   WHY: Ensuring the plan is secure prevents adversaries from learning or compromising it while guaranteeing integrity during updates.
   HOW: Verify access controls on the repository where the plan is stored and review system access logs. Confirm that modification history is tracked and that only authorized personnel have rights to update the document.
   FAIL: If access logs reveal broad or unauthorized access, or if the plan lacks version control and audit trails.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Look for a robust review history: Frequent, documented reviews with minor adjustments indicate that the plan is living and situationally aware.
   • Ask for sample evidence of coordination during an incident – a quick guide is to check incident logs and post-incident reviews that reference the contingency plan.
   • Verify that distribution methods include both digital and perhaps in-person training sessions to ensure operational awareness.
   • Key indicators of poor implementation include static, outdated documents with no evidence of testing or learning, and incomplete distributions or access control weaknesses.

By focusing on these practical checks, you can quickly determine whether the implementation of CP-2 is active, current, and effective rather than a mere tick-box exercise.

title: POLICY AND PROCEDURES
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Visible Evidence: Look for formally published contingency planning policy and procedures available on internal intranet portals or printed materials accessible to relevant personnel. Verify that the documents are timestamped and include clearly defined roles, responsibilities and review dates. Also, check for official distributions via email or documented briefings to all assigned roles.
• Technical Artifacts: Evidence such as version-controlled policy documents, meeting minutes discussing revisions, incident response training records, and audit logs that show periodic review and updates. Especially confirm that the designated official’s identity and responsibilities are clearly noted and endorsed at senior management levels.
• Operational Patterns: Regularly scheduled reviews and drills outlined in logged calendars or automated reminders in workflow systems, along with empirical evidence of cross-functional coordination (e.g., records of joint security and privacy program meetings) demonstrate active implementation.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Artificial Documentation: Organizations sometimes produce policy documents that appear compliant on paper but aren’t actively disseminated or integrated into operational processes. The presence of a policy file in a repository alone isn’t enough if it isn’t referenced or enforced in daily operations.
• Incomplete Dissemination: Policies shared in meetings or training sessions that do not adequately cover the comprehensive roles, responsibilities, and contingency procedures; or outdated drafts re-circulated as current.
• Insufficient Review Logs: Creating a schedule for reviews without supporting evidence (e.g., meeting minutes or revision logs) can be an indicator of superficial compliance rather than actual periodic updates.

VALIDATION STEPS:
CHECK: Verify the existence of a formally published, version-controlled contingency planning policy and procedures document.
WHY: The documented and distributed policy proves that organizational roles, responsibilities, and periodic reviews are defined and enacted.
HOW: Request access to the official repository or policy intranet page; verify document metadata (creation, update dates, revision history) and check for evidence of dissemination (e.g., distribution lists or announcement emails).
FAIL: Absence of a timestamped, version-controlled document or missing distribution evidence (e.g., no clear approval by designated authority).

CHECK: Confirm that a designated official is clearly established and identifiable in the policy and is actively involved in managing the policy’s lifecycle.
WHY: Demonstrating a clear point of authority ensures accountability and structured management of the contingency planning process.
HOW: Identify the designated official through the policy/communication materials; interview key personnel to corroborate this assignment and ask for examples of initiatives led by this official.
FAIL: If the designated official is either not named or appears to be a nominal appointment without any supporting evidence of active management.

CHECK: Review revision history and meeting minutes for periodic updates to the policy and procedures, triggered by both time intervals and significant events.
WHY: Regular reviews and updates indicate that the policy is actively maintained and reflects current risks and operational realities.
HOW: Inspect revision logs, meeting minutes or maintenance schedules; check for the frequency of updates (both time-bound and event-triggered) and ensure that updates reflect evolving advanced threats or changes in organizational structure.
FAIL: Lack of documented evidence for scheduled reviews/updates and absence of recorded discussions around contingency events or situational changes.

CHECK: Observe training records or drill exercise documentation that points to the operational application of these policies and procedures.
WHY: Actual training and exercises demonstrate that the policy is not just a document but is actively operationalized.
HOW: Request training logs or drill reports, then review these records for consistency with the contingency planning policy and correspondence with the designated official’s communications.
FAIL: No record of ongoing training or exercises, or if the exercises fail to involve the roles and responsibilities detailed in the policy.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Shortcut: Look for digital footprints such as automated email reminders and task management entries related to periodic reviews and updates. These can quickly confirm if the policy lifecycle management is operational.
• Watch for Signs of Poor Implementation: Policies that become static, with minimal or no revisions over extended periods, typically indicate that the procedures are not actively maintained. Lack of participation in relevant training or drill exercises is another red flag.
• Critical Details: Ensure the chain-of-command is explicitly documented and that there is evidence of cross-functional engagement. The absence of any cross-departmental coordination evidence may suggest the policy is not effectively integrated into the broader organizational risk management framework.
content:
Statement:
a. Develop, document, and disseminate to [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles]: 1. [Selection (one or more): Organization-level; Mission/business process-level; System-level] contingency planning policy that: (a) Addresses purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, coordination among organizational entities, and compliance; and (b) Is consistent with applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines; and 2. Procedures to facilitate the implementation of the contingency planning policy and the associated contingency planning controls; b. Designate an [Assignment: organization-defined official] to manage the development, documentation, and dissemination of the contingency planning policy and procedures; and c. Review and update the current contingency planning: 1. Policy [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] and following [Assignment: organization-defined events]; and 2. Procedures [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] and following [Assignment: organization-defined events].

Guidance:
Contingency planning policy and procedures address the controls in the CP family that are implemented within systems and organizations. The risk management strategy is an important factor in establishing such policies and procedures. Policies and procedures contribute to security and privacy assurance. Therefore, it is important that security and privacy programs collaborate on the development of contingency planning policy and procedures. Security and privacy program policies and procedures at the organization level are preferable, in general, and may obviate the need for mission- or system-specific policies and procedures. The policy can be included as part of the general security and privacy policy or be represented by multiple policies that reflect the complex nature of organizations. Procedures describe how policies or controls are implemented and can be directed at the individual or role that is the object of the procedure.

title: SIGNED COMPONENTS
methodology:
Control ID: CM-14 SIGNED COMPONENTS

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   - The presence of software/firmware update logs noting digital signature verification events indicates operational enforcement.
   - Technical artifacts such as metadata on installed components, certificate details in executable headers, and records from signature validation tools demonstrate compliance.
   - Observable operational patterns include automated scanning/validations during installation and updates that reject unsigned or improperly signed components.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   - Organizations sometimes rely solely on documentation or a static approved list without enforcing runtime verification, allowing unsigned components to bypass controls.
   - Surface-level implementations include only reviewing metadata without verifying certificate chains or expiration dates.
   - Technical gaps frequently missed: improper configuration of validation tools, exclusion of firmware components from automated checks, and lack of periodic updates to the list of approved certificate authorities.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify that digital signature validation is integrated into the installation process of new software and firmware.
     WHY: Ensures that only components signed by approved certificates are installed on systems.
     HOW: Review installation logs and observe automated validation outputs; use command line utilities (e.g., signtool, OpenSSL) on recent updates to confirm that signatures match approved certificates; verify that failed validations log an error and block installation.
     FAIL: Absence of logs indicating signature verification, installation of unsigned or improperly signed components, or use of outdated/unknown certificates.

   CHECK: Confirm that a configured, organization-approved list of trusted certificates is maintained and actively referenced during component installation.
     WHY: Validates that only recognized and approved certificate authorities are used in the verification process.
     HOW: Check system configuration files or central management databases that store the list of trusted certificates; correlate these with a sample of verified signatures from installed components.
     FAIL: Unapproved or default certificate lists, missing references, or mismatches between the approved list and certificates in use.

   CHECK: Perform an automated scan on a selection of recent software/firmware updates to confirm that digital signatures are executed and validated.
     WHY: Provides hands-on, operational evidence that the control is active during the update process.
     HOW: Use scripting or automated tools to inspect recent updates; simulate the installation of a tampered component and ensure it is rejected, while a properly signed component is accepted.
     FAIL: Lack of automated alerts for unsigned components or scenario where a tampered component is installed without interruption.

   CHECK: Review security event logs for successful and failed verification events related to software/firmware installations.
     WHY: Consistent logging indicates active monitoring and enforcement of the signature verification control.
     HOW: Access the centralized log management system; filter logs for events related to digital signature verification; match log events with approved certificate fingerprints.
     FAIL: Sparse or missing log records, absence of events linked to signature verification attempts, or no correlation with certificate authorities.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   - Shortcut your verification by targeting high-risk systems (e.g., servers or endpoint devices frequently updated) and performing deep dives there.
   - A common pitfall is relying solely on documentation; always cross-check with live system data, especially logs and automated validation outputs.
   - Critical indicator of poor implementation: logs showing frequent bypass or errors in certificate trust chain construction. Ensure that any certificate expiration or CRL (Certificate Revocation List) events are properly handled.
   - If possible, cross-reference the approved certificate list against known vendor public keys on a periodic basis to confirm integrity.
   - Simulate a fake update with a self-signed certificate and document that the system properly blocks the installation.

This methodology focuses on practical evidence and operational effectiveness, ensuring that digital signature verification is active and enforced in real-world implementations.
content:
Statement:
Prevent the installation of [Assignment: organization-defined software and firmware components] without verification that the component has been digitally signed using a certificate that is recognized and approved by the organization.

Guidance:
Software and firmware components prevented from installation unless signed with recognized and approved certificates include software and firmware version updates, patches, service packs, device drivers, and basic input/output system updates. Organizations can identify applicable software and firmware components by type, specific items, or a combination of both. Digital signatures and organizational resolution of such signatures is a method of code authentication.

title: DATA ACTION MAPPING
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: A documented and regularly updated data action map that visually (or in table format) outlines each discrete data action along with corresponding PII elements, processing system components, and their respective ownership or operational responsibilities.
• Technical Artifacts: Artifacts may include configuration management systems, metadata in data catalogs, or system diagrams showing data flows with call-outs for PII processing stages (collection, transformation, etc.). Logs from automated discovery tools may also confirm system operations.
• Operational Patterns: Regular maintenance reviews, automated scans, or integration with data loss prevention (DLP) tools indicating continuous mapping validation and observed updates in response to system changes.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Fake Compliance: Organizations may provide static documentation without linking it to the dynamic actual system operations or fail to update the document reflecting system changes. Outdated process diagrams or disconnect between documentation and live configurations are common pitfalls.
• Surface-Level Implementations: Over-relying on generic data flow diagrams that do not detail discrete data actions at each point in the data lifecycle, or aggregating disparate data processes into one broad map without context on PII specifics.
• Technical Gaps: Missing details on PII elements in individual processing flows, absence of component ownership, or lack of evidence on how data lifecycle stages are mapped in real-time systems.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Verify the existence of a dynamic data action map that identifies each discrete processing operation, the specific PII elements involved, and associated system components with owner/operator details.
   WHY: This confirms that the organization understands exactly how PII is handled at every stage, ensuring risk assessment accuracy.
   HOW: Interview data governance teams; review the latest version of the map; correlate entries with system inventory and PII registers; and cross-check change logs for updates.
   FAIL: If the map is static, outdated, or generic in nature, failing to pinpoint discrete data actions or link them to specific system components and ownership.

2. CHECK: Assess integration of the data action map with automated monitoring tools or change management systems.
   WHY: Automated integration proves that the mapping isn’t just a policy document but is actively tied to operational systems and is updated as changes occur.
   HOW: Request demonstration of tool integration (e.g., dashboards from data loss prevention systems or configuration management systems) that reflect the data action map; inspect change management logs that trigger map updates.
   FAIL: Manual or absent integration signals a potential disconnect between documented maps and real-time system operations.

3. CHECK: Validate the mapping across the complete data lifecycle (collection, transformation, use, disclosure, retention, and disposal).
   WHY: Ensuring all lifecycle stages are included confirms that the map isn’t narrowly focused, thereby providing holistic privacy risk insight.
   HOW: Use walkthroughs during system audits to trace a sample PII item from collection to disposal; ask for evidential documentation and tool outputs that capture each stage.
   FAIL: Documentation that omits any stage of the data lifecycle or shows vague process descriptors is indicative of superficial compliance.

4. CHECK: Confirm that the data action mapping process includes periodic reviews and updates to reflect system evolution.
   WHY: Regular review ensures that the map remains a living document accurately reflecting operational changes and exposures.
   HOW: Check for documented procedures for map review, evidence of periodic audits (e.g., meeting minutes, update logs, or review schedules), and validation procedures executed by internal or external auditors.
   FAIL: Lack of time-stamped updates or omission of review process evidence indicates a static, ineffectual mapping exercise.

EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Inquire directly for screenshots or live demonstrations of integrated mapping tools that show real-time updates to quickly verify active monitoring.
• Critical Indicator: The absence of linkage between map elements and automated system alerts or change logs is a red flag.
• Detail Matters: Precise identification of PII elements and their specific processing steps (rather than generalized flow charts) highlights robust data action mapping.
• Watch Out: Documentation that appears ‘canned’ for regulatory submissions may indicate lack of active implementation—always validate against live system data and operational logs.
content:
Statement:
Develop and document a map of system data actions.

Guidance:
Data actions are system operations that process personally identifiable information.
