The processing of such information encompasses the full information life cycle, which includes collection, generation, transformation, use, disclosure, retention, and disposal. A map of system data actions. Data actions include discrete data actions, elements of personally identifiable information being processed in the data actions, system components involved in the data actions, and the owners or operators of the system components. Understanding what personally identifiable information is being processed, how it is processed, and by whom provides contextual factors important to assessing the degree of privacy risk created by the system.

title: INFORMATION LOCATION
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Documented inventory: Look for an up-to-date and detailed inventory that identifies the location of organization‐defined information across system components. This could be stored in a configuration management database (CMDB) or secure document repository.
   • Access records: Verify that there is documentation listing the users with access to these systems, such as access control lists (ACLs), user role assignments, or identity management configurations.
   • Change logs: Evidence of change management processes reflecting updates to the location of information, including time-stamped records of system or component relocations.
   • Automated tool reports: Output from automated discovery or data mapping tools showing where sensitive information is processed and stored.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Outdated or static spreadsheets: Organizations might present a static list that has not been updated to reflect current system architecture or access changes.
   • Documentation without correlation: Documents that exist but do not tie information locations to active systems or verified technical configurations.
   • Ignoring automated outputs: Claiming compliance through manual reporting while neglecting evidence available through automated tool scans and system logs.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify the existence of an updated inventory or mapping document listing the location of sensitive information and system components.
   WHY: An accurate, current inventory confirms that the organization is aware of where information is processed and stored.
   HOW: Review the configuration management database (CMDB) or secure document repository for records correlating data types with system components, and compare against the latest network architecture diagrams.
   FAIL: Missing or outdated inventory records and discrepancies between documented and actual system configurations.

   CHECK: Confirm that access control lists (ACLs) or user management records include all individuals who have permissions to access relevant system components.
   WHY: This ensures that only authorized users have access to the sensitive data and that the organization is tracking who can view or process this information.
   HOW: Examine ACLs in identity management systems and cross-reference with system logs or authorization reports. Interview system administrators if necessary to verify accuracy.
   FAIL: Presence of unauthorized users or outdated permissions lists.

   CHECK: Review change management logs to ensure that any modifications to the physical/virtual locations of information are properly documented.
   WHY: Documented changes indicate that the organization is actively monitoring and updating its records when system configurations change.
   HOW: Inspect change management systems for records of location changes, review associated change tickets, and validate that mapped documents have been updated accordingly.
   FAIL: Absence of change logs or records that do not reflect recent system changes.

   CHECK: Validate automated discovery tool outputs for identifying information locations on specified system components.
   WHY: Automated reports provide objective, real-time evidence that complements manual documentation, reducing the risk of human error or outdated records.
   HOW: Run or review recent reports from automated tools (e.g., data mapping or network discovery tools) that scan the system to locate and map sensitive data. Compare outputs with documented evidence.
   FAIL: Discrepancies between automated tool outputs and the documented information location records.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Verification Shortcut: Ask system architects or administrators to provide recent screenshots or live demonstrations of the automated tools in use, which can be harder to fabricate compared to static documents.
   • Critical Indicator: A common sign of poor implementation is a mismatch between the documented list of system components and the tool-generated inventory, suggesting incomplete or infrequent updates.
   • Detail to Watch: Ensure that the change management logs are timestamped and reference specific system components; vague or generic entries are red flags.
   • Additional Note: Cross-verification with physical asset inventories and network segmentation diagrams can further corroborate the actual information locations.
content:
Statement:
a. Identify and document the location of [Assignment: organization-defined information] and the specific system components on which the information is processed and stored; b. Identify and document the users who have access to the system and system components where the information is processed and stored; and c. Document changes to the location (i.e., system or system components) where the information is processed and stored;

Guidance:
Information location addresses the need to understand where information is being processed and stored. Information location includes identifying where specific information types and information reside in system components and how information is being processed so that information flow can be understood and adequate protection and policy management provided for such information and system components. The security category of the information is also a factor in determining the controls necessary to protect the information in the system component where the information resides (see [FIPS 199]). The location of the information and system components is also a factor in the architecture and design of the system (see SA-4, SA-8, SA-17).

Enhancements:
INFORMATION LOCATION | AUTOMATED TOOLS TO SUPPORT INFORMATION LOCATION: Use automated tools to identify [Assignment: organization-defined information by information type] on [Assignment: organization-defined system components] to ensure controls are in place to protect organizational information and individual privacy. Discussion: The use of automated tools helps to increase the effectiveness and efficiency of the information location capability implemented within the system. Automation also helps organizations manage the data produced during information location activities and share such information across the organization. The output of automated information location tools can be used to guide and inform system architecture and design decisions. Related Controls: None. References: [FIPS 199], [SP 800-60-1], [SP 800-60-2].

title: USER-INSTALLED SOFTWARE
methodology:
Below is the practical validation methodology for CM-11 (User-Installed Software):

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible evidence includes published policies and procedures governing software installation (often available on an intranet or policy server) that clearly define approved applications, sources (such as an organization-designated app store), and installation privileges.
   • Technical artifacts: Group Policy Objects (GPOs), endpoint configuration management settings, and software inventory databases that restrict non-privileged installations. Logs and automated monitoring dashboards (from centralized SIEM or endpoint management tools) showing alerts for unauthorized installations further demonstrate enforcement.
   • Operational patterns: Regular automated scans and periodic manual audits verifying that only authorized software has been installed, and users not in a privileged role rarely (or never) trigger installation events outside authorized channels.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Faking compliance by having a policy document that states tight controls, but in reality, users retain admin rights on their workstations, allowing them to bypass policies easily.
   • Relying solely on manual approval processes in environments where automated controls (such as enforcement through GPOs or endpoint restriction tools) are not implemented. This leaves room for unauthorized installations to slip through unnoticed.
   • Installing monitoring tools in a way that they log data but no one actively reviews alerts or remediation actions, giving a false sense of compliance.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   • CHECK: Verify that documented policies on user-installed software exist and are easily accessible to both IT and end-users.
     WHY: Clear policies that specify allowed versus prohibited software installations and conditions for privileged installations set the baseline for compliance.
     HOW: Request a review of the official policy repository, interview policy administrators, and confirm that policy distribution mechanisms (intranet portals, newsletters, training sessions) are in place.
     FAIL: Policies are missing, outdated, or don’t address approved installation mechanisms and user roles.

   • CHECK: Confirm that technical controls restrict software installation to users with explicit administrative or privileged status, using automated mechanisms.
     WHY: Technical enforcement safeguards guarantee that even if users possess some privileges, only approved channels allow changes to system software, thereby reducing risk.
     HOW: Inspect system configuration settings and Active Directory group memberships. Review GPOs or endpoint management configurations that prevent local software installation for non-privileged accounts. Execute tests to attempt installation using a standard user account.
     FAIL: Standard users can install software without triggering any automated control, or configuration settings are missing/disabled.

   • CHECK: Identify and review logs and alerts generated by automated monitoring systems for unauthorized or non-compliant software installations.
     WHY: Operational effectiveness is best validated through real-time monitoring and how incidents are managed.
     HOW: Access SIEM or centralized logging systems to search for events related to software installations. Verify that alerts have been generated and properly escalated based on defined policy thresholds. Correlate installation events with approved app store accesses.
     FAIL: Logs are incomplete, monitoring systems are not triggering alerts or the alerts are routinely ignored with no remediation track record.

   • CHECK: Validate that periodic audits or reviews are conducted to ensure ongoing compliance with the installation policies.
     WHY: Regular reviews ensure that any gaps or unauthorized installations are detected and remedied, confirming that the policy enforcement is not a one-off event.
     HOW: Look for scheduled audit reports, interview personnel responsible for conducting compliance reviews, and check for remediation logs tied to non-compliance events.
     FAIL: There are no audit trails, or periodic reviews are not documented or seem to be retroactive rather than proactive.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: Start by checking the endpoint configuration management tool settings to quickly see if non-privileged installations are blocked. This often reveals if enforcement is automated or merely nominal.
   • Critical indicator: Look for discrepancies between documented policies and actual system/user configurations. If policies on paper state strict control but workstations enable local admin rights, there is a serious implementation gap.
   • Key detail: Pay attention to the frequency and resolution of alerts – repeated alerts for unauthorized installations without timely remediation suggest an ineffective monitoring process.
   • Don’t be fooled by logged policies alone. Confirm real-world user actions by testing installation attempts and reviewing incident response logs to ensure controls are active and enforceable.

This comprehensive methodology targets practical verification methods that rely on observable system configurations and real operational data rather than solely on documented policies. It helps auditors focus on operational effectiveness and the actual enforcement of CM-11.
content:
Statement:
a. Establish [Assignment: organization-defined policies] governing the installation of software by users;  b. Enforce software installation policies through the following methods: [Assignment: organization-defined methods]; and  c. Monitor policy compliance [Assignment: organization-defined frequency].

Guidance:
If provided the necessary privileges, users can install software in organizational systems. To maintain control over the software installed, organizations identify permitted and prohibited actions regarding software installation. Permitted software installations include updates and security patches to existing software and downloading new applications from organization-approved “app stores.” Prohibited software installations include software with unknown or suspect pedigrees or software that organizations consider potentially malicious. Policies selected for governing user-installed software are organization-developed or provided by some external entity. Policy enforcement methods can include procedural methods and automated methods.

Enhancements:
USER-INSTALLED SOFTWARE | ALERTS FOR UNAUTHORIZED INSTALLATIONS: [Withdrawn: Incorporated into CM-8(3).]
USER-INSTALLED SOFTWARE | SOFTWARE INSTALLATION WITH PRIVILEGED STATUS: Allow user installation of software only with explicit privileged status. Discussion: Privileged status can be obtained, for example, by serving in the role of system administrator.
USER-INSTALLED SOFTWARE | AUTOMATED ENFORCEMENT AND MONITORING: Enforce and monitor compliance with software installation policies using [Assignment: organization-defined automated mechanisms]. Discussion: Organizations enforce and monitor compliance with software installation policies using automated mechanisms to more quickly detect and respond to unauthorized software installation which can be an indicator of an internal or external hostile attack. Related Controls: None. References: None.

title: SOFTWARE USAGE RESTRICTIONS
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence includes an updated and actively maintained software inventory that cross-references installed software with licensing records and contract agreements. Automated license management tools and endpoint monitoring systems are often deployed.
• Technical artifacts to review include software inventory databases, license management system logs, automated audit reports, and network logs demonstrating controls over peer-to-peer file sharing technology.
• Operational patterns such as regular reconciliation of software installations against purchased licenses, scheduled internal audits, and active monitoring of network traffic for unauthorized file-sharing activity indicate that the control is active.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may simply produce static screenshots of a software inventory or provide outdated documentation to suggest compliance without demonstrating current, automated enforcement.
• Manual, infrequent reconciliations can be employed to mask non-compliance. Superficial inventory reports that don’t tie back to active endpoint monitoring are common pitfalls.
• In many cases, systems may register software installations but lack controls for peer-to-peer file sharing, making it easy to bypass restriction requirements despite appearing compliant on paper.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Verify the Active Software Inventory and License Reconciliation
   WHY: Demonstrates that software usage is tracked in real-time against licensed quantities.
   HOW: Review automated inventory reports and cross-reference them with current license agreements; check for recent reconciliation timestamps.
   FAIL: Stale reports or discrepancies between installed software counts and licensed quantities indicate a failure in enforcing controls.

2. CHECK: Inspect Automated License Management and Auditing Tools
   WHY: Automated tools reduce the risk of human error and indicate ongoing tracking of software usage.
   HOW: Access system logs, dashboards, or reports from license management tools and confirm that they are regularly updated and reflect all installed and used software.
   FAIL: Evidence of manual processes with infrequent updates or incomplete data sources suggests potential non-compliance.

3. CHECK: Review Network Controls on Peer-to-Peer File Sharing
   WHY: Ensures that P2P technologies are monitored and controlled to prevent unauthorized distribution of copyrighted material.
   HOW: Examine firewall and intrusion detection system logs for policies/blocking events related to peer-to-peer file sharing; review configurations and any triggered alerts.
   FAIL: Lack of activity logs or outdated configurations that allow for P2P traffic indicates a control gap.

4. CHECK: Conduct On-Site Endpoint Verification
   WHY: Sampling endpoint machines for installed software provides direct evidence and ensures inventory and license controls are applied in practice.
   HOW: Randomly select a set of endpoints and use automated tools (or manual checks if necessary) to list installed software; compare with centralized inventory records.
   FAIL: Mismatches between the observed software on endpoints and centralized records indicate lapses in monitoring and enforcement.

EXPERT TIPS
• Verification shortcuts include correlating timestamped audit logs with license renewal dates—recent activity confirms ongoing compliance.
• Watch for critical indicators such as endpoints showing unauthorized or untracked software installations, which are often early signs of a breakdown in inventory control.
• Ensure that the controls not only exist in documentation but have related, observable technical artifacts (logs, dashboard outputs, automated reconciliation processes) that are current and complete.
• Always probe for integration issues: isolated systems that don’t share data between endpoints, license management, and network monitoring can collectively undermine the control.
content:
Statement:
a. Use software and associated documentation in accordance with contract agreements and copyright laws; b. Track the use of software and associated documentation protected by quantity licenses to control copying and distribution; and c. Control and document the use of peer-to-peer file sharing technology to ensure that this technology is not used for the unauthorized distribution, display, performance, or reproduction of copyrighted work.

Guidance:
Discussion: Software license tracking can be accomplished by manual or automated methods, depending on organizational needs. Examples of contract agreements include software license agreements and non-disclosure agreements.

title: CONFIGURATION MANAGEMENT PLAN
methodology:
Below is a practical, experience-based validation methodology for reviewing the CM-9 Configuration Management Plan control.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• The existence of a documented configuration management plan that clearly defines roles and responsibilities, outlines configuration management processes/procedures, and identifies configuration items throughout the system development life cycle.
• Records indicating that the plan has been formally reviewed, approved, and stored in a secure location. Evidence of access control measures protecting the plan from unauthorized disclosure or tampering.
• Artifacts such as configuration item inventories, change logs, and periodic review meeting records that show active operational use of the plan.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may produce a static document without updating it to reflect current system changes or without demonstrating a process in practice.
• Faking approval by having a signature page that is not tied to actual, independent review or by delaying change control updates.
• Implementing access restrictions in policy but not in technical systems (e.g., storing the plan in unprotected shared drives).

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Verify that the configuration management plan explicitly defines roles, responsibilities, and procedures for both developmental and operational configuration management.
WHY: Clear role definitions are critical for independent oversight and ensuring proper separation between development and configuration control.
HOW: Review the document sections detailing roles, check for clarity in responsibilities, and validate against organizational charts or job descriptions. Confirm that responsibilities for configuration control are not mixed with development roles.
FAIL: If the plan is vague on roles/responsibilities or if the same personnel managing development are also managing configuration, this indicates a potential conflict of interest.

CHECK: Confirm the inclusion and management of configuration items throughout the system development life cycle.
WHY: Effective identification and management of configuration items ensure that system changes are tracked and controlled.
HOW: Identify sections detailing configuration items and compare them with the actual system inventory. Check for records or logs that demonstrate the tracking of changes to these items.
FAIL: A missing or incomplete inventory list of configuration items or absence of change tracking evidence suggests poor control.

CHECK: Validate that the plan has been formally reviewed and approved by the designated independent personnel or roles and is securely maintained against unauthorized modifications.
WHY: Formal review and approved access controls help ensure the integrity and currency of the plan.
HOW: Look for approval signatures, version history, and associated meeting minutes. Additionally, inspect access control settings on the plan’s repository (ideally an HSM or similar tool) to ensure only authorized personnel have modification rights.
FAIL: If the plan lacks evidence of independent review or if the access control mechanisms are weak (e.g., stored in an unprotected location), it is a red flag.

CHECK: Review implementation evidence such as change logs, configuration audits, and related documentation that demonstrate the plan’s effective operational use.
WHY: Artifacts like change logs show that the plan is not merely theoretical, but actively used to manage system changes.
HOW: Interview configuration management staff, inspect automated change management systems, and verify that alterations in configuration items are consistently logged and traceable.
FAIL: Inconsistent or missing change logs, or absence of records linking system changes to the configuration management plan, are indicators of poor implementation.

CHECK: Inspect the separation of duties if the plan assigns configuration management responsibilities to personnel not involved in system development.
WHY: This separation ensures quality control and reduces the risk of conflict of interest in change management processes.
HOW: Verify role assignments in the plan, then cross-check with personnel assignments to ensure that those responsible for configuration management are independent of the development and integration teams.
FAIL: Evidence that development personnel are managing configuration or that the roles are not clearly separated indicates non-compliance.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• When reviewing the configuration management plan, always check for recent update cycles and version control history – a stale document often signals poor adherence.
• Focus less on the policy narrative and more on the operational evidence – automated tools, digital logs, and system configurations that prove the plan is active.
• Cross-reference the documented roles in the plan with interviews of staff to ensure they understand and execute their responsibilities.
• Look for any “shadow” processes where undocumented workarounds bypass the prescribed plan – this is a critical indicator of practical non-compliance.
• When evaluating protection measures, verify that both technical controls (e.g., file permissions, encryption) and procedural barriers (e.g., access requests and logs) are in place and active.

This methodology emphasizes checking for real evidence, technical artifacts, and active operational processes rather than relying solely on documented policies.
content:
Statement:
Develop, document, and implement a configuration management plan for the system that: a. Addresses roles, responsibilities, and configuration management processes and procedures; b. Establishes a process for identifying configuration items throughout the system development life cycle and for managing the configuration of the configuration items; c. Defines the configuration items for the system and places the configuration items under configuration management; d. Is reviewed and approved by [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles]; and e. Protects the configuration management plan from unauthorized disclosure and modification.

Guidance:
Discussion: Configuration management activities occur throughout the system development life cycle. As such, there are developmental configuration management activities (e.g., the control of code and software libraries) and operational configuration management activities (e.g., control of installed components and how the components are configured). Configuration management plans satisfy the requirements in configuration management policies while being tailored to individual systems. Configuration management plans define processes and procedures for how configuration management is used to support system development life cycle activities.

Enhancements:
CONFIGURATION MANAGEMENT PLAN | ASSIGNMENT OF RESPONSIBILITY Assign responsibility for developing the configuration management process to organizational personnel that are not directly involved in system development. Discussion: In the absence of dedicated configuration management teams assigned within organizations, system developers may be tasked with developing configuration management processes using personnel who are not directly involved in system development or system integration. This separation of duties ensures that organizations establish and maintain a sufficient degree of independence between the system development and integration processes and configuration management processes to facilitate quality control and more effective oversight. Related Controls: None. References: [SP 800-128].

title: SYSTEM COMPONENT INVENTORY
methodology:
Below is a detailed, practical validation methodology for CM-8 SYSTEM COMPONENT INVENTORY:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• An operational, centralized system/component inventory (often within a CMDB) that is actively maintained, showing detailed attributes (e.g., serial numbers, hardware specifications, software license information, network addresses, and geographic location).
• Automated logs and evidence from configuration management tools that update inventories during component installation, removal, and system updates.
• Operational evidence of automated unauthorized component detection, including scan logs, quarantine or isolation actions, and notification records.
• Evidence of unique identifiers in the inventory to prevent duplicate accounting, and assignment details that link each component with its accountable owner.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may produce a static spreadsheet or policy documents that claim an inventory exists but do not reflect real-time changes or integration with system events.
• Failing to update the inventory at critical events (e.g., installations, removals, or system modifications) while merely referencing a policy that requires updates.
• Duplicate entries in the inventory due to improper tracking across varied networks (IPv4 vs. IPv6) or mis-assignment of components to systems, which may be hidden by superficial reviews.
• Relying on manual updates without evidence of automated mechanisms to track, monitor and correlate real-time changes.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Verify that a centralized, up-to-date system component inventory exists and is integrated with automated change management tools.
WHY: This confirms that the inventory is not merely theoretical but is continuously maintained and reflective of actual system components.
HOW: Review the CMDB or similar asset management tool configuration; check logs or automated triggers that update inventory records upon installation, removal, or configuration changes.
FAIL: Absence of integration with change management tools and evidence that only static, infrequently updated spreadsheets are used.

CHECK: Confirm that the inventory lists all required attributes at the appropriate level of granularity (e.g., unique identifier, system association, serial numbers, component types, physical locations, and ownership assignment).
WHY: Detailed granularity ensures effective accountability and prevents duplicate accounting of components.
HOW: Perform a sampling review of inventory records and verify that each entry has complete fields, including system assignments and unique identifiers. Cross-check these against hardware asset databases and software deployment lists.
FAIL: Inventory records missing key fields or features; evidence of duplicated components across systems without clear attribution.

CHECK: Evaluate the automated mechanisms for unauthorized component detection and location tracking.
WHY: Verifying that automated detection is in place shows active monitoring and proactive response to unauthorized additions.
HOW: Examine system logs from scanning tools, review configuration of automated detection tools, and confirm that alerts, isolation actions, or notifications are generated in response to unauthorized components.
FAIL: Absence of logs demonstrating periodic unauthorized component scans or a lack of correlation between automated alerts and inventory updates.

CHECK: Validate that changes in the inventory are synchronized with key system events (installations, removals, updates) and that there is evidence of review frequency as defined by the organization.
WHY: Ensuring responsiveness to changes prevents stale or inaccurate data, reducing potential vulnerabilities.
HOW: Look for timestamped update logs correlated with incident/change management records; interview system administrators to verify the operational review cycle.
FAIL: Discrepancies between system maintenance events and inventory update records; no timestamp or misaligned review frequencies.

CHECK: Check for evidence that each system component is assigned to a single system and accompanied by clear administrative accountability (e.g., naming responsible individuals, roles, or positions).
WHY: This prevents duplicate accounting and ensures prompt remedial measures if a component becomes compromised or requires maintenance.
HOW: Review sample inventory records to ensure each component has an assignment field that specifies a system and a responsible party. Validate this against role-based access control and change logs.
FAIL: Records have ambiguous system assignments or lack the necessary administrative contact information, indicating a potential gap in accountability.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Focus on automated and real-time evidence (like configuration management tool logs, automated scanning reports, and change alerts) rather than static policy documents that can be easily faked.
• Check timestamp details in inventory updates to confirm that changes correlate with actual system events and maintenance windows.
• Pay special attention to duplicate records; they are a key indicator of poor enforcement of uniqueness criteria.
• Interview operational personnel if needed – experienced administrators can quickly identify if the inventory truly reflects on-the-ground reality.
• Confirm that the integration between the inventory system and detection mechanisms (like unauthorized component detection) is functioning as designed – this is often where manual processes fail.

This methodology, with a focus on concrete, automated, and operational evidence, will help confirm the effective implementation of CM-8 SYSTEM COMPONENT INVENTORY.
content:
Statement:
a. Develop and document an inventory of system components that: 1. Accurately reflects the system; 2. Includes all components within the system; 3. Does not include duplicate accounting of components or components assigned to any other system; 4. Is at the level of granularity deemed necessary for tracking and reporting; and 5. Includes the following information to achieve system component accountability: [Assignment: organization-defined information deemed necessary to achieve effective system component accountability]; b. Review and update the system component inventory [Assignment: organization-defined frequency].

Guidance:
Discussion: System components are discrete, identifiable information technology assets that include hardware, software, and firmware. Organizations may choose to implement centralized system component inventories that include components from all organizational systems. In such situations, organizations ensure that the inventories include system-specific information required for component accountability. The information necessary for effective accountability of system components includes the system name, software owners, software version numbers, hardware inventory specifications, software license information, and for networked components, the machine names and network addresses across all implemented protocols (e.g., IPv4, IPv6). Inventory specifications include date of receipt, cost, model, serial number, manufacturer, supplier information, component type, and physical location. Preventing duplicate accounting of system components addresses the lack of accountability that occurs when component ownership and system association is not known, especially in large or complex connected systems. Effective prevention of duplicate accounting of system components necessitates use of a unique identifier for each component. For software inventory, centrally managed software that is accessed via other systems is addressed as a component of the system on which it is installed and managed. Software installed on multiple organizational systems and managed at the system level is addressed for each individual system and may appear more than once in a centralized component inventory. Restricting a system association for each software instance in the centralized inventory to avoid duplicate accounting of components. Scanning systems implementing multiple network protocols (e.g., IPv4 and IPv6) can result in duplicate components being identified in different address spaces. The implementation of CM-8(7) can help to eliminate duplicate accounting of components.

Enhancements:
SYSTEM COMPONENT INVENTORY | UPDATES DURING INSTALLATION AND REMOVAL Update the inventory of system components as part of component installations, removals, and system updates. Discussion: Organizations can improve the accuracy, completeness, and consistency of system component inventories if the inventories are updated as part of component installations or removals or during general system updates. If inventories are not updated at these key times, there is a greater likelihood that the information will not be appropriately captured and documented. System updates include hardware, software, and firmware components. Related Controls: PM-16.
SYSTEM COMPONENT INVENTORY | AUTOMATED MAINTENANCE Maintain the currency, completeness, accuracy, and availability of the inventory of system components using [Assignment: organization-defined automated mechanisms]. Discussion: Organizations maintain system inventories to the extent feasible. For example, virtual machines can be difficult to monitor because such machines are not visible to the network when not in use. In such cases, organizations maintain as up-to-date, complete, and accurate an inventory.
SYSTEM COMPONENT INVENTORY | AUTOMATED UNAUTHORIZED COMPONENT DETECTION (a) Detect the presence of unauthorized hardware, software, and firmware components within the system using [Assignment: organization-defined automated mechanisms] [Assignment: organization-defined frequency]; and (b) Take the following actions when unauthorized components are detected: [Selection (one or more): disable network access by such components; isolate the components; notify [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles]]. Discussion: Automated unauthorized component detection is applied in addition to the monitoring for unauthorized remote connections and mobile devices. Monitoring for unauthorized system components may be accomplished on an ongoing basis or by the periodic scanning of systems for that purpose. Automated mechanisms may also be used to prevent the connection of unauthorized components (see CM-7(9)). Automated mechanisms can be implemented in systems or in separate system components. When acquiring and implementing automated mechanisms, organizations consider whether such mechanisms depend on the ability of the system component to support an agent or suppliant in order to be detected since some types of components do not have or cannot support agents (e.g., IoT devices, sensors). Isolation can be achieved, for example, by placing unauthorized system components in separate domains or subnets or quarantining such components. This type of component isolation is commonly referred to as “sandboxing.” Related Controls: AC-19, CA-7, RA-5, SC-3, SC-44, SI-3, SI-4, SI-7.
SYSTEM COMPONENT INVENTORY | ACCOUNTABILITY INFORMATION Include in the system component inventory information, a means for identifying by [Selection (one or more): name; position; role], individuals responsible and accountable for administering those components. Discussion: Identifying individuals who are responsible and accountable for administering system components ensures that the assigned components are properly administered and that organizations can contact those individuals if some action is required (e.g., when the component is determined to be the source of a breach, needs to be recalled or replaced, or needs to be relocated). Related Controls: AC-3.
SYSTEM COMPONENT INVENTORY | NO DUPLICATE ACCOUNTING OF COMPONENTS [Withdrawn: Incorporated into CM-8].
SYSTEM COMPONENT INVENTORY | ASSESSED CONFIGURATIONS AND APPROVED DEVIATIONS Include assessed component configurations and any approved deviations to current deployed configurations in the system component inventory. Discussion: Assessed configurations and approved deviations focus on configuration settings established by organizations for system components, the specific components that have been assessed to determine compliance with the required configuration settings, and any approved deviations from established configuration settings. Related Controls: None.
SYSTEM COMPONENT INVENTORY | CENTRALIZED REPOSITORY Provide a centralized repository for the inventory of system components.
SYSTEM COMPONENT INVENTORY | AUTOMATED LOCATION TRACKING Support the tracking of system components by geographic location using [Assignment: organization-defined automated mechanisms]. Discussion: The use of automated mechanisms to track the location of system components can increase the accuracy of component inventories. Such capability may help organizations rapidly identify the location and responsible individuals of system components that have been compromised, breached, or are otherwise in need of mitigation actions. The use of tracking mechanisms can be coordinated with senior agency officials for privacy if there are implications that affect individual privacy. Related Controls: None.
SYSTEM COMPONENT INVENTORY | ASSIGNMENT OF COMPONENTS TO SYSTEMS (a) Assign system components to a system; and (b) Receive an acknowledgement of this assignment [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles] of this assignment. Discussion: System components that are not assigned to a system may be unmanaged, lack the required protection, and become an organizational vulnerability. Related Controls: None.

title: LEAST FUNCTIONALITY
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for CM-7 (Least Functionality) based on real-world verification:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: System configuration screens or hardened images clearly document the enabled ports, protocols, and services. Hardening guides or baselines (e.g., CIS benchmarks) in use can indicate removal of nonessential software.
   • Technical Artifacts: Logs from automated network scanners and Intrusion Detection/Prevention Systems (IDS/IPS) that show only mission-essential services running; system audit logs confirming periodic reviews and removals; configuration management system output listing enabled/disabled services.
   • Operational Patterns: Regularly scheduled change control or system scans verifying that only approved functions are active; observable enforcement of an allow-by-exception model for software execution when periodic automated reviews occur.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Faking Compliance: Organizations might simply create a static document asserting that only mission-essential capabilities are enabled, without ensuring the actual system images or runtime settings match.
   • Surface-level Implementations: Leaving default software enabled but simply ‘documenting’ that it is not used, rather than actually disabling or removing it. Skipping regular reviews and relying on one-time configurations that can drift over time.
   • Technical Gaps: Not integrating automated scans to detect unauthorized ports, protocols, or functions; failure to update approved lists when system functions change; weak enforcement of deny-by-exception policies leading to incidental execution of nonessential software.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   • CHECK: Verify system configuration files, baseline images, or management consoles to ensure that only mission-essential services, ports, and protocols are enabled.
     WHY: A configuration that lists only approved functions is direct evidence of least functionality in effect.
     HOW: Log into system management interfaces, inspect configuration files (e.g., firewall rules, systemd service lists, or similar artifacts) and compare against an inventory of allowed functions.
     FAIL: Presence of residual default services, ports, or protocols not consistent with the organization’s minimal functionality list.

   • CHECK: Review automated scan reports (e.g., Nessus, Nmap, or internal network scans) to verify that unauthorized services and ports are not discoverable on the systems under review.
     WHY: Scanning tools provide observable evidence that only approved functions are accessible externally/network-wise.
     HOW: Run or obtain recent scan logs and cross-reference findings against the list of mission-essential capabilities.
     FAIL: Discovery of unauthorized ports or services, particularly commonly exploited ones like FTP, Bluetooth, or other nonessential protocols.

   • CHECK: Inspect logs and change management records that demonstrate periodic review and update of allowed and prohibited functionalities.
     WHY: Consistent review records and audit logs show operational commitment to maintaining least functionality over time.
     HOW: Access configuration management system logs, audit trails, or patch management tools to verify that reviews are scheduled and are being adhered to; confirm that removal or disabling actions are logged.
     FAIL: Lack of evidence for periodic review or change records that indicate outdated or unused functions are still enabled.

   • CHECK: Validate enforcement of software restrictions by examining endpoint protection policies (e.g., deny-by-exception for unauthorized software and allow-by-exception for authorized software).
     WHY: Integration of software whitelisting mechanisms demonstrates the practical application of least functionality at the application layer.
     HOW: Review endpoint security policies, check enforcement logs or alerts from application control solutions, and verify that non-authorized executables are being blocked.
     FAIL: Detection of multiple instances of unauthorized software execution or absence of enforcement logs corresponding to the policy.

   • CHECK: Confirm that any user-installed or third-party code execution is confined to protected environments with limited privileges as defined in the control enhancements.
     WHY: This ensures that even if unauthorized function execution does occur, its impact is contained.
     HOW: Check virtualization or sandbox configurations and their associated audit logs; interview system administrators if necessary.
     FAIL: Evidence that high-privilege functions or direct system interactions occur outside of confined environments.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: Focus on automated tools’ output (e.g., network and host scanning) as these are difficult to falsify. Real-time alerts from IDS/IPS and endpoint monitoring can quickly reveal drifts from approved configurations.
   • Critical Indicators: Look for stale configuration items in system baselines, such as unused ports or legacy protocols remaining active. Strong correlation between documented baselines and automated scan results is key.
   • Detail Matters: Confirm that reviews are not solely documented on paper but are tracked via logs and configuration management tools. Pay close attention to systems undergoing transitions (e.g., IPv4 to IPv6) as these are common hotspots for drift from minimal functionality.

Using this methodology, auditors can practically validate that the system adheres to the least functionality principle by combining observable technical data with process validation, ensuring that the control is effectively implemented and maintained.
content:
Statement:
a. Configure the system to provide only [Assignment: organization-defined mission essential capabilities]; and b. Prohibit or restrict the use of the following functions, ports, protocols, software, and/or services: [Assignment: organization-defined prohibited or restricted functions, system ports, protocols, software, and/or services].

Guidance:
Systems provide a wide variety of functions and services. Some of the functions and services routinely provided by default may not be necessary to support essential organizational missions, functions, or operations. Additionally, it is sometimes convenient to provide multiple services from a single system component, but doing so increases risk over limiting the services provided by that single component. Where feasible, organizations limit component functionality to a single function per component. Organizations consider removing unused or unnecessary software and disabling unused or unnecessary physical and logical ports and protocols to prevent unauthorized connection of components, transfer of information, and tunneling. Organizations employ network scanning tools, intrusion detection and prevention systems, and end-point protection technologies, such as firewalls and host-based intrusion detection systems, to identify and prevent the use of prohibited functions, protocols, ports, and services. Unsecure protocols include Bluetooth, FTP, and peer-to-peer networking. Related Controls: AC-18.

Enhancements:
LEAST FUNCTIONALITY | PERIODIC REVIEW (a) Review the system [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] to identify unnecessary and/or nonsecure functions, ports, protocols, software, and services; and (b) Disable or remove [Assignment: organization-defined functions, ports, protocols, software, and services within the system deemed to be unnecessary and/or nonsecure]. Discussion: Organizations review functions, ports, protocols, and services provided by systems or system components to determine the functions and services that are candidates for elimination. Such reviews are especially important during transition periods from older technologies to newer technologies (e.g., transition from IPv4 to IPv6). These technology transitions may require implementing the older and newer technologies simultaneously during the transition period and returning to minimum essential functions, ports, protocols, and services at the earliest opportunity. Organizations can either decide the relative security of the function, port, protocol, and/or service or base the security decision on the assessment of other entities. Unsecure protocols include Bluetooth, FTP, and peer-to-peer networking. Related Controls: AC-18.
LEAST FUNCTIONALITY | PREVENT PROGRAM EXECUTION Prevent program execution in accordance with [Selection (one or more): [Assignment: organization-defined policies, rules of behavior, and/or access agreements regarding software program usage and restrictions]; rules authorizing the terms and conditions of software program usage]. Discussion: Prevention of program execution addresses organizational policies, rules of behavior, and/or access agreements that restrict software usage and the terms and conditions imposed by the developer or manufacturer, including software licensing and copyrights. Restrictions include prohibiting auto-execute features, restricting roles allowed to approve program execution, permitting or prohibiting specific software programs, or restricting the number of program instances executed at the same time. Related Controls: CM-8, PL-4, PL-9, PM-5, PE-6.
LEAST FUNCTIONALITY | REGISTRATION COMPLIANCE Ensure compliance with [Assignment: organization-defined registration requirements for functions, ports, protocols, and services].
LEAST FUNCTIONALITY | UNAUTHORIZED SOFTWARE — DENY-BY-EXCEPTION (a) Identify [Assignment: organization-defined software programs not authorized to execute on the system]; (b) Employ an allow-all, deny-by-exception policy to prohibit the execution of unauthorized software programs on the system; and (c) Review and update the list of unauthorized software programs [Assignment: organization-defined frequency]. Discussion: Unauthorized software programs can be limited to specific versions or from a specific source. The concept of prohibiting the execution of unauthorized software may also be applied to user actions, system ports and protocols, IP addresses/ranges, websites, and MAC addresses. Related Controls: None.
LEAST FUNCTIONALITY | AUTHORIZED SOFTWARE — ALLOW-BY-EXCEPTION (a) Identify [Assignment: organization-defined software programs authorized to execute on the system]; (b) Employ a deny-all, permit-by-exception policy to allow the execution of authorized software programs on the system; and (c) Review and update the list of authorized software programs [Assignment: organization-defined frequency]. Discussion: Authorized software programs can be limited to specific versions or from a specific source. To facilitate a comprehensive authorized software process and increase the strength of protection for attacks that bypass application level authorized software, software programs may be decomposed into and monitored at different levels of detail. These levels include applications, application programming interfaces, application modules, scripts, system processes, system services, kernel functions, registries, drivers, and dynamic link libraries. The concept of permitting the execution of authorized software may also be applied to user actions, system ports and protocols, IP addresses/ranges, websites, and MAC addresses. Organizations consider verifying the integrity of authorized software programs using digital signatures, cryptographic checksums, or hash functions. Verification of authorized software can occur either prior to execution or at system startup. The identification of authorized URLs for websites is addressed in CA-3(5) and SC-7. Related Controls: CM-2, CM-6, CM-8, CM-10, PL-9, PM-5, SA-10, SC-34, SI-7.
LEAST FUNCTIONALITY | CONFINED ENVIRONMENTS WITH LIMITED PRIVILEGES Require that the following user-installed software execute in a confined physical or virtual machine environment with limited privileges: [Assignment: organization-defined user-installed software]. Discussion: Organizations identify software that may be of concern regarding its origin or potential for containing malicious code. For this type of software, user installations occur in confined environments of operation to limit or contain damage from malicious code that may be executed. Related Controls: CM-11, SC-44.
LEAST FUNCTIONALITY | CODE EXECUTION IN PROTECTED ENVIRONMENTS Allow execution of binary or machine-executable code only in confined physical or virtual machine environments and with the explicit approval of [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles] when such code is: (a) Obtained from sources with limited or no warranty; and/or (b) Without the provision of source code. Discussion: Code execution in protected environments applies to all sources of binary or machine-executable code, including commercial software and firmware and open-source software. Related Controls: CM-10, SC-44.

title: CONFIGURATION SETTINGS
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Documented configuration baselines that clearly list settings for systems including servers, workstations, and network devices. These baselines reflect the most restrictive secure configurations that align with operational requirements.
• Technical evidence such as configuration files, system registries, or automated reports confirming that the implemented settings match the documented baseline.
• Logs or monitoring dashboards that record changes to configurations along with approvals for any deviation.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations might maintain well-crafted policy documents without updating actual system configurations to match.
• Relying solely on automated configuration management tools without periodic manual review may hide deviations or exceptions.
• Falsified change logs or lacking real-time monitoring of configuration changes, with deviations being retroactively approved without proper validation.

VALIDATION STEPS:
CHECK: Verify that a secure configuration baseline exists and is documented for all critical system components.
WHY: A documented baseline is the foundation for ensuring only the most secure settings are employed, making deviations easily identifiable.
HOW: Review official IT security documentation, secure configuration checklists, or hardening guides; cross-reference with actual system profiles (using configuration management tools if available).
FAIL: Absence of a comprehensive, version-controlled configuration document or misalignment between documentation and system implementation.

CHECK: Validate that the implemented settings on a sample of systems (e.g., servers, workstations) match the secure configuration baseline.
WHY: Ensures the secure configuration is not only theoretical but actively deployed across the environment.
HOW: Use automated configuration scanners or manual inspection (e.g., checking registry settings, file permissions, and service configurations) to compare live settings against the baseline.
FAIL: Frequent deviations from the baseline without documented and approved exceptions.

CHECK: Inspect change management logs and approval records to ensure that any deviations from the configuration baseline are documented, reviewed, and approved.
WHY: Proper deviation handling and documented approvals are critical for maintaining a secure posture while supporting operational needs.
HOW: Audit change management systems and logs, reviewing a sample of configuration modifications to see if deviations are justified and approved by competent authority.
FAIL: Lack of detailed logs or missing evidence of formal approval for configuration changes.

CHECK: Assess the monitoring mechanisms in place for configuration changes, ensuring automatic alerts and periodic reviews are functioning as intended.
WHY: Active monitoring and alerting are key to quickly identifying unauthorized changes or compliance drift.
HOW: Review system dashboards, automated reports, and incident response records; perform a test change to confirm alert triggers where possible.
FAIL: Infrequent or non-existent alerts, or evidence that changes are not being monitored in real time.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Look for discrepancies between documented policies and live configurations. Often, outdated baselines exist on paper, but IT operations might be running different settings.
• Prioritize checks on critical systems and those exposed to external networks, as these are high-risk.
• Use automated scanning tools that compare configurations across the environment and flag non-compliance; manual confirmation should support automated findings.
• Review the timeliness and rigor of change management and monitoring processes—if deviations are approved too easily or not logged properly, the control may be superficial.
• Validate that configuration changes have a traceable audit trail—if you cannot track who approved and implemented the changes, the control is weakened.

This methodology emphasizes observable evidence and real-time operational data to ensure that configuration settings not only exist in policy documents but are effectively implemented and maintained in practice.
content:
Statement:
a. Establish and document configuration settings for components employed within the system that reflect the most restrictive mode consistent with operational requirements using [Assignment: organization-defined common secure configurations]; b. Implement the configuration settings; c. Identify, document, and approve any deviations from established configuration settings for [Assignment: organization-defined operational requirements]; and d. Monitor and control changes to the configuration settings in accordance with organizational policies and procedures.

Guidance:
Configuration settings are the parameters that can be changed in the hardware, software, or firmware components of the system that affect the security and privacy posture or functionality of the system. Information technology products for which configuration settings can be defined include mainframe computers, servers, workstations, operating systems, mobile devices, input/output devices, protocols, and applications. Parameters that impact the security posture of systems include registry settings; account, file, or directory permission settings; and parameters impacting the privacy posture of systems, including the parameters required to satisfy other privacy controls. Organizations establish organization-wide configuration settings and subsequently derive specific configuration settings for systems. The established settings become part of the configuration baseline for the system. Common secure configurations (also known as security configuration checklists, lockdown and hardening guides, and security reference guides) provide recognized, standardized, and established benchmarks that stipulate secure configuration settings for information technology.

title: ACCESS RESTRICTIONS FOR CHANGE
methodology:
Below is the expert methodology for validating the implementation of CM-5 Access Restrictions for Change.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Clearly defined change management system logs that capture who initiated a change and when, along with any dual authorization events. 
• Automated enforcement mechanisms that restrict access to change functions based on role and time (e.g., change windows).
• Physical security logs (badge reader data, door access logs) tied to systems housing change management consoles or critical hardware.
• Evidence in change management systems that only authorized (and if applicable, dual authorized) individuals can approve and implement changes.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may only document policies without enforcing them: e.g., manual or ad hoc access control without automated checks.
• Faking compliance by generating reports that misrepresent live audit logs or by backdating log entries.
• Using shared accounts for change control thereby bypassing individual accountability and dual authorization requirements.
• Superficial integration with physical access systems where controls are defined on paper but not monitored in practice.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Verify that change management systems enforce role-based and time-based restrictions for initiating changes.
WHY: Automated controls provide tangible evidence that only authorized personnel can trigger changes during approved windows.
HOW: Use administrative consoles and configuration screens to review access-control settings and change windows; request a live demonstration of enforced change windows.
FAIL: If changes can be initiated outside of prescribed windows or without role restrictions.

CHECK: Inspect audit logs and system-generated change records that reflect access control decisions and changes.
WHY: Consistent, tamper-resistant audit logs indicate active implementation and enforcement of access restrictions.
HOW: Extract recent change logs and verify that each change record includes the identities of both initiators and approvers (for dual authorization implementations), along with time stamps and reference to approved change orders.
FAIL: Absence of audit trails, incomplete logs without dual authorization evidence, or logs that can be easily manipulated.

CHECK: Review configurations of automated enforcement mechanisms for both logical (software-based) and physical access restrictions.
WHY: Automation minimizes opportunities for bypass and ensures that change activities are monitored and limited to qualified personnel.
HOW: Examine system settings for change management applications; check integrations with identity management systems (e.g., directory services) and physical access logs for server rooms or data centers.
FAIL: If settings rely solely on manual oversight or if there’s no linkage between physical and logical security controls.

CHECK: Validate the implementation of dual authorization for critical system changes (if applicable under organizational policy).
WHY: Dual authorization reduces the risk of unauthorized or collusive changes by ensuring that no single individual has unilateral control over critical operations.
HOW: Select a sample of significant changes and verify that log records, change tickets, or approval workflows require two separate and independently identified approvals.
FAIL: Single-authorization events or evidence of shared credentials being used to bypass dual approval processes.

CHECK: Perform a live test or a retrospective review by challenging the change management process – for example, attempt to simulate an unauthorized change to see if it’s prevented by the automated controls and logged accordingly.
WHY: A practical test helps validate that the controls are active and operational rather than theoretical in nature.
HOW: Coordinate with the change management team to schedule an authorized test (or inspect a documented recent test) where attempts are made to commit unauthorized changes, and observe system responses and log entries.
FAIL: If the system allows the unauthorized change to be initiated or the attempt is not properly recorded and flagged.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• When reviewing logs, focus on anomalies such as changes made during off-hours or by accounts that typically do not perform change functions.
• Cross-reference system logs with physical access logs to ensure that people making critical system changes were physically in authorized areas when required.
• Dual authorization logs should not only list approvals but also indicate if one of the approvals came from a delegated or proxy account, which is a red flag.
• Automated testing (using scripts to simulate change attempts) is a powerful shortcut. Ensure that these tests are independent and cannot be easily disabled.
• Check for periodic independent reviews of access controls (e.g., internal audits) to ensure that documented controls translate into practical enforcement.

This methodology provides practical steps and evidence to validate that the access restrictions for change are implemented effectively and cannot be easily bypassed.
content:
Statement:
Define, document, approve, and enforce physical and logical access restrictions associated with changes to the system.

Guidance:
Changes to the hardware, software, or firmware components of systems or the operational procedures related to the system can potentially have significant effects on the security of the systems or individuals’ privacy. Therefore, organizations permit only qualified and authorized individuals to access systems for purposes of initiating changes. Access restrictions include physical and logical access controls (see AC-3 and PE-3), software libraries, workflow automation, media libraries, abstract layers (i.e., changes implemented into external interfaces rather than directly into systems), and change windows (i.e., changes occur only during specified times).

Enhancements:
(1) ACCESS RESTRICTIONS FOR CHANGE | AUTOMATED ACCESS ENFORCEMENT AND AUDIT RECORDS
(a) Enforce access restrictions using [Assignment: organization-defined automated mechanisms]; and  
(b) Automatically generate audit records of the enforcement actions.
Discussion: Organizations log system accesses associated with applying configuration changes to ensure that configuration change control is implemented and to support after-the-fact actions should organizations discover any unauthorized changes.
Related Controls: AU-2, AU-6, AU-7, AU-12, CM-6, CM-11, SI-12.
(2) ACCESS RESTRICTIONS FOR CHANGE | REVIEW SYSTEM CHANGES
[Withdrawn: Incorporated into CM-3(7).]
(3) ACCESS RESTRICTIONS FOR CHANGE | SIGNED COMPONENTS
[Withdrawn: Moved to CM-14.]
(4) ACCESS RESTRICTIONS FOR CHANGE | DUAL AUTHORIZATION
Enforce dual authorization for implementing changes to [Assignment: organization-defined system components and system-level information].
Discussion: Organizations employ dual authorization to help ensure that any changes to selected system components and information cannot occur unless two qualified individuals approve and implement such changes. The two individuals possess the skills and expertise to determine if the proposed changes are correct implementations of approved changes. The individuals are also accountable for the changes. Dual authorization may also be known as two-person control. To reduce the risk of collusion, organizations consider rotating dual authorization duties to other individuals. System-level information includes operational procedures.
Related Controls: AC-2, AC-5, CM-3.

title: IMPACT ANALYSES
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Documents and artifacts such as change request records, risk assessment reports, and meeting minutes clearly reference security and privacy impact analyses for proposed changes.
• Logs and system audit trails show that specific changes were preceded by impact evaluations, including commentary on how they influence security controls and privacy protections.
• Evidence of separate testing environments being used for assessing changes before operational deployment, including test run reports and isolated network logs.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may produce templated or retroactively filled risk assessment documents that lack detailed technical analysis and merely check compliance boxes.
• A superficial review using outdated or generic impact templates that fail to address the specifics of the current system change.
• Test environments that are not truly segregated, or where evidence of testing is missing, can mask ineffective impact analyses.

VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Review a sample of change requests to verify that each change has an associated impact analysis report.
WHY: Robust impact analyses documented alongside change requests demonstrate that security and privacy risks were evaluated before implementation.
HOW: Randomly select several finalized change records and inspect attached risk assessments and impact analysis reports; confirm that they address both the technical aspects and privacy implications.
FAIL: If change records lack detailed impact analysis documentation or if the documents are generic, quote non-specific risks, it indicates potential lack of in-depth review.

CHECK: Confirm evidence of testing in a separate test environment for changes flagged as high impact.
WHY: Testing in an isolated environment confirms that the organization proactively assesses the potential flaws and weaknesses before affecting operational systems.
HOW: Check system logs for test run activities, review test reports, and validate network architecture diagrams that distinguish between operational and test environments.
FAIL: Absence of test run reports or evidence that the test environment is not clearly isolated from production suggests that the enhancement is not properly implemented.

CHECK: Interview or review credentials of personnel responsible for conducting impact analyses.
WHY: Ensuring that analyses are performed by qualified personnel underscores the technical rigor of the evaluations.
HOW: Validate training records, job descriptions, or certifications for team members involved in security and privacy risk assessments; cross-check interview responses with documentation.
FAIL: If personnel lack evidence of specialized skills or training in security/privacy or if interviews reveal superficial analysis methods, this indicates inadequate expertise.

CHECK: Examine risk assessment outcomes for any additions of controls or modifications to existing controls following a system change.
WHY: Adjustments made to controls in response to identified risks are vital indicators of a proactive and effective impact analysis process.
HOW: Review post-change reviews and remediation plans, specifically looking for action items added to address newly identified risks.
FAIL: If no changes to controls are recommended despite significant system changes, or if follow-up actions are missing, the impact analysis might have been ineffective.

EXPERT TIPS
• Focus on the detail in risk evaluation reports – well-documented, specific technical and privacy implications are far more telling than generic statements.
• Verify that the separate test environment is truly isolated; check network segmentation and access logs to ensure that production data is not inadvertently used.
• Look for evidence of iterative review; follow-up reports or mitigation tracking can offer clear indicators of both initial review quality and operational fidelity.
• Cross-reference with supply chain partner communications if system changes affect third parties – this is often a red flag if missing or skimmed over.
• Ensure that documentation reflects real-time analysis rather than a post-hoc addendum after a change is already in production.

By following these steps, you ensure that the impact analyses process adheres to the intent of the control by addressing both operational and technical effectiveness rather than just meeting documentation requirements.
content:
Statement:
Analyze changes to the system to determine potential security and privacy impacts prior to change implementation.

Guidance:
Organizational personnel with security or privacy responsibilities conduct impact analyses. Individuals conducting impact analyses possess the necessary skills and technical expertise to analyze the changes to systems as well as the security or privacy ramifications. Impact analyses include reviewing security and privacy plans, policies, and procedures to understand control requirements; reviewing system design documentation and operational procedures to understand control implementation and how specific system changes might affect the controls; reviewing the impact of changes on organizational supply chain partners with stakeholders; and determining how potential changes to a system create new risks to the privacy of individuals and the ability of implemented controls to mitigate those risks. Impact analyses also include risk assessments to understand the impact of the changes and determine if additional controls are required.

Enhancements:
(1) IMPACT ANALYSES | SEPARATE TEST ENVIRONMENTS Analyze changes to the system in a separate test environment before implementation in an operational environment, looking for security and privacy impacts due to flaws, weaknesses, incompatibility, or intentional malice.

Discussion: A separate test environment requires an environment that is physically or logically separate and distinct from the operational environment. The separation is sufficient to ensure that activities in the test environment do not impact activities in the operational environment and that information in the operational environment is not inadvertently transmitted to the test environment.

Related Controls: SA-11, SC-7.

title: CONFIGURATION CHANGE CONTROL
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for the CM-3 (Configuration Change Control) control, with concrete checks and expert insights:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Evidence that the organization has defined and documented the types of changes that are configuration-controlled (e.g., change classification matrices, baseline inventories, or system configuration registers).
• Technical artifacts such as audit logs, automated change management tool reports, detailed change tickets, and associated approval records that show the lifecycle of configuration changes.
• Operational patterns like routine change reviews, automated notifications to approval authorities, and alerts upon unauthorized changes—demonstrating active control.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations sometimes fake compliance by merely producing static policies without demonstrating actual, operational change control in daily operations. For example, a paper trail might exist without automated enforcement.
• Surface-level implementations include change logs that are not tied to automated systems, or approval workflows that are bypassed (e.g., changes implemented directly in production without documented review).
• Technical gaps often arise when organizations neglect automated mechanisms that document, notify, and restrict changes, or when testing/validation of changes is conducted in a silo without integration into central auditing systems.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Verify that the organization has documented which system changes are configuration-controlled.
WHY: This establishes the scope of change control and ensures that all relevant modifications are managed.
HOW: Review configuration management policy documentation and determine if change types are classified; ask for a change register or change classification matrix stored centrally, then correlate with sample automated change tickets.
FAIL: Absence of clear documentation or a change register that outlines the types of controlled changes.

CHECK: Review evidence for an automated change management system that logs proposed changes, automated notifications, and approval status.
WHY: Automated documentation reduces the risk of unauthorized changes and ensures that approvals and actions are recorded in real time.
HOW: Inspect the output from the change management tool (e.g., dashboards or audit logs) and verify that change proposals generate notifications to defined approval authorities. Look for timestamped approval records and any alerts regarding pending decisions.
FAIL: Reliance on manual logs with no evidence of automation or absence of notifications for change approvals.

CHECK: Evaluate the process for testing, validation, and documentation of changes prior to final implementation.
WHY: Verifying that changes are not only approved but also tested and validated helps prevent disruptions and ensures security/privacy impact analysis is effective.
HOW: Request evidence such as test results, validation reports, or records from a staging environment. Confirm whether these records include clear links to corresponding change control documentation.
FAIL: Missing test/validation reports, or documentation showing that changes proceed directly to production without testing.

CHECK: Assess the existence of automated mechanisms that trigger security responses (e.g., alerts, system halts) if unauthorized configuration modifications occur.
WHY: Automated security responses are critical to mitigate risks associated with unauthorized changes and indicate mature monitoring and enforcement.
HOW: Examine system logs for events that show detection and response actions triggered by unauthorized configuration changes; interview system administrators and inspect configurations in the security monitoring tool.
FAIL: No logs or alerts related to unauthorized changes, or documented instances where such mechanisms were bypassed.

CHECK: Confirm that the configuration change control process involves security and privacy representatives during the review and decision process.
WHY: Involving experts ensures that both security and privacy impacts are appropriately considered, reducing risk from side effects of changes.
HOW: Check meeting minutes, change approval records, or tool workflows that list the inclusion of designated security/privacy personnel in the review process.
FAIL: Lack of identifiable participation by security/privacy representatives or absence of records indicating their input in change decisions.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Directly sample recent configuration change tickets from the automated system and trace them from proposal to deployment, verifying that every step (notification, approval, testing, implementation) was executed and logged.
• Critical indicator: Notice a gap between documented policies versus real-time automated logs — if there is a disconnect, it likely indicates manual backdoors or insufficient enforcement.
• Detail that matters: The timing and consistency of approvals and the presence of alerts or notifications when proposed changes exceed the defined time period without a decision is an important metric. Also, crossvalidate change records with system logs; discrepancies are a red flag.

This comprehensive methodology not only checks the existence of a defined configuration change control process but also its real operational enforcement through automated systems and proper involvement of all relevant stakeholders.
content:
Statement:
a. Determine and document the types of changes to the system that are configuration-controlled;
b. Review proposed configuration-controlled changes to the system and approve or disapprove such changes with explicit consideration for security and privacy impact analyses;
c. Document configuration change decisions associated with the system;
d. Implement approved configuration-controlled changes to the system;



Enhancements:
(1) CONFIGURATION CHANGE CONTROL | AUTOMATED DOCUMENTATION, NOTIFICATION, AND PROHIBITION OF CHANGES
Use [Assignment: organization-defined automated mechanisms] to:
(a) Document proposed changes to the system;
(b) Notify [Assignment: organization-defined approval authorities] of proposed changes to the system and request change approval;
(c) Highlight proposed changes to the system that have not been approved or disapproved within [Assignment: organization-defined time period];
