Confirm that logs have entries that include organizational identifiers and, where applicable, cryptographic markers if dissociation is implemented.
  FAIL: Audit logs with only generic or internally scoped information; missing or mismatched organizational markers; lack of digital signatures or evidence of cryptographically managed fields if identity disassociation is required.

• CHECK: Inspect configuration files and system settings governing audit data transmission across boundaries.
  WHY: These configuration artifacts will indicate if defined methods for sharing or protecting audit logs are actively enforced.
  HOW: Retrieve syslog (or equivalent) configuration files, API endpoint details, and audit log forwarding rules; validate against organization-defined methods and external sharing agreements. Look for settings that specify the designated external endpoints and protocols for secure exchange.
  FAIL: Configurations that show no references to external endpoints, out-dated or generic export settings; absence of encryption or authentication settings for outbound audit log transmissions.

• CHECK: Validate cross-organizational sharing agreements and their technical implementation.
  WHY: Ensures that documented processes are actually integrated into technical systems.
  HOW: Review signed cross-organization information exchange agreements and correlate them with system settings; request evidence from external partner systems that audit logs are being accepted, processed, and correlated as defined.
  FAIL: Agreements that exist only on paper, with no corresponding technical artifact showing active integration; isolated audit logs with no matching external entries or reconciliation reports.

• CHECK: Perform a simulated transaction that triggers an audit event crossing boundaries.
  WHY: Testing real-time operational effectiveness confirms that the control functions under live conditions.
  HOW: Coordinate with both internal and external audit administrators to initiate a controlled request that should be logged on both sides. Trace the event across systems, verifying that the original individual’s identity is preserved (or appropriately transformed if dissociation measures are active) throughout the log chain.
  FAIL: Absence of the triggered log entry on the external system; discrepancies in the audit record preventing effective correlation; audit records that appear only in internal logs without any cross-reference.

• CHECK: Analyze the implementation of privacy-enhancing measures (if applicable).
  WHY: Ensures that dissociability is applied when required to protect privacy while maintaining accountability.
  HOW: Examine cryptographic or tokenization mechanisms in place within the log transmission process. Verify that identifiers are either consistently preserved for accountability or cryptographically transformed to minimize privacy risks. Document any evidence of algorithms or procedures applied.
  FAIL: Lack of evidence for any cryptographic measure when policy dictates; audit logs that include full PII without any mitigation even in a cross-organizational context.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Verification Shortcuts: Use SIEM correlation functionalities to quickly trace an event from origin to external endpoint. Automated alerts for missing or anomalous external audit entries are a strong indicator of operational issues.
• Critical Indicators: Consistency in metadata fields (timestamps, organization identifiers, cryptographic fingerprints) across the audit trail is key. Inconsistencies or missing fields often suggest superficial implementation.
• Details to Prioritize: Ensure that both technical configurations and live evidence of coordinated audit logging are present. The presence of signed and active cross-organization data feeds, along with effective simulation tests, matter most.
• Watch Out: Do not rely solely on documentation or periodically generated reports. Cross-reference live system outputs with configuration files and external system logs to avoid compliance that’s only on paper.

This methodology focuses on confirming that the implemented cross-organizational audit logging control is not only defined on a policy level, but actively enforced and operating as required in a live environment.
content:
Statement:
Employ [Assignment: organization-defined methods] for coordinating [Assignment: organization-defined audit information] among external organizations when audit information is transmitted across organizational boundaries.

Guidance:
When organizations use systems or services of external organizations, the audit logging capability necessitates a coordinated, cross-organization approach. For example, maintaining the identity of individuals who request specific services across organizational boundaries may often be difficult, and doing so may prove to have significant performance and privacy ramifications. Therefore, it is often the case that cross-organizational audit logging simply captures the identity of individuals who issue requests at the initial system, and subsequent systems record that the requests originated from authorized individuals. Organizations consider including processes for coordinating audit information requirements and protection of audit information in information exchange agreements.

Enhancements:
(1) CROSS-ORGANIZATIONAL AUDIT LOGGING | IDENTITY PRESERVATION
Preserve the identity of individuals in cross-organizational audit trails.

Discussion: Identity preservation is applied when there is a need to be able to trace actions that are performed across organizational boundaries to a specific individual.
Related Controls: IA-2, IA-4, IA-5, IA-8.
(2) CROSS-ORGANIZATIONAL AUDIT LOGGING | SHARING OF AUDIT INFORMATION
Provide cross-organizational audit information to [Assignment: organization-defined organizations] based on [Assignment: organization-defined cross-organizational sharing agreements].

Discussion: Due to the distributed nature of the audit information, cross-organization sharing of audit information may be essential for effective analysis of the auditing being performed. For example, the audit records of one organization may not provide sufficient information to determine the appropriate or inappropriate use of organizational information resources by individuals in other organizations. In some instances, only individuals’ home organizations have the appropriate knowledge to make such determinations, thus requiring the sharing of audit information among organizations.
Related Controls: IR-4, SI-4.
(3) CROSS-ORGANIZATIONAL AUDITING | DISSOCIABILITY
Implement [Assignment: organization-defined measures] to disassociate individuals from audit information transmitted across organizational boundaries.

Discussion: Preserving identities in audit trails could have privacy ramifications, such as enabling the tracking and profiling of individuals, but may not be operationally necessary. These risks could be further amplified when transmitting information across organizational boundaries. Implementing privacy-enhancing cryptographic techniques can disassociate individuals from audit information and reduce privacy risk while maintaining accountability.

title: ALTERNATE AUDIT LOGGING CAPABILITY
methodology:
Control AU-15 (Alternate Audit Logging Capability) was withdrawn and its requirements moved to AU-5(5). Nevertheless, for environments implementing an alternate auditing capability, auditors should verify that a secondary audit logging mechanism is in place and functions as designed in the event of primary audit log failure. Below is a practical validation methodology:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   - Visible Evidence: System configuration screens or command-line outputs showing separate log target definitions for an alternate audit logging destination (e.g., a dedicated log server, remote syslog, or alternative storage location).
   - Technical Artifacts: Configuration files, scripts, or policies that define failover parameters, alternate log file locations, and secured access controls. Example evidence might include custom logging daemon settings indicating backup log paths or alternate destinations.
   - Operational Patterns: Demonstrated failover logs in the alternate repository during instances of primary logging disruption; automated alerts generated when primary audit logging fails.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   - Organizations may simply duplicate the primary log configurations in an alternate location without real separation, resulting in both logs residing on the same system.
   - Surface-level implementations include having an alternate logging destination defined in policy but not actively used or tested.
   - Technical gaps include misconfigured file permissions, lack of automated switching on failure, or failure to properly secure and isolate the alternate log repository.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify Alternate Logging Configuration
   WHY: Confirm that the system is configured to write audit events to an alternate log destination in case of primary logging failure.
   HOW: Review system configuration files and administration consoles for explicit definitions of an alternate log target (e.g., separate server IP, distinct file paths, or remote syslog configurations). Look for parameters that clearly differentiate alternate logging from primary logging.
   FAIL: If configuration files or consoles do not show discrete settings for an alternate audit logging channel, or if the same destination is used for all logging activities.

   CHECK: Test Failover to Alternate Audit Logging
   WHY: Ensure the alternate logging mechanism activates when the primary logging system becomes unavailable.
   HOW: In a controlled environment, simulate a failure of the primary log collection service. Trigger an audit-related event (non-disruptively) and verify that the event is captured by the alternate logging system. Document evidence from logs showing the failover transition.
   FAIL: If events during the simulation are missing in the alternate logs or if no evidence of failover activation is found.

   CHECK: Assess Isolation and Security of the Alternate Logs
   WHY: Alternate logs must be protected from common vulnerabilities affecting the primary system, ensuring integrity and preventing tampering.
   HOW: Examine the location and storage settings of alternate logs. Check access controls, encryption settings, and that the alternate logs are stored in a different system or zone than the primary logs. Validate permissions and audit trail protection.
   FAIL: If the alternate log files are stored on the same physical or virtual server as primary logs or if file permissions/encryption settings are weak or misconfigured.

   CHECK: Validate Monitoring/Alerting for Audit Logging Failures
   WHY: Automated monitoring should detect failures in the primary logging mechanism and alert administrators to initiate investigation.
   HOW: Review monitoring tool configurations and incident alert logs to ensure alerts are set up for primary audit log failures. Confirm that notifications have been sent during test scenarios. Assess integration logs between the audit logging system and the alerting system.
   FAIL: If there are no alerts, or if monitoring logs do not reflect activation of the alternate logging capability during the simulated failure.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   - Verification Shortcut: Ask system administrators for a documented test plan where the failover capability was exercised and then review the associated log evidence. Simulation in a test environment is a robust proof-of-function.
   - Critical Indicator: The clear distinction in storage and management between primary and alternate logs. If both are stored together or managed using the same credentials, the alternate capability is ineffective.
   - Key Detail: Ensure that the alternate audit log configurations are not simply a backup copy; they must route log events through a separate, secure channel. Automated alerts and independent monitoring are strong indicators of effective implementation.
   - Additional Consideration: Confirm that the alternate logging mechanism is part of periodic testing and not just a documented procedure on paper. Authentic operational evidence, such as timestamped logs from both channels, is essential.

This methodology focuses on obtaining tangible, automated evidence and testing results that prove the alternate audit logging capability is not merely a documented policy but an actively managed control that can detect and capture audit events when primary channels fail.
content:
Statement:
[Withdrawn: Moved to AU-5(5).]

title: SESSION AUDIT
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Visible evidence includes active session audit recording tools running on critical systems, with startup scripts or configuration settings that automatically trigger session capture as part of system initialization.
• Technical artifacts such as system logs, dedicated session capture files, audit trails stored on secured logging servers, and evidence of remote viewing capabilities (if implemented) are key indicators.
• Operational patterns include a defined set of circumstances or triggers (e.g., suspicious behavior) that initiate session auditing, along with periodic reviews and legal documentation showing consultation with legal/civil liberties advisors.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations might simply enable logging without capturing full session detail (e.g., just keystrokes or web requests) which can be faked through superficial configuration changes without true integration of session capture.
• Some setups may record sessions only on-demand, bypassing automated startup auditing, thus missing early session data.
• Overreliance on manual triggers or poorly integrated tools may give a false sense of coverage while leaving gaps in real-time auditing or the ability to remotely view sessions.

VALIDATION STEPS:
1. CHECK: Verify that session audit mechanisms are configured to initiate at system startup.
   WHY: Automated startup logging ensures that session data is captured from the beginning of every session, minimizing tampering risk.
   HOW: Review startup scripts, system configuration files, and logs to confirm that auditing services are auto-started. Cross-check with system logs showing audit activity immediately after boot.
   FAIL: No evidence of audit process activation during system boot, or missing related configuration parameters.

2. CHECK: Confirm that session audit logs capture comprehensive session content (keystrokes, file transfers, websites visited, and any remote access activities).
   WHY: Full session capture is necessary to provide detailed visibility for forensic analysis and legal compliance.
   HOW: Examine audit logs and capture files from monitored systems to verify the presence of detailed session records. Validate that both live and recorded content (including remote viewing if applicable) are archived.
   FAIL: Logs are incomplete, only record partial data, or show timestamps with large gaps indicative of bypassed auditing procedures.

3. CHECK: Validate that session auditing has been integrated with legal and privacy guidelines by reviewing consultation records and documented criteria for triggering audits.
   WHY: Ensuring legal counsel reviewed session auditing practices protects privacy rights and adheres to regulatory requirements.
   HOW: Interview responsible personnel and review documented evidence (meeting minutes, legal review documents) that the audit policy was developed with input from legal/civil liberties advisors and complies with relevant policies.
   FAIL: Absence of any legal or policy consultation documentation; processes in place that do not reflect legal review or targeted circumstances.

4. CHECK: Evaluate the remote viewing/listening capability (if implemented) to ensure it is only accessible to authorized users and operational during active audits.
   WHY: Unauthorized or non-real-time access may compromise privacy and indicate poor integration of auditing controls.
   HOW: Test access controls for remote session viewing, inspect configuration files for role-based permissions, and cross-check user activity logs for remote access events.
   FAIL: Evidence of misconfigured permissions, unauthorized remote access, or lack of logs showing controlled usage of remote viewing tools.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Verification shortcuts include using automated scripts to check for expected process names and audit file modifications at system startup; these are hard to fake if audit mechanisms are properly implemented.
• Look for anomalies in session log timestamps, size, and frequency; consistency in early session logging (from system start) is a strong indicator of proper configuration.
• Pay extra attention to integration documentation regarding legal consultations; its absence can often indicate a rushed or superficial implementation.
• Cross-reference audit logs with actual session recordings during live incidents or tests to validate real-time capture and storage integrity. This helps ensure the 'capture and view' capability is operational and not just a policy on paper.

In summary, effective AU-14 implementation is demonstrated by robust, automatically triggered session recording, comprehensive and secure log preservation, controlled and authorized remote session access, and clear evidence of legally vetted operational procedures.
content:
Statement:
a. Provide and implement the capability for [Assignment: organization-defined users or roles] to [Selection (one or more): record; view; hear; log] the content of a user session under [Assignment: organization-defined circumstances]; and
b. Develop, integrate, and use session auditing activities in consultation with legal counsel and in accordance with applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines.

Guidance:
Session audits can include monitoring keystrokes, tracking websites visited, and recording information and/or file transfers. Session audit capability is implemented in addition to event logging and may involve implementation of specialized session capture technology. Organizations consider how session auditing can reveal information about individuals that may give rise to privacy risk as well as how to mitigate those risks. Because session auditing can impact system and network performance, organizations activate the capability under well-defined situations (e.g., the organization is suspicious of a specific individual). Organizations consult with legal counsel, civil liberties officials, and privacy officials to ensure that any legal, privacy, civil rights, or civil liberties issues, including the use of personally identifiable information, are appropriately addressed.

Enhancements:
(1) SESSION AUDIT | SYSTEM START-UP
Initiate session audits automatically at system start-up.

Discussion: The automatic initiation of session audits at startup helps to ensure that the information being captured on selected individuals is complete and not subject to compromise through tampering by malicious threat actors.
Related Controls: None.
(2) SESSION AUDIT | CAPTURE AND RECORD CONTENT
[Withdrawn: Incorporated into AU-14].
(3) SESSION AUDIT | REMOTE VIEWING AND LISTENING
Provide and implement the capability for authorized users to remotely view and hear content related to an established user session in real time.

Discussion: None.

title: MONITORING FOR INFORMATION DISCLOSURE
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence of active monitoring includes configured dashboard views showing alerts from open-source sites, social media feeds, code-sharing platforms, etc. Look for logs from automated tools (e.g., notification emails, API alerts, incident tickets) that record evidence of detected unauthorized disclosures.
• Observable operational practices include regularly updated lists of monitored sites and documented periodic reviews of these sites to ensure relevance.
• Technically, evidence such as system configuration files for monitoring scripts, vendor service logs, and integration records with incident response notifications prove that the monitoring control is active.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may simply maintain a list of sites without active monitoring or use outdated/not functional automated tools that do not trigger actual alerts.
• Documentation may indicate reviews, but if logs and audit trails do not show real alerts or updated configurations, the implementation has likely failed.
• Surface-level implementations include dummy alerts that are never actioned or simulated notifications without real external content scanning.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Verify Active Automated Monitoring
   WHY: Real-time notifications from configured tools provide direct evidence of monitoring for unauthorized disclosures.
   HOW: Identify and review the configuration of automated tools (e.g., scanning scripts, third-party services) including trigger conditions, and then sample the most recent alert logs or dashboard outputs for evidence of active monitoring.
   FAIL: No active logs, dashboards, or notifications recorded, or evidence of alerts with timestamps in the expected review period.

2. CHECK: Validate Notification and Escalation Processes
   WHY: Ensuring that discovered incidents are properly communicated to designated personnel proves operational incident response integration.
   HOW: Inspect incident response logs and sample recent alerts to confirm that notifications were sent to the defined personnel/roles. Optionally, simulate an alert and verify the escalation workflow in a controlled environment.
   FAIL: Lack of evidence showing that notifications were sent out, or misconfigured contact details in the alerting system.

3. CHECK: Review Periodic Monitoring Site List Updates
   WHY: Regular reviews ensure that the list of monitored open-source sites remains current and effective in detecting unauthorized disclosures.
   HOW: Request documentation or change logs for the monitored sites list. Check for time-stamped records showing periodic review and updates per organizational frequency expectations.
   FAIL: Outdated records, no periodic review logs, or a static list that hasn’t been modified over long periods despite evolving threat landscape.

4. CHECK: Assess Controls Against Unauthorized Replication Detection
   WHY: Verification that discovery mechanisms for unauthorized replication (e.g., monitoring website copies, scanning for cloned content) are functioning indicates a robust monitoring capability.
   HOW: Review tool output that specifically tracks replication or cloning events. Interview technical staff to understand the scanning process and validate with sample results that show detection of unauthorized replication.
   FAIL: Absence of tools configured for replication detection, or absence of evidence that scanning processes are operational.

EXPERT TIPS
• Leverage integrated dashboards and logging systems rather than relying solely on manual reports. Automated evidence (timestamps, alert traces) is much harder to fake.
• Check configuration details against organizational threat profiles—the set of keywords, URLs, and sites monitored should align with current risk assessments.
• Validate that incident notifications are sent to the correct personnel by cross-checking contact logs; a common gap is misconfigured email or alert channels that result in missed critical incidents.
• Look for consistency between policy documentation and observable evidence; discrepancies here are strong indicators of poor implementation.
content:
Statement:
a. Monitor [Assignment: organization-defined open-source information and/or information sites] [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] for evidence of unauthorized disclosure of organizational information; and
b. If an information disclosure is discovered:
   1. Notify [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles]; and
   2. Take the following additional actions: [Assignment: organization-defined additional actions].

Guidance:
Unauthorized disclosure of information is a form of data leakage. Open-source information includes social networking sites and code-sharing platforms and repositories. Examples of organizational information include personally identifiable information retained by the organization or proprietary information generated by the organization.

Enhancements:
(1) MONITORING FOR INFORMATION DISCLOSURE | USE OF AUTOMATED TOOLS
Monitor open-source information and information sites using [Assignment: organization-defined automated mechanisms].

Discussion: Automated mechanisms include commercial services that provide notifications and alerts to organizations and automated scripts to monitor new posts on websites.
Related Controls: None.
(2) MONITORING FOR INFORMATION DISCLOSURE | REVIEW OF MONITORED SITES
Review the list of open-source information sites being monitored [Assignment: organization-defined frequency].

Discussion: Reviewing the current list of open-source information sites being monitored on a regular basis helps to ensure that the selected sites remain relevant. The review also provides the opportunity to add new open-source information sites with the potential to provide evidence of unauthorized disclosure of organizational information.
Related Controls: None.
(3) MONITORING FOR INFORMATION DISCLOSURE | UNAUTHORIZED REPLICATION OF INFORMATION
Employ discovery techniques, processes, and tools to determine if external entities are replicating organizational information in an unauthorized manner.

Discussion: The unauthorized use or replication of organizational information by external entities can cause adverse impacts on organizational operations and assets, including damage to reputation. Such activity can include the replication of an organizational website by an adversary or hostile threat actor who attempts to impersonate the web-hosting organization. Discovery tools, techniques, and processes used to determine if external entities are replicating organizational information in an unauthorized manner include scanning external websites, monitoring social media, and training staff to recognize the unauthorized use of organizational information.
Related Controls: None.
References: None.

title: AUDIT RECORD GENERATION
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible audit log interfaces where administrators can view configuration settings that specify which event types (from AU-2a and AU-2c) are being logged by each system component.
   • Technical artifacts including configuration files, database tables, or system settings that detail audit event selections and their associated logging parameters.
   • Operational patterns such as regularly generated audit files (or centralized audit trails) that consistently include the required fields (timestamp, event type, user, etc.), and evidence of time correlation across system components.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations may simply upload static documents or screenshots that claim configuration settings exist without evidence of active, system-generated logs.
   • Superficial implementations where logging is enabled by default but audit logs do not contain the required detailed content per AU-3, or where event selection isn’t truly configurable in real time.
   • Gaps in time synchronization between systems that mimic a system-wide audit trail but fail to meet the tolerated time correlation standards.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   • CHECK: Confirm Audit Record Generation Capability
     WHY: Ensures the system is configured to generate audit records for all defined event types.
     HOW: Review system configuration files or administrative settings to verify that all event types defined in AU-2a and AU-2c are selected and actively being logged. Additionally, trigger sample events and check system logs for corresponding entries.
     FAIL: Evidence that certain event types are missing from the logs, or that sample events do not produce any audit record.
   
   • CHECK: Verify the Configurability of Event Type Selections
     WHY: Validates that designated personnel or roles can select and adjust which events are logged.
     HOW: Log in as an authorized admin, modify the logging settings, and confirm that the changes are reflected in the system settings and subsequent audit records. Cross-check with change logs if available.
     FAIL: Inability to modify audit settings or logs that do not reflect changes made by authorized personnel.
   
   • CHECK: Assess the Audit Record Content Against AU-3 Requirements
     WHY: Audit logs must include detailed content (e.g., time stamps, event type, user identity) so that they are useful in security investigations.
     HOW: Extract a sample of audit records and verify that each record includes required elements (e.g., source, time stamp, event category). Compare against the content specifications in AU-3.
     FAIL: Incomplete records missing key fields or records that only contain generic information not meeting AU-3 standards.
   
   • CHECK: Evaluate System-Wide Audit Trail and Time Correlation (Enhancement 1)
     WHY: A time-correlated audit trail increases the ability to reconstruct events across multiple components.
     HOW: Check integration logs or centralized audit trail systems to see that events from disparate components have time stamps that are synchronized within the organization-defined tolerance. Validate by comparing time stamps from multiple sources during a coordinated test event.
     FAIL: Discrepancies where log time stamps vary beyond the acceptable tolerance, indicating poor time correlation.
   
   • CHECK: Confirm Use of Standardized Audit Log Formats (Enhancement 2)
     WHY: Standardized formats ensure interoperability, making it easier to analyze and correlate audit records across systems.
     HOW: Review a sample of system audit logs to verify that they follow a standardized, consistent format (e.g., use of common fields, structured JSON or CSV). Assess if logs can be ingested into common SIEM tools without additional conversions.
     FAIL: Audit records in multiple inconsistent formats or a proprietary format that requires extensive conversion processes.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: Use log aggregation tools or SIEM systems to automatically correlate time stamps and quickly surface any discrepancies in the audit records.
   • Critical Indicator: Missing or inconsistent time stamps are a strong sign that the audit trail is not properly time-correlated, reducing the effectiveness of audit investigations.
   • Detail Focus: Pay close attention to the configurable interface used by authorized individuals. If changes cannot be logged or if modification control is weak, then the dynamic audit capability (part c) is not effectively implemented.
   • Verify in Real-Time: Simulate events and changes in logging settings to confirm that both the record generation and adjustment capabilities work under live conditions, not just in system configurations or documentation.

These practical validations help ensure that audit record generation is not only configured per policy but also operates effectively in a live system environment.
content:
Statement:
a. Provide audit record generation capability for the event types the system is capable of auditing as defined in AU-2a on [Assignment: organization-defined system components]; b. Allow [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles] to select the event types that are to be logged by specific components of the system; and c. Generate audit records for the event types defined in AU-2c that include the audit record content defined in AU-3.

Guidance:
Audit records can be generated from many different system components. The event types specified in AU-2d are the event types for which audit logs are to be generated and are a subset of all event types for which the system can generate audit records.

Enhancements:
(1) AUDIT RECORD GENERATION | SYSTEM-WIDE AND TIME-CORRELATED AUDIT TRAIL
Complete audit records from [Assignment: organization-defined system components] into a system-wide (logical or physical) audit trail that is time-correlated to within [Assignment: organization-defined level of tolerance for the relationship between time stamps of individual records in the audit trail].

Discussion: Audit trails are time-correlated if the time stamps in the individual audit records can be reliably related to the time stamps in other audit records to achieve a time ordering of the records within organizational tolerances.
Related Controls: AU-8, SC-45.
(2) AUDIT RECORD GENERATION | STANDARDIZED FORMATS
Produce a system-wide (logical or physical) audit trail composed of audit records in a standardized format.

Discussion: Audit records that follow common standards promote interoperability and information exchange between devices and systems. Promoting interoperability and information exchange facilitates the production of event information that can be readily analyzed and correlated. If logging mechanisms do not conform to standardized formats, systems may convert individual audit records into standardized formats when compiling system-wide audit trails.
Related Controls: None.
(3) AUDIT RECORD GENERATION | CHANGES BY AUTHORIZED INDIVIDUALS
Provide and implement the capability for [Assignment: organization-defined individuals or roles] to change the logging to be performed on [Assignment: organization-defined system components] based on [Assignment: organization-defined selectable event criteria] within [Assignment: organization-defined time thresholds].

Discussion: Permitting authorized individuals to make changes to system logging enables organizations to extend or limit logging as necessary to meet organizational requirements. Logging that is limited to conserve system resources may be extended (either temporarily or permanently) to address certain threat situations. In addition, logging may be limited to a specific set of event types to facilitate audit reduction, analysis, and reporting. Organizations can establish time thresholds in which logging actions are changed (e.g., near real-time, within minutes, or within hours).
Related Controls: AC-3.

title: AUDIT RECORD RETENTION
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence includes the existence of an archival system, retention schedule settings visibly configured in SIEM or log management platforms, and documented retention policies accompanied by logs reflecting adherence to those policies.
• Technical artifacts include system configuration files specifying retention periods, backup job logs, archival storage records (with logs dated over the required period), and metadata for deletion or archiving events.
• Operational patterns observed are regular backup rotations, archival procedures that show periodic verification of older audit records, and monitoring alerts when log deletion or archiving deviates from the defined schedule.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations sometimes achieve apparent compliance by having well-documented retention policies that are not enforced by the underlying technical systems, merely relying on manual processes.
• Surface-level implementations may include log systems that are backed up, but without automated checks to ensure that the logs are not purged or that the long-term retrieval capabilities (e.g., format conversion) are functioning.
• Common technical gaps include misconfigured retention settings (e.g., a retention period that is shorter than documented), lack of centralization in log management, and failure to maintain necessary archival indexes or documentation that supports log interpretation over time.

VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK 1:
CHECK: Verify that the log management system’s configuration specifies a retention period that matches the organization’s retention policy.
WHY: Ensures that the technical controls are configured to keep audit records as mandated.
HOW: Review configuration files or settings in the SIEM/log management tool; compare these settings with the organization’s retention policy and validate the metadata of stored logs.
FAIL: If the log configuration shows a retention period shorter than mandated, or if settings are missing entirely.

CHECK 2:
CHECK: Confirm that audit records beyond the active retention period are archived and can be retrieved for long-term investigations.
WHY: Demonstrates that long-term retrieval mechanisms (such as format conversion, backup, or archival storage) are operational.
HOW: Run a retrieval test on archived records – attempt to extract a sample audit record from the long-term storage system; verify that the record is in a usable format and properly documented.
FAIL: If archived records are inaccessible, corrupted, or if retrieval procedures are undocumented/inconsistent.

CHECK 3:
CHECK: Inspect system audit trails for record management activities, especially changes to retention configurations and deletion/archival events.
WHY: Operational monitoring of retention settings ensures that changes are tracked and compliant with policies.
HOW: Access system logs or management consoles to review timestamps and logs associated with retention configuration changes, then cross-check that there are valid reasons and proper approvals for any deviations.
FAIL: If logs lack evidence of tracking changes, or if unauthorized modifications are present.

CHECK 4:
CHECK: Validate evidence of responses to FOIA/subpoena requests using historical audit records.
WHY: Proves that the retention processes support real-world incident response and compliance needs.
HOW: Interview system administrators and review incident response reports or retrieval logs for historical audit record requests; confirm that procedures were followed and records were successfully provided.
FAIL: If there is no evidence of past retrievals or if retrieval processes are incomplete or inconsistent.

EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Directly query the SIEM or log management system’s API for retention settings and compare with documented policies – automation here minimizes human error.
• Critical indicator: Discrepancies between configured retention periods and actual stored record ages signal poor implementation.
• Focus on ensuring that the process for converting archived logs into readable formats is active; obsolete formats or unreadable archives are a red flag.
• Always check for tamper-evident properties in audit records to ensure that no records were improperly altered or deleted externally.
• Look for timestamped audit trails that log every configuration change to the retention policy as a critical indicator of a mature, enforced control environment.
content:
Statement:
Retain audit records for [Assignment: organization-defined time period consistent with records retention policy] to provide support for after-the-fact investigations of incidents and to meet regulatory and organizational information retention requirements.

Guidance:
Organizations retain audit records until it is determined that the records are no longer needed for administrative, legal, audit, or other operational purposes. This includes the retention and availability of audit records relative to Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) requests, subpoenas, and law enforcement actions. Organizations develop standard categories of audit records relative to such types of actions and standard response processes for each type of action. The National Archives and Records Administration (NARA) General Records Schedules provide federal policy on records retention.

Enhancements:
(1) AUDIT RECORD RETENTION | LONG-TERM RETRIEVAL CAPABILITY Employ [Assignment: organization-defined measures] to ensure that long-term audit records generated by the system can be retrieved.

Discussion: Organizations need to access and read audit records requiring long-term storage (on the order of years). Measures employed to help facilitate the retrieval of audit records include converting records to newer formats, retaining equipment capable of reading the records, and retaining the necessary documentation to help personnel understand how to interpret the records.
Related Controls: None.
References: [OMB A-130].

title: NON-REPUDIATION
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible logs or audit trails that record events with cryptographic evidence (e.g., digital signatures or message receipts) tied to specific user identities.
• Cryptographic artifacts such as digital signature certificates, time-stamped transaction logs, and checksum validations indicating data integrity and binding between an identity and its action.
• Operational patterns that show the use of automated validation routines (e.g., periodic checksum verifications) and chain-of-custody processes for reviewed or released information.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may rely solely on static policy documentation or screenshots of configuration settings without real dynamic evidence (such as end-to-end signing and verification of transactions).
• Manually maintained logs with no automated binding between user identity and operations, making them susceptible to tampering.
• Bypass implementations where signing occurs only at initial creation but does not extend to subsequent actions or transfers, resulting in gaps in non-repudiation throughout the information lifecycle.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Verify that digital signatures are attached to data creation, transmission, or release events.
   WHY: Digital signatures provide irrefutable, cryptographic evidence that binds a user’s identity to a transaction.
   HOW: Examine sample logs and transactions in the audit system to confirm each critical event (e.g., document creation, message sending) contains a valid signature and associated certificate details. Run automated checks to confirm these digital signatures comply with FIPS or relevant standards.
   FAIL: If events lack digital signatures or use non-cryptographic means (or the cryptographic material appears outdated or invalid), the binding is insufficient.

2. CHECK: Assess the automated cryptographic validation of identity-to-information binding (e.g., digital checksums or hash validations) performed at predefined frequencies.
   WHY: Routine automated validations ensure that the integrity and identity binding of intelligence items have not been compromised between events.
   HOW: Review system configurations and logs for scheduled cryptographic validity checks. Confirm that upon a binding error, alerts/logs capture a defined corrective action.
   FAIL: Absence of automated verification or missing log entries for binding validation events indicates inadequate non-repudiation enforcement.

3. CHECK: Inspect the chain-of-custody logs along with integrated credentials for information reviewers or releasers at transfer or release points.
   WHY: Maintaining a documented chain-of-custody with user credentials ensures that any alterations or transfers are traceable to a responsible party.
   HOW: Retrieve and analyze audit logs to confirm entries capture reviewer/releaser credentials, timestamps, and secure handoffs. Validate that documented chain-of-custody records are tamper-resistant.
   FAIL: Sparse or incomplete chain-of-custody documentation or missing reviewer identities compromise non-repudiation guarantees.

4. CHECK: Confirm that identity binding is validated at defined transfer or review points before information exit between security domains.
   WHY: Validating identity bindings at transfer points ensures that data are not modified by unauthorized entities between review and release.
   HOW: Review system event logs, and examine how transfers between security domains are handled. Verify cryptographic validations (e.g. checksums, signatures) are logged at these points and observe any incident response triggered by detection of a binding error.
   FAIL: If identity bindings are not verified at transfer/review points, or if system alerts are not generated upon validation failure, non-repudiation controls are not robust.

5. CHECK: Examine the implementation of digital message receipts for receipt confirmations of transmitted data.
   WHY: Digital receipts confirm the sender’s and receiver’s acknowledgment of communication, which is critical for non-repudiation of message delivery.
   HOW: Test a sample communication flow from sender to receiver. Verify that message receipt logs include confirmation of reception that is digitally signed by the receiver, and that these records are time-stamped and immutable.
   FAIL: If message exchange logs lack receipt confirmation or if signatures are absent/incomplete, this suggests a gap in non-repudiation implementation.

EXPERT TIPS
• Prioritize automated evidence gathering such as digital signature verification logs over static documents, as this evidence is much harder to manipulate.
• Focus on end-to-end validation; even if individual components (i.e., creation or sending) are secure, check for gaps during intermediate steps like transfers.
• Critically examine the chain-of-custody records: incomplete logs or inconsistent timestamps are red flags.
• Look for repeated, periodic cryptographic validations, as irregular patterns may suggest manual intervention or inadequate automation.
• Ensure that error handling for validation failures (e.g., alerting and corrective actions) is actively implemented and documented in system logs.

By following these checks and steps, auditors can ensure that non-repudiation measures are not only documented in policy but are actively enforced and practically effective.
content:
Statement:
Provide irrefutable evidence that an individual (or process acting on behalf of an individual) has performed [Assignment: organization-defined actions to be covered by non-repudiation].

Guidance:
Types of individual actions covered by non-repudiation include creating information, sending and receiving messages, and approving information. Non-repudiation protects against claims by authors of not having authored certain documents, senders of not having transmitted messages, receivers of not having received messages, and signatories of not having signed documents. Non-repudiation services can be used to determine if information originated from an individual or if an individual took specific actions (e.g., sending an email, signing a contract, approving a procurement request, or receiving specific information). Organizations obtain non-repudiation services by employing various techniques or mechanisms, including digital signatures and digital message receipts.

Enhancements:
(1) NON-REPUDIATION | ASSOCIATION OF IDENTITIES
(a) Bind the identity of the information producer with the information to [Assignment: organization-defined strength of binding]; and
(b) Provide means for authorized individuals to determine the identity of the producer of the information.

Discussion: Binding identities to the information supports audit requirements that provide organizational personnel with the means to identify who produced specific information in the event of an information transfer. Organizations determine and approve the strength of attribute binding between the information producer and the information based on the security category of the information and other relevant risk factors.
(2) NON-REPUDIATION | VALIDATE BINDING OF INFORMATION PRODUCER IDENTITY
(a) Validate the binding of the information producer identity to the information at [Assignment: organization-defined frequency]; and
(b) Perform [Assignment: organization-defined actions] in the event of a validation error.

Discussion: Validating the binding of the information producer identity to the information prevents the modification of information between production and review. The validation of bindings can be achieved by, for example, using cryptographic checksums. Organizations determine if validations are in response to user requests or generated automatically.
(3) NON-REPUDIATION | CHAIN OF CUSTODY
Maintain reviewer or releaser credentials within the established chain of custody for information reviewed or released.

Discussion: Chain of custody is a process that tracks the movement of evidence through its collection, safeguarding, and analysis life cycle by documenting each individual who handled the evidence, the date and time the evidence was collected or transferred, and the purpose for the transfer. In the case of human reviews, maintaining the credentials of reviewers or releasers provides.
(4) NON-REPUDIATION | VALIDATE BINDING OF INFORMATION REVIEWER IDENTITY
(a) Validate the binding of the information reviewer identity to the information at the transfer or release points prior to release or transfer between [Assignment: organization-defined security domains]; and
(b) Perform [Assignment: organization-defined actions] in the event of a validation error.

Discussion: Validating the binding of the information reviewer identity to the information at transfer or release points prevents the unauthorized modification of information between review and the transfer or release. The validation of bindings can be achieved by using cryptographic checksums. Organizations determine if validations are in response to user requests or generated automatically.
(5) NON-REPUDIATION | DIGITAL SIGNATURES
[Withdrawn: Incorporated into SI-7.]
References: [FIPS 140-3], [FIPS 180-4], [FIPS 186-4], [FIPS 202], [SP 800-177].

title: PROTECTION OF AUDIT INFORMATION
methodology:
Control AU-9 – PROTECTION OF AUDIT INFORMATION

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Audit logs and tools are stored on hardened media (e.g., hardware-enforced write-once media) or on physically separate components from the main system. 
• Cryptographic hashes/signatures applied to audit files indicating integrity protection. 
• Clearly defined role-based access controls restricting management and read access to audit logging tools and records. 
• Automated alerting mechanisms (via SIEM or other monitoring systems) that notify designated personnel when unauthorized access, modification, or deletion is detected.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may simply document that audit logs are protected without ensuring physical separation or enforced write-once protections. 
• Relying solely on configuration files or policies that are not backed by automated logging verification. 
• Granting broad administrative access rather than a tightly controlled subset for audit functions, which may hide unauthorized activities. 
• Using re-writable media or storing logs on the same system as the audited application, thereby exposing audit logs to the same vulnerabilities.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Verify audit logs are stored on hardened, write-once (or write-protected) media or physically separate components.
WHY: Ensures that even if the primary system is compromised, the logs remain untampered and available for forensic analysis.
HOW: Review system configurations and physical storage documentation; inspect hardware inventory for WORM or separate media; validate with system diagrams that show audit log separation.
FAIL: Audit logs stored on re-writable media or on the same physical component as the main system, increasing risk of tampering.

CHECK: Confirm that cryptographic protections (e.g., signed hash functions or digital signatures) are applied to audit information.
WHY: This ensures integrity of audit records and helps detect any unauthorized modifications.
HOW: Examine audit log configuration files and log file headers for evidence of cryptographic signatures or hash values; test verification of a sample log’s integrity using publicly available keys if available.
FAIL: No evidence of cryptographic hashes, signatures, or any measures in place to detect integrity breaches of audit records.

CHECK: Validate that role-based access controls strictly limit who can access and modify audit logs and audit tools.
WHY: Minimizes the risk of unauthorized tampering by ensuring only a defined subset of privileged users have necessary access.
HOW: Review access control lists (ACLs), Active Directory/LDAP groups, or RBAC configurations; inspect permissions on audit repository directories and logging tool executables; verify that audit management roles are separate from other privileged users.
FAIL: Broad or default administrative rights are used for audit system management; lack of separation between roles resulting in excessive access.

CHECK: Ensure that alerting mechanisms are operational and appropriately notify designated personnel upon detection of unauthorized access, modification, or deletion of audit information.
WHY: Early detection facilitates a rapid incident response, limiting potential damage.
HOW: Check the system’s SIEM or alert configurations for rules tied to audit log events; review historical alerts to confirm they were triggered on suspicious audit log activity; if feasible, simulate a small, controlled unauthorized access event and observe if an alert is generated with appropriate recipients.
FAIL: No evidence of automated alerts, misconfigurations in alert routing, or historical logs showing missed alert events when unauthorized access was attempted.

CHECK: Review system architecture to ensure audit log storage is isolated even at the OS level (e.g., on a component running a different OS).
WHY: Isolation reduces the risk that vulnerabilities affecting one platform could compromise audit logs stored on the same system.
HOW: Examine architecture diagrams and system configuration details; interview system administrators regarding the segregation of audit storage from the primary system; verify that audit logs are maintained on a separate operational environment.
FAIL: Audit records stored on the system or OS that is also vulnerable to the issues affecting the audited component, indicating insufficient segregation.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Always cross-check configuration settings with actual system behavior using automated tools (e.g., scripts that verify file system permissions and media status) rather than just reviewing documentation.
• Focus on evidence of operational controls (such as real-time alerts and cryptographic checks) that are difficult to fake in static policy documents.
• Pay special attention to the separation of duties for audit management. If the same set of administrators can alter audit fundamentals and have access to audit data, the control is effectively weakened.
• Watch out for settings that appear compliant on paper but where logs are stored on easily compromised media or systems (e.g., general-purpose OS without hardened security configurations).

These steps should help in practically verifying that audit information and logging tools are protected as required by AU-9, ensuring both technical controls and operational effectiveness are in place.
content:
Statement:
a. Protect audit information and audit logging tools from unauthorized access, modification, and deletion; and b. Alert [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles] upon detection of unauthorized access, modification, or deletion of audit information.

Guidance:
Audit information includes all information needed to successfully audit system activity, such as audit records, audit log settings, audit reports, and personally identifiable information. Audit logging tools are those programs and devices used to conduct system audit and logging activities. Protection of audit information focuses on technical protection and limits the ability to access and execute audit logging tools to authorized individuals. Physical protection of audit information is addressed by both media protection controls and physical and environmental protection controls.

Enhancements:
(1) PROTECTION OF AUDIT INFORMATION | HARDWARE WRITE-ONCE MEDIA  Discussion: Writing audit trails to hardware-enforced, write-once media applies to the initial generation of audit trails (i.e., the collection of audit records that represents the information to be used for detection, analysis, and reporting purposes) and to the backup of those audit trails. Writing audit trails to hardware-enforced, write-once media does not apply to the initial generation of audit records prior to being written to an audit trail. Write-once, read-many (WORM) media includes Compact Disc-Recordable (CD-R), Blu-Ray Disc Recordable (BD-R), and Digital Versatile Disc-Recordable (DVD-R). In contrast, the use of switchable write-protection media, such as tape cartridges, Universal Serial Bus (USB) drives, Compact Disc Re-Writable (CD-RW), and Digital Versatile Disc-Read Write (DVD-RW) results in write-protected but not write-once media.
(2) PROTECTION OF AUDIT INFORMATION | STORE ON SEPARATE PHYSICAL SYSTEMS OR COMPONENTS  Store audit records [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] in a repository that is part of a physically different system or system component than the system or component being audited. Discussion: Storing audit records in a repository separate from the audited system or system component helps to ensure that a compromise of the system being audited does not also result in a compromise of the audit records. Storing audit records on separate physical systems or components also preserves the confidentiality and integrity of audit records and facilitates the management of audit records as an organization-wide activity.
(3) PROTECTION OF AUDIT INFORMATION | CRYPTOGRAPHIC PROTECTION Implement cryptographic mechanisms to protect the integrity of audit information and audit tools. Discussion: Cryptographic mechanisms used for protecting the integrity of audit information include signed hash functions using asymmetric cryptography. This enables the distribution of the public key to verify the hash information while maintaining the confidentiality of the secret key used to generate the hash.
(4) PROTECTION OF AUDIT INFORMATION | ACCESS BY SUBSET OF PRIVILEGED USERS Authorize access to management of audit logging functionality to only [Assignment: organization-defined subset of privileged users or roles]. Discussion: Individuals or roles with privileged access to a system and who are also the subject of an audit by that system may affect the reliability of the audit inhibiting audit activities or modifying audit records. Requiring privileged access to be further defined between audit-related privileges and other privileges limits the number of users or roles with audit-related privileges.
(6) PROTECTION OF AUDIT INFORMATION | READ-ONLY ACCESS Authorize read-only access to audit information to [Assignment: organization-defined subset of privileged users or roles]. Discussion: Restricting privileged user or role authorizations to read-only helps to limit the potential damage to organizations that could be initiated by such users or roles, such as deleting audit records to cover up malicious activity.
(7) PROTECTION OF AUDIT INFORMATION | STORE ON COMPONENT WITH DIFFERENT OPERATING SYSTEM Store audit information on a component running a different operating system than the system or component being audited. Discussion: Storing auditing information on a system component running a different operating system reduces the risk of a vulnerability specific to the system, resulting in a compromise of the audit records.

title: TIME STAMPS
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Audit logs visibly display time stamps that include detailed date, time, and either a UTC marker or an explicit local offset. For example, logs may include entries like '2023-10-12T14:35:27Z' or '2023-10-12T10:35:27-04:00'.
• System configurations (e.g., NTP or chrony configurations) demonstrate that internal clocks are regularly synchronized with an authoritative time source.
• Technical artifacts, such as configuration files for log management agents or system audit daemons, reference use of internal system clocks and define the granularity (to the second, millisecond, etc.) expected by the organization.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations might modify log formats to include the expected time string, but the underlying source may be manual or not automated (e.g., scripts that insert static times rather than leveraging the system’s time service).
• Some may claim NTP synchronization in documentation without ensuring that the actual implementation synchronizes within the organization-defined granularity. 
• Logs might appear correct superficially, but detailed inspection may reveal that time stamps are not consistently generated by automated processes. For example, local systems might use their own unsynchronized clocks, resulting in time drift.

VALIDATION STEPS:
CHECK: Verify that audit log entries include time stamps with either UTC indications or a fixed local offset.
WHY: This confirms the system is generating audit records with the required time information and format.
HOW: Retrieve a sample of recent audit records from various systems and check for entries formatted with the full date, time, and explicit time offset. For example, search logs for patterns matching ISO 8601 (e.g., 2023-10-12T14:35:27Z or with an offset like -04:00).
FAIL: Absence of the time component or a missing time offset in audit records indicates a failure in implementation.

CHECK: Examine system and application configuration files to ensure that internal clocks are being used to generate time stamps and that the expected granularity is set.
WHY: This provides direct evidence of the intended implementation in the configuration, linking the system time settings to log generation.
HOW: Review configuration files for audit logging services (e.g., syslog, auditd, or custom logging applications) and note any parameters that specify time formats or refer to synchronization services like NTP.
FAIL: Missing or misconfigured settings (e.g., time format not including an offset or granularity not reflecting organizational requirements) suggests non-compliance.

CHECK: Validate synchronization with an authoritative time source by inspecting NTP (or equivalent time synchronization tool) status.
WHY: System clocks must be accurate to ensure that the generated time stamps meet the required granularity and reflect a consistent reference time.
HOW: Execute commands such as ntpq -p, timedatectl status, or equivalent on multiple devices to verify that the system clocks are synchronized correctly. Look for details such as offsets and jitter that meet the organization’s standards.
FAIL: Evidence of unsynchronized or drifting clocks, such as significant offsets or lack of recent synchronization events, indicates the control is not effectively implemented.

CHECK: Perform a cross-system correlation check by comparing time stamps for related events across different systems.
WHY: Consistency in audit record time stamps across systems is critical for accurate event correlation and forensic investigations.
HOW: Select an event that is logged on multiple systems and verify the recorded times are within the expected granularity range defined by the organization (e.g., differences within hundreds of milliseconds). This could involve comparing logs from interconnected systems or services that interact frequently.
FAIL: Large time discrepancies beyond the defined granularity or mismatches in time stamps signal potential issues with time synchronization or logging implementation.

EXPERT TIPS:
• As a shortcut, immediately prioritize checking the configuration of time synchronization services (such as NTP) since a faulty time source often cascades into incorrect audit record timestamps.
• Pay particular attention to audit log samples from different environments (production, test, backup) to spot inconsistencies that might be masked in isolated environments.
• Critical indicators of poor implementation include logs with manually adjusted or static time stamps, evidence of unsynchronized internal clocks, and inconsistent time stamp formats.
• Note that merely having a policy is insufficient; tangible evidence (e.g., automated logs, synchronized time service status) is needed to verify that the control is active and operational.
• Finally, check for any override or fallback configurations that might allow local time adjustments to bypass the synchronization requirements, as these are common weak points in implementation.
content:
Statement:
a. Use internal system clocks to generate time stamps for audit records; and b. Record time stamps for audit records that meet [Assignment: organization-defined granularity of time measurement] and that use Coordinated Universal Time, have a fixed local time offset from Coordinated Universal Time, or that include the local time offset as part of the time stamp.

Guidance:
Time stamps generated by the system include date and time. Time is commonly expressed in Coordinated Universal Time (UTC), a modern continuation of Greenwich Mean Time (GMT), or local time with an offset from UTC. Granularity of time measurements refers to the degree of synchronization between system clocks and reference clocks (e.g., clocks synchronizing within hundreds of milliseconds or tens of milliseconds). Organizations may define different time granularities for different system components. Time service can be critical to other security capabilities such as access control and identification and authentication, depending on the nature of the mechanisms used to support those capabilities.

Enhancements:
(1) TIME STAMPS | SYNCHRONIZATION WITH AUTHORITATIVE TIME SOURCE [Withdrawn: Moved to SC-45(1).]
(2) TIME STAMPS | SECONDARY AUTHORITATIVE TIME SOURCE

title: AUDIT RECORD REDUCTION AND REPORT GENERATION
methodology:
Control AU-7 focuses on the automated processing of audit logs for record reduction and report generation without modifying the raw data's content or order. Below is a practical methodology to validate its implementation:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • The existence of a dashboard or interface showing on-demand, automated search and report generation from centralized audit logs.
   • Technical artifacts such as system configuration files, scripts, or applications that execute log reduction algorithms and produce summary reports while retaining original logs separately.
   • Operational patterns where audit records are continuously forwarded to an audit repository and parallel processing systems generate filtering and sorting based on defined criteria, including system resource details, user identities, event types, IP addresses, timestamps, etc.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Relying solely on static, pre-generated reports without actual on-demand capabilities might create an illusion of compliance.
   • Implementation of log reduction tools that summarize or aggregate log data but inadvertently alter the chronological order or omit key fields required for incident investigations.
   • Systems that only host documentation or policy statements regarding report generation with limited actual monitoring or verification of the underlying process.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify the automated audit event processing pipeline (ingestion, reduction and report generation) is active and searchable.
   WHY: This ensures the system performs on-demand analysis and reporting without manual intervention and maintains the integrity of original logs.
   HOW: Review system logs and configuration files, trigger an on-demand report generation while comparing the report output with the raw audit records to confirm that time order and complete content are preserved. Use automation tools to simulate event searches based on defined audit fields.
   FAIL: Inability to generate ad-hoc reports or any modifications to the underlying raw logs (such as missing timestamps or altered record orders) indicate non-compliance.

   CHECK: Validate that the audit record reduction mechanism only processes copies of audit data without altering or reordering the original records.
   WHY: This is crucial to ensure forensic integrity of the audit trail during incident investigations.
   HOW: Identify the raw audit logs’ storage location and perform a side-by-side comparison with the reported data output. Confirm that time stamps and event ordering remain consistent between raw logs and summary reports.
   FAIL: Any evidence of changed timestamps, record order, or edited content in the original audit logs suggests failure to comply.

   CHECK: Assess automated sorting and search functionality based on the organization-defined audit record fields.
   WHY: Proper sorting and search capabilities confirm that the system can identify and outline events of interest per defined criteria, ensuring operational effectiveness during investigations.
   HOW: Execute targeted searches using sample parameters like user ID, IP address, event type. Validate that the resulting outputs accurately reflect the corresponding records in the audit log repository.
   FAIL: Inability to refine search results to expected subsets or discrepancies between reported and actual audit record content indicate poor implementation.

   (Optional) CHECK: Review configuration and system documentation to verify alignment with incident investigation requirements.
   WHY: While automation is preferred over policy, cross-checking configuration details helps verify that the automated mechanisms are set to handle all defined criteria.
   HOW: Interview technical staff and inspect configuration files for evidence of automatic processing settings, criteria definitions, and periodic reviews.
   FAIL: Outdated configurations or missing parameter definitions as per organizational criteria raise concerns over the system’s readiness.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Always trigger an on-demand report test and immediately compare the results against raw log files. This immediate comparison is a shortcut to detect if the audit record integrity is maintained.
   • Check timestamps and sequential identifiers in both the raw and processed logs. Any discrepancies are a red flag.
   • Prioritize the use of automated tools that log every access and search operation as part of the audit process itself. Such meta-audit trails, if available, provide quick forensic insights into how effectively the system is implemented.
   • Validate that the system’s architecture clearly separates raw log storage from the reduction/report generation process, ensuring that backups and original records remain unaltered.

These verification steps are designed to emphasize operational effectiveness and ensure that the audit log reduction and report generation capability is not only documented, but also practically and securely implemented.
content:
Statement:
Provide and implement an audit record reduction and report generation capability that: a. Supports on-demand audit record review, analysis, and reporting requirements and after-the-fact investigations of incidents; and b. Does not alter the original content or time ordering of audit records.

Guidance:
Audit record reduction is a process that manipulates collected audit log information and organizes it into a summary format that is more meaningful to analysts. Audit record

Enhancements:
(1) AUDIT RECORD REDUCTION AND REPORT GENERATION | AUTOMATIC PROCESSING  Provide and implement the capability to process, sort, and search audit records for events of interest based on the following content: [Assignment: organization-defined fields within audit records].  Discussion: Events of interest can be identified by the content of audit records, including system resources involved, information objects accessed, identities of individuals, event types, event locations, event dates and times, Internet Protocol addresses involved, or event success or failure. Organizations may define event criteria to any degree of granularity required, such as locations selectable by a general networking location or by specific system component. Related Controls: None.
(2) AUDIT RECORD REDUCTION AND REPORT GENERATION | AUTOMATIC SORT AND SEARCH [Withdrawn: Incorporated into AU-71(1).]

title: AUDIT RECORD REVIEW, ANALYSIS, AND REPORTING
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for AU-6 (Audit Record Review, Analysis, and Reporting):

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Evidence includes automated SIEM dashboards showing continuous log ingestion and correlation across multiple sources (system logs, physical access logs, vulnerability scans, etc.).
   • Technical artifacts: system audit logs, review reports, change logs showing adjustments to review frequency when new risk information is received, centralized log repositories, and timestamps indicating periodic review.
   • Operational patterns: regular trigger-based alerts, documented rapid response to suspicious events, and integration between audit record management and incident response.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations may list review frequency or analysis procedures in policies but not follow through operationally (e.g., infrequent manual reviews that don’t match system events).
   • Surface-level implementations include having audit logs stored but not actively analyzed or correlated with physical monitoring data or external risk feeds.
   • Gaps often missed: integration of automated analysis tools (SIEM), failure to correlate audit record repositories from different system components, and lack of dynamic adjustment to log review practices based on emerging threats.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   • CHECK: Confirm the periodic review of audit records using automated SIEM dashboards. 
     WHY: Automated dashboards with time-stamped alerts demonstrate active, ongoing audit record analysis at the defined frequency. 
     HOW: Verify that the SIEM or equivalent tool logs show automated review processes and correlate review triggers (e.g., anomalous pattern detection) with timestamps matching documented review periods. 
     FAIL: Absence of automated, time-stamped audit review logs or dashboards that show sporadic or manual entries only.
   
   • CHECK: Validate that audit log analysis is integrated with incident response processes and that suspicious events are reported to designated personnel. 
     WHY: Evidence of integration with incident response confirms that findings lead to operational actions. 
     HOW: Examine incident response records, ticketing systems, or post-incident reports for entries triggered by audit log reviews; interview personnel to corroborate that alerts from audit record analysis are routinely escalated. 
     FAIL: Lack of evidence of incident tickets or absence of correlation between audit log alerts and follow-up incident response actions.
   
   • CHECK: Inspect correlation capabilities across diverse audit record sources (e.g., combining system access logs with physical access monitoring logs). 
     WHY: Correlation across different data repositories improves situational awareness and indicates a comprehensive review process. 
     HOW: Review configuration settings within the SIEM which integrate digital and physical access logs, and analyze reports showing cross-referenced events (e.g., an alert combining an abnormal remote login with a physical facility entry record). 
     FAIL: Disparate or siloed log repositories with no evidence of cross-correlation, or outdated integration settings that have not been maintained.
   
   • CHECK: Verify that there is an adjustment process in place to change audit review frequency or depth based on new risk information (e.g., law enforcement intelligence). 
     WHY: Dynamically adjusting the review process in response to emerging risks ensures the control remains effective over time. 
     HOW: Review change management logs, meeting records, or configuration change requests that document adjustments to audit review settings along with the risk assessment inputs that led to the changes. 
     FAIL: Static audit review settings that do not reflect risk changes or absence of documented evidence linking risk alerts to adjustments in audit analysis processes.
   
   • (Optional) CHECK: Ensure that automated mechanisms are in place for centralized review and analysis of audit records. 
     WHY: A centralized approach (e.g., via a SIEM) prevents working in silos and promotes organization-wide situational awareness. 
     HOW: Examine system architecture diagrams and SIEM configuration screens that list multiple log sources, and validate that automated alerts are sent to the designated review team. 
     FAIL: Evidence of only localized log storage with no centralized aggregation or manual processes that bypass automated review tools.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Tip: Always drill down into the SIEM’s alert history reports rather than relying solely on high-level dashboards. Look for evidence of alerts that were triggered automatically and matched with incident response follow-up. 
   • Tip: Prioritize review evidence that cannot be effortlessly fabricated—time-stamped, system-generated logs that correlate various data points (e.g., physical and digital access) are more reliable than static policy documents. 
   • Tip: Critical indicators of poor implementation include long periods with no logged reviews or adjustments, mismatches between documented policy and actual SIEM configuration, or isolated logs with no cross-correlation of data sources.

This methodology emphasizes practical, observable evidence and operational integration over mere policy documentation, ensuring the control is effective in its intended environment.
content:
Statement:
Review and analyze system audit records [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] for indications of [Assignment: organization-defined inappropriate or unusual activity] and the potential impact of the inappropriate or unusual activity; b. Report findings to [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles]; and c. Adjust the level of audit record review, analysis, and reporting within the system when there is a change in risk based on law enforcement information, intelligence information, or other credible sources of information.

Guidance:
Audit record review, analysis, and reporting covers information security- and privacy-related logging performed by organizations, including logging that results from the monitoring of account access, secure remote access, wireless connectivity, mobile device connection, configuration settings, system component inventory, use of maintenance tools and non-local maintenance, physical access, temperature and humidity, equipment delivery and removal, communications at system interfaces, and use of mobile code or Voice over Internet Protocol (VoIP). Findings can be reported to organizational entities that include the incident response team, help desk, and security or privacy offices. If organizations are prohibited from reviewing and analyzing audit records or unable to conduct such activities, the review or analysis may be carried out by other organizations granted such authority. The frequency, scope, and/or depth of the audit record review, analysis, and reporting may be adjusted to meet organizational needs based on new information received.

Enhancements:
(1) AUDIT RECORD REVIEW, ANALYSIS, AND REPORTING | AUTOMATED PROCESS INTEGRATION Integrate audit record review, analysis, and reporting processes using [Assignment: organization-defined automated mechanisms].
