Discussion: Organizational processes that benefit from integrated audit record review, analysis, and reporting include incident response, continuous monitoring, contingency planning, investigation and response to suspicious activities, and Inspector General audits.
(2) AUDIT RECORD REVIEW, ANALYSIS, AND REPORTING | AUTOMATED SECURITY ALERTS [Withdrawn: Incorporated into SI-4.]
(3) AUDIT RECORD REVIEW, ANALYSIS, AND REPORTING | CORRELATE AUDIT RECORD REPOSITORIES Analyze and correlate audit records across different repositories to gain organization-wide situational awareness. Discussion: Organization-wide situational awareness includes awareness across all three levels of risk management (i.e., organizational level, mission/business process level, and information system level) and supports cross-organization awareness. Related Controls: AU-12, IR-4.
(4) AUDIT RECORD REVIEW, ANALYSIS, AND REPORTING | CENTRAL REVIEW AND ANALYSIS Provide and implement the capability to centrally review and analyze audit records from multiple components within the system. Discussion: Automated mechanisms for centralized reviews and analyses include Security Information and Event Management products. Related Controls: AU-2, AU-12.
(5) AUDIT RECORD REVIEW, ANALYSIS, AND REPORTING | INTEGRATED ANALYSIS OF AUDIT RECORDS Integrate analysis of audit records with analysis of [Selection (one or more): vulnerability scanning information; performance data; system monitoring information; Assignment: organization-defined data/information collected from other sources] to further enhance the ability to identify inappropriate or unusual activity. Discussion: Integrated analysis of audit records does not require vulnerability scanning, the generation of performance data, or system monitoring. Rather, integrated analysis requires that the analysis of information generated by scanning, monitoring, or other data collection activities is integrated with the analysis of audit record information. Related Controls: AU-12, IR-4.
(6) AUDIT RECORD REVIEW, ANALYSIS, AND REPORTING | CORRELATION WITH PHYSICAL MONITORING Correlate information from audit records with information obtained from monitoring physical access to further enhance the ability to identify suspicious, inappropriate, unusual, or malevolent activity. Discussion: The correlation of physical audit record information and the audit records from systems may assist organizations in identifying suspicious behavior or supporting evidence of such behavior. For example, the correlation of an individual's identity for logical access to certain systems with the additional physical security information that the individual was present at the facility when the logical access occurred may be useful in investigations.

title: RESPONSE TO AUDIT LOGGING PROCESS FAILURES
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Evidence that alerts are automatically generated when audit logging failures occur (e.g., notifications in SIEM dashboards, email alerts, or SMS messages).
• Configurations and system logs that record audit log failure events, including those related to hardware/software errors, log capture failures, or storage capacity limits.
• Operational records showing that designated personnel or roles consistently receive and acknowledge alerts within the organization-defined time period.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may simply configure manual, periodic log reviews and claim compliance without real-time alerting, which can delay response times.
• Software configuration files might exist on paper or be improperly set up so that alerts are generated but not delivered to the intended personnel.
• Dummy logs or test alerts might be injected to simulate failures without proving the mechanism works under real conditions.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Verify the configuration of automated alerting in the SIEM or log management system for audit logging process failures.
   WHY: This proves that system-triggered alerts are in place to notify the designated roles when failures occur.
   HOW: Retrieve the configuration settings for audit log failure alerting, then trigger a controlled audit logging error (e.g., simulating a storage threshold breach) in a test environment and review the alert logs to confirm that a notification is sent within the defined timeframe.
   FAIL: Absence of triggered alerts or alerts not reaching the designated recipients indicates a failure.

2. CHECK: Examine system and audit logs for records of audit logging failure events and corresponding response actions.
   WHY: Operational log entries showing responses to actual failures confirm the control is active and effective.
   HOW: Query the audit logs for events categorized as audit logging failures and verify that subsequent actions (such as system shutdown, log overwriting, or traffic delay) were executed according to policy. Compare timestamps to ensure responses were within the defined period.
   FAIL: Missing or inconsistent logs, or timestamps indicating delayed response, would signal non-compliance.

3. CHECK: Validate the integration of real-time alerting mechanisms, especially for enhancements like STORAGE CAPACITY WARNING and REAL-TIME ALERTS.
   WHY: Effective real-time alerts are essential to manage critical failures before they impact operations.
   HOW: Inspect real-time monitoring dashboards and configuration files to confirm that alerts trigger when storage capacity nears the defined threshold. If possible, simulate the condition (e.g., by adjusting thresholds temporarily in a controlled environment) and check for immediate notifications sent.
   FAIL: Delayed or absent alerts upon threshold conditions indicate an implementation weakness.

4. CHECK: Review system response configurations (e.g., shutdown or traffic rejection) that are triggered by specific audit logging failures.
   WHY: Confirming that automatic system controls (such as a full shutdown or partial degradation) are configured demonstrates proactive risk management.
   HOW: Inspect system configuration files or automated scripts that implement these responses. Conduct a simulation or review change logs that reflect these actions being invoked during testing or actual events.
   FAIL: Lack of clear, actionable responses or evidence that the defined actions are implemented correctly is a red flag.

5. CHECK: Interview key personnel or review incident response records to verify that designated roles have acknowledged and acted upon alerts.
   WHY: Operational effectiveness relies on both technical controls and people awareness/responsiveness.
   HOW: Check incident response logs, email notifications, or helpdesk ticket records proving that alerts have been escalated and addressed promptly by the designated roles.
   FAIL: Evidence that alerts were ignored or not processed according to the policy indicates a breakdown in the process.

EXPERT TIPS
• Use simulated audit log failure events to test the entire alerting and response chain; this not only validates configuration but also the operational readiness of the response team.
• Pay close attention to timestamps and response intervals in logs; even minor delays might disqualify the mechanism as compliant with defined time frames.
• Ensure that test events are isolated from production, yet mimic real-time conditions to validate true performance under similar loads.
• Verify that there is a fallback process in place when the primary logging capability fails, which is often a missed detail in many systems.

The above methodology emphasizes real-time, automated checks and observable evidence rather than relying solely on policy documentation, ensuring operational effectiveness of the audit logging process failure response mechanism.
content:
Statement:
a. Alert [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles] within [Assignment: organization-defined time period] in the event of an audit logging process failure; and b. Take the following additional actions: [Assignment: organization-defined additional actions].

Guidance:
Audit logging process failures include software and hardware errors, failures in audit log capturing mechanisms, and reaching or exceeding audit log storage capacity. Organization-defined actions include overwriting oldest audit records, shutting down the system, and stopping the generation of audit records. Organizations may choose to define additional actions for audit logging process failures based on the type of failure, the location of the failure, the severity of the failure, or a combination of such factors. When the audit logging process failure is related to storage, the response is carried out for the audit log storage repository (i.e., the distinct system component where the audit logs are stored), the system on which the audit logs reside, the total audit log storage capacity of the organization (i.e., all audit log storage repositories combined), or all three. Organizations may decide to take no additional actions after alerting designated roles or personnel.

Enhancements:
(1) RESPONSE TO AUDIT LOGGING PROCESS FAILURES | STORAGE CAPACITY WARNING Provide a warning to [Assignment: organization-defined personnel, roles, and/or locations] within [Assignment: organization-defined time period] when allocated audit log storage volume reaches [Assignment: organization-defined percentage] of repository maximum audit log storage capacity. Discussion: Organizations may have multiple audit log storage repositories distributed across multiple system components with each repository having different storage volume capacities. Related Controls: None.
(2) RESPONSE TO AUDIT LOGGING PROCESS FAILURES | REAL-TIME ALERTS Provide an alert within [Assignment: organization-defined real-time period] to [Assignment: organization-defined personnel, roles, and/or locations] when the following audit failure events occur: [Assignment: organization-defined audit logging failure events requiring real-time alerts]. Discussion: Alerts provide organizations with urgent messages. Real-time alerts provide these messages at information technology speed (i.e., the time from event detection to alert occurs in seconds or less). Related Controls: None.
(3) RESPONSE TO AUDIT LOGGING PROCESS FAILURES | CONFIGURABLE TRAFFIC VOLUME THRESHOLDS Enforce configurable network communications traffic volume thresholds reflecting limits on audit log storage capacity and [Selection: reject; delay] network traffic above those thresholds. Discussion: Organizations have the capability to reject or delay the processing of network communications traffic if audit logging information about such traffic is determined to exceed the storage capacity of the system audit logging function. The rejection or delay response is triggered by the established organizational traffic volume thresholds that can be adjusted based on changes to audit log storage capacity. Related Controls: None.
(4) RESPONSE TO AUDIT LOGGING PROCESS FAILURES | SHUTDOWN ON FAILURE Invoke a [Selection: full system shutdown; partial system shutdown; degraded operational mode with limited mission or business functionality available] in the event of [Assignment: organization-defined audit logging failures], unless an alternate audit logging capability exists. Discussion: Organizations determine the types of audit logging failures that can trigger automatic system shutdowns or degraded operations. Because of the importance of ensuring mission and business continuity, organizations may determine that the nature of the audit logging failure is not severe that it warrants a complete shutdown of the system.

title: AUDIT LOG STORAGE CAPACITY
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Audit log systems have configuration files or management dashboards that show the allocated storage capacity, current utilization, and retention settings matching organizational requirements.
• Automated log rotation and archival processes (including off-loading to alternate storage if enabled) are in place and logged.
• Monitoring alerts (or scheduled reports) indicate that storage usage is tracked and actions are taken when thresholds are met.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may simply document a policy for log retention without properly configuring or provisioning sufficient capacity, resulting in log truncation or loss.
• Relying solely on manual checks or outdated configurations that do not reflect the live system can create a false sense of compliance.
• Implementing an automated off-load process on paper while the actual process fails or is misconfigured (e.g., wrong frequency or target location) is a common technical gap.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Verify log management configuration to confirm that the allocated storage capacity aligns with the defined log retention requirements.
   WHY: This provides direct evidence that the system is set to store logs for the period defined by the organization, which is critical to prevent loss of audit records.
   HOW: Review system configuration files, log management console settings, or command-line queries that show allocated capacity, retention periods, and thresholds. Query disk usage statistics for the partition dedicated to logs.
   FAIL: Missing or misconfigured capacity settings, or a discrepancy between retention requirements and actual capacity allocated.

2. CHECK: Inspect automatic log rotation and archival/off-loading processes (if the organization has implemented the enhancement for transferring logs to alternate storage).
   WHY: Active, scheduled log transfers indicate that the organization is actively managing storage capacity and protecting log availability.
   HOW: Audit the scheduler or job logs to confirm regular, successful transfers of audit logs. Check log files for timestamps that denote off-loading events, and verify that they match the defined schedule.
   FAIL: Absence of transfer logs/entries, irregular schedule, errors in the log transfer jobs, or evidence that logs remain on the local system beyond expected durations.

3. CHECK: Confirm operational monitoring and alerting mechanisms on log storage usage.
   WHY: Real-time monitoring and automated alerts are critical to quickly identify and resolve issues before log capacity is exhausted.
   HOW: Review the configuration of monitoring tools or SIEM alerts to ensure thresholds for log storage utilization are defined and that alerts are being generated and acted upon. Look for historical alerts and remediation records.
   FAIL: Lack of alerts, disabled monitoring, or records of capacity issues that were not addressed, suggesting ineffective operational management.

4. CHECK: Validate actual audit log sizes over a defined period and compare them with expected growth as per retention policy.
   WHY: Cross-checking recorded log sizes against expected values ensures that logs are not prematurely rotated or overwritten.
   HOW: Retrieve historical audit log records, compare sizes against capacity allocation, review retention logs, and confirm that no logs were lost due to storage-capacity issues. This step can include automated scripts calculating daily/weekly log volume trends.
   FAIL: Observing persistent discrepancies between expected and actual log sizes or missing log entries that indicate records were dropped.

EXPERT TIPS
• When verifying storage capacity, cross-check actual disk usage figures with the configured retention settings; discrepancies often indicate that logs are being written faster than anticipated.
• Pay close attention to logs related to alert triggers and job scheduler outputs – these are the ‘canary in the coal mine’ for storage issues.
• Check for redundancy in the off-loading process; if logs are only copied to alternate storage without verification, it might be a weak point.
• Always validate that the configuration changes (e.g., increased capacity or adjusted transfer frequency) are both documented and reflected in the live system configurations.
content:
Statement:
Allocate audit log storage capacity to accommodate [Assignment: organization-defined audit log retention requirements].

Guidance:
Organizations consider the types of audit logging to be performed and the audit logging process requirements when allocating audit log storage capacity. Allocating sufficient audit log storage capacity reduces the likelihood of such capacity being exceeded and resulting in the potential loss or reduction of audit logging capability.

Enhancements:
(1) AUDIT LOG STORAGE CAPACITY | TRANSFER TO ALTERNATE STORAGE Transfer audit logs [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] to a different system, system component, or media other than the system or system component conducting the logging. Discussion: Audit log transfer, also known as off‐loading, is a common process in systems with limited audit log storage capacity and thus supports availability of the audit logs. The initial audit log storage is only used in a transitory fashion until the system can communicate with the secondary or alternate system allocated to audit log storage, at which point the audit logs are transferred. Transferring audit logs to alternate storage is similar to AU-9(2) in that audit logs are transferred to a different entity. However, the purpose of selecting AU-9(2) is to protect the confidentiality and integrity of audit records. Organizations can select either control enhancement to obtain the benefit of increased audit log storage capacity and preserving the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of audit records and logs.

title: CONTENT OF AUDIT RECORDS
methodology:
Below is the practical validation methodology for AU-3 – Content of Audit Records:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Audit logs visibly stored in centralized log management consoles or SIEM tools showing event records with clearly defined fields. Configuration screens or system settings that specify which fields are captured.
• Technical Artifacts: System audit log files (e.g., syslog, Windows Security logs, auditd logs) that include key fields such as event type, time stamps, source/destination addresses, process IDs, and user or object identities. Configuration files or audit policies specifying logging parameters.
• Operational Patterns: Consistent and automated generation of logs during typical system and security events. Periodic review logs, and cross-correlation evidence, such as time-synced events from multiple systems.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Fake Compliance: Organizations might enable auditing in policies but fail to capture the detailed required fields – for example, logging only a generic event code without capturing who triggered the event or where it occurred. Some may inject dummy fields to indicate compliance.
• Surface-Level Implementations: Relying solely on default logging without configuring additional fields. Using applications that provide only minimal logging that shows a timestamp and event code, but omit detailed context like user identity or precise event outcome.
• Technical Gaps: Failing to configure proper time synchronization (NTP), leaving disparate clocks that hinder the correlation of events. Overlooking logging of critical audit trails on less monitored subsystems or privileged functions.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK 1: Verify Presence of Full Audit Record Fields
WHY: Ensures that logs contain all critical information elements (event type, timestamp, location, source, outcome, and identity) required by the control.
HOW: Randomly select a sample of system-generated audit records and inspect for complete fields. Use queries in your SIEM or log management tool to extract recent events and confirm inclusion of each required element.
FAIL: Absence or partial entries – for example, logs that only show a timestamp or event type without identity or outcome details.

CHECK 2: Validate Time Synchronization and Accurate Timestamps
WHY: Consistent timestamps across multiple systems are crucial for accurate event correlation and incident reconstruction.
HOW: Check configuration settings (e.g., NTP configuration) on audited systems. Correlate events logged from different sources to confirm that recorded times are accurate and in sync.
FAIL: Evidence of significant time drift or inconsistencies across records that could indicate misconfigured or disabled time synchronization services.

CHECK 3: Review Audit Policy and Configuration Files
WHY: Configuration files are the blueprint for what gets logged; reviewing them confirms that the audit system is set to capture all required information.
HOW: Examine audit configuration files (for example, auditd.conf on Linux or Group Policy settings on Windows) to verify that fields like source addresses, outcome, and identities are included in the audit logging parameters.
FAIL: Policies or configuration files that lack explicit directives for recording the necessary fields or revert to basic/default settings.

CHECK 4: Cross-Verify Audit Records with Actual System Activity
WHY: Ensures logs are dynamically generated from actual events rather than being manually curated or faked.
HOW: Simulate a controlled event (e.g., a login attempt or file access) and immediately verify that the corresponding audit record includes all required fields. Cross-reference with user session data and system events.
FAIL: If the simulated events either do not appear in audit logs or appear with incomplete, inconsistent information.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut Verification: Use automated log scanning tools to search for mandatory fields in records. Tools that can flag missing required audit information are invaluable.
• Critical Indicators: Pay special attention to logs from security-critical events like authentication attempts. Inadequate detail around these events is a red flag.
• Consider Aggregation: Ensure logs from autonomous systems are being centralized, which helps identify discrepancies that may be hidden in isolated systems.
• Don’t Rely Solely on Documentation: Real implementation must be evidenced through live system checks. Configuration parameters and on-disk log records often reveal departures from documented controls.

This methodology focuses on verifying that the audit records are not only present and complete, but are also reliably generated in alignment with the intended security posture. The emphasis is on operational evidence that cannot be easily faked through documentation alone.
content:
Statement:
Ensure that audit records contain information that establishes the following: a. What type of event occurred; b. When the event occurred; c. Where the event occurred; d. Source of the event; e. Outcome of the event; and f. Identity of any individuals, subjects, or objects/entities associated with the event.

Guidance:
Audit record content that may be necessary to support the auditing function includes event descriptions (item a), time stamps (item b), source and destination addresses (item c), or process identifiers (items d and f), source or fail indications (item e), and filenames involved (items a, c, e, and f). Event outcomes include indicators of event success or failure and event-specific results, such as system security and privacy posture after the event occurred. Organizations consider how audit records can reveal information about individuals that may give rise to privacy risks and how best to mitigate such risks. For example, there is potential to reveal personally identifiable information in the audit trail, especially if the trail records inputs or is based on patterns or time of usage.

Enhancements:
(1) EVENT LOGGING | COMPILATION OF AUDIT RECORDS FROM MULTIPLE SOURCES [Withdrawn: Incorporated into AU-12.]
(2) EVENT LOGGING | SELECTION OF AUDIT EVENTS BY COMPONENT [Withdrawn: Incorporated into AU-12.]
(3) EVENT LOGGING | REVIEWS AND UPDATES [Withdrawn: Incorporated into AU-12.]
(4) EVENT LOGGING | PRIVILEGED FUNCTIONS [Withdrawn: Incorporated into AC-6(9).]
(1) CONTENT OF AUDIT RECORDS | ADDITIONAL AUDIT INFORMATION Generate audit records containing the following additional information: [Assignment: organization-defined additional information].

title: EVENT LOGGING
methodology:
Control AU-2 (EVENT LOGGING) Validation Methodology

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence includes system configuration files or admin consoles that list the event types being logged. Look for a clearly defined list of event categories (e.g., successful/failed logins, administrative actions, credential usage) that the system is capable of capturing.
• Technical artifacts include log aggregator configurations (syslog, SIEM dashboards) that show real-time logging of the assigned event types, audit log files with timestamps, and retention policies.
• Operational patterns might include periodic log reviews, change control records for updates to logging settings, and coordinated incident response playbooks that reference these event types.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations might claim compliance by maintaining a policy document that lists required events without ensuring the system’s logging mechanism is actively capturing them.
• Relying solely on manually maintained logs or screenshots instead of automated, time-stamped logs can be a red flag.
• A superficial implementation might involve enabling logging for many events but failing to coordinate or review these logs, making them ineffective for real investigations.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
• CHECK: Verify the configuration settings of the logging system to confirm that the list of event types matches the organization-defined list.
  WHY: This demonstrates that the system is configured to capture the required events.
  HOW: Review configuration files, admin interface settings, or SIEM input definitions. Check for evidence that error events, authentication events, file accesses, and privilege use are logged.
  FAIL: A mismatch between documented event types and actual logging configurations, or absence of critical event types.

• CHECK: Examine a sample of audit logs to confirm they capture the event details (timestamp, event type, user context, etc.) for the defined events.
  WHY: Detailed logs provide evidence that events are not only configured but actively captured in an operational setting.
  HOW: Query the SIEM or log repository for recent entries corresponding to sensitive event types (failed logins, admin actions, etc.). Verify that logs contain adequate detail for after-the-fact analysis.
  FAIL: Sparse or incomplete log details that do not provide sufficient forensic information.

• CHECK: Review the records of coordination efforts between the logging function and other organizational entities (e.g., security team, incident response, privacy officers).
  WHY: It demonstrates that event logging is integrated into the broader organizational security strategy and continuously refined.
  HOW: Analyze meeting minutes, change request records, or incident post-mortems referencing modifications to logging events and frequency reviews.
  FAIL: Lack of documented coordination or evidence of periodic review of the logging configuration.

• CHECK: Validate the update process for the logged event types by reviewing timestamps or version changes in the logging configuration, and evidence that the rationale for each event type is documented.
  WHY: A well-maintained update mechanism ensures that the logging policy remains relevant and effective as system threats evolve.
  HOW: Inspect change logs, configuration management databases, or audit trail documentation that shows updates tied to organizational risk assessments.
  FAIL: No trace of periodic review or updates, or an outdated rationale that no longer reflects current security needs.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Use automated SIEM queries to confirm the presence and regular appearance of key event types (e.g., repeated failed logins or privilege escalation events). This minimizes reliance on static documentation.
• Critical indicator: The absence of detailed context (user ID, source IP, success/failure flags) in log entries is a strong sign of a poorly executed implementation.
• Focus on integration: Verify that log aggregation systems (such as centralized SIEM solutions) are not only collecting logs but are also tied into alerting mechanisms and regular review cycles.
• Remember that mere existence of policy documents does not guarantee real effectiveness—operational evidence in live or archived logs is paramount.

This methodology focuses on practical, evidence-based checks that prevent the common pitfall of faking compliance through documentation alone, ensuring that event logging under AU-2 functions as intended to support thorough after-the-fact investigations.

title: POLICY AND PROCEDURES
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for NIST-AU-1 (Audit and Accountability Policy and Procedures) control.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: Look for a formally published audit and accountability policy along with associated procedures. This might be available on the internal intranet, part of the security manual, or printed documentation posted in common areas. There should also be an organizational chart or appointment letter showing the designated official responsible for this control.
   • Technical Artifacts: Review version-controlled documents (e.g., policy and procedure repositories) that include a revision history, timestamps, and evidence of periodic updates triggered by events (such as security incidents or regulatory changes). You may also find system logs indicating training sessions or acknowledgement receipts for policy dissemination.
   • Operational Patterns: Regular updates or reviews in response to audits, incident reports, or changes in legal requirements. Evidence of periodic training sessions and policy acknowledgment sign-offs during onboarding can indicate the control is actively maintained.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations might simply produce a static document that exists on paper but is not actively updated based on events or kept current with applicable laws and regulations.
   • Documentation may be overly generic, lacking in the required specifics like roles, responsibilities, and management commitment, making it ineffective in practical scenarios.
   • Some organizations designate a role on paper without ensuring the individual is empowered or actively managing the policy lifecycle, resulting in policies that are not actually disseminated or enforced.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify the existence of a formally documented audit and accountability policy that includes purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, coordination among organizational entities, and compliance requirements.
   WHY: A comprehensive, detailed policy document proves that the organization has clearly defined audit requirements and is aligned with legal and regulatory expectations.
   HOW: Review policy documents from official repositories, check for sections addressing the required elements, and confirm that the document is version-controlled with documented review dates and revision details.
   FAIL: If the document is missing, lacks the required sections, or shows no evidence of periodic review/update (revision history is absent or outdated).

   CHECK: Confirm that there are documented procedures which facilitate the implementation of the audit and accountability policy and controls.
   WHY: Detailed implementation procedures ensure that the policy is actionable and that personnel understand how to fulfill their responsibilities.
   HOW: Retrieve and examine procedural documents, look for step-by-step guidelines on control implementation, and verify that these procedures are publicly disseminated among relevant roles (e.g., via training records or intranet postings).
   FAIL: If the procedures are either missing, are too generic (simply restating the policy), or haven’t been disseminated to the staff impacted.

   CHECK: Validate the appointment of a designated official responsible for managing the development and dissemination of the policy and procedures.
   WHY: A clearly appointed individual or role signifies accountability and ensures active maintenance and enforcement of the control.
   HOW: Look for organizational charts, appointment letters, or documentation that explicitly assigns this responsibility. Interview key staff if necessary.
   FAIL: Absence of any formal designation or if the documentation indicates that this responsibility has been informally assigned without proper communication or authority.

   CHECK: Review evidence that the audit and accountability policy and procedures are regularly reviewed and updated per defined frequencies or triggered events (such as audit findings, incidents, or regulatory changes).
   WHY: Regular review and timely updates are indicators that the organization actively manages its risk and stays compliant with evolving standards.
   HOW: Examine revision logs, meeting minutes, or audit reports that discuss policy updates. Confirm with the designated official that scheduled reviews (or triggered reviews) are indeed practiced.
   FAIL: No evidence of scheduled reviews or triggered revisions, outdated documents with no revision history, or failure to respond to security incidents/regulatory changes.

   CHECK: Look for training and dissemination records to ensure that the policy and procedures are communicated to the intended personnel or roles.
   WHY: Dissemination of the policy ensures that staff are aware of their responsibilities and can implement controls as required.
   HOW: Check training logs, acknowledgement forms, internal email distribution lists, or intranet announcements confirming receipt and comprehension of the policy.
   FAIL: Lack of training evidence, or if policy documents exist that have not been communicated effectively to the targeted personnel.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • When reviewing documentation, always check for a clear revision history and documented dates of review – this is a red flag when missing or inconsistent.
   • Interview key personnel (especially the designated official) to determine if policies are not just documents on a shelf but are actively enforced and understood.
   • Cross-reference policy and procedural documentation with actual system logs or incident reports to ensure that the theoretical controls are reflected in operational practice.
   • Look for integration of the audit and accountability review process with overall risk management and incident response programs as strong indicators of an operationally effective control.
   • Pay particular attention to the dissemination evidence – a policy that exists only in a central repository but lacks targeted communication means is a common pitfall.

This step-by-step methodology focuses on observable, operational evidence of effectiveness, making it difficult for organizations to simply “fake” compliance without true active implementation.
content:
Statement:
a. Develop, document, and disseminate to [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles]:

1. [Selection (one or more): Organization-level; Mission/business process-level; System-level] audit and accountability policy that:

(a) Addresses purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, coordination among organizational entities, and compliance; and

(b) Is consistent with applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines; and

2. Procedures to facilitate the implementation of the audit and accountability policy and the associated audit and accountability controls;

b. Designate an [Assignment: organization-defined official] to manage the development, documentation, and dissemination of the audit and accountability policy and procedures; and

c. Review and update the current audit and accountability:

1. Policy [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] and following [Assignment: organization-defined events]; and

2. Procedures [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] and following [Assignment: organization-defined events].

Guidance:
Audit and accountability policy and procedures address the controls in the AU family that are implemented within systems and organizations. The risk management strategy is an important factor in establishing such policies and procedures. Policies and procedures contribute to security and privacy assurance. Therefore, it is important that security and privacy programs collaborate on the development of audit and accountability policy and procedures. Security and privacy program policies and procedures at the organization level are preferable, in general, and may obviate the need for mission- or system-specific policies and procedures. The policy can be included as part of the general security and privacy policy or be represented by multiple policies that reflect the complex nature of organizations. Procedures can be established for security and privacy programs, for mission or business processes, and for systems, if needed. Procedures describe how the policies or controls are implemented and can be directed at the individual or role that is the object of the procedure. Procedures can be documented in system security and privacy plans or in one or more separate documents. Events that may precipitate an update to audit and accountability policy and procedures include assessment or audit findings, security incidents or breaches, or changes in applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines. Simply restating controls does not constitute an organizational policy or procedure.

title: TRAINING FEEDBACK
methodology:
AT-6 TRAINING FEEDBACK – METHODOLOGY

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Look for regularly generated training feedback reports distributed to designated personnel (e.g., senior management, training coordinators). Confirm that these reports address both awareness and role-based training results, including failures.
• Technical Artifacts: Review automated email logs, dashboard snapshots, or integrated training management systems that show recurrent distribution of training performance data. Check system alerts indicating when critical failures occur.
• Operational Patterns: Identify recurring review meetings where training feedback is discussed, and observe changes in training strategies in response to feedback. This operational linkage shows that feedback drives actionable adjustments in the training program.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Faked Compliance: Organizations might produce a single static report and claim ongoing feedback without an actual automated or periodic process. They might rely solely on policy documents or meeting minutes that don’t align with system logs.
• Surface-level Implementations: Relying on generic ‘training completed’ notifications rather than comprehensive feedback that includes performance outcomes, especially for personnel in critical roles.
• Technical Gaps: Missing integration between the training management system and actual feedback dissemination channels; only high-level summaries without correlating incident logs, which reduces accountability.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
• CHECK: Verify that a centralized repository (or dashboard) exists where training feedback data is aggregated and updated at the organization-defined frequency.
  WHY: A dynamic repository indicates an active and systematic feedback process that regularly informs designated personnel.
  HOW: Use automated queries or dashboard snapshots from the training management system to view timestamps and distribution history of feedback reports.
  FAIL: Absence of archived reports, missing timestamps, or evidence that feedback is only produced manually on an ad hoc basis.

• CHECK: Confirm that designated personnel (as defined by the organization) have received the feedback, and that there is documented acknowledgement or follow-up action.
  WHY: Direct evidence of receipt by senior managers or key stakeholders validates that feedback reaches operational decision-makers.
  HOW: Review email logs or confirmed meeting attendance records; interview selected personnel to cross-validate receipt and impact of training feedback.
  FAIL: Lack of corresponding email or meeting logs, or reported disconnect between the feedback generation process and the actual recipient’s awareness.

• CHECK: Assess that both awareness training results and role-based training results are separately and clearly reflected in the feedback materials.
  WHY: Distinguishing between types of training ensures that the feedback is relevant to the personnel’s specific responsibilities and critical risk roles.
  HOW: Analyze sample training feedback reports to ensure detailed sections on awareness training (including completion rates and quiz scores) and role-based training (especially highlighting individuals in critical positions with performance failures).
  FAIL: Reports that merge training data without clear separation or lack specificity in results for critical structured roles.

• CHECK: Look for evidence that training feedback has triggered follow-up actions (e.g., targeted re-training, policy reviews, or managerial interventions).
  WHY: The operational impact of the feedback mechanism validates that it is not only a reporting exercise, but a tool for continuous improvement.
  HOW: Evaluate meeting minutes, incident reports, or corrective action plans that reference training feedback outcomes, particularly highlighting remediation actions for poor-performing critical roles.
  FAIL: Absence of linked follow-up actions or subsequent improvements in training engagement following negative feedback.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Request direct access to live dashboards or recent system logs; real-time data is less likely to be falsified than retrospective static reports or solely paper-based evidence.
• Critical Indicator: Pay attention to feedback frequency consistency. Large gaps between periodic feedback reports suggest superficial implementation.
• Detail Focus: Specificity in the feedback (e.g., test scores, number and type of training lapses in critical roles) is key—generic reports are a common evasion tactic.
• Verification Shortcut: Cross-reference training failure incidents against system alerts; discrepancies are red flags for manipulated feedback data.

This methodology emphasizes reviewing operational effectiveness and observable technical evidence as opposed to relying solely on policy documents or static reports. The focus on automated logs, direct interviews, and impact assessments ensures that the control is not only in place but actively driving training improvements.
content:
Statement:
Provide feedback on organizational training results to the following personnel [Assignment: organization-defined frequency]: [Assignment: organization-defined personnel].

Guidance:
Training feedback includes awareness training results and role-based training results. Training results, especially failures of personnel in critical roles, can be indicative of a potentially serious problem. Therefore, it is important that senior managers are made aware of such situations so that they can take appropriate response actions. Training feedback supports the evaluation and update of organizational training described in AT-2b and AT-3b.

title: CONTACTS WITH SECURITY GROUPS AND ASSOCIATIONS
methodology:
This methodology addresses how to practically validate the implementation of AT-5 (Contacts with Security Groups and Associations), even though it has been withdrawn and incorporated into PM-15. The approach below focuses on demonstrating that the organization actively engages with external security communities and maintains verifiable contacts, rather than simply stating that such contacts exist.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: Look for records showing active membership or participation in recognized security groups or associations. This may include meeting minutes, official communications, branded membership certificates, event attendance records, and affiliation listings on internal intranets or public websites.
   • Technical Artifacts: Evidence may include maintained mailing lists/email groups, calendar invites for recurring security-related meetings, and logs of interactions (such as forum posts or conference call recordings) with these external bodies.
   • Operational Patterns: An organization following operational procedures (possibly detailed in PM-15) will have documented feedback loops with these associations. Look for scheduled briefings, action items derived from group interactions, and periodic reviews of collective security intelligence within the incident response or governance processes.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Faking Compliance: Organizations might create a superficial list of external contacts or upload static snapshots of membership without showing actual ongoing interactions.
   • Surface-level Implementations: A checklist or a one-time registration event is not sufficient; lacking evidence of recent or recurring participation (e.g., monthly/quarterly meetings) may indicate gaps.
   • Technical Gaps: Failing to integrate contact records with other PM activities (like risk management reviews) or not maintaining updated contact rosters often points to poor actual engagement.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify Evidence of Active Membership
   WHY: Regular participation and current membership in recognized security groups indicates proactive engagement.
   HOW: Review archived meeting minutes, membership certificates, and active contact lists from security associations. Confirm that these records have dates and are periodically updated (e.g., in the last 12 months).
   FAIL: If documentation is outdated or if there is no record of interaction within a recent period, it suggests the control is not effectively implemented.

   CHECK: Inspect Communication Channels
   WHY: Active use of mailing lists, collaborative platforms, or email groups demonstrates that the organization benefits from the shared intelligence of these groups.
   HOW: Identify and review logs from group emails or collaboration platforms (e.g., internal forum posts, email distribution lists) to confirm regular exchanges. Request samples of communications referencing key group discussions.
   FAIL: Static or one-time communications, along with inactive mailing lists, indicate only superficial compliance.

   CHECK: Interview Key Staff
   WHY: Direct interviews provide evidence of how security champions or the designated security liaison engages with external associations.
   HOW: Conduct structured interviews with security officers or designated liaisons to gather verbal or recorded evidence of periodic interaction, impact stories, and how their inputs affect internal processes in PM-15.
   FAIL: Inability of staff to cite recent examples or detail ongoing interactions signals a lack of operational engagement.

   CHECK: Correlate with PM-15 Processes
   WHY: Since AT-5 has been incorporated into PM-15, cross-referencing PM-15’s procedures and outputs confirms integration of external inputs into the overall security program.
   HOW: Review the PM-15 documentation for sections that reference external contacts or exchanges with security associations. Validate that outcomes from these interactions (such as risk advisories, policy updates, or incident intelligence) are evident in the security management cycle.
   FAIL: Absence of references linking external group inputs to PM-15 outputs may indicate that while documentation exists, it might not be effectively implemented.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Verification Shortcuts: Use a sample-based approach; for instance, check the last three months of meeting logs and communication samples, which are harder to fake or have a discrepancy due to actual operational activity.
   • Critical Indicators: Pay close attention to the recency, frequency, and impact of interactions with security groups; sporadic or one-off interactions hint at poor implementation.
   • Details That Matter: Ensure that documented memberships are not mere formality – they should be tied to actionable outcomes (e.g., changes in policy, updated threat intelligence, or coordinated incident responses) that demonstrate the value of these external contacts.

This methodology emphasizes actionable evidence over paper compliance, ensuring that the organization’s engagement with security groups and associations is ongoing and integrated into its broader security management framework.
content:
Statement:
Withdrawn: Incorporated into PM-15.

title: TRAINING RECORDS
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
────────────────────────────────────────
• Existence of a centralized training management system (TMS) that logs security and privacy training events and their attendees.
• Visible records such as completed training certificates, attendance logs, and automated system reports showing training completion dates and participant details.
• Operational practices like routine notifications, automated reminders, and periodic audits of training records that indicate ongoing oversight.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
────────────────────────────────────────
• Organizations might simply store static spreadsheets or word documents claiming training occurred without system timestamps or audit trails.
• Paper logs or unverified manual entries can be used to simulate compliance, yet these records often lack integrity and automated oversight.
• Infrequent, outdated, or unsystematic record-keeping where records exist solely in email threads rather than a validated system.

VALIDATION STEPS
────────────────────────────────────────
CHECK 1: Verify that a dedicated training management system is used to document and monitor security and privacy training events.
WHY: A centralized, automated system provides reliable, time-stamped, and auditable evidence that training activities are being properly tracked.
HOW: Request a live demonstration or obtain a secure screenshot of the TMS showing various training events, role-based training records, and filtering options by dates.
FAIL: Use of static documents (spreadsheets, PDFs) or a lack of digital audit trails indicating unsystematic record governance.

CHECK 2: Inspect training records for individual participants ensuring they include dates, course content, and completion status.
WHY: Detailed records per individual verify that not only scheduling but actual training attendance and completion are implemented correctly.
HOW: Randomly select individual training records from the database and verify against payroll or work records that these individuals received the correct training for their roles.
FAIL: Missing details, inconsistent formats, incomplete records, or mismatches between scheduled training and individual completion logs.

CHECK 3: Validate retention settings to confirm records are maintained for the organization-defined time period, in compliance with regulatory requirements.
WHY: Retention policies ensure historical training data is available for audits and investigations over a defined period.
HOW: Examine the TMS retention policy configuration and cross-check a sample of training records with their archived status to confirm they follow the organization’s retention guidelines.
FAIL: Records are not archived or deleted prematurely, or retention policies are not enforced by the TMS.

CHECK 4: Evaluate automated reporting and alert mechanisms that monitor training status and non-compliance instances.
WHY: Regular reporting and alerts demonstrate proactive operational oversight ensuring that training gaps are identified and addressed quickly.
HOW: Request examples of automated reports (dashboards, alerts) that are generated periodically, and check if they include follow-ups for overdue training or incomplete records.
FAIL: Absence of automated reporting; reliance solely on periodic manual reviews, which can lead to delayed identification of gaps.

EXPERT TIPS
────────────────────────────────────────
• Look for integration between the TMS and other HR or Access Control systems: It’s a strong indicator that training records correlate directly with role changes and system permissions.
• Always cross-reference training dates and completion status with employee records to avoid discrepancies or post-hoc editing of the records.
• Pay attention to the retention policy enforcement – if training records are archived or purged outside of defined timelines, it could indicate procedural weaknesses.
• A live demonstration of system functionality is more reliable than static screenshots or logs provided in response to a request; ensure you request real-time access if possible.

These steps focus on verifying that training records are actively managed, authenticated, and retained according to both policy and operational practice, providing tangible proof of compliance that is difficult to fabricate.
content:
Statement:
a. Document and monitor information security and privacy training activities, including security and privacy awareness training and specific role-based security and privacy training; and
b. Retain individual training records for [Assignment: organization-defined time period].

Guidance:
Documentation for specialized training may be maintained by individual supervisors at the discretion of the organization. The National Archives and Records Administration provides guidance on records retention for federal agencies.

title: ROLE-BASED TRAINING
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for AT-3 Role-Based Training:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Training Records and Artifacts: Observable training logs, certificates, and digital course completion records in the Learning Management System (LMS) that show role-specific training given before system access is authorized and at required reoccurrence intervals.
• Learning Management System (LMS) Integration: Evidence that training modules are assigned automatically to individuals based on defined roles. Screenshots or audit logs from the LMS reflect the assignment, attendance, and completion data.
• Updated Course Content: Visible change logs and annotated training materials that incorporate lessons learned from recent internal or external security incidents, ensuring that training content reflects current operational risks.
• Communication Artifacts: Internal emails, announcements, or dashboard reports that reveal the scheduling and issuance of role-based training, including environmental, physical security, and PII specific modules where applicable.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Superficial Documentation: Organizations may create generic training documentation or a checklist without ensuring that the content is actually tailored to specific roles or updated to include incident lessons.
• Automated Compliance Fudges: Relying solely on system-generated reports with sign-offs while actual dialogue and comprehension among personnel remain untested.
• Incomplete Integration: Systems that assign training based on roles but fail to update training content in response to evolving threats or post-incident lessons are usually missing the mark on true compliance.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Confirm that training records contain role-specific identifiers and timing information.
WHY: It demonstrates that training is being administered before system access and periodically thereafter as required by policy.
HOW: Review LMS audit logs, training certificates, or email confirmations for a sample set of users across different roles; verify dates (initial training and refreshers) are logged and match the policy schedule.
FAIL: If training records are generic, missing timing details, or lack role identifiers, compliance is questionable.

CHECK: Assess training content to verify inclusion of role-specific material, including lessons learned from recent security incidents.
WHY: This proves that the content is not generic and dynamically incorporates incident insights and role-specific risks.
HOW: Review a sample of training modules for different roles and inspect change logs or annotated revision history. Interview personnel to confirm they recall recent incident-informed changes.
FAIL: If the training material is outdated, generic, or does not reference recent incident findings, the control may be superficially implemented.

CHECK: Validate automation - ensure role definitions in the Identity Management system are linked to automated training assignments and reminders.
WHY: Automated evidence shows these processes are integrated and continuously monitored.
HOW: Examine system configuration settings, integration logs between the HR/Identity system and the LMS to confirm that role-based training actions (assignment, notifications, reminders) occur as per the policy.
FAIL: Discrepancies between role definitions and training assignments (e.g., roles missing training requirements) indicate integration failures.

CHECK: Verify periodic training updates and refresh events occur at organization-defined intervals and after significant system changes.
WHY: This indicates ongoing operational effectiveness rather than a one-off activity.
HOW: Look at the training schedule/calendar within the LMS. Check incident response logs and follow-up training notifications post-system upgrades or incidents. Cross-check with personnel interviews to validate awareness of such sessions.
FAIL: Absence of such schedule entries or missing evidence of training refresh after systemic changes shows poor implementation.

CHECK: Collect and review feedback or assessment results from individuals who received role-based training.
WHY: Feedback and testing results provide operational insights into whether the training is effective and comprehensible.
HOW: Analyze post-training quiz scores, surveys, or follow-up interviews for key roles to assess understanding and practical application of the training material. Automated survey tools or LMS assessment tools are key sources.
FAIL: Uniformly low assessment scores or no available feedback indicate that even though the training might be recorded as completed, its effectiveness is doubtful.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Ask targeted questions during interviews with personnel at various levels to quickly gauge whether they understand the key concepts of role-specific threats they face. Good indicators are detailed, specific answers as opposed to generic responses.
• Key Detail: Check the revision history of training modules for evidence that recent security incidents have been integrated. This is a critical indicator of proactive risk management.
• Tip: Utilize LMS dashboard analytics to review completion rates, refresh intervals, and rescheduled training for non-completers. High numbers of overdue or incomplete training flags could indicate process failures.
• Critical Indicator: Ensure that the technical integration (e.g., via APIs between HR systems and LMS) actually enforces the training policy. Manual overrides or disconnected workflows are common pitfalls.

This methodology ensures that the control is practiced rather than merely documented, emphasizing automation and observable evidence to validate operational effectiveness.
content:
Statement:
a. Provide role-based security and privacy training to personnel with the following roles and responsibilities: [Assignment: organization-defined roles and responsibilities]: 1. Before authorizing access to the system, information, or performing assigned duties, and [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] thereafter; and 2. When required by system changes; b. Provide role-based training content [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] and following [Assignment: organization-defined events]; and c. Incorporate lessons learned from internal or external security incidents or breaches into role-based training.

Guidance:
Organizations determine the content of training based on the assigned roles and responsibilities of individuals as well as the security and privacy requirements of organizations and the systems to which personnel have authorized access, including technical training specifically tailored for assigned duties. Roles that may require role-based training include senior leaders or management officials (e.g., head of agency/chief executive officer, chief information officer, senior accountable official for risk management, senior agency information security officer, senior agency official for privacy), system owners; authorizing officials; system security officers; privacy officers; acquisition and procurement officials; enterprise architects; systems engineers; software developers; systems security engineers; privacy engineers; system, network, and database administrators; auditors; personnel conducting configuration management activities; personnel performing verification and validation activities; personnel with access to system-level software; control assessors; personnel with contingency planning and incident response duties; personnel with privacy management responsibilities; and personnel with access to personally identifiable information. Comprehensive role-based training addresses management, operational, and technical roles and responsibilities covering physical, personnel, and technical controls roles. Role-based training also includes policies, procedures, tools, methods, and artifacts for the security and privacy roles defined. Organizations provide the training necessary for individuals to fulfill their responsibilities related to operations and supply chain risk management within the context of organizational security and privacy programs. Role-based training also applies to contractors who provide services to federal agencies. Types of training include web-based and computer-based training, classroom-style training, and hands-on training (including micro-training). Updating role-based training...

Enhancements:
(1) ROLE-BASED TRAINING | ENVIRONMENTAL CONTROLS Provide [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles] with initial and [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] training in the employment and operation of environmental controls. Discussion: Environmental controls include fire suppression and detection devices or systems, sprinkler systems, handheld fire extinguishers, fixed fire hoses, smoke detectors, temperature or humidity, heating, ventilation, air conditioning, and power within the facility. Related Controls: PE-1, PE-11, PE-13, PE-14, PE-15.
(2) ROLE-BASED TRAINING | PHYSICAL SECURITY CONTROLS Provide [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles] with initial and [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] training in the employment and operation of physical security controls. Discussion: Physical security controls include physical access control devices, physical intrusion and detection alarms, operating procedures for facility security guards, and monitoring or surveillance equipment. Related Controls: PE-2, PE-3, PE-4.
(3) ROLE-BASED TRAINING | PRACTICAL EXERCISES Provide practical exercises in security and privacy training that reinforce training objectives. Discussion: Practical exercises for security include training for software developers that addresses simulated attacks that exploit common software vulnerabilities or spear or whale phishing attacks targeted at senior leaders or executives. Practical exercises for privacy include modules with quizzes on identifying and processing personally identifiable information in various scenarios or scenarios on conducting privacy impact assessments. Related Controls: None.
(4) ROLE-BASED TRAINING | SUSPICIOUS COMMUNICATIONS AND ANOMALOUS SYSTEM BEHAVIOR [Withdrawn: Moved to AT-24].
(5) ROLE-BASED TRAINING | PROCESSING PERSONALLY IDENTIFIABLE INFORMATION Provide [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles] with initial and [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] training in the employment and operation of personally identifiable information processing and transparency controls. Discussion: Personally identifiable information processing and transparency controls include the organization’s authority to process personally identifiable information and personally identifiable information processing purposes. Role-based training for federal agencies addresses the types of information that may constitute personally identifiable information and the risks, considerations, and obligations associated with its processing. Such training also considers the authority to process personally identifiable information documented in privacy policies and notices, system of records notices, computer matching agreements and.

title: LITERACY TRAINING AND AWARENESS
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Evidence of scheduled security and privacy literacy training programs for new and existing users, including managers, contractors, and senior executives.
• Training records, completion certificates, attendance logs, and timestamps indicating periodic updates or refresher sessions, especially in response to system changes or security incidents.
• Artifacts such as updated training materials, simulation exercise records (e.g., social engineering/phishing tests), and change logs that confirm integration of lessons learned from security incidents.
• Observable operational patterns where training outcomes are measured (e.g., quiz/test results, simulated exercise reports) and subsequent improvements are documented.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations may provide outdated training materials or a generic training certificate without evidence of updated content or scenario-based exercises.
• Compliance may be faked by issuing attendance records that do not reflect genuine user engagement or by reusing materials for extended periods without incorporating recent threat intelligence.
• Overreliance on documentation (such as policy documents claiming periodic reviews) without cross-verification against actual training content delivery and incident response improvements.
• Minimal or superficial practical exercises that fail to simulate real-world attack vectors like phishing or insider threat scenarios.

VALIDATION STEPS:
CHECK 1: Verify Training Completion and Update Records
WHY: Valid, up-to-date records demonstrate that training is performed at the defined frequency and updated appropriately after defined events.
HOW: Extract user training logs and certificates from the Learning Management System (LMS); check timestamps and version numbers of training modules; cross-reference with incident logs showing updates.
FAIL: Absence of timestamps, outdated courses, or lack of evidence showing re-training following system changes or incidents.

CHECK 2: Assess Inclusion of Practical Exercises and Simulation Data
WHY: Practical exercises (e.g., simulated phishing or social engineering attempts) are key to assessing user readiness and the operational effectiveness of training.
HOW: Review exercise schedules, simulation reports, and participation logs; verify that results and lessons learned from these exercises are documented and integrated into subsequent training updates.
FAIL: No records of simulation exercises or results, or evidence that exercises are conducted on an ad-hoc basis without systematic integration of lessons learned.

CHECK 3: Review Change Logs and Incident Response Integration in Training Content
WHY: Effective training should evolve with threat landscapes and incorporate lessons from real security incidents and breaches.
HOW: Examine version histories of training content; validate that updates have been made following defined events or internal/external incidents; interview training coordinators for corroboration.
FAIL: Static training content that lacks revision history or does not reflect recent incident learnings.

CHECK 4: Random User Interviews and Knowledge Assessments
WHY: Interviews and spot-checks provide direct insight into user retention and understanding, confirming that training is more than a paper exercise.
HOW: Perform random user interviews or administer short quizzes; compare responses with the training curriculum to assess practical awareness of social engineering, insider threats, and suspicious communications.
FAIL: Users are unable to recall key training points or demonstrate understanding of critical security indicators, suggesting superficial training delivery.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Verification shortcuts include checking LMS audit logs and reviewing automated alerts triggered by simulated phishing exercises to validate training effectiveness.
• Look for clear linkages between training content updates and post-incident review reports—effective programs reflect current threat landscapes.
• Critical indicators of poor implementation include static training materials lacking revision dates, low participation in practical exercises, and inconsistent follow-up actions after incident simulations.
• Do not rely solely on policy documents; always validate against observable technical artifacts and direct evidence from the training systems.
• Ensure that the training records align with organizational-defined frequencies and that the content update process is dynamic and incident-responsive.
content:
Statement:
a. Provide security and privacy literacy training to system users (including managers, senior executives, and contractors):
   1. As part of initial training for new users [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] thereafter; and  
   2. When required by system changes or following [Assignment: organization-defined events];
b. Employ the following techniques to increase the security and privacy awareness of system users [Assignment: organization-defined awareness techniques]; and 
c. Update literacy training and awareness content [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] and following [Assignment: organization-defined events]; and 
d. Incorporate lessons learned from internal or external security incidents or breaches into literacy training and awareness techniques.

Guidance:
Organizations provide basic and advanced levels of literacy training to system users, including measures to test the knowledge level of users. Organizations determine the content of literacy training and awareness based on specific organizational requirements, the systems to which personnel have authorized access, and work environments (e.g., telework). The content helps users to maintain security and personal privacy and to respond to suspected incidents. The content addresses the need for operations security and the handling of personally identifiable information.

Enhancements:
(1) LITERACY TRAINING AND AWARENESS | PRACTICAL EXERCISES  Provide practical exercises in literacy training that simulate events and incidents. Discussion: Practical exercises include no-notice social engineering attempts to collect information, gain unauthorized access, or simulate the adverse impact of opening malicious email attachments or invoking, via spear phishing attacks, malicious web links. Related Controls: CA-2, CA-7, CP-4, IR-3.
(2) LITERACY TRAINING AND AWARENESS | INSIDER THREAT  Provide literacy training on recognizing and reporting potential indicators of insider threat.
(3) LITERACY TRAINING AND AWARENESS | SOCIAL ENGINEERING AND MINING  Provide literacy training on recognizing and reporting potential and actual instances of social engineering and social mining. Discussion: Social engineering is an attempt to trick an individual into revealing information or taking an action that can be used to breach, compromise, or otherwise adversely impact a system. Social engineering includes phishing, pretexting, impersonation, baiting, quid pro quo, thread-jacking, social media exploitation, and tailgating. Social mining is an attempt to gather information about the organization that may be used to support future attacks. Literacy training includes information on how to communicate the concerns of employees and management regarding potential and actual instances of social engineering and mining through organizational channels based on established policies and procedures. Related Controls: PM-12.
(4) LITERACY TRAINING AND AWARENESS | SUSPICIOUS COMMUNICATIONS AND ANOMALOUS SYSTEM BEHAVIOR  Provide literacy training on recognizing suspicious communications and anomalous behavior in organizational systems using [Assignment: organization-defined indicators of malicious code]. Discussion: A well-trained workforce provides another organizational control that can be employed as part of a defense-in-depth strategy to protect against malicious code coming into organizations via email or the web applications. Personnel are trained to look for indications of potentially suspicious email (e.g., receiving an unexpected email, receiving an email containing strange or poor grammar, or receiving an email from an unfamiliar sender that appears to be from a known sponsor or contractor). Personnel are also trained on how to respond to suspicious email or web communications. For this process to work effectively, personnel are trained and made aware of what constitutes suspicious communications. Training personnel on how to recognize anomalous behaviors in systems can provide organizations with early warning for the presence of malicious code. Recognition of anomalous behavior by organizational personnel can supplement malicious code detection and protection tools and systems employed by organizations. Related Controls: None.
(5) LITERACY TRAINING AND AWARENESS | ADVANCED PERSISTENT THREAT  Provide literacy training on the advanced persistent threat. Discussion: An effective way to detect advanced persistent threats (APT) and to preclude successful attacks is to provide specific literacy training for individuals. Threat literacy training includes educating individuals on the various ways that APTs can infiltrate the organization (e.g., through websites, emails, advertisement pop-ups, articles, and social media).
(6) LITERACY TRAINING AND AWARENESS | CYBER THREAT ENVIRONMENT (a) Provide literacy training on the cyber threat environment; and (b) Reflect current cyber threat information in system operations. Discussion: Since threats continue to change over time, threat literacy training by the organization is dynamic. Moreover, threat literacy training is not performed in isolation from the system operations that support organizational mission and business functions. Related Controls: RA-3.

title: POLICY AND PROCEDURES
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Policy Document Availability: There is a formal, accessible, and dated awareness and training policy document that clearly states purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, and compliance requirements. The document is aligned with relevant laws, executive orders, and organizational standards.
• Dissemination Evidence: Evidence that the policy and related procedures have been disseminated to the defined personnel through training platforms, email distributions, or intranet postings. Records of acknowledged receipt by employees bolster compliance.
• Designated Official Identification: A designated official’s name, title, and responsibilities are clearly outlined in the documents; this person actively manages the awareness and training lifecycle. 
• Review and Update Records: Log(s) or audit trails that record periodic reviews and updates triggered by assessments, incidents, or regulatory changes.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Outdated or Paper-only Policies: Organizations may simply document a policy without actively enforcing or updating it. Relying solely on static documentation that has not been reviewed for years is a frequent lapse.
• Superficial Dissemination: Issuing the policy once (or attaching it to a newsletter) without tracking acknowledgement or understanding among intended personnel. This results in policies that are not properly absorbed in day-to-day operations.
• Nominal Designation without Active Management: Naming a policy owner on paper without demonstrating proactive oversight, regular communications, or follow-up actions.
• Lack of Operational Evidence: Having documentation but no operational artifacts (like training logs, audit trails, or incident responses) that validate the practical application of the procedures.

VALIDATION STEPS:
1. CHECK: Review the latest awareness and training policy document and accompanying procedures.
   WHY: A current, formally approved document shows that the organization has defined the purpose, scope, roles, and compliance requirements as required.
   HOW: Request digital copies from the security/HR repository; verify the document’s last review date and alignment with regulatory citations; check that it includes clearly stated organizational roles and responsibilities.
   FAIL: If the document is outdated, missing key components (e.g., scope or roles), or lacks reference to legal and regulatory requirements, compliance fails.

2. CHECK: Verify dissemination mechanisms and training records.
   WHY: Actual dissemination and training records indicate that employees have received and acknowledged the policy and procedures, demonstrating active implementation.
   HOW: Examine training logs, email acknowledgements, intranet postings, or LMS (Learning Management System) records that show when and how personnel were informed. Cross-check a sample of personnel files for training acknowledgements.
   FAIL: If there is no record of dissemination (e.g., missing training logs or acknowledgements) or if the process appears ad hoc, this indicates non-compliance.

3. CHECK: Identify and interview or review the role of the designated official responsible for managing the training policy.
   WHY: A real and active designated official ensures accountability for maintaining, updating, and enforcing policies and procedures.
   HOW: Request documentation delineating the designated official’s responsibilities and check recent emails or meeting minutes that reflect active engagement in awareness and training updates. Optionally, interview the designated official to confirm ongoing responsibilities.
   FAIL: If there is no actionable evidence of the designated official’s ongoing management (e.g., no meeting records, lack of communications), then the control is not effectively implemented.

4. CHECK: Review audit logs or incident response records that triggered policy review actions.
   WHY: Actual events leading to policy updates demonstrate that the review cycle is operational and responds to security incidents, audit findings, or legal changes.
   HOW: Evaluate change logs or review minutes from governance meetings that document periodic policy reviews and updates following relevant events. Verify dates and events triggering changes.
   FAIL: If logs do not exist or if updates appear arbitrary and unsupported by actual triggers, the operational effectiveness of the control is in doubt.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Shortcut through System Logs: Use centralized policy management or Governance, Risk and Compliance (GRC) tools to quickly retrieve change history and policy dissemination trails.
• Spot Check Communication Channels: Randomly sample emails or intranet posts that announce policy changes. Consistent and timely communication is a strong indicator of operational enforcement.
• Cross-Reference Incident Reports: Rapidly check incident reports for mentions of policy reviews following breaches or compliance issues; a lack of such correlation can be a red flag.
