• Focus on Active Engagement: Verify that employees are not merely ‘ticking the box’ by checking for quiz or assessment results post-training which indicate real understanding, not just passive acknowledgment.
• Verify Integration with Broader Programs: Policies that are explicitly tied to broader security and privacy programs and regularly integrated in risk management reviews are more robust than isolated documents.

This methodology prioritizes observable, automated evidence such as system logs, training databases, and change management records over merely having the documents available. A robust compliance program will show not just that policies exist, but that they are actively reviewed, disseminated, and integrated into everyday operations.
content:
Statement:
a. Develop, document, and disseminate to [Assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles]:
   1. [Selection (one or more): Organization-level; Mission/business process-level; System-level] awareness and training policy that:
      (a) Addresses purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, coordination among organizational entities, and compliance; and
      (b) Is consistent with applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines; and
   2. Procedures to facilitate the implementation of the awareness and training policy and the associated awareness and training controls;
b. Designate an [Assignment: organization-defined official] to manage the development, documentation, and dissemination of the awareness and training policy and procedures; and
c. Review and update the current awareness and training:
   1. Policy [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] and following [Assignment: organization-defined events]; and
   2. Procedures [Assignment: organization-defined frequency] and following [Assignment: organization-defined events].

Guidance:
Awareness and training policy and procedures address the controls in the AT family that are implemented within systems and organizations. The risk management strategy is an important factor in establishing such policies and procedures. Policies and procedures contribute to security and privacy assurance. Therefore, it is important that security and privacy programs collaborate on the development of awareness and training policy and procedures. Security and privacy program policies and procedures at the organization level are preferable, in general, and may obviate the need for mission- or system-specific policies and procedures. The policy can be included as part of the general security and privacy policy or be represented by multiple policies that reflect the complex nature of organizations. Procedures can be established for security and privacy programs, for mission or business processes, and for systems, if needed. Procedures describe how the policies or controls are implemented and can be directed at the individual or role that is the object of the procedure. Procedures can be documented in system security and privacy plans or in one or more separate documents. Events that may precipitate an update to awareness and training policy and procedures include assessment or audit findings, security incidents or breaches, or changes in applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines. Simply restating controls does not constitute an organizational policy or procedure.

title: REFERENCE MONITOR
methodology:
1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   - CHECK: Verify that the core access control mechanism is implemented as a distinct, isolated module (the reference monitor) that cannot be bypassed by other parts of the system.
     WHY: A clearly defined, encapsulated module limits the attack surface and ensures that all access decisions funnel through a tamperproof mechanism.
     HOW: Review system architecture diagrams and source code annotations to locate the reference monitor; check for digital signatures or cryptographic integrity checks on the binary; confirm that all access control calls pass through this module.
     FAIL: Evidence that access control decisions are being made in multiple locations or that portions of the code can be altered without detection.

2. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   - CHECK: Confirm that the reference monitor is configured to be always invoked during any access control decision.
     WHY: If the monitor isn’t invoked on every access request, gaps in policy enforcement can be exploited.
     HOW: Execute tests that simulate both typical and edge-case access requests while monitoring system logs and audit trails to ensure the reference monitor processes every request; review kernel or operating system audit configurations.
     FAIL: Discovery of any access requests that bypass the reference monitor (e.g., missing audit logs, direct system calls not routed through the control module).

3. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   - CHECK: Determine that the reference monitor is small enough to be completely analyzed and tested for vulnerabilities.
     WHY: A smaller, well-defined module is easier to thoroughly review, ensuring that no latent flaws or hidden backdoors exist.
     HOW: Inspect code metrics (such as module size, cyclomatic complexity, lines of code) and review independent analysis reports or test suites focused on the reference monitor; verify that any changes to this module require re-validation through signed releases or formal review processes.
     FAIL: If the module is overly large, intertwined with other system logic, or lacks independent test coverage and integrity checks.

4. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   - CHECK: Examine the operational logs and security event data to verify that all access decisions are being logged and that anomalous operations (e.g., tampering attempts) are detected.
     WHY: Consistent logging and monitoring provide operational evidence that the system’s reference monitor is being enforced and is operating as intended.
     HOW: Use automated log analysis tools or SIEM dashboards to verify that no access events are missing from the logs under normal and stress-test conditions; correlate access logs with expected reference monitor operations.
     FAIL: Identification of unlogged access attempts, missing audit trails, or evidence of log tampering.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   - Organizations sometimes claim compliance by simply referencing a policy or by having documentation that states access controls are in place, without showing proof that all access related to subjects and objects flows through a tamperproof module.
   - Surface-level implementations may include auxiliary access checks that only work in non-production environments or can be bypassed by low-level system calls; reliance solely on SIEM-generated reports without ensuring the integrity of the underlying control mechanism is insufficient.
   - Technical gaps often include lack of rigorous testing (especially for tamper resistance) and inadequate separation of the reference monitor from user-accessible code.

EXPERT TIPS
   - Shortcut Verification: Directly initiate a set of controlled access attempts (both permitted and prohibited actions) and monitor system responses and audit logs for signs that the reference monitor is processing all decisions.
   - Critical Indicator: Watch for any evidence of duplicate or parallel access control checks – this might signal an incomplete or fragmented reference monitor implementation.
   - Key Detail: Confirm that integrity mechanisms (e.g., cryptographic hashes, secure boot measures) are implemented on the reference monitor module to guard against tampering.
   - Note: Emphasize the importance of automated testing and continuous monitoring to detect any deviations from the tamperproof, always-invoked enforcement model.
content:
Statement:
Implement a reference monitor for [Assignment: organization-defined access control policies] that is tamperproof, always invoked, and small enough to be subject to analysis and testing, the completeness of which can be assured.

Guidance:
A reference monitor is a set of design requirements on a reference validation mechanism that, as a key component of an operating system, enforces an access control policy over all subjects and objects. A reference validation mechanism is always invoked, tamper-proof, and small enough to be subject to analysis and tests, the completeness of which can be assured (i.e., verifiable). Information is represented internally within systems using abstractions known as passive. Active entities, also known as subjects, are associated with individuals, devices, or processes acting on behalf of individuals. Passive entities, also known as objects, are associated with data structures, such as records, buffers, communications ports, tables, files, and inter-process pipes. Reference monitors enforce access control policies that restrict access to objects based on the identity of subjects or groups to which the subjects belong. The system enforces the access control policy based on the rules established by the policy. The tamper-proof property of the reference monitor prevents determined adversaries from compromising the functioning of the reference validation mechanism. The always invoked property prevents adversaries from bypassing the mechanism and violating the security policy. The smallness property helps to ensure completeness in the analysis and testing of the mechanism to detect any weaknesses or deficiencies (i.e., latent flaws) that would prevent the enforcement of the security policy.

title: ACCESS CONTROL DECISIONS
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for AC-24 (ACCESS CONTROL DECISIONS):

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: Separate, clearly documented system components for access control decisions and access enforcement. Architecture diagrams and system logs that distinctly record when an access request is evaluated (decision) and when it is enforced.
   • Technical Artifacts: Logs with timestamped records showing authorization decision events separate from enforcement actions; configuration files or audit records revealing use of cryptographic protocols (e.g., TLS) for transmitting authorization tokens; policy configurations that indicate attribute-based decisions (with or without user identity) as per design requirements.
   • Operational Patterns: Consistent audit trails where every access request triggers an authorization decision event and its subsequent enforcement. Regular network monitoring alerts for anomalous or insecure transmissions of access authorization data.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Faked Compliance: Organizations might simply update policy documentation without implementing a separate mechanism that logs and distinguishes decision versus enforcement events. They may also use the same module for both decision and enforcement without maintaining clear separation.
   • Surface-Level Implementations: Relying solely on built-in system authentication logs without scrutinizing if these logs include independent authorization decision events; or using weak cryptographic configurations that technically “transmit” data but are easily intercepted.
   • Technical Gaps: Omitting checks for secure transmission of authorization tokens and failing to validate that decisions are based on the correct security or privacy attributes (and not inadvertently exposing identities when privacy is required).

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   • CHECK: Verify that system logs clearly document both access control decision events and subsequent enforcement actions.
     WHY: Clear log separation confirms that each access request is being evaluated (decision) prior to enforcement, showing operational separation.
     HOW: Review audit logs and system event records; verify that timestamps for decision events precede enforcement events and that decision logs include authorization attributes (and not merely user identity unless appropriate).
     FAIL: If logs only show a combined record without clear distinction between decision-making and enforcement or are missing for certain access requests.

   • CHECK: Confirm that authorization tokens or access control metadata are transmitted securely (e.g., via TLS or other cryptographic mechanisms) between decision-making and enforcement components.
     WHY: Secure transmission prevents tampering, spoofing, or compromise during the transfer of access authorization information.
     HOW: Inspect network configurations, perform packet captures (where permitted) to verify encryption, and review system configuration files for encryption standards in use.
     FAIL: Evidence of unencrypted transmissions, use of outdated or weak cryptographic protocols, or misconfigurations that expose tokens.

   • CHECK: Evaluate that the system supports attribute-based access control decisions that meet organizational definitions (e.g., decisions based solely on specified security or privacy attributes when required).
     WHY: Validates that the implementation honors privacy requirements and does not inadvertently rely solely on user or process identity.
     HOW: Examine configuration files, and test scenarios where access requests include only attribute data (masking user identity) to ensure the system still makes correct authorization decisions.
     FAIL: If logs or system behavior reveal reliance on user identity when policy mandates use of other attributes or if the decision component fails without user identity.

   • CHECK: Validate separation (where defined) between the components making the authorization decisions and those enforcing them in distributed or layered architectures.
     WHY: Separation minimizes risk by ensuring one compromised component cannot override the other, and it verifies a robust, distributed access control model.
     HOW: Review system architecture diagrams, configuration management databases (CMDB), and perform cross-component log correlation to ensure decision and enforcement actions originate from distinct interfaces or modules.
     FAIL: If the same system component is used for both functions without proper delineation, or if there is no independent evidence that decisions were made prior to enforcement.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut Verification: Start by reviewing correlation logs that detail the time-sequence of decision and enforcement. They are less likely to be manipulated than high-level policy documents.
   • Critical Indicator: Lack of separate decision logs or the presence of combined logs typically indicates a superficial implementation where decision logic is embedded in enforcement modules.
   • Detail Matters: Ensure that cryptographic mechanisms used for transmitting authorization tokens are current and properly configured. A system relying on legacy encryption or misconfigured protocols constitutes a high-risk gap.
   • Advanced Check: In distributed systems, simulate access requests across different nodes to verify that the authorization decision logic is correctly invoked and that tokens cannot be intercepted by unauthorized components.

This methodology emphasizes automated/observable evidence over mere documentation and ensures that operational effectiveness is confirmed through direct checks that address common evasion tactics.
content:
Statement:
[Selection: Establish procedures; Implement mechanisms] to ensure [Assignment: organization-defined access control decisions] are applied to each access request prior to access enforcement.

Guidance:
Access control decisions (also known as authorization decisions) occur when authorization information is applied to specific accesses. In contrast, access enforcement occurs when systems enforce access control decisions. While it is common to have access control decisions and access enforcement implemented by the same entity, it is not required, and it is not always an optimal implementation choice. For some architectures and distributed systems, different entities may make access control decisions and enforce access.

Enhancements:
ACCESS CONTROL DECISIONS | TRANSMIT ACCESS AUTHORIZATION INFORMATION: Transmit [Assignment: organization-defined access authorization information] using [Assignment: organization-defined controls] to [Assignment: organization-defined systems] that enforce access control decisions. Discussion: Authorization processes and access control decisions may occur in separate parts of systems or in separate systems. In such instances, authorization information is transmitted securely (e.g., using cryptographic mechanisms) so that timely access control decisions can be enforced at the appropriate locations. To support the access control decisions, it may be necessary to transmit as part of the access authorization information supporting security and privacy attributes. This is because in distributed systems, there are various access control decisions that need to be made, and different entities make these decisions in a serial fashion, each requiring those attributes to make the decisions. Protecting access authorization information ensures that such information cannot be altered, spoofed, or compromised during transmission.
ACCESS CONTROL DECISIONS | NO USER OR PROCESS IDENTITY: Enforce access control decisions based on [Assignment: organization-defined security or privacy attributes] that do not include the identity of the user or process acting on behalf of the user. Discussion: In certain situations, it is important that access control decisions can be made without information regarding the identity of the users issuing the requests. These are generally instances where preserving individual privacy is of paramount importance. In other situations, it may not be acceptable to make access control decisions without knowing the identity of the user.

title: DATA MINING PROTECTION
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Evidence of automated query rate limiting and anomaly detection: Look for system configurations that limit high frequency or atypical database queries. Also, examine if alerts or logs consistently record triggering events.
• Technical artifacts in database management systems: Configured thresholds on query frequency, logs showing alerts when query patterns exceed expected parameters, and evidence of applied data masking or differential privacy techniques.
• Operational patterns: Regular review of alert logs by staff, change management records reflecting updates to detection rules, and integration between database systems and SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) modules that monitor query behavior.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations might simply post policy statements without technical enforcement: Relying on documentation, or poorly configured threshold settings that do not adapt to actual query volumes.
• Faked compliance by manually overriding limits in testing environments, or simply enabling logging without active review or analysis of the data.
• Implementation that only covers certain endpoints or query patterns while leaving other possible data mining vectors unchecked (for instance, ignoring indirect aggregation queries).

VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Verify automated query rate limiting configurations across critical databases.
WHY: This confirms an active technical measure to increase the work factor for unauthorized data mining attempts.
HOW: Access database configuration settings and query logs; validate if a threshold for the number/frequency of queries has been set that triggers alerts or sessions termination.
FAIL: No limits set or thresholds far exceed normal query rates, indicating potential exploitation risks.

CHECK: Inspect SIEM or anomaly detection logs for alerts associated with atypical database query patterns.
WHY: Capturing anomalous behavior is essential to detect potential data mining in real time.
HOW: Correlate database access logs with SIEM alerts, and examine if unusual patterns (e.g., large volumes of requests from a single user or IP) are being flagged.
FAIL: Absence of corresponding SIEM entries or manually reset alerts without follow-up investigation.

CHECK: Evaluate implementation of advanced methods like differential privacy or homomorphic encryption where appropriate.
WHY: These methods mitigate the risk of re-identification and unauthorized extraction of sensitive data from mining processes.
HOW: Request technical architecture diagrams and system configurations that describe how these techniques are applied to sensitive fields or records, and test if adjustments in query parameters yield non-linear response variations typical of these techniques.
FAIL: The system returns precise answers or shows linear relationships in query results, indicating these advanced defenses are not active.

CHECK: Confirm documentation and change management records align with periodic reviews of query behavior controls.
WHY: This ensures continuous improvement and validation of the technical controls.
HOW: Review records for regular audits, updates in database settings, and incident responses tied to query anomaly detection; interviews with operational staff can also validate process maturity.
FAIL: Outdated records, infrequent reviews, or lack of evidence that alerts were ever actioned.

EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Directly query the SIEM dashboard for recent alerts related to database queries—if no alerts exist over a long period, verify if the thresholds are too high or logging is ineffective.
• Critical indicator: Watch for manual log overrides or tampered audit trails—these are red flags that controls might be faked or ineffectively monitored.
• Note: Focus on dynamic behavior monitoring (real-time or near-real-time) rather than solely on static configurations; operational effectiveness is measured by how the system responds to unexpected query patterns and anomalies.
content:
Statement:
Employ [Assignment: organization-defined data mining prevention and detection techniques] for [Assignment: organization-defined data storage objects] to detect and protect against unauthorized data mining.

Guidance:
Data mining is an analytical process that attempts to find correlations or patterns in large data sets for the purpose of data or knowledge discovery. Data storage objects include database records and database fields. Sensitive information can be extracted from data mining operations. When information is personally identifiable information, it may lead to unanticipated revelations about individuals and give rise to privacy risks. Prior to performing data mining activities, organizations determine whether such activities are authorized. Organizations may be subject to applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, or policies that address data mining requirements. Organizational personnel consult with the senior agency official for privacy and legal counsel regarding such requirements. Data mining prevention and detection techniques include limiting the number and frequency of database queries to increase the work factor needed to determine the contents of databases, limiting types of responses provided to database queries, applying differential privacy techniques or homomorphic encryption, and notifying personnel when atypical database queries or accesses occur. Data mining protection focuses on protecting information from data mining while such information resides in organizational data stores. In contrast, AU-13 focuses on monitoring for organizational information that may have been mined or otherwise obtained from data stores.

title: PUBLICLY ACCESSIBLE CONTENT
methodology:
Control AC-22: PUBLICLY ACCESSIBLE CONTENT

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• Designated Roles: There should be a clearly published list or role definition (within internal directories or access control systems) that identifies the individuals responsible for managing publicly accessible content.
• Training Records: Evidence of periodic training sessions (training logs, course completion certificates) for individuals regarding what information is considered public versus nonpublic.
• Pre-Publication Review: Workflow logs that show content approval and review process steps before publication, including annotations or digital sign-offs.
• Periodic Audits: Automated monitoring or manual audit reports that verify that posted public content does not contain nonpublic data.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Sole Reliance on Policies: Organizations may create policy documents without enforcing them operationally; training bits might exist only on paper, not in actual training systems.
• Insufficient Content Reviews: Some organizations use a checklist approach that touches only on obvious items while missing subtler nonpublic identifiers (metadata, comments in code, hidden files).
• Inadequate Access Controls: Relying on front-facing filters that only check for names or keywords, which can be easily bypassed if content uses synonyms or obfuscation.

3. VALIDATION STEPS:
CHECK: Designated Content Manager List
WHY: Validates that only authorized personnel can manage public information.
HOW: Verify directories, role definitions, or AD group memberships that list individuals with content posting privileges; interview managers to confirm knowledge of responsibilities.
FAIL: Absence of a clearly maintained list, or roles that are not monitored, indicates potential unauthorized access risk.

CHECK: Training Program Documentation and Participation Logs
WHY: Ensures that individuals are well educated on what constitutes nonpublic data and best practices in removal prior to posting.
HOW: Review training logs, certificates, and training materials, and spot-check user accounts for recent completions; if available, check learning management system reports.
FAIL: Training records missing or incomplete records that do not cover the full scope of publicly accessible content guidelines.

CHECK: Automated Review Workflow Evidence
WHY: Demonstrates that a formal approval process is in place which minimizes the likelihood of nonpublic information being posted.
HOW: Inspect automated logs or ticketing systems showing pre-publication reviews; review system configurations for content management workflows.
FAIL: Lack of audit trails or evidence of bypassed workflows indicates ad-hoc posting practices.

CHECK: Periodic Content Review Audits
WHY: Ensures ongoing operational monitoring of public content to catch accidental disclosures of nonpublic information.
HOW: Review scheduled audit reports; check sample public pages for metadata or hidden nonpublic markers, and confirm remediations were applied on discoveries.
FAIL: Missing or outdated audit reports, or evidence that nonpublic information was not removed following audits, signals an ineffective review process.

CHECK: Incident Response and Remediation Records
WHY: Confirms that discovered issues or breaches in public content review are being effectively tracked and corrected.
HOW: Look for logged incidents or remediation tickets in incident management tools and verify that they were resolved within the prescribed timeframe.
FAIL: Incidents lacking follow-up evidence or unresolved tickets can indicate poor operational management.

4. EXPERT TIPS:
• Shortcut Tip: Check system access and change logs that show when content was modified and which account was used. Monitor for unusual activity patterns that could signal unauthorized postings.
• Key Indicator: Repeated instances of bypass or delayed remediation in audit logs often point to insufficient training or an ineffective review process.
• Critical Detail: Ensure that automated processes capture metadata—it often hides nonpublic information like comments, revision histories, or hidden files that are not easily visible in a UI.
• Verification Shortcut: Use search tools across public content domains to detect sensitive keywords; this can provide a quick red flag to deeper manual investigation.
• Attention Focus: Controls must be dynamically updated to account for new types of sensitive data (e.g., financial, medical) that might leak via public channels.

This comprehensive validation methodology emphasizes operational effectiveness, arguing for concrete evidence in workflow logs, training records, and automated review processes which cannot be easily faked compared to mere policy possession. Continual improvement through real-time audits and feedback remains important to detect and respond to evasions rapidly.
content:
Statement:
a. Designate individuals authorized to make information publicly accessible;  
b. Train authorized individuals to ensure that publicly accessible information does not contain nonpublic information;  
c. Review the proposed content of information prior to posting onto the publicly accessible system to ensure that nonpublic information is not included; and  
d. Review the content on the publicly accessible system for nonpublic information
[Assignment: organization-defined frequency] and remove such information, if discovered.

Guidance:
In accordance with applicable laws, executive orders, directives, policies, regulations, standards, and guidelines, the public is not authorized to have access to nonpublic information, including information protected under the [PRIVACT] and proprietary information. Publicly accessible content addresses systems that are controlled by the organization and accessible to the public, typically without identification or authentication. Posting information on non-organizational systems (e.g., non-organizational public websites, forums, and social media) is covered by organizational policy. While organizations may have individuals who are responsible for developing and implementing policies about the information that can be made publicly accessible, publicly accessible content addresses the management of the individuals who make such information publicly accessible.

title: INFORMATION SHARING
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION:
• CHECK: Verify that the system provides an interface or dashboard where users can view the access restrictions applied to sensitive information and the corresponding access authorizations granted to each sharing partner.
  WHY: This confirms that authorized users have real-time visibility into access relationships and can validate that sharing decisions adhere to prescribed restrictions.
  HOW: Inspect the user interface screens and audit trails where information sharing activities are logged. Check for contextual help or decision-support prompts that indicate access authorizations and restrictions.
  FAIL: Absence of any interface elements or logs that enable users to verify partner access details, or if only static policy documents are provided without real-time data.

• CHECK: Identify automated decision-support mechanisms that assist users in determining if sharing a piece of information complies with the defined restrictions.
  WHY: Automated checks reduce human error and ensure that sharing decisions are enforced consistently.
  HOW: Review system configurations for enabled automated tools. Validate these by triggering information-sharing events and confirming that decision support (e.g., warnings, approvals, or block messages) is enforced via logs or system notifications.
  FAIL: Manual processes that rely solely on user discretion without technical enforcement, or system responses that are inconsistent with policy (e.g., missing alerts when sharing sensitive content).

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES:
• Organizations may simulate compliance by maintaining policy documents or test interfaces that show decision support, while in production the mechanisms might be bypassed or disabled due to legacy integrations.
• Some systems might log only a subset of sharing events or might aggregate logs in a way that obscures individual decision support verifications, giving a false impression of control activity.
• Superficial integrations may appear to enforce restrictions; however, if the authorization data is not updated in real time or if the decision-support tool is not properly integrated with the information repository, the control is effectively ineffective.

VALIDATION STEPS:
1. CHECK: Inspect the real-time user interface where sharing decisions are made.
   WHY: Direct observation of the control in action confirms that users can see both the allowed access levels and the associated restrictions.
   HOW: Log in as an authorized user and initiate a sharing transaction. Observe if the interface displays current access authorizations, restrictions, and if an automated decision prompt is shown.
   FAIL: If the interface does not clearly display partner access details or lacks decision prompts, indicating potential bypassed enforcement.

2. CHECK: Review system and audit logs for information sharing events.
   WHY: Logs provide hard evidence that the automated mechanisms or manual processes are actively supporting sharing decisions.
   HOW: Query logs for events related to information sharing decisions. Look for entries that detail the user’s decision process, including alerts fired or decisions taken based on current access authorizations.
   FAIL: Lack of detailed logging or log entries that do not correlate with user interface activity suggests the control may not be functioning as intended.

3. CHECK: Evaluate integration between the information repository and the access control systems.
   WHY: Effective integration ensures that both access restrictions and partner authorizations are current and automatically enforced during sharing activities.
   HOW: Review system documentation and configuration files, then verify that real-time updates from the access control system influence the sharing decisions. Test a scenario where access authorizations change and confirm that the changes are reflected immediately in the sharing decision interface.
   FAIL: Data lag or discrepancies between systems, which indicates a potential gap that could allow outdated or incorrect access decisions.

4. CHECK: If automated decision support enhancements are enabled, test the automated enforcement by simulating borderline cases (e.g., sharing content with mixed classifications).
   WHY: This confirms that not only is the decision support visible, but it actively enforces appropriate restrictions.
   HOW: Perform test scenarios where a user attempts to share information that overlaps multiple security categories. Verify that the automated mechanism alerts, blocks, or prompts for escalation as defined by policy.
   FAIL: If the system permits sharing without triggering the expected automated responses, the control is likely being bypassed.

EXPERT TIPS:
• Use live testing rather than relying solely on static configurations or documentation. Real-world scenarios expose integration issues and delays.
• Pay particular attention to the timeliness of data updates between the access control repository and the sharing mechanism. Delays can result in inaccurate sharing decisions.
• Verify that audit logs include both digital signatures (or equivalent evidence of user action) and timestamps that correlate directly with the decision support evidence. Any gaps may indicate a superficial implementation that can be easily faked.
• Shortcuts include using automated scripts to simulate sharing events and correlate UI changes with backend log entries – discrepancies here are strong indicators of poor implementation.
content:
Statement:
a. Enable authorized users to determine whether access authorizations assigned to a sharing partner match the information’s access and use restrictions for [Assignment: organization-defined information sharing circumstances where user discretion is required]; and

b. Employ [Assignment: organization-defined automated mechanisms or manual processes] to assist users in making information sharing and collaboration decisions.

Guidance:
Information sharing applies to information that may be restricted in some manner based on some formal or administrative determination. Examples of such information include, contract-sensitive information, classified information related to special access programs or compartments, privileged information, proprietary information, and personally identifiable information. Security and privacy risk assessments as well as applicable laws, regulations, and policies can provide useful inputs to these determinations. Depending on the circumstances, sharing partners may be defined at the individual, group, or organizational level. Information may be defined by content, type, security category, or special access program or compartment. Access restrictions may include non-disclosure agreements (NDA). Information flow techniques and security attributes may be used to provide automated assistance to users making sharing and collaboration decisions.

Enhancements:
INFORMATION SHARING | AUTOMATED DECISION SUPPORT: Employ [Assignment: organization-defined automated mechanisms] to enforce information-sharing decisions by authorized users based on access authorizations of sharing partners and access restrictions on information to be shared. Discussion: Automated mechanisms are used to enforce information sharing decisions.
INFORMATION SHARING | INFORMATION SEARCH AND RETRIEVAL: Implement information search and retrieval services that enforce [Assignment: organization-defined information sharing restrictions]. Discussion: Information search and retrieval services identify information system resources relevant to an information need.

title: USE OF EXTERNAL SYSTEMS
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for AC-20 (Use of External Systems) structured into key sections:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: Look for approved external system usage agreements, documented terms and conditions, and clear listings of organization-defined types of external systems that are allowed or prohibited. Physical and digital artifacts such as signed contracts, system configuration screens that enforce restrictions, and displayed access control policies provide evidence.
   • Technical Artifacts: Firewall and VPN configurations restricting external system access; audit logs showing monitoring and enforcement of access; automated alerts on usage of non-authorized external systems; configuration management databases (CMDB) listing approved external systems; and system connection agreements archived for review.
   • Operational Patterns: Regular reviews of external access logs, periodic verification (through vulnerability assessments or independent attestations) that external systems meet policy controls, and enforcement of restrictions (e.g., blocking non-approved personally owned devices) are key operational indicators.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Superficial Compliance: Organizations might only create policies and contractual documents without enforcing or validating controls in practice. Relying solely on documentation rather than operational monitoring is a common evasion.
   • Incomplete Technical Implementation: Configurations might exist in policy or interface screenshots but not be actively monitored. For instance, using remote logging without actual alert thresholds or ignoring regular external system assessments.
   • Gaps in Enforcement: Organizations may have approved connection agreements on file but fail to link them across systems or periodically validate that external systems have maintained necessary controls. In addition, using deprecated log configurations or outdated contractual terms may be used to superficially satisfy compliance.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   • CHECK: Verify that signed external system usage agreements or terms and conditions are in place for every type of external system allowed.
     WHY: These documents confirm that controls and responsibilities are clearly defined per organizational policy and are essential for establishing trust.
     HOW: Review a sample set of contractual agreements and terms documents; cross-reference them with the list of allowed external systems maintained by IT security. Confirm dates of review and evidence of periodic re-validation.
     FAIL: Lack of formal agreements, missing review dates, or agreements that do not align with the current policy scope indicate a failure.

   • CHECK: Examine firewall, VPN, and gateway configurations that enforce external system access restrictions.
     WHY: These configurations are automated and provide concrete evidence that controls are active, and unauthorized systems are being blocked from accessing organizational resources.
     HOW: Audit network device configurations, check access control lists (ACLs) and firewall rule sets, and verify logging data (e.g., attempted accesses from non-authorized IPs or devices).
     FAIL: Incomplete or misconfigured rules, outdated configurations, or missing logs of enforcement denote non-compliance.

   • CHECK: Look at audit logs and monitoring tools to ensure that remote or external system accesses are being tracked and anomalies are flagged.
     WHY: Continuous monitoring is critical to detect unauthorized or non-compliant external accesses, proving that operational effectiveness is active.
     HOW: Gather log data from security information and event management (SIEM) systems for remote access events, correlate with approved agreements, and check for automated alerting mechanisms.
     FAIL: Sparse log evidence, reliance on manual reviews only, or absence of alert triggers signal control failure.

   • CHECK: Validate that periodic independent assessments (e.g., vulnerability scans, external attestation reports) are performed on external systems used to access, process, store, or transmit organization-controlled information.
     WHY: These assessments independently confirm that the external systems meet the security controls required by the organization’s policies, ensuring a higher level of trust.
     HOW: Review assessment schedules, reports, and remediation records to confirm that assessments have covered relevant external systems, with findings tracked to closure.
     FAIL: Missing assessment records, outdated or incomplete reports, or evidence of unresolved high-risk findings indicate poor implementation.

   • CHECK: For systems using organization-controlled portable storage devices, verify that restrictions (technical or procedural) are implemented on external systems.
     WHY: This check confirms that additional layers of control are active to mitigate risks from mobile storage on less-controlled external systems.
     HOW: Review technical configurations on portable storage device management software, examine access logs for external systems, and check deployment of encryption or usage policies.
     FAIL: Inconsistent enforcement, no evidence of technical controls on portable storage device use, or absence of monitoring logs.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut Verification: Focus on automated logs and network configuration artifacts rather than relying solely on policy documents; digital evidence is far less likely to be fabricated post-facto.
   • Critical Indicators: Pay particular attention to periodic (and recent) independent assessment reports and current, live configuration settings—these are harder to fake and demonstrate true operational compliance.
   • Cross-Verification: Randomly sample external system usage data and correlate it with signed agreements; any discrepancies can reveal poor implementation.
   • Detail Focus: Ensure that review dates and periodic re-assessments are current. Old agreement records or outdated configurations are a red flag.
   • Assess Remediation: Look for follow-up actions on failed assessments. A robust implementation shows not only documentation, but active remediation and governance.

This methodology emphasizes verification steps based on automated, observable evidence and operational practices, minimizing the risk of superficial compliance.
content:
Statement:
a. [Selection (one or more): Establish [Assignment: organization-defined terms and conditions]; Identify [Assignment: organization-defined controls asserted to be implemented on external systems]], consistent with the trust relationships established with other organizations owning, operating, and/or maintaining external systems, allowing authorized individuals to:
1. Access the system from external systems; and
2. Process, store, or transmit organization-controlled information using external systems; and

b. Prohibit the use of [Assignment: organizationally-defined types of external systems].

Guidance:
Discussion: External systems are systems that are used by but not part of organizational systems, and for which the organization has no direct control over the implementation of required controls or the assessment of control effectiveness. External systems include personally owned systems, components, or devices; privately owned computing and communications devices in commercial or public facilities; systems owned or controlled by nonfederal organizations; systems managed by contractors; and federal information systems that are not owned by, operated by, or under the direct supervision or authority of the organization. External systems also include systems owned or operated by other components within the same organization and systems within the organization with different authorization boundaries. Organizations have the option to prohibit the use of any type of external system or prohibit the use of specified types of external systems (e.g., prohibit the use of any external system that is not organizationally owned or prohibit the use of personally-owned systems).

For some external systems (i.e., systems operated by other organizations), the trust relationships that have been established between those organizations and the originating organization may be such that no explicit terms and conditions are required. Systems within these organizations may not be considered external. These situations occur when, for example, there are pre-existing information exchange agreements (either implicit or explicit) established between organizations or components or when such agreements are specified by applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, or standards. Authorized individuals include organizational personnel, contractors, or other individuals with authorized access to organizational systems and over which organizations have the authority to impose specific rules of behavior regarding system access. Restrictions that organizations impose on authorized individuals need not be uniform, as the restrictions may vary depending on trust relationships between organizations. Therefore, organizations may choose to impose different security restrictions on contractors than on state, local, or tribal governments.

Enhancements:
USE OF EXTERNAL SYSTEMS | LIMITS ON AUTHORIZED USE
Permit authorized individuals to use an external system to access the system or to process, store, or transmit organization-controlled information only after:
 (a) Verification of the implementation of controls on the external system as specified in the organization’s security and privacy policies and security and privacy plans; or
 (b) Retention of approved system connection or processing agreements with the organizational entity hosting the external system.
Discussion: Limiting authorized use recognizes circumstances where individuals using external systems may need to access organizational systems. Organizations need assurance that the external systems contain the necessary controls so as not to compromise, damage, or otherwise harm organizational systems. Verification that the required controls have been implemented can be achieved by external, independent assessments, attestations, or other means, depending on the confidence level required by organizations.
Related Controls: CA-2.
USE OF EXTERNAL SYSTEMS | PORTABLE STORAGE DEVICES — RESTRICTED USE
Restrict the use of organization-controlled portable storage devices by authorized individuals on external systems using [Assignment: organization-defined restrictions].
Discussion: Limits on the use of organization-controlled portable storage devices in external systems include restrictions on how the devices may be used and under what conditions the devices may be used.
Related Controls: MP-7, SC-41.
USE OF EXTERNAL SYSTEMS | NON-ORGANIZATIONALLY OWNED SYSTEMS — RESTRICTED USE
Restrict the use of non-organizationally owned systems or system components to process, store, or transmit organizational information using [Assignment: organization-defined restrictions].
Discussion: Non-organizationally owned systems or system components include systems or system components owned by other organizations as well as personally owned devices. There are potential risks to using non-organizationally owned systems or components. In some cases, the risk is sufficiently high as to prohibit such use (see AC-20 b.). In other cases, restrictions include requiring the implementation of approved controls prior to authorizing the connection of non-organizationally owned systems and components; limiting access to types of information, services, or applications; using virtualization techniques to limit processing and storage activities to servers or system components provisioned by the organization; and agreeing to the terms and conditions for usage. Organizations consult with the Office of the General Counsel regarding legal issues associated with using personally owned devices, including requirements for conducting forensic analyses during investigations after an incident.
Related Controls: None.
USE OF EXTERNAL SYSTEMS | NETWORK ACCESSIBLE STORAGE DEVICES — PROHIBITED USE

title: ACCESS CONTROL FOR MOBILE DEVICES
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence includes a well-maintained mobile device management (MDM) dashboard showing the enrollment and configuration status of organization-controlled mobile devices. Evidence such as lists of authorized devices, current configuration baselines (e.g., encryption settings, OS patch levels), and network access logs demonstrating compliance when devices are outside controlled areas is critical.
• Technical artifacts: Automatically generated audit logs from the MDM solution, connection authorization records, and configuration files deployed on devices (e.g., profiles enforcing encryption or connectivity restrictions) can serve as strong evidence.
• Operational patterns: Regular remote connection logs, periodic reviews of mobile device status (including random inspections) and automated alerts for non-compliant devices illustrate that the control is active.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations might simply upload static configuration documents or screenshots of an MDM dashboard without providing real-time or historical evidence of enforcement.
• Failing to integrate automated monitoring: Some might show evidence of a policy but lack observable, time-stamped logs or live network access controls, which limits the assurance of operational effectiveness.
• Overreliance on self-attestation from users or manual sign-offs that do not provide independent technical validation often results in gaps that can be exploited.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Verify that all organization-controlled mobile devices have current and enforced configuration profiles (including encryption and connection requirements) deployed by the MDM solution.
   WHY: A valid, automatically deployed configuration confirms that the organization’s baseline settings and connectivity restrictions (especially when outside controlled areas) are enforced.
   HOW: Access the MDM console to review deployed profiles, verify versioning, and check audit logs or real-time reports for active enforcement of policies.
   FAIL: If devices are found without current configuration profiles or if the profiles’ settings (e.g., encryption, allowed wireless interfaces) deviate from the approved baseline.

2. CHECK: Examine network access control logs to confirm that mobile devices are only connecting after proper authorization and using validated secure configurations.
   WHY: Authorization records showing that only pre-verified devices can access network resources demonstrate practical compliance with AC-19.
   HOW: Query the network access management system or firewall logs for records matching the MDM enrolled devices, and cross-check these with a list of authorized devices.
   FAIL: Lack of logs or evidence that unauthorized devices have connected, or reliance on non-automated authorizations (e.g., manual sign-offs with no timestamped records).

3. CHECK: Validate evidence of periodic random reviews, inspections, or automated monitoring of mobile devices (especially in scenarios where devices transition from controlled to uncontrolled environments).
   WHY: Regular operational enforcement (including random audits) reassures that security controls are not just documented but actively enforced.
   HOW: Review the schedule and records from security inspections, MDM-generated compliance reports, and check notification logs for non-compliance alerts.
   FAIL: If there are no scheduled reviews or if records show that compliance inspections are infrequent or missing.

4. CHECK: Cross-check device configuration management logs for any deviations or manual exceptions, and verify that risk mitigation (like full device encryption) is implemented for sensitive mobile devices.
   WHY: Observing a process where exceptions are audited and corrected, alongside encryption enforcement, indicates that mobile devices are actively managed and secured.
   HOW: Retrieve and analyze configuration change logs from the MDM system, review incident reports around non-compliance events, and verify encryption settings on a sample of controlled mobile devices.
   FAIL: Discovery of unencrypted devices or records showing manual overrides or exceptions without proper authorization.

EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Directly query the MDM’s compliance API to obtain real-time status and historical enforcement details, avoiding static report pitfalls.
• Critical indicators of poor implementation include outdated configuration profiles, missing encryption settings, or sporadic audit trail entries, which are strong signals that controls can be bypassed.
• Ensure that any mobile device claiming compliance is cross-verified with independent network logs and that any manual exceptions are rigorously logged and periodically reviewed.
• Look for discrepancies in device inventory lists and active connection logs—such mismatches can reveal unauthorized or non-compliant devices masquerading as compliant.
• Prioritize real-time logs over static documentation: dynamic evidence of policies in effect (especially during off-hours or remote access events) is much harder to fake.
content:
Statement:
a. Establish configuration requirements, connection requirements, and implementation guidance for organization-controlled mobile devices, to include when such devices are outside of controlled areas; and
b. Authorize the connection of mobile devices to organizational systems.

Guidance:
A mobile device is a computing device that has a small form factor such that it can easily be carried by a single individual; is designed to operate without a physical connection; possesses local, non-removable or removable data storage; and includes a self-contained power source. Mobile device functionality may also include voice communication capabilities, on-board sensors that allow the device to capture information, and/or built-in features for synchronizing local data with remote locations. Examples include smart phones and tablets. Mobile devices are typically associated with a single individual. The processing, storage, and transmission capability of the mobile device may be comparable to or merely a subset of notebook/desktop systems, depending on the nature and intended purpose of the device. Protection and control of mobile devices is behavior or policy-based and requires users to take physical action to protect and control such devices when outside of controlled areas. Controlled areas are spaces for which organizations provide physical or procedural controls to meet the requirements established for protecting information and systems.

Enhancements:
ACCESS CONTROL FOR MOBILE DEVICES | USE OF WRITABLE AND PORTABLE STORAGE DEVICES
[Withdrawn: Incorporated into MP-7.]
ACCESS CONTROL FOR MOBILE DEVICES | USE OF PERSONALLY OWNED PORTABLE STORAGE DEVICES
[Withdrawn: Incorporated into MP-7.]
ACCESS CONTROL FOR MOBILE DEVICES | USE OF PORTABLE STORAGE DEVICES WITH NO IDENTIFIABLE OWNER
[Withdrawn: Incorporated into MP-7.]
ACCESS CONTROL FOR MOBILE DEVICES | RESTRICTIONS FOR CLASSIFIED INFORMATION
(a) Prohibit the use of unclassified mobile devices in facilities containing systems processing, storing, or transmitting classified information unless specifically permitted by the authorizing official; and
(b) Enforce the following restrictions on individuals permitted by the authorizing official to use unclassified mobile devices in facilities containing systems processing, storing, or transmitting classified information:
    (1) Connection of unclassified mobile devices to classified systems is prohibited;
    (2) Connection of unclassified mobile devices to unclassified systems requires approval from the authorizing official; and
    (3) Use of internal or external modems or wireless interfaces within the unclassified mobile devices is prohibited; and
    (4) Unclassified mobile devices and the information stored on those devices are subject to random reviews and inspections by [Assignment: organization-defined security officials], and if classified information is found, the incident handling policy is followed.
(c) Restrict the connection of classified mobile devices to classified systems in accordance with [Assignment: organization-defined security policies].
Discussion: None.
ACCESS CONTROL FOR MOBILE DEVICES | FULL DEVICE OR CONTAINER-BASED ENCRYPTION
Employ [Selection: full-device encryption; container-based encryption] to protect the confidentiality and integrity of information on [Assignment: organization-defined mobile devices].
Discussion: Container-based encryption provides a more fine-grained approach to data and information encryption on mobile devices, including encrypting selected data structures such as files, records, or fields.
Related Controls: SC-12, SC-13, SC-28.
References: [SP 800-114], [SP 800-124].

title: WIRELESS ACCESS
methodology:
Below is a detailed validation methodology for AC-18 Wireless Access control.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Observe dedicated wireless access points (APs) deployed in controlled areas with labeling and secured physical enclosures; signage indicating authorized wireless zones; and internal network documentation mapping wireless infrastructures.
• Technical Artifacts: Review configuration files for wireless devices (APs, onboard wireless modules in systems) that show enforced authentication (e.g., 802.1X, WPA2/3) and encryption settings. Examine authorized wireless connection profiles in system management consoles and logs that record connection attempts with MAC addresses, SSIDs, authentication success/failure, and encryption standards.
• Operational Patterns: Continuous monitoring outputs (e.g., SIEM alerts for rogue connections), periodic wireless surveys documenting radio frequency anomalies, and automated discovery scans that list only authorized SSIDs. Verification of change management records for wireless configurations also demonstrates active enforcement.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Fake Compliance by Using Default Configurations: Organizations may deploy wireless devices with out-of-the-box settings that superficially indicate encryption but have weak pre-shared keys or outdated protocols.
• Incomplete Implementation: Declaring policies and having manuals without enforcing them technically; for example, having a documented list of approved devices while actual device configurations remain open.
• Gaps in Monitoring: Relying solely on front-end policies without integrating comprehensive logging, meaning unauthorized changes or rogue APs remain undetected.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK 1: Verify AP Configuration Settings
WHY: Directly inspecting the AP configuration files (or management console) ensures that the required authentication and encryption (e.g., WPA2/3, 802.1X) settings are enabled in operational devices.
HOW: Log in to a sample of wireless devices, inspect configuration screens or files to confirm proper settings, then cross-check against configuration baselines. Use automated configuration scanning tools where available.
FAIL: Default or weak encryption settings, use of deprecated protocols (e.g., WEP) or absence of mutual authentication indicate non-compliance.

CHECK 2: Examine Wireless Connection Logs and Authentication Records
WHY: Verification of live logs confirms that wireless connections required proper authentication and that unauthorized attempts are logged and monitored.
HOW: Extract logs from wireless controllers and SIEM systems, review records showing successful and failed authentication attempts, verify that logs indicate use of approved methods and encryption standards.
FAIL: Sparse, outdated, or missing logs, or logs that show baselines with open or unauthenticated connections, indicate a lack of proper enforcement.

CHECK 3: Review Change Management and Authorization Records
WHY: This check confirms that each wireless access type is pre-authorized before being activated and that any changes follow a controlled process.
HOW: Request evidence of change requests/approvals for any wireless network configurations and review documented authorization lists to ensure all active wireless connections are pre-approved.
FAIL: Absence of change management tickets or authorization evidence, or wireless capabilities enabled without documentation, suggest weak implementation.

CHECK 4: Conduct or Review a Wireless Survey
WHY: A wireless RF survey validates that transmission power levels, antenna configurations, and network perimeters are adjusted to restrict signal leakage beyond intended boundaries.
HOW: Use wireless surveying tools to measure signal strength external to controlled areas. Compare findings with approved physical layouts and configuration guidelines.
FAIL: Excessively strong signal detections outside controlled boundaries or unmonitored antennas indicate a failure in applying mitigative configuration settings.

CHECK 5: Validate User and Device Restriction Settings
WHY: Confirming that only explicitly authorized users or devices can modify wireless configurations proves operational control enforcement.
HOW: Audit system access control configurations, verify the list of users/devices permitted to reconfigure wireless settings via system access reviews, and check for audit logs capturing any unauthorized configuration attempts.
FAIL: Broad or default permission settings that allow any user to configure wireless settings demonstrate a significant implementation deficiency.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Use automated configuration management tools that can scan for wireless settings across your network. These tools can highlight deviations from baselines.
• Key Indicator: Look for any evidence of rogue SSIDs or unmanaged APs. Even if documentation exists, the presence of undocumented devices is a red flag.
• Attention to Detail: Emphasize examining the wireless log data in real time in addition to retrospective reviews; continuous logging is critical.
• Verification Focus: Do not rely solely on policy documents or self-attestations, seek physical configuration evidence and operational logs that are timestamped and detail active enforcement.
• Extra: Investigate if periodic wireless surveys are performed; outdated surveys or non-existent testing indicate possible lapses in controlling the wireless attack surface.

This methodology emphasizes direct, technical, and observable evidence rather than solely relying on documented policies, ensuring that the wireless access controls are not only present on paper but are effectively implemented and operational.
content:
Statement:
a. Establish configuration requirements, connection requirements, and implementation guidance for each type of wireless access; and
b. Authorize each type of wireless access to the system prior to allowing such connections.

Guidance:
Wireless technologies include microwave, packet radio (ultra-high frequency or very high frequency), 802.11x, and Bluetooth. Wireless networks use authentication protocols that provide authenticator protection and mutual authentication.

Enhancements:
WIRELESS ACCESS | AUTHENTICATION AND ENCRYPTION
Protect wireless access to the system using authentication of [Selection (one or more): users; devices] and encryption.
Discussion: Wireless networking capabilities represent a significant potential vulnerability that can be exploited by adversaries. To protect systems with wireless access points, strong authentication of users and devices along with strong encryption can reduce susceptibility to threats by adversaries involving wireless technologies. Related Controls: SC-8, SC-12, SC-13.
WIRELESS ACCESS | MONITORING UNAUTHORIZED CONNECTIONS
[Withdrawn: Incorporated into SI-4.]
WIRELESS ACCESS | DISABLE WIRELESS NETWORKING
Disable, when not intended for use, wireless networking capabilities embedded within system components prior to issuance and deployment.
WIRELESS ACCESS | RESTRICT CONFIGURATIONS BY USERS
Identify and explicitly authorize users allowed to independently configure wireless networking capabilities.
Discussion: Organizational authorizations to allow selected users to configure wireless networking capabilities are enforced, in part, by the access enforcement mechanisms employed within organizational systems. Related Controls: SC-7, SC-15.
WIRELESS ACCESS | ANTENNAS AND TRANSMISSION POWER LEVELS
Select radio antennas and calibrate transmission power levels to reduce the probability that signals from wireless access points can be received outside of organization-controlled boundaries.
Discussion: Actions that may be taken to limit unauthorized use of wireless communications outside of organization-controlled boundaries include reducing the power of wireless transmissions so that the transmissions are less likely to emit a signal that can be captured outside of the physical perimeters of the organization, employing measures such as emissions security to control wireless emanations, and using directional or beamforming antennas that reduce the likelihood that unintended receivers will be able to intercept signals. Prior to taking such mitigating actions, organizations can conduct periodic wireless surveys to understand the radio frequency profile of organizational systems as well as other systems that may be operating in the area. Related Controls: PE-19. References: [SP 800-94], [SP 800-97].

title: REMOTE ACCESS
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Documented remote access policies and configurations exist. Look for formal documents that specify usage restrictions, configuration/connection requirements, and the approved types of remote access (e.g., VPN, dial-up, wireless).
• Active VPN configuration files, firewall rules, and access control lists that reflect remote access settings, including cryptographic settings and connection restrictions.
• Operational evidence such as real-time connection logs, automated alerts, and approved lists of remote users indicating that only authorized remote accesses are in use.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations sometimes produce policy documentation without implementing any technical controls. For example, a policy on paper might state VPN usage requirements but the actual VPN configuration does not enforce encryption or authorized access.
• Superficial controls where remote access is enabled but logging and monitoring are either incomplete or not actively reviewed. In such cases, compliance may be faked by merely having a configuration screen without deeper enforcement.
• Gaps often include outdated configuration files or practices that do not match documented guidelines (e.g., weak encryption algorithms or bypassing approved access points).

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Verify that documented remote access policies align with technical configurations and operational practices.
   WHY: This proves that the policy is more than a document and has been implemented consistently.
   HOW: Review the remote access policy and compare it with VPN/firewall configuration files and network diagrams. Confirm that the documented restrictions and connection requirements (e.g., enforced encryption, approved access points) are applied in device settings.
   FAIL: Discrepancies between documentation and configuration, such as missing encryption settings or allowance of non-authorized remote methods.

2. CHECK: Inspect active VPN and remote access configuration settings.
   WHY: Direct examination of technical artifacts demonstrates that the system enforces specific connection requirements and cryptographic protections.
   HOW: Log into the VPN concentrator and firewall devices to check for encryption configurations, access control lists, and that remote users are channeled through approved network access control points. Validate that only pre-authorized remote access types (e.g., secure VPN tunnels) are enabled.
   FAIL: Finding default or misconfigured settings, use of non-compliant protocols, or remote access methods not backed by authorization.

3. CHECK: Analyze logs and monitoring data for remote access sessions.
   WHY: Operational evidence from logs confirms that remote access is actively monitored, and unauthorized access attempts are detected and responded to.
   HOW: Use automated log analysis tools or SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) to review VPN and firewall logs. Look for records showing authentication attempts, connection durations, encryption algorithms used, and alerts regarding any anomalous remote activity.
   FAIL: Lack of consistent logging data, missing alerts, or logs that do not cover remote access endpoints indicate a gap in operational monitoring.

4. CHECK: Validate that all remote access is pre-authorized and periodically reviewed.
   WHY: Authorization before use is a key requirement. Evidence of regular reviews enhances the credibility of the control.
   HOW: Interview system administrators or review change management records to confirm that remote access methods and users were formally approved before being enabled. Check for periodic reviews in system security plans.
   FAIL: Absence of approval processes for remote access settings or evidence that remote access was enabled without documented authorization.

EXPERT TIPS
• A quick verification shortcut is to request a live demo of the VPN configuration screen – well-managed systems will have clearly configured parameters that match policy requirements.
• Watch for inconsistencies between the documented policy and the actual network device settings – particularly with encryption protocols and approved remote access endpoints.
• Prioritize evidence from automated monitoring and active logs over static documentation. Operational data is hard to fake and gives insight into real-time enforcement.
• Critical indicator of a poor implementation is when remote access logs show unmonitored sessions or a high number of unauthorized access attempts, signaling that the control may be bypassed.
• In practice, ensure that any changes in remote access configurations are accompanied by formal authorization and are verifiable through change management records.
content:
Statement:
a. Establish and document usage restrictions, configuration/connection requirements, and implementation guidance for each type of remote access allowed; and  b. Authorize the type of remote access to the system prior to allowing its use.

Guidance:
Remote access is access to organizational systems (or processes acting on behalf of users) that communicate through external networks such as the Internet. Types of remote access include dial-up, broadband, and wireless. Organizations use encrypted virtual private networks (VPNs) to enhance confidentiality and integrity for remote connections. The use of encrypted VPNs provides sufficient assurance to the organization that it can effectively treat such connections as internal networks if the cryptographic mechanisms used are implemented in accordance with applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines. Still, VPN connections traverse external networks, and the encrypted VPN does not enhance the availability of remote connections. VPNs with encrypted tunnels can also affect the ability to adequately monitor network communications traffic for malicious code. Remote access controls apply to systems other than public web servers or systems designed for public access. Authorization of each remote access type addresses authorization prior to allowing remote access without specifying the specific formats for such authorization. While organizations may use information exchange and system connection security agreements to manage remote access to other systems, such agreements are addressed as part of CA-3. Enforcing access restrictions for remote access is addressed via AC-3.

Enhancements:
REMOTE ACCESS | MONITORING AND CONTROL
Employ automated mechanisms to monitor and control remote access methods.
Discussion: Monitoring and control of remote access methods allows organizations to detect attacks and help ensure compliance with remote access policies by auditing the connection activities of remote users on a variety of system components, including servers,
REMOTE ACCESS | AUTHORIZED ACCESS
Route remote access through authorized and managed network access control points.
Discussion: Organizations consider the Trusted Internet Connections (TIC) initiative [DHS TIC] requirements for external network connections since limiting the number of access control points for remote access reduces attack surfaces.
Related Controls: SC-7.
REMOTE ACCESS | PROTECTION OF CONFIDENTIALITY AND INTEGRITY USING ENCRYPTION
Implement cryptographic mechanisms to protect the confidentiality and integrity of remote access sessions.
Discussion: Virtual private networks can be used to protect the confidentiality and integrity of remote access sessions. Transport Layer Security (TLS) is an example of a cryptographic protocol that provides end-to-end communications security over networks and is used for Internet communications and online transactions.
Related Controls: SC-8, SC-12, SC-13.
REMOTE ACCESS | MANAGED ACCESS CONTROL POINTS
Route remote access through authorized and managed network access control points.
Discussion: Organizations consider the Trusted Internet Connections (TIC) initiative [DHS TIC] requirements for external network connections since limiting the number of access control points for remote access reduces attack surfaces.
Related Controls: SC-7.
REMOTE ACCESS | PRIVILEGED COMMANDS AND ACCESS
(a) Authorize the execution of privileged commands and access to security-relevant information via remote access only in a format that provides assessable evidence and for the following needs: [Assignment: organization-defined needs]; and
(b) Document the rationale for remote access in the security plan for the system.
Discussion: Remote access to systems represents a significant potential vulnerability that can be exploited by adversaries. As such, restricting the execution of privileged commands and access to security-relevant information via remote access reduces the exposure of the organization and the susceptibility to threats by adversaries to the remote access capability.
Related Controls: AC-6, SC-12, SC-13.
REMOTE ACCESS | MONITORING FOR UNAUTHORIZED CONNECTIONS
[Withdrawn: Incorporated into SI-4.]
REMOTE ACCESS | PROTECTION OF MECHANISM INFORMATION
Protect information about remote access mechanisms from unauthorized use and disclosure.
Discussion: Remote access to organizational information by non-organizational entities can increase the risk of unauthorized use and disclosure about remote access mechanisms. The organization considers including remote access requirements in the information exchange agreements with other organizations, as applicable. Remote access requirements can also be included in rules of behavior (see PL-4) and access agreements (see PS-6).
Related Controls: AT-2, AT-3, PS-6.
REMOTE ACCESS | ADDITIONAL PROTECTION FOR SECURITY FUNCTION ACCESS
[Withdrawn: Incorporated into AC-3(10).]
REMOTE ACCESS | DISABLE INSENSITIVE NETWORK PROTOCOLS
[Withdrawn: Incorporated into CM-7.]
REMOTE ACCESS | DISCONNECT OR DISABLE ACCESS

title: SECURITY AND PRIVACY ATTRIBUTES
methodology:
Below is an expert validation methodology for AC-16 (Security and Privacy Attributes) with practical signs of implementation, common evasion techniques, concrete validation steps, and expert tips:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Look for system configuration files and dashboards that list security and privacy attribute types and their permitted values. Screenshots of attribute binding being shown in output devices (e.g., human‐readable markings) also serve as evidence.
• Technical Artifacts: Examine audit logs that chronicle changes to attribute values, configuration management databases (CMDB) that document binding associations, and access control policy files enforcing permitted value ranges.
• Operational Patterns: Regular attribute updates (especially dynamic associations) in response to changes in user status, data state, or data flow events. Documented processes that ensure authorized individuals modify and maintain attributes.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Faking Compliance: Some organizations may only upload a configuration document listing required attributes without showing automated binding or dynamic enforcement in the running system.
• Surface-level Implementations: Relying solely on static documentation without automated audit trails, using manual processes that aren’t monitored centrally, or producing templates without real operational mapping in system logs.
• Technical Gaps: Missing integration between attribute assignment and enforcement mechanisms, lack of integrity checks for attribute binding, and absence of correlation between audit logs and actual attribute modifications.

3. VALIDATION STEPS

CHECK: Verify that systems actively bind defined security and privacy attributes (types and values) to information objects whether in storage, process, or transmission.
WHY: This confirms that the system implements attribute binding as required for enforcing security and privacy policies.
HOW: 1) Review system configuration files or data structure definitions that specify attribute types and allowed value ranges. 2) Observe sample data objects to ensure assigned attributes are present. 3) Use automated queries to inspect metadata in databases.
FAIL: Absence of attribute values in sample objects or a gap between defined configurations and operational metadata.

CHECK: Validate that permitted attribute values (or ranges) are enforced and any noncompliant settings are blocked.
WHY: Ensuring only defined, approved attribute values are used is key to maintaining security and privacy policies.
