HOW: 1) Examine system logs and configuration policies for programmed value checks. 2) Attempt to assign a value outside the permitted range in a controlled test. 3) Verify that the system rejects or flags out-of-range values.
FAIL: If test inputs are accepted without proper validation, or if logs show no enforcement of attribute value ranges.

CHECK: Confirm that changes to attribute values are audited and retrievable from audit logs.
WHY: Auditable logs of attribute changes are essential for forensic tracking and accountability.
HOW: 1) Inspect audit log files for entries related to attribute modifications. 2) Correlate log timestamps with administrative actions. 3) Use automated log analysis tools to track change patterns.
FAIL: Lack of audit records, missing correlating timestamps or administrator IDs, or logs that are incomplete/inconsistent.

CHECK: Assess dynamic attribute association mechanisms by testing attribute changes in response to triggered events (e.g., change of user privilege or data aggregation scenarios).
WHY: Dynamic binding ensures that attribute associations remain accurate as information undergoes status or context changes.
HOW: 1) Initiate a controlled scenario where the attribute should change (e.g., update user role or data state). 2) Use monitoring and logging tools to verify that the attribute is updated in real time. 3) Review follow-up audit records for confirmation.
FAIL: If the attribute remains static despite the underlying change or if there is no log evidence capturing the dynamic update.

CHECK: Verify that only authorized individuals (or validated processes) can modify or assign security and privacy attributes.
WHY: Preventing unauthorized attribute changes is critical to protecting integrity and ensuring policy-driven enforcement.
HOW: 1) Review access control lists and role-based access controls around attribute management interfaces. 2) Inspect logs of attribute change events to validate the identity of the modifier against authorized user lists. 3) Conduct a test using accounts without privileges and ensure modifications are blocked.
FAIL: If unauthorized modifications are allowed or the system lacks appropriate identity correlation in audit logs.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Verification Shortcuts: Utilize automated tools to correlate system configuration changes with audit logs. Ensure to perform time-based testing (e.g., attribute updates over time) to confirm dynamic handling.
• Critical Indicators: Look for discrepancies between defined attribute policies and live system behavior. Missing audit trails for attribute changes or static attribute states in dynamic scenarios are major red flags.
• Details That Matter: Focus on verifying that attribute bindings can’t be easily stripped or bypassed by altering metadata. Check that integration points between components maintain consistent interpretation of these attributes across distributed systems.

This practical validation approach ensures you are not verifying mere documentation, but operational compliance through observable, automated, and dynamically enforced mechanisms in line with AC-16 requirements.
content:
Statement:
a. Provide the means to associate [Assignment: organization-defined types of security and privacy attributes] with [Assignment: organization-defined security and privacy attribute values] for information in storage, in process, and/or in transmission; b. Ensure that the attribute associations are made and retained with the information; c. Establish the following permitted security and privacy attributes from the attributes defined in AC-16a for [Assignment: organization-defined systems]: [Assignment: organization-defined security and privacy attributes]; d. Determine the following permitted attribute values or ranges for each of the established attributes: [Assignment: organization-defined attribute values or ranges for established attributes]; e. Audit changes to attributes; and; f. Review [Assignment: organization-defined security and privacy attributes] for applicability [Assignment: organization-defined frequency].

Guidance:
Discussion: Information is represented internally within systems using abstractions known as data structures. Internal data structures can represent different types of entities, both active and passive. Active entities, also known as subjects, are typically associated with individuals, devices, or processes acting on behalf of individuals. Passive entities, also known as objects, are typically associated with data structures, such as records, buffers, tables, files, inter-process pipes, and communications ports. Security attributes, a form of metadata, are abstractions that represent the basic properties or characteristics of active and passive entities with respect to safeguarding information. Privacy attributes, which may be used independently or in conjunction with security attributes, represent the basic properties or characteristics of active and passive entities with respect to the management of personally identifiable information. Attributes can be either explicitly or implicitly associated with the information contained in organizational systems or system components.

Attributes may be associated with active entities (i.e., subjects) that have the potential to send or receive information, cause information to flow among objects, or change the system state. These attributes may also be associated with passive entities (i.e., objects) that contain or receive information. The association of attributes to subjects and objects by a system is referred to as binding and is inclusive of setting the attribute value and the attribute type. Attributes, when bound to a subject's information, facilitate the enforcement of security and privacy policies for access control and information flow control, including data retention limits, permitted uses of personally identifiable information, and identification of personal information that data objects. Such enforcement occurs through organizational processes or system functions or mechanisms. The binding techniques implemented by systems affect the strength of attribute binding to information. Binding strength and the assurance associated with binding techniques play important parts in the trust that organizations have in the information flow management process. The binding techniques affect the number and degree of additional reviews required by organizations. The content or assigned values of attributes can directly affect the ability of individuals to access organizational information.

Organizations can define the types of attributes needed for systems to support missions or business functions. There are many values that can be assigned to a security attribute. By specifying the permitted attribute ranges and values, organizations ensure that attribute values are meaningful and relevant. Labeling refers to the association of attributes with the subjects and objects represented by the internal data structures within systems. This facilitates system-based enforcement of information security and privacy policies. Labels include classification of information in accordance with legal and compliance requirements (e.g., top secret, secret, confidential, controlled unclassified, information impact level; high value asset information, access authorizations, nationality; data life cycle protection (i.e., encryption and data expiration), personally identifiable information processing permissions, including individual consent to personally identifiable information processing, and contractor affiliation. A related term to labeling is marking. Marking refers to the association of attributes with objects in a human-readable form and displayed on system media. Marking enables manual, procedural, or process-based enforcement of information security and privacy policies. Security and privacy labels may have the same value as media markings (e.g., top secret, secret, confidential). See MP-3 (Media Marking).

Enhancements:
(1) SECURITY AND PRIVACY ATTRIBUTES | DYNAMIC ATTRIBUTE ASSOCIATION
Dynamically associate security and privacy attributes with [Assignment: organization-defined subjects and objects] in accordance with the following security and privacy policies as information is created and combined: [Assignment: organization-defined security and privacy policies].
Discussion: Dynamic association of attributes is appropriate whenever the security or privacy characteristics of information change over time. Attributes may change due to information aggregation issues (i.e., characteristics of individual data elements are different from the combined elements), changes in individual access authorizations (i.e., privileges), changes in the security category of information, or changes in security or privacy policies. Attributes may also change situationally.
Related Controls: None.
(2) SECURITY AND PRIVACY ATTRIBUTES | ATTRIBUTE VALUE CHANGES BY AUTHORIZED INDIVIDUALS
Provide authorized individuals (or processes acting on behalf of individuals) the capability to define or change the value of associated security and privacy attributes.
Discussion: The content or assigned values of attributes can directly affect the ability of individuals to access organizational information. Therefore, it is important for systems to be able to inform the ability to create or modify attributes to authorized individuals.
Related Controls: None.
(3) SECURITY AND PRIVACY ATTRIBUTES | MAINTENANCE OF ATTRIBUTE ASSOCIATIONS BY SYSTEM
Maintain the association and integrity of [Assignment: organization-defined security and privacy attributes] to [Assignment: organization-defined subjects and objects].
Discussion: Maintaining the association and integrity of security and privacy attributes to subjects and objects with sufficient access and integrity helps to ensure that the attribute associations can be used as the basis of automated policy actions. The integrity of specific items, such as security configuration files, may be maintained through the use of an integrity monitoring mechanism that detects anomalies and changes that deviate from “known good” baselines. Automated policy actions include retention date expirations, access control decisions, information flow control decisions, and information disclosure decisions.
Related Controls: None.
(4) SECURITY AND PRIVACY ATTRIBUTES | ASSOCIATION OF ATTRIBUTES BY AUTHORIZED INDIVIDUALS
Provide the capability to associate [Assignment: organization-defined security and privacy attributes] with [Assignment: organization-defined subjects and objects] by authorized individuals (or processes acting on behalf of individuals).
Discussion: Systems, in general, provide the capability for privileged users to assign security and privacy attributes to system-defined subjects (e.g., users) and objects (e.g., directories, files, and ports). Some systems provide additional capability for general users to assign security and privacy attributes to additional objects (e.g., files, emails). The association of attributes by authorized individuals is described in the design documentation. The support provided by systems can include prompting users to select security and privacy attributes to be associated with information objects, employing automated mechanisms to categorize information with attributes based on defined policies, or ensuring that the combination of the security or privacy attributes selected is valid. Organizations consider the creation, deletion, or modification of attributes when defining auditable events.
Related Controls: None.
(5) SECURITY AND PRIVACY ATTRIBUTES | ATTRIBUTE DISPLAYS ON OBJECTS TO BE OUTPUT
Display security and privacy attributes in human-readable form on each object that the system transmits to output devices to identify [Assignment: organization-defined special dissemination, handling, or distribution instructions] using [Assignment: organization-defined human-readable, standard naming conventions].
Discussion: System outputs include printed pages, screens, or equivalent items. System output devices include printers, notebook computers, video displays, smart phones, and tablets. To mitigate the risk of unauthorized exposure of information (e.g., shoulder surfing), the outputs display full attribute values when unmasked by the subscriber.
Related Controls: None.
(6) SECURITY AND PRIVACY ATTRIBUTES | MAINTENANCE OF ATTRIBUTE ASSOCIATION
Require personnel to associate and maintain the association of [Assignment: organization-defined security and privacy attributes] with [Assignment: organization-defined subjects and objects] in accordance with [Assignment: organization-defined security and privacy policies].
Discussion: Maintaining attribute association requires individual users (as opposed to the system) to maintain associations of defined security and privacy attributes with subjects and objects.
Related Controls: None.
(7) SECURITY AND PRIVACY ATTRIBUTES | CONSISTENT ATTRIBUTE INTERPRETATION
Provide a consistent interpretation of security and privacy attributes transmitted between distributed system components.
Discussion: To enforce security and privacy policies across multiple system components in distributed systems, organizations provide a consistent interpretation of security and privacy attributes employed in access enforcement and flow enforcement decisions. Organizations can establish agreements and processes to help ensure that distributed system components interpret attributes with consistent interpretations in automated access enforcement and flow enforcement actions.
Related Controls: None.
(8) SECURITY AND PRIVACY ATTRIBUTES | ASSOCIATION TECHNIQUES AND TECHNOLOGIES
Implement [Assignment: organization-defined techniques and technologies] in associating security and privacy attributes to information.
Discussion: The association of security and privacy attributes to information within systems is important for conducting automated access enforcement and flow enforcement actions. The association of such attributes to information (i.e., binding) can be accomplished with technologies and techniques that provide different levels of assurance. For example, systems can cryptographically bind attributes to information using digital signatures that support cryptographic keys protected by hardware devices (sometimes known as hardware roots of trust).
Related Controls: SC-12, SC-13.
(9) SECURITY AND PRIVACY ATTRIBUTES | ATTRIBUTE REASSIGNMENT — REGARDING MECHANISMS
Change security and privacy attributes associated with information only via regarding mechanisms validated using [Assignment: organization-defined techniques or procedures].
Discussion: A regrading mechanism is a trusted process authorized to re-classify and re-label data in accordance with a defined policy exception. Validated regrading mechanisms are.

title: AUTOMATED MARKING
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for AC-15 Automated Marking – recognizing that the control statement has been withdrawn and its intent folded into MP-3. The focus here is on ensuring that automated marking (or automated labeling) of media/assets is effectively implemented and operational.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: In environments where media (physical or electronic) are marked with classification or handling instructions, you should see real time, automated labels or markings applied as soon as the asset is created or modified. Evidence could include system-generated labels on files or media, digital watermarks, or metadata tags that reflect the intended markings.
• Technical Artifacts: Look for system logs or audit trails from the automated marking tool integrated within the media management process. Configuration files or automated workflows that trigger marking actions upon asset creation can serve as technical evidence. Screenshots or exports from management dashboards showing a history of marking events are valuable.
• Operational Patterns: There should be demonstrable patterns of consistent, repeatable automated actions where new assets or modified items are automatically labeled according to organizational classification schemes. Regular, system-generated reports on marking activities and exception alerts when marking fails can be observed.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Faked Compliance: Organizations might simply attach a manually generated label or even a static printed sticker to media, passing superficial inspection without having real automated systems in place.
• Surface-level Implementations: Systems that generate labels at creation but do not update them upon reclassification, thereby failing to reflect dynamic classification needs, are common pitfalls.
• Technical Gaps: A common gap is integration failure – the automated marking system might not tie into asset creation or modification processes fully. In some cases, logs may be generated but not actively reviewed, and configuration drift may lead to inconsistent enforcement.

3. VALIDATION STEPS (Structured as Checks):

CHECK: Verify the integration of automated marking within the asset/media management workflow.
WHY: This shows that the system is designed to automatically apply required markings as assets are created or modified, ensuring consistency and reducing human error.
HOW: Review system architecture diagrams and configuration settings in the media management tool to confirm that an automated marking module is deployed; ask to see a workflow demonstration (preferably automated) where a new asset goes through the marking process.
FAIL: Evidence that assets are marked manually or that the automated module is disabled or bypassed during integration.

CHECK: Examine system audit logs and reports for automated marking events.
WHY: Audit trails that log marking events provide direct insight into operational behavior, evidencing regular and successful automation in applying markings.
HOW: Query centralized log management and SIEM tools for records tagged with marking events; verify timestamps, asset identifiers, and that the markings reflect up-to-date classification policies.
FAIL: Absence of log entries for marking events or logs that only show exceptions with no evidence of successful marking operations.

CHECK: Conduct a test by creating or modifying a test asset in a controlled environment.
WHY: A live demonstration that the automated tool applies correct markings to new or updated assets provides clear, uncompromisable evidence of operational effectiveness.
HOW: Use a non-production asset to simulate creation or modifications, observe the automated marking process, and verify that the asset’s metadata or physical label is updated as expected by the system.
FAIL: The asset is not automatically marked, or it only receives a temporary/manual label that does not reflect the automated process.

CHECK: Review the configuration management database (CMDB) or related asset inventory for consistency with media marking policies.
WHY: Consistency between declared media classifications in the CMDB and the automated markings applied demonstrates thorough integration and ongoing compliance.
HOW: Sample assets from the CMDB and verify that their automated markings in the asset tracking system or associated logs match the documented classification levels.
FAIL: Significant discrepancies exist between the asset records in the CMDB and the automated markings in the system.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Verification Shortcuts: If available, utilize dashboards or integrated reporting features in the media management tool to quickly view the recency and frequency of marking events. This gives an at-a-glance view of operational health.
• Critical Indicators: Watch for stale records in the logs or repeated marking exceptions. These often indicate that the automated marking process is not fully integrated or is encountering configuration issues.
• Details That Matter: The timing of marking events relative to asset creation or critical modifications is crucial. Delays or inconsistencies may signal that the process is being overridden or skipped. Also, confirm that marking policies dynamically adjust to reflect reclassification events rather than being a one‐time action.

This methodology emphasizes dynamic, observable evidence of the automated marking process rather than solely relying on static documentation or self-attestation. A fully implemented system will integrate automated tools at all stages of asset management, maintain detailed audit logs, and produce consistent, verifiable output reflecting current classification policies.

Guidance:
[Withdrawn: Incorporated into MP-3. ]

title: PERMITTED ACTIONS WITHOUT IDENTIFICATION OR AUTHENTICATION
methodology:
Control AC-14 (PERMITTED ACTIONS WITHOUT IDENTIFICATION OR AUTHENTICATION) requires that organizations clearly identify and document which user actions can be performed without prior identification or authentication. Below is a practical methodology for validation.

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Screenshots or system interfaces that display publicly accessible areas (e.g., public websites, informational pages) where no logon prompt appears. Configuration panels or system settings explicitly listing actions exempt from authentication.
• Technical Artifacts: Security plan entries that document and justify these user actions; system configuration files and firewall rules that allow unauthenticated traffic to specific endpoints; audit logs showing unauthenticated accesses for designated public functions.
• Operational Patterns: Consistent logging of unauthenticated accesses; periodic reviews that confirm that only intended functions (e.g., public pages, call-receiving endpoints) are available without authentication.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Fake Compliance: Organizations may simply document exceptions in policy without actually implementing enforcement. For example, enabling a default ‘guest’ account always used bypassing proper controls.
• Surface-level Implementations: Using separate pages for unauthenticated access that mimic secure areas, but fail to enforce proper segmentation at the network or application layer.
• Frequently Missed Gaps: Lack of detailed rationale in the security plan; incomplete configuration of firewall/network segmentation; inadequate logging and monitoring of unauthenticated actions which can mask unauthorized access.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Verify that public-facing functions (eg, a public website or call endpoint) are accessible without authentication.
WHY: This confirms that the system intentionally allows specific actions without authentication and that the control is deliberately implemented.
HOW: Attempt to access the designated public-facing resources from a device with no stored credentials; note the absence of logon prompts and review whether only the predefined functions are available.
FAIL: If areas that should be publicly accessible prompt for login or if unintended functions are open without authentication.

CHECK: Review the security plan to ensure it contains a detailed listing and rationale of the actions permitted without identification or authentication.
WHY: Ensures the exemption is not accidental but is a planned exception justified by risk analysis and documented within system security parameters.
HOW: Compare the documented list of actions in the security plan to the actual system configuration and functional testing results. Confirm that the rationale aligns with the system’s mission and business functions.
FAIL: If the security plan lacks detail, does not justify the exemption or does not match the operational configuration.

CHECK: Analyze system configuration settings and network/firewall rules that support unauthenticated access for designated functions.
WHY: These artifacts validate that the control is technically implemented and logically segmented.
HOW: Request and inspect configuration files, network policies, and firewall deployment documentation that explicitly allow the identified unauthenticated actions. Use automated configuration audit tools where applicable.
FAIL: If configuration files do not explicitly list or enforce these unauthenticated actions, or if policies are overly broad.

CHECK: Validate the audit logs to ensure that unauthenticated access events for the permitted actions are being tracked and reviewed regularly.
WHY: Logging provides evidence that unauthenticated actions are monitored and are being controlled within an operational security framework.
HOW: Retrieve and review a sample of audit logs over a defined period; verify that any access events without authentication correspond only to the clearly defined public actions.
FAIL: If logs are missing, incomplete, or if unauthenticated access events appear for functions not explicitly designated.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Verification Shortcuts: Use automated tools to scan for open endpoints and verify access patterns; cross-check with documented configurations to quickly spot discrepancies. A simple network scan that reveals unexpected open ports or endpoints may indicate improper unauthenticated access.
• Critical Indicators: Pay close attention to audit logs—if unauthenticated actions are not consistently logged or if logs appear retrofitted, it’s a red flag. Also, validate that exceptions (like a physical switch used for bypass) are secure and truly isolated from sensitive functionalities.
• Key Details: Ensure that your testing distinguishes between public and sensitive areas, and that all public information systems have clear, documented boundaries. Inadequate segmentation or ambiguous documentation often signify a poorly implemented control.

This methodology emphasizes practical review and automated evidence capture to demonstrate that the permitted actions without identification or authentication are intentionally set, properly documented, and effectively monitored.
content:
Statement:
a. Identify [Assignment: organization-defined user actions] that can be performed on the system without identification or authentication consistent with organizational mission and business functions; and

b. Document and provide supporting rationale in the security plan for the system, user actions not requiring identification or authentication.

Guidance:
Specific user actions may be permitted without identification or authentication if organizations determine that identification and authentication are not required for the specified user actions. Organizations may allow a limited number of user actions without identification or authentication, including when individuals access public websites or other publicly accessible federal systems, when individuals use mobile phones to receive calls, or when facsimiles are received. Organizations identify actions that normally require identification and authentication but may, under certain circumstances, allow identification or authentication mechanisms to be bypassed. Such bypasses may occur, for example, via a software-readable physical switch that commands bypass of the logon functionality and is protected from accidental or unmonitored use. Permitting actions without identification or authentication does not apply to situations where identification and authentication have already occurred and are not repeated but rather to situations where identification and authentication have not yet occurred. Organizations may indicate that there are no user actions that can be performed on organizational systems without identification and authentication, and therefore, the value for the assignment operation can be “none.”

title: SUPERVISION AND REVIEW — ACCESS CONTROL
methodology:
Below is a practical validation methodology for what was previously known as AC-13 – SUPERVISION AND REVIEW — ACCESS CONTROL. Although the guidance has been withdrawn and its intent is now integrated into AC-2 (Account Management) and AU-6 (Audit and Accountability), assessors should still verify that supervision and review activities around access control exist and are effective. 

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible Evidence: Look for periodic access review meeting minutes, documented procedures, and approval records for account changes. Automated alerts or dashboard reports that aggregate account modifications can also be observed. 
• Technical Artifacts: Access control systems should generate logs or reports showing account creation, modification, and deactivation. SIEM dashboards, audit logs from identity management systems, and produced reports from review tools are strong technical evidence. 
• Operational Patterns: Regularly scheduled reviews (monthly/quarterly) that confirm account validity, monitor privileges, and address exceptions indicate active implementation of supervisory reviews. 

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Faking Compliance: Organizations might provide outdated policy documents or generic meeting minutes that do not reflect active or recent review processes. 
• Surface-Level Implementation: Simply having a policy that mandates reviews without integrated technical controls (automated detection, report generation) or lacking periodic follow-up actions. 
• Technical Gaps: Failing to link account changes with corresponding audit reviews or lacking comprehensive logging (e.g., missing revalidation events) are common pitfalls. 

3. VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Verify that a documented, periodic access review process is in place, including actual review logs and meeting minutes.
WHY: This shows both managerial intent and operational evidence compared to mere policy existence.
HOW: Request copies of recent access review meeting minutes, review logs from identity management systems and SIEM, and confirm that the process includes a reconciliation of active accounts against approvals.
FAIL: No evidence of recent review activities, or review documentation that does not align with actual system logs, indicates a failure of supervision and review.

CHECK: Assess the integration between account management (AC-2) and audit review (AU-6) mechanisms for access changes.
WHY: Successful integration proves that supervision is built into the operational lifecycle of access management.
HOW: Validate that every access change recorded in AC-2 is accompanied by corresponding audit trails in AU-6, and perform spot checks on recent account modifications to ensure proper review and remediation processes were executed.
FAIL: Discrepancies between account changes and audit logs, or missing links between the two systems, suggest a bypassed or ineffective review process.

CHECK: Confirm the presence and functionality of automated alerting or dashboard reports that track anomalies in access privileges.
WHY: Automation reduces the risk of manual oversight and highlights potential issues in near real-time.
HOW: Review SIEM/IT dashboard configurations to ensure they include alerts for access rights modifications, especially those involving privileged accounts, and test the alert mechanism if feasible.
FAIL: Lack of automated logging, missing or misconfigured alerts, or manual-only processes that are not regularly validated signal inadequate practical implementation.

CHECK: Test a sample user account’s lifecycle—from provisioning to deprovisioning—and review the supervision trail.
WHY: A complete lifecycle review provides insight into whether each access change has been appropriately supervised and approved.
HOW: Select a sample set of user accounts (including temporary and privileged accounts), follow through onboarding, modifications, and termination processes, and check for corresponding compliance evidence (e.g., approval chains and review logs).
FAIL: If the sample shows accounts active without review events or missing de-provisioning actions despite user departures, it confirms a breakdown in supervision and review.

4. EXPERT TIPS
• Verification Shortcuts: Focus on the existence of an automated system that correlates access management changes with audit logs. If such dashboards exist and are actively monitored, it is a strong indicator of effective supervision.
• Critical Indicators: Consistency between documented review events (meeting minutes, approval records) and system logs. Discrepancies or delays in updating logs can be a red flag for process breakdowns.
• Detail Focus: Pay attention to how anomalies are handled—escalation procedures and remedial actions should be clearly defined and evidenced in system tickets or follow-up documentation.

This methodology helps ensure that even though AC-13 has been formally withdrawn, its spirit—namely, effective supervision and continuous review of access controls—is practically and operationally embedded in the organization’s processes.
content:
Guidance:
[Withdrawn: Incorporated into AC-2 and AU-6. ]

title: SESSION TERMINATION
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• Visible evidence: Users are automatically logged out after defined inactivity periods or when certain trigger events occur. Session interfaces display logout and timeout warning messages when applicable.
• Technical artifacts: System configurations, session management policies, and log files showing session initiation and termination events. Code or configuration snippets verifying timeout settings are available.
• Operational patterns: Consistent behavior of automatic logout across interactive sessions; visible system messages alerting users a session is ending; audit logs with entries marking termination due to inactivity or triggered events.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Faking compliance: Organizations might document policy requirements without actually enforcing session disconnect in the live environment, such as setting overly long timeouts that are ineffective.
• Surface-level implementations: Merely providing a logout button without enforcing inactivity-based automatic termination or bypassing session kill via multiple tabs or background processes.
• Technical gaps: Inadequate logging of termination events; misconfigured parameters where warning messages display but sessions remain active; failure to integrate this with critical applications or remote sessions.

VALIDATION STEPS
1. CHECK: Inactivity Timeout Enforcement
   WHY: Verifies that user sessions are terminated automatically after an organization-defined period of inactivity.
   HOW: Review system configuration files or settings for defined inactivity limits, then perform a live test by idling a session and verifying that the session is terminated as expected. Cross-check with audit logs for corresponding termination entries.
   FAIL: If the session remains active beyond the defined inactivity period, or if there is no corresponding log entry for termination.

2. CHECK: Trigger Event Session Termination
   WHY: Ensures that non-timeout trigger events (such as incident response or time-of-day restrictions) force session termination as specified.
   HOW: Simulate a trigger event (if safe in a test environment) or review configuration and event logs to determine if termination occurred due to such triggers. Confirm that alternative session-resident processes are properly killed.
   FAIL: If sessions remain active despite simulated or documented trigger events or if associated log entries are missing.

3. CHECK: User-Initiated Logout & Termination Messaging
   WHY: Confirms that the system not only terminates inactive sessions but also provides clear logout logging and messaging to users, enhancing usability and clarity.
   HOW: Initiate a logout via the provided user mechanism. Observe if an explicit logout message is displayed and if any timeout warning appears prior to termination. Verify this behavior against system screenshots or UI logs.
   FAIL: If the logout message is absent, ambiguous, or if timeout warnings are not displayed, leading to user confusion.

4. CHECK: Audit Log Verification
   WHY: Audit logs provide proof of operational effectiveness by capturing automatic termination events.
   HOW: Examine audit records to ensure that each session termination event is recorded with a corresponding reason (inactivity, triggered event, or user-initiated logout). Use automated log analysis tools to correlate expected termination events with observed behavior.
   FAIL: Inconsistent or missing records where sessions should have been terminated automatically.

EXPERT TIPS
• Shortcut: Directly simulate an inactivity period on a test account in a controlled environment. This hands-on approach quickly reveals whether the automatic termination is effectively implemented.
• Critical Indicator: Consistent and timely log entries for session terminations are essential. Lack thereof typically indicates a configuration or operational oversight.
• Detail importance: Confirm that the system enforces session termination even when users try to resume activity (e.g., via multiple browser tabs), and that the termination mechanism is uniform across all platforms (local, remote, and network accessed sessions).
• Reminder: Ensure that enhancements, such as user-initiated logout messages and timeout warnings, are operationally tested as these are often overlooked in favor of merely checking documentation.

Overall, focus your validation on direct observation and operational evidence rather than solely on policy documentation to ensure true control effectiveness.
content:
Statement:
Automatically terminate a user session after [Assignment: organization-defined conditions or trigger events requiring session disconnect].

Guidance:
Session termination addresses the termination of user-initiated logical sessions (in contrast to SC-10, which addresses the termination of network connections associated with communications sessions (i.e., network disconnect)). A logical session (for local, network, and remote access) is initiated whenever a user (or process acting on behalf of a user) accesses an organizational system. Such user sessions can be terminated without terminating network sessions. Session termination ends all processes associated with a user's logical session except for those processes that are specifically created by the user (i.e., session owner) to continue after the session is terminated. Conditions or trigger events that require automatic termination of the session include organization-defined periods of user inactivity, targeted responses to certain types of incidents, or time-of-day restrictions on system use.

Enhancements:
SESSION TERMINATION | USER-INITIATED LOGOUTS
Provide a logout capability for user-initiated communications sessions whenever authentication is used to gain access to [Assignment: organization-defined information resources].
Discussion: Information resources to which users gain access via authentication include local workstations, databases, and password-protected websites or web-based services.
Related Controls: None.
SESSION TERMINATION | TERMINATION MESSAGE
Display an explicit logout message to users indicating the termination of authenticated communications sessions.
Discussion: Logout messages for web access can be displayed after authenticated sessions have been terminated. However, for certain types of sessions, including file transfer protocol (FTP) sessions, systems typically send logout messages as final messages prior to terminating sessions.
Related Controls: None.
SESSION TERMINATION | TIMEOUT WARNING MESSAGE
Display an explicit message to users indicating that the session will end in [Assignment: organization-defined time until end of session].
Discussion: To increase usability, notify users of pending session termination and prompt users to continue the session. The pending session termination time period is based on the parameters defined in the AC-12 base control.
Related Controls: None.

title: DEVICE LOCK
methodology:
Validation Methodology for AC-11: DEVICE LOCK

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible Evidence: Observe that systems automatically display a lock screen after the organization-defined inactivity period. Check that users can also manually initiate device locks (e.g., using OS keyboard shortcuts such as Windows+L or equivalent).
   • Technical Artifacts: Review OS or application configuration settings (e.g., Group Policy settings, registry keys, system preferences for screensaver/lock timeout) that define the inactivity period and lock behavior. Event logs showing lock events serve as another artifact.
   • Operational Patterns: Regular occurrence of lock event logs after inactivity and the inability to bypass the lock screen without reauthentication demonstrate active control.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Fake Compliance: Organizations may simply document a policy or set a timeout value without actually enforcing it at the OS or application level.
   • Surface-Level Implementations: Configuring a screensaver without a proper lock prompt, or relying solely on user action without testing inactivity triggers, can create gaps.
   • Technical Gaps: Failing to verify that the lock screen obscures previously displayed sensitive information (i.e., missing pattern-hiding displays) or that the lock engages across all workstations (desktop, laptops, mobile devices) are frequent misses.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify the automatic initiation of a device lock after the defined period of inactivity.
   WHY: This confirms that the system protects against unauthorized access during user absence.
   HOW: Inspect OS/application configuration settings (e.g., Group Policy, registry, screensaver settings) and audit event logs for lock trigger events occurring after the specified time; perform a live test by leaving a device idle for the defined period.
   FAIL: If the system remains unlocked beyond the defined inactivity period or if no corresponding log events are found.

   CHECK: Test the user-initiated device lock functionality.
   WHY: Ensures that users can lock their devices manually when leaving them unattended.
   HOW: Initiate a lock using the designated method (e.g., Windows+L, manual lock button in mobile OS, or application-level lock button) and verify that the lock screen activates immediately.
   FAIL: If the manual lock command does not result in a secure lock screen or provides an easy bypass.

   CHECK: Validate that the device lock persists until reauthentication with established credentials.
   WHY: This ensures that the lock screen actually prevents access and that previous session data is not exposed.
   HOW: After engaging the lock, attempt to access the device using non-authenticated methods and confirm that reauthentication (password, multifactor, etc.) is required to regain access.
   FAIL: If the device allows any form of access without full reauthentication or if sensitive workstation data remains visible after locking.

   CHECK: Assess the implementation of pattern-hiding displays (if applicable as an enhancement).
   WHY: This additional measure conceals any sensitive information on-screen before the lock engages.
   HOW: Visually inspect or test the lock screen to ensure that the device does not display previews or remnants of previously displayed data; review configuration settings related to screen obfuscation.
   FAIL: If the screen still reveals sensitive or identifiable information post-lock.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: Focus on log files and direct system settings rather than merely relying on policy documents, as these artifacts are harder to fake.
   • Critical Indicator: Regular and timely lock events in system logs are a strong sign of correct implementation. Conversely, absence of such events or inconsistent timing is a warning signal.
   • Details that Matter: Make sure that the lock configuration applies uniformly across all endpoints and that the reauthentication mechanisms are robust; test on multiple OS platforms if applicable.
   • Extra Tip: During inspections, try to perform a simulated attack by attempting to bypass the lock; failure to bypass confirms operational effectiveness.

This methodology emphasizes practical, operational evidence of device lock enforcement rather than theoretical compliance, ensuring that the control is both implemented and effective in protecting against unauthorized access.
content:
Statement:
a. Prevent further access to the system by [Selection (one or more): initiating a device lock after [Assignment: organization-defined time period] of inactivity; requiring the user to initiate a device lock before leaving the system unattended]; and b. Retain the device lock until the user reestablishes access using established identification and authentication procedures.

Guidance:
Discussion: Device locks are temporary actions taken to prevent logical access to organizational systems when users stop work and move away from the immediate vicinity of those systems but do not want to log out because of the temporary nature of their absences. Device locks can be implemented at the operating system level or at the application level. A proximity lock may be used to initiate the device lock (e.g., via a Bluetooth-enabled device or dongle). User-initiated device locking is behavior or policy-based and, as such, requires users to take physical action to initiate the device lock. Device locks are not an acceptable substitute for logging out of systems, such as when organizations require users to log out at the end of workdays.

Enhancements:
(1) DEVICE LOCK | PATTERN-HIDING DISPLAYS Conceal, via the device lock, information previously visible on the display with a publicly viewable image.

title: CONCURRENT SESSION CONTROL
methodology:
**PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION**
• Evidence includes configuration files or system settings that explicitly set a maximum number of concurrent sessions per account type. This might be visible in centralized authentication management systems or in session management modules of applications.
• Technical artifacts include logs that record session creation and terminations, showing that new sessions are rejected once the defined limit is reached.
• Operational patterns, such as automated alerts or throttling mechanisms when threshold limits are approached/exceeded, are a sign the control is active.

**COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES**
• Organizations sometimes merely document a policy setting without implementing enforcement in the system. For example, a configuration file might detail a session limit that isn’t referenced by the actual session management code.
• Some deploy static user interface messages that warn individuals without triggering session terminations or limitations.
• Overlooking non-administrative accounts or failing to differentiate account types leads to inconsistent enforcement (only a subset of accounts properly utilizing concurrent session controls).

**VALIDATION STEPS**
1. CHECK: Verify that the authentication/session management system’s configuration explicitly defines a maximum concurrent session count for relevant accounts.
   WHY: A correctly set parameter indicates that session limits are recognized at the system level.
   HOW: Access configuration files, dashboards, or administrative consoles of the authentication system and look for a parameter that sets the concurrent session threshold; cross-check with policy.
   FAIL: If configuration lacks such a parameter or shows a default/no limit, the control is potentially not enforced.

2. CHECK: Perform a controlled access test by simultaneously initiating sessions using a test account.
   WHY: Real-time enforcement can be validated by observing if the system blocks or terminates sessions beyond the limit.
   HOW: Using automation or manual testing, open multiple sessions (e.g., via different browsers or devices) until the threshold is met and attempt an additional login. Document the response (error message, session termination, etc.).
   FAIL: If the test account permits more concurrent sessions than defined or does not block/reject the exceeding session, the control is not effective.

3. CHECK: Review system logs for session management events around concurrent access.
   WHY: Logs should include evidence of attempts to exceed the threshold, including rejections, forced logouts, or warnings.
   HOW: Query system or application logs for session creation and termination events, especially looking for logged incidents where session limits were enforced (e.g., “session limit reached” entries).
   FAIL: Absence of any logged enforcement actions or discrepancies between the defined limit and logged session counts indicates implementation gaps.

4. CHECK: Assess integration points between the session management system and centralized user account controls.
   WHY: A well-integrated system ensures that the limit applies across all interfaces and not just in isolated instances.
   HOW: Review technical documentation and run cross-functional tests where login attempts from diverse system interfaces (web, API, etc.) occur to verify consistent enforcement.
   FAIL: Inconsistent application of session limits across interfaces or gaps in integration point configuration suggests evasion of the defined control.

**EXPERT TIPS**
• Shortcut: Use automated testing tools that simulate multiple concurrent login attempts across different endpoints to measure enforcement accuracy quickly.
• Critical Indicator: Look for anomalies such as loopholes in specific applications/subsystems where session limits are not enforced; such instances are a red flag for potential bypasses.
• Detail Focus: Confirm that the control is applied not only globally but adjusted for different account types if defined in the policy (e.g., tighter controls for administrative versus regular accounts).
• Ensure that configuration changes are auditable and that logs cannot be easily manipulated; authenticity and integrity of session logs are paramount.
• Pay attention to the timing of log entries—synchronized and timestamped events help verify that the session control operates in real time, highlighting its effectiveness in a live environment.
content:
Statement:
Limit the number of concurrent sessions for each [Assignment: organization-defined account and/or account type] to [Assignment: organization-defined number].

Guidance:
Discussion: Organizations may define the maximum number of concurrent sessions for system accounts globally, by account type, by account, or any combination thereof. For example, organizations may limit the number of concurrent sessions for system administrators or other individuals working in particularly sensitive domains or mission-critical applications. Concurrent session control addresses concurrent sessions for system accounts. It does not, however, address concurrent sessions by single users via multiple system accounts.

title: PREVIOUS LOGON NOTIFICATION
methodology:
Below is the practical validation methodology for AC-9 Previous Logon Notification:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   - Visible evidence: At the user’s logon prompt or immediately after authentication, a dynamic message displays the date and time of the previous successful logon. Enhanced implementations may also show numbers of unsuccessful attempts, counts over a period, account changes, or additional details such as IP addresses.
   - Technical artifacts: Log files (e.g., system security logs, audit logs) that record logon events, with timestamps and detailed messages, and configuration files or scripts that generate the notification display.
   - Operational patterns: Consistent display of personalized logon messages at every authentication event and cross-verification with backend logs that include corresponding timestamps.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   - Faking compliance by configuring a static message that never updates, rather than dynamically pulling the last logon information.
   - Relying solely on documentation or screenshots without operational evidence of actual system queries to audit logs.
   - Partial implementations that only show the date without the time, or that omit counts of unsuccessful attempts if required by organizational enhancements.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   CHECK: Verify that the logon screen displays a dynamic message showing the previous successful logon date and time.
   WHY: A dynamic and accurate message confirms the system is retrieving and communicating user-specific logon history.
   HOW: Perform a controlled logon and note the displayed previous logon information. Cross-reference with system audit logs to ensure the displayed value matches recorded timestamps.
   FAIL: If the message is missing, static, or inconsistent with log file entries, the implementation has failed.

   CHECK: Validate the inclusion and accuracy of additional data (e.g., unsuccessful logon attempts, account changes) if the organization’s enhancements are in use.
   WHY: Enhanced notifications provide critical security context, alerting the user to suspicious activity or unauthorized account modifications.
   HOW: Enable several successive failed logon attempts on a test account, follow with a successful logon, and check that the notification reflects the number of failed attempts. Likewise, simulate an account change (if possible) and verify that the change information is displayed.
   FAIL: Inconsistencies between simulated events and displayed notifications indicate bypass or incomplete implementation.

   CHECK: Corroborate the notification details with automated system logs that capture logon events.
   WHY: Log file evidence provides an independent source verifying that the notification message is generated based on real recorded data.
   HOW: Use automated log analysis tools (or manually review logs) to extract events for the test account. Confirm that the log events (including timestamps and counts) exactly match what was reported to the user at logon.
   FAIL: Mismatches between log events and displayed notifications suggest deficiencies in information gathering or presentation.

   CHECK: Test for consistent repeatability across different user sessions and system reboots.
   WHY: Consistent behavior under varying operational conditions ensures that the control is reliably active and not a one-off configuration.
   HOW: Conduct multiple logon sessions with the same and different users. Document whether each logon session accurately reflects the most recent past login and event counts.
   FAIL: Inconsistent or intermittent notifications indicate a potential flaw in the implementation that could be exploited.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   - Shortcut: Use scripted logon sessions to automatically record and compare the displayed notification with log records; discrepancies are a red flag.
   - Critical indicators: The actual retrieval of fresh log data (not a hard-coded message), and matching event counts (unsuccessful attempts, account changes) that reflect real security activity.
   - Details matter: Pay attention to the format and timing; even a slight delay or mismatch in displayed versus logged time can signal that the information is not being properly refreshed from the audit logs. Consistent inclusion of user-specific details is crucial for operational effectiveness.

This step-by-step methodology ensures that the control is not only documented but is actively and reliably implemented in real operational conditions.
content:
Statement:
Notify the user, upon successful logon to the system, of the date and time of the last logon.

Guidance:
Discussion: Previous logon notification is applicable to system access via human user interfaces and access to systems that occurs in other types of architectures. Information about the last successful logon allows the user to recognize if the date and time provided is not consistent with the user's last access.

Enhancements:
(1) PREVIOUS LOGON NOTIFICATION | UNSUCCESSFUL LOGONS Notify the user, upon successful logon, of the number of unsuccessful logon attempts since the last successful logon. Discussion: Information about the number of unsuccessful logon attempts since the last successful logon allows the user to recognize if the number of unsuccessful logon attempts is consistent with the user’s actual logon attempts.
(2) PREVIOUS LOGON NOTIFICATION | SUCCESSFUL AND UNSUCCESSFUL LOGONS Notify the user, upon successful logon, of the number of [Selection: successful logons; unsuccessful logon attempts; both] during [Assignment: organization-defined time period]. Discussion: Information about the number of successful and unsuccessful logon attempts within a specified time period allows the user to recognize if the number and type of logon attempts are consistent with the user’s actual logon attempts.
(3) PREVIOUS LOGON NOTIFICATION | NOTIFICATION OF ACCOUNT CHANGES Notify the user, upon successful logon, of changes to [Assignment: organization-defined security-related characteristics or parameters of the user's account] during [Assignment: organization-defined time period]. Discussion: Information about changes to security-related account characteristics within a specified time period allows users to recognize if changes were made without their knowledge.
(4) PREVIOUS LOGON NOTIFICATION | ADDITIONAL LOGON INFORMATION Notify the user, upon successful logon, of the following additional information: [Assignment: organization-defined additional information]. Discussion: Organizations can specify additional information to be provided to users upon logon, including the location of the last logon. User location is defined as information that can be determined by systems, such as Internet Protocol (IP) addresses from which network logons occurred, notifications of local logons, or device identifiers.

title: SYSTEM USE NOTIFICATION
methodology:
Below is the detailed methodology for validating the implementation of AC-8 – System Use Notification:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Visible evidence includes a banner or message displayed on the login screen that clearly outlines system use policies including monitoring and consent. 
   • Technical artifacts: Automated screenshot captures or system configuration files that enforce the banner display and log banner acknowledgment actions. 
   • Operational patterns include the inability to log in without first acknowledging the banner, with audit trails showing user acknowledgment and timestamp records.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations sometimes implement a mere static message at the bottom of the login page that can be easily bypassed or ignored rather than a full-screen mandatory banner.
   • Surface-level implementations may include banners that display only part of the required notification content or are dismissible without record.
   • Gaps include failure to enforce display until acknowledged or lack of integration with logging systems that capture explicit user agreement.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   • CHECK: Verify mandatory banner display on initial login screen
     WHY: A truly enforced banner prevents system access until the user acknowledges the notification, demonstrating that the organization ensures informed consent.
     HOW: Attempt to access the system with a test account and capture a screenshot of the initial login interface; confirm that the banner is presented in accordance with the defined wording (e.g., explanation of monitoring and usage conditions).
     FAIL: If the banner is absent, easily bypassable, or not retained until acknowledgment, the control is not effectively implemented.

   • CHECK: Examine banner content for required elements
     WHY: The content must include explicit statements regarding U.S. Government system usage, monitoring, recording, and the consequences of unauthorized access.
     HOW: Review the printed banner message against the regulatory checklist (include phrases such as “accessing a U.S. Government system,” “system may be monitored, recorded, and audited,” etc.); use automated content review tools to verify completeness.
     FAIL: Missing or incomplete notifications that omit any mandated component.

   • CHECK: Ensure that user interaction is required before further access
     WHY: The requirement to retain the banner until explicit user acknowledgment (e.g., clicking a consent button or entering login credentials) is crucial for operational control.
     HOW: Test the login process by monitoring network traffic and interactive prompts to confirm that the user must acknowledge the banner; check system logs for recorded acknowledgments.
     FAIL: If users can bypass the prompt or if there is no recorded evidence of acknowledgment, the control fails.

   • CHECK: Validate implementation on publicly accessible systems (if applicable)
     WHY: Public systems need a pre-access notification that includes references tailored to privacy and legal guidance.
     HOW: Access publicly available resources and verify that the banner or pre-access page is displayed with the required information; cross-check with technical configurations or public documentation.
     FAIL: Absence or improper display of the notification on publicly accessible systems indicates non-compliance.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Shortcut: Use automated testing scripts (e.g., Selenium) to simulate user login and capture banner screenshots and acknowledgment logs; this ensures consistency over manual testing. 
   • Critical Indicator: Any mechanism that permits bypassing or quick dismissal of the banner without logging the acknowledgment is a red flag.
   • Detail Matter: Ensure that multi-lingual environments are considered if your system serves diverse user demographics; verify that language-appropriate banners are in use. 
   • Practical Note: Always cross-verify with system logs; even if the banner appears correctly, lack of logging user consent signifies a weak implementation.

By following these steps, auditors can obtain automated, observable evidence that system use notifications are not only present but are actively enforced in line with AC-8 requirements.
content:
Statement:
a. Display [Assignment: organization-defined system use notification message or banner] to users before granting access to the system that provides privacy and security notices consistent with applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines and state that: 1. Users are accessing a U.S. Government system; 2. System usage may be monitored, recorded, and subject to audit; 3. Unauthorized use of the system is prohibited and subject to criminal and civil penalties; and 4. Use of the system indicates consent to monitoring and recording; b. Retain the notification message or banner on the screen until users acknowledge the usage conditions and take explicit actions to log on to or further access the system; and c. For publicly accessible systems: 1. Display system use information [Assignment: organization-defined conditions], before granting further access to the publicly accessible system; 2. Display references, if any, to monitoring, recording, or auditing that are consistent with privacy accommodations for such systems that generally prohibit those activities;

Guidance:
Discussion: System use notifications can be implemented using messages or warning banners displayed before individuals log in to systems. System use notifications are used only for access via logon interfaces with human users. Notifications are not required when human interfaces do not exist. Based on an assessment of risk, organizations consider whether or not a secondary system use notification is needed to access applications or other system resources after the initial network logon. Organizations consider system use notification messages or banners displayed in multiple languages based on organizational needs and the demographics of system users. Organizations consult with the privacy office for input regarding privacy messaging and the Office of the General Counsel or organizational equivalent for legal review and approval of warning banner content.

title: UNSUCCESSFUL LOGON ATTEMPTS
methodology:
PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
• System configuration settings (via local OS policies, Group Policy Objects, or application config files) that specify the maximum number of allowed consecutive failed logon attempts and the subsequent action (lockout, delay, notification, etc.).
• Audit logs or SIEM event records showing user accounts being locked out, delayed, or otherwise acted upon after reaching the predefined threshold.
• Observable testing events where simulated invalid logons trigger the configured security response.

COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
• Organizations may only document the policy or configuration while actual log retention or response mechanisms are not in place or actively monitored.
• Faking logs by manually inserting failed logon entries, which do not reflect the true behavior of the authentication system.
• Implementing a soft limit or manual reset without an automatic trigger, which may give the appearance of control without successful operational effectiveness.

VALIDATION STEPS
CHECK: Verify Authentication Configuration Settings
WHY: To confirm that system policies (e.g., Windows Account Lockout Policy, UNIX PAM configurations, or application-level settings) enforce a limit on consecutive invalid logon attempts and define an appropriate response.
HOW: Access the authentication configuration documentation or use administrative tools (e.g., Group Policy Management Console for Windows, command-line tools for Unix/Linux) to review settings like lockout threshold, lockout duration, or delay algorithm.
FAIL: If the configuration does not exist, is set to a permissive value, or the documented policy and the technical configuration are misaligned.

CHECK: Inspect Audit and SIEM Logs for Response Events
WHY: Log evidence that failed logon attempts trigger the pre-configured actions is critical for assessing real-world enforcement.
HOW: Query SIEM or audit logs for events indicating failed logon attempts and subsequent account lockouts or delays. Confirm that there are corresponding log entries with timestamps that match the configured thresholds.
FAIL: Absence of clear log entries or discrepancies between expected outcomes and log event patterns indicate ineffective control enforcement.

CHECK: Conduct a Controlled Live Test
WHY: A live test validates the operational effectiveness of the control in real time and exposes potential misconfigurations.
HOW: Using a test account, deliberately perform consecutive failed logon attempts (ensuring you are operating in a controlled environment) and monitor for automatic lockout, delay in response, or administrative notifications as configured. Document the threshold reached and the resulting system behavior.
FAIL: If the test account is not locked or if no automated feedback (such as notification or delayed logon) occurs after reaching the defined limit, it evidences non-compliance or implementation issues.

CHECK: Validate Documentation Against Technical Implementation
WHY: Cross-referencing ensures that what is written in policy documents is truly implemented in the infrastructure.
HOW: Compare the documented organization-defined thresholds, time periods, and remediation actions with what is configured within the system. Look for inconsistencies or mismatches using configuration files and system logs.
FAIL: If documentation does not match technical settings or if observed values differ (e.g., policy states a 5-attempt lockout but configuration is set to 10), then the control is not effectively implemented.

EXPERT TIPS
• Verification shortcuts include using automated queries/scripts to extract logon events and configuration settings from systems – these reduce the risk of human error and reveal real system behavior over the surface-level policy.
• Pay attention to time stamps in logs; repeated events spaced over very long intervals may indicate manual resets rather than automated enforcement.
• Ensure that both client-side (e.g., application login portals) and server-side (e.g., OS or database servers) configurations are checked, as bypasses might exist if only one side is enforced.
• Remember that administrators sometimes exempt service accounts or apply different thresholds – verify that exceptions are documented and justified.
• For mobile devices (if applicable), verify that after the set number of unsuccessful attempts the defined purging or wiping action is automated and logged.

This multi-layered validation approach emphasizes actual, observable control behavior over mere policy existence, making it robust against superficial compliance faking.
content:
Statement:
a. Enforce a limit of [Assignment: organization-defined number] consecutive invalid logon attempts by a user during a [Assignment: organization-defined time period]; and b. Automatically [Selection (one or more): lock the account or node for an [Assignment: organization-defined time period]; lock the account or node until released by an administrator; delay next logon prompt per [Assignment: organization-defined delay algorithm]; notify system administrator; take other [Assignment: organization-defined action]] when the maximum number of unsuccessful attempts is exceeded.

Guidance:
Discussion: The need to limit unsuccessful logon attempts and take subsequent action when the maximum number of attempts is exceeded applies regardless of whether the logon occurs via a local or network connection. Due to the potential for denial of service, automatic lockouts initiated by systems are usually temporary and automatically release after a predetermined, organization-defined time period. If a delay algorithm is selected, organizations may employ different algorithms for different components of the system based on the capabilities of those components. Responses to unsuccessful logon attempts may be implemented at the operating system and the application levels. Organization-defined actions that may be taken when the limit of allowed consecutive invalid logon attempts is exceeded include prompting the user to answer a secret question in addition to the username and password, invoking a lockdown mode with limited user capabilities (instead of full lockout), allowing users to only logon from specified Internet Protocol (IP) addresses, requiring a CAPTCHA to prevent automated attacks, or applying user profiles such as location, time of day, IP address, device, or Media Access Control (MAC) address. If automatic system lockout or execution of a delay algorithm is not implemented in support of the availability objective, organizations consider a combination of other actions to help prevent brute force attacks. In addition to the above, organizations can prompt users to respond to a secret question before the number of allowed unsuccessful logon attempts is exceeded. Automatically unlocking an account after a specified period of time is generally not permitted. However, exceptions may be required based on operational mission or need.

Enhancements:
(1) UNSUCCESSFUL LOGON ATTEMPTS | AUTOMATIC ACCOUNT LOCK [Withdrawn: Incorporated into AC-7.]
(2) UNSUCCESSFUL LOGON ATTEMPTS | PURGE OR WIPE MOBILE DEVICE Purge or wipe information from [Assignment: organization-defined mobile devices] based on [Assignment: organization-defined purging or wiping requirements and techniques] after [Assignment: organization-defined number] consecutive, unsuccessful device logon attempts. Discussion: A mobile device is a computing device that has a small form factor such that it can be carried by a single individual; is designed to operate without a physical connection; possesses local, non-removable or removable data storage; and includes a self-contained power source. Purging or wiping the device applies only to mobile devices for which the organization-defined number of unsuccessful logons occurs. The logon is to the mobile.

title: LEAST PRIVILEGE
methodology:
Below is a complete practical validation methodology for ensuring that the principle of least privilege (AC-6) is implemented effectively:

1. PRACTICAL SIGNS OF IMPLEMENTATION
   • Evidence that least privilege is implemented includes visible, segregated account and role definitions in the identity management system with clearly delineated privilege levels.
   • Technical artifacts such as system configuration management records, role-based access control (RBAC) listings, process privileges (via OS or container configuration tools), and automated audit logs showing privileged command executions support compliance.
   • Operational patterns like regular privilege reviews, documented access request workflows, and enforced separation between privileged and non-privileged accounts indicate that the control is active.

2. COMMON EVASIONS/BYPASSES
   • Organizations may simply document least privilege policies without enforcing them at the technical level; for example, roles might be defined but never applied in the application configuration.
   • Creating generic accounts for convenience rather than specific roles that truly limit privileges (using overly permissive group memberships in directories) is a frequent pitfall.
   • Surface-level logging that records event existence without ensuring that actions of privileged users are correlated with risk analysis may be in place, yet fall short during forensic or real-time review.

3. VALIDATION STEPS
   The following checks focus on observable, operational evidence:

   CHECK: Verify Role and Account Segregation
   WHY: Ensures that only authorized accounts and roles with a necessity to perform specific tasks have elevated privileges.
   HOW: Review the identity management settings and role definitions in a centralized tool (e.g., Active Directory, IAM platforms). Compare against a list of roles that are permitted access to security functions, verifying that non-privileged accounts are enforced elsewhere.
   FAIL: Discovery of shared or generic accounts with elevated privileges, or evidence that roles have overlapping privileges not strictly required for the assigned tasks.

   CHECK: Validate Privilege Levels for System Processes
   WHY: System processes should only operate at the minimal privilege level required to function; verifying this prevents exploitation through privilege escalation.
   HOW: Examine operating system configuration files and process/service manifests. Use automated tools to list current privileges assigned to critical processes and compare against documented minimum requirements.
   FAIL: Processes running with administrator or root privileges when their function does not demand such levels.

   CHECK: Confirm Logging and Monitoring of Privileged Functions
   WHY: Logging privileged functions provides necessary evidence of use and misuse, adding a layer of accountability and forensic capability.
   HOW: Inspect audit logs and SIEM dashboards to verify that executions of privileged commands and functions (especially those altering security settings) are logged. Use scripted queries to filter events related to privilege changes.
   FAIL: Absence of detailed logging entries for privileged actions or gaps in log retention that could hide misuse or policy deviations.

   CHECK: Check for Regular Review of User and Role Privileges
   WHY: Periodic reviews ensure that accounts do not accumulate privileges over time and that roles remain aligned with current business tasks.
   HOW: Validate records of privilege reviews (meeting minutes, automated audit trails in IAM systems) to ensure that reviews occur at the defined frequency. Interview administrators to confirm the process is actively followed.
   FAIL: Outdated review records or evidence of infrequent review cycles, suggesting that privilege creep may not be managed.

   CHECK: Assess Network Access Controls for Privileged Commands
   WHY: Network exposure of privileged commands can be a serious vulnerability if not tightly controlled and justified.
   HOW: Review network configuration settings and firewall rules to ensure that access to privileged commands is limited to authorized IP addresses or roles. Check the security plan for documented rationale behind any permitted remote access.
   FAIL: Unrestricted or poorly restricted network access to privileged interfaces or commands without clear documented operational need.

4. EXPERT TIPS
   • Verification Shortcut: Automate scans using configuration management and identity governance tools to obtain up-to-date listings of role allocations and process privileges. These tools can often provide a dashboard view for quick assessments of misconfigurations.
   • Critical Indicator: Look out for any anomalies such as generic or default accounts repeatedly used for high-privilege operations; these are clear signs of poor enforcement in practice.
   • Detail to Note: Ensure that documentation (such as in the security plan) is actively reflected in system configurations – policies that are not mirrored in the actual technical controls are a red flag.
   • Additional Advice: Consider conducting periodic pen-tests focusing on privilege escalation paths. They frequently uncover hidden privilege creep or misconfigurations missed by routine audits.

This methodology focuses on practical, observable evidence rather than mere documentation and is tailored to uncover superficial compliance that does not equate to true operational effectiveness in applying the principle of least privilege.
content:
Statement:
Employ the principle of least privilege, allowing only authorized accesses for users (or processes acting on behalf of users) that are necessary to accomplish assigned organizational tasks.

Guidance:
Organizations employ least privilege for specific duties and systems. The principle of least privilege is also applied to system processes, ensuring that the processes have access to systems and operate at privilege levels no higher than necessary to accomplish organizational missions or business functions. Organizations consider the creation of additional processes, roles, and accounts as necessary to achieve least privilege. Organizations apply least privilege to the development, implementation, and operation of organizational systems.

Enhancements:
(1) LEAST PRIVILEGE | AUTHORIZE ACCESS TO SECURITY FUNCTIONS  Authorize access for [Assignment: organization-defined individuals or roles] to:  (a) [Assignment: organization-defined security functions (deployed in hardware, software, and firmware)]; and  (b) [Assignment: organization-defined security-relevant information].
(2) LEAST PRIVILEGE | NON-PRIVILEGED ACCESS FOR NONSECURITY FUNCTIONS Require that users of system accounts (or roles) with access to [assignment: organization-defined security functions or security-relevant information] use non-privileged accounts or roles, when accessing nonsecurity functions.
(3) LEAST PRIVILEGE | NETWORK ACCESS TO PRIVILEGED COMMANDS Authorize network access to [assignment: organization-defined privileged commands] for [assignment: organization-defined compelling operational needs] and document the rationale for such access in the security plan for the system.
(4) LEAST PRIVILEGE | SEPARATE PROCESSING DOMAINS Provide separate processing domains to enable finer-grained allocation of user privileges.
(5) LEAST PRIVILEGE | PRIVILEGED ACCOUNTS Restrict privileged accounts on the system to [assignment: organization-defined personnel or roles].
(6) LEAST PRIVILEGE | PRIVILEGED ACCESS BY NON-ORGANIZATIONAL USERS Prohibit privileged access to the system by non-organizational users. Discussion: An organizational user is an employee or an individual considered by the organization to have the equivalent status of an employee.
